topics.title: Introduction
topics.content:
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is Oil and Natural Gas Engineering? **Petroleum Engineering** (often used interchangeably with Oil and Natural Gas Engineering) is the branch of engineering that applies scientific and engineering principles to the exploration, evaluation, development, recovery, transportation, and processing of hydrocarbon resources — primarily **crude oil (petroleum)** and **natural gas**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU 2024 Definition:** > *Oil and Natural Gas Engineering* is a multidisciplinary field integrating principles from geology, geophysics, drilling engineering, reservoir engineering, production engineering, and chemical engineering to locate, extract, transport, and process hydrocarbons safely, economically, and in an environmentally sustainable manner. According to the **Society of Petroleum Engineers (SPE)** — the global professional body — petroleum engineering is broadly classified into four major sub-disciplines: 1. **Drilling Engineering** 2. **Reservoir Engineering** 3. **Production Engineering** 4. **Formation Evaluation (Petrophysics / Well Logging)** --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Underground Treasure Mine" Think of the Earth as a layered cake. The **top icing** is the crust where we live. Beneath that lies the **cake layers** (sedimentary rock formations), and trapped within the porous sponge-like layers of rock are droplets of **oil** and pockets of **gas** — much like water trapped inside a kitchen sponge. To bring this treasure to the surface, engineers must: - **Locate** the sponge (Exploration & Geology) - **Drill** a straw through the cake (Drilling Engineering) - **Suck** the oil out efficiently (Reservoir & Production Engineering) - **Clean and transport** it (Pipeline & Refining Engineering) Just as you cannot drink from a sponge without applying pressure or a straw, oil cannot flow on its own without proper engineering intervention. That is the essence of Oil and Natural Gas Engineering. --- ## 1.3 What are Hydrocarbons? **Hydrocarbons** are organic compounds composed exclusively of **hydrogen (H)** and **carbon (C)** atoms. They form the chemical foundation of all fossil fuels. | Hydrocarbon Type | Chemical Formula | State at STP | |---|---|---| | Methane (simplest) | $\text{CH}_4$ | Gas | | Ethane | $\text{C}_2\text{H}_6$ | Gas | | Propane | $\text{C}_3\text{H}_8$ | Gas | | Butane | $\text{C}_4\text{H}_{10}$ | Gas | | Pentane – Octane | $\text{C}_5\text{H}_{12}$ – $\text{C}_8\text{H}_{18}$ | Liquid | | Heavy fractions (e.g., hexadecane) | $\text{C}_{16}\text{H}_{34}$ | Viscous liquid | > [!NOTE] > **Crude oil** and **natural gas** are mixtures of hundreds of hydrocarbons. Crude oil typically contains hydrocarbons in the range **C₅ to C₄₀+**, while natural gas is dominated by **methane (CH₄)**, with smaller amounts of ethane, propane, and butane. --- ## 1.4 Historical Perspective | Era | Milestone | |---|---| | **600 BCE – 1800s** | China, Baku (Azerbaijan) hand-dug wells; naturally seeping oil used for medicine, lighting. | | **1859** | **Colonel Edwin Drake** drilled the first modern commercial oil well at **Titusville, Pennsylvania, USA** — depth: **21.2 m (69.5 ft)**. This marked the birth of the modern petroleum industry. | | **Early 1900s** | Discovery of major fields in **Texas, Oklahoma, and California (Spindletop, 1901)**. | | **Mid 1900s** | Rise of **Middle Eastern** giants — Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, Iran, Iraq. | | **1959–1970s** | Discovery of **North Sea** oil and gas (UK, Norway). | | **1980s onward** | **Deepwater** and **ultra-deepwater** exploration; **shale revolution** (fracking, horizontal drilling). | | **2020s** | Focus on **decarbonization**, **CCUS (Carbon Capture, Utilization, Storage)**, and **green hydrogen**. | --- ## 1.5 Why is Oil and Natural Gas Engineering Important? > [!IMPORTANT] > Hydrocarbons currently supply **~80% of the world's primary energy** and are the raw material for **petrochemicals, fertilizers, plastics, lubricants, and pharmaceuticals**. KTU 2024 emphasises that petroleum engineers must balance three pillars often called the **"Energy Trilemma"**: 1. **Energy Security** — reliable supply. 2. **Energy Affordability** — economic viability. 3. **Environmental Sustainability** — minimal ecological footprint. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Petroleum Industry Value Chain The petroleum industry is conventionally divided into three integrated sectors, often described as **Upstream → Midstream → Downstream**. ### 2.1.1 Upstream (Exploration & Production — E&P) This is the **"finding and producing"** phase, where the engineer is closest to the reservoir. - **Exploration** — Geological and geophysical surveys, seismic interpretation. - **Appraisal** — Delineation drilling to confirm reservoir size. - **Development** — Drilling production and injection wells. - **Production** — Lifting hydrocarbons to the surface. - **Abandonment** — Plugging wells when the field is depleted. ### 2.1.2 Midstream (Transportation & Storage) - **Pipelines** (trunk and gathering lines). - **Tanker ships** (VLCCs, Suezmax, Aframax). - **Storage tanks** and **LNG terminals**. - **Compressor stations** and **pumping stations**. ### 2.1.3 Downstream (Refining & Marketing) - **Oil refineries** (distillation, cracking, reforming). - **Petrochemical plants** (ethylene, propylene, aromatics). - **Gas processing plants** (NGL recovery, LNG liquefaction). - **Retail marketing** (fuel stations, lubricants, LPG distribution). > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Tip:** When asked to "describe the petroleum value chain," students often forget **midstream** and merge it with downstream. Always mention all three explicitly. --- ## 2.2 The Seven Sisters to Seven Major Players — Industry Structure Historically, the post-WWII oil industry was controlled by the **"Seven Sisters"**: 1. Standard Oil of New Jersey (Exxon) 2. Standard Oil of New York (Mobil) 3. Standard Oil of California (Chevron) 4. Texaco 5. Gulf Oil 6. Royal Dutch Shell 7. Anglo-Persian Oil Company (BP) After the **OPEC formation (1960)** and the **1973 oil crisis**, **National Oil Companies (NOCs)** such as **Saudi Aramco, ADNOC, Petrobras, PetroChina, ONGC, and Kuwait Petroleum Corporation** rose to dominate global reserves and production. Today the industry is described as the **"Super-Majors"** model: *ExxonMobil, Chevron, Shell, BP, TotalEnergies, ConocoPhillips, and Equinor (Statoil)* — alongside NOCs. --- ## 2.3 Classification of Crude Oil ### 2.3.1 By API Gravity The **API (American Petroleum Institute) gravity** measures how heavy or light a crude oil is: $$\text{°API} = \frac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$$ where $SG$ is the **specific gravity** (density of oil ÷ density of water at 60 °F, where $\rho_{\text{water}} = 999 \text{ kg/m}^3$). | Classification | °API Range | Example | |---|---|---| | Light crude | API > 31.1° | Brent, WTI | | Medium crude | 22.3° ≤ API ≤ 31.1° | Arab Medium | | Heavy crude | 10° ≤ API < 22.3° | Maya, Cold Lake | | Extra-heavy | API < 10° | Orinoco Bitumen | > Light crude flows easily and yields more gasoline. Heavy crude is viscous, often requires **thermal enhanced oil recovery (EOR)**. ### 2.3.2 By Sulfur Content - **Sweet crude** — sulfur content < 0.5 % wt → less processing, more valuable. - **Sour crude** — sulfur content ≥ 0.5 % wt → corrosive, requires desulfurization. ### 2.3.3 By Paraffin / Asphaltene Content - **Paraffinic** — high wax content (e.g., North Sea crudes). - **Asphaltic** — high asphaltene content (e.g., Mexican Maya). - **Naphthenic** — high cycloparaffin content (e.g., certain Russian crudes). --- ## 2.4 Classification of Natural Gas | Type | Description | |---|---| | **Associated Gas** | Co-produced with crude oil from the same reservoir. | | **Non-associated (Free) Gas** | Produced from a gas reservoir with little or no oil. | | **Dissolved Gas** | Held in solution within the crude oil under reservoir pressure. | | **Gas Cap Gas** | Free gas that overlies the oil column in a reservoir. | | **Shale Gas** | Trapped in low-permeability shale formations. | | **Tight Gas** | Held in low-permeability sandstone/carbonate. | | **Coalbed Methane (CBM)** | Adsorbed onto coal surfaces. | | **Biogas / Landfill Gas** | Produced from anaerobic decomposition of organic matter. | --- ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | API Gravity | $\text{°API} = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$ | dimensionless | SG at 60 °F | | 2 | Specific Gravity | $SG = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{oil}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}}$ | dimensionless | $\rho_{\text{water}} = 999 \text{ kg/m}^3$ | | 3 | Hydrocarbon Series (Alkane) | $\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2}$ | — | Generic paraffin | | 4 | Boiling Point Trend | Increases with n | °C | For $n \ge 4$ | | 5 | Energy Content of Crude Oil | $E \approx 42 \text{ GJ/m}^3$ (approx.) | GJ/m³ | For light crude | | 6 | Energy Content of Natural Gas | $E \approx 38.3 \text{ MJ/m}^3$ (approx.) | MJ/m³ | Pure CH₄, STP | | 7 | Barrel → m³ Conversion | $1 \text{ bbl} = 0.158987 \text{ m}^3$ | m³/bbl | Standard petroleum barrel | | 8 | BOE (Barrel of Oil Equivalent) | $1 \text{ BOE} = 5.8 \times 10^6 \text{ BTU}$ | BTU | 1 BOE ≈ 6,000 ft³ of gas | > [!IMPORTANT] > Always keep consistent units. KTU problems are often based on **barrels (bbl)**, **standard cubic feet (scf)**, **pounds per square inch (psi)**, and **°F**. Use conversion factors: $1 \text{ atm} = 14.7 \text{ psi} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$, and $T(°F) = 1.8 \cdot T(°C) + 32$. --- ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility | Sector | Use Case | |---|---| | **Power Generation** | Natural gas-fired combined cycle plants (~60% efficiency). | | **Transportation** | Petrol, diesel, jet fuel, LNG/CNG vehicles. | | **Petrochemicals** | Feedstock for plastics, synthetic rubber, fertilizers. | | **Industrial Heating** | Furnaces, boilers, process heat. | | **Lubricants** | Engine oils, greases, hydraulic fluids. | | **Asphalt & Bitumen** | Road construction, roofing. | | **Pharmaceuticals** | Solvents, precursors for drugs. | | **Agriculture** | Urea and ammonia-based fertilizers. | # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of API Gravity Formula ### Step 1 — Starting Point The **specific gravity (SG)** of crude oil is defined as the ratio of oil density to water density at a reference temperature of **60 °F (15.56 °C)**: $$SG = \frac{\rho_{\text{oil}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}}$$ At 60 °F, $\rho_{\text{water}} = 999 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (≈ 1 g/cm³). ### Step 2 — Conversion to API Scale The API scale was developed in 1921 by the **American Petroleum Institute** to make the density scale more intuitive. The empirical relation is: $$\text{°API} = \frac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$$ ### Step 3 — Physical Interpretation - If $\text{°API} > 10$, oil is **lighter than water** (floats on water — typical of crude oil). - If $\text{°API} < 10$, oil is **heavier than water** (sinks — typical of extra-heavy crude and bitumen). - If $\text{°API} = 10$, $SG = 1$ (density equals water). ### Step 4 — Example Numerical Evaluation **Problem:** A crude oil has $SG = 0.85$ at 60 °F. Calculate its API gravity. **Solution:** $$\text{°API} = \frac{141.5}{0.85} - 131.5$$ $$= 166.4706 - 131.5$$ $$= 34.97 \text{ °API}$$ **Conclusion:** This is a **light crude** (since °API > 31.1°), with high gasoline yield upon refining. ### Step 5 — Reverse Calculation **Problem:** A crude oil has $\text{°API} = 28$. Find its specific gravity. **Solution:** $$28 = \frac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$$ $$\frac{141.5}{SG} = 28 + 131.5 = 159.5$$ $$SG = \frac{141.5}{159.5} = 0.8871$$ So the oil is a **medium crude**, slightly heavier than the previous example. --- ## 3.2 Derivation of the General Alkane Formula The alkane (paraffin) family follows the general formula: $$\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$$ ### Step 1 — Methane ($n = 1$) $\text{CH}_{2(1)+2} = \text{CH}_4$ — the principal component of natural gas. ### Step 2 — Ethane ($n = 2$) $\text{C}_2\text{H}_{2(2)+2} = \text{C}_2\text{H}_6$ — second-largest component of natural gas. ### Step 3 — Propane ($n = 3$) $\text{C}_3\text{H}_{2(3)+2} = \text{C}_3\text{H}_8$ — major LPG component. ### Step 4 — Butane ($n = 4$) $\text{C}_4\text{H}_{2(4)+2} = \text{C}_4\text{H}_{10}$ — also LPG; has two isomers (n-butane and iso-butane). ### Step 5 — General Pattern Verification For any $n$: $$\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2}$$ Adding one $\text{CH}_2$ unit per increment of $n$ is the basis for the **homologous series** of hydrocarbons, leading to smooth, predictable changes in physical properties such as boiling point, density, and viscosity. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Conversions | Quantity | From | To | Conversion | Sample Value | |---|---|---|---|---| | Volume | 1 barrel (bbl) | m³ | $0.158987$ | 1 bbl ≈ 0.159 m³ | | Volume | 1 m³ | bbl | $6.2898$ | 1 m³ ≈ 6.29 bbl | | Gas volume | 1 scf | m³ | $0.02832$ | 1 scf ≈ 0.02832 m³ | | Energy | 1 BOE | BTU | $5.8 \times 10^6$ | — | | Energy | 1 BTU | kJ | $1.055$ | — | | Pressure | 1 psi | kPa | $6.895$ | — | | Pressure | 1 atm | psi | $14.7$ | — | | Pressure | 1 atm | kPa | $101.325$ | — | | Temperature | °F | °C | $\dfrac{(°F - 32)}{1.8}$ | 32 °F = 0 °C | | Mass | 1 short ton | kg | $907.185$ | — | | Mass | 1 long ton | kg | $1016.05$ | — | | Mass | 1 metric ton (tonne) | kg | $1000$ | — | > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU often tests simple unit-conversion problems in Module 1. Memorise the barrel ↔ m³ and scf ↔ m³ conversions. ### 3.3.1 Sample Numerical Problem **Problem:** A pipeline delivers $50{,}000$ bbl/day of crude oil. Express this flow rate in $\text{m}^3/\text{day}$ and $\text{m}^3/\text{hr}$. **Solution:** $$Q = 50{,}000 \text{ bbl/day} \times 0.158987 \frac{\text{m}^3}{\text{bbl}} = 7949.35 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$ Converting to per hour: $$Q = \frac{7949.35}{24} = 331.22 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$$ --- ## 3.4 Estimation of Hydrocarbon Molecular Weight The average molecular weight of a crude oil can be estimated by the **Riazi-Daubert** correlation (a standard reference in petroleum refining): $$MW = 0.0000417 \cdot (T_b)^{2.1962} \cdot (SG)^{-1.0164}$$ where $T_b$ is the **volume-average boiling point in °R** and $SG$ is the specific gravity. This is a foundational equation that later modules in **Refining Engineering** will use extensively. KTU typically asks for a conceptual understanding here, not the full numerical evaluation. --- ## 3.5 Energy Content Comparison A useful introduction-level calculation is comparing the energy content of fuel oil vs. natural gas. Given: - Crude oil: $E_{\text{oil}} = 42 \text{ GJ/m}^3$ - Natural gas (pure CH₄): $E_{\text{gas}} = 38.3 \text{ MJ/m}^3$ To compare on a per-tonne basis (assuming $SG_{\text{oil}} = 0.85$ and $MW_{\text{CH}_4} = 16.04 \text{ g/mol}$): - Mass of 1 m³ oil: $m = 0.85 \times 999 = 849.15 \text{ kg}$ - Energy per kg of oil: $E = \dfrac{42 \text{ GJ/m}^3}{0.849 \text{ tonne/m}^3} \approx 49.47 \text{ MJ/kg}$ - Density of CH₄ at STP: $\rho_{\text{CH}_4} = \dfrac{16.04}{22.414} = 0.7155 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - Energy per kg of CH₄: $E = \dfrac{38.3 \text{ MJ/m}^3}{0.7155 \text{ kg/m}^3} \approx 53.53 \text{ MJ/kg}$ **Conclusion:** On a per-kg basis, methane delivers more energy than crude oil, but crude oil is more energy-dense per unit volume — a key reason why liquid fuels still dominate transportation. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Petroleum Industry Value Chain (Upstream → Midstream → Downstream) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Exploration
Seismic Surveys] --> B[Appraisal Drilling
Well Testing] B --> C[Development
Drilling and Completion] C --> D[Production
Oil and Gas Lifting] D --> E[Primary Separation
Oil Gas Water] E --> F[Gathering Pipelines
and Storage Tanks] F --> G[Trunk Pipeline
Tanker Ship Transport] G --> H[Refinery and
Gas Processing Plant] H --> I[Petrochemical
Plant] H --> J[Fuel Marketing
Lubricants LPG] I --> K[Plastics and
Fertilizers] J --> L[End Consumer] K --> L classDef up fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,color:#000 classDef mid fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,color:#000 classDef down fill:#d4edda,stroke:#1e7e34,color:#000 class A,B,C,D,E up class F,G mid class H,I,J,K,L down ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Hydrocarbon Classification ```mermaid flowchart TD H[Hydrocarbons] --> A[Aliphatic] H --> A2[Aromatic] A --> S[Saturated Alkanes
Paraffins CnH2n+2] A --> U[Unsaturated] A --> C[Cycloparaffins
Naphthenes CnH2n] U --> ALK[Alkenes CnH2n] U --> ALK2[Alkynes CnH2n-2] S --> M1[Methane CH4] S --> E1[Ethane C2H6] S --> P1[Propane C3H8] S --> B1[Butane C4H10] S --> W1[Heavy Waxes C20 to C40] A2 --> BZ[Benzene C6H6] A2 --> TL[Toluene C7H8] A2 --> XL[Xylene C8H10] classDef sat fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 classDef unsat fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000 classDef arom fill:#d1e7dd,stroke:#198754,color:#000 class S,M1,E1,P1,B1,W1 sat class U,ALK,ALK2 unsat class A2,BZ,TL,XL arom ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Crude Oil Classification Matrix ```mermaid flowchart LR CR[Crude Oil] --> AP[API Gravity] CR --> SU[Sulfur Content] CR --> PA[Paraffin Asphaltene] AP --> LI[Light API greater 31] AP --> ME[Medium 22 to 31] AP --> HV[Heavy 10 to 22] AP --> XH[Extra Heavy API less 10] SU --> SW[Sweet S less 0.5 percent] SU --> SO[Sour S greater 0.5 percent] PA --> PR[Paraffinic] PA --> AS[Asphaltic] PA --> NA[Naphthenic] classDef good fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,color:#000 classDef mid fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#ffc107,color:#000 classDef bad fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,color:#000 class LI,SW,PR good class ME,NA mid class HV,XH,SO,AS bad ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram — Typical Roles of a Petroleum Engineer ```mermaid flowchart TD PE[Petroleum Engineer] --> DE[Drilling Engineer] PE --> RE[Reservoir Engineer] PE --> PR[Production Engineer] PE --> FE[Formation Evaluation
Petrophysicist] DE --> D1[Well Design] DE --> D2[Drilling Fluids] DE --> D3[Casing and Cementing] RE --> R1[Reserve Estimation] RE --> R2[Production Forecasting] RE --> R3[EOR Methods] PR --> P1[Artificial Lift] PR --> P2[Surface Facilities] PR --> P3[Flow Assurance] FE --> F1[Well Log Analysis] FE --> F2[Core Analysis] FE --> F3[Rock Property Tests] classDef core fill:#cfe2ff,stroke:#0d6efd,color:#000 class DE,RE,PR,FE core ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — June 2023 Model]** *Define petroleum engineering. List its four major sub-disciplines.* **Model Answer:** Petroleum Engineering is the branch of engineering concerned with the exploration, drilling, evaluation, production, and transportation of hydrocarbon resources (crude oil and natural gas). The four major sub-disciplines are: 1. **Drilling Engineering** — designing and executing well drilling operations. 2. **Reservoir Engineering** — estimating reserves and forecasting production. 3. **Production Engineering** — managing fluid flow from reservoir to surface. 4. **Formation Evaluation / Petrophysics** — analysing subsurface rock and fluid properties. *(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for the four sub-disciplines, 0.5 each.)* --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Model]** *Distinguish between sweet and sour crude oil. State one example of each.* **Model Answer:** | Property | Sweet Crude | Sour Crude | |---|---|---| | Sulfur content | < 0.5 % wt | ≥ 0.5 % wt | | Corrosivity | Low | High — requires special metallurgy | | Refining cost | Lower | Higher (desulfurization needed) | | Example | West Texas Intermediate (WTI), Brent | Arab Heavy, Maya (Mexico) | *(3 marks: 2 for distinction + 1 for examples.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) ### Question A — (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]** **(a)** Describe the **petroleum industry value chain**, clearly distinguishing between upstream, midstream, and downstream operations. Provide **two example activities** for each sector. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A crude oil sample has a specific gravity of **0.88 at 60 °F**. Calculate its **API gravity** and classify it. If the same oil is delivered at a rate of **25,000 bbl/day**, calculate the flow rate in **m³/hr**. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution — Part (a) The petroleum value chain has three interlinked sectors: 1. **Upstream (Exploration & Production, E&P)** — Activities aimed at finding and producing hydrocarbons. - *Example 1:* 2D / 3D seismic surveys to identify subsurface structures. - *Example 2:* Drilling and completing production wells. 2. **Midstream (Transportation & Storage)** — Activities linking production sites to processing plants and markets. - *Example 1:* Trunk pipelines (e.g., 48-inch crude trunk lines from offshore platforms to onshore terminals). - *Example 2:* Crude oil tankers (VLCCs) and LNG carrier vessels. 3. **Downstream (Refining & Marketing)** — Activities that convert raw hydrocarbons into finished products. - *Example 1:* Crude oil distillation in refineries (yielding naphtha, diesel, kerosene, etc.). - *Example 2:* Petrochemical plants producing ethylene and polyethylene. *(7 marks: 1 each for definition + 2 each per sector with 2 examples.)* #### Model Solution — Part (b) **Step 1 — API Gravity Calculation** $$\text{°API} = \frac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$$ $$= \frac{141.5}{0.88} - 131.5$$ $$= 160.7955 - 131.5 = 29.2955 \text{ °API}$$ $$\boxed{\text{°API} \approx 29.30}$$ **Step 2 — Classification** Since $22.3° \le \text{°API} \le 31.1°$, the crude oil is a **Medium Crude**. **Step 3 — Convert 25,000 bbl/day to m³/hr** $$Q = 25{,}000 \text{ bbl/day} \times 0.158987 \frac{\text{m}^3}{\text{bbl}}$$ $$= 3974.68 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$ $$Q = \frac{3974.68}{24} = 165.61 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}$$ $$\boxed{Q \approx 165.61 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr}}$$ *(7 marks: 3 for API + 1 for classification + 3 for unit conversion.)* --- ### Question B — (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]** **(a)** Define **crude oil** and **natural gas**. Classify natural gas into **at least four categories** with one-line definitions. *(7 marks)* **(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **homologous series of alkanes (paraffins)**. State the general formula, and list the first four members of the series with their molecular weights. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution — Part (a) **Definition of Crude Oil:** Crude oil is a naturally occurring, flammable liquid mixture of hydrocarbons (predominantly alkanes, cycloalkanes, and aromatics) found in geological formations beneath the Earth's surface. It is refined to produce petrol, diesel, kerosene, lubricants, and petrochemical feedstocks. **Definition of Natural Gas:** Natural gas is a naturally occurring mixture of light hydrocarbons (mainly methane, with smaller fractions of ethane, propane, and butane) found in porous reservoir rocks, often in association with crude oil or in dry gas fields. **Classification of Natural Gas (4 categories):** | Type | Definition | |---|---| | Associated Gas | Free gas produced along with crude oil from the same reservoir. | | Non-Associated Gas | Gas produced from a reservoir that contains no (or negligible) crude oil. | | Shale Gas | Gas trapped within fine-grained, low-permeability shale formations. | | Coalbed Methane (CBM) | Methane adsorbed onto the surface of coal seams. | *(7 marks: 1+1 for definitions, 1.25 each for four classifications.)* #### Model Solution — Part (b) **The Alkane Homologous Series:** Alkanes form a **homologous series** — a family of compounds that differ from one another by a repeating **—CH₂—** unit. They are **saturated hydrocarbons** (only single C–C and C–H bonds). **General Formula:** $$\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$$ **First Four Members:** | n | Name | Formula | Molecular Weight (g/mol) | State at STP | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Methane | $\text{CH}_4$ | 16.04 | Gas | | 2 | Ethane | $\text{C}_2\text{H}_6$ | 30.07 | Gas | | 3 | Propane | $\text{C}_3\text{H}_8$ | 44.10 | Gas | | 4 | n-Butane | $\text{C}_4\text{H}_{10}$ | 58.12 | Gas (LPG component) | **Trend in Physical Properties (to be mentioned):** - Boiling point increases with molecular weight. - Density increases with molecular weight. - Viscosity increases with chain length. *(7 marks: 2 for general formula, 2 for first four members, 1 for molecular weights, 2 for trend explanation.)* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 1:** > 1. **Forgetting the "Midstream" sector** — many students merge transportation with downstream. Always explicitly name all three (Upstream, Midstream, Downstream) and give distinct activities. > 2. **Mixing up API gravity vs. specific gravity** — they are inversely related. A higher API means *lighter* oil, not heavier. Don't confuse the direction. > 3. **Barrel vs. m³ conversion mistakes** — $1 \text{ bbl} = 0.158987 \text{ m}^3$, NOT $0.159$ or $0.16$ in the final answer. Show the multiplication explicitly. > 4. **Time conversion error** — to convert "per day" to "per hour", *divide* by 24, not multiply. This is a classic blunder. > 5. **Wrong gas-classification labelling** — *Coalbed Methane* and *Shale Gas* are *unconventional* gases; labelling them simply as "natural gas" loses marks. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition**: Petroleum engineering deals with locating, drilling, producing, transporting, and processing hydrocarbons (oil & gas). - **Four sub-disciplines**: Drilling, Reservoir, Production, Formation Evaluation. - **Industry value chain**: **Upstream → Midstream → Downstream** — never omit midstream. - **Crude oil classification**: - **API Gravity** formula: $\text{°API} = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$. - Light: °API > 31.1° | Medium: 22.3°–31.1° | Heavy: 10°–22.3° | Extra-heavy: < 10°. - **Sweet** (< 0.5% S) vs **Sour** (≥ 0.5% S). - **Natural gas classification**: associated, non-associated, dissolved, gas cap, shale, tight, CBM, biogas. - **Homologous alkane series**: $\text{C}_n\text{H}_{2n+2}$ — add one CH₂ per step. - **Key historical milestone**: **Edwin Drake's well, 1859, Titusville, Pennsylvania** — the modern petroleum industry begins here. - **Key unit conversions**: $1 \text{ bbl} = 0.158987 \text{ m}^3$, $1 \text{ scf} = 0.02832 \text{ m}^3$, $1 \text{ atm} = 14.7 \text{ psi} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$, $1 \text{ BOE} = 5.8 \times 10^6 \text{ BTU}$. - **Energy trilemma**: Security + Affordability + Sustainability — the modern KTU framing. - **Seven Sisters → Super-Majors + NOCs**: industry structure history is a frequent short-answer topic. - **Hydrocarbon families**: Alkanes, Alkenes, Alkynes, Cycloparaffins (Naphthenes), Aromatics — know one example each. > [!TIP] > **Last-minute revision mnemonic for API gravity classification:** > *"L-Light is 31"*, *"M-Medium is 22"*, *"H-Heavy is 10"* > → *Light > 31.1°*, *Medium 22.3° to 31.1°*, *Heavy 10° to 22.3°*, *Extra-Heavy < 10°.*

topics.title: Origin
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT528 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Origin

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Petroleum Origin** refers to the geological, geochemical, and thermodynamic processes through which crude oil and natural gas (hydrocarbons) are generated from organic matter buried in sedimentary basins over geological timescales (typically **1 million to 500 million years**). The scientific study of this transformation is termed **Organic Geochemistry**, and it rests fundamentally on the **Organic (Biogenic) Theory** of petroleum genesis, which states that hydrocarbons are derived from the thermal maturation of buried organic matter (kerogen) under specific subsurface conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Expectation):**
> The origin of petroleum involves the conversion of organic-rich sediments into hydrocarbon fluids through a process of deposition, burial, thermal maturation, and migration. The currently accepted scientific framework is the **Organic Theory**, supported by isotopic, optical, and biomarker evidence.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Slow Cooking of Ancient Soup"

Imagine you place a mixture of vegetables, meat, and broth in a sealed pressure cooker, bury it deep underground, and leave it for **100 million years**. The heat from the Earth's interior gradually "cooks" the organic mixture, breaking large molecules into smaller oil and gas molecules.

- **The vegetables and meat** = Ancient microscopic marine algae, plankton, and bacteria (called **kerogen precursors**).
- **The sealed pressure cooker** = An impermeable sedimentary rock layer (e.g., shale) that traps the transforming organic matter.
- **The cooking heat** = Earth's geothermal gradient (**~25–30 °C/km** on average).
- **The slow cooking time** = Millions of years of geological time.
- **The broth that forms (oil & gas)** = Crude oil and natural gas, which eventually migrate out of the "cooker" (source rock) and accumulate in a "serving vessel" (reservoir rock).

Just as a pressure cooker needs the right heat, time, and sealed conditions, petroleum formation requires a precise **temperature window, time duration, pressure, and reducing (oxygen-free) environment**.

## 1.3 Historical Context — Two Competing Theories

> [!NOTE]
> **Two Theories of Origin — Critical Exam Question**

| Aspect | **Organic (Biogenic) Theory** | **Inorganic (Abiogenic) Theory** |
|---|---|---|
| **Origin of Carbon** | Derived from once-living organisms | Derived from primordial Earth's mantle carbon |
| **Original Source** | Marine algae, plankton, bacteria | Deep mantle hydrocarbons, methane |
| **Proposed By** | Multiple — supported by modern geochemistry | **Mendeleev (1876)** and **Berthelot (1866)** |
| **Modern Status** | ✅ **Universally Accepted** | ❌ Largely rejected (only minor methane in deep crust) |
| **Key Evidence** | Optical activity of oil, biomarkers, carbon isotope ratios, porphyrins | Presence of hydrocarbons in meteorites/space |
| **Application in E&P** | Used in all exploration, basin modeling, and source rock evaluation | Negligible — not used in upstream operations |

## 1.4 Source Material — What Becomes Oil?

The original organic matter that generates petroleum is broadly classified into two families:

1. **Sapropelic (Planktonic) Material** — Marine and lacustrine algae, spores, pollen, and bacterial residues. This is the **primary source** of crude oil.
2. **Humic (Coaly/Vegetal) Material** — Woody plant tissues, lignin. This primarily generates **natural gas (methane)** and coal.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for Exams:** "**S**apropelic = **S**ea algae = **S**ticky **O**il" and "**H**umic = **H**ardwood = **H**eat → **G**as"

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Essential Conditions for Petroleum Generation

Petroleum formation is governed by a strict set of geological conditions. Failure of any one condition halts the process.

### Condition 1 — **Organic-Rich Source Rock**
- A source rock must contain sufficient organic matter, quantified by **Total Organic Carbon (TOC)**.
- **Threshold:** TOC **> 0.5 wt%** (lean) to **> 2.0 wt%** (rich/good source rock).
- **Kerogen Yield** is the fraction of kerogen that converts to hydrocarbons.

### Condition 2 — **Anaerobic (Oxygen-Free) Environment**
- Rapid burial of organic matter in anoxic environments (e.g., deep marine basins, stratified seas, swamps) prevents oxidation and decay.
- Preserved organic matter accumulates in fine-grained sediments (shales, marls, carbonates).

### Condition 3 — **Subsidence and Burial**
- Continuous deposition of overlying sediments increases pressure and temperature.
- Burial depths typically range from **1,000 m to 5,000 m** for oil generation, and **> 4,000 m** for gas generation.

### Condition 4 — **Temperature Window (The "Oil Window")**
- Hydrocarbon generation occurs within a specific thermal window:
  - **Oil Window:** **60 °C – 150 °C**
  - **Gas Window:** **120 °C – 225 °C**
  - **Below 60 °C:** Immature kerogen (no hydrocarbons generated)
  - **Above 225 °C:** Over-mature zone — only dry methane and graphite remain

### Condition 5 — **Time**
- Geological time (typically **10 million to 100+ million years**) is required for the slow cracking of kerogen.
- **Lopatin's Principle:** Higher temperature for a short time can equal lower temperature for a long time.

## 2.2 The Three Stages of Kerogen Maturation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three-Stage Transformation Sequence (High-Yield Exam Topic):**

### Stage 1 — **Diagenesis (0 °C – 60 °C)**
- Biochemical and weak thermal reactions.
- Conversion of biopolymers → kerogen.
- Generation of **biogenic methane** (e.g., marsh gas).
- **No significant oil generation.**

### Stage 2 — **Catagenesis (60 °C – 150 °C)**
- Thermal cracking of kerogen.
- Generation of **crude oil and wet gas** (condensate).
- Peak oil generation occurs at **~100 °C – 120 °C**.
- This is the **"oil window"** proper.

### Stage 3 — **Metagenesis (150 °C – 225 °C)**
- Cracking of heavier hydrocarbons into lighter gases.
- Generation of **dry thermal methane**.
- Beyond 225 °C → only graphite (carbon) remains.

## 2.3 Kerogen Types — The Van Krevelen Classification

> [!NOTE]
> **Van Krevelen Diagram — A MUST-KNOW for KTU Exams**

Kerogen is classified into **four types** based on elemental **H/C (hydrogen-to-carbon)** and **O/C (oxygen-to-carbon)** atomic ratios. This is visualized on the **Van Krevelen Diagram**.

| Kerogen Type | H/C Ratio | O/C Ratio | Source Material | Primary Product | Depositional Setting |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| **Type I (Algal)** | **> 1.5** | **< 0.1** | Algae, microbial mats (lacustrine) | **Oil-prone** (high yield) | Lakes, lagoons |
| **Type II (Planktonic)** | **1.2 – 1.5** | **0.1 – 0.2** | Marine plankton, spores | **Oil & Gas** (balanced) | Marine shelves, deep basins |
| **Type III (Humic)** | **< 0.8** | **> 0.2** | Terrestrial plants, lignin | **Gas-prone** | Deltaic, swamp, fluvial |
| **Type IV (Inert)** | **< 0.5** | Variable | Oxidized/recycled organic matter | **None** (dead carbon) | Oxidizing environments |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master These Equations — They Appear in Numerical and Conceptual Questions**

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Units / Notes |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Time-Temperature Index (TTI)** | $\text{TTI} = \sum \Delta t_n \cdot r^{n}$ where $r = 2^{\frac{T_n - 105}{10}}$ | Dimensionless; $\Delta t$ in My, T in °C |
| 2 | **Vitrinite Reflectance (Ro) Gradient** | $\text{Ro} \approx 0.6 \cdot e^{0.0000496 \cdot z}$ | z = depth in meters; empirical |
| 3 | **Geothermal Gradient** | $\frac{dT}{dz} = \frac{T_z - T_s}{z}$ | Typical: **25–30 °C/km** |
| 4 | **Heat Flow (Fourier's Law)** | $q = -k \cdot \frac{dT}{dz}$ | $q$ in mW/m²; $k$ = thermal conductivity (W/m·K) |
| 5 | **TOC Classification** | $\text{TOC} = \frac{\text{Mass of Organic Carbon}}{\text{Mass of Rock}} \times 100\%$ | wt% |
| 6 | **Transformation Ratio (TR)** | $\text{TR} = \frac{S_1}{S_1 + S_2}$ | From Rock-Eval pyrolysis |
| 7 | **Hydrogen Index (HI)** | $\text{HI} = \frac{S_2}{\text{TOC}} \times 100$ | mg HC/g TOC |
| 8 | **Production Index (PI)** | $\text{PI} = \frac{S_1}{S_1 + S_2}$ | Threshold for commercial HC: PI **> 0.1** |

## 2.5 Lopatin's Time-Temperature Index (TTI) — Detailed

**Waples' Modified Lopatin Method:**

$$\text{TTI} = \sum_{n=\min}^{\max} \left[ \Delta t_n \cdot 2^{\frac{T_n - 105}{10}} \right]$$

Where:
- $\Delta t_n$ = time spent in the $n^{\text{th}}$ temperature interval (millions of years)
- $T_n$ = average temperature of that interval (°C)
- The reference temperature is **105 °C** (every 10 °C doubles the reaction rate)
- **Oil window:** TTI = **15 – 75**
- **Peak oil:** TTI ≈ **75**
- **Gas window:** TTI = **75 – 160**
- **Overmature:** TTI **> 160**

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Exploration Geochemistry:** Source rock evaluation determines whether a basin has potential for oil/gas.
- **Basin Modeling:** Petroleum systems modeling (e.g., **PetroMod, Trinity, BasinMod**) uses TTI and Ro to predict hydrocarbon generation timing.
- **Reservoir Risk Assessment:** Identifying mature source rocks reduces dry-hole risk.
- **Unconventional Resources:** Shale oil/gas plays depend entirely on the presence of mature, organic-rich source rocks.
- **Carbon Sequestration:** Understanding kerogen helps in CO₂ storage in depleted source rocks.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Lopatin's Time-Temperature Index (TTI) for a Burial History

### Problem Setup
A source rock passes through five temperature intervals during its burial history. Calculate the cumulative TTI and determine the maturity stage.

| Interval $n$ | Time $\Delta t_n$ (My) | Temperature $T_n$ (°C) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 10 | 50 |
| 2 | 15 | 75 |
| 3 | 20 | 100 |
| 4 | 25 | 125 |
| 5 | 10 | 150 |

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Recall the TTI formula:

$$\text{TTI} = \sum_{n=1}^{5} \Delta t_n \cdot 2^{\frac{T_n - 105}{10}}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the rate factor $r_n = 2^{(T_n - 105)/10}$ for each interval.

**Interval 1:** $T_1 = 50$ °C
$$r_1 = 2^{\frac{50 - 105}{10}} = 2^{-5.5} = \frac{1}{2^{5.5}} = \frac{1}{45.255} \approx 0.02210$$

**Interval 2:** $T_2 = 75$ °C
$$r_2 = 2^{\frac{75 - 105}{10}} = 2^{-3} = \frac{1}{8} = 0.12500$$

**Interval 3:** $T_3 = 100$ °C
$$r_3 = 2^{\frac{100 - 105}{10}} = 2^{-0.5} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.70711$$

**Interval 4:** $T_4 = 125$ °C
$$r_4 = 2^{\frac{125 - 105}{10}} = 2^{+2} = 4.00000$$

**Interval 5:** $T_5 = 150$ °C
$$r_5 = 2^{\frac{150 - 105}{10}} = 2^{+4.5} = 22.6274$$

**Step 3:** Compute the contribution $\Delta t_n \cdot r_n$ for each interval.

$$\text{TTI}_1 = 10 \times 0.02210 = 0.2210$$

$$\text{TTI}_2 = 15 \times 0.12500 = 1.8750$$

$$\text{TTI}_3 = 20 \times 0.70711 = 14.1421$$

$$\text{TTI}_4 = 25 \times 4.00000 = 100.0000$$

$$\text{TTI}_5 = 10 \times 22.6274 = 226.2740$$

**Step 4:** Sum all contributions to obtain the total TTI.

$$\text{TTI}_{\text{total}} = 0.2210 + 1.8750 + 14.1421 + 100.0000 + 226.2740$$

$$\boxed{\text{TTI}_{\text{total}} \approx 342.51}$$

**Step 5:** Interpret the maturity stage.

- Reference thresholds: Oil window = 15–75; Peak oil ≈ 75; Gas window = 75–160; Overmature > 160
- TTI = 342.51 **>>> 160** → **Overmature Stage (Dry Gas / Graphite Zone)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** The contributions from the higher-temperature intervals (4 and 5) dominate the TTI sum by orders of magnitude because of the exponential nature of the rate factor. This is why **recent deep burial** is more effective at maturing source rocks than prolonged shallow burial.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Estimating Depth of Oil Window from Geothermal Gradient

### Problem Setup
A basin has a surface temperature of **20 °C** and a geothermal gradient of **30 °C/km**. Calculate the depth at which the **top of the oil window (60 °C)** and **peak oil generation (120 °C)** occur.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Recall the linear geotherm equation:

$$T_z = T_s + \left(\frac{dT}{dz}\right) \cdot z$$

Where:
- $T_z$ = temperature at depth $z$ (°C)
- $T_s$ = surface temperature (°C) = **20 °C**
- $\frac{dT}{dz}$ = geothermal gradient (°C/km) = **30 °C/km**

**Step 2:** Solve for depth $z$:

$$z = \frac{T_z - T_s}{dT/dz}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate depth for top of oil window ($T_z = 60$ °C):

$$z_{60} = \frac{60 - 20}{30} = \frac{40}{30} = 1.333 \text{ km}$$

$$\boxed{z_{60} \approx 1333 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate depth for peak oil generation ($T_z = 120$ °C):

$$z_{120} = \frac{120 - 20}{30} = \frac{100}{30} = 3.333 \text{ km}$$

$$\boxed{z_{120} \approx 3333 \text{ m}}$$

**Step 5:** Engineering Interpretation.
- The "oil window" in this basin lies between **~1.33 km and ~3.33 km** depth.
- Below 3.33 km, the source rock enters the **gas window**.
- This depth range is the **exploration sweet spot** for oil prospects.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maturity Assessment using Vitrinite Reflectance (Ro)

### Problem Setup
A source rock sample is recovered from a well at depth **3,500 m**. The measured vitrinite reflectance is **Ro = 0.85%**. Using the empirical relationship $\text{Ro} = 0.6 \cdot e^{0.0000496 \cdot z}$, verify the consistency of the measurement and identify the maturity stage.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1:** Substitute $z = 3500$ m into the empirical equation.

$$\text{Ro}_{\text{predicted}} = 0.6 \cdot e^{0.0000496 \times 3500}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the exponent.

$$0.0000496 \times 3500 = 0.1736$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the exponential.

$$e^{0.1736} \approx 1.1896$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by the prefactor.

$$\text{Ro}_{\text{predicted}} = 0.6 \times 1.1896 = 0.7138\%$$

**Step 5:** Compare with measured value and classify.

$$\text{Ro}_{\text{measured}} = 0.85\% \quad \text{vs.} \quad \text{Ro}_{\text{predicted}} = 0.71\%$$

**Step 6:** Maturity Classification (Standard Thresholds):
- **Ro < 0.5%** → Immature
- **Ro = 0.5 – 0.7%** → Early oil window
- **Ro = 0.7 – 1.0%** → Peak oil window ✅
- **Ro = 1.0 – 1.3%** → Late oil / wet gas
- **Ro = 1.3 – 2.0%** → Dry gas window
- **Ro > 2.0%** → Overmature (graphite)

Measured Ro = **0.85%** → **Peak Oil Window (Mature Source Rock)**

> [!NOTE]
> **Cross-Validation Note:** The predicted Ro (0.71%) is slightly lower than measured (0.85%), suggesting a **higher local geothermal gradient** or **ancient heating event** (e.g., igneous intrusion) that accelerated maturation. This is a common real-world discrepancy.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Maturity Indicators

| Indicator | Immature | Oil Window | Gas Window | Overmature |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Ro (%)** | < 0.5 | 0.5 – 1.0 | 1.0 – 2.0 | > 2.0 |
| **TTI** | < 15 | 15 – 75 | 75 – 160 | > 160 |
| **Temperature (°C)** | < 60 | 60 – 150 | 150 – 225 | > 225 |
| **Sporinite Color** | Yellow | Orange → Brown | Dark Brown → Black | Black (destroyed) |
| **Bitumen Staining** | None | Light brown | Dark brown | None (cracked) |
| **H/C of Kerogen** | High | Moderate | Low | Very Low |
| **PI (Production Index)** | < 0.1 | 0.1 – 0.4 | 0.4 – 0.5 | Variable |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Petroleum Origin & Migration System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Marine/Lacustrine Organic Matter - Algae, Plankton, Bacteria]
    B[Sedimentation and Rapid Burial in Anoxic Environment]
    C[Kerogen Formation - Insoluble Organic Matter]
    D[Diagenesis Stage - 0 to 60 C]
    E[Catagenesis Stage - 60 to 150 C - Oil Window]
    F[Metagenesis Stage - 150 to 225 C - Gas Window]
    G[Primary Migration - Expulsion from Source Rock]
    H[Secondary Migration - Movement through Carrier Beds]
    I[Reservoir Rock - Porous and Permeable Trap]
    J[Cap Rock - Impermeable Seal]
    K[Petroleum Accumulation - Oil and Gas Field]

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F --> G
    G --> H
    H --> I
    I --> J
    J --> K
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Kerogen Type Classification (Van Krevelen Logic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Org[Source Organic Matter] --> K1[Type I - Algal Lacustrine]
    Org --> K2[Type II - Marine Planktonic]
    Org --> K3[Type III - Humic Terrestrial]
    Org --> K4[Type IV - Oxidized Inert]

    K1 --> P1[Product: High Oil Yield]
    K2 --> P2[Product: Oil and Gas Mix]
    K3 --> P3[Product: Dry Gas Dominant]
    K4 --> P4[Product: No Hydrocarbon Yield]

    style Org fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style P1 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825
    style P2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825
    style P3 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style P4 fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#455a64
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Maturity Window Thermal Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start[Burial Begins: Organic-Rich Sediment]
    Start --> D1[Diagenesis: 0 to 60 C - Biogenic Methane Only]
    D1 --> D2[Early Catagenesis: 60 to 90 C - Heavy Oil Generation]
    D2 --> D3[Peak Oil Window: 90 to 120 C - Maximum Oil Yield]
    D3 --> D4[Late Catagenesis: 120 to 150 C - Wet Gas and Condensate]
    D4 --> D5[Metagenesis: 150 to 225 C - Dry Thermal Methane]
    D5 --> D6[Overmature Zone: above 225 C - Graphite Residue]

    style D3 fill:#ffeb3b,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000
    style D5 fill:#a5d6a7,stroke:#1b5e20
    style D6 fill:#424242,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Petroleum System Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SR[Source Rock - Organic Rich Shale]
    MR[Migration Pathway - Carrier Beds and Faults]
    RR[Reservoir Rock - Sandstone or Carbonate]
    CR[Cap Rock - Shale or Salt Seal]
    TR[Trap Geometry - Structural or Stratigraphic]
    ACC[Hydrocarbon Accumulation]

    SR --> MR
    MR --> RR
    RR --> CR
    CR --> TR
    TR --> ACC

    style SR fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style ACC fill:#ffcc80,stroke:#bf360c
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Notes:** All node IDs use alphanumeric prefixes (e.g., `node1`, `K1`, `D3`) to avoid reserved-keyword conflicts. All labels are plain uppercase text without embedded markdown formatting.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]

**Define the Organic Theory of petroleum origin. List any three pieces of geological evidence that support it.**

#### Model Answer (Expected Key Points):

**Definition:** The Organic Theory states that crude oil and natural gas are formed from the thermal and biochemical transformation of buried organic matter (mainly marine algae, plankton, and bacteria) over geological time under anaerobic conditions.

**Three Supporting Evidence:**

1. **Optical Activity of Crude Oil:** Petroleum rotates plane-polarized light, which is a property inherited from biological molecules (e.g., amino acids, sugars). Inorganic synthesis cannot produce optically active compounds.

2. **Presence of Biomarkers (Biological Markers):** Crude oils contain complex organic molecules such as **porphyrins, hopanes, and steranes** that can only be derived from chlorophyll and cell membranes of living organisms.

3. **Carbon Isotope Ratios:** The δ¹³C values of petroleum (typically **-25‰ to -35‰**) closely match those of marine organic matter, confirming a biological origin.

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]

**What is the "Oil Window"? Explain its significance in petroleum exploration with reference to the temperature range.**

#### Model Answer (Expected Key Points):

**Definition:** The "Oil Window" is the specific range of subsurface temperatures (and corresponding burial depths) within which a thermally mature source rock generates and expels liquid hydrocarbons (crude oil).

**Temperature Range:** **60 °C to 150 °C**

**Corresponding Depth Range:** Approximately **2 km to 5 km** (depending on geothermal gradient)

**Significance in Exploration:**

1. **Defines Drilling Targets:** Exploration wells are drilled where source rocks are projected to lie within the oil window.
2. **Predicts Hydrocarbon Phase:** Tells whether the basin will generate oil, gas, or both.
3. **Risk Reduction:** Identifies thermally mature vs. immature or overmature zones.
4. **Subdivided into:** Early mature (60–90 °C), Peak oil (90–120 °C), and Late mature (120–150 °C) zones.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam – Model Question | 14 Marks]

**(a)** Explain in detail the three stages of kerogen maturation — Diagenesis, Catagenesis, and Metagenesis. Include the temperature range, products generated, and key geochemical changes at each stage. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Describe the Van Krevelen classification of kerogen. With the help of a labeled H/C vs. O/C diagram, explain the four kerogen types and their hydrocarbon generation potential. **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(a):

**Stage 1 — Diagenesis (0 °C to 60 °C)**

- **Process:** Biochemical and weak thermal alteration of recently deposited organic matter.
- **Key Reactions:** Microbial activity, polymerization of biochemicals, formation of humic substances.
- **Products:** **Biogenic methane** (e.g., marsh gas), CO₂, H₂O, and immature kerogen.
- **Geochemical Change:** H/C and O/C ratios remain high; organic matter is still evolving.
- **Duration:** From deposition to ~50–60 °C burial.

**Stage 2 — Catagenesis (60 °C to 150 °C)**

- **Process:** Thermal cracking of kerogen (C–C bond breaking) becomes dominant.
- **Key Reactions:** Thermal decomposition of kerogen into bitumen, then into oil and gas.
- **Products:** **Crude oil** (peak at 100–120 °C), **wet gas**, and **condensate**.
- **Geochemical Change:** H/C ratio decreases as hydrogen is released into hydrocarbons; O/C continues to drop.
- **Duration:** Millions of years; corresponds to the **oil window**.

**Stage 3 — Metagenesis (150 °C to 225 °C)**

- **Process:** Secondary cracking of previously generated oil into thermogenic gas.
- **Key Reactions:** C–C bond breaking in heavy hydrocarbons → light hydrocarbons → methane.
- **Products:** **Dry thermal methane** is the dominant product.
- **Geochemical Change:** Kerogen becomes highly carbonaceous; H/C ratio approaches zero.
- **Endpoint:** Above 225 °C, only **graphite (carbon)** and inert residue remain.

---

#### Model Solution for Question A(b):

**Van Krevelen Classification of Kerogen:**

The Van Krevelen diagram plots the atomic **H/C ratio** (y-axis) versus the **O/C ratio** (x-axis). It is used to classify kerogen into four types based on elemental composition and to predict hydrocarbon generation potential.

**The Four Kerogen Types:**

| Type | H/C Ratio | O/C Ratio | Source | Hydrocarbon Potential |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| **I** | > 1.5 | < 0.1 | Algal (lacustrine) | **Excellent oil source** |
| **II** | 1.2 – 1.5 | 0.1 – 0.2 | Marine planktonic | **Good oil and gas source** |
| **III** | < 0.8 | > 0.2 | Humic (terrestrial) | **Gas-prone** |
| **IV** | < 0.5 | Variable | Oxidized/recycled | **Inert — no generation** |

**Evolution Paths (during maturation):**

- All kerogen types follow **downward-curving trajectories** on the Van Krevelen diagram as maturation increases (both H/C and O/C decrease).
- **Type I** starts with very high H/C and evolves most rapidly toward oil generation.
- **Type II** is the most common marine source rock — excellent for both oil and gas.
- **Type III** remains gas-prone throughout its evolution.
- **Type IV** lies in a "dead" zone — produces essentially no hydrocarbons.

**Labeled Diagram (Schematic):**

```
   H/C Ratio
     |
 1.8 |  * I (Type I)
     |   \
 1.4 |    \  * II (Type II)
     |     \    \
 1.0 |      \    \  * III (Type III)
     |       \    \    \
 0.6 |        \    \    \  * IV (Type IV - Inert)
     |         \    \    \
 0.2 |          \____\____\____ (Mature endpoint, low H/C)
     |__________________________
     0    0.1   0.2   0.3   0.4   O/C Ratio
```

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Always remember to draw the **evolution paths (arrows)** showing maturation trends. This earns full marks in part (b).

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Model Question | 14 Marks]

**(a)** What is Lopatin's Time-Temperature Index (TTI)? Derive and explain its formula. Discuss the maturity thresholds (oil window, gas window, overmature) associated with TTI values. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A sedimentary basin has a surface temperature of 25 °C and a geothermal gradient of 28 °C/km. Calculate: (i) the depth corresponding to the top of the oil window (60 °C), (ii) the depth of peak oil generation (110 °C), and (iii) the depth at which the gas window ends (220 °C). **[7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(a):

**Definition:** The **Time-Temperature Index (TTI)** is a numerical maturity index developed by **Lopatin (1971)** and refined by **Waples (1980)** that quantifies the combined effect of time and temperature on kerogen maturation.

**Formula:**

$$\text{TTI} = \sum_{n=\min}^{\max} \left[ \Delta t_n \cdot 2^{\frac{T_n - 105}{10}} \right]$$

**Logical Basis:**

- **Every 10 °C increase in temperature doubles the reaction rate** (chemical kinetics principle).
- The reference temperature is **105 °C** (chosen as the "zero" point on the exponential scale).
- $r = 2^{(T - 105)/10}$ is the **rate factor** — it equals 1 at 105 °C, doubles every 10 °C above, and halves every 10 °C below.
- $\Delta t_n$ = the time (in millions of years) spent in the $n^{\text{th}}$ temperature interval.

**Maturity Thresholds:**

| TTI Value | Maturity Stage | Products |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **< 15** | Immature | Biogenic gas only |
| **15 – 75** | Oil Window | Crude oil (peak ≈ 75) |
| **75 – 160** | Gas Window | Wet gas, condensate, then dry gas |
| **> 160** | Overmature | Dry gas, graphite |

**Application:** TTI is used in **basin modeling** to predict the timing and extent of hydrocarbon generation from a source rock's burial history.

---

#### Model Solution for Question B(b):

**Given:**
- Surface temperature $T_s = 25$ °C
- Geothermal gradient $\frac{dT}{dz} = 28$ °C/km

**Formula:**
$$z = \frac{T_z - T_s}{dT/dz}$$

**Part (i):** Top of oil window, $T_z = 60$ °C

$$z_{60} = \frac{60 - 25}{28} = \frac{35}{28} = 1.25 \text{ km}$$

$$\boxed{z_{60} = 1250 \text{ m}}$$

**Part (ii):** Peak oil generation, $T_z = 110$ °C

$$z_{110} = \frac{110 - 25}{28} = \frac{85}{28} \approx 3.036 \text{ km}$$

$$\boxed{z_{110} \approx 3036 \text{ m}}$$

**Part (iii):** End of gas window, $T_z = 220$ °C

$$z_{220} = \frac{220 - 25}{28} = \frac{195}{28} \approx 6.964 \text{ km}$$

$$\boxed{z_{220} \approx 6964 \text{ m}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**
- The oil window in this basin lies between **~1.25 km and ~3.0 km**.
- The gas window extends from ~3.0 km to ~7.0 km.
- Wells targeting oil should be drilled to depths of **2–3 km**, while gas prospects may extend to **5–7 km**.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Kerogen with Bitumen:** Kerogen is **insoluble** in organic solvents; bitumen is **soluble**. Students often interchange these terms. (Penalty: -1 to -2 marks)
>
> 2. **Wrong Temperature for Oil Window:** Many students write "oil window = 100–150 °C." The correct range is **60–150 °C** (with peak oil at 100–120 °C).
>
> 3. **Confusing Type I and Type II Kerogen:** Type I is **lacustrine algal** (very high H/C); Type II is **marine planktonic** (moderate H/C). Do not interchange.
>
> 4. **In TTI Problems:** Forgetting to convert time to **millions of years** before applying the formula. If time is given in years, divide by $10^6$ first.
>
> 5. **In Lopatin's formula:** Using **additive rate factor** instead of **multiplicative** — remember $r$ is exponential, not linear.
>
> 6. **Van Krevelen diagram:** Forgetting to **plot both axes** with correct labels and units. The x-axis is O/C (atomic) and y-axis is H/C (atomic) — dimensionless ratios.
>
> 7. **Maturity indicators:** Spelling "vitrinite" as "vitrinite" (common confusion with "vitreous" or "vitreinite"). Correct spelling: **Vitrinite Reflectance (Ro)**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Origin of Petroleum**

### 🔑 Core Concepts
- **Origin of Petroleum** = transformation of buried organic matter into hydrocarbons over geological time.
- The **Organic Theory** is universally accepted; the **Inorganic Theory** (Mendeleev) is rejected.
- **Petroleum = Crude Oil + Natural Gas** — generated together but in different proportions depending on kerogen type.

### 🔬 Three Stages of Maturation
- **Diagenesis** (0–60 °C) → Biogenic methane; no oil.
- **Catagenesis** (60–150 °C) → Oil Window (peak at 100–120 °C).
- **Metagenesis** (150–225 °C) → Dry thermal gas; ends in graphite.

### 🧪 Kerogen Types (Van Krevelen)
- **Type I** — Lacustrine algae → **High oil yield** (H/C > 1.5, O/C < 0.1)
- **Type II** — Marine plankton → **Oil + gas** (H/C 1.2–1.5, O/C 0.1–0.2)
- **Type III** — Humic/terrestrial → **Gas-prone** (H/C < 0.8, O/C > 0.2)
- **Type IV** — Inert/oxidized → **No hydrocarbons** (H/C < 0.5)

### 📏 Critical Numerical Thresholds
- **TOC threshold for source rock:** > 0.5 wt% (good source: > 2 wt%)
- **Oil Window:** 60–150 °C
- **Gas Window:** 120–225 °C
- **Vitrinite Reflectance (Ro):** 0.5–1.0% = oil; 1.0–2.0% = gas; > 2.0% = overmature
- **TTI:** 15–75 = oil; 75–160 = gas; > 160 = overmature

### 📐 Must-Know Formulas
- **Geothermal depth:** $z = (T_z - T_s) / (dT/dz)$
- **Lopatin's TTI:** $\text{TTI} = \sum \Delta t_n \cdot 2^{(T_n - 105)/10}$
- **Vitrinite Reflectance (empirical):** $\text{Ro} = 0.6 \cdot e^{0.0000496 \cdot z}$
- **Production Index (PI):** $\text{PI} = S_1 / (S_1 + S_2)$ — Threshold > 0.1

### 🎯 Exam-Heavy Keywords (Use These in Answers for Bonus Marks)
- "**Sapropelic vs. Humic** source materials"
- "**Anaerobic, reducing environment**"
- "**Vitrinite reflectance as a paleothermometer**"
- "**Lopatin's principle of time-temperature equivalence**"
- "**Primary migration vs. secondary migration**"
- "**Biomarkers and porphyrins** as evidence of biogenic origin"
- "**Optical activity** of crude oils as evidence of biological origin"

### 📚 Quick Comparison Snapshot
- **Organic Theory** ✅ → Modern science, biomarkers, isotopic evidence
- **Inorganic Theory** ❌ → Mendeleev, abandoned except for trace mantle methane
- **Sapropelic** → Marine → Oil
- **Humic** → Terrestrial → Gas

### 💡 Mnemonics
- **"OSG"** for Kerogen: **O**il (Type I & II) → **S**apropelic, **G**as (Type III) → Humic
- **"DDM"** for Stages: **D**iagenesis, **D**eep catagenesis, **M**etagenesis
- **"Rocks Generate Oil Gas"** — **R**educe, **G**eneration, **O**il, **G**as

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Formation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT528 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Formation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🛢️ FORMATION OF PETROLEUM & NATURAL GAS — KTU 2024 SCHEME

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> *Formation* in petroleum engineering refers to the complete set of **geological, geochemical, and thermodynamic processes** by which organic matter (kerogen) deposited in fine-grained sedimentary source rocks is progressively transformed into crude oil and natural gas through burial, heating, and maturation over geological time scales (typically 1–500 million years).

The term "formation" in Module 1 covers four sequential stages:
1. **Deposition of organic matter** (in source rock)
2. **Maturation of kerogen** (diagenesis → catagenesis → metagenesis)
3. **Primary & secondary migration** of generated hydrocarbons
4. **Accumulation in reservoir rocks** (under cap rock / trap)

The formally accepted modern theory is the **Organic Theory of Petroleum Origin**, which posits that hydrocarbons are formed from the thermal maturation of buried organic matter under elevated temperature and pressure.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Slow Cooking" of Hydrocarbons

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: Pressure Cooker Buried Underground 🍲**

Imagine a giant *pressure cooker buried deep inside the Earth*:
- The **ingredients** (raw vegetables + meat) = dead marine algae, plankton, and terrestrial plants (organic matter).
- The **sealed pot** = impermeable shale/clay cap rock.
- The **heat source** = Earth's geothermal gradient (~25–30 °C/km, with a standard value of **30 °C/km**).
- The **cooking duration** = millions of years of geological time.
- The **final dish (oil & gas)** = crude oil and natural gas.

Just as slow cooking breaks down tough raw ingredients into tender, energy-rich food, slow burial (millions of years) and rising heat break down complex kerogen molecules into simpler hydrocarbons (oil at ~60–120 °C, gas at ~120–225 °C).

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Geothermal gradient:** **30 °C/km** (standard, varies 20–50 °C/km)
> - **Surface temperature (avg):** 20 °C
> - **Oil window temperature:** **60–120 °C**
> - **Gas window temperature:** **120–225 °C**
> - **Minimum burial depth for oil generation:** ~2,000 m
> - **Vitrinite reflectance (%Ro) for oil window:** **0.6–1.2 %**
> - **Vitrinite reflectance (%Ro) for gas window:** **1.2–2.5 %**
> - **Time scale of formation:** 10⁶ – 5 × 10⁸ years
> - **Density of kerogen (immature):** ~1,000–1,100 kg/m³
> - **Density of crude oil (avg):** ~870 kg/m³ (API ~30°)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2️⃣ DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS — THE 4-STAGE FORMATION MODEL

## 2.1 Stage 1: Deposition of Organic Matter (Sedimentation)

- Organic matter (plankton, algae, spores, pollen) settles in **anoxic, low-energy marine or lacustrine environments** (e.g., deep ocean floors, swamps, deltas).
- Rapid burial under fine-grained sediments (clay/silt) prevents complete decomposition by oxygen.
- The resulting fine-grained, organic-rich sedimentary rock is the **Source Rock**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Total Organic Carbon (TOC)** is a key parameter. A rock with **TOC > 0.5 wt%** is considered a potential source rock; **> 2.0 wt%** is an excellent source rock.

---

## 2.2 Stage 2: Kerogen Maturation (The "Cooking" Process)

As burial depth increases, organic matter passes through three stages:

| Stage | Depth (approx.) | Temperature (°C) | Process | Product |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Diagenesis** | 0 – 1,000 m | 20 – 50 | Microbial activity, dewatering | Kerogen, biogenic methane |
| **Catagenesis** | 1,000 – 4,000 m | 50 – 150 | Thermal cracking (oil window) | Crude oil + wet gas |
| **Metagenesis** | 4,000 – 7,000 m | 150 – 225 | Cracking of oil to gas (gas window) | Dry gas (methane) |

---

## 2.3 Stage 3: Migration of Hydrocarbons

- **Primary Migration:** Expulsion of hydrocarbons **from the source rock** into permeable carrier beds (e.g., sandstones).
- **Secondary Migration:** Movement of hydrocarbons **through carrier beds** upward (driven by buoyancy, since oil/gas is less dense than water) toward a **trap**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Migration is driven by **buoyancy force** (F = (ρ_water − ρ_oil) × g × V) acting against capillary pressure and hydrodynamic flow.

---

## 2.4 Stage 4: Accumulation in a Trap

A **petroleum trap** requires three elements (the "**Petroleum Trinity**"):
1. **Reservoir rock** — porous & permeable (e.g., sandstone, carbonate).
2. **Cap rock** — impermeable seal (e.g., shale, salt).
3. **Geological trap geometry** — structural (anticline, fault) or stratigraphic (unconformity, reef).

---

## 2.5 Kerogen Classification — The KTU High-Yield Table

| Kerogen Type | Origin (Biological Precursor) | H/C Ratio | O/C Ratio | Main Hydrocarbon Product |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Type I** (Algal) | Algae, lacustrine plankton | High (>1.5) | Low (<0.1) | **Oil-prone** (excellent) |
| **Type II** (Planktonic/Marine) | Marine plankton, spores | Moderate (~1.2) | Moderate (~0.15) | **Oil & Gas** (very good) |
| **Type III** (Humic/Coaly) | Terrestrial plants, wood | Low (<0.7) | High (>0.3) | **Gas-prone** (mainly) |
| **Type IV** (Inert/Residue) | Recycled/oxidized organic matter | Very low | High | **None** (no generation) |

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet 📋

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas are **board-exam essentials**. Master the units & boundary conditions.

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Geothermal Gradient** $T_z = T_0 + G \cdot z$ | $T_z$ = temp at depth $z$, $G$ = gradient (°C/km), $T_0$ = surface temp | Determines which maturation window a formation is in |
| 2 | **Buoyancy Force** $F_b = (\rho_w - \rho_o) \cdot g \cdot V$ | $\rho_w$ = water density, $\rho_o$ = oil density, $V$ = volume | Primary driver of secondary migration |
| 3 | **API Gravity** $°API = \dfrac{141.5}{\rho_{oil}} - 131.5$ | $\rho_{oil}$ in g/cc at 15.6 °C | Classifies crude as light ($>31.1°$), medium, heavy |
| 4 | **Porosity** $\phi = \dfrac{V_{voids}}{V_{total}}$ | Void volume / bulk volume | Determines hydrocarbon storage capacity |
| 5 | **Permeability (Darcy's Law)** $Q = \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$ | $Q$ = flow rate, $k$ = permeability, $\mu$ = viscosity | Determines flow into wellbore |
| 6 | **Time-Temperature Index (TTI)** $TTI = \sum r^n \cdot \Delta t$ | $r = 2$ (factor per 10 °C), $n$ = temp index | Lopatin's method for maturation |
| 7 | **Transformation Ratio** $TR = \dfrac{S_1}{S_1 + S_2}$ (from Rock-Eval) | $S_1$ = free HC, $S_2$ = residual HC | Measures kerogen conversion to oil |
| 8 | **Vitrinite Reflectance (maturity indicator)** | $%R_o$ measured under reflected light | Standard for thermal maturity |

---

## 2.7 Why This Matters in Engineering 🛠️

- **Exploration geologists** use formation knowledge to identify prospective basins.
- **Reservoir engineers** apply formation mechanics for production forecasting.
- **Drilling engineers** must understand formation pressures to avoid blowouts.
- **Refinery design** depends on the API gravity and kerogen-derived composition.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3️⃣ STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Geothermal Temperature at Depth

We start from the fundamental heat conduction model for a homogeneous crust.

**Step 1: Fourier's Law of Heat Conduction**
$$q = -k \cdot \frac{dT}{dz}$$

where $q$ = heat flux (W/m²), $k$ = thermal conductivity (W/m·K), $T$ = temperature, $z$ = depth.

**Step 2: Assume Steady State (constant heat flux)**
Heat flux $q$ is constant with depth (no internal heat sources assumed).

**Step 3: Integrate from surface (z = 0, T = T₀) to depth z**
$$\int_{T_0}^{T_z} dT = -\frac{q}{k} \int_0^z dz$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the integral**
$$T_z - T_0 = -\frac{q}{k} \cdot z$$

**Step 5: Define geothermal gradient**
$$G = -\frac{q}{k} \quad \text{(positive when temperature increases with depth)}$$

**Step 6: Final working equation**
$$\boxed{T_z = T_0 + G \cdot z}$$

> **Logic Note:** This linear relationship is valid in the *sedimentary layer*. Igneous/metamorphic basement has a different (lower) gradient.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example: Identifying the Hydrocarbon Window (Board-style)

**Problem (KTU-style):** The surface temperature of a basin is $20°C$ and the geothermal gradient is $30 °C/km$. At what depth range would you expect the **oil window** to occur?

> [!NOTE]
> **Given:**
> - $T_0 = 20 °C$
> - $G = 30 °C/km$
> - Oil window temperature range: $60 °C$ to $120 °C$

**Step 1: Solve for depth at $T = 60 °C$ (top of oil window)**
$$z_{top} = \frac{T - T_0}{G} = \frac{60 - 20}{30} = \frac{40}{30}$$

$$z_{top} = 1.333 \text{ km} \approx 1,333 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Solve for depth at $T = 120 °C$ (bottom of oil window)**
$$z_{bottom} = \frac{120 - 20}{30} = \frac{100}{30}$$

$$z_{bottom} = 3.333 \text{ km} \approx 3,333 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Conclusion**
$$\boxed{\text{Oil Window Depth} = 1{,}333 \text{ m to } 3{,}333 \text{ m}}$$

> **Validation:** A similar KTU board question can ask for the *gas window* (120–225 °C) using the same formula. **Always convert units carefully (km ↔ m).**

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Time-Temperature Index (Lopatin's Method)

Lopatin (1971) proposed that **reaction rates double for every 10 °C rise in temperature**. This is a classic Arrhenius-type empirical relationship.

**Step 1: Define temperature index (n)**
$$n = \frac{T_{avg} - 100}{10}$$

where $T_{avg}$ is the average temperature in °C over a time interval $\Delta t$.

**Step 2: Define rate factor (r)**
$$r = 2^n$$

Step 2 is essentially the **base-2 doubling rule** per 10 °C. Note that for every 10 °C increase, $n$ increases by 1, and $r$ doubles.

**Step 3: Compute TTI for a single interval**
$$TTI_i = r^n \cdot \Delta t = 2^n \cdot \Delta t$$

**Step 4: Sum over the entire burial history**
$$\boxed{TTI = \sum_{i=1}^{N} 2^{n_i} \cdot \Delta t_i}$$

**Boundary Conditions (from KTU syllabus):**
- $TTI < 10$ → Immature (no hydrocarbon generation)
- $TTI = 10$–$15$ → Early oil generation begins
- $TTI = 15$–$160$ → Main oil window
- $TTI > 160$ → Gas window / over-mature

---

## 3.4 Worked Example: TTI Calculation

**Problem:** A source rock experienced three burial stages:
| Stage | Avg. Temp (°C) | Duration (Myr) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 50 | 20 |
| 2 | 80 | 15 |
| 3 | 110 | 10 |

Compute the TTI and identify the maturation stage.

**Step 1: Compute $n$ for each stage**
$$n_1 = \frac{50 - 100}{10} = -5$$
$$n_2 = \frac{80 - 100}{10} = -2$$
$$n_3 = \frac{110 - 100}{10} = +1$$

**Step 2: Compute $2^n$ for each stage**
$$2^{-5} = 0.03125$$
$$2^{-2} = 0.25$$
$$2^{+1} = 2$$

**Step 3: Compute $TTI_i$ for each stage**
$$TTI_1 = 0.03125 \times 20 = 0.625$$
$$TTI_2 = 0.25 \times 15 = 3.75$$
$$TTI_3 = 2 \times 10 = 20$$

**Step 4: Sum total TTI**
$$\boxed{TTI_{total} = 0.625 + 3.75 + 20 = 24.375}$$

**Step 5: Maturation Interpretation**
Since $TTI = 24.375$, the source rock lies in the **Main Oil Window** (TTI = 15–160). This is consistent with the geothermal gradient model.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Buoyancy Force Driving Secondary Migration

A hydrocarbon droplet in water experiences a net upward force.

**Step 1: Net gravitational force (downward) on oil droplet**
$$F_g = \rho_o \cdot g \cdot V$$

**Step 2: Buoyant force (upward) by displaced water**
$$F_b = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot V$$

**Step 3: Net force on the droplet (upward, since $\rho_w > \rho_o$)**
$$F_{net} = F_b - F_g = (\rho_w - \rho_o) \cdot g \cdot V$$

$$\boxed{F_{net} = (\rho_w - \rho_o) \cdot g \cdot V}$$

> **Logic Note:** This is the *fundamental reason oil and gas migrate upward* in a tilted or horizontal reservoir until they hit a cap rock. This is **why anticlines and faults are preferred exploration targets**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4️⃣ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: The Petroleum Formation Process (End-to-End)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Dead Organic Matter Plankton Algae Plants] --> B[Deposition in Anoxic Basin]
    B --> C[Burial under Sediments]
    C --> D{Stage of Diagenesis T 20 to 50 C}
    D --> E[Formation of Kerogen]
    E --> F{Catagenesis Oil Window T 60 to 120 C}
    F -->|Yes| G[Crude Oil + Wet Gas Generated]
    F -->|Higher T| H{Metagenesis Gas Window T 120 to 225 C}
    H --> I[Dry Methane Gas]
    G --> J[Primary Migration Expulsion from Source Rock]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Secondary Migration via Carrier Beds Buoyancy Driven]
    K --> L[Trapping under Cap Rock in Reservoir]
    L --> M[Petroleum Reservoir Discovered]
    M --> N[Drilling and Production]
    style A fill:#fff5e6,stroke:#8b5a00
    style E fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003d80
    style G fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#800000
    style I fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600
    style M fill:#fff0b3,stroke:#806600
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Kerogen Classification & Product Potential

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Organic Matter Input] --> B{Depositional Environment}
    B -->|Lacustrine Algal| C[Type I Algal Kerogen]
    B -->|Marine Planktonic| D[Type II Marine Kerogen]
    B -->|Terrestrial Woody| E[Type III Humic Kerogen]
    B -->|Recycled Oxidized| F[Type IV Inert Kerogen]
    C -->|High H/C Ratio| G[Excellent Oil Source]
    D -->|Moderate H/C Ratio| H[Oil and Gas Source]
    E -->|Low H/C Ratio| I[Mainly Gas Source]
    F -->|No Generation| J[No Hydrocarbon Potential]
    style G fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#800000
    style H fill:#fff0b3,stroke:#806600
    style I fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600
    style J fill:#d9d9d9,stroke:#404040
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Architecture of the Petroleum System (KTU Functional Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Source[Source Rock Subsystem]
        s1[Organic Rich Shale]
        s2[Maturation Kerogen Cooking]
        s3[Hydrocarbon Expulsion]
    end
    subgraph Migration[Migration Subsystem]
        m1[Primary Migration out of Source]
        m2[Secondary Migration via Carrier Beds]
        m3[Buoyancy Drives Upward Movement]
    end
    subgraph Reservoir[Reservoir Trap Subsystem]
        r1[Porous Sandstone Carbonate]
        r2[Impermeable Cap Rock Seal]
        r3[Structural or Stratigraphic Trap]
    end
    s1 --> s2 --> s3
    s3 --> m1 --> m2 --> m3
    m3 --> r1
    r1 --> r2
    r2 --> r3
    r3 --> Output[Accumulation Petroleum Reservoir]
    style Source fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003d80
    style Migration fill:#fff5e6,stroke:#8b5a00
    style Reservoir fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600
    style Output fill:#fff0b3,stroke:#806600
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Hydrocarbon Windows vs Depth & Temperature

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Surface T 20 C] --> B[Depth 0 to 1000 m Diagenesis T 20 to 50 C]
    B --> C[Depth 1000 to 2000 m Early Catagenesis T 50 to 80 C]
    C --> D[Depth 2000 to 3500 m Main Oil Window T 60 to 120 C]
    D --> E[Depth 3500 to 5000 m Wet Gas Zone T 120 to 150 C]
    E --> F[Depth 5000 to 7000 m Dry Gas Window T 150 to 225 C]
    F --> G[Depth 7000 m plus Overmature T greater than 225 C]
    style A fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    style B fill:#e6f3ff,stroke:#003d80
    style D fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#800000
    style E fill:#fff0b3,stroke:#806600
    style F fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#006600
    style G fill:#d9d9d9,stroke:#404040
```
