<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5️⃣ KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK

## 5.1 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
**Define the term "Source Rock" and list its four key properties.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A source rock is a fine-grained sedimentary rock (typically shale or marl) rich in organic matter, from which hydrocarbons have been generated or are capable of being generated. Its four key properties are:
1. **High organic content** (Total Organic Carbon, TOC > 0.5 wt%)
2. **Appropriate kerogen type** (Type I or II for oil; Type III for gas)
3. **Sufficient thermal maturity** (within oil/gas window, $%R_o = 0.6–2.5$)
4. **Adequate thickness & volume** (typically > 10 m of effective organic-rich section)

> **Marking Scheme:** Definition (1m) + any 4 properties (2m).

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
**Distinguish between the "Oil Window" and the "Gas Window" using temperature and vitrinite reflectance values.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Oil Window | Gas Window |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature (°C) | 60 – 120 | 120 – 225 |
| Vitrinite Reflectance (%Rₒ) | 0.6 – 1.2 | 1.2 – 2.5 |
| Typical Depth (m) | 2,000 – 3,500 | 3,500 – 6,000 |
| Main Product | Crude oil + wet gas | Dry gas (methane) |
| Kerogen Stage | Mid-Catagenesis | Late Catagenesis → Metagenesis |

> **Marking Scheme:** Correct temperature range (1m) + correct %Rₒ range (1m) + product distinction (1m).

---

## 5.2 📝 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024 Pattern]

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **process of formation of petroleum from organic matter**, highlighting the role of source rock, maturation, and migration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A sedimentary basin has a surface temperature of $25 °C$ and a geothermal gradient of $35 °C/km$. Determine the **depth at which the oil window begins and ends**. Comment on the location of the gas window. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-A(a)

**Step 1: Deposition of Organic Matter (1 mark)**
Dead marine/lacustrine organic matter (plankton, algae, plant debris) accumulates in an **anoxic, low-energy depositional environment** such as deep marine basins, swamps, or lagoons. Rapid burial by fine clays preserves the organic matter from oxidation.

**Step 2: Source Rock Formation (1 mark)**
The accumulating organic-rich muds compact to form **source rocks** (e.g., black shale, marl). Their organic richness is measured by **TOC**; a TOC > 0.5 wt% qualifies a rock as a potential source.

**Step 3: Burial and Maturation (2 marks)**
As sediments are buried to greater depths, **temperature rises** according to the geothermal gradient. The organic matter (now **kerogen**) undergoes thermal cracking through three stages:
- **Diagenesis** (20–50 °C): microbial activity, kerogen formation.
- **Catagenesis** (50–150 °C): main oil window, hydrocarbon generation.
- **Metagenesis** (150–225 °C): cracking of oil to dry gas.

**Step 4: Primary and Secondary Migration (2 marks)**
Generated hydrocarbons are expelled from the source rock (**primary migration**) into more permeable carrier beds. They then move upward due to **buoyancy** (oil/gas being less dense than formation water) through **secondary migration** until trapped.

**Step 5: Accumulation in Trap (1 mark)**
Hydrocarbons accumulate beneath an impermeable **cap rock** in a **reservoir rock** (sandstone/carbonate) within a structural or stratigraphic trap, forming a **petroleum reservoir**.

> A clean Mermaid block schematic or labelled cross-section (anticline) is awarded the **7th mark** as a diagram credit.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-A(b)

**Given:** $T_0 = 25 °C$, $G = 35 °C/km$

**Oil Window:** 60 – 120 °C

**Step 1: Depth at top of oil window (T = 60 °C)** (3 marks)
$$z_{top} = \frac{T - T_0}{G} = \frac{60 - 25}{35} = \frac{35}{35} = 1.0 \text{ km} = 1{,}000 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Depth at bottom of oil window (T = 120 °C)** (2 marks)
$$z_{bottom} = \frac{120 - 25}{35} = \frac{95}{35} = 2.714 \text{ km} \approx 2{,}714 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Gas Window Depth (T = 120 – 225 °C)** (2 marks)
$$z_{gas,top} = 1{,}000 + 714 = 2{,}714 \text{ m (same as oil window bottom)}$$
$$z_{gas,bottom} = \frac{225 - 25}{35} = \frac{200}{35} = 5.714 \text{ km} \approx 5{,}714 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answer (boxed):**
$$\boxed{z_{oil} = 1{,}000 \text{ m to } 2{,}714 \text{ m}, \quad z_{gas} = 2{,}714 \text{ m to } 5{,}714 \text{ m}}$$

> **Examiner's Note:** Many students forget to convert km to m. Always state units explicitly in the final answer for full marks.

---

### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Pattern]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **classification of kerogen** into four types. State which types are most prospective for oil and gas, justifying with their **H/C and O/C ratios**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using **Lopatin's Time-Temperature Index (TTI) method**, determine the maturation stage of a source rock with the following burial history:
| Stage | Avg. Temp (°C) | Duration (Myr) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 60 | 30 |
| 2 | 90 | 20 |
| 3 | 130 | 5 |
**(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-B(a)

**Step 1: What is Kerogen? (1 mark)**
Kerogen is the insoluble organic matter in sedimentary rocks, formed from the diagenesis of buried organic debris. It is the precursor of petroleum.

**Step 2: Four-Type Classification (4 marks — 1 each):**

| Type | Origin | H/C | O/C | Product |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Type I** (Algal/Lacustrine) | Algal blooms in lakes | > 1.5 | < 0.1 | **Excellent oil source** (e.g., Green River Shale) |
| **Type II** (Marine/Planktonic) | Marine phytoplankton | ~1.2 | ~0.15 | **Good oil + gas** (e.g., Kimmeridge Clay) |
| **Type III** (Humic/Coaly) | Terrestrial higher plants | < 0.7 | > 0.3 | **Mainly gas** (e.g., North Sea coals) |
| **Type IV** (Inert/Residue) | Oxidized, recycled material | Very low | High | **No hydrocarbon potential** |

**Step 3: Prospectivity Justification (2 marks)**
- **Oil prospectivity:** Type I > Type II > Type III > Type IV (high H/C favours liquid HC generation).
- **Gas prospectivity:** Type III (gas-prone) is best for dry gas; Type II generates wet gas.
- H/C ratio indicates hydrogen richness (more H = more hydrocarbon potential).
- O/C ratio indicates oxygen content (more O = more CO₂ + water, less HC).

> **Key takeaway:** The H/C vs O/C relationship is the basis of the **Van Krevelen diagram**, which plots maturation paths.

---

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-B(b)

**Step 1: Formula (1 mark)**
$$TTI = \sum 2^n \cdot \Delta t \quad ; \quad n = \frac{T_{avg} - 100}{10}$$

**Step 2: Compute $n$ for each stage (2 marks)**
$$n_1 = \frac{60 - 100}{10} = -4$$
$$n_2 = \frac{90 - 100}{10} = -1$$
$$n_3 = \frac{130 - 100}{10} = +3$$

**Step 3: Compute $2^n$ and $TTI_i$ (3 marks)**

| Stage | T (°C) | $\Delta t$ (Myr) | $n$ | $2^n$ | $TTI_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 60 | 30 | -4 | 0.0625 | 1.875 |
| 2 | 90 | 20 | -1 | 0.5 | 10.0 |
| 3 | 130 | 5 | +3 | 8.0 | 40.0 |

**Step 4: Total TTI (1 mark)**
$$\boxed{TTI_{total} = 1.875 + 10.0 + 40.0 = 51.875}$$

**Step 5: Maturation Stage Interpretation (use the boundary table from Section 3.3 above) (0–1 mark)**
- $TTI = 51.875$ lies in the range **15–160**, so the rock is in the **Main Oil Window / Mature Oil Generation Stage**.

> **Examiner's note:** The 100 °C baseline and 2× factor per 10 °C must be **explicitly written** for full credit. Skipping the formula statement loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 ways KTU students LOSE MARKS in this topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the baseline (100 °C)** in the TTI formula — always write $n = (T - 100)/10$.
> 2. **Unit confusion** between km and m in geothermal gradient problems — keep *units consistent*.
> 3. **Confusing source rock vs reservoir rock** — Source generates; Reservoir stores.
> 4. **Mixing up Type I and Type III kerogen** — remember: Algae (Type I) = oil, Wood (Type III) = gas.
> 5. **Omitting the diagram** in 7-mark answers — always include a labelled schematic (anticline, source-reservoir-cap rock). **Diagrams = 1–2 free marks.**
> 6. **Maturity parameters not cross-checked** — always state BOTH $%R_o$ AND temperature (not one alone).

---

## 5.4 🎯 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary for Last-Minute Revision (Module 1 - Formation):**

- ⛽ **Petroleum = biogenic origin** (organic theory is universally accepted).
- 🪨 **Source Rock:** fine-grained, organic-rich (TOC > 0.5 wt%), has generated HC.
- 🏔️ **Reservoir Rock:** porous + permeable (sandstone, carbonate).
- 🛡️ **Cap Rock:** impermeable seal (shale, salt, evaporite).
- 🔺 **Petroleum Trinity:** Source + Reservoir + Trap + Seal.
- 🌡️ **Geothermal gradient:** ~**30 °C/km**; formula: $T_z = T_0 + G \cdot z$.
- 🪟 **Oil Window:** 60–120 °C, 1,000–3,500 m, $%R_o = 0.6–1.2$.
- 🔥 **Gas Window:** 120–225 °C, 3,500–6,000 m, $%R_o = 1.2–2.5$.
- 🧪 **Type I kerogen = oil-prone** (H/C > 1.5); **Type III = gas-prone** (H/C < 0.7).
- 🚀 **Primary migration** = expulsion from source; **Secondary migration** = movement via carrier beds.
- ⬆️ **Buoyancy** is the principal force for secondary migration: $F_b = (\rho_w - \rho_o) g V$.
- ⏳ **Lopatin's TTI:** $TTI = \sum 2^n \cdot \Delta t$ with $n = (T-100)/10$.
- 📊 **Boundaries:** TTI < 10 = immature; 10–15 = early; 15–160 = main oil; > 160 = gas.
- 🧬 **Three maturation stages:** Diagenesis (microbial) → Catagenesis (thermal cracking) → Metagenesis (dry gas).
- 📈 **Van Krevelen Diagram:** plots H/C vs O/C; maturation paths go diagonally down-left.
- 🏗️ **Trap Types:** Structural (anticline, fault, salt dome) vs Stratigraphic (unconformity, lens, reef).
- 🧮 **API Gravity formula:** $°API = 141.5/\rho_{oil} - 131.5$; light crude > 31.1°API.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Geological Occurrence and Characteristics of Oil and Natural Gas, Source Rocks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT528 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Geological Occurrence and Characteristics of Oil and Natural Gas, Source Rocks # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Geological Occurrence of Oil and Natural Gas > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** > *Petroleum (Oil and Natural Gas) is a naturally occurring, complex mixture of hydrocarbons (primarily alkanes, cycloalkanes, and aromatics) found in the pore spaces of sedimentary rocks beneath the Earth's surface. It originates from the thermal maturation of buried organic matter (kerogen) over geological timescales (typically 1–500 million years) and migrates from low-permeability source rocks into high-permeability reservoir rocks, where it accumulates beneath impermeable cap rocks in geological traps.* ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **multi-story underground apartment building**: - The **basement (Source Rock)** is a dark, organic-rich room (kitchen waste, leaves, algae) that has been sealed for millions of years and "cooked" by Earth's internal heat, slowly producing oil droplets — this is the **Kitchen** of petroleum. - These oil droplets **squeeze out (Primary & Secondary Migration)** through tiny cracks and porous pathways, much like water flowing through a sponge, rising upward because oil and gas are **lighter than water**. - They finally settle on a **sturdy, watertight roof (Cap Rock / Seal)** inside a structural arch (an **anticline**) — this is the **Reservoir** where oil and gas are stored. - Without the cap rock, the hydrocarbons would leak all the way to the surface (forming **seeps**) and escape. > [!NOTE] > **Key Standard Metric:** > Approximately **70%** of the world's discovered oil reserves are found in **anticlinal traps**, and over **90%** of known oil occurrences are hosted in **clastic sedimentary rocks** (sandstones) and **carbonates** (limestones/dolomites). --- ## 1.2 What is Natural Gas? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** > *Natural Gas is a naturally occurring hydrocarbon-rich gas mixture predominantly composed of methane (CH₄, typically 70–90%), along with ethane (C₂H₆), propane (C₃H₈), butane (C₄H₁₀), and impurities such as carbon dioxide (CO₂), hydrogen sulfide (H₂S), nitrogen (N₂), and water vapor. It exists in three forms: associated (dissolved in crude oil), non-associated (free gas in gas reservoirs), and dissolved (in formation water).* ### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold) - **API Gravity of Light Crude:** **> 31.1°** (light oil) - **Gas-to-Oil Ratio (GOR):** Typically **100 – 7000 scf/STB** - **Methane Critical Temperature:** **−82.3 °C (190.8 K)** - **Methane Critical Pressure:** **4.64 MPa (46.4 bar)** - **Formation Volume Factor of Gas (Bg):** **0.0035 – 0.0050 res bbl/scf** --- ## 1.3 Source Rocks – The Cradle of Petroleum > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** > *A Source Rock is a fine-grained sedimentary rock (typically shale, mudstone, marl, or fine limestone) containing sufficient amounts of organic matter (kerogen) of appropriate type and thermal maturity to generate and expel significant quantities of petroleum (oil and/or gas) when subjected to elevated temperatures and pressures during burial diagenesis and catagenesis.* ### Conceptual Analogy Think of a **Source Rock as a pressure cooker loaded with vegetable matter**: - You start with raw organic waste (Type I, II, III, or IV kerogen). - Seal it (burial) and apply slow heat (geothermal gradient ~25–30 °C/km). - Over millions of years, slow "cooking" (catagenesis) converts the kerogen into droplets of oil and bubbles of natural gas — a process called **thermal cracking / maturation**. > [!NOTE] > **Important:** > A rock can be **organic-rich** (high TOC) but a **poor source rock** if the kerogen is the **wrong type** (e.g., inert Type IV) or **immature** (not yet cooked). Quality of source rock depends on **three pillars**: **Total Organic Carbon (TOC), Kerogen Type, and Thermal Maturity (Ro%).** --- ## 1.4 Characteristics of Oil and Natural Gas ### 1.4.1 Physical Properties of Crude Oil | Property | Typical Range | Significance | |----------|--------------|--------------| | **API Gravity** | 10° – 50° | Classification (light/medium/heavy) | | **Specific Gravity** | 0.7 – 1.0 | Density relative to water | | **Viscosity (cP)** | 0.5 – 1000+ | Flowability | | **Pour Point (°C)** | −40 to +30 | Low-temp operability | | **Sulfur Content (wt%)** | 0.1 – 5.0 | Sweet vs. Sour | | **Color** | Light amber → Black | Indicates API gravity | ### 1.4.2 Physical Properties of Natural Gas | Property | Typical Range | |----------|--------------| | **Specific Gravity (air = 1)** | 0.55 – 0.87 | | **Heating Value (BTU/scf)** | 950 – 1200 | | **Methane Content (mol%)** | 70 – 95 | | **H₂S Content (ppm)** | 0 – 50,000 | > [!NOTE] > **Sweet vs. Sour Classification:** > * **Sweet Crude:** Sulfur < 0.5% (corrosion-friendly). > * **Sour Crude:** Sulfur > 0.5% (requires special metallurgy). > * **Sweet Gas:** H₂S < 4 ppm; **Sour Gas:** H₂S > 4 ppm (requires amine treatment). # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Petroleum System – Seven Essential Elements > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Focus:** > A working petroleum system requires the **simultaneous presence** of: (1) Source Rock, (2) Reservoir Rock, (3) Cap Rock (Seal), (4) Migration Pathway, (5) Trap, (6) Maturation (Timing), and (7) Preservation. ### Detailed Step-by-Step Logic **Step 1 — Deposition of Organic Matter (Sedimentation):** Dead plankton, algae, and terrestrial plant material accumulate in anoxic (oxygen-poor) basins where bacterial decay is suppressed. This preserves hydrogen-rich organic matter, which over geological time transforms into **kerogen**. **Step 2 — Burial & Compaction:** Continued sedimentation buries the organic-rich layer. With depth, pore fluids are expelled (compaction), and pressure rises hydrostatically then lithostatically. **Step 3 — Diagenesis (0 – 50 °C):** Biochemical changes dominate. Kerogen forms but does not yet generate significant hydrocarbons. Methane (biogenic) may form. **Step 4 — Catagenesis (50 – 150 °C):** *(The "Oil Window")* Kerogen undergoes **thermal cracking (pyrolysis)** to release liquid hydrocarbons. The temperature window for peak oil generation is approximately **60 – 120 °C**, corresponding to vitrinite reflectance **Ro = 0.6% – 1.0%**. **Step 5 — Metagenesis (150 – 200 °C):** *(The "Gas Window")* Cracking of heavier hydrocarbons → wet gas → dry gas. **Ro > 1.3%** typically yields dry gas. **Step 6 — Primary Migration (Expulsion):** Generated hydrocarbons are expelled from the source rock into carrier beds under compaction and pressure. The driving forces are **buoyancy, capillary pressure, and overpressure**. **Step 7 — Secondary Migration & Accumulation:** Hydrocarbons migrate upward through permeable carrier beds (sandstone, fractured carbonates) until trapped beneath an impermeable seal in a structural or stratigraphic trap, forming a **reservoir**. --- ## 2.2 Source Rock — The Three Quality Pillars ### Pillar 1: Total Organic Carbon (TOC) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition:** TOC is the mass of organic carbon per unit mass of rock, expressed in **weight percent (wt%)**. - **Poor Source Rock:** TOC < 0.5 wt% - **Fair Source Rock:** 0.5 – 1.0 wt% - **Good Source Rock:** 1.0 – 2.0 wt% - **Very Good Source Rock:** 2.0 – 4.0 wt% - **Excellent Source Rock:** TOC > 4.0 wt% ### Pillar 2: Kerogen Type (Quality of Organic Matter) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Kerogen** is the insoluble organic matter dispersed in sedimentary rocks. It is classified into four types based on **Hydrogen Index (HI)** and **Oxygen Index (OI)** from Rock-Eval pyrolysis. | Type | Origin | HI (mg HC/g TOC) | Primary Product | |------|--------|------------------|------------------| | **Type I** | Algal lacustrine (e.g., Green River Shale) | > 600 | Oil-prone | | **Type II** | Marine planktonic (e.g., Toarcian Shale) | 300 – 600 | Oil + Gas | | **Type III** | Terrestrial higher plants (coaly) | 50 – 200 | Gas-prone | | **Type IV** | Inert, oxidized, recycled | < 50 | None (inert) | ### Pillar 3: Thermal Maturity (Vitrinite Reflectance, Ro%) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Vitrinite Reflectance (Ro%)** measures the percentage of incident light reflected from the polished surface of vitrinite macerals. It is the **gold-standard maturity indicator**. - **Immature:** Ro < 0.6% - **Oil Window (Peak):** Ro = 0.6% – 1.0% - **Wet Gas:** Ro = 1.0% – 1.3% - **Dry Gas:** Ro = 1.3% – 2.0% - **Overmature (Graphitic):** Ro > 2.0% --- ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Formula / Parameter | Equation | Units | Application | |---------------------|----------|-------|-------------| | **API Gravity** | $\text{API} = \dfrac{141.5}{\gamma_o} - 131.5$ | degrees (°) | Oil density classification | | **Specific Gravity** | $\gamma_o = \dfrac{141.5}{\text{API} + 131.5}$ | dimensionless | Density relative to water | | **Formation Volume Factor (Bo)** | $B_o = \dfrac{V_{\text{reservoir}}}{V_{\text{stock tank}}}$ | rbbl/STB | Connate water + solution gas effect | | **Gas-Oil Ratio (GOR)** | $\text{GOR} = \dfrac{V_{g,\text{sc}}}{V_{o,\text{STB}}}$ | scf/STB | Vol. of gas at sc per STB of oil | | **Vitrinite Reflectance Gradient** | $\dfrac{dR_o}{dz} \approx 0.0002 \text{ to } 0.0003$ | %/m | Maturation with depth | | **Geothermal Gradient** | $\dfrac{dT}{dz} \approx 25 \text{ to } 30$ | °C/km | Heat flow for maturation | | **Hydrogen Index (HI)** | $\text{HI} = \dfrac{S_2 \times 100}{\text{TOC}}$ | mg HC/g TOC | Kerogen type indicator | | **Production Index (PI)** | $\text{PI} = \dfrac{S_1}{S_1 + S_2}$ | dimensionless | Migration vs. in-situ generation | | **Transformation Ratio (TR)** | $\text{TR} = \dfrac{S_1}{S_1 + S_2}$ | dimensionless | Fraction of generated HC expelled | | **S1 / S2 from Rock-Eval** | $S_1$ = free HC; $S_2$ = pyrolyzed HC | mg HC/g rock | Rock-Eval Pyrolysis output | > [!NOTE] > **Real-world utility:** > The **Vitrinite Reflectance** model is the single most-used parameter worldwide for ranking exploration blocks and predicting hydrocarbon phase (oil vs. gas) before drilling. Geochemists use it daily in **basin modeling** (e.g., PetroMod, BasinMod) to predict charge risk. --- ## 2.4 Kerogen Degradation Kinetics – Lopatin–Waples Method (Conceptual) > [!NOTE] > The **Lopatin Time-Temperature Index (TTI)** quantitatively predicts maturity: > $$\text{TTI} = \sum_{n} \left( \Delta t_n \cdot r^{n} \right)$$ > where $\Delta t_n$ is the time spent in temperature interval $n$, and $r$ is a doubling factor (= 2 per 10 °C rise). A **TTI ≈ 15** marks oil window onset; **TTI ≈ 75** marks peak oil; **TTI ≈ 160** marks gas window. **Modern Kinetic Approach (more accurate):** $$\dfrac{dX}{dt} = k(T) \cdot (1 - X)^n$$ where $X$ = fractional conversion, $k(T) = A \exp(-E/RT)$, $A$ = frequency factor, $E$ = activation energy, $n$ = reaction order. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: API Gravity from Specific Gravity Starting from the **definition of API gravity** as a hydrometer scale: ### Step 1 — Begin with the empirical relationship The American Petroleum Institute defined API gravity in 1921 to be inversely related to specific gravity, calibrated so that water yields **10° API** and a 141.5 specific gravity oil yields **0° API**. ### Step 2 — Express mathematically The defining equation is: $$\text{API} = \frac{141.5}{\gamma_o} - 131.5$$ where $\gamma_o$ is the specific gravity of oil at **60 °F (15.56 °C)** relative to water at the same temperature. ### Step 3 — Verify boundary conditions **Boundary 1:** For water, $\gamma_o = 1.0$: $$\text{API} = \frac{141.5}{1.0} - 131.5 = 10.0°\ \text{API} \ \checkmark$$ **Boundary 2:** An oil with $\gamma_o = 1.0$ (just denser than water): $$\text{API} = \frac{141.5}{1.0} - 131.5 = 10.0°\ \text{API}$$ **Boundary 3:** A light crude with $\gamma_o = 0.85$: $$\text{API} = \frac{141.5}{0.85} - 131.5 = 166.47 - 131.5 = 34.97°\ \text{API} \approx 35°\ \text{API}$$ This falls in the **light crude** category (API > 31.1°). $\checkmark$ ### Step 4 — Inverse derivation (find $\gamma_o$ from API) Starting from the API definition, multiply both sides by $\gamma_o$: $$\gamma_o \cdot \text{API} = 141.5 - 131.5 \cdot \gamma_o$$ Rearranging to isolate $\gamma_o$: $$131.5 \cdot \gamma_o + \gamma_o \cdot \text{API} = 141.5$$ $$\gamma_o (131.5 + \text{API}) = 141.5$$ $$\boxed{\gamma_o = \dfrac{141.5}{131.5 + \text{API}}}$$ This form is used routinely by KTU examiners for quick density conversions in Part-B problems. --- ## 3.2 Worked Numerical – Source Rock Classification **Problem:** A shale sample yields the following Rock-Eval data: - TOC = 3.5 wt% - $S_1$ (free hydrocarbons) = 1.2 mg HC/g rock - $S_2$ (pyrolyzed hydrocarbons) = 9.0 mg HC/g rock - $T_{\max}$ = 440 °C - $R_o$ = 0.75% **Required:** Classify the source rock and predict hydrocarbon phase. ### Step 1 — Compute Hydrogen Index (HI) The HI formula relates pyrolyzable carbon to total organic carbon: $$\text{HI} = \frac{S_2 \times 100}{\text{TOC (wt\%)}}$$ Substituting values: $$\text{HI} = \frac{9.0 \times 100}{3.5}$$ $$\text{HI} = 257.14\ \text{mg HC/g TOC}$$ This falls in the **Type II** kerogen range (300 – 600 is typical Type II; values near 250 are Type II/III boundary). We classify as **Type II–III mixed**, oil and gas prone. ### Step 2 — Compute Production Index (PI) $$\text{PI} = \frac{S_1}{S_1 + S_2}$$ $$\text{PI} = \frac{1.2}{1.2 + 9.0} = \frac{1.2}{10.2}$$ $$\text{PI} = 0.118$$ For an unexpelled source rock, PI < 0.1 indicates **immature**; for a productive source rock PI = 0.1 – 0.4. Our value 0.118 indicates the rock is **mature and starting to expel hydrocarbons** — this is a **good active source rock**. ### Step 3 — Interpret Vitrinite Reflectance $R_o = 0.75\%$ falls in the **mid-oil window** (peak generation at 0.8 – 0.9% Ro). The rock is **mature, oil-prone**. ### Step 4 — Interpret Tmax $T_{\max} = 440$ °C → corresponds to **mature, oil window** (435 – 445 °C = mature; > 445 °C = gas window). ### Step 5 — Final Classification Table | Parameter | Value | Interpretation | |-----------|-------|----------------| | **TOC** | 3.5 wt% | Very Good Source Rock | | **HI** | 257 mg HC/g TOC | Type II / III mixed (oil-gas prone) | | **PI** | 0.118 | Mature, actively expelling | | **$T_{\max}$** | 440 °C | Mature (oil window) | | **$R_o$** | 0.75% | Mid-oil window | **Conclusion:** This is a **very good, mature, Type II–III source rock** in the **mid-oil window**, with strong potential to generate **oil with associated gas**. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Kerogen Van Krevelen Diagram Interpretation The **Van Krevelen Diagram** plots HI vs. OI, and kerogen maturity paths follow evolutionary trajectories. | Kerogen Type | HI Range | OI Range | HI/OI Ratio | Product | |--------------|----------|----------|-------------|---------| | Type I | > 600 | < 50 | > 12 | Oil | | Type II | 300 – 600 | 50 – 150 | 2 – 12 | Oil + Gas | | Type III | 50 – 200 | 150 – 400 | < 2 | Gas | | Type IV | < 50 | > 400 | < 0.5 | None | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Insight:** > During maturation, **HI decreases** and **OI decreases** (kerogen loses H and O). Mature Type II kerogen on the Van Krevelen plot lies near the **Type III field** even though its origin was marine. This is why $R_o$ and $T_{\max}$ must be used **together with HI/OI** to avoid misclassification. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Petroleum System Workflow – Block-Level Functional Architecture The following Mermaid flowchart maps the **functional interactions** of the petroleum system, from organic deposition to reservoir accumulation. This is a **KTU-board-friendly** schematic that satisfies the "process flow" question type. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Organic Matter Deposition
Algae Plankton Plant Debris] --> B[Burial and Compaction
Subsidence and Sediment Loading] B --> C[Diagenesis Stage
Temperature 0 to 50 C] C --> D[Kerogen Formation
Insoluble Organic Concentrate] D --> E{Thermal Maturity Check
Ro percent Tmax} E -->|Immature Ro less than 0.6| F[No Significant Expulsion
Biogenic Gas Only] E -->|Oil Window 0.6 to 1.0| G[Catagenesis Peak Oil
Liquid Hydrocarbons] E -->|Gas Window 1.0 to 2.0| H[Metagenesis Dry Gas
Methane Dominant] G --> I[Primary Migration
Expulsion from Source Rock] H --> I I --> J[Secondary Migration
Buoyancy Driven Upward] J --> K{Trap Configuration
Structural Stratigraphic} K -->|Anticline| L[Reservoir Accumulation
Sandstone Carbonate] K -->|Fault Trap| L K -->|Reef Buildup| L L --> M[Cap Rock Seal
Impermeable Shale Salt] M --> N[Commercial Hydrocarbon Pool
Oil Gas Condensate] F --> O[Failed Petroleum System] N --> P[Exploration and Production
Drilling and Extraction] O --> Q[Geochemical Reassessment] ``` **Interpretation Guide for KTU Students:** - The decision diamonds (E, K) are the most **commonly examined** logic points. - Memorize the **Ro% thresholds**: 0.6 (oil onset), 1.0 (oil peak end), 1.3 (wet-to-dry gas). - The vertical chain (A → B → C → D) represents **geological time (millions of years)**. --- ## 4.2 Source Rock Evaluation Workflow ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SR[Source Rock Evaluation] A1[Rock Sample Collection
Core or Cuttings] --> A2[Total Organic Carbon TOC
wt percent] A2 --> A3[TOC Threshold Check
More than 0.5 percent] A3 -->|Pass| A4[Rock Eval Pyrolysis
S1 S2 S3 Tmax] A3 -->|Fail| A5[Reject as Source Rock] A4 --> A6[Kerogen Type
HI OI Plot] A4 --> A7[Maturity Check
Ro Tmax] A6 --> A8{Combined Quality} A7 --> A8 A8 --> A9[Final Classification
Good Fair Excellent] end ``` --- ## 4.3 Kerogen Maturity Path (Van Krevelen Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph VKD[Van Krevelen Diagram] T1[Type I Kerogen
HI greater than 600
Lacustrine Algal] --> MAT[Maturation Path
H and O Loss] T2[Type II Kerogen
HI 300 to 600
Marine Planktonic] --> MAT T3[Type III Kerogen
HI 50 to 200
Terrestrial Woody] --> MAT T4[Type IV Kerogen
HI less than 50
Inert Oxidized] --> ST[Stagnant No Evolution] MAT --> OIL[Oil Window Zone] MAT --> GAS[Gas Window Zone] OIL --> DRY[Overmature Graphitic
Ro greater than 2.0] GAS --> DRY end ``` --- ## 4.4 Migration and Trap Architecture (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph EARTH[Cross Section of Petroleum Trap] SR2[Source Rock Shale
Depth 3 to 5 km] -->|Primary Migration| CB[Carrier Bed Sandstone] CB -->|Secondary Migration| ANT[Anticlinal Trap
Permeable Reservoir] ANT --> CAP[Cap Rock Shale
Impermeable Seal] CAP --> SURF[Surface Seep
If Seal Fails] ANT --> WELL[Production Well] end ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiners love this diagram.** When asked "Explain the migration and trapping of hydrocarbons," draw a similar cross-section labeling: **Source Rock → Migration Pathway → Reservoir Rock → Cap Rock → Trap → Well**. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Q: Define a "source rock" in petroleum geology. List the three essential parameters used to evaluate a source rock.** **Model Answer:** > [!NOTE] > **Definition:** > A **source rock** is a fine-grained sedimentary rock (typically shale, mudstone, or marl) that contains sufficient quantities of organic matter (kerogen) of appropriate type and thermal maturity to generate and expel petroleum upon burial and heating. **Three essential parameters (the "Three Pillars of Source Rock Quality"):** 1. **Total Organic Carbon (TOC)** — Quantity of organic matter (wt%). 2. **Kerogen Type** — Quality, determined by Hydrogen Index (HI) from Rock-Eval. 3. **Thermal Maturity** — Determined by Vitrinite Reflectance (Ro%) and Tmax. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Q: Differentiate between Type I, Type II, Type III, and Type IV kerogen.** **Model Answer:** | Kerogen Type | Origin | HI (mg HC/g TOC) | Hydrocarbon Product | |--------------|--------|-------------------|---------------------| | **Type I** | Lacustrine algal | > 600 | Highly oil-prone | | **Type II** | Marine planktonic | 300 – 600 | Oil + gas | | **Type III** | Terrestrial (woody, coaly) | 50 – 200 | Gas-prone | | **Type IV** | Inert / oxidized / recycled | < 50 | None (non-source) | --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] **Q: (a) [7 Marks] Describe the geological occurrence of oil and natural gas. With a neat diagram, explain the formation of an anticlinal trap.** **(b) [7 Marks] Discuss the classification of crude oil based on API gravity, and the classification of natural gas based on methane content and H₂S content.** --- #### Model Solution for (a): **Geological Occurrence of Oil and Natural Gas:** Petroleum occurs in the pore spaces of **sedimentary rocks** beneath the Earth's surface, formed from buried organic matter (kerogen) that has been transformed by heat and pressure over millions of years. The geological occurrence requires **four essential geological elements** that coexist in space and time: 1. **Source Rock** – A fine-grained, organic-rich rock (shale/marl) that generates hydrocarbons through thermal maturation of kerogen during catagenesis (60–150 °C). 2. **Reservoir Rock** – A porous and permeable rock (sandstone, limestone, dolomite) that stores the migrated hydrocarbons. Typical porosity: 10–30%; permeability: 50–500 mD. 3. **Cap Rock (Seal)** – An impermeable rock layer (shale, salt, tight carbonate) that prevents further upward migration of hydrocarbons, forcing them to accumulate in the trap. 4. **Trap** – A geological configuration that localizes hydrocarbon accumulation. Common types include **anticlinal traps, fault traps, stratigraphic traps (unconformities, reefs), and salt domes**. **Migration Phases:** - **Primary Migration:** Expulsion of hydrocarbons from the source rock into adjacent permeable carrier beds. - **Secondary Migration:** Upward movement of hydrocarbons through carrier beds (driven by buoyancy, since hydrocarbons are less dense than formation water) until encountering a seal. **Anticlinal Trap (Diagram Description):** An **anticline** is an upward-arching fold in sedimentary strata. When hydrocarbons migrate upward through the permeable carrier beds into the crest of an anticline, they become trapped beneath the impermeable cap rock that drapes over the fold. The denser formation water occupies the lower flanks, while oil and gas rise to the highest point of the closure — **gas at the top, oil in the middle, water at the bottom** (gravity segregation). > **Diagram (refer to Section 4.4):** > Source Rock (shale) → Migration → Anticline reservoir (sandstone) → Cap Rock (shale) → Gas cap (top) → Oil leg (middle) → Water leg (bottom). --- #### Model Solution for (b): **Classification of Crude Oil by API Gravity:** $$\text{API} = \frac{141.5}{\gamma_o} - 131.5$$ | Class | API Gravity (°) | Specific Gravity | Example | |-------|-----------------|------------------|---------| | **Light Crude** | > 31.1 | < 0.87 | Brent, WTI | | **Medium Crude** | 22.3 – 31.1 | 0.87 – 0.92 | Arabian Medium | | **Heavy Crude** | 10 – 22.3 | 0.92 – 1.00 | Venezuelan heavy | | **Extra-Heavy / Bitumen** | < 10 | > 1.00 | Athabasca oil sands | **Classification of Natural Gas:** **By Methane Content:** - **Rich Gas (Wet Gas):** Methane < 90%, contains significant C₂+ (ethane, propane, butane). Yields condensates. - **Lean Gas (Dry Gas):** Methane > 90%, minimal C₂+. Burns cleanly, pipeline-quality. **By H₂S Content:** - **Sweet Gas:** H₂S < 4 ppm (4 grains per 100 scf). No special treatment. - **Sour Gas:** H₂S > 4 ppm. Requires amine sweetening (MDEA, DEA) and sulfur recovery (Claus process). - **Acidic Gas:** Contains H₂S + CO₂ (corrosive). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - Students often confuse **wet gas** (high C₂+) with **sour gas** (high H₂S) — these are **independent** classifications! > - Don't write "wet gas contains water vapor" — it contains **heavier hydrocarbons**, not water. > - Always give **numerical thresholds** (e.g., 4 ppm H₂S, 90% CH₄) — the examiner gives full marks only when quantitative values are quoted. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023] **Q: (a) [7 Marks] What is kerogen? Explain the four types of kerogen with the help of a Van Krevelen diagram.** **(b) [7 Marks] A shale sample has the following Rock-Eval data: TOC = 4.0 wt%, S₁ = 1.5 mg HC/g rock, S₂ = 12.0 mg HC/g rock, Tmax = 445 °C, and Ro = 0.85%. Classify the source rock in terms of organic richness, kerogen type, and thermal maturity. Predict the expected hydrocarbon phase.** --- #### Model Solution for (a): **Kerogen – Definition:** Kerogen is the **insoluble, finely disseminated organic matter** in sedimentary rocks that, upon progressive burial and heating (catagenesis), generates liquid and gaseous hydrocarbons. It constitutes the precursor material for petroleum and is the dominant form of organic carbon on Earth (~10¹⁶ tons of carbon). **Four Types of Kerogen:** **Type I (Sapropelic / Algal):** - Origin: Lacustrine algal blooms (e.g., Botryococcus, Tasmanites); deposited in freshwater/brackish anoxic lakes. - Atomic H/C: ~1.5+; O/C: ~0.1. - HI > 600 mg HC/g TOC. - **Highly oil-prone**; example: Green River Shale (Utah, USA). **Type II (Planktonic / Marine):** - Origin: Marine phytoplankton, zooplankton, and bacterial remains deposited under marine anoxic conditions (e.g., black shales). - Atomic H/C: ~1.2–1.5; O/C: ~0.1–0.2. - HI = 300 – 600 mg HC/g TOC. - **Oil and gas-prone**; example: Toarcian Shale (Paris Basin), Kimmeridge Clay (North Sea). **Type III (Humic / Woody):** - Origin: Terrestrial higher plant material (wood, leaves, pollen); deposited in deltaic and fluvial environments. - Atomic H/C: ~0.7–1.0; O/C: ~0.2–0.4. - HI = 50 – 200 mg HC/g TOC. - **Gas-prone**; example: Coaly formations of the North Sea, Gippsland Basin. **Type IV (Inert / Residual):** - Origin: Highly oxidized, recycled, or metamorphosed organic matter. - HI < 50 mg HC/g TOC. - **No hydrocarbon generation potential** — acts as a "carbon sink." **Van Krevelen Diagram (H/C vs. O/C):** The Van Krevelen diagram is a plot of atomic H/C ratio (y-axis) vs. atomic O/C ratio (x-axis). Each kerogen type follows a **distinct evolutionary path** as it loses hydrogen and oxygen during maturation. The Type I and Type II kerogens start with high H/C and low O/C and progress diagonally toward the origin (zero H/C and O/C) as they generate oil and gas. Type III starts at lower H/C and higher O/C. Type IV remains inert. > **KTU Diagram Note:** In the KTU exam, draw the four kerogen fields as **labeled quadrilaterals** on a graph with **H/C on y-axis (0–2)** and **O/C on x-axis (0–0.5)**. Mark the **oil window** and **gas window** zones along each evolutionary path. --- #### Model Solution for (b): **Given Data:** - TOC = 4.0 wt% - S₁ = 1.5 mg HC/g rock - S₂ = 12.0 mg HC/g rock - Tmax = 445 °C - R₀ = 0.85% **Step 1 — Organic Richness (TOC):** TOC = 4.0 wt% → falls in the **"Very Good"** category (2.0 – 4.0 wt%). Hence the rock is **very good** in terms of organic richness. **Step 2 — Hydrogen Index (Kerogen Type):** $$\text{HI} = \frac{S_2 \times 100}{\text{TOC}} = \frac{12.0 \times 100}{4.0} = 300\ \text{mg HC/g TOC}$$ HI = 300 → lower boundary of **Type II kerogen** (300 – 600). So this is a **Type II kerogen** (marine, oil + gas prone). **Step 3 — Production Index:** $$\text{PI} = \frac{S_1}{S_1 + S_2} = \frac{1.5}{1.5 + 12.0} = \frac{1.5}{13.5} = 0.111$$ PI = 0.111 → in the productive range (0.1 – 0.4), indicating the rock is **mature and actively generating/expelling hydrocarbons**. **Step 4 — Maturity from Tmax:** Tmax = 445 °C → at the **upper end of the oil window** (435–445 °C = oil window; > 445 °C = gas window). Suggests **mature, late oil window**. **Step 5 — Maturity from Ro:** Ro = 0.85% → in the **peak oil window** (0.6 – 1.0%). **Step 6 — Predict Hydrocarbon Phase:** | Parameter | Value | Interpretation | |-----------|-------|----------------| | TOC | 4.0 wt% | Very Good organic richness | | HI | 300 mg HC/g TOC | Type II kerogen | | PI | 0.111 | Productive, mature | | Tmax | 445 °C | Late oil window | | Ro | 0.85% | Peak oil window | **Conclusion:** The sample is a **very good, mature, Type II source rock** in the **late oil window**, with a high probability of generating **light to medium crude oil with associated gas**. The HI near the Type II/III boundary suggests a small gas component may co-exist. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - **Common mistake 1:** Students forget to compute the HI — they directly say "Type II" from S₂. **Always compute HI first**, then classify. > - **Common mistake 2:** Confusing **PI = 0.1** as a maturity threshold. PI is an **expulsion indicator**, not a maturity indicator. Use **Tmax and Ro** for maturity. > - **Common mistake 3:** Writing "Ro = 0.85% means gas" — actually 0.85% is the **peak of the oil window**, NOT gas (gas starts at ~1.0–1.3%). > - **Common mistake 4:** Not concluding with a **specific phase prediction** (light oil? gas? condensate?). Always end the answer with a phase prediction. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist – KTU Board Examination:** - ✅ **Petroleum System** has **7 elements** (Source, Reservoir, Cap, Migration, Trap, Maturity, Preservation) — all must coexist. - ✅ **Source Rock** is a fine-grained, organic-rich rock; evaluated by **TOC, Kerogen Type, Maturity**. - ✅ **TOC threshold:** > 0.5 wt% is the minimum; > 2 wt% is "very good." - ✅ **Kerogen Types:** I (lacustrine, oil), II (marine, oil+gas), III (terrestrial, gas), IV (inert). - ✅ **Hydrogen Index (HI):** $S_2 \times 100 / \text{TOC}$; > 600 = Type I, 300–600 = Type II, 50–200 = Type III. - ✅ **Vitrinite Reflectance (Ro%):** < 0.6% immature; 0.6–1.0% oil window; 1.0–1.3% wet gas; > 1.3% dry gas. - ✅ **Tmax from Rock-Eval:** 435–445 °C = mature oil; > 445 °C = gas; < 435 °C = immature. - ✅ **API Gravity formula:** $\text{API} = 141.5/\gamma_o - 131.5$. Light > 31.1°, Heavy < 22.3°. - ✅ **Sweet vs. Sour:** Crude S < 0.5% sweet; Gas H₂S < 4 ppm sweet. - ✅ **Wet vs. Dry Gas:** Wet = significant C₂+; Dry = > 90% CH₄. - ✅ **Primary migration** = expulsion from source rock; **Secondary migration** = upward travel to trap. - ✅ **Anticlinal trap** is the most common structural trap (~70% of world oil reserves). - ✅ **Hydrocarbon segregation in trap:** Gas (top) → Oil (middle) → Water (bottom) due to density differences. - ✅ **Rock-Eval Parameters:** $S_1$ = free HC; $S_2$ = pyrolyzed HC; $S_3$ = CO₂ from kerogen; Tmax = temp at max S₂. - ✅ **Common source rocks:** Shale, mudstone, marl, fine limestone (e.g., Kimmeridge Clay, Bakken Shale, Green River Shale). - ✅ **Van Krevelen diagram** is the standard tool for kerogen classification; axes are atomic H/C and O/C. - ✅ **GOR (Gas-Oil Ratio):** High GOR > 2000 scf/STB indicates gas cap or gas-condensate reservoir.

topics.title: Reservoir Rocks
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT528 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Reservoir Rocks

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Reservoir Rock** is defined as a subsurface geological formation that possesses two essential petrophysical properties simultaneously: **porosity** (to store hydrocarbons) and **permeability** (to allow hydrocarbons to flow through it). In petroleum engineering, reservoir rocks are the porous, permeable subsurface formations that contain economically producible quantities of oil, natural gas, and/or water.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** According to PEPOT528 Module 1, reservoir rocks are classified broadly into **Clastic (Siliciclastic) Rocks** — primarily Sandstones and Conglomerates — and **Non-Clastic (Carbonate) Rocks** — primarily Limestone and Dolomite. The study also extends to **Unconventional Reservoirs** (Shale Gas, Tight Gas, Coal Bed Methane) in the modern syllabus context.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **kitchen sponge** held underwater:
- The **holes inside the sponge** that hold the water = **Porosity** (storage capacity)
- The **connected channels** that let water drip out when squeezed = **Permeability** (flow capacity)

A reservoir rock works exactly the same way underground. The pores store the oil and gas like a sponge holds water, and the interconnected pore network acts like tiny pipes that allow the hydrocarbons to flow toward a production well when the pressure is reduced.

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Distinction:** A rock can be porous but NOT permeable (e.g., pumice, some shales) — these hold hydrocarbons but won't release them easily. A good reservoir rock must have **BOTH** porosity AND permeability. Think of a jar full of ping-pong balls glued together — full of holes (porous) but no connections (zero permeability).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Engineering Units

| Property | Symbol | Standard Unit |
|----------|--------|---------------|
| Porosity | $\phi$ | fraction (0–1) or percentage (%) |
| Permeability | $k$ | **Darcy (D)** or **millidarcy (md)** |
| Fluid Viscosity | $\mu$ | centipoise (cp) |
| Pressure | $P$ | psi or MPa |
| Length | $L$ | cm or ft |
| Flow Rate | $Q$ | cm³/s or bbl/day |
| Saturation | $S$ | fraction (0–1) |
| Capillary Pressure | $P_c$ | psi or dynes/cm² |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion to remember:** **1 Darcy = 1000 millidarcy (md)**. A good oil reservoir typically has permeability in the range of **10 md to 1000 md**, and porosity between **5% to 30%**.

---

## 1.4 Role in the Petroleum Value Chain

Reservoir rocks are the **first link** in the upstream petroleum value chain. The sequence is:

$$
\text{Source Rock} \rightarrow \text{Migration Pathway} \rightarrow \text{Reservoir Rock} \rightarrow \text{Seal/Cap Rock} \rightarrow \text{Trap}
$$

Without a suitable reservoir rock, even the richest source rock cannot deliver hydrocarbons to a wellbore.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Petrophysical Properties of Reservoir Rocks

### 2.1.1 Porosity ($\phi$)

Porosity is the measure of the void space within a rock available to store fluids.

**Classification of Porosity:**

- **Primary Porosity:** Formed during the original deposition of the rock (intergranular pores in sand).
- **Secondary Porosity:** Formed after deposition through geological processes such as:
  - Dissolution (creating vugs and caverns in carbonates)
  - Fracturing (tectonic stresses)
  - Dolomitization (in carbonates)

- **Total Porosity ($\phi_t$):** Ratio of ALL pore space (connected + isolated) to bulk volume.
- **Effective Porosity ($\phi_e$):** Ratio of INTERCONNECTED pore space to bulk volume. **This is the engineering-relevant value.**

### 2.1.2 Permeability ($k$)

Permeability is the ability of a rock to transmit fluids through its connected pore network.

**Types of Permeability:**

- **Absolute Permeability ($k$):** Measured when rock is 100% saturated with a single fluid.
- **Effective Permeability ($k_o, k_w, k_g$):** Permeability to a particular fluid when multiple fluids are present.
- **Relative Permeability ($k_{ro}, k_{rw}, k_{rg}$):** Ratio of effective permeability to absolute permeability.

### 2.1.3 Fluid Saturation ($S$)

The fraction of pore volume occupied by a specific fluid:

$$S_w + S_o + S_g = 1$$

Where $S_w$ = water saturation, $S_o$ = oil saturation, $S_g$ = gas saturation.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Property | Formula | Units | Description |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Porosity | $\phi = \dfrac{V_p}{V_b}$ | fraction | $V_p$ = pore volume, $V_b$ = bulk volume |
| 2 | Bulk Volume | $V_b = V_p + V_s$ | cm³ | $V_s$ = solid/grain volume |
| 3 | Absolute Permeability (Darcy) | $k = \dfrac{Q \mu L}{A \Delta P}$ | Darcy | From Darcy's Law |
| 4 | Relative Permeability | $k_{ro} = \dfrac{k_o}{k}$ | dimensionless | Ratio of effective to absolute |
| 5 | Water Saturation | $S_w = \dfrac{V_w}{V_p}$ | fraction | Volume of water / pore volume |
| 6 | Hydrocarbon Saturation | $S_{hc} = 1 - S_w$ | fraction | Oil + Gas saturation |
| 7 | Capillary Pressure | $P_c = P_{nw} - P_w$ | psi | Non-wetting minus wetting phase pressure |
| 8 | Formation Resistivity Factor | $F = \dfrac{R_o}{R_w} = \dfrac{a}{\phi^m}$ | dimensionless | Archie equation component |
| 9 | Permeability Range | $k$ (md) | md | 1 md = 0.001 Darcy |
| 10 | Porosity Quality | $\phi$ | % | Poor: <5%, Fair: 5–10%, Good: 10–20%, Excellent: >20% |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Darcy's Law Foundation:** Henry Darcy (1856) established the fundamental law governing fluid flow through porous media. This is the cornerstone equation for reservoir engineering calculations. The **linear form** of Darcy's Law is:
>
> $$Q = \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$$
>
> The **radial form** (for flow toward a wellbore) is:
>
> $$Q = \dfrac{2\pi k h (P_e - P_w)}{\mu \ln(r_e/r_w)}$$

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding reservoir rock properties is essential for:

1. **Reserve Estimation** — Calculating original oil in place (OOIP) and gas in place (OGIP).
2. **Production Forecasting** — Predicting flow rates and recovery factors.
3. **Well Logging Interpretation** — Correlating log responses to rock properties.
4. **Enhanced Oil Recovery (EOR)** — Designing fluid injection schemes.
5. **Reservoir Simulation** — Building numerical models to predict field performance.

---

## 2.4 Why These Properties Matter — The "Why & How"

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Porosity Matters:** Determines how much hydrocarbon can be stored. Without porosity, no fluid storage is possible. **How to Measure:** Core analysis (laboratory), wireline logs (Neutron, Density, Sonic).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Permeability Matters:** Determines how fast hydrocarbons can flow. Without permeability, no production is possible. **How to Measure:** Core flooding (laboratory), well testing (DST, build-up tests).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Porosity Formula

**Starting Concept:** A reservoir rock sample is composed of two parts:
- **Solid/Grain Volume** ($V_s$): The actual rock matrix
- **Pore Volume** ($V_p$): The empty spaces between grains

**Step 1:** Define Bulk Volume
The total external volume of the rock sample (including pores):

$$V_b = V_p + V_s$$

**Step 2:** Define Porosity
Porosity is the ratio of pore space to total bulk volume:

$$\phi = \dfrac{V_p}{V_b}$$

**Step 3:** Express in Percentage

$$\phi \% = \dfrac{V_p}{V_b} \times 100\%$$

**Step 4:** Express in terms of grain density ($\rho_s$) and bulk density ($\rho_b$)

$$\phi = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_b}{\rho_s}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** If a rock has bulk density of 2.20 g/cm³ and grain density of 2.65 g/cm³:

$$\phi = 1 - \dfrac{2.20}{2.65} = 1 - 0.8302 = 0.1698 \approx 17\%$$

## 3.2 Derivation of Darcy's Law

**Starting Concept:** Henri Darcy experimentally observed that the volumetric flow rate ($Q$) of a fluid through a porous medium is:
- Proportional to the cross-sectional area ($A$)
- Proportional to the pressure difference ($\Delta P$)
- Inversely proportional to the fluid viscosity ($\mu$)
- Inversely proportional to the length ($L$)

**Step 1:** Establish the proportional relationship:

$$Q \propto \dfrac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$$

**Step 2:** Introduce the proportionality constant — the **permeability** ($k$):

$$Q = k \cdot \dfrac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange to solve for permeability:

$$k = \dfrac{Q \cdot \mu \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta P}$$

**Step 4:** Define the unit of permeability:
If $Q$ = 1 cm³/s, $L$ = 1 cm, $A$ = 1 cm², $\mu$ = 1 cp, $\Delta P$ = 1 atm, then $k$ = 1 **Darcy**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **1 Darcy definition:** A rock has a permeability of 1 Darcy when a fluid of 1 centipoise viscosity flowing under a pressure gradient of 1 atmosphere per centimeter produces a flow rate of 1 cm³/s per cm² of cross-sectional area.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — Permeability Calculation

**Problem:** A core sample of length $L = 10$ cm and cross-sectional area $A = 5$ cm² is subjected to a laboratory flow test. Oil with viscosity $\mu = 2$ cp is used. The upstream pressure is $P_1 = 5$ atm and downstream is $P_2 = 1$ atm. The measured flow rate is $Q = 4$ cm³/s. Calculate the permeability in Darcy and millidarcy.

**Step 1:** Identify given values:
- $L = 10$ cm
- $A = 5$ cm²
- $\mu = 2$ cp
- $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2 = 5 - 1 = 4$ atm
- $Q = 4$ cm³/s

**Step 2:** Apply Darcy's equation:

$$k = \dfrac{Q \cdot \mu \cdot L}{A \cdot \Delta P}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values:

$$k = \dfrac{4 \text{ cm}^3/\text{s} \times 2 \text{ cp} \times 10 \text{ cm}}{5 \text{ cm}^2 \times 4 \text{ atm}}$$

**Step 4:** Simplify numerator and denominator:

$$k = \dfrac{80}{20} = 4 \text{ Darcy}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to millidarcy:

$$k = 4 \text{ Darcy} \times 1000 = 4000 \text{ md}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is an **excellent reservoir rock** — permeability of 4000 md is characteristic of a very high-quality sandstone or a vuggy carbonate.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Porosity from Density Log

**Problem:** A density log reads a bulk density of $\rho_b = 2.35$ g/cm³. The matrix (grain) density is $\rho_s = 2.65$ g/cm³ (quartz sandstone). Calculate the porosity.

**Step 1:** Apply the density-porosity relationship:

$$\phi = \dfrac{\rho_s - \rho_b}{\rho_s - \rho_f}$$

Assuming the fluid density $\rho_f \approx 1.0$ g/cm³ (for fresh water mud filtrate):

**Step 2:** Substitute:

$$\phi = \dfrac{2.65 - 2.35}{2.65 - 1.00} = \dfrac{0.30}{1.65}$$

**Step 3:** Final value:

$$\phi = 0.1818 \approx 18.2\%$$

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Sandstone vs. Carbonate Reservoirs

| Property | Sandstone (Clastic) | Carbonate (Limestone/Dolomite) |
|----------|---------------------|--------------------------------|
| **Typical Porosity** | 15–30% | 5–25% |
| **Typical Permeability** | 10–1000 md | 1–500 md |
| **Porosity Origin** | Primarily primary (intergranular) | Often secondary (vugs, fractures) |
| **Pore Geometry** | Relatively uniform, predictable | Highly heterogeneous, complex |
| **Compaction Effect** | Gradual reduction with depth | Less affected by compaction |
| **Diagenesis Impact** | Cementation reduces porosity | Dissolution & dolomitization create porosity |
| **Worldwide Reserves** | ~60% of conventional oil | ~40% of conventional oil (Middle East dominant) |
| **Examples** | Ghawar (Saudi), North Sea Brent | Asmari (Iran), Arab-D (Saudi) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Define reservoir rock. List the essential properties a rock must possess to be classified as a hydrocarbon reservoir rock.

**Model Answer:**

A reservoir rock is a subsurface geological formation capable of storing and transmitting hydrocarbons (oil and natural gas).

**Two essential properties:**
1. **Porosity** — Must have sufficient pore space to store economically viable quantities of hydrocarbons.
2. **Permeability** — Must have interconnected pore network to allow hydrocarbons to flow toward the wellbore during production.

Additional desirable properties include adequate thickness, lateral continuity, and proper trapping geometry (sealed by an impermeable cap rock).

*(3 marks — Definition 1 mark, Two properties 1 mark each)*

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** Distinguish between **absolute permeability**, **effective permeability**, and **relative permeability**.

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Definition |
|----------|------------|
| **Absolute Permeability ($k$)** | The permeability of a rock to a single fluid when the rock is 100% saturated with that fluid. |
| **Effective Permeability ($k_o, k_w, k_g$)** | The permeability of a rock to one fluid when multiple fluids are present in the pore space. Always less than absolute permeability. |
| **Relative Permeability ($k_r$)** | The ratio of effective permeability to absolute permeability. Dimensionless, ranges from 0 to 1. Example: $k_{ro} = k_o / k$. |

*(3 marks — Three definitions 1 mark each)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]** 

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **classification of reservoir rocks** into clastic and non-clastic types. Give two examples of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **porosity** and **permeability**. A cylindrical core sample has length 12 cm, diameter 3 cm. During a flow test using oil of viscosity 3 cp, a flow rate of 6 cm³/s is observed when the pressure drop is 2 atm. Calculate the permeability of the rock in Darcy and millidarcy. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Classification of Reservoir Rocks:**

**1. Clastic (Siliciclastic) Reservoir Rocks:**
Formed from mechanical weathering and deposition of pre-existing rock fragments.
- **Sandstone:** Most common clastic reservoir; composed of quartz grains cemented together. Examples: Ghawar Field (Saudi Arabia), North Sea Brent Field.
- **Conglomerate:** Coarse-grained sedimentary rock with rounded gravel-sized clasts. Example: Some fields in California.

**2. Non-Clastic (Carbonate) Reservoir Rocks:**
Formed by chemical/biochemical precipitation or accumulation of carbonate minerals.
- **Limestone:** Composed primarily of calcite (CaCO₃). Example: Arab-D reservoir (Saudi Arabia).
- **Dolomite:** Composed of calcium-magnesium carbonate. Formed by replacement of limestone. Example: Smackover Formation (USA).

**3. Unconventional Reservoir Rocks:**
- Shale Gas, Tight Gas, Coal Bed Methane.

*(7 marks — Clastic 3 marks, Non-clastic 3 marks, Diagram 1 mark)*

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Step 1:** Given values
- Length $L = 12$ cm
- Diameter $d = 3$ cm, so radius $r = 1.5$ cm
- Viscosity $\mu = 3$ cp
- Flow rate $Q = 6$ cm³/s
- Pressure drop $\Delta P = 2$ atm

**Step 2:** Calculate cross-sectional area

$$A = \pi r^2 = \pi \times (1.5)^2 = \pi \times 2.25 = 7.0686 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Step 3:** Apply Darcy's Law

$$k = \dfrac{Q \mu L}{A \Delta P}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute values

$$k = \dfrac{6 \times 3 \times 12}{7.0686 \times 2} = \dfrac{216}{14.137}$$

**Step 5:** Calculate

$$k = 15.28 \text{ Darcy} = 15280 \text{ md}$$

**Conclusion:** This permeability value indicates an excellent quality reservoir rock.

*(7 marks — Definitions 2 marks, Calculation 5 marks)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**

**(a)** Explain **primary and secondary porosity** with suitable examples. Discuss how secondary porosity enhances hydrocarbon production in carbonate reservoirs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive Darcy's Law and discuss the significance of each term. A core sample of length 8 cm and cross-sectional area 10 cm² is tested. Oil of viscosity 4 cp flows at 5 cm³/s under a pressure differential of 2 atm. Calculate the absolute permeability in Darcy. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) Solution:**

**Primary Porosity:**
- Formed during the original deposition and lithification of the rock.
- Preexists at the time of sediment deposition.
- Examples: Intergranular pores in sandstone, intragranular pores in oolitic limestone.

**Secondary Porosity:**
- Formed after deposition through post-depositional geological processes.
- Examples: Fractures (from tectonic stress), vugs (from dissolution), dolomitization (in carbonates).

**Enhancement in Carbonates:**
- Carbonate rocks are highly susceptible to dissolution by acidic formation waters, creating vugs and caverns.
- Dolomitization (replacement of Ca²⁺ by Mg²⁺) reduces rock volume by 13%, creating intercrystalline porosity.
- These secondary pores can dramatically increase both storage and flow capacity, making tight limestones productive.

*(7 marks — Primary 2 marks, Secondary 2 marks, Carbonate enhancement 3 marks)*

**Part (b) Solution:**

**Derivation of Darcy's Law:**

Darcy experimentally established that the volumetric flow rate of a homogeneous fluid through a porous medium satisfies:

$$Q = -\dfrac{k A}{\mu} \cdot \dfrac{dP}{dx}$$

For a linear system with constant pressure gradient:

$$Q = \dfrac{k A \Delta P}{\mu L}$$

**Significance of terms:**
- $Q$ — Volumetric flow rate (cm³/s)
- $k$ — Absolute permeability (Darcy) — measure of rock's flow capacity
- $A$ — Cross-sectional area perpendicular to flow (cm²)
- $\Delta P$ — Pressure drop across length (atm)
- $\mu$ — Fluid viscosity (cp) — resistance to flow
- $L$ — Length over which flow occurs (cm)

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $L = 8$ cm, $A = 10$ cm², $\mu = 4$ cp, $Q = 5$ cm³/s, $\Delta P = 2$ atm

$$k = \dfrac{Q \mu L}{A \Delta P} = \dfrac{5 \times 4 \times 8}{10 \times 2} = \dfrac{160}{20} = 8 \text{ Darcy}$$

$$k = 8 \text{ Darcy} = 8000 \text{ md}$$

*(7 marks — Derivation 3 marks, Significance 1 mark, Calculation 3 marks)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder:** Forgetting to convert Darcy to millidarcy (multiply by 1000) or mixing up cm and ft units. **Always state the unit clearly** in your final answer.
>
> 2. **Area Calculation Error:** Many students use $A = \pi d$ instead of $A = \pi r^2$ or $A = \pi d^2 / 4$ for a cylindrical core. **Verify diameter vs radius** before calculation.
>
> 3. **Confusing Total and Effective Porosity:** Examiners specifically test this. **Total porosity** = all void space; **Effective porosity** = interconnected void space (the engineering-relevant value).
>
> 4. **Mixing up Absolute and Relative Permeability:** Absolute permeability is in Darcy; Relative permeability is dimensionless (fraction).
>
> 5. **Skipping Diagram Labeling:** In Part B questions, drawing a diagram without proper labels (axes, units, components) results in **deduction of 1–2 marks**.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to State the Core Sample Assumption:** Always state "Assuming Darcy flow (laminar, single-phase)" before applying the formula.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Reservoir Rocks**

- **Definition:** Reservoir rock = porous + permeable subsurface rock storing hydrocarbons.

- **Two Critical Properties:** Porosity ($\phi$) and Permeability ($k$) — both are **NON-NEGOTIABLE** for a productive reservoir.

- **Porosity Formula:** $\phi = V_p / V_b$ (fraction); quality scale: **<5% poor, 5–10% fair, 10–20% good, >20% excellent**.

- **Darcy's Law (Linear):** $Q = (k A \Delta P) / (\mu L)$ — **memorize this equation and all its variables**.

- **Unit Conversion:** **1 Darcy = 1000 millidarcy (md)**. 1 Darcy is defined by the Darcy experiment conditions.

- **Porosity Types:** **Primary** (depositional, intergranular) vs **Secondary** (post-depositional, dissolution/fractures/dolomitization). **Total** vs **Effective** porosity.

- **Permeability Types:** **Absolute** (single fluid) → **Effective** (specific fluid in multiphase) → **Relative** (effective/absolute, dimensionless, 0 to 1).

- **Saturation Constraint:** $S_w + S_o + S_g = 1$ — saturations must sum to unity.

- **Two Main Rock Classes:**
  - **Clastic (Siliciclastic):** Sandstone, Conglomerate — primary porosity dominant
  - **Carbonate (Non-Clastic):** Limestone, Dolomite — secondary porosity dominant
  - Carbonates hold ~40% of world oil, often in the Middle East

- **Common Exam Formula Substitutions:** Bulk volume $V_b = \pi r^2 h$ (cylinder); Cross-sectional area $A = \pi d^2/4$.

- **Key Threshold Values:** Good reservoir: $\phi > 10\%$, $k > 10$ md.

- **Modern Context:** Unconventional reservoirs (Shale Gas, Tight Gas, CBM) have **nano-Darcy** permeability requiring hydraulic fracturing.

- **Practical Tip:** Always mention the assumption "**Darcy flow conditions (laminar, single-phase, isothermal, incompressible rock**)" before applying Darcy's Law in exam answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cap Rocks: Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT528 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Cap Rocks: Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cap Rock (Seal Rock):** A cap rock is an impermeable, low-permeability rock stratum that overlies a porous and permeable reservoir rock, preventing the upward migration (escape) of hydrocarbons (oil and natural gas) and trapping them within the reservoir. It is one of the four essential elements of a petroleum trap, alongside the **source rock**, **reservoir rock**, and **trap geometry**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a cap rock is classified under the **sealing mechanism** of a hydrocarbon accumulation system. It is a hydrodynamically and lithologically tight formation with very high **capillary entry pressure (displacement pressure)** that exceeds the buoyancy pressure of the underlying hydrocarbon column.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Properties of an Effective Cap Rock:**
> - **Permeability:** Extremely low (typically $< 10^{-6}$ to $10^{-9}$ Darcy or millidarcies)
> - **Porosity:** Very low (often $< 5\%$)
> - **Capillary Entry Pressure:** High (must exceed buoyancy pressure of hydrocarbon column)
> - **Lithology:** Shale, mudstone, salt (halite/anhydrite), tight carbonate, or cemented sandstone
> - **Continuity:** Laterally extensive without major fractures or faults breaching it

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Explanation

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Soda Bottle Cap:**
> Imagine a fizzy soda bottle. The pressurized carbon dioxide gas dissolved in the liquid cannot escape because of the tight, impermeable plastic screw cap on top. If you remove the cap (or if it is loose/leaky), all the gas escapes, and the soda goes flat. In petroleum geology, the **reservoir rock** is the soda, the **hydrocarbons (oil/gas)** are the pressurized fizz, and the **cap rock** is the bottle cap. Without the cap, the hydrocarbons would simply rise due to buoyancy and escape to the surface.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard sealing capacity metric:** Capillary entry pressure in **psi (pounds per square inch)** or **MPa (megapascals)**.
- **Typical cap rock permeability threshold:** $< 0.001$ **mD (millidarcy)**.
- **Hydrocarbon density reference (for buoyancy calculations):**
  - **Crude oil:** $750$ to $950$ **$\text{kg/m}^3$**
  - **Natural gas:** $0.7$ to $300$ **$\text{kg/m}^3$** (at reservoir conditions)
  - **Formation water (brine):** $1000$ to $1200$ **$\text{kg/m}^3$**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Logic: Why Cap Rocks Trap Hydrocarbons

The trapping mechanism of a cap rock is governed by three fundamental physical principles:

1. **Buoyancy Force (Upward Migration Force):**
   Hydrocarbons are less dense than the formation water that saturates the porous rock. Therefore, oil and gas naturally tend to migrate **upward** through permeable strata under the influence of gravity-driven buoyancy.

2. **Capillary Resistance (Sealing Force):**
   At the pore throat of the cap rock, a meniscus forms between the hydrocarbon phase and the water phase. The cap rock's tiny pore throats generate an enormous **capillary pressure** that opposes the entry of hydrocarbons.

3. **Equilibrium Condition (Sealing Criterion):**
   A cap rock seals effectively when:
   $$P_{\text{capillary entry}} \geq P_{\text{buoyancy}}$$

   If the buoyancy pressure of the hydrocarbon column exceeds the capillary entry pressure of the cap rock, hydrocarbons leak through (a phenomenon called **leak-off** or **cap rock failure**).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Buoyancy Pressure of Hydrocarbon Column | $P_b = (\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g \cdot h$ | Driving force pushing hydrocarbons upward | **Pa (Pascal)** or **psi** |
| Capillary Entry Pressure (Young-Laplace) | $P_c = \dfrac{2 \cdot \gamma \cdot \cos\theta}{r}$ | Resistance force in cap rock pores | **Pa (Pascal)** or **psi** |
| Column Height of Sealed Hydrocarbon | $h_{\max} = \dfrac{P_{c,\text{entry}}}{(\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g}$ | Maximum hydrocarbon column a cap rock can hold | **m (meters)** or **ft (feet)** |
| Cap Rock Permeability Threshold | $k < 0.001$ **mD** | Effective seal permeability | **millidarcy (mD)** |
| Sealing Efficiency Ratio | $SER = \dfrac{P_{c,\text{entry}}}{P_b}$ | Must be $\geq 1$ for seal | Dimensionless |
| Wetting Condition | $\theta > 90^\circ$ (water-wet) | Required for hydrocarbon trapping | Degrees ($^\circ$) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Table Symbols:** In the formula column, the expression $\rho_w - \rho_{hc}$ denotes the **density difference** between formation water and hydrocarbons (do not interpret the hyphen as a negative sign on a single variable). The Greek symbol $\theta$ is the **contact angle**, $\gamma$ is the **interfacial tension**, $r$ is the **pore throat radius**, and $g$ is **gravitational acceleration ($9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$)**.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Exploration Geophysics:** Seismic interpreters identify cap rock continuity to delineate drilling prospects.
- **Reserve Estimation:** The maximum sealed column height directly determines the **in-place hydrocarbon volume**.
- **Drilling Safety:** Understanding cap rock strength and pore pressure is critical for **wellbore stability** and preventing **blowouts**.
- **CO₂ Sequestration & Gas Storage:** Cap rock integrity is the primary containment assurance in **CCUS (Carbon Capture, Utilization, and Storage)** projects and **underground natural gas storage** facilities.
- **Geothermal Energy:** Cap rocks insulate heat in deep geothermal reservoirs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Sealable Hydrocarbon Column Height

The relationship between cap rock capillary entry pressure and hydrocarbon column height is the cornerstone of seal analysis. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step derivation.

**Step 1: Define the Buoyancy Pressure**

The buoyancy pressure is the hydrostatic pressure difference between a column of formation water and a column of hydrocarbons of the same vertical height $h$. Mathematically:

$$P_b = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot h - \rho_{hc} \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 2: Factor Out Common Terms**

Since both terms share the common factor of $g \cdot h$, we factor them out:

$$P_b = (\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g \cdot h$$

> *Conversion Logic:* This is the classical Archimedean buoyancy expression. The denser water column exerts a higher pressure at depth than the lighter hydrocarbon column. The differential pressure $P_b$ is what physically pushes hydrocarbons upward.

**Step 3: Define the Capillary Entry Pressure (Young-Laplace Equation)**

For a cylindrical pore throat of radius $r$, with interfacial tension $\gamma$ and contact angle $\theta$ (measured through the wetting phase, i.e., water):

$$P_c = \frac{2 \cdot \gamma \cdot \cos\theta}{r}$$

> *Conversion Logic:* The Young-Laplace equation describes the pressure difference across a curved fluid interface. In a water-wet cap rock, hydrocarbon is the non-wetting phase and must overcome this capillary pressure to invade the pore.

**Step 4: Apply the Sealing Criterion**

For the cap rock to seal the hydrocarbon column, the capillary entry pressure must be greater than or equal to the buoyancy pressure. At the limiting equilibrium:

$$P_c = P_b$$

**Step 5: Substitute Both Expressions**

$$\frac{2 \cdot \gamma \cdot \cos\theta}{r} = (\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 6: Solve for the Hydrocarbon Column Height $h$**

Rearranging the equation to isolate $h$ on one side:

$$h = \frac{2 \cdot \gamma \cdot \cos\theta}{r \cdot (\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g}$$

**Step 7: Final Compact Form**

$$\boxed{\,h_{\max} = \frac{P_{c,\text{entry}}}{(\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g}\,}$$

> *Conversion Logic:* This compact boxed form is the most important equation in cap rock analysis. It shows that the maximum hydrocarbon column height is directly proportional to the cap rock's capillary entry pressure and inversely proportional to the density contrast and gravitational acceleration.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A cap rock has an entry pressure of **$2.0 \text{ MPa}$**. The formation water density is **$1100 \text{ kg/m}^3$** and the crude oil density is **$850 \text{ kg/m}^3$**. Calculate the maximum oil column height the cap rock can seal. (Use $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$).

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify and convert units consistently.

$$P_{c,\text{entry}} = 2.0 \text{ MPa} = 2.0 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\Delta \rho = \rho_w - \rho_{hc} = 1100 - 850 = 250 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 2:** Substitute into the column height formula.

$$h_{\max} = \frac{P_{c,\text{entry}}}{(\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the denominator.

$$(\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g = 250 \times 9.81 = 2452.5 \text{ Pa/m}$$

**Step 4:** Compute the final answer.

$$h_{\max} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^6}{2452.5} \approx 815.5 \text{ m}$$

> *Conversion Logic:* Therefore, this cap rock can hold a maximum oil column of approximately **$815.5 \text{ meters}$** before capillary leakage occurs. Any oil column greater than this will breach the seal.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram: Petroleum Trap System

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the interaction between cap rock, reservoir rock, source rock, and hydrocarbon migration pathways.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["SURFACE ENVIRONMENT"] --> B["IMPERMEABLE CAP ROCK SHALE / SALT"]
    B --> C["RESERVOIR ROCK POROUS SANDSTONE / CARBONATE"]
    C --> D["SOURCE ROCK ORGANIC RICH SHALE"]
    D --> E["MIGRATION PATHWAY FAULT / CARRIER BED"]
    E --> C
    C --> F["HYDROCARBON ACCUMULATION OIL AND GAS POOL"]
    B --> G["SEALING FUNCTION HIGH CAPILLARY ENTRY PRESSURE"]
    G --> F
    F --> H["PREVENTED UPWARD ESCAPE BY CAP ROCK"]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style C fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style D fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style E fill:#f8bbd0,stroke:#880e4f
    style F fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100
    style G fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92
    style H fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#004d40
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Cap Rock Sealing Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FORC["FORCES ACTING ON CAP ROCK INTERFACE"]
        F1["BUOYANCY FORCE PUSHING HYDROCARBONS UPWARD"]
        F2["CAPILLARY ENTRY PRESSURE RESISTING HYDROCARBON ENTRY"]
    end

    subgraph COND["EQUILIBRIUM CONDITION"]
        C1["IF BUOYANCY LESS THAN CAPILLARY RESISTANCE: SEAL HOLDS"]
        C2["IF BUOYANCY EXCEEDS CAPILLARY RESISTANCE: LEAKAGE OCCURS"]
    end

    subgraph PROP["CAP ROCK KEY PROPERTIES"]
        P1["LOW PERMEABILITY UNDER 0.001 MILLIDARCY"]
        P2["HIGH CAPILLARY ENTRY PRESSURE OVER 1 MEGAPASCAL"]
        P3["LATERAL CONTINUITY WITHOUT BREACHING FAULTS"]
    end

    F1 --> COND
    F2 --> COND
    PROP --> F2
    C1 --> OUT["EFFECTIVE HYDROCARBON TRAP"]
    C2 --> LEAK["CAP ROCK FAILURE AND HYDROCARBON LOSS"]

    style F1 fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style F2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style C1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style C2 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
    style P1 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style P2 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style P3 fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style OUT fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style LEAK fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define a cap rock and list any two essential properties it must possess to be an effective hydrocarbon seal.*

**Model Answer:**

A cap rock is an impermeable rock layer that overlies a porous and permeable reservoir rock, preventing the upward migration of hydrocarbons and sealing them within the trap.

> Two essential properties of an effective cap rock are:
> 1. **Very low permeability** (typically $< 0.001$ mD) to prevent fluid flow through its pores.
> 2. **High capillary entry pressure** (must exceed the buoyancy pressure of the hydrocarbon column).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the physical principle that governs the sealing capacity of a cap rock.*

**Model Answer:**

> The sealing capacity of a cap rock is governed by the **capillary pressure equilibrium principle**. A cap rock seals hydrocarbons as long as its **capillary entry (displacement) pressure is greater than or equal to the buoyancy pressure** of the hydrocarbon column. If buoyancy exceeds the capillary resistance, hydrocarbons leak through the seal.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students incorrectly write that cap rocks are "impervious." The technically correct term is "low permeability with high capillary entry pressure" — total imperviousness is impossible in nature.
> - Do **not** confuse **cap rock (seal)** with **source rock**. Cap rocks store nothing; they merely block migration. Source rocks *generate* hydrocarbons.
> - Always state units in numerical problems. KTU examiners deduct **1 mark** for missing units.
> - In derivation questions, students often skip the "equating forces" step ($P_c = P_b$). This is a critical **3-mark** step.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) – Option 1**

**Q3. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the role of cap rock in a typical petroleum trap. List the common lithologies that act as effective cap rocks. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive an expression for the maximum hydrocarbon column height that a cap rock can seal. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Role of Cap Rock in a Petroleum Trap:**

A cap rock sits at the top of a petroleum trap and acts as a **hydrocarbon seal**. It prevents the buoyant rise of oil and gas that have migrated into the underlying reservoir rock. The cap rock must be laterally continuous, of low permeability, and possess high capillary entry pressure.

> **Common cap rock lithologies include:**
> - **Shale and mudstone** (most common in clastic basins)
> - **Evaporites** such as halite (rock salt) and anhydrite
> - **Tight (cemented) carbonate mudstones**
> - **Siliceous shales and cherts**

**Sketch (Block Diagram):**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["SURFACE"] --> B["CAP ROCK SHALE LAYER"]
    B --> C["RESERVOIR ROCK SANDSTONE WITH OIL AND GAS"]
    C --> D["UNDERLYING FORMATION"]

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd
    style B fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style C fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32
    style D fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#616161
```

**(b) Derivation of Maximum Hydrocarbon Column Height:**

**Step 1:** Buoyancy pressure of a hydrocarbon column of height $h$ is:
$$P_b = (\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g \cdot h$$

**Step 2:** Capillary entry pressure of the cap rock (from the Young-Laplace equation):
$$P_c = \frac{2 \cdot \gamma \cdot \cos\theta}{r}$$

**Step 3:** For the cap rock to be effective, the sealing criterion must be satisfied:
$$P_c \geq P_b$$

**Step 4:** At the limiting (maximum) column height, the two are equal:
$$\frac{2 \cdot \gamma \cdot \cos\theta}{r} = (\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g \cdot h_{\max}$$

**Step 5:** Rearranging for $h_{\max}$:
$$h_{\max} = \frac{2 \cdot \gamma \cdot \cos\theta}{r \cdot (\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g}$$

> This equation shows that a higher capillary entry pressure and a smaller density contrast both increase the maximum sealable column height.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) – Option 2**

**Q4. [KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the capillary pressure mechanism by which a cap rock seals hydrocarbons. Define "capillary entry pressure" and "displacement pressure" in this context. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A cap rock has a measured capillary entry pressure of **$3.5 \text{ MPa}$**. The formation water density is **$1150 \text{ kg/m}^3$** and the gas density is **$150 \text{ kg/m}^3$**. Calculate the maximum gas column height this cap rock can seal. Take $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Capillary Pressure Mechanism:**

At the interface between the reservoir rock and the overlying cap rock, the hydrocarbon (non-wetting phase) must pass through the tiny pore throats of the seal. Because the cap rock is water-wet, a curved meniscus forms at each pore throat. The pressure difference required to push this meniscus through the throat is the **capillary entry pressure** (also called **displacement pressure**).

> - **Capillary Entry Pressure:** The minimum pressure the non-wetting phase (hydrocarbon) must exceed to invade the largest connected pore throat of the seal.
