> - **Displacement Pressure:** Synonymous term for capillary entry pressure, denoting the pressure at which the non-wetting phase displaces the wetting phase from the pore.

As long as the buoyancy pressure of the hydrocarbon column remains below this threshold, the meniscus holds and the seal is effective.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $P_{c,\text{entry}} = 3.5 \text{ MPa} = 3.5 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$
- $\rho_w = 1150 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $\rho_{gas} = 150 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$

**Step 1:** Compute the density contrast.
$$\Delta \rho = 1150 - 150 = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 2:** Apply the column height formula.
$$h_{\max} = \frac{P_{c,\text{entry}}}{(\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate the denominator.
$$(\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g = 1000 \times 9.81 = 9810 \text{ Pa/m}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate the maximum gas column.
$$h_{\max} = \frac{3.5 \times 10^6}{9810} \approx 356.8 \text{ m}$$

> **Result:** The cap rock can seal a maximum gas column of approximately **$356.8$ meters** under the given conditions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Checklist for Cap Rock: Definition**

- **Definition:** Cap rock = impermeable seal above a reservoir that prevents hydrocarbon escape.
- **Four elements of a trap:** Source rock, reservoir rock, **cap rock (seal)**, and trap geometry — all four are required.
- **Common lithologies:** Shale, mudstone, halite (rock salt), anhydrite, tight carbonate.
- **Key properties:** Low permeability ($< 0.001$ mD), high capillary entry pressure, lateral continuity, no breaching faults.
- **Sealing criterion:** $P_{c,\text{entry}} \geq P_b$ (capillary pressure must exceed buoyancy pressure).
- **Maximum column height formula:** $h_{\max} = \dfrac{P_{c,\text{entry}}}{(\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g}$
- **Young-Laplace equation:** $P_c = \dfrac{2 \cdot \gamma \cdot \cos\theta}{r}$ — describes the capillary resistance in the cap rock.
- **Buoyancy equation:** $P_b = (\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g \cdot h$ — drives hydrocarbons upward.
- **Exam keywords to use:** "capillary entry pressure," "displacement pressure," "wetting phase," "buoyancy," "leak-off," "trap integrity."
- **Real-world relevance:** CCUS, underground gas storage, geothermal insulation, drilling safety, reserve estimation.
- **Pitfall to avoid:** Cap rocks are not "completely impervious" — they have measurable (but very low) permeability and act as capillary seals, not absolute barriers.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT528 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Characteristics of Petroleum Fluids** refer to the **physical, chemical, thermal, and thermodynamic properties** that uniquely define the behavior of crude oil, natural gas, and associated reservoir fluids. These properties govern storage, transportation, processing, refining, and end-use applications across the entire petroleum value chain.

In the context of **PEPOT528 – Oil and Natural Gas Engineering**, Module 1 establishes the foundational vocabulary of petroleum characterization. The topic "Characteristics" broadly encompasses:

- **Crude Oil Characteristics** — physical state, density, API gravity, viscosity, pour point, flash point, sulfur content, wax content, etc.
- **Natural Gas Characteristics** — composition, specific gravity, heating value, Wobbe Index, compressibility, dew point, etc.
- **Reservoir Fluid Classification** — black oil, volatile oil, retrograde gas, wet gas, dry gas.
- **Compositional Characteristics** — paraffins, naphthenes, aromatics, asphaltenes, and non-hydrocarbon impurities (H₂S, CO₂, N₂).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of petroleum fluids like blood types in medicine.** Just as doctors classify human blood as A, B, AB, or O based on specific markers, petroleum engineers classify crude oil as light, medium, or heavy — and natural gas as dry, wet, or sour — based on measurable characteristics. These classifications determine *everything* downstream: how the fluid is transported (pipeline vs. tanker), how it is processed (simple stabilization vs. complex refining), and what products it can yield.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Standard Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Standard Temperature** | **15.56 °C (60 °F)** | Reference for API gravity & specific gravity |
| **Standard Pressure** | **101.325 kPa (14.696 psia)** | Volumetric measurement baseline |
| **Density of Fresh Water** | **1000 kg/m³** (at 4 °C) | API gravity reference |
| **Universal Gas Constant (R)** | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** | Gas law calculations |
| **API Gravity of Water** | **10 °API** | Anchor for API scale |
| **Air Molecular Weight** | **28.97 g/mol** | Gas specific gravity reference |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Crude Oil — Physical Characteristics

#### 2.1.1 Specific Gravity (SG)
The **specific gravity** of crude oil is the ratio of oil density to the density of fresh water at standard conditions. It is **dimensionless** and temperature-dependent.

#### 2.1.2 API Gravity (°API)
The American Petroleum Institute (API) gravity is the industry-standard measure of how heavy or light a petroleum liquid is compared to water.

- **°API > 31.1** → Light crude oil
- **°API 22.3 – 31.1** → Medium crude oil
- **°API < 22.3** → Heavy crude oil
- **°API < 10** → Extra-heavy crude oil (e.g., Athabasca oil sands)

#### 2.1.3 Viscosity
Resistance to flow. Measured in **centipoise (cP)** or **Pa·s**. **1 cP = 10⁻³ Pa·s**. Lower viscosity = easier flow through pipelines.

#### 2.1.4 Pour Point
**Lowest temperature** at which the oil will flow under gravity. Critical for cold-region pipeline design.

#### 2.1.5 Flash Point & Fire Point
- **Flash Point**: Lowest temperature at which oil vapors ignite momentarily.
- **Fire Point**: Temperature at which vapors sustain combustion for ≥5 seconds.

#### 2.1.6 Sulfur Content
- **Sweet crude**: < 0.5 % sulfur by mass
- **Sour crude**: > 0.5 % sulfur by mass

#### 2.1.7 Wax (Paraffin) Content
Long-chain n-alkanes (C₁₈–C₆₀+) that crystallize at low temperatures, causing flow assurance problems.

### 2.2 Crude Oil — Chemical Characteristics

| Fraction | Carbon Range | Boiling Range | Major Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **LPG (Liquefied Petroleum Gas)** | C₃ – C₄ | –42 °C to –1 °C | Domestic fuel, auto fuel |
| **Naphtha** | C₅ – C₁₁ | 30 °C – 200 °C | Petrochemical feedstock, gasoline |
| **Kerosene / Jet Fuel** | C₁₂ – C₁₅ | 150 °C – 250 °C | Aviation turbine fuel |
| **Diesel / Gas Oil** | C₁₅ – C₂₅ | 250 °C – 350 °C | Transportation fuel |
| **Lubricating Oil** | C₂₅ – C₄₀ | 350 °C – 500 °C | Machinery lubrication |
| **Residue / Bitumen** | C₄₀+ | > 500 °C | Road construction, roofing |

### 2.3 Natural Gas — Physical Characteristics

#### 2.3.1 Specific Gravity of Gas (γ_g)
Ratio of gas molecular weight to air molecular weight.

- **γ_g < 0.6** → Light / Dry gas
- **0.6 < γ_g < 0.8** → Typical pipeline-quality gas
- **γ_g > 0.8** → Heavy / Rich gas

#### 2.3.2 Heating Value
- **HHV (Higher Heating Value)** — includes latent heat of water vapor condensation.
- **LHV (Lower Heating Value)** — excludes latent heat (water leaves as vapor).

#### 2.3.3 Wobbe Index (WI)
Indicates interchangeability of fuel gases. Critical for burner design and gas turbine operation.

#### 2.3.4 Compressibility Factor (Z)
Real-gas deviation factor. **Z = 1** for ideal gas; Z deviates at high pressure/low temperature.

### 2.4 Reservoir Fluid Classification (McCain)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **McCain's fluid classification** is based on initial producing gas-oil ratio (GORₖ) and API gravity:

| Fluid Type | GOR (scf/STB) | API Gravity | Stock Tank Color |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Black Oil** | ≤ 2000 | < 45 | Very dark |
| **Volatile Oil** | 2000 – 3300 | > 40 | Brown to dark green |
| **Retrograde Gas** | > 3300 | 40 – 60 (condensate) | Straw to light green |
| **Wet Gas** | > 50,000 (essentially gas with condensate at surface) | — | — |
| **Dry Gas** | ∞ (no surface liquid) | — | — |

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is a Board Exam–ready cheat sheet.** All formulas are mathematically verified and align with the **KTU 2024 Scheme** PEPOT528 syllabus.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | API Gravity | $°API = \dfrac{141.5}{SG_{oil}} - 131.5$ | °API | $SG_{oil}$ at 60 °F |
| 2 | Specific Gravity (oil) | $SG_{oil} = \dfrac{\rho_{oil}}{\rho_{water}}$ | dimensionless | $\rho_{water} = 1000\ kg/m^3$ |
| 3 | Gas Specific Gravity | $\gamma_g = \dfrac{M_{gas}}{M_{air}} = \dfrac{M_{gas}}{28.97}$ | dimensionless | — |
| 4 | Ideal Gas Law | $PV = nRT$ | SI: Pa, m³, K | $R = 8.314\ J/(mol \cdot K)$ |
| 5 | Real Gas Law | $PV = ZnRT$ | — | Z from Standing-Katz chart |
| 6 | Standard Volume | $V_{sc} = \dfrac{ZnRT_{sc}}{P_{sc}}$ | m³ | $T_{sc} = 288.7\ K$, $P_{sc} = 101.325\ kPa$ |
| 7 | Wobbe Index | $WI = \dfrac{HHV}{\sqrt{\gamma_g}}$ | — | Indicates gas interchangeability |
| 8 | Gas Density | $\rho_g = \dfrac{PM_g}{ZRT}$ | kg/m³ | At reservoir P, T |
| 9 | Formation Volume Factor (Oil) | $B_o = \dfrac{V_{reservoir}}{V_{stock\ tank}}$ | bbl/STB or RB/STB | > 1 due to dissolved gas |
| 10 | Solution Gas-Oil Ratio | $R_s = \dfrac{V_{gas\ dissolved}}{V_{stock\ tank\ oil}}$ | scf/STB | Pressure-dependent |
| 11 | Viscosity Conversion | $1\ cP = 10^{-3}\ Pa \cdot s$ | SI | Common in petroleum units |
| 12 | Pour Point Depression | $\Delta T = k \cdot C_{wax}$ | °C | $k$ is oil-specific constant |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refinery Design**: API gravity determines the distillation column profile and product slate.
- **Pipeline Hydraulics**: Viscosity governs pressure drop calculations and pumping requirements.
- **Reservoir Simulation**: $B_o$, $R_s$, and Z-factor feed into material balance equations.
- **Gas Marketing & Pricing**: Heating value and Wobbe Index determine commercial acceptability.
- **Corrosion & Safety**: H₂S content (sour gas) dictates metallurgy (carbon steel vs. CRA), PPE, and shutdown systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: API Gravity from Specific Gravity

Starting from the definition of API gravity, the **141.5** constant arises from calibrating the API scale so that water has **°API = 10** and most oils fall in a convenient positive range.

**Step 1: Define the relationship empirically.**

$$°API = \frac{141.5}{SG_{oil}} - 131.5$$

**Step 2: Verify with water (SG = 1.0).**

$$°API = \frac{141.5}{1.0} - 131.5 = 141.5 - 131.5 = 10\ °API$$

**Step 3: Verify dimensional consistency.**

The constant **131.5** is chosen such that the formula is **dimensionless in spirit** — it works with SG (dimensionless) and produces a numerical scale.

**Step 4: Solve for SG from API.**

$$SG_{oil} = \frac{141.5}{°API + 131.5}$$

**Step 5: Numerical example.**

A crude oil has $°API = 35$. Find its specific gravity.

$$SG_{oil} = \frac{141.5}{35 + 131.5} = \frac{141.5}{166.5} = 0.850$$

This indicates **light crude oil** (floats on water, as $SG < 1$).

### 3.2 Derivation: Gas Density at Reservoir Conditions

**Step 1: Begin with the Real Gas Law.**

$$PV = ZnRT$$

**Step 2: Express n in terms of mass and molecular weight.**

$$n = \frac{m}{M_g}$$

**Step 3: Substitute into the gas law.**

$$PV = \frac{Zm}{M_g}RT$$

**Step 4: Rearrange for mass per unit volume (density).**

$$\rho_g = \frac{m}{V} = \frac{PM_g}{ZRT}$$

**Step 5: Insert reservoir conditions.**

For a gas at $P = 30\ MPa$, $T = 350\ K$, $M_g = 20\ g/mol = 0.020\ kg/mol$, $Z = 0.85$:

$$\rho_g = \frac{(30 \times 10^6)(0.020)}{(0.85)(8.314)(350)}$$

$$\rho_g = \frac{6.0 \times 10^5}{2473.4} = 242.6\ kg/m^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> This explains why gas reservoirs at **high pressure and moderate temperature** can store enormous gas volumes in surprisingly small rock pore spaces.

### 3.3 Derivation: Wobbe Index

The Wobbe Index quantifies how much heat energy is delivered through a fixed-size orifice at constant pressure.

**Step 1: Heat energy delivered per unit time.**

$$\dot{Q} = C \cdot \frac{HHV}{\sqrt{\gamma_g}}$$

where $C$ is a constant depending on orifice size and supply pressure.

**Step 2: Define the Wobbe Index as the proportionality factor.**

$$WI = \frac{HHV}{\sqrt{\gamma_g}}$$

**Step 3: Numerical example.**

Given: $HHV = 38\ MJ/m^3$, $\gamma_g = 0.65$.

$$WI = \frac{38}{\sqrt{0.65}} = \frac{38}{0.8062} = 47.13\ MJ/m^3$$

Two gases with **similar Wobbe Index** (within ±5%) are **interchangeable** in burners without readjustment.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Crude Oil Classification by Multiple Parameters

| Crude Name | Region | °API | Sulfur (% wt) | Pour Point (°C) | Viscosity (cP) | Classification |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **WTI** (West Texas Intermediate) | USA | 39.6 | 0.24 | –20 | 4 | Light Sweet |
| **Brent Blend** | North Sea | 38.3 | 0.40 | –2 | 7 | Light Sweet |
| **Arabian Light** | Saudi Arabia | 33.0 | 1.77 | –30 | 6 | Medium Sour |
| **Maya** | Mexico | 22.0 | 3.30 | –12 | 130 | Heavy Sour |
| **Athabasca Bitumen** | Canada | 8.0 | 4.20 | +10 | > 100,000 | Extra-Heavy Sour |
| **Mumbai High** | India | 35.0 | 0.15 | –24 | 5 | Light Sweet |
| **Bombay High (Bombay U)** | India (Heera) | 30.0 | 0.35 | –18 | 9 | Medium Sweet |

### 3.5 Derivation: Solution Gas-Oil Ratio and Formation Volume Factor (Standing's Correlations)

**Standing's Correlation for $R_s$:**

$$R_s = \gamma_g \left[ \frac{P}{18.2 + 1.4 P} \right] \cdot 10^{(0.00091 T - 0.0125 °API)}$$

where $P$ is in psia, $T$ in °F, °API is oil gravity.

**Standing's Correlation for $B_o$:**

$$B_o = 0.9759 + 0.00012 \left[ R_s \sqrt{\frac{\gamma_g}{\gamma_o}} + 1.4 T \right]^{1.2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Standing's empirical correlations (1947) remain industry-standard for black oil PVT properties in **KTU examinations** when PVT laboratory data is unavailable.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Petroleum Fluid Characteristics Taxonomy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Petroleum Fluids] --> B[Crude Oil]
    A --> C[Natural Gas]
    A --> D[Condensate]

    B --> B1[Physical Properties]
    B --> B2[Chemical Properties]
    B --> B3[Thermal Properties]

    B1 --> B1a[API Gravity]
    B1 --> B1b[Viscosity]
    B1 --> B1c[Pour Point]
    B1 --> B1d[Density / SG]

    B2 --> B2a[Sulfur Content]
    B2 --> B2b[Wax Content]
    B2 --> B2c[Asphaltene]

    B3 --> B3a[Flash Point]
    B3 --> B3b[Fire Point]
    B3 --> B3c[Heat of Combustion]

    C --> C1[Composition]
    C --> C2[Thermodynamic]
    C --> C3[Combustion]

    C1 --> C1a[Methane C1]
    C1 --> C1b[Ethane C2]
    C1 --> C1c[Propane C3]
    C1 --> C1d[N2 CO2 H2S]

    C2 --> C2a[Z Factor]
    C2 --> C2b[Density]
    C2 --> C3c[Dew Point]

    C3 --> C3a[HHV]
    C3 --> C3b[LHV]
    C3 --> C3c[Wobbe Index]

    D --> D1[Retrograde]
    D --> D2[Lease Condensate]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Crude Oil Classification by API Gravity

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Start([Crude Oil Sample]) --> Lab[Laboratory Analysis]
    Lab --> API{Measure API Gravity}

    API -->|< 10| XH[Extra Heavy Crude]
    API -->|10 to 22.3| H[Heavy Crude]
    API -->|22.3 to 31.1| M[Medium Crude]
    API -->|> 31.1| L[Light Crude]

    XH --> XH1[Example: Athabasca Bitumen]
    XH --> XH2[Requires thermal recovery]

    H --> H1[Example: Maya Mexico]
    H --> H2[Requires dilution for transport]

    M --> M1[Example: Arabian Light]
    M --> M2[Standard pipeline transport]

    L --> L1[Example: WTI Brent]
    L --> L2[High refinery yield to gasoline]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Schematic: Natural Gas Processing Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RawGas[Raw Natural Gas from Wellhead] --> C1{Contains H2S?}
    C1 -->|Yes > 4 ppm| Sour[Sour Gas]
    C1 -->|No| Sweet[Sweet Gas]

    Sour --> S1[Amine Treating Unit]
    Sweet --> P1[Acid Gas Removal if CO2]
    S1 --> Dehydration[TEG Dehydration]
    P1 --> Dehydration

    Dehydration --> Q1{Gas Oil Ratio at Surface?}
    Q1 -->|High Liquid Yield| WetGas[Wet Gas to Fractionation]
    Q1 -->|Negligible Liquid| DryGas[Dry Gas to Pipeline]

    WetGas --> NGL[NGL Recovery Plant]
    DryGas --> Sales[Sales to End User]
    NGL --> Sales

    Sales --> EndUse[Power Generation / Industrial / Domestic Fuel]
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Architecture: Reservoir Fluid PVT Property Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Res[Reservoir Conditions P T] --> Res1[Single Phase Fluid]
    Res1 --> F1[Formation Volume Factor Bo]
    Res1 --> F2[Solution GOR Rs]
    Res1 --> F3[Viscosity mu]
    Res1 --> F4[Compressibility c]

    F1 --> MB[Material Balance Equation]
    F2 --> MB
    F3 --> Flow[Flow Equations Darcy]
    F4 --> MB

    MB --> R1[Oil in Place]
    Flow --> R2[Recovery Factor]
    R1 --> R3[Reserves Estimation]
    R2 --> R3

    style Res fill:#f9c74f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style R3 fill:#90be6d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define API gravity. Classify crude oils based on API gravity.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **API Gravity** is an inverse measure of crude oil density developed by the American Petroleum Institute. The mathematical definition is:
>
> $$°API = \frac{141.5}{SG_{oil}} - 131.5$$
>
> **Classification:**
> - **Light crude**: °API > 31.1 (floats easily, high gasoline yield)
> - **Medium crude**: 22.3 ≤ °API ≤ 31.1
> - **Heavy crude**: °API < 22.3
> - **Extra-heavy**: °API < 10 (e.g., bitumen)
>
> *(3 marks: 1 definition + 1 formula + 1 classification — examiner's expectation)*

#### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between sweet crude and sour crude with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> - **Sweet crude**: Sulfur content **< 0.5% by mass**. Requires less desulfurization. Example: **WTI (West Texas Intermediate)**, °API ~ 39.6, S ~ 0.24%.
> - **Sour crude**: Sulfur content **> 0.5% by mass**. Requires hydrodesulfurization (HDS) and special metallurgy. Example: **Arabian Heavy**, °API ~ 28, S ~ 2.8%.
>
> Sulfur in crude is present as H₂S, mercaptans, sulfides, and thiophenes. It causes corrosion, catalyst poisoning, and SO₂ emissions upon combustion. *(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Module Convention**: Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Students answer **ONE full question** per module (internal choice).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the physical and chemical characteristics of crude oil in detail. Discuss the significance of each property in petroleum processing. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A crude oil sample has a specific gravity of 0.875. Calculate its API gravity and classify the crude. If the sulfur content is 0.8% by mass, determine whether it is sweet or sour. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Physical and Chemical Characteristics of Crude Oil:**

1. **Physical Characteristics:**
   - **Density / Specific Gravity** — Mass per unit volume; determines buoyancy and transport cost.
   - **API Gravity** — Inverse density scale; determines refining economics.
   - **Viscosity** — Resistance to flow; affects pipeline pressure drop.
   - **Pour Point** — Lowest flow temperature; affects cold-region operations.
   - **Flash Point & Fire Point** — Safety parameters for storage and transport.
   - **Color & Odor** — Indicates composition (lighter oils are straw-colored, heavy oils are black).
   - **Wax Content** — High-molecular-weight paraffins; cause flow assurance issues.

2. **Chemical Characteristics:**
   - **Elemental Composition** — C (83–87%), H (10–14%), S (0–6%), N (0–1%), O (0–2%), metals (Ni, V, Fe in trace).
   - **Hydrocarbon Families** — Paraffins (alkanes), Naphthenes (cycloalkanes), Aromatics, Asphaltenes, Resins.
   - **Sulfur Compounds** — H₂S, RSH, RSR, thiophenes.
   - **Nitrogen Compounds** — Pyridines, quinolines.
   - **Metals** — Ni and V in porphyrin structures.

3. **Significance in Processing:**
   - High sulfur → requires HDS unit; impacts refinery configuration.
   - High wax → needs dewaxing unit; affects lube oil yield.
   - High asphaltene → causes fouling in heat exchangers.
   - API gravity → determines the yield slate (light oils favor gasoline; heavy oils favor diesel and residue).

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:** $SG_{oil} = 0.875$

**Step 1: Calculate API Gravity.**

$$°API = \frac{141.5}{SG_{oil}} - 131.5$$

$$°API = \frac{141.5}{0.875} - 131.5$$

$$°API = 161.71 - 131.5 = 30.21\ °API$$

**Step 2: Classify the crude.**

Since $22.3 \leq °API \leq 31.1$, the oil is **Medium crude**.

**Step 3: Determine sweet/sour status.**

Sulfur content = 0.8% > 0.5% threshold.

Therefore, the crude is classified as **Sour crude**.

**Final Answer:** °API = **30.21** → **Medium Sour crude oil**.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Describe the characteristics of natural gas. Explain the Wobbe Index and its engineering significance. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A natural gas sample has the following composition (mole %): CH₄ = 85%, C₂H₆ = 8%, C₃H₈ = 4%, CO₂ = 2%, N₂ = 1%. Calculate the gas specific gravity, molecular weight, and density at 10 MPa and 60 °C. Take Z = 0.88. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Characteristics of Natural Gas & Wobbe Index:**

1. **Physical Characteristics:**
   - **Colorless, odorless** (odorant mercaptan added for leak detection).
   - **Density** lower than air → rises and dissipates (safety advantage in unconfined spaces).
   - **Boiling point** of methane = –161.5 °C → requires cryogenic liquefaction (LNG).
   - **Compressibility** — high; follows real gas law with Z factor.
   - **Heating value** — primary energy metric (HHV, LHV).

2. **Chemical Characteristics:**
   - **Hydrocarbon content** — C₁ (methane dominant), C₂, C₃, C₄, C₅⁺.
   - **Non-hydrocarbon impurities** — H₂S (sour), CO₂ (corrosive, reduces heating value), N₂ (inert diluent), He, H₂O (forms hydrates).

3. **Wobbe Index (WI):**
   - Defined as: $WI = \dfrac{HHV}{\sqrt{\gamma_g}}$
   - **Significance:**
     - Indicates **interchangeability** of fuel gases in burners.
     - Two gases with WI within ±5% can be swapped without burner adjustment.
     - Critical for **LNG imports**, city gas distribution, and **gas turbine** fuel selection.
     - High WI → better flame stability, higher heat release per unit flow.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute Molecular Weight of the mixture.**

$$M_{gas} = \sum y_i M_i$$

| Component | $y_i$ | $M_i$ (g/mol) | $y_i M_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| CH₄ | 0.85 | 16.04 | 13.634 |
| C₂H₆ | 0.08 | 30.07 | 2.406 |
| C₃H₈ | 0.04 | 44.10 | 1.764 |
| CO₂ | 0.02 | 44.01 | 0.880 |
| N₂ | 0.01 | 28.01 | 0.280 |
| **Total** | **1.00** | — | **18.964** |

$$M_{gas} = 18.96\ g/mol = 0.01896\ kg/mol$$

**Step 2: Compute Gas Specific Gravity.**

$$\gamma_g = \frac{M_{gas}}{M_{air}} = \frac{18.96}{28.97} = 0.6545$$

**Step 3: Compute Gas Density at 10 MPa, 60 °C (333.15 K), Z = 0.88.**

$$\rho_g = \frac{PM_g}{ZRT} = \frac{(10 \times 10^6)(0.01896)}{(0.88)(8.314)(333.15)}$$

$$\rho_g = \frac{1.896 \times 10^5}{2437.0} = 77.8\ kg/m^3$$

**Final Answers:**
- $M_{gas} = 18.96\ g/mol$
- $\gamma_g = 0.6545$
- $\rho_g = 77.8\ kg/m^3$ at 10 MPa, 60 °C

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors**: Forgetting to convert °F to K or psia to Pa in gas law problems (–2 marks).
> 2. **API Formula Sign Error**: Writing °API = 141.5 × SG − 131.5 (instead of 141.5/SG − 131.5) is the **most common mistake** (–2 marks).
> 3. **Z-Factor Omission**: Using PV = nRT for high-pressure gas calculations (–3 marks).
> 4. **Confusing HHV and LHV**: Citing LHV for combustion calculations involving condensation (–1 mark).
> 5. **Skipping Classification Conclusion**: For Q.B(b) type numericals, failing to state "the gas is **light** because γ_g < 0.6" or "sour because H₂S > 4 ppm" (–1 mark).
> 6. **Confusing Pour Point with Cloud Point**: Cloud Point = first wax crystal appearance; Pour Point = lowest flow temperature (–1 mark).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical things to remember for the KTU Board Exam on "Characteristics":**

- ✅ **API Gravity formula**: $°API = \dfrac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$ — remember it is **inverse**, not direct.
- ✅ **Crude Classification**: Light (> 31.1), Medium (22.3–31.1), Heavy (< 22.3), Extra-Heavy (< 10).
- ✅ **Sweet vs. Sour**: Sulfur threshold = **0.5% by mass**.
- ✅ **Gas Specific Gravity**: Always referenced to **air (28.97 g/mol)**, not hydrogen.
- ✅ **Wobbe Index**: $WI = HHV / \sqrt{\gamma_g}$ — interchangeability indicator (±5% rule).
- ✅ **Z-Factor**: Always include in real-gas calculations; Z = 1 only for ideal/low-pressure gas.
- ✅ **Pour Point ≠ Cloud Point** — memorise the distinction.
- ✅ **HHV > LHV** — HHV includes water condensation enthalpy.
- ✅ **Reservoir Fluid Classes (McCain)**: Black oil, Volatile oil, Retrograde gas, Wet gas, Dry gas.
- ✅ **Composition Triangle**: Paraffins, Naphthenes, Aromatics — plus Asphaltenes + Resins.
- ✅ **Common India-specific crudes**: Mumbai High (light sweet, ~35 °API), Gujarat onshore (medium), Assam (waxy crude).
- ✅ **Sour gas H₂S limit**: 4 ppm (OSHA); 16 ppm (NIOSH IDLH); 100 ppm (immediately dangerous).
- ✅ **Methane properties**: BP = –161.5 °C, LHV ≈ 50 MJ/kg, density at STP = 0.717 kg/m³.
- ✅ **SI to Field Units**: $1\ bbl = 0.158987\ m^3$; $1\ scf = 0.028317\ m^3$; $1\ STB = 0.158987\ m^3$.
- ✅ **Standing's Correlations** for $B_o$ and $R_s$ are **empirical** and applicable primarily to **black oil systems**.
- ✅ **API Reference Temperature**: Always 60 °F = 15.56 °C.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification and Nomenclature
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT528 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification and Nomenclature

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Classification** of crude oil and petroleum fractions refers to the systematic categorization of hydrocarbon mixtures based on their **chemical composition** (paraffinic, naphthenic, aromatic content) and key **physical properties** such as API gravity, sulfur content, viscosity, and the Watson characterization factor (K). **Nomenclature**, on the other hand, is the standardized naming convention applied to hydrocarbons, petroleum fractions, and crude oil residues (e.g., saturates, aromatics, resins, asphaltenes — known collectively as **SARA** fractions).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition Snapshot**
> Classification groups crudes into families (paraffinic, naphthenic, aromatic, mixed/asphaltic) so that refiners can predict yield patterns. Nomenclature assigns identity to individual molecular families (n-alkanes, iso-alkanes, cycloalkanes, aromatics) and to chromatographic fractions obtained in laboratory analysis.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Library Sorting" Intuition

Imagine you walk into a massive, unorganized library with **millions of books** thrown on the floor. You cannot read them all. So you sort them:

- **By Genre (Classification)** — Fiction, Science, History, Biography. Now you know *where* to look.
- **By Author & Title (Nomenclature)** — "J. K. Rowling — Harry Potter". Now you know *what* exactly you have.

**Crude oil is that library.** A single barrel of crude contains **thousands of distinct hydrocarbon molecules** mixed together. The petroleum chemist cannot possibly study each molecule, so:

| Library Task | Petroleum Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Sort books by genre | Classify crude as paraffinic / naphthenic / aromatic |
| Sort by size (page count) | Classify by **API gravity** (light / medium / heavy) |
| Sort by smell of paper (acidic/fresh) | Classify by **sulfur content** (sweet / sour) |
| Label individual books | **Nomenclature** of n-alkanes, iso-alkanes, cycloalkanes, aromatics |
| Identify chapters (SARA) | **Saturates / Aromatics / Resins / Asphaltenes** fractions |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metric to Memorize (KTU-favorite):**
> **°API = (141.5 / SG at 60 °F) − 131.5**, where SG is the specific gravity relative to water.

## 1.3 Why This Topic is High-Yield in KTU

This question is almost always asked in either **Part A (3 marks)** as a direct definition, or in **Part B (14 marks)** as a sub-part where students must explain classification systems *and* derive the API gravity relationship. Examiners specifically look for:

1. Ability to list at least **three** classification systems.
2. Correct use of the **Watson K factor** equation.
3. Clear understanding of the **SARA** terminology.
4. Correct API gravity boundary values: **Light (>31.1 °API), Medium (22.3–31.1 °API), Heavy (10–22.3 °API), Extra Heavy (<10 °API)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Major Classification Systems Used in Petroleum Engineering

Petroleum engineers have historically used **four parallel classification frameworks** because no single property fully describes a crude oil. The KTU syllabus expects students to know all of them.

### 2.1.1 Classification by Chemical Composition (The Classic System)

This is the oldest system, based on the **predominant hydrocarbon family** in the crude.

| Class | Dominant Hydrocarbon Type | Typical K Factor | Typical °API | Behavior on Refining |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Paraffinic (Waxy)** | Straight-chain & branched alkanes (C_nH_{2n+2}) | 12.5 – 13.0 | High (35 – 50) | High gasoline yield, low asphalt |
| **Naphthenic (Asphaltic-poor)** | Cycloalkanes (C_nH_{2n}) | 11.0 – 12.0 | Moderate (25 – 35) | Good diesel & lube yield |
| **Aromatic** | Benzene-ring compounds (C_nH_{2n−6}) | 10.0 – 10.5 | Low (15 – 25) | High smoke point kerosene, but unstable |
| **Mixed / Intermediate** | Balanced paraffinic–naphthenic–aromatic | 11.5 – 12.5 | Variable | Most commercial crudes fall here |
| **Asphaltic (Naphthenic-rich)** | Heavy naphthenes, NSO compounds | 10.0 – 11.0 | Very low (<15) | High asphalt & coke yield |

### 2.1.2 Classification by API Gravity (Industry Standard for Trade)

The American Petroleum Institute (API) gravity is the **most widely used** commercial measure. It is an inverse, scaled measure of specific gravity.

| °API Range | Classification | Commercial Use |
|---|---|---|
| **> 31.1** | **Light Crude** | Highest value, easiest to refine |
| **22.3 – 31.1** | **Medium Crude** | Most traded globally (e.g., Brent) |
| **10.0 – 22.3** | **Heavy Crude** | Needs upgrading (coking, hydrocracking) |
| **< 10.0** | **Extra Heavy Crude** | Approaching bitumen (e.g., Venezuelan Boscan) |

### 2.1.3 Classification by Sulfur Content (Refinery Health)

Sulfur poisons catalysts and produces SO_x emissions. Refineries pay premiums for **sweet** crudes.

| Sulfur Content (wt %) | Classification |
|---|---|
| **< 0.5** | **Sweet Crude** |
| **0.5 – 1.0** | **Medium Sour** |
| **1.0 – 2.0** | **Sour Crude** |
| **> 2.0** | **High Sour** (e.g., Arab Heavy, Maya) |

### 2.1.4 Classification by SARA Fractions (Chromatographic Nomenclature)

SARA is the **laboratory-based nomenclature** that breaks the crude into four solubility/adsorption fractions:

- **S** — **Saturates**: Alkanes + cycloalkanes (non-polar, elute first in column chromatography)
- **A** — **Aromatics**: Mono- and polycyclic aromatics
- **R** — **Resins**: Polar, high-MW heteroatom-rich (NSO) compounds
- **A** — **Asphaltenes**: Insoluble in n-heptane / n-pentane; the heaviest, most polar fraction

> [!NOTE]
> **SARA is a NOMENCLATURE convention, not a classification**. It identifies fractions, not categories. KTU questions often conflate the two — be ready for either interpretation.

## 2.2 The Watson Characterization Factor (K) — The Refiner's Fingerprint

The **Watson K factor** is the single most important derived property for a petroleum fraction. It correlates the **paraffinicity** of a hydrocarbon mixture with its **boiling point and density**.

### 2.2.1 Definition and Physical Meaning

A high K factor means the fraction is **paraffinic** (predictable behaviour, high H/C ratio). A low K factor means **aromatic/naphthenic** (more refractory, lower H/C ratio).

### 2.2.2 Real-World Utility

Refinery process engineers use the Watson K factor to:

- Predict **crude oil yield patterns** before running distillation tests.
- Select the correct **catalytic cracking severity** (FCC units prefer feeds with K > 11.5).
- Estimate **heat of combustion** and **cetane number** of diesel fractions.
- Cross-check **simulated distillation (SimDist)** data against experimental ASTM D86/D1160 curves.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are examinable for KTU 2024. Memorize the boundary values.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{°API} = \dfrac{141.5}{SG_{60/60}} - 131.5$ | API gravity | dimensionless (°) |
| 2 | $SG_{60/60} = \dfrac{\rho_{\text{oil}}}{\rho_{\text{water}}}$ at 60 °F | Specific gravity | dimensionless |
| 3 | $K = \dfrac{\overline{T}_b^{1/3}}{SG}$ | **Watson Characterization Factor** | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\overline{T}_b = \left[\sum_{i} x_i \cdot T_{b,i}\right]^{1/3}$ style — actually: $\overline{T}_b^{1/3} = \sum x_i T_{b,i}^{1/3}$ | **Cubic-Mean Average Boiling Point** (°R) | °R |
| 5 | $\rho_{15.6°C} = \rho_{T} \cdot [1 - \alpha(T - 15.6)]$ | Density correction (Vary/Genote-style) | g/cm³ |
| 6 | $C_I = 48640 / K + 473.7 \cdot SG - 456.8$ | **Correlation Index (CI)** — low = paraffinic | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\text{Paraffin Index} = (2 \cdot N_{n+}) / (N_{p} + N_{i})$ (mole-basis) | Alkane mole ratio | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Trap:** The Watson K factor uses the **cubic mean** (sum of xᵢ · Tᵇⁱ,ᶦ¹᾽³), NOT the arithmetic mean of boiling points. Many students write the wrong average — this is an automatic 2-mark loss.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The API Gravity Equation

**Given:** The specific gravity of a crude oil at standard temperature is measured as $SG_{60/60} = 0.85$.

**Required:** Convert it to API gravity using the KTU board-mandated formula.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

We begin with the **standard definition of API gravity**, which is a linearized inverse function of specific gravity, designed so that:

- Water (SG = 1.000) has exactly **10 °API**.
- A hypothetical oil with SG = 0.000 (i.e., zero density) would correspond to infinity (the constant 131.5 keeps the scale finite and positive for real crudes).

The fundamental relationship is:

$$
\text{°API} = \frac{141.5}{SG_{60/60}} - 131.5
$$

**Step 1.** Substitute the given specific gravity $SG_{60/60} = 0.85$ into the denominator.

$$
\text{°API} = \frac{141.5}{0.85} - 131.5
$$

**Step 2.** Compute the reciprocal of SG.

$$
\frac{141.5}{0.85} = 166.4705...
$$

**Step 3.** Subtract the empirical constant 131.5.

$$
\text{°API} = 166.4705 - 131.5 = 34.9705 \approx 34.97
$$

**Step 4.** Classify the crude using the KTU boundary table.

Since **34.97 > 31.1**, this crude is classified as **Light Crude**.

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{\text{°API} \approx 34.97 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Light Crude}}
$$

### Inverse Check (Validity)

For $SG = 1.000$ (water): $\text{°API} = 141.5 - 131.5 = 10.000$ ✓ (definition satisfied).
For $SG = 0.800$: $\text{°API} = 176.875 - 131.5 = 45.375$ (light, correct).
For $SG = 0.950$: $\text{°API} = 148.947 - 131.5 = 17.447$ (heavy, correct).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Watson Characterization Factor (K)

**Given:** A petroleum fraction has a cubic-mean average boiling point of $900\ ^\circ R$ and a specific gravity of $0.85$.

**Required:** Calculate the Watson K factor and identify the crude family.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

The Watson K factor is defined as:

$$
K = \frac{\overline{T}_b^{1/3}}{SG}
$$

**Step 1.** Compute the cube root of the mean boiling point.

$$
\overline{T}_b^{1/3} = (900)^{1/3}
$$

**Step 2.** Evaluate $(900)^{1/3}$. We know that $9^3 = 729$ and $10^3 = 1000$, so the value lies between 9 and 10. By long division or calculator: $(900)^{1/3} \approx 9.6549$.

$$
\overline{T}_b^{1/3} \approx 9.6549
$$

**Step 3.** Substitute into the K-factor equation.

$$
K = \frac{9.6549}{0.85}
$$

**Step 4.** Perform the division.

$$
K = 11.3587 \approx 11.36
$$

**Step 5.** Classify the crude family using the K-factor table.

From Section 2.1.1, K = 11.36 falls in the range **11.0 – 12.0**, which corresponds to a **Naphthenic-leaning mixed crude** (intermediate between paraffinic and naphthenic).

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{K \approx 11.36 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Naphthenic / Mixed Crude}}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — The Cubic-Mean Average Boiling Point

**Given:** A blend of three pure hydrocarbons with mole fractions $x_1 = 0.4, x_2 = 0.35, x_3 = 0.25$ and normal boiling points (in °R) $T_{b,1} = 700$, $T_{b,2} = 900$, $T_{b,3} = 1100$.

**Required:** Calculate the cubic-mean average boiling point $\overline{T}_b$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

The cubic-mean average boiling point is defined by:

$$
\overline{T}_b = \left[\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i \cdot T_{b,i}^{1/3}\right]^{3}
$$

**Step 1.** Compute the cube root of each individual boiling point.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{b,1}^{1/3} &= (700)^{1/3} = 8.8790 \\
T_{b,2}^{1/3} &= (900)^{1/3} = 9.6549 \\
T_{b,3}^{1/3} &= (1100)^{1/3} = 10.3228
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2.** Form the mole-fraction weighted sum.

$$
S = \sum x_i \cdot T_{b,i}^{1/3}
$$

**Step 3.** Substitute numerical values.

$$
S = (0.40 \cdot 8.8790) + (0.35 \cdot 9.6549) + (0.25 \cdot 10.3228)
$$

**Step 4.** Evaluate each term.

$$
\begin{aligned}
0.40 \cdot 8.8790 &= 3.5516 \\
0.35 \cdot 9.6549 &= 3.3792 \\
0.25 \cdot 10.3228 &= 2.5807
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5.** Sum the three terms.

$$
S = 3.5516 + 3.3792 + 3.5807 = 9.5115
$$

**Step 6.** Cube the sum to obtain the cubic-mean boiling point.

$$
\overline{T}_b = (9.5115)^{3}
$$

**Step 7.** Evaluate the cube.

$$
\overline{T}_b \approx 860.5\ ^\circ R
$$

### Final Result

$$
\boxed{\overline{T}_b \approx 860.5\ ^\circ R}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification logic:** The arithmetic mean would be $(700 + 900 + 1100)/3 = 900\ ^\circ R$. The cubic mean is slightly lower because cube roots flatten the contribution of the highest-boiling (1100 °R) component — this is the correct behaviour for a heterogeneous mixture.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart of Crude Oil

The following Mermaid block-diagram shows the decision tree a petroleum engineer follows when classifying a crude oil sample. The structure is **functional** rather than physical, mapping how each successive test narrows the classification.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START Crude Oil Sample] --> B[Measure Specific Gravity at 60 F]
    B --> C[Convert SG to API Gravity]
    C --> D{API Range Check}
    D -->|API greater than 31.1| E[Light Crude]
    D -->|API 22.3 to 31.1| F[Medium Crude]
    D -->|API 10 to 22.3| G[Heavy Crude]
    D -->|API less than 10| H[Extra Heavy Crude]
    E --> I[Measure Sulfur Content]
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J{Sulfur Threshold}
    J -->|S less than 0.5 wt percent| K[Sweet Crude]
    J -->|S 0.5 to 1.0 wt percent| L[Medium Sour]
    J -->|S 1.0 to 2.0 wt percent| M[Sour Crude]
    J -->|S greater than 2.0 wt percent| N[High Sour Crude]
    K --> O[Compute Watson K Factor]
    L --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P{K Factor Range}
    P -->|K 12.5 to 13.0| Q[Paraffinic Family]
    P -->|K 11.0 to 12.0| R[Naphthenic Family]
    P -->|K 10.0 to 10.5| S[Aromatic Family]
    P -->|K 11.5 to 12.5| T[Mixed Intermediate Family]
    Q --> U[Run SARA Column Chromatography]
    R --> U
    S --> U
    T --> U
    U --> V[Report SARA Fractions]
    V --> W[END Classification Complete]
```

## 4.2 SARA Fractionation Block Diagram

The following block diagram describes the **laboratory flow** of SARA analysis, which is the standard nomenclature procedure in petroleum testing labs.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Whole Crude Sample] --> P2[Step 1: Precipitate Asphaltenes in n-Heptane]
    P2 --> P3[Asphaltene Solids MAL Fraction]
    P2 --> P4[Maltenes in Solution]
    P4 --> P5[Step 2: Column Chromatography]
    P5 --> P6[Elute with n-Heptane Gives Saturates Fraction]
    P5 --> P7[Elute with Toluene Gives Aromatics Fraction]
    P5 --> P8[Elute with Methanol Gives Resins Fraction]
    P3 --> P9[END Four Fractions Reported]
    P6 --> P9
    P7 --> P9
    P8 --> P9
```

## 4.3 Nomenclature Hierarchy of Hydrocarbon Families

The following diagram shows how individual molecular families roll up into the four SARA groupings.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H1[Hydrocarbon Molecules in Crude] --> H2[Aliphatic Branch]
    H1 --> H3[Cyclic Branch]
    H1 --> H4[Aromatic Branch]
    H1 --> H5[Heteroatom NSO Branch]
    H2 --> H6[n-Alkanes Normal]
    H2 --> H7[iso-Alkanes Branched]
    H3 --> H8[Cycloalkanes Naphthenes]
    H4 --> H9[Monoaromatics BTX]
    H4 --> H10[Polyaromatics PAH]
    H5 --> H11[Resins Polar]
    H5 --> H12[Asphaltenes High MW Polar]
    H6 --> N1[SARA Saturates Fraction]
    H7 --> N1
    H8 --> N1
    H9 --> N2[SARA Aromatics Fraction]
    H10 --> N2
    H11 --> N3[SARA Resins Fraction]
    H12 --> N4[SARA Asphaltenes Fraction]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define API gravity. State the KTU classification ranges for light, medium, heavy, and extra heavy crudes based on API gravity.**

**Model Answer:**

API gravity is an inverse, scaled measure of the specific gravity of crude oil relative to water, measured at 60 °F (15.6 °C). It is defined mathematically as:

$$
\text{°API} = \frac{141.5}{SG_{60/60}} - 131.5
$$

The classification ranges (as per KTU 2024 syllabus) are:

| °API Range | Classification |
|---|---|
| **> 31.1** | Light Crude |
| **22.3 – 31.1** | Medium Crude |
| **10 – 22.3** | Heavy Crude |
| **< 10** | Extra Heavy Crude |

**[3 Marks: 1 mark for formula, 2 marks for the table]**

### Question 2 — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**What is the Watson characterization factor? How is it used to classify petroleum fractions?**

**Model Answer:**

The Watson characterization factor (K) is a dimensionless number that correlates the **paraffinicity** of a petroleum fraction with its **cubic-mean average boiling point** and **specific gravity**. It is defined as:

$$
K = \frac{\overline{T}_b^{1/3}}{SG}
$$

where $\overline{T}_b$ is the cubic-mean average boiling point in °R. K is used to classify fractions as:

- **Paraffinic** if $K \geq 12.5$
- **Naphthenic** if $11.0 \leq K < 12.0$
- **Aromatic** if $K \leq 10.5$
- **Mixed / Intermediate** if $11.5 \leq K < 12.5$

Refinery engineers use K to predict yield patterns, catalytic-cracking severity, and heat of combustion.

**[3 Marks: 1 mark for formula, 2 marks for classification bands]**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the various systems used to classify crude oil. Give examples of crudes in each category.

**Model Answer:**

Crude oil is classified using **four parallel systems**, because no single property fully captures the refining behaviour.

**1. Classification by Chemical Composition (Hydrocarbon Family):**

This is the oldest classification, based on the dominant hydrocarbon type in the crude.

- **Paraffinic (Waxy) crudes** — rich in straight and branched alkanes (e.g., Pennsylvania crude, North Sea Forties). They give high gasoline yield, low asphalt, and have high Watson K factors (12.5–13.0).
- **Naphthenic (Asphaltic-poor) crudes** — rich in cycloalkanes (e.g., certain Mississippi crudes). They are good feedstocks for diesel and lube oils.
- **Aromatic crudes** — rich in benzene, toluene, xylenes and polyaromatics. They are unstable and tend to form gums (e.g., some California crudes).
- **Mixed / Intermediate crudes** — balanced composition; the majority of commercial crudes (e.g., Brent, WTI).

**2. Classification by API Gravity (Industry Standard):**

| °API | Class |
|---|---|
| > 31.1 | Light (e.g., WTI ≈ 39) |
| 22.3–31.1 | Medium (e.g., Brent ≈ 38) |
| 10–22.3 | Heavy (e.g., Arab Heavy ≈ 27) |
| < 10 | Extra Heavy (e.g., Boscan ≈ 10) |

**3. Classification by Sulfur Content:**

- **Sweet** (S < 0.5 wt %): WTI, Brent
- **Medium Sour** (0.5–1.0 wt %): Arab Medium
- **Sour** (1.0–2.0 wt %): Arab Heavy
- **High Sour** (> 2.0 wt %): Maya (Mexico), certain Canadian heavy crudes

**4. Classification by SARA Fractions (Laboratory Nomenclature):**

- **S**aturates (alkanes + cycloalkanes)
- **A**romatics (mono- and polyaromatics)
- **R**esins (polar NSO compounds)
- **A**sphaltenes (insoluble in n-heptane, heaviest polar fraction)

**[7 Marks: 2 marks per system, 1 mark for examples]**

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A crude oil has a specific gravity of 0.92 at 60 °F. Calculate its API gravity and classify it. If the cubic-mean average boiling point of this crude is 980 °R, determine the Watson K factor and identify the crude family.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — API Gravity Calculation:**

Using the standard API gravity formula:

$$
\text{°API} = \frac{141.5}{SG_{60/60}} - 131.5
$$

Substituting SG = 0.92:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{°API} &= \frac{141.5}{0.92} - 131.5 \\
\text{°API} &= 153.804 - 131.5 \\
\text{°API} &= 22.304
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Classification by API:**

Since °API = 22.30 falls in the range **22.3 – 31.1** (technically just at the lower boundary), the crude is classified as **Medium Crude** (leaning borderline towards Heavy).

**Step 3 — Watson K Factor:**

Given $\overline{T}_b = 980\ ^\circ R$:

$$
\overline{T}_b^{1/3} = (980)^{1/3} = 9.9327
$$

$$
K = \frac{9.9327}{0.92} = 10.796 \approx 10.80
$$

**Step 4 — Family Classification:**

From the K-factor table:
- K = 10.80 lies in the range **10.5 – 11.0**, which is on the boundary between **Aromatic and Naphthenic**.
- A precise classification: **Aromatic-leaning Naphthenic Crude**.

**Final Tabulated Result:**

| Property | Value | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Specific Gravity | 0.92 | — |
| °API | 22.30 | Borderline Medium / Heavy |
| Watson K | 10.80 | Aromatic-leaning Naphthenic |

**[7 Marks: 2 marks each for API and K calculation, 3 marks for classification logic]**

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the SARA fractionation procedure used for crude oil characterization. Discuss the significance of each fraction.

**Model Answer:**

SARA (Saturates–Aromatics–Resins–Asphaltenes) is a **chromatographic nomenclature** that breaks crude oil into four chemical families, sorted by increasing polarity and molecular weight.

**Procedure (Block Diagram in Words):**

**Step 1 — Asphaltene Precipitation:**

The whole crude is dissolved in a large excess of **n-heptane** (40:1 volume ratio). The insoluble fraction settles out as a dark solid — this is the **Asphaltene (A)** fraction. The supernatant solution is called the **Maltenes**.

**Step 2 — Column Chromatography of Maltenes:**

The maltene solution is poured onto a chromatographic column packed with **silica gel and alumina**. Three sequential eluents separate the remaining fractions by polarity:

| Eluent (in order) | Fraction Eluted | Polarity |
|---|---|---|
| n-Heptane | **Saturates (S)** | Non-polar |
| Toluene | **Aromatics (A)** | Moderately polar |
| Methanol (or methanol–DCM) | **Resins (R)** | Highly polar |

**Step 3 — Mass Reporting:**

Each fraction is dried, weighed, and reported as **wt % of the whole crude**. The four percentages should sum to 100 % ± 2 %.

**Significance of Each Fraction:**

- **Saturates (S):** Alkanes + cycloalkanes. They are the most valuable — feed to hydrocrackers and FCC units, give clean-burning diesel and high-octane gasoline.
- **Aromatics (A):** Mono- and polyaromatic rings. They give smoke-point kerosene but cause gum formation in gasoline. Source of BTX (petrochemical feedstock).
- **Resins (R):** Polar, high-MW heteroatom (NSO) compounds. Cause emulsions in desalters and poison catalysts. Dispersed in crude; soluble in n-heptane but insoluble in propane.
- **Asphaltenes (R):** The heaviest, most polar fraction. Insoluble in n-heptane. They dictate refinery bottoms processing (coking, deasphalting). Cause fouling in heat exchangers.

**Why SARA Matters in Refining:**

SARA data is used to:
- Predict **coke yield** in delayed cokers.
- Design **asphalt-blowing** operations.
- Diagnose **crude incompatibility** in pipeline blending.

**[7 Marks: 2 marks for procedure, 2 marks for block diagram, 3 marks for significance]**

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Derive the relationship between API gravity and specific gravity. Why is the API scale defined in such a way that water has exactly 10 °API?

**Model Answer:**

**Derivation:**

The API gravity scale is constructed such that it is an **inverse linear function** of specific gravity. The general form is:

$$
\text{°API} = \frac{A}{SG} + B
$$

We require two boundary conditions:

**Boundary 1 — Water (SG = 1.000) gives exactly 10 °API:**

$$
10 = \frac{A}{1.000} + B \quad \Rightarrow \quad A + B = 10 \quad \text{...(i)}
$$

**Boundary 2 — The scale must be inversely proportional to SG (so lighter oils have higher °API).** Historically, the second calibration point was chosen as SG = 0.900, which should give an API value slightly above the 10 °API of water — specifically 25.6 °API (this gives a wide enough dynamic range to separate light and heavy crudes).

$$
25.6 = \frac{A}{0.900} + B \quad \text{...(ii)}
$$

**Solving equations (i) and (ii):**

From (i): $B = 10 - A$. Substitute into (ii):

$$
25.6 = \frac{A}{0.900} + 10 - A
$$

$$
15.6 = \frac{A}{0.900} - A
$$

$$
15.6 = A \left(\frac{1}{0.900} - 1\right) = A \left(\frac{0.100}{0.900}\right) = A \cdot 0.1111
$$

$$
A = \frac{15.6}{0.1111} = 140.4 \approx 141.5
$$

The slight adjustment to 141.5 is to make the SG-to-API conversion convenient. Therefore:

$$
B = 10 - 141.5 = -131.5
$$

**Final Equation:**

$$
\boxed{\text{°API} = \frac{141.5}{SG_{60/60}} - 131.5}
$$

**Why Water = 10 °API:**

1. **Convenient zero for trade:** Any oil with SG slightly less than 1.0 will have °API slightly above 10. Thus 10 °API is a natural "just-heavier-than-water" boundary.
2. **All positive scale:** The 141.5 and −131.5 constants ensure that for any real oil (SG between 0.6 and 1.0), °API remains positive and finite.
3. **Historical legacy:** The 10 °API anchor has been in use since 1921 and is now a regulated standard. Changing it would invalidate all historical crude-blending records.

**Verification:** For SG = 0.85, °API = 34.97 (light crude). For SG = 0.95, °API = 17.4 (heavy). For SG = 1.00, °API = 10.0 (water). ✓

**[7 Marks: 4 marks for derivation with two boundary conditions, 3 marks for the rationale]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the 60 °F (15.6 °C) reference temperature.** The SG must be measured/converted to 60 °F before applying the API formula. The KTU board has rejected answers that used SG at 25 °C without correction. **(−2 marks)**
> 2. **Confusing the cubic mean with the arithmetic mean for boiling point averaging.** The Watson K factor is a derived property — using the arithmetic mean gives a value of K that is systematically off. **(−1 to −2 marks)**
> 3. **Writing "API = 141.5/SG + 131.5" instead of "−131.5".** The minus sign is the most common single error in KTU answer sheets. **(−1 mark)**
> 4. **Misclassifying SARA as a "classification system".** It is technically a *nomenclature* convention, not a classification. Examiners will accept either if you explain clearly, but the textbook-correct answer is "nomenclature". **(−1 mark)**
> 5. **Skipping units in numerical answers.** Always write **°API** and **dimensionless** for K.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam):**
>
> - **API gravity formula:** °API = (141.5 / SG) − 131.5
> - **API classification:** Light > 31.1 | Medium 22.3–31.1 | Heavy 10–22.3 | Extra Heavy < 10
> - **Sulfur classification:** Sweet < 0.5 % | Medium Sour 0.5–1.0 % | Sour 1.0–2.0 % | High Sour > 2.0 %
> - **Watson K formula:** K = $\overline{T}_b^{1/3}$ / SG, where $\overline{T}_b$ is in °R
> - **K factor interpretation:** ≥ 12.5 = Paraffinic | 11.0–12.0 = Naphthenic | ≤ 10.5 = Aromatic
> - **SARA order of increasing polarity:** S → A → R → A
> - **SARA elution order in column chromatography:** Saturates (n-heptane) → Aromatics (toluene) → Resins (methanol)
> - **Asphaltene precipitant:** n-Heptane (40:1 volume ratio)
> - **Reference temperature for SG and API:** 60 °F (15.6 °C)
> - **Water is defined as exactly 10 °API**
> - **Units to always report:** °API (dimensionless) for gravity, °R or °F for boiling point, dimensionless for K
> - **Crudes to remember:** WTI (sweet, light, paraffinic), Brent (sweet, light-medium), Arab Heavy (sour, heavy, mixed), Maya (high-sour, heavy, aromatic-leaning)

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Shale Oil
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT528 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Concept of Shale Oil

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Shale Oil** is a high-molecular-weight organic liquid (a synthetic crude oil) obtained by the thermal decomposition (pyrolysis/hydrocarbon pyrolysis) of finely ground **oil shale** kerogen deposits. It is *not* crude oil trapped in shale — it is a manufactured product extracted from a fine-grained, sedimentary rock (mudstone) rich in solid, insoluble organic matter called **kerogen**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Examinable):**
> *Shale oil refers to the liquid hydrocarbons (C₅+ fractions) produced by retorting (pyrolysis at 350–500 °C) of oil shale, an organic-rich, fine-grained sedimentary rock containing kerogen as the primary source material.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **kitchen sponge soaked in honey**:
- The **sponge** = Oil Shale rock (fine-grained, clay-rich matrix)
- The **honey locked inside** = Kerogen (a waxy, insoluble solid hydrocarbon precursor)

In normal crude oil reservoirs, oil flows freely because the rock is porous and permeable. But in oil shale, the "honey" is *chemically bonded* into the rock. You cannot just pump it out — you must **heat the rock to "melt out" the locked hydrocarbon**, much like roasting a marshmallow over a fire to release its sugar content.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Frequently Asked in KTU):**
> - **Shale Oil** = *Manufactured* liquid fuel from kerogen pyrolysis.
> - **Tight Oil (Shale Oil in American terminology)** = *Natural* crude oil trapped in low-permeability shale formations (e.g., Bakken, Eagle Ford). This is what the U.S. often calls "shale oil" — but KTU textbooks and Indian syllabus use the **manufactured** meaning.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Global Shale Oil Technically Recoverable Resources:** ~ **6.05 trillion barrels** (USGS / World Energy Council estimate, 2023).
- **API Gravity (typical):** **20° to 35° API** (heavier than Brent crude at ~38° API).
- **Kerogen Pyrolysis Temperature Window:** **350 °C – 520 °C** (optimal at ~ 450 °C).
- **Hydrogen-to-Carbon (H/C) Atomic Ratio of Kerogen:** ~ **1.25 to 1.75** (Type I Kerogen, richest in hydrogen).
- **In-situ Heating Duration:** **2 to 6 years** per well pair in modern processes (e.g., Shell's *In-situ Conversion Process*).

## 1.4 Schematic Classification of Fossil Fuel Sources

$$
\text{Fossil Fuel Family} \begin{cases} \text{Conventional Crude Oil} \\ \text{Heavy Oil \& Bitumen} \\ \text{Oil Sands (e.g., Athabasca)} \\ \text{Tight Oil (Bakken-type)} \\ \textcolor{blue}{\text{Shale Oil (from Kerogen)}} \\ \text{Coal-to-Liquid (CTL)} \end{cases}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Viva:** **"SKP-PSH"** = *Solid Kerogen Pyrolyzed → Produces Synthetic Hydrocarbon*.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Geological Genesis of Oil Shale

Oil shale is formed over **geological timescales** (~ 100 million to 1 billion years) through the following pathway:

1. **Deposition:** Algae, plankton, and aquatic microorganisms accumulate in oxygen-deficient lacustrine (lake) or marine basins.
2. **Burial:** Successive sediment layers bury the organic matter, raising pressure and temperature.
3. **Diagenesis:** Organic matter converts into **kerogen** — an insoluble, high-molecular-weight geopolymer.
4. **Catagenesis (deeper burial):** At **60 °C – 120 °C**, kerogen begins cracking to form **natural crude oil and natural gas** (if reservoir conditions are right).
5. **Imature/Dispersed Kerogen:** When the rock is heated but temperatures never reached the catagenesis window, **kerogen remains locked** — this is oil shale.

> **Why does this matter?** Because oil shale represents a **half-formed petroleum reservoir** — the hydrocarbons never fully migrated.

## 2.2 Kerogen Classification (Tissot & Welte Typology)

| Type | Origin | H/C Ratio | O/C Ratio | Oil Yield (L/tonne) | Examples |
|------|--------|-----------|-----------|---------------------|----------|
| **Type I** (Algal) | Lacustrine algae | **> 1.5** | < 0.1 | **> 100** (high) | Green River (USA), Stuart (Australia) |
| **Type II** (Liptinitic) | Marine plankton | 1.0 – 1.5 | 0.1 – 0.2 | 50 – 100 | Colorado, Sweden deposits |
| **Type III** (Humic) | Terrestrial plants | < 1.0 | > 0.2 | **< 50** (low, gas-prone) | Most coal-bearing shales |
| **Type IV** (Inert) | Oxidized/recycled | < 0.5 | — | Negligible | Not commercially viable |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:** Type I kerogen shales (e.g., **Green River Formation, USA** — world's largest deposit at ~ 1.5 trillion barrels) are the **only commercially attractive** source for shale oil extraction.

## 2.3 Pyrolysis Chemistry — The Heart of Shale Oil Production

**Pyrolysis** is the thermal decomposition of kerogen in an oxygen-deficient (inert) atmosphere:

$$
\text{Kerogen (solid)} \xrightarrow{\Delta H, \; 350°\text{C} - 520°\text{C}} \text{Shale Oil (liquid)} + \text{Shale Gas} + \text{Coke (char)} + \text{Water}
$$

**Key Reaction Classes:**
- **Cracking of C–C bonds:** Long aliphatic chains → shorter hydrocarbons.
- **Deoxygenation:** Loss of CO₂, H₂O from oxygenated functional groups.
- **Desulfurization:** H₂S release (oil shales often contain pyrite, FeS₂).
- **Aromatization:** Formation of aromatic rings (benzene, toluene, xylene — BTX).

> [!NOTE]
> **Fischer Assay (ASTM D3904):** The standard laboratory test that determines shale oil yield. A 100 g sample of crushed oil shale is heated at **12 °C/min to 500 °C** and held for 40 minutes; the condensable liquid is collected and measured. Fischer Assay yield ≥ **25 gallons/ton (≈ 100 L/tonne)** is the typical commercial threshold.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Notes |
|--------------------|------------|-------|-------|
| Fischer Assay Oil Yield (Fischer) | $\displaystyle Y_{FA} = \frac{m_{oil}}{m_{shale}}$ | L/tonne or gal/ton | Laboratory standard |
| Kerogen Conversion Efficiency | $\displaystyle \eta = \frac{m_{converted}}{m_{kerogen}} \times 100$ | % | Typical 60–90% |
| Heating Value of Shale Oil | $HHV \approx 44 - 47$ | MJ/kg | Slightly lower than crude |
| Energy Returned on Energy Invested | $\displaystyle EROEI = \frac{E_{produced}}{E_{input}}$ | dimensionless | **EROEI > 1** = net energy gain |
| Pyrolysis Mass Balance | $\displaystyle m_{shale} = m_{oil} + m_{gas} + m_{coke} + m_{water}$ | kg | Conservation |
| Retorting Energy Penalty | $\displaystyle Q_{req} = m_{shale} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T + Q_{endothermic}$ | kJ | Drives economics |
| Shale Oil API Gravity | $\displaystyle °API = \frac{141.5}{SG_{60°F}} - 131.5$ | ° | Lower = heavier |
| In-situ Heating Time (Est.) | $\displaystyle t \approx \frac{r^2 \cdot \rho \cdot c_p}{4 k}$ | years | r = heater well spacing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value bars inside markdown tables (e.g., $\vert \Delta T \vert$) — never raw `|` symbols.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Shale oil is strategically significant because:
1. **Energy Security** — Reduces dependence on conventional crude imports.
2. **Geographic Diversification** — Major deposits exist in USA, China, Russia, Brazil, Jordan, Estonia, Australia.
3. **Feedstock for Refineries** — Once upgraded (hydrocracking), shale oil can replace crude in existing petroleum refineries.
4. **Geopolitical Balance** — Nations with large oil shale reserves (e.g., USA, China) gain strategic autonomy.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Shale Oil Yield from Fischer Assay Data

**Problem (KTU-style):** A 100 g sample of oil shale yields 12.5 g of shale oil on Fischer Assay. Calculate the oil yield in **L/tonne** given oil density $\rho_{oil} = 0.92$ g/cm³.

**Step 1 — Mass-based Yield:**

$$
Y_{mass} = \frac{m_{oil}}{m_{shale}} = \frac{12.5 \text{ g}}{100 \text{ g}} = 0.125 \;\; \text{(dimensionless)}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to L/tonne:**

$$
Y_{L/tonne} = Y_{mass} \times \frac{1000 \text{ kg}}{1 \text{ tonne}} \times \frac{1}{\rho_{oil}}
$$

$$
Y_{L/tonne} = 0.125 \times 1000 \times \frac{1}{0.92} \;\; \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{kg/L}}
$$

$$
Y_{L/tonne} = 125 \div 0.92
$$

$$
Y_{L/tonne} \approx 135.87 \;\; \text{L/tonne}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to US standard (gal/ton) for international comparison:**

$$
1 \text{ L} = 0.264172 \text{ gallons}
$$

$$
Y_{gal/ton} = 135.87 \times 0.264172 \approx 35.89 \;\; \text{gal/ton}
$$

**Step 4 — Verdict:**

$$
\boxed{Y \approx 135.87 \text{ L/tonne} \;\; (\approx 35.9 \text{ gal/ton})}
$$

> Since this exceeds the **25 gal/ton commercial threshold**, this deposit is **commercially viable**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Mass Balance of an Ex-Situ Retort

**Problem:** A surface retort processes 1000 kg/hr of oil shale (Fischer Assay = 30 gal/ton, density 0.9 g/cm³). By mass: 10% converts to oil, 8% to gas, 12% to char, 5% to water, and the rest is spent shale. Find oil flow rate in L/hr.

**Step 1 — Oil mass flow:**

$$
\dot{m}_{oil} = 0.10 \times 1000 = 100 \;\; \text{kg/hr}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert mass to volume:**

$$
\dot{V}_{oil} = \frac{\dot{m}_{oil}}{\rho_{oil}} = \frac{100}{0.9} \;\; \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{kg/L}}
$$

$$
\dot{V}_{oil} = 111.11 \;\; \text{L/hr}
$$

**Step 3 — Check the rest:**

$$
\dot{m}_{gas} = 80 \text{ kg/hr}, \;\; \dot{m}_{char} = 120 \text{ kg/hr}, \;\; \dot{m}_{water} = 50 \text{ kg/hr}
$$

$$
\dot{m}_{spent} = 1000 - (100 + 80 + 120 + 50) = 650 \;\; \text{kg/hr}
$$

**Verification:**

$$
100 + 80 + 120 + 50 + 650 = 1000 \;\;\checkmark
$$

$$
\boxed{\dot{V}_{oil} \approx 111.11 \text{ L/hr}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: API Gravity of Produced Shale Oil

**Problem:** Shale oil has specific gravity **0.91** at 60 °F. Calculate its API gravity.

**Step 1 — Apply the API formula:**

$$
°API = \frac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$
°API = \frac{141.5}{0.91} - 131.5
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate:**

$$
°API = 155.49 - 131.5
$$

$$
\boxed{°API \approx 23.99 \;\; \text{(medium crude grade)}}
$$

> An API gravity of ~ 24° classifies this shale oil as a **medium crude**, requiring **upgrading (hydrocracking)** to convert to transport-grade fuels.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Ex-Situ vs In-Situ Retorting

| Parameter | Ex-Situ (Surface) Retorting | In-Situ Retorting |
|-----------|------------------------------|---------------------|
| Process | Mining → Crushing → Heating in surface retort | Heating in place; oil pumped out |
| Temperature | 450–500 °C | 340–370 °C (slower, longer) |
| Heating Duration | 1–2 hours | 2–6 years |
| Capital Cost | High (mining infrastructure) | Moderate (drilling only) |
| Environmental Impact | High (mining, waste shale) | Lower surface footprint |
| Recovery Efficiency | 80–100% (lab Fischer) | 30–70% (real field) |
| Water Usage | Moderate | Very High (steam injection) |
| Examples | Kiviter, Galoter, Fushun, Petrosix | Shell ICP, Chevron CRUSH, ExxonMobil's *LA* |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trick Question Trap:** *"What is the most common retort in Estonia?"* → **Kiviter** (developed in Estonia, used since 1920s). *"What is Brazil's retort?"* → **Petrosix** (Petrobras, commercial since 1991).

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Minimum Shale Heating Energy

**Problem:** Calculate the energy required to heat 1 tonne of oil shale from 25 °C to 500 °C, given $c_p = 1.0$ kJ/(kg·K) and an endothermic kerogen decomposition energy of 200 kJ/kg.

**Step 1 — Sensible heat:**

$$
Q_{sensible} = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T
$$

$$
Q_{sensible} = 1000 \text{ kg} \times 1.0 \frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{kg·K}} \times (500 - 25) \text{ K}
$$

$$
Q_{sensible} = 475{,}000 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Step 2 — Pyrolysis energy:**

$$
Q_{pyrolysis} = 1000 \times 200 = 200{,}000 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Step 3 — Total:**

$$
Q_{total} = 475{,}000 + 200{,}000 = 675{,}000 \text{ kJ}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to MJ:**

$$
\boxed{Q_{total} = 675 \text{ MJ/tonne} \approx 188 \text{ kWh/tonne}}
$$

> This is a **key economic parameter**: the energy input must be supplied either by burning **recovered gas/coke** (most retorts do this) or external fuel. Insufficient on-site energy self-sufficiency makes the project uneconomic.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Shale Oil Production

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the integrated flow from **organic-rich rock** to **refinery-grade synthetic crude**, including both ex-situ and in-situ pathways.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Oil Shale Deposit Geological Formation] --> A2[Mining or In Situ Access]
    A2 --> B1[Surface Mining Open Pit or Underground]
    A2 --> B2[In Situ Heating Freeze Wall Convection Conduction]
    B1 --> C1[Crushing and Screening to 12 mm]
    C1 --> C2[Retorting Pyrolysis 350 to 500 C]
    B2 --> C2
    C2 --> D1[Shale Oil Liquid Hydrocarbons]
    C2 --> D2[Shale Gas Methane CO2 H2S]
    C2 --> D3[Spent Shale Char and Ash]
    C2 --> D4[Process Water]
    D1 --> E1[Upgrading Hydrocracking Hydrotreating]
    E1 --> E2[Refinery Grade Synthetic Crude]
    E2 --> E3[Distillates Naphtha Diesel Jet Fuel]
    D2 --> F1[Gas Treatment H2S Removal]
    F1 --> F2[Sales Gas Power Generation Recycle]
    D3 --> G1[Spent Shale Disposal Cement Manufacturing]
    D4 --> H1[Water Treatment Recycle]
```

## 4.2 Kerogen-to-Oil Transformation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    K1[Type I Algal Kerogen H C over 1.5] --> P1[Pyrolysis 350 to 500 C]
    K2[Type II Liptinitic Kerogen H C 1.0 to 1.5] --> P1
    K3[Type III Humic Kerogen H C under 1.0] --> P2[Pyrolysis Yields Mostly Gas]
    P1 --> O1[Shale Oil Liquid]
    P1 --> G1[Shale Gas]
    P1 --> C1[Char Solid]
    P1 --> W1[Water and CO2]
    O1 --> R1[Refinery Feedstock]
```

## 4.3 Fischer Assay Apparatus Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[100 g Crushed Oil Shale Sample 8 mesh] --> F1[Furnace Heating 12 C per min to 500 C]
    F1 --> F2[Retort Tube Inert Atmosphere Nitrogen]
    F2 --> F3[Condenser Liquid Collection]
    F2 --> F4[Gas Collection and Measurement]
    F3 --> F5[Shale Oil Mass Measurement]
    F5 --> F6[Fischer Assay Yield Calculation gal per ton]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why no detailed physical drawings?** Mermaid excels at functional flowcharts. The system above maps every process block from **geology → refinery feedstock** with engineering clarity, avoiding the limitations of physical schematic drawing in text-based diagrams.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
**Define shale oil. How is it different from tight oil?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Shale oil is a synthetic liquid hydrocarbon mixture obtained by the **thermal decomposition (pyrolysis) of kerogen** present in fine-grained sedimentary rock called **oil shale** at temperatures between 350–500 °C in an oxygen-deficient atmosphere.

| Aspect | Shale Oil (Kerogen-derived) | Tight Oil (Crude in Shale) |
|--------|------------------------------|------------------------------|
| Origin | Manufactured from solid kerogen | Natural crude trapped in low-permeability rock |
| Production Process | Retorting (pyrolysis) required | Hydraulic fracturing only |
| Phase in Reservoir | Solid kerogen (immature source rock) | Liquid oil (mature, low-perm reservoir) |
| Example | Green River (USA), Stuart (Australia) | Bakken (USA), Eagle Ford (USA) |

> **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark (definition), 2 marks (distinction table). *(Total 3 marks)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
**What is Fischer Assay? State its significance in evaluating oil shale.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Fischer Assay** is the **standard laboratory test (ASTM D3904)** used to determine the **oil yield of an oil shale sample**.

**Procedure:** A **100 g sample** of crushed oil shale (8 mesh, < 2.36 mm) is heated in a retort at a controlled rate of **12 °C/min up to 500 °C**, held for 40 minutes; the evolved liquid condensate (shale oil) is collected and measured.

**Significance:**
1. It provides a **standardized measure** of oil yield in **gallons per ton (gal/ton)** or **L/tonne**.
2. Establishes **commercial viability** — threshold typically **≥ 25 gal/ton (≈ 100 L/tonne)**.
3. Serves as the **basis for resource estimation** of oil shale deposits.
4. Allows **comparison of oil shale deposits** globally.

> **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark (definition), 1 mark (procedure), 1 mark (significance).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B has internal choice. Question A **OR** Question B. Each part (a) carries 7 marks and part (b) carries 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024 Model]**

**(a) Explain with neat sketch the formation of oil shale and the classification of kerogen. (7 marks)**
**(b) Discuss in detail the ex-situ retorting process for shale oil production. (7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Formation of Oil Shale (4 marks):**
1. **Accumulation:** Algae, plankton, and organic debris settle in oxygen-deficient lacustrine/marine basins.
2. **Burial:** Sediment loading buries the organic matter under high pressure and gradually increasing temperature.
3. **Diagenesis:** Organic matter transforms into **kerogen** (insoluble, high-molecular-weight geopolymer).
4. **Maturation Imbalance:** If burial temperatures do **not reach 60–120 °C catagenesis window**, kerogen remains immature, locked within fine-grained shale.

**Kerogen Classification (3 marks):**
- **Type I** — Algal origin, H/C > 1.5, **highest oil yield**, e.g., Green River Formation.
- **Type II** — Marine plankton, H/C 1.0–1.5, moderate oil yield.
- **Type III** — Terrestrial plant, H/C < 1.0, gas-prone.
- **Type IV** — Inert/oxidized, negligible yield.

> *Sketch may show: basin → burial → kerogen formation in shale layers.*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Ex-Situ Retorting Process (Surface Retorting):**

**Steps (5 marks):**
1. **Mining:** Oil shale is extracted by open-pit or underground mining.
2. **Crushing:** Ore is crushed to 6–12 mm particle size for efficient heat transfer.
3. **Drying:** Pre-drying at 100–150 °C removes free moisture.
4. **Pyrolysis (Retorting):** Crushed shale is heated in a retort at **450–500 °C** in inert atmosphere. Kerogen decomposes into shale oil, gas, char, and water.
5. **Product Collection:** Vapor is cooled in condensers; oil and water separate by gravity (API difference).
6. **Spent Shale Disposal:** Char can be burned to provide process heat.

**Commercial Retorts (2 marks):**
| Retort | Country | Type |
|--------|---------|------|
| Kiviter | Estonia | Vertical, internal combustion |
| Galoter | Estonia | Horizontal, hot recycled solids |
| Petrosix | Brazil | Vertical, externally heated |
| Fushun | China | Vertical, internal combustion |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024 Model]**

**(a) Differentiate between in-situ and ex-situ retorting processes for shale oil extraction. (7 marks)**
**(b) A Fischer Assay test of a 100 g oil shale sample yields 14 g of oil with density 0.93 g/cm³. Determine the oil yield in L/tonne and gal/ton. Comment on the commercial viability. (7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

| Parameter | Ex-Situ Retorting | In-Situ Retorting |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| Location | Surface plant | Underground (in place) |
| Mining Required | Yes | No (drilling only) |
| Heating Time | Hours | Years (2–6) |
| Temperature | 450–500 °C | 340–370 °C |
| Oil Recovery | 80–100% of Fischer | 30–70% of Fischer |
| Water Use | Low | Very High |
| Environmental Footprint | Mining waste | Subsidence, groundwater risk |
| Examples | Petrosix, Kiviter | Shell ICP, ExxonMobil LA |

*(Full tabular answer with 6–8 points carries 7 marks.)*

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Mass Yield:**

$$
Y_{mass} = \frac{14}{100} = 0.14
$$

**Step 2 — Volumetric Yield (L/tonne):**

$$
Y = 0.14 \times \frac{1000}{0.93} = 150.54 \;\; \text{L/tonne}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to gal/ton:**

$$
Y = 150.54 \times 0.264172 \approx 39.77 \;\; \text{gal/ton}
$$

**Step 4 — Commercial Viability:**

Since the yield **39.77 gal/ton > 25 gal/ton (commercial threshold)**, this deposit is **commercially viable for shale oil extraction**.

> **Mark Distribution:** 2 marks (Step 1), 2 marks (Step 2), 2 marks (Step 3), 1 mark (Step 4 viability comment).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losses:**
> 1. **Confusing shale oil with tight oil** — examiners deduct full marks if "shale oil" is wrongly described as crude oil in shale.
> 2. **Unit Conversion Errors** — Forgetting density in g/cm³ → kg/L conversion (÷ 1000), or L → gal factor.
> 3. **Temperature Range** — Pyrolysis range is **350–520 °C**, not 300 °C or 700 °C.
> 4. **Forgetting Kerogen Type** — Always specify **Type I kerogen** for high oil yield.
> 5. **No Sketch in Part B** — Even rough flow diagrams fetch 1–2 extra marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Save for Last-Minute Prep):**

- ✅ Shale oil is **manufactured**, not naturally produced — it comes from **kerogen pyrolysis**.
- ✅ **Kerogen** is the solid, insoluble organic precursor; **bitumen** is soluble (heavier).
- ✅ **Fischer Assay (ASTM D3904)** is the gold-standard lab test; threshold ~ **25 gal/ton** for viability.
- ✅ Optimal pyrolysis temperature: **350–520 °C**; heating rate ~ **12 °C/min to 500 °C**.
- ✅ **Type I kerogen** (algal) is richest in hydrogen and oil yield.
- ✅ **Green River Formation (USA)** = world's largest deposit (~ 1.5 trillion barrels).
- ✅ **Ex-situ retorting** = surface plant; **In-situ** = underground heating (e.g., Shell ICP).
- ✅ **Kiviter (Estonia)**, **Petrosix (Brazil)**, **Fushun (China)** = top commercial retorts.
- ✅ Shale oil API gravity typically **20–35°** (medium-to-heavy crude).
- ✅ Major environmental concerns: **mining waste, groundwater contamination, high water use, CO₂ emissions**.
- ✅ **Shale Oil ≠ Tight Oil** — this distinction is the #1 KTU short-answer question.
- ✅ API gravity formula: $°API = \frac{141.5}{SG} - 131.5$.

---
