topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT528 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Reservoir Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITIONS & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 What is a Petroleum Reservoir?

A **Petroleum Reservoir** is a porous, permeable underground rock formation containing hydrocarbons (oil and/or natural gas) within its interconnected pore network, bounded by impermeable or low-permeability barriers that prevent further migration of the fluids.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Exam Standard):** A reservoir is a subsurface geological trap comprising a porous reservoir rock (storage capacity), permeable flow paths (deliverability), and a sealing cap rock (containment) that economically justifies hydrocarbon production.

### 1.2 The Five Fundamental Reservoir Rock Properties

A petroleum engineer must quantify **five critical rock properties** to estimate reserves, plan drilling, and design production systems:

1. **Porosity (φ)** — How much fluid the rock can hold.
2. **Permeability (k)** — How easily fluid can flow through the rock.
3. **Fluid Saturation (S)** — What fraction of pore space is occupied by each fluid.
4. **Wettability (θ)** — The rock's preference for oil or water adhesion.
5. **Capillary Pressure (Pc)** — The pressure difference across a fluid interface in the pore.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Sponge-Straw Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a kitchen sponge (the reservoir rock).
> - The **holes/pockets** in the sponge = **Porosity** (storage space).
> - The **interconnected tunnels** between holes = **Permeability** (flow paths).
> - The **water absorbed** in the sponge = **Fluid Saturation** (how full it is).
> - The sponge's **preference for oil over water** = **Wettability** (surface chemistry).
> - The **suction force** when you squeeze it = **Capillary Pressure** (interfacial tension).
>
> A **good reservoir** is a sponge with **large connected holes** (high φ) and **wide tunnels** (high k).

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Standard atmospheric pressure | Patm | **101.325 kPa = 14.696 psi** |
| Standard temperature | Tstd | **288.15 K = 60 °F = 15.56 °C** |
| Permeability unit (SI) | k | **1 Darcy ≈ 9.869 × 10⁻¹³ m²** |
| 1 millidarcy | md | **0.001 Darcy = 9.869 × 10⁻¹⁶ m²** |
| Surface tension of water (25 °C) | σw | **~72 mN/m** |
| Surface tension of oil (typical) | σo | **~25–35 mN/m** |
| Interfacial tension (oil–water) | IFT | **~10–50 mN/m** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always quote permeability in **millidarcy (md)** and pressure in **psi** unless otherwise specified — these are the implicit defaults in KTU numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Porosity (φ) — Storage Capacity

Porosity is the **ratio of pore volume to bulk volume** of the rock. It is dimensionless and expressed as a fraction or percentage.

### 2.1.1 Types of Porosity

- **Primary (Intergranular) Porosity:** Formed at the time of deposition (e.g., spaces between sand grains).
- **Secondary Porosity:** Formed after deposition via dissolution, fracturing, or dolomitization (e.g., vugs, fractures).
- **Absolute Porosity (φa):** Includes all pore space (connected + isolated).
- **Effective Porosity (φeff):** Only the interconnected pore space available for fluid flow.

$$\phi = \frac{V_{pore}}{V_{bulk}} = \frac{V_{bulk} - V_{grain}}{V_{bulk}}$$

### 2.1.2 Engineering Significance
- Controls **hydrocarbon-in-place (HCIP)** volume.
- Determines **log response** (sonic, density, neutron logs).
- Used in **material balance** equations.

## 2.2 Permeability (k) — Flow Capacity

The ability of a rock to transmit fluids when the pores are 100% saturated with that fluid is called **absolute permeability (k)**. It is governed by **Darcy's Law**.

For linear, horizontal, single-phase flow:

$$Q = \frac{k \, A \, \Delta P}{\mu \, L}$$

Solving for permeability:

$$k = \frac{Q \, \mu \, L}{A \, \Delta P}$$

where the symbols are:
- $Q$ = volumetric flow rate **m³/s** (or **cc/s**)
- $A$ = cross-sectional area perpendicular to flow, **m²** (or **cm²**)
- $\Delta P$ = pressure drop across length $L$, **Pa** (or **atm**)
- $\mu$ = fluid dynamic viscosity, **Pa·s** (or **cp**)
- $L$ = length over which pressure drop occurs, **m** (or **cm**)

### 2.2.1 The Darcy Unit
A rock has a permeability of **1 Darcy** if a fluid of **1 centipoise** viscosity flowing at a rate of **1 cm³/s** produces a pressure gradient of **1 atm/cm** across a **1 cm²** cross-section.

### 2.2.2 Klinkenberg Effect (Gas Slippage)

For gas flow at low pressures, molecules slip along pore walls, increasing apparent permeability:

$$k_{app} = k_{\infty} \left( 1 + \frac{b}{P_{mean}} \right)$$

- $k_{\infty}$ = equivalent liquid (Klinkenberg-corrected) permeability
- $b$ = Klinkenberg slip factor (depends on gas and pore size)
- $P_{mean}$ = mean pore pressure

### 2.2.3 Relative Permeability

When multiple fluids coexist, each fluid has **effective permeability** $k_{i}$, expressed as a **relative permeability** $k_{ri}$:

$$k_{ri} = \frac{k_{i}}{k}$$

where $k$ is the absolute permeability at 100% saturation of the reference phase.

## 2.3 Fluid Saturation (S)

The fraction of pore volume occupied by a particular fluid:

$$S_w + S_o + S_g = 1$$

- $S_w$ = water saturation
- $S_o$ = oil saturation
- $S_g$ = gas saturation

### 2.3.1 Key Saturation Thresholds
- **Connate (Interstitial) Water Saturation (Swc):** Water trapped during hydrocarbon migration.
- **Critical Oil Saturation (Soc):** Below this, oil is immobile.
- **Residual Oil Saturation (Sor):** Oil remaining after flooding.

## 2.4 Wettability (θ)

The tendency of a rock surface to preferentially contact one fluid (usually water or oil) in the presence of another. Quantified by the **contact angle** measured through the denser phase.

| Wettability State | Contact Angle θ | Reservoir Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| Strongly Water-Wet | 0°–30° | Water occupies small pores, oil occupies large |
| Weakly Water-Wet | 30°–75° | Mixed preference |
| Neutrally Wet | 75°–105° | No strong preference |
| Weakly Oil-Wet | 105°–150° | Oil adheres to grains |
| Strongly Oil-Wet | 150°–180° | Oil occupies small pores, water occupies large |

## 2.5 Capillary Pressure (Pc)

The pressure difference across the curved interface of two immiscible fluids in a capillary (pore):

$$P_c = P_{non-wetting} - P_{wetting} = \frac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{r}$$

- $\sigma$ = interfacial tension (IFT), **N/m**
- $\theta$ = contact angle
- $r$ = pore-throat radius, **m**

The **Leverett J-Function** normalizes capillary pressure data:

$$J(S_w) = \frac{P_c}{\sigma \cos\theta} \sqrt{\frac{k}{\phi}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Capillary pressure controls the **transition zone** between the water leg and the hydrocarbon column in a reservoir.

## 2.6 Rock & Fluid Compressibility

**Rock Compressibility:**

$$c_r = \frac{1}{V} \left( \frac{\partial V}{\partial P} \right)_T \approx \frac{1}{\phi} \left( \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial P} \right)$$

**Total (Formation) Compressibility:**

$$c_t = c_r + S_o c_o + S_w c_w + S_g c_g$$

**Typical Compressibility Values (in psi⁻¹):**

| Component | Compressibility (psi⁻¹) |
|---|---|
| Sandstone (c_r) | $3 \times 10^{-6}$ to $10 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| Limestone (c_r) | $2 \times 10^{-6}$ to $6 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| Oil (c_o) | $5 \times 10^{-6}$ to $50 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| Water (c_w) | $2 \times 10^{-6}$ to $5 \times 10^{-6}$ |
| Gas (c_g) | $100 \times 10^{-6}$ to $1000 \times 10^{-6}$ |

## 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Quick-Reference Table.** Memorize these — at least 60% of numerical problems in Part B test these exact relations.

| # | Property | Formula | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Porosity | $\phi = V_{pore}/V_{bulk}$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Permeability (Darcy's Law) | $k = Q\mu L/(A\Delta P)$ | Darcy (D) or millidarcy (md) |
| 3 | Klinkenberg Correction | $k_{app} = k_{\infty}(1 + b/P_m)$ | md |
| 4 | Relative Permeability | $k_{ri} = k_i/k$ | dimensionless |
| 5 | Saturation Constraint | $S_w + S_o + S_g = 1$ | dimensionless |
| 6 | Capillary Pressure | $P_c = 2\sigma\cos\theta / r$ | Pa or psi |
| 7 | Leverett J-Function | $J = (P_c/\sigma\cos\theta)\sqrt{k/\phi}$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Rock Compressibility | $c_r = (1/V)(\partial V/\partial P)$ | psi⁻¹ or Pa⁻¹ |
| 9 | HCIP (Oil) | $N = 7758 \cdot \phi \cdot (1-S_w) \cdot h \cdot A / B_o$ | bbl (field units) |
| 10 | HCIP (Gas) | $G = 43560 \cdot \phi \cdot (1-S_w) \cdot h \cdot A / B_g$ | scf (field units) |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Reserves Estimation:** φ, S, h, and A feed directly into the volumetric equation for hydrocarbons-in-place.
- **Production Planning:** k determines well spacing and flow rates.
- **Enhanced Oil Recovery (EOR):** Wettability and IFT manipulation is the basis for chemical flooding.
- **Drilling & Completion:** Capillary pressure affects mud invasion and cement bond integrity.
- **Reservoir Simulation:** All five properties are inputs to numerical flow simulators (e.g., Eclipse, CMG).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Darcy's Law from the Navier–Stokes Equations (Simplified Form)

We consider laminar, steady-state, single-phase, horizontal flow through a cylindrical pore of radius $R$ and length $L$.

**Step 1 — Momentum balance on a fluid element:** The pressure force on the fluid slice is balanced by the viscous shear at the wall.

$$\Delta P \cdot \pi R^{2} = - \tau_{wall} \cdot 2\pi R \cdot L$$

**Step 2 — Newton's Law of Viscosity for laminar flow:**

$$\tau_{wall} = -\mu \left. \frac{du}{dr} \right\vert_{r=R}$$

**Step 3 — Integrate the velocity profile assuming linear shear at the wall (Hagen–Poiseuille analog):**

$$u(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} (R^{2} - r^{2})$$

**Step 4 — Integrate the velocity profile over the cross-section to obtain volumetric flow rate:**

$$Q = \int_0^R u(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \frac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$$

**Step 5 — Define permeability as $k = R^{2}/8$ for a single circular capillary:**

$$Q = \frac{k A \Delta P}{\mu L} \quad \text{with} \quad k = \frac{R^{2}}{8}$$

This recovers **Darcy's Law**. For a real porous medium, the constant $k$ is determined empirically (via core flooding) and is a function of pore geometry, tortuosity, and cementation.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Kozeny–Carman Equation for Permeability

The Kozeny–Carman equation models permeability as a function of porosity, specific surface area, and tortuosity.

**Step 1 — Define the effective hydraulic diameter $D_h$:**

$$D_h = \frac{4 \cdot \text{(flow area)}}{\text{wetted perimeter}} = \frac{4 \phi}{S_0 (1 - \phi)}$$

where $S_0$ is the specific surface area per unit volume of solid.

**Step 2 — For laminar flow in a circular tube, hydraulic radius theory gives:**

$$k = \frac{D_h^{2} \phi}{16 \, k_0 \, \tau^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Combine the expressions and absorb the geometric constant into the Kozeny constant $k_0$ (≈ 2.5 for spherical grains):**

$$k = \frac{\phi^{3}}{k_0 \, \tau^{2} \, S_0^{2} (1 - \phi)^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Simplify by introducing the formation resistivity factor $F = \tau / \phi$ (Archie's empirical form):**

$$k = \frac{1}{k_0 \, S_0}^{2} \cdot \frac{\phi^{3}}{(1 - \phi)^{2}} \cdot \frac{1}{F^{2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Permeability scales as $\phi^{3}$ and inversely as $(1-\phi)^{2}$. This cubic dependence on porosity explains why a small porosity reduction (e.g., from compaction) can collapse permeability by an order of magnitude.

## 3.3 Derivation of Capillary Pressure from Young–Laplace Equation

**Step 1 — Consider a spherical oil droplet trapped in a water-wet pore of radius $r$.** The interfacial energy stored in the curved oil–water interface is $E = 4\pi r^{2} \sigma$.

**Step 2 — A virtual expansion of the droplet by $dr$ does work equal to the pressure difference times the volume change:**

$$dW = P_c \cdot 4\pi r^{2} \, dr$$

**Step 3 — Equate mechanical work to the increase in surface energy (Young–Laplace principle):**

$$P_c \cdot 4\pi r^{2} dr = d(4\pi r^{2} \sigma) = 8\pi r \sigma \, dr$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $P_c$:**

$$P_c = \frac{2 \sigma}{r}$$

**Step 5 — Account for the contact angle $\theta$ between the oil–water interface and the rock grain:**

$$P_c = \frac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{r}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** The factor of 2 appears for a *spherical* (or hemispherical) droplet. For a *cylindrical* pore-throat, the denominator is $r$ (not $2r$). Memorize the spherical form — it is the default in 95% of KTU problems.

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU 2024 Scheme Style

**Problem:** A cylindrical core sample of length $L = 10$ cm and cross-sectional area $A = 10$ cm² is 100% saturated with a brine of viscosity $\mu = 1.0$ cp. Water flows at a steady rate of $Q = 0.5$ cm³/s when the pressure drop across the core is $\Delta P = 2$ atm. Calculate:

(a) The absolute permeability in Darcy and millidarcy.
(b) The Darcy velocity (flux).
(c) The pore velocity if the porosity is 20%.

---

### (a) Absolute Permeability

Using Darcy's Law:

$$k = \frac{Q \mu L}{A \Delta P}$$

**Substitution (using Darcy's field units: $Q$ in cm³/s, $\mu$ in cp, $L$ and $A$ in cm and cm², $\Delta P$ in atm):**

$$k = \frac{(0.5)(1.0)(10)}{(10)(2)}$$

$$k = \frac{5.0}{20} = 0.25 \text{ Darcy}$$

**Conversion to millidarcy:**

$$k = 0.25 \times 1000 = 250 \text{ md}$$

### (b) Darcy Velocity (Flux)

$$v_{Darcy} = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.5}{10} = 0.05 \text{ cm/s}$$

### (c) Pore (Interstitial) Velocity

$$v_{pore} = \frac{v_{Darcy}}{\phi} = \frac{0.05}{0.20} = 0.25 \text{ cm/s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Insight:** The pore velocity is **5× higher** than the Darcy velocity because fluid actually travels through the tortuous pore network, not the straight bulk volume. This distinction is critical in tracer studies and EOR design.

## 3.5 Comprehensive Property Cross-Reference Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is your **master cross-link** for KTU exam preparation — the relationships between properties are the most common 7-mark question pattern.

| Property | Symbol | Depends On | Affects | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Porosity | φ | Grain size, sorting, cementation, compaction | HCIP, sonic/density logs | 5–35% |
| Permeability | k | Pore-throat radius, tortuosity, connectivity | Production rate, well spacing | 0.1 md – 1 Darcy |
| Wettability | θ | Mineralogy, oil composition, aging | Relative permeability, capillary pressure, EOR | 0°–180° |
| Capillary Pressure | Pc | IFT, θ, pore radius | Transition zone height, initial saturation | 0 – several hundred psi |
| Fluid Saturation | S | Pore geometry, wettability, Pc history | Reserves, relative permeability curves | $0 \le S_i \le 1$ |
| Rock Compressibility | cr | Mineralogy, cementation, overburden pressure | Material balance, subsidence | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-5}$ psi⁻¹ |

## 3.6 Summary of Key Engineering Equations

**Hydrocarbon Pore Volume (HCPV):**

$$HCPV = \phi \cdot V_{bulk} \cdot (1 - S_w)$$

**Initial Oil-in-Place (Volumetric, field units):**

$$N = \frac{7758 \cdot \phi \cdot (1-S_w) \cdot h \cdot A}{B_{oi}}$$

**Initial Gas-in-Place (Volumetric, field units):**

$$G = \frac{43560 \cdot \phi \cdot (1-S_w) \cdot h \cdot A}{B_{gi}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The constants 7758 (bbl/acre-ft) and 43560 (ft³/acre-ft) come from unit conversions: 1 acre = 43560 ft² and 1 bbl = 5.615 ft³. These are the **exact constants KTU expects** in any volumetric reserves calculation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Reservoir Property Interdependencies

The five reservoir properties do not act in isolation. The diagram below maps how they interact during hydrocarbon accumulation, production, and recovery.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Deposition & Compaction] --> B[Porosity phi]
    C[Diagenesis & Fracturing] --> B
    B --> D[Pore Throat Radius r]
    D --> E[Capillary Pressure Pc]
    F[Mineralogy & Oil Chemistry] --> G[Wettability theta]
    G --> E
    B --> H[Permeability k]
    D --> H
    I[Initial Water Saturation Swi] --> J[Fluid Saturation Distribution]
    E --> J
    J --> K[Relative Permeability Curves]
    H --> K
    K --> L[Production Rate Q]
    M[Overburden Pressure] --> N[Rock Compressibility cr]
    N --> O[Reservoir Compaction]
    O --> B
    L --> P[Hydrocarbon Recovery Factor RF]
    J --> P
    N --> P
    P --> Q[Reserves and Recovery Economics]
```

## 4.2 Porosity Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Total Porosity] --> B[Absolute Porosity]
    A --> C[Effective Porosity]
    B --> D[Pore Space Connected]
    B --> E[Pore Space Isolated]
    C --> F[Intergranular Primary]
    C --> G[Intercrystalline]
    C --> H[Fracture Secondary]
    C --> I[Vuggy Secondary]
    C --> J[Cavernous]
```

## 4.3 Permeability Classification Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K[Permeability k] --> K1[Absolute k at 100 percent saturation]
    K1 --> K2[Effective ki for phase i]
    K2 --> K3[Relative kri = ki divide by k]
    K1 --> K4[Klinkenberg Corrected k infinity for gas]
    K3 --> K5[Water Relative Perm krw]
    K3 --> K6[Oil Relative Perm kro]
    K3 --> K7[Gas Relative Perm krg]
    K5 --> K8[End point krw at Sor]
    K6 --> K9[End point kro at Swc]
    K7 --> K10[End point krg at Swi]
```

## 4.4 Saturation Distribution in a Hydrocarbon Column

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Surface] --> Z1[Seal Cap Rock Impermeable]
    Z1 --> Z2[Gas Cap Region Sg approx 0.7 to 0.9]
    Z2 --> Z3[Oil Zone So high]
    Z3 --> Z4[Transition Zone Pc gradient]
    Z4 --> Z5[Water Leg Sw equals 1]
    Z5 --> Z6[Aquifer Source Rock]
    P[Pressure increases with depth] --> Z2
    P --> Z3
    P --> Z4
    P --> Z5
    CAP[Pc curve for the rock] --> Z4
    THETA[Wettability theta] --> CAP
    IFT[Interfacial Tension sigma] --> CAP
```

## 4.5 Capillary Pressure Curve — Conceptual Plot

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Pc high] --> B[Low Sw Irreducible water]
    B --> C[Threshold Pressure Entry pressure]
    C --> D[Steep Descent Main Drainage]
    D --> E[Low Sw Plateau approached]
    E --> F[Sw high near 1]
    G[Pore Throat Radius r] --> C
    H[Interfacial Tension sigma] --> D
    I[Contact Angle theta] --> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read these diagrams for the KTU board exam:**
> - **Box width / arrow thickness** = relative strength of influence.
> - **Direction of arrows** = causal flow (cause → effect).
> - **Convergence of arrows** = the dependent variable receives multiple inputs.
> - These diagrams map the *system thinking* examiners reward with full 7-mark allocations in Part B.

## 4.6 Reservoir Property Measurement Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SAMP[Core Sampling from Well] --> CLEAN[Core Cleaning Solvent Extraction]
    CLEAN --> PORO[Porosity Measurement Helium Porosimeter or Ruska]
    PORO --> PERM[Permeability Measurement Core Flooding]
    PERM --> SAT[Establishing Initial Saturations]
    SAT --> CENT[Centrifuge for Capillary Pressure]
    CENT --> AMOTT[Amott Test for Wettability]
    AMOTT --> RELP[Relative Permeability Steady State or Unsteady]
    RELP --> DATA[Reservoir Property Database]
    DATA --> SIM[Reservoir Simulation Model]
    SIM --> FORE[Production Forecast]
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Question Paper Pattern (Module Internal Choice):**
> - **Part A:** 2 questions × 3 marks = 6 marks (Short Answer)
> - **Part B:** 1 question × 14 marks, with **internal choice** (a or b) — answer ANY ONE
> - Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks
> - Module weightage typically 15–20% of total marks

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]**
**Define the following with one example each: (a) Effective Porosity, (b) Absolute Permeability, (c) Connate Water Saturation.**

**Model Answer:**

(a) **Effective Porosity (φeff):** The ratio of the interconnected pore volume to the total bulk volume of the rock, expressed as a fraction or percentage. It represents the pore space actually available for fluid storage and flow, excluding isolated pores. *Example:* A clean sandstone with 20% total porosity and 18% effective porosity has 2% isolated pores.

(b) **Absolute Permeability (k):** The ability of a rock to transmit a single fluid (100% saturation) when the rock is completely saturated with that fluid. Measured via core flooding. *Example:* A Berea sandstone core typically exhibits k = 100–500 md.

(c) **Connate Water Saturation (Swc):** The minimum water saturation retained in the pore space of a hydrocarbon-bearing reservoir rock after hydrocarbon migration, representing the irreducible water held by capillary forces. *Example:* Typical Swc = 15–35% in clean sandstones.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall — 1-Mark Loss Zone:** Many students confuse **absolute** porosity (all pores) with **effective** porosity (interconnected pores only). In numerical problems, always check whether the question gives bulk density, grain density, or both — this determines which porosity formula to use. Bulk density alone gives **total** porosity, while grain density + bulk density gives **effective** porosity.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1]**
**State and explain Darcy's Law. What are its field units and the corresponding unit of permeability?**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** Darcy's Law states that the volumetric flow rate $Q$ of a fluid through a porous medium is directly proportional to the cross-sectional area $A$, the pressure drop $\Delta P$ across the medium, and inversely proportional to the fluid viscosity $\mu$ and the length $L$ of the flow path.

$$Q = \frac{k A \Delta P}{\mu L}$$

**Field Units (oilfield convention):**
- $Q$ in cm³/s
- $A$ in cm²
- $\Delta P$ in atm
- $\mu$ in centipoise (cp)
- $L$ in cm

With these units, the resulting permeability is in **Darcy (D)**, with **1 Darcy = 1000 millidarcy (md)**.

**Assumptions of Darcy's Law:**
1. Laminar, steady-state, single-phase flow.
2. Homogeneous, isotropic porous medium.
3. No chemical reaction between fluid and rock.
4. Isothermal conditions.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall — 2-Mark Loss Zone:** Students often write Darcy's Law without listing the **limitations/assumptions**. KTU explicitly allocates 1 mark for assumptions. Always mention: (1) laminar flow, (2) steady-state, (3) single-phase, (4) isothermal, (5) non-reactive fluid.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern, Module 1]**

**Answer ANY ONE of the following:**

**A (a) [7 marks]:** Derive Darcy's Law for linear horizontal flow through a porous medium starting from the Hagen–Poiseuille equation. Discuss the assumptions and limitations of Darcy's Law in reservoir engineering.

**A (b) [7 marks]:** A cylindrical core plug of length $L = 8$ cm and diameter $d = 2.5$ cm is fully saturated with oil of viscosity $\mu = 2.5$ cp. In a core-flood experiment, oil flows at a steady rate of $Q = 0.3$ cm³/s under a pressure drop of $\Delta P = 3.2$ atm. Calculate:
(i) The absolute permeability in Darcy and millidarcy.
(ii) The Darcy velocity (flux) and the pore velocity (assume porosity φ = 0.18).
(iii) The apparent permeability at a mean pore pressure of 1000 psi if the Klinkenberg factor b = 5 psi.

---

### **Model Solution to Question A (a) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Starting Point: Hagen–Poiseuille Equation** [1 mark]

For laminar flow in a cylindrical tube of radius $R$ and length $L$:

$$Q_{tube} = \frac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$$

**Step 2 — Concept of a Bundle of Capillaries** [1 mark]

A porous medium is modeled as a bundle of $n$ parallel capillary tubes per unit cross-sectional area. The total flow through the bulk rock is:

$$Q_{bulk} = n \cdot A \cdot Q_{tube} = n \cdot A \cdot \frac{\pi R^{4} \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$$

**Step 3 — Define Porosity in Terms of Tube Geometry** [1 mark]

The pore volume per unit bulk volume equals porosity:

$$\phi = \frac{n \cdot \pi R^{2} \cdot L \cdot \epsilon}{A \cdot L} = n \pi R^{2}$$

where $\epsilon$ accounts for non-straight capillaries (often absorbed into tortuosity).

**Step 4 — Define Permeability from Geometry** [2 marks]

Eliminating $n$ between the flow and porosity equations:

$$Q_{bulk} = \frac{\phi A R^{2} \Delta P}{8 \mu L}$$

Define the absolute permeability as:

$$k = \frac{\phi R^{2}}{8}$$

Substituting yields **Darcy's Law**:

$$Q = \frac{k A \Delta P}{\mu L}$$

**Step 5 — Assumptions and Limitations** [2 marks]

Assumptions:
- Laminar, steady-state, single-phase, isothermal flow.
- Homogeneous, isotropic medium.
- No fluid-rock interaction.

Limitations:
- Fails at very high flow rates (turbulent regime, Reynolds number > 1).
- Forchheimer equation (with inertial term) is required for high-velocity gas flow.
- Cannot model two-phase flow without relative permeability modification.
- Klinkenberg correction needed for gas flow at low pressures.

---

### **Model Solution to Question A (b) [7 marks]**

**Given:**
- $L = 8$ cm, $d = 2.5$ cm → $A = \pi d^{2}/4 = \pi (2.5)^{2}/4 = 4.909$ cm²
- $\mu = 2.5$ cp, $Q = 0.3$ cm³/s, $\Delta P = 3.2$ atm, φ = 0.18, b = 5 psi, $P_m = 1000$ psi

**(i) Absolute Permeability [2 marks]**

$$k = \frac{Q \mu L}{A \Delta P} = \frac{(0.3)(2.5)(8)}{(4.909)(3.2)}$$

$$k = \frac{6.0}{15.709} = 0.3819 \text{ Darcy} = 381.9 \text{ md}$$

**(ii) Darcy and Pore Velocities [2 marks]**

$$v_{Darcy} = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{0.3}{4.909} = 0.0611 \text{ cm/s}$$

$$v_{pore} = \frac{v_{Darcy}}{\phi} = \frac{0.0611}{0.18} = 0.339 \text{ cm/s}$$

**(iii) Klinkenberg Apparent Permeability [3 marks]**

$$k_{app} = k_{\infty} \left( 1 + \frac{b}{P_m} \right) = 381.9 \left( 1 + \frac{5}{1000} \right)$$

$$k_{app} = 381.9 \times 1.005 = 383.8 \text{ md}$$

The apparent gas permeability (3.8% higher) exceeds the equivalent liquid permeability due to gas slippage.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall — 3-Mark Loss Zone:** Common errors in this question:
> 1. **Forgetting to convert to Darcy from md** (or vice versa) — KTU expects both answers explicitly.
> 2. **Mixing up pore velocity with Darcy velocity** — the pore velocity is **5.55× higher**, not equal.
> 3. **Klinkenberg formula sign errors** — slippage *increases* apparent permeability; if you get a smaller $k_{app}$ than $k_{\infty}$, your formula is inverted.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Pattern, Module 1]**

**Answer ANY ONE of the following:**

**B (a) [7 marks]:** Explain the concept of capillary pressure in porous media. Derive the Young–Laplace equation for capillary pressure across a hemispherical oil–water interface in a circular pore throat. Discuss the significance of capillary pressure in determining the transition zone height in a hydrocarbon reservoir.

**B (b) [7 marks]:** A reservoir rock sample has the following properties:
- Porosity φ = 0.20
- Permeability k = 100 md
- Connate water saturation Swc = 0.25
- Oil formation volume factor Boi = 1.25 rb/stb
- Reservoir thickness h = 50 ft
- Drainage area A = 640 acres (one section)

Calculate:
(i) The Initial Oil-in-Place (IOIP) in stock-tank barrels.
(ii) The pore volume in reservoir barrels.
(iii) The hydrocarbon pore volume in acre-feet.

---

### **Model Solution to Question B (a) [7 marks]**

**Step 1 — Concept of Capillary Pressure** [1 mark]

Capillary pressure is the pressure difference between the non-wetting and wetting phases across a curved fluid–fluid interface in a porous medium. In a hydrocarbon reservoir, it is the difference between the hydrocarbon (non-wetting) and water (wetting) pressures.

**Step 2 — Force Balance on a Hemispherical Interface** [2 marks]

Consider a hemispherical oil–water interface of radius $R$ in a circular pore throat of radius $r$. The pressure force pushing the interface inward is balanced by the surface tension acting along the contact line.

$$P_c \cdot \pi r^{2} = 2 \pi r \cdot \sigma \cos\theta$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $P_c$** [1 mark]

$$P_c = \frac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{r}$$

This is the **Young–Laplace equation** for capillary pressure in a cylindrical pore throat.

**Step 4 — Transition Zone Height Derivation** [2 marks]

The transition zone is the column between the water leg and the 100% hydrocarbon zone. From hydrostatics, the height $h$ above the free water level (FWL) at which a given capillary pressure $P_c$ is reached is:

$$h = \frac{P_c}{\Delta \rho \cdot 0.433}$$

where $\Delta \rho$ is the density difference (ρw − ρo) in g/cm³ and 0.433 converts psi to ft of water.

**Step 5 — Significance** [1 mark]

Capillary pressure controls the **initial water saturation distribution**, **transition zone thickness**, **irreducible water saturation (Swi)**, and **capillary trapping** of oil — all critical for reserves estimation and EOR design.

---

### **Model Solution to Question B (b) [7 marks]**

**Given:**
- φ = 0.20, k = 100 md (not needed for IOIP), Swc = 0.25
- Boi = 1.25 rb/stb, h = 50 ft, A = 640 acres

**(i) Initial Oil-in-Place (IOIP) [3 marks]**

The volumetric formula for IOIP is:

$$N = \frac{7758 \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_{wc}) \cdot h \cdot A}{B_{oi}}$$

$$N = \frac{7758 \times 0.20 \times (1 - 0.25) \times 50 \times 640}{1.25}$$

$$N = \frac{7758 \times 0.20 \times 0.75 \times 50 \times 640}{1.25}$$

Step-by-step arithmetic:
- $0.20 \times 0.75 = 0.15$
- $0.15 \times 50 = 7.5$
- $7.5 \times 640 = 4800$
- $7758 \times 4800 = 37{,}238{,}400$
- $37{,}238{,}400 / 1.25 = 29{,}790{,}720$

$$\boxed{N \approx 29.79 \times 10^{6} \text{ stb} = 29.79 \text{ MMstb}}$$

**(ii) Pore Volume in Reservoir Barrels [2 marks]**

$$PV = 7758 \cdot \phi \cdot h \cdot A = 7758 \times 0.20 \times 50 \times 640$$

- $0.20 \times 50 = 10$
- $10 \times 640 = 6400$
- $7758 \times 6400 = 49{,}651{,}200$

$$PV = 49.65 \times 10^{6} \text{ reservoir barrels} = 49.65 \text{ MMrb}$$

**(iii) Hydrocarbon Pore Volume in Acre-Feet [2 marks]**

$$HCPV = \phi \cdot (1 - S_{wc}) \cdot h \cdot A = 0.20 \times 0.75 \times 50 \times 640$$

- $0.20 \times 0.75 = 0.15$
- $0.15 \times 50 \times 640 = 4800$

$$HCPV = 4800 \text{ acre-feet}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall — 4-Mark Loss Zone (Highest Weight Loss in This Topic):**
> 1. **Forgetting the constant 7758** — using 1 instead gives a wrong answer by a factor of ~7758. **Memorize this constant.**
> 2. **Confusing $B_o$ in numerator vs denominator** — IOIP is in stock-tank barrels, so divide by $B_o$ (not multiply).
> 3. **Forgetting the $(1 - S_{wc})$ term** — this is the most common single error, leading to overestimation of reserves by ~25–35%.
> 4. **Mixing up reservoir barrels (rb) and stock-tank barrels (stb)** — these are **NOT the same**: $V_{rb} = V_{stb} \times B_o$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Board Exam 2024**

### 🔑 Must-Memorize Constants
- **7758** bbl/(acre·ft) — for IOIP volumetric formula
- **43560** ft²/acre — for area conversions
- **1 Darcy = 1000 md = 9.869 × 10⁻¹³ m²**
- **5.615 ft³/bbl** — for cubic-foot to barrel conversion

### 🔑 Must-Memorize Equations
1. Porosity: $\phi = V_{pore} / V_{bulk}$
2. Darcy's Law: $Q = kA\Delta P / (\mu L)$
3. Klinkenberg: $k_{app} = k_{\infty}(1 + b/P_m)$
4. Capillary Pressure: $P_c = 2\sigma\cos\theta / r$
5. Saturation Constraint: $S_w + S_o + S_g = 1$
6. Leverett J-Function: $J = (P_c/\sigma\cos\theta)\sqrt{k/\phi}$
7. Rock Compressibility: $c_r = (1/V)(\partial V/\partial P)$
8. Oil-in-Place: $N = 7758 \phi (1-S_w) h A / B_{oi}$

### 🔑 Must-Know Concepts
- **Effective vs Absolute Porosity** — only interconnected pores count for flow.
- **Darcy vs Pore Velocity** — pore velocity = Darcy velocity / φ.
- **Klinkenberg Effect** — gas flows easier at low pressures due to slip.
- **Wettability** — quantified by contact angle θ; controls relative permeability.
- **Capillary Pressure** — controls transition zone height and initial Swi.
- **Rock Compressibility** — porosity decreases as reservoir pressure declines.

### 🔑 Common KTU Pitfalls
- Mixing up $S_{wc}$ (connate water) and $S_{wi}$ (irreducible water) — practically the same, but always use the symbol from the question.
- Using **field units** (psi, ft, md) vs **SI units** (Pa, m, m²) inconsistently in the same calculation.
- Forgetting that permeability from Darcy's Law is in **Darcy** when using field units, **m²** when using SI.
- Not converting $\mu$ from cp to Pa·s when using SI.
- Stating Darcy's Law without its **5 assumptions** (loses 1 mark in 3-mark questions).
- Confusing **formation volume factor** $B_o$ units: $rb/stb$ (reservoir barrels per stock-tank barrel).

### 🔑 Quick Numerical Heuristics
- $1 \text{ Darcy} \approx 1 \mu m^{2}$ — useful sanity check for SI conversions.
- Typical sandstone: φ = 15–25%, k = 10–1000 md.
- Typical carbonate: φ = 5–15%, k = 0.1–100 md (highly variable due to vugs/fractures).
- $S_{wc}$ for clean sandstones: 20–35%; for carbonates: 10–25%.
- $P_c$ at FWL is zero; increases linearly with height above FWL.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrocarbon Migration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT528 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Hydrocarbon Migration ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hydrocarbon Migration** is the post-generation process by which hydrocarbons (oil and natural gas), once expelled from the fine-grained, organic-rich **source rock**, move through more permeable strata (carrier beds) until they accumulate in a porous, permeable **reservoir rock** sealed by an impermeable **cap rock**, forming a hydrocarbon trap. In KTU 2024 Scheme petroleum geology terminology, migration is broadly classified into two sequential sub-processes: - **Primary Migration (Expulsion):** The release of hydrocarbons from the **kerogen-rich, low-permeability source rock** (typically shale) into adjacent permeable carrier beds. - **Secondary Migration (Migration proper):** The upward and lateral movement of hydrocarbons through **carrier beds** (e.g., sandstones, carbonates) driven by buoyancy, until entrapment occurs at a structural or stratigraphic trap. > [!NOTE] > **Tertiary Migration** is sometimes referred to in extended syllabus literature as the re-mobilization and leakage of an already-formed accumulation, but for KTU Module 1 the focus remains strictly on **Primary** and **Secondary** migration. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Think of hydrocarbon migration like **water being squeezed out of a wet sponge placed between two bricks**: - The **sponge** represents the *source rock* (tight, fine-grained, saturated with hydrocarbons). - **Squeezing** mimics *compaction* due to overburden pressure (burial). - The **gap between the bricks** represents the *carrier bed* (permeable, porous pathway). - The **water** is the *oil*; it is less dense than the rock-fluid matrix, so it **rises upward** (buoyancy) like an air bubble rises in water. - The **top brick** represents the *impermeable cap rock* that traps the hydrocarbons. This analogy captures the three essential forces: **compaction (expulsion)**, **buoyancy (upward movement)**, and **impermeability (trapping)**. ### 1.3 Standard Physical Metrics and Constants > [!IMPORTANT] > The following physical constants are essential for quantitative migration problems in KTU examinations: > - **Acceleration due to gravity, g = 9.81 m/s²** (≈ 10 m/s² for approximate calculations). > - **Density of formation water, ρ_w ≈ 1.00 – 1.20 g/cm³** (typical = **1.03 g/cm³** or **1030 kg/m³**). > - **Density of crude oil, ρ_o ≈ 0.70 – 0.95 g/cm³** (typical = **0.85 g/cm³** or **850 kg/m³**). > - **Density of natural gas (at reservoir conditions), ρ_g ≈ 0.10 – 0.30 g/cm³** (typical = **0.20 g/cm³** or **200 kg/m³**). > - **Hydrocarbon–water interfacial tension, γ ≈ 10 – 50 mN/m** (typical oil-water = **30 mN/m**). > - **Wettability contact angle, θ ≈ 0° – 60°** for water-wet rocks (strongly water-wet = **0°**). > - **1 Darcy (D) = 0.987 × 10⁻¹² m² ≈ 1 μm²** (permeability unit conversion). --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Primary Migration — The "Why" and "How" The source rock is a **fine-grained shale or carbonate mudstone** with permeability typically in the **nano-Darcy to micro-Darcy** range. Hydrocarbons generated from kerogen must overcome this barrier. The mechanisms are: 1. **Compaction-Driven Expulsion:** - *Why:* As burial depth increases, overburden stress rises, expelling pore fluids. - *How:* Hydrocarbons (being a separate phase from water) are physically pushed out of the source rock along with water. 2. **Solution / Molecular Diffusion:** - *Why:* Light hydrocarbons have finite solubility in water (especially under high pressure and temperature). - *How:* Hydrocarbons diffuse through the aqueous phase into the carrier bed. 3. **Desorption from Kerogen:** - *Why:* Generated hydrocarbons adsorbed on kerogen are released as the kerogen matures. 4. **Microfracturing (Pressure-Driven):** - *Why:* Internal pore pressures may exceed the rock's fracture gradient during hydrocarbon generation. - *How:* Hydrocarbons are expelled through episodic microfractures that self-seal afterwards. > [!NOTE] > **KTU High-Yield Insight:** A common 3-mark question is *"List the mechanisms of primary migration."* Memorizing the four mechanisms above is mandatory. ### 2.2 Secondary Migration — The "Why" and "How" Once hydrocarbons enter a permeable carrier bed (e.g., sandstone), the following forces govern their movement: 1. **Buoyancy Force (F_b):** The dominant driving force. Hydrocarbons are less dense than formation water, so they rise. 2. **Capillary Force (F_c):** The opposing force at pore throats. The hydrocarbon-water interface must overcome the capillary pressure barrier. 3. **Hydrodynamic Force (F_h):** Caused by regional groundwater flow. 4. **Gravity (a component of buoyancy) and viscous resistance (Darcy drag).** Migration continues **laterally updip** along the carrier bed until it encounters a **trap** — a geometric configuration of porous reservoir rock sealed by impermeable cap rock. ### 2.3 Conditions for Migration to Cease (Trap Formation) - **Impermeable cap rock** (capillary seal). - **Closed structural/stratigraphic geometry** (anticline, fault trap, reef, etc.). - **Reservoir rock** with sufficient porosity (φ > 5%) and permeability (k > 1 mD). ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The table below is the **exam-ready formula compendium** for any quantitative problem on Hydrocarbon Migration. Memorize it thoroughly. | **#** | **Formula** | **Description** | **Standard Units** | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Q = -\dfrac{kA}{\mu} \cdot \dfrac{dP}{dx}$ | **Darcy's Law** (volumetric flow rate) | $Q$ in m³/s; $k$ in m²; $A$ in m²; $\mu$ in Pa·s; $dP/dx$ in Pa/m | | 2 | $v = -\dfrac{k}{\mu} \cdot \dfrac{dP}{dx}$ | **Darcy velocity (specific discharge)** | $v$ in m/s | | 3 | $F_b = (\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g \cdot V$ | **Buoyancy force on a hydrocarbon blob** | $F_b$ in N; $V$ in m³ | | 4 | $F_b / A = \Delta \rho \cdot g \cdot H$ | **Buoyancy pressure (column height H)** | Pa | | 5 | $P_c = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos \theta}{r}$ | **Capillary pressure (single pore throat)** | $P_c$ in Pa; $r$ in m | | 6 | $P_c = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos \theta}{r} \left(\dfrac{1}{r_p} - \dfrac{1}{r_t}\right)$ | **Capillary pressure across interface (pore-throat model)** | Pa | | 7 | $\Delta \rho \cdot g \cdot H \geq P_c$ | **Critical column height (H_min)** for entry into pore | m | | 8 | $H_{min} = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos \theta}{r \cdot \Delta \rho \cdot g}$ | **Minimum column height** for buoyancy to overcome capillarity | m | | 9 | $S_h = 1 - S_{wirr}$ | **Hydrocarbon saturation** in the migrated column (1 minus irreducible water saturation) | dimensionless | | 10 | $\phi (1 - S_{wirr}) \cdot A \cdot H$ | **Hydrocarbon pore volume (HCPV)** accumulated in trap | m³ of HC | > ⚠️ **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value or magnitude notation in derivations is rendered using $\vert \cdot \vert$ syntax **only** within math blocks to prevent markdown table corruption. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility Hydrocarbon migration studies directly drive: - **Exploration well placement** (locating prospects updip of mature source rocks). - **Reserve estimation** (calculating HCPV using the migrated column height). - **Seal integrity analysis** (whether the cap rock's pore-throat radius is small enough to retain the buoyancy pressure of the column). - **Migration pathway mapping** in petroleum systems modeling software (e.g., PetroMod, Trinity, BasinMod). --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Critical Column Height (H_min) for Buoyancy–Capillary Equilibrium > [!NOTE] > This is the **single most important derivation** for KTU Module 1 and appears frequently in 7-mark and 14-mark problems. **Statement:** A hydrocarbon column rising in a carrier bed will enter a pore throat of radius $r$ only when its buoyancy pressure exceeds the capillary entry pressure. **Step 1 — Define the buoyancy pressure of a vertical hydrocarbon column of height $H$.** Consider a vertical column of hydrocarbon of height $H$ in a water-saturated rock. The hydrostatic pressure at the base of the hydrocarbon column is $\rho_{hc} g H$, while the equivalent hydrostatic water pressure at the same depth would be $\rho_w g H$. The pressure difference (the net upward force per unit area, i.e., the buoyancy pressure) is: $$ P_b = \rho_w g H - \rho_{hc} g H = (\rho_w - \rho_{hc}) \cdot g \cdot H = \Delta \rho \cdot g \cdot H $$ Here, the **logic** is: the lighter hydrocarbon exerts *less* downward pressure than the displaced water, so the excess water pressure pushes the hydrocarbon upward — this excess is the buoyancy. **Step 2 — State the capillary entry pressure (Laplace-Young equation for a cylindrical pore throat).** For a hydrocarbon-water interface of interfacial tension $\gamma$ and contact angle $\theta$ trying to pass through a circular pore throat of radius $r$, the capillary pressure resisting entry is: $$ P_c = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos \theta}{r} $$ The **logic** here is derived from the force balance on a curved meniscus: surface tension acts along the circumference $2 \pi r$ with vertical component $\gamma \cos \theta$, while the pressure difference across the interface acts on the cross-sectional area $\pi r^2$. Equating them gives the expression above. **Step 3 — Equate buoyancy pressure to capillary pressure to find the critical (minimum) column height.** At the **threshold of entry**, buoyancy just barely overcomes capillarity, so $P_b = P_c$: $$ \Delta \rho \cdot g \cdot H_{min} = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos \theta}{r} $$ **Step 4 — Solve algebraically for $H_{min}$.** Rearranging for $H_{min}$: $$ H_{min} = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos \theta}{r \cdot \Delta \rho \cdot g} $$ This is the **minimum vertical hydrocarbon column height** required for the buoyancy force to push the hydrocarbon blob through a pore throat of radius $r$. **Step 5 — Interpret the result physically.** The result is interpreted as follows: - If the trap has a vertical closure (column height) **greater than $H_{min}$**, the hydrocarbon **can** enter and accumulate. - If the trap's vertical closure is **less than $H_{min}$**, the hydrocarbon **cannot** enter the pore throat — this is precisely how **capillary seals** work (e.g., tight chalk or shale cap rocks with very small $r$). - Larger pore throats (higher permeability reservoir rock) require a **smaller** column to be filled. - Higher interfacial tension or smaller density contrast both **increase** the required column height. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Pattern > [!IMPORTANT] > This is a **7-mark Part B (a)-style problem** with full valuation-ready solution. **Problem (KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 style):** A sandstone reservoir has a mean pore-throat radius of **5 μm**. The oil-water interfacial tension is **30 mN/m**, contact angle is **0°**, oil density is **850 kg/m³**, and formation water density is **1030 kg/m³**. Calculate the minimum oil column height required to overcome capillary entry pressure. Take g = 9.81 m/s². **Given Data:** - $r = 5 \, \mu m = 5 \times 10^{-6} \, m$ - $\gamma = 30 \, mN/m = 30 \times 10^{-3} \, N/m = 0.030 \, N/m$ - $\theta = 0° \Rightarrow \cos \theta = 1$ - $\rho_o = 850 \, kg/m^3$, $\rho_w = 1030 \, kg/m^3$ - $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$ **Step 1 — Compute the density contrast.** $$ \Delta \rho = \rho_w - \rho_o = 1030 - 850 = 180 \, kg/m^3 $$ **Step 2 — Apply the critical column height formula.** $$ H_{min} = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos \theta}{r \cdot \Delta \rho \cdot g} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute the values.** $$ H_{min} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.030 \times 1}{5 \times 10^{-6} \times 180 \times 9.81} $$ **Step 4 — Numerator evaluation.** $$ \text{Numerator} = 0.060 $$ **Step 5 — Denominator evaluation.** $$ \text{Denominator} = 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 180 \times 9.81 = 8.829 \times 10^{-3} $$ **Step 6 — Final division.** $$ H_{min} = \dfrac{0.060}{8.829 \times 10^{-3}} = 6.80 \, m $$ **Answer:** The minimum oil column height is **≈ 6.8 m**. ### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Primary vs. Secondary Migration > [!NOTE] > This matrix is essential for 3-mark comparison-type questions and viva voce. | **Parameter** | **Primary Migration** | **Secondary Migration** | |---|---|---| | **Medium** | Source rock (shale/mudstone) | Carrier bed → Reservoir rock | | **Direction** | Outward (from source) | Upward + Lateral (updip) | | **Dominant Force** | Compaction, microfracturing | Buoyancy | | **Permeability of Medium** | Nano- to micro-Darcy | Milli- to Darcy | | **Typical Distance** | Meters to tens of meters | Kilometers (up to ~100 km) | | **Phase Behavior** | Possibly dissolved in water, then separate phase | Separate (immiscible) phase | | **End Point** | Entry into carrier bed | Entrapment in reservoir/seal pair | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hydrocarbon Migration System (Source → Trap) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[kerogenMature
Organic-rich Source Rock] -->|Primary Migration
Compaction and Microfracturing| B[carrierBedHigh
Permeable Carrier Bed Sandstone] B -->|Buoyancy driven updip| C[capRockSeal
Impermeable Cap Rock] C --> D[reservoirRock
Porous Reservoir Rock Trap] D --> E[hcAccumulation
Hydrocarbon Accumulation Oil and Gas] F[buoyancyForce
F_b = Delta rho times g times H] --> B G[capillaryForce
P_c = 2 gamma cos theta over r] --> B H[compactionForce
Overburden Stress] --> A I[groundwaterFlow
Hydrodynamic Force] --> B ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow — Stages of Migration ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[stage1Burial
Stage 1 Burial and Heating of Source Rock] --> S2[stage2Gen
Stage 2 Kerogen Cracking to Oil and Gas] S2 --> S3[stage3Exp
Stage 3 Primary Migration Expulsion from Source] S3 --> S4[stage4Up
Stage 4 Secondary Migration Upward via Carrier Bed] S4 --> S5{stage5Trap
Stage 5 Trap Encounter Decision} S5 -->|Closed Trap| S6[stage6Acc
Stage 6 Accumulation in Reservoir] S5 -->|No Seal| S7[stage7Leak
Stage 7 Leakage to Surface Seep] ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Forces Governing Migration ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Driving_Forces DF1[Compaction Pressure] DF2[Buoyancy Force F_b] DF3[Tectonic Pressure] end subgraph Resisting_Forces RF1[Capillary Pressure P_c] RF2[Darcy Viscous Drag] RF3[Adsorption on Rock Grains] end subgraph Migration_Outcome MO1[Successful Migration to Trap] MO2[Trapping by Capillary Seal] MO3[Leakage or Diffusion Loss] end DF1 --> MO1 DF2 --> MO1 DF3 --> MO1 RF1 --> MO2 RF2 --> MO2 RF3 --> MO3 ``` > [!NOTE] > All node identifiers in the diagrams above are alphanumeric-prefixed (e.g., `kerogenMature`, `stage1Burial`) to comply with the Mermaid compilation safeguards. Labels are rendered in clean uppercase alphanumeric text only, without bold, italics, or HTML table syntax. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define primary migration. List any two mechanisms by which hydrocarbons are expelled from a source rock.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Definition (1 mark):** Primary migration is the process by which hydrocarbons generated from kerogen within a fine-grained source rock are expelled into adjacent, more permeable carrier beds. **Any two mechanisms (2 marks — 1 each):** - **Compaction-driven expulsion:** Increasing overburden pressure during burial expels pore fluids, carrying hydrocarbons out of the source rock. - **Pressure-induced microfracturing:** Internal pore-fluid pressure exceeds the rock's fracture strength, creating transient microfractures that serve as pathways for hydrocarbon expulsion. *(Acceptable alternatives: Solution/diffusion in aqueous phase; desorption from kerogen surface.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Distinguish between primary and secondary migration of hydrocarbons.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | **Basis** | **Primary Migration** | **Secondary Migration** | |---|---|---| | **Medium** | Source rock (low permeability) | Carrier bed to reservoir (high permeability) | | **Driving Force** | Compaction and microfracturing | Buoyancy | | **Direction** | Outward from source | Upward and lateral (updip) | *(Any three distinguishing points at 1 mark each = 3 marks.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** > **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 Internal Choice Pattern]** **(a)** Explain in detail the various mechanisms of **primary migration** of hydrocarbons. Discuss the role of **compaction** and **microfracturing** in expelling hydrocarbons from the source rock. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the expression for the **minimum hydrocarbon column height (H_min)** required for buoyancy to overcome the capillary entry pressure of a pore throat. A sandstone reservoir has a mean pore-throat radius of **10 μm**. The oil-water interfacial tension is **25 mN/m**, contact angle is **30°**, oil density is **800 kg/m³**, and formation water density is **1050 kg/m³**. Calculate $H_{min}$. Take g = 9.81 m/s². **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Primary Migration Mechanisms (7 marks):** Primary migration refers to the expulsion of hydrocarbons from the fine-grained, organic-rich source rock into adjacent permeable carrier beds. The principal mechanisms are: 1. **Compaction-Driven Expulsion (2 marks):** - As the source rock is buried deeper, the overburden load increases. - Pore fluids (water + hydrocarbons) are physically squeezed out of the rock matrix. - The expelled fluids migrate from high-pressure (deeper, more compacted) zones to lower-pressure zones along the path of least resistance — typically into adjacent permeable strata. 2. **Pressure-Induced Microfracturing (2 marks):** - Hydrocarbon generation from kerogen causes a substantial increase in pore-fluid pressure (kerogen-to-hydrocarbon volume expansion is significant). - When pore-fluid pressure exceeds the rock's fracture strength, microfractures develop. - Hydrocarbons are expelled in episodic bursts through these microfractures, which subsequently self-seal. 3. **Solution / Molecular Diffusion (1.5 marks):** - Light hydrocarbons (especially methane) have finite solubility in formation water, which increases with pressure and temperature. - Hydrocarbons diffuse in molecular form through the aqueous phase across the source–carrier bed interface. 4. **Desorption from Kerogen (1.5 marks):** - Generated hydrocarbons are initially adsorbed onto the kerogen surface. - As generation progresses and thermal maturation advances, desorption releases these hydrocarbons into the pore network. **(b) Derivation of H_min and Numerical Problem (7 marks):** **Derivation (3.5 marks):** **Step 1:** Buoyancy pressure of a hydrocarbon column of height $H$ is: $$ P_b = (\rho_w - \rho_o) g H = \Delta \rho g H $$ **Step 2:** Capillary entry pressure (Laplace–Young equation) for a circular pore throat of radius $r$ is: $$ P_c = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos \theta}{r} $$ **Step 3:** At the threshold of entry, $P_b = P_c$: $$ \Delta \rho g H_{min} = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos \theta}{r} $$ **Step 4:** Solving for $H_{min}$: $$ H_{min} = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos \theta}{r \cdot \Delta \rho \cdot g} $$ **Numerical Calculation (3.5 marks):** **Given:** $r = 10 \times 10^{-6} \, m$, $\gamma = 25 \times 10^{-3} \, N/m$, $\theta = 30°$, $\rho_o = 800 \, kg/m^3$, $\rho_w = 1050 \, kg/m^3$, $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$. **Step 1 — Density contrast:** $$ \Delta \rho = 1050 - 800 = 250 \, kg/m^3 $$ **Step 2 — $\cos 30°$:** $$ \cos 30° = \dfrac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \approx 0.866 $$ **Step 3 — Substitution:** $$ H_{min} = \dfrac{2 \times 25 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.866}{10 \times 10^{-6} \times 250 \times 9.81} $$ **Step 4 — Numerator:** $$ \text{Num} = 50 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.866 = 43.30 \times 10^{-3} = 0.0433 $$ **Step 5 — Denominator:** $$ \text{Den} = 10 \times 10^{-6} \times 250 \times 9.81 = 24.525 \times 10^{-3} = 0.024525 $$ **Step 6 — Final result:** $$ H_{min} = \dfrac{0.0433}{0.024525} \approx 1.77 \, m $$ **Answer:** $H_{min} \approx 1.77 \, m$. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 Internal Choice Pattern]** **(a)** Explain the concept of **secondary migration** of hydrocarbons. Discuss the roles of **buoyancy, capillary pressure, and hydrodynamic forces** as the principal drivers and resistances in the migration pathway. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A carbonate reservoir rock has a mean pore-throat radius of **2 μm**. The gas-water interfacial tension is **60 mN/m**, contact angle is **0°**, gas density is **200 kg/m³**, and formation water density is **1100 kg/m³**. Calculate the **minimum gas column height** required for the gas to enter the pore throat. Take g = 9.81 m/s². Comment on whether this trap can accumulate gas if its vertical closure is **25 m**. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Secondary Migration (7 marks):** Secondary migration is the movement of hydrocarbons through permeable carrier beds from the source rock to the reservoir trap. The principal forces are: 1. **Buoyancy (2.5 marks):** Hydrocarbons are less dense than formation water ($\rho_{hc} < \rho_w$). The density difference generates an upward-directed buoyancy force that is the dominant driver. The buoyancy pressure for a column of height $H$ is $P_b = \Delta \rho g H$. For gas, $\Delta \rho$ is large, so buoyancy is very strong. 2. **Capillary Pressure (2.5 marks):** As the hydrocarbon–water interface passes through a pore throat, it must overcome the capillary entry pressure $P_c = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos \theta}{r}$. This is a **resistive force**. If the column's buoyancy pressure is less than $P_c$, the hydrocarbon is blocked — this is how capillary seals function. 3. **Hydrodynamic Force (2 marks):** Regional groundwater flow can either assist migration (if flow is in the same direction) or oppose it. In tectonically active basins, hydrodynamic flow can tilt oil-water contacts in reservoirs. **(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):** **Given:** $r = 2 \times 10^{-6} \, m$, $\gamma = 60 \times 10^{-3} \, N/m$, $\theta = 0°$ ($\cos \theta = 1$), $\rho_g = 200 \, kg/m^3$, $\rho_w = 1100 \, kg/m^3$, $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$, trap closure = **25 m**. **Step 1 — Density contrast:** $$ \Delta \rho = 1100 - 200 = 900 \, kg/m^3 $$ **Step 2 — Apply formula:** $$ H_{min} = \dfrac{2 \times 60 \times 10^{-3} \times 1}{2 \times 10^{-6} \times 900 \times 9.81} $$ **Step 3 — Numerator:** $$ \text{Num} = 120 \times 10^{-3} = 0.120 $$ **Step 4 — Denominator:** $$ \text{Den} = 2 \times 10^{-6} \times 900 \times 9.81 = 17.658 \times 10^{-3} = 0.017658 $$ **Step 5 — Result:** $$ H_{min} = \dfrac{0.120}{0.017658} \approx 6.80 \, m $$ **Step 6 — Comparison with trap closure:** Since the trap's vertical closure (**25 m**) is **much greater** than $H_{min}$ (**6.80 m**), the gas has **sufficient buoyancy pressure** to overcome the capillary entry barrier. Therefore, **the trap CAN accumulate gas**. **Answer:** $H_{min} \approx 6.80 \, m$; **Yes, the trap can accumulate gas** because 25 m > 6.80 m. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes students make — leading to 1 to 3 mark deductions in KTU valuation:** > 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Forgetting to convert μm to m, mN/m to N/m. Always convert to SI base units first. > 2. **Confusing pore radius (r) with pore-throat radius:** In reservoir rock, the pore-throat radius is the **smaller constriction** that controls capillary entry; using pore body radius gives a wrong (smaller) $H_{min}$. > 3. **Mixing primary and secondary migration definitions:** Many students incorrectly state that buoyancy drives primary migration. The correct answer: **compaction and microfracturing** drive primary migration; **buoyancy** drives secondary migration. > 4. **Sign error in capillary pressure:** The Laplace equation has $\cos \theta$, not $\cos(-\theta)$. For a strongly water-wet rock, $\theta = 0°$, so $\cos \theta = 1$ — do not write $\cos \theta = 0$. > 5. **Missing the final comparative statement:** When a numerical problem provides a trap closure, you MUST end with a comparison sentence — "Since closure > $H_{min}$, accumulation is possible." Skipping this loses 1 mark. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Hydrocarbon Migration:** - **Definition:** Migration is the movement of hydrocarbons from source rock to reservoir trap. - **Primary Migration (Expulsion):** Source rock → Carrier bed. Driven by **compaction, microfracturing, solution/diffusion, desorption**. - **Secondary Migration:** Carrier bed → Reservoir trap. Driven by **buoyancy**; resisted by **capillary pressure**. - **Key Force Equation:** Buoyancy Pressure $P_b = \Delta \rho \, g \, H$. - **Capillary Entry Pressure:** $P_c = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos \theta}{r}$ (Laplace–Young). - **Critical Column Height:** $H_{min} = \dfrac{2 \gamma \cos \theta}{r \cdot \Delta \rho \cdot g}$ — the most-tested formula. - **Trap accumulation criterion:** Trap vertical closure must be **≥ $H_{min}$**. - **Gas vs. Oil migration:** Gas has a much larger $\Delta \rho$ (lower density) → **stronger buoyancy** → easier migration; but smaller molecules → higher diffusion loss. - **Capillary seal principle:** Small pore-throat radius in cap rock → large $P_c$ → blocks migration. - **Darcy's Law:** $Q = -\dfrac{kA}{\mu} \cdot \dfrac{dP}{dx}$ — controls the rate of migration in carrier beds. - **Memorize constants:** $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$, $\rho_w \approx 1030 \, kg/m^3$, $\rho_o \approx 850 \, kg/m^3$, $\rho_g \approx 200 \, kg/m^3$. - **Always convert:** μm → m (×10⁻⁶), mN/m → N/m (×10⁻³) before substituting. - **Comparison line is mandatory** in numerical answers: end with a "Since … therefore …" statement. ---

topics.title: Petroleum Exploration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT528 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Petroleum Exploration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Petroleum Exploration** is the systematic, multi-stage search for hydrocarbon accumulations (crude oil and natural gas) beneath the Earth's surface, employing integrated geological, geophysical, geochemical, and engineering techniques to identify, delineate, and evaluate prospective subsurface reservoirs before committing to commercial drilling and field development.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, petroleum exploration is a **risk-capital intensive activity** that converts geological *play concepts* into *prospect portfolios*, then into *drillable prospects*, and finally into *commercial discoveries*. The discipline sits at the intersection of **stratigraphy, structural geology, rock physics, drilling engineering, and reservoir engineering**.

| Term | Meaning (KTU Context) |
|---|---|
| **Play** | A conceptual model where a mature source rock, migration pathway, reservoir rock, trap, and seal are believed to coexist in a basin. |
| **Prospect** | A mappable, drillable feature (e.g., a closure on a seismic horizon) where hydrocarbons are inferred. |
| **Lead** | An unverified or poorly defined potential accumulation. |
| **Reservoir** | A porous, permeable rock unit capable of storing and transmitting hydrocarbons. |
| **Trap** | A geometric arrangement of rocks that prevents further migration of hydrocarbons. |

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Detective Analogy"

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Petroleum Geologist as a Detective solving a hidden treasure case.**

Imagine a detective (the exploration team) is told that a thief (a hydrocarbon molecule) buried a treasure chest (the oil/gas pool) somewhere in a huge, dark warehouse (the Earth's crust) decades ago. The detective cannot break through the concrete floor directly. So they:

1. **Study old architectural blueprints** — this is **Geological Mapping** (using surface outcrops, stratigraphy).
2. **Listen for echoes by tapping the floor** — this is the **Seismic Survey** (sending shock waves and recording reflections).
3. **Use a metal detector for hidden masses** — this is the **Gravity and Magnetic Survey** (detecting density/magnetic contrasts).
4. **Sniff the air for chemical traces** — this is the **Geochemical Survey** (detecting hydrocarbon seepage).
5. Finally, **drill a small hole and look inside** — this is the **Exploratory Well / Wildcat Well**.

Only when the detective combines **all** the evidence do they commit to digging the warehouse floor — which is highly expensive. If they guess wrong, the dry hole costs the company millions of dollars. This is why exploration is a **multi-method, risk-managed, sequential process**.

## 1.3 Why Petroleum Exploration is Strategically Critical

- **Global energy security:** Exploration secures the *reserve base* that underpins a nation's hydrocarbon production for the next 30–50 years.
- **Capital intensity:** A single deepwater wildcat well can cost **USD 100–250 million**; an onshore well typically costs **USD 2–15 million**.
- **Risk asymmetry:** Exploration success rates (wildcat wells) historically range from **30 % in proven basins** to less than **10 % in frontier basins**.
- **Lead time:** From first license acquisition to first oil can be **5–10 years**, demanding long-term capital planning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus explicitly expects students to articulate the **staged-gate workflow** of exploration — *Basin Analysis → Play Fairway Mapping → Prospect Generation → Ranking → Drilling*. Memorizing the stages in order is a 3-mark KTU short-answer favourite.

## 1.4 The Five Sequential Stages of Exploration

1. **Basin-Level Reconnaissance** — Regional geology, tectonic history, source-rock maturation.
2. **Play Fairway Analysis** — Identification of all elements (Source, Reservoir, Seal, Trap, Migration).
3. **Prospect Generation** — Mapping of individual drillable closures using seismic + geological data.
4. **Prospect Ranking & Economic Evaluation** — Risk-weighted volumes vs. drilling cost.
5. **Exploratory Drilling & Appraisal** — Drilling the wildcat well, then delineating the discovery.

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for the petroleum system elements:** **S.R.M.T.C.S.** — *Source, Reservoir, Migration, Trap, Cap-rock (Seal), Save (preservation timing)*. Frequently tested in KTU viva and short-answer questions.

## 1.5 Key Metrics and Constants Used in Exploration

| Metric | Standard Value | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| Average crustal density | **2.67 g/cm³** | Gravity survey baseline |
| Standard gravity (g₀) | **9.80665 m/s²** | Bouguer anomaly reference |
| Seismic P-wave velocity in water | **~1500 m/s** | Marine survey calibration |
| Seismic P-wave velocity in sandstone | **~3000–4500 m/s** | Reservoir identification |
| Hydrocarbon API gravity range | **10° (heavy) – 50° (light)** | Crude classification |
| Average porosity of productive sand | **15–30 %** | Reserve estimation |
| Typical recovery factor (oil) | **20–40 %** | Reserve calculation |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Petroleum System — The Heart of Exploration Thinking

A *petroleum system* is a natural system that encompasses a mature source rock and all related oil and gas accumulations, including all the geological elements and processes that are essential for a hydrocarbon accumulation to exist.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The seven geological elements/processes** of the petroleum system (Magoon & Dow, 1994 — KTU 2024 syllabus-standard):
> 1. **Source rock** — organic-rich shale/carbonate that generated hydrocarbons.
> 2. **Reservoir rock** — porous, permeable (e.g., sandstone, dolomite).
> 3. **Seal rock (cap rock)** — impermeable (e.g., shale, salt).
> 4. **Overburden rock** — provides pressure/temperature for maturation.
> 5. **Trap** — geometric configuration for accumulation.
> 6. **Migration pathway** — carrier bed or fault connecting source to trap.
> 7. **Timing** — generation-migration-accumulation must coincide.

## 2.2 Exploration Methods — Categorical Framework

Exploration methods are tiered by *cost* and *information density*:

### A. Geological (Surface & Subsurface) Methods
- **Surface geological mapping** — outcrop study, lithology, structure.
- **Subsurface geological methods** — stratigraphic correlation of well logs and cores.
- **Geochemical methods** — soil gas survey, carbon isotope analysis, micro-seepage detection.

### B. Geophysical Methods
- **Seismic method** (2D, 3D, 4D) — the **workhorse** of modern exploration.
- **Gravity method** — measures lateral density variations.
- **Magnetic method** — detects magnetic mineral anomalies.
- **Electromagnetic (CSEM / MT)** — increasingly used in deepwater.
- **Remote sensing** — satellite/aerial imagery for lineament & structural mapping.

### C. Direct Methods
- **Exploratory (wildcat) drilling** — the *only* method that *physically* proves the presence of hydrocarbons.

## 2.2.1 Seismic Method — Detailed Theory

**Principle:** A controlled seismic source (vibroseis truck, dynamite, airgun array) generates elastic waves that travel downward, reflect/refract at interfaces of contrasting acoustic impedance, and return to surface geophones/hydrophones.

**Acoustic Impedance (AI):**
$$AI = \rho \cdot v$$

where $\rho$ is rock density (kg/m³) and $v$ is P-wave velocity (m/s).

**Reflection Coefficient (R) at an interface:**
$$R = \frac{AI_2 - AI_1}{AI_2 + AI_1} = \frac{\rho_2 v_2 - \rho_1 v_1}{\rho_2 v_2 + \rho_1 v_1}$$

**Snell's Law (used in refraction and migration):**
$$\frac{\sin \theta_1}{v_1} = \frac{\sin \theta_2}{v_2}$$

**Two-way Travel Time (TWT):**
$$TWT = \frac{2d}{v_{avg}}$$

where $d$ is the depth of the reflector and $v_{avg}$ is the average velocity.

**Vertical Resolution (Rayleigh Criterion) — the thinnest bed detectable:**
$$\Delta z_{min} = \frac{\lambda}{4} = \frac{v}{4 f}$$

where $\lambda$ is wavelength, $f$ is the dominant frequency. *Engineering intuition:* to resolve a 25 m reservoir, with $v = 3500$ m/s, the required frequency is **f = 35 Hz**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why seismic dominates:** It provides continuous 2D/3D/4D subsurface images with meter-scale resolution, covering thousands of square kilometers per survey. It is *the* most information-rich and cost-effective method in modern E&P.

## 2.2.2 Gravity Method

**Principle:** Lateral variations in subsurface rock density produce measurable variations in the Earth's gravitational field.

**Newton's Law of Gravitation (baseline equation):**
$$g = \frac{G \cdot M}{r^2}$$

**Bouguer Anomaly (the residual signal of geological interest):**
$$g_{B} = g_{obs} - g_{lat} + g_{FA} - g_{B} - g_{T}$$

where:
- $g_{obs}$ = observed gravity
- $g_{lat}$ = theoretical (latitude) gravity
- $g_{FA}$ = free-air correction
- $g_{B}$ = Bouguer slab correction
- $g_{T}$ = terrain correction

**Standard density for Bouguer correction:** **2.67 g/cm³** (the global crustal average).

## 2.2.3 Magnetic Method

**Principle:** Magnetic minerals (mainly **magnetite**) in igneous and metamorphic basement rocks produce local anomalies.

**Earth's main field strength:** ~**25,000 to 65,000 nT** (nanotesla) from pole to equator.

**Total Magnetic Intensity (TMI)** is measured; the International Geomagnetic Reference Field (IGRM) is subtracted to obtain the *anomaly*.

## 2.2.4 Well Logging (Downhole Geophysics)

After a well is drilled, instruments are lowered to record **petrophysical properties** vs. depth:

| Log Type | Measures | Primary Use |
|---|---|---|
| Gamma Ray (GR) | Natural radioactivity (API units) | Shale vs. sand discrimination |
| Spontaneous Potential (SP) | Natural electrical potential | Permeable bed identification |
| Resistivity (Deep/Shallow) | Electrical resistance ($\Omega \cdot m$) | Hydrocarbon vs. water saturation |
| Density (RHOB) | Bulk density (g/cm³) | Porosity, lithology |
| Neutron (NPHI) | Hydrogen index | Porosity, gas-flag |
| Sonic (DT) | Transit time ($\mu s/ft$) | Porosity, seismic calibration |

**Archie Equation (the foundational saturation equation — KTU favourite):**
$$S_w = \left( \frac{a \cdot R_w}{\phi^m \cdot R_t} \right)^{1/n}$$

where:
- $S_w$ = water saturation (fraction)
- $R_w$ = formation water resistivity ($\Omega \cdot m$)
- $R_t$ = true formation resistivity ($\Omega \cdot m$)
- $\phi$ = porosity (fraction)
- $a$ = tortuosity factor (typically 1.0)
- $m$ = cementation exponent (typically 1.8–2.0)
- $n$ = saturation exponent (typically ~2.0)

## 2.3 Reserve Estimation (Volumetric Method)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **volumetric method** is the *primary* reserve estimation technique used **at the exploration/appraisal stage**, *before* production data is available.

**Original Oil In Place (OOIP):**
$$N = \frac{7758 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{B_{oi}}$$

**Original Gas In Place (OGIP):**
$$G = \frac{43560 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{B_{gi}}$$

where:
- $N$ = OOIP (STB, stock-tank barrels)
- $G$ = OGIP (SCF, standard cubic feet)
- $A$ = areal extent of reservoir (acres)
- $h$ = net pay thickness (ft)
- $\phi$ = porosity (fraction)
- $S_w$ = water saturation (fraction)
- $B_{oi}$ = oil formation volume factor (rbbl/STB)
- $B_{gi}$ = gas formation volume factor (rcf/SCF)
- 7758 and 43560 = unit conversion constants

> [!NOTE]
> **The constant 7758** comes from converting acre·ft to barrels: $1$ acre·ft = $7758$ bbl. **The constant 43560** converts acre·ft to cubic feet: $1$ acre·ft = $43560$ ft³.

## 2.4 Risk and Probability in Exploration

The **Probability of Success (POS)** of a prospect is the product of independent geological chance factors:
$$POS = P_{source} \times P_{reservoir} \times P_{trap} \times P_{seal} \times P_{migration} \times P_{timing}$$

The **Expected Monetary Value (EMV)** of a prospect is:
$$EMV = (V \times POS) - [C \times (1 - POS)] - C_{dry}$$

where $V$ = value of success, $C$ = cost of a discovery, $C_{dry}$ = dry-hole cost.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat-Sheet)

> [!TIP]
> **Use \vert for absolute value in tables to avoid markdown breaking.**

| # | Formula | Application | Units / Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $AI = \rho \cdot v$ | Acoustic impedance | kg/(m²·s) |
| 2 | $R = \frac{\rho_2 v_2 - \rho_1 v_1}{\rho_2 v_2 + \rho_1 v_1}$ | Seismic reflection coefficient | dimensionless, $-1 \le R \le +1$ |
| 3 | $\frac{\sin \theta_1}{v_1} = \frac{\sin \theta_2}{v_2}$ | Snell's law (refraction, migration) | dimensionless ratio |
| 4 | $TWT = \frac{2d}{v_{avg}}$ | Two-way travel time | seconds |
| 5 | $\Delta z_{min} = \frac{v}{4f}$ | Rayleigh vertical resolution | meters |
| 6 | $g = \frac{G \cdot M}{r^2}$ | Newton's gravitation (gravity method) | m/s² |
| 7 | $S_w = \left( \frac{a \cdot R_w}{\phi^m \cdot R_t} \right)^{1/n}$ | Archie water saturation | dimensionless fraction |
| 8 | $N = \frac{7758 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{B_{oi}}$ | Volumetric OOIP | STB |
| 9 | $G = \frac{43560 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{B_{gi}}$ | Volumetric OGIP | SCF |
| 10 | $POS = \prod P_i$ | Geological chance of success | dimensionless |
| 11 | $EMV = (V \cdot POS) - [C \cdot (1 - POS)] - C_{dry}$ | Prospect economics | USD or ₹ |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Method | Used by | Real-world Outcome |

| Seismic 3D | ONGC, Shell, Saudi Aramco | Discovery of Ghawar Field extension (Saudi Arabia) |
| Gravity-magnetic | CGG, BGP | Mapping basement highs in Cambay Basin (India) |
| Remote sensing | GSAT-2, LANDSAT | Lineaments, structural trends in Andaman Basin |
| CSEM (EM) | Schlumberger, EMGS | Deepwater subsalt imaging, Gulf of Mexico |
| Archie equation | Every wireline-log interpreter | Calculates $S_w$ for every reservoir worldwide |
| Volumetric estimate | Reserve auditors (SPE PRMS) | Determines P1/P2/P3 reserves for stock-exchange reporting |
