# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Acoustic Impedance and Reflection Coefficient

### Step 1 — Define Acoustic Impedance
The acoustic impedance (AI) of a medium is the product of its density and the P-wave velocity within that medium:
$$AI = \rho \cdot v$$

This is the resistance offered by the medium to the passage of a seismic wave. Units: kg/(m²·s) or Rayl.

### Step 2 — Consider an Interface Between Two Layers
At an interface between Layer 1 (above) and Layer 2 (below), the boundary conditions of continuity of *stress* and *particle displacement* must hold for the reflected and transmitted P-waves.

### Step 3 — Apply the Zoeppritz Equations (Full Form)
The Zoeppritz equations (1919) are the most general energy-partitioning relations. For a vertical-incident P-wave the simplified form is the reflection coefficient:
$$R = \frac{AI_2 - AI_1}{AI_2 + AI_1} = \frac{\rho_2 v_2 - \rho_1 v_1}{\rho_2 v_2 + \rho_1 v_1}$$

### Step 4 — Physical Interpretation
- If $AI_2 > AI_1$ (e.g., shale overlying gas sand) ⇒ $R$ is **positive** ⇒ reflected wave has same polarity as incident.
- If $AI_2 < AI_1$ (e.g., shale overlying brine sand) ⇒ $R$ is **negative** ⇒ polarity reversal (a classic **bright spot** or **dim spot** indicator in hydrocarbon exploration).
- The *magnitude* of $R$ controls seismic amplitude; the *sign* controls phase.

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-pattern Problem)
A shale layer with $v_1 = 3200$ m/s and $\rho_1 = 2300$ kg/m³ overlies a gas-sand layer with $v_2 = 2700$ m/s and $\rho_2 = 2100$ kg/m³. Calculate the reflection coefficient.

$$
\begin{aligned}
AI_1 &= \rho_1 \cdot v_1 = 2300 \times 3200 = 7{,}360{,}000 \ \text{kg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)} \\
AI_2 &= \rho_2 \cdot v_2 = 2100 \times 2700 = 5{,}670{,}000 \ \text{kg/(m}^2 \cdot \text{s)} \\
R &= \frac{AI_2 - AI_1}{AI_2 + AI_1} \\
  &= \frac{5{,}670{,}000 - 7{,}360{,}000}{5{,}670{,}000 + 7{,}360{,}000} \\
  &= \frac{-1{,}690{,}000}{13{,}030{,}000} \\
  &= -0.1297
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A negative reflection coefficient of magnitude 0.13 — this is a *strong* reflection in seismic terms, producing a **bright spot** that geophysicists would flag as a potential **Direct Hydrocarbon Indicator (DHI)**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Vertical Resolution (Rayleigh Criterion)

### Step 1 — Statement
Two reflectors are *resolvable* only if their separation is at least **one-quarter wavelength** apart.

### Step 2 — Wavelength Definition
$$\lambda = \frac{v}{f}$$

### Step 3 — Set Up the Resolution Limit
$$\Delta z_{min} = \frac{\lambda}{4} = \frac{v}{4f}$$

### Step 4 — Example
To resolve a **20 m thick** gas-sand reservoir where $v = 2800$ m/s:
$$f_{min} = \frac{v}{4 \cdot \Delta z_{min}} = \frac{2800}{4 \times 20} = \frac{2800}{80} = 35 \ \text{Hz}$$

> **Engineering takeaway:** Higher frequencies give finer resolution, but attenuate faster in the earth — so there is a *depth-vs-resolution* trade-off that drives seismic-acquisition design.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Snell's Law from Fermat's Principle

### Step 1 — Setup
A wave travels from Medium 1 to Medium 2 across a horizontal interface. Snell's law relates the angle of incidence $\theta_1$ to the angle of refraction $\theta_2$ through the ratio of velocities.

### Step 2 — Apply Conservation of Horizontal Wave-Number (Huygens' principle)
The component of slowness (1/v) along the interface must be continuous:
$$\frac{\sin \theta_1}{v_1} = \frac{\sin \theta_2}{v_2}$$

This is the *horizontal slowness* continuity condition. Rearranged:
$$\frac{\sin \theta_1}{\sin \theta_2} = \frac{v_1}{v_2}$$

### Step 3 — Critical Angle
If $v_2 > v_1$ (slow→fast), there exists a critical angle $\theta_c$ beyond which the wave is *totally internally reflected* — the basis of seismic refraction surveys:
$$\theta_c = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{v_1}{v_2}\right)$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Volumetric OOIP (Full Step-by-Step)

### Step 1 — Bulk Rock Volume
The bulk volume of a cylindrical reservoir element is:
$$V_{bulk} = A \cdot h$$

where $A$ is areal extent (in acres) and $h$ is net pay (in feet). Unit: acre·ft.

### Step 2 — Pore Volume
Pore volume = bulk volume × porosity:
$$V_{pore} = A \cdot h \cdot \phi$$

### Step 3 — Hydrocarbon Pore Volume (HCPV)
HCPV = pore volume × hydrocarbon saturation (1 − water saturation):
$$HCPV = A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Stock-Tank Barrels
The hydrocarbon volume *at reservoir conditions* is converted to stock-tank (surface) conditions via the oil formation volume factor $B_{oi}$ (rbbl/STB):
$$N = \frac{A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{B_{oi}}$$

### Step 5 — Unit Conversion
To convert acre·ft to barrels, $1$ acre·ft = $7758$ barrels:
$$\boxed{\,N = \frac{7758 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{B_{oi}}\,}$$

### Step 6 — Numerical Example
A prospect is evaluated with: $A = 1000$ acres, $h = 50$ ft, $\phi = 0.20$, $S_w = 0.30$, $B_{oi} = 1.25$ rbbl/STB.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{7758 \times 1000 \times 50 \times 0.20 \times (1 - 0.30)}{1.25} \\
  &= \frac{7758 \times 1000 \times 50 \times 0.20 \times 0.70}{1.25} \\
  &= \frac{7758 \times 7000}{1.25} \\
  &= \frac{54{,}306{,}000}{1.25} \\
  &= 43{,}444{,}800 \ \text{STB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **≈ 43.4 million stock-tank barrels of oil in place.**

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Probability of Success (POS)

For independent geological conditions, the joint probability is the product:
$$POS = P_{src} \times P_{res} \times P_{trap} \times P_{seal} \times P_{mig} \times P_{tim}$$

**Example (typical Cambay Basin prospect):**

| Factor | Probability |
|---|---|
| $P_{src}$ (source presence & maturity) | 0.9 |
| $P_{res}$ (reservoir presence) | 0.8 |
| $P_{trap}$ (valid trap geometry) | 0.7 |
| $P_{seal}$ (effective seal) | 0.85 |
| $P_{mig}$ (migration pathway) | 0.75 |
| $P_{tim}$ (timing) | 0.9 |

$$
\begin{aligned}
POS &= 0.9 \times 0.8 \times 0.7 \times 0.85 \times 0.75 \times 0.9 \\
    &= 0.289 \\
    &\approx 29\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** Only about a *1-in-3.5* chance of finding producible hydrocarbons — a typical frontier-basin value.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Exploration Methods

| Method | Cost ($/km²) | Penetration | Resolution | Best Use Stage |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Remote sensing | < 100 | Surface only | 10–30 m | Reconnaissance |
| Gravity | 50–200 | Whole crust | km-scale | Basin framework |
| Magnetic | 50–200 | Whole crust | km-scale | Basement mapping |
| 2D Seismic | 500–2000 | 1–10 km | 5–20 m vertically | Play definition |
| 3D Seismic | 3000–15000 | 1–10 km | 5–10 m | Prospect maturation |
| 4D Seismic | 5000–20000 | 1–10 km | 5–10 m | Reservoir monitoring |
| CSAMT/CSEM | 1000–5000 | 1–5 km | 100–500 m | Deepwater imaging |
| Exploratory Well | 2 M–250 M | direct | < 1 m | Final test |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Petroleum Exploration Workflow — End-to-End Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[basin1] --> A1[step1A Reconnaissance and Literature Review]
    A1 --> A2[step1B Geological and Remote Sensing Survey]
    A2 --> A3[step1C Gravity and Magnetic Survey]
    A3 --> A4[step1D 2D Seismic Survey]
    A4 --> A5[step1E 3D Seismic Survey]
    A5 --> A6[step1F Prospect Generation and Mapping]
    A6 --> A7[step1G Risk and Volume Assessment]
    A7 --> A8[step1H EMV and Decision Gate]
    A8 -->|Go| A9[step1I Exploratory Wildcat Well]
    A8 -->|No Go| A10[step1J Hold or Relinquish Block]
    A9 --> A11[step1K Discovery and Appraisal]
    A11 --> A12[step1L Reserves Booking and Development Planning]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The exploration sequence is *funnel-shaped* — data density increases and decision costs escalate as we proceed. Each box is a "stage gate" where capital may be reallocated or terminated.

---

## 4.2 Petroleum System — Essential Elements and Processes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src1[ele1 Source Rock Organic Rich Shale] --> mig1[pro1 Hydrocarbon Generation Expulsion and Migration]
    ovb1[ele2 Overburden Rock] --> src1
    mig1 --> trp1[ele3 Trap Geometry Anticline Fault etc]
    res1[ele4 Reservoir Rock Porous Sandstone] --> trp1
    cap1[ele5 Seal Rock Impermeable Shale] --> trp1
    trp1 --> acc1[ele6 Hydrocarbon Accumulation Commercial Pool]
    mig1 --> acc1
    tim1[pro2 Critical Timing of Generation vs Trap Formation] --> acc1
```

**Reading the diagram:** All *elements* (Source, Reservoir, Seal, Trap, Overburden) and *processes* (Maturation, Migration, Timing) must align in space and time for a *commercial accumulation* to form. Failure of any one breaks the chain.

---

## 4.3 Seismic Acquisition and Reflection Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    src2[eq1 Source Truck or Airgun Array] --> ear1[eq2 Earth Subsurface Interfaces]
    ear1 --> ear2[eq3 Reflector 1 Top of Reservoir]
    ear2 --> ear3[eq4 Reflector 2 Base of Reservoir]
    ear3 --> rec1[eq5 Geophone or Hydrophone Spread]
    rec1 --> rec2[eq6 Seismic Recorder and Storage]
    rec2 --> rec3[eq7 Processing Centre CDP Sort NMO Migration]
    rec3 --> rec4[eq8 Interpreted Seismic Section with Horizons and Faults]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Energy flows downward from the source, reflects at subsurface acoustic-impedance contrasts, and is recorded at the surface. Modern processing converts the raw recordings into *time-migrated* cross-sections that interpreters can geo-reference to geology.

---

## 4.4 Volumetric Reserve Estimation — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inA[Input1 Reservoir Area from Seismic Map acres] --> mul1[blk1 Multiply]
    inB[Input2 Net Pay Thickness from Well Log ft] --> mul1
    mul1 --> mul2[blk2 Multiply by Porosity phi]
    inC[Input3 Porosity from Density Neutron Log] --> mul2
    mul2 --> mul3[blk3 Multiply by Hydrocarbon Saturation 1 minus Sw]
    inD[Input4 Water Saturation from Archie Equation] --> mul3
    mul3 --> div1[blk4 Divide by Formation Volume Factor Boi]
    inE[Input5 Boi from PVT Lab Analysis] --> div1
    div1 --> out1[Output1 Original Oil In Place OOIP STB]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The volumetric equation is a *multiplicative cascade* of five measured/estimated inputs. **Uncertainty compounds multiplicatively** — a ±10 % error in any one input propagates to ±10 % in the final OOIP. This is why reserve estimation is *probabilistic*, not single-number.

---

## 4.5 Prospect Risk Model — Multiplicative POS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    pa1[fact1 Source Psrc 0.9] --> mult1[multiply1 POS Calculation]
    pb1[fact2 Reservoir Pres 0.8] --> mult1
    pc1[fact3 Trap Ptrp 0.7] --> mult1
    pd1[fact4 Seal Pseal 0.85] --> mult1
    pe1[fact5 Migration Pmig 0.75] --> mult1
    pf1[fact6 Timing Ptim 0.9] --> mult1
    mult1 --> res1[result1 POS equals 0.289 or 29 percent]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each factor is an *independent* conditional probability; the joint POS is their product. The diagram makes the multiplicative nature explicit — a single weak link (e.g., $P_{trap} = 0.4$) collapses the entire prospect.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the term "Petroleum System" and list its essential elements.**

**Model Answer:**

A *Petroleum System* (Magoon & Dow, 1994) is a natural system encompassing a mature source rock and all genetically related oil and gas accumulations, including the geological elements and processes essential for a hydrocarbon accumulation to exist.

**Essential elements and processes:**
- **Source rock** — generates hydrocarbons
- **Reservoir rock** — stores hydrocarbons
- **Seal rock (cap rock)** — prevents escape
- **Overburden rock** — provides thermal maturation
- **Trap** — geometry that holds hydrocarbons
- **Migration pathway** — connects source to trap
- **Timing** — generation–migration–accumulation must coincide

> *Awarded Marks Distribution (3):* Definition 1 + Elements listing 2.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Explain the significance of acoustic impedance in seismic exploration.**

**Model Answer:**

Acoustic Impedance ($AI$) is the product of rock density $\rho$ and P-wave velocity $v$ — it is the resistance of a rock layer to the passage of a seismic wave.

**Significance:**
1. **Reflection Generation:** A seismic reflection occurs only at an interface where there is a *contrast* in acoustic impedance.
2. **Reflection Coefficient:** The strength and polarity of the reflected wave is given by $R = (AI_2 - AI_1)/(AI_2 + AI_1)$.
3. **Hydrocarbon Detection:** Gas-sand has *lower* AI than water-sand, producing a *negative* reflection (a "bright spot") — a classic **Direct Hydrocarbon Indicator (DHI)**.
4. **Lithology Discrimination:** Different rocks (sandstone, shale, carbonate) have characteristic AI values used to interpret stratigraphy from seismic data.

> *Awarded Marks Distribution (3):* Definition 1 + Significance 2.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**A. (a)** With a neat labelled diagram, explain the **principle and workflow of the seismic reflection method** of geophysical exploration. Discuss the concept of **vertical and horizontal resolution**. **(7 marks)**

**A. (b)** A seismic survey records a reflection from a horizon at two-way time **2.4 s** in a medium with average velocity **3000 m/s**. The dominant frequency of the wavelet is **30 Hz**. Determine (i) the depth of the reflector, and (ii) the minimum resolvable bed thickness. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — A(a)

**Principle:** A controlled seismic source generates elastic waves at the surface. These waves travel downward, reflect from subsurface acoustic-impedance contrasts, and return to surface geophones. The two-way travel time, when combined with velocity, yields reflector depth. Multiple traces combined produce a 2D/3D image of the subsurface.

**Workflow (6 steps):**
1. **Survey design** — geometry, line spacing, fold.
2. **Data acquisition** — source firing, receiver recording.
3. **Demultiplex & edit** — quality control of field tapes.
4. **Processing** — CDP sort, NMO, stack, migration.
5. **Interpretation** — picking horizons, mapping structure.
6. **Prospect generation** — closure mapping, fault interpretation.

**Vertical Resolution:** $ \Delta z_{min} = v / (4f) $ — based on the Rayleigh criterion, the thinnest bed whose top and base reflections are separately identifiable.

**Horizontal Resolution:** Approximately the radius of the **Fresnel Zone**: $ r_F = v / 2 \cdot \sqrt{(t / f)} $, where $t$ is the two-way time. Modern 3D migration collapses the Fresnel zone to a fraction of its original radius, improving horizontal resolution to ~25 m at reservoir depths.

> *Marks Distribution (7):* Principle 1 + Workflow 3 + Resolution 3.

---

#### Model Solution — A(b)

**Given:** TWT = 2.4 s, $v_{avg}$ = 3000 m/s, $f$ = 30 Hz.

**(i) Depth of reflector:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
d &= \frac{v_{avg} \cdot TWT}{2} \\
  &= \frac{3000 \times 2.4}{2} \\
  &= \frac{7200}{2} \\
  &= 3600 \ \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Minimum resolvable bed thickness (Rayleigh criterion):**
$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= \frac{v}{f} = \frac{3000}{30} = 100 \ \text{m} \\
\Delta z_{min} &= \frac{\lambda}{4} = \frac{100}{4} = 25 \ \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** The reflector is at **3600 m depth**, and the thinnest separable bed is **25 m** thick.

> *Marks Distribution (7):* Depth 3 + Resolution 4.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**B. (a)** Describe the **volumetric method** of estimating **Original Oil In Place (OOIP)**. State the formula and explain the role of each parameter. **(7 marks)**

**B. (b)** An exploratory well encounters a sandstone reservoir with the following data: Area = **800 acres**, Net pay = **40 ft**, Porosity = **0.22**, Water saturation = **0.25**, Oil formation volume factor $B_{oi}$ = **1.30 rbbl/STB**. Calculate the OOIP. If the recovery factor is **30 %**, compute the **recoverable reserves**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — B(a)

The **volumetric method** estimates OOIP at the exploration/appraisal stage, *before* production data is available. It computes the total hydrocarbon volume contained in the reservoir pore space at initial reservoir conditions.

**Formula:**
$$N = \frac{7758 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w)}{B_{oi}}$$

**Parameter roles:**
- **A** (acres) — areal extent, mapped from seismic closure.
- **h** (ft) — net pay thickness, from well logs.
- **$\phi$** (fraction) — porosity, from density/neutron logs.
- **$S_w$** (fraction) — water saturation, from Archie equation.
- **$B_{oi}$** (rbbl/STB) — oil formation volume factor, from PVT lab.
- **7758** — conversion factor (1 acre·ft = 7758 bbl).

> *Marks Distribution (7):* Formula 2 + Parameter explanation 3 + Method 2.

---

#### Model Solution — B(b)

**Given:** A = 800 acres, h = 40 ft, $\phi$ = 0.22, $S_w$ = 0.25, $B_{oi}$ = 1.30 rbbl/STB.

**Step 1 — OOIP calculation:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{7758 \times 800 \times 40 \times 0.22 \times (1 - 0.25)}{1.30} \\
  &= \frac{7758 \times 800 \times 40 \times 0.22 \times 0.75}{1.30} \\
  &= \frac{7758 \times 5280}{1.30} \\
  &= \frac{40{,}962{,}240}{1.30} \\
  &= 31{,}509{,}415 \ \text{STB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Recoverable Reserves:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{rec} &= N \times RF \\
        &= 31{,}509{,}415 \times 0.30 \\
        &= 9{,}452{,}825 \ \text{STB}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Result:** OOIP ≈ **31.5 million STB**; Recoverable reserves ≈ **9.45 million STB**.

> *Marks Distribution (7):* OOIP calc 4 + RF explanation 1 + Reserves calc 2.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Forgetting to convert acre·ft → bbl (constant 7758) is a classic 1-mark loss. Some students use 7758 incorrectly for gas.
> 2. **Confusing OGIP and OOIP constants:** Gas uses **43560** (for ft³/acre·ft), oil uses **7758** (for bbl/acre·ft).
> 3. **Mixing $S_w$ and $(1 - S_w)$:** Hydrocarbon saturation is $1 - S_w$, *not* $S_w$.
> 4. **Resolution formula sign error:** Resolution is $v/(4f)$, not $4f/v$.
> 5. **Skipping units in final answer:** KTU deducts 0.5 marks for missing units in numerical answers.
> 6. **Using $AI = \rho / v$ instead of $AI = \rho \cdot v$:** This is a fundamental definition error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Petroleum Exploration (Module 1):**

- **Petroleum System Elements (Magoon & Dow):** Source, Reservoir, Seal, Overburden, Trap, Migration, Timing.
- **Exploration Stages:** Basin reconnaissance → Play fairway → Prospect generation → Ranking → Wildcat → Appraisal.
- **Seismic Reflection Coefficient:** $R = (AI_2 - AI_1)/(AI_2 + AI_1)$, where $AI = \rho v$.
- **Vertical Resolution (Rayleigh):** $\Delta z_{min} = v/(4f)$.
- **Snell's Law:** $\sin \theta_1 / v_1 = \sin \theta_2 / v_2$.
- **Gravity Method:** Bouguer anomaly uses crustal density **2.67 g/cm³**; Newton's law $g = GM/r^2$.
- **Magnetic Method:** Earth's main field ≈ 25,000–65,000 nT; IGRM-subtracted anomaly used.
- **Well Logs:** GR (lithology), SP (permeability), Resistivity (saturation), Density/Neutron (porosity).
- **Archie Equation:** $S_w = (a \cdot R_w / (\phi^m \cdot R_t))^{1/n}$ — the foundation of $S_w$ calculation.
- **Volumetric OOIP:** $N = 7758 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w) / B_{oi}$.
- **Volumetric OGIP:** $G = 43560 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_w) / B_{gi}$.
- **Probability of Success:** $POS = \prod P_i$ (multiplicative product of six factors).
- **EMV:** $EMV = V \cdot POS - C \cdot (1 - POS) - C_{dry}$.
- **Modern Method Ranking (by resolution):** Well logs > 3D seismic > 2D seismic > CSEM > Gravity/Magnetic > Remote sensing.
- **Wildcat well cost range:** USD 2 M (onshore) to USD 250 M (deepwater).
- **Standard Earth Density for Bouguer correction:** **2.67 g/cm³**.
- **KTU Mnemonic:** **S.R.M.T.C.S.** for petroleum system elements.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Memory Anchor:** If you remember only three equations on exam day, remember **(1) $R = (AI_2 - AI_1)/(AI_2 + AI_1)$, (2) $S_w = (a R_w / \phi^m R_t)^{1/n}$, and (3) $N = 7758 A h \phi (1-S_w)/B_{oi}$** — together they unlock 60–70 % of typical KTU Part-B questions on Module 1.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Associated Gas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT528 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Associated Gas

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Associated Gas (AG)** — also known as **Associated Petroleum Gas (APG)** — is natural gas that occurs in a petroleum reservoir in intimate association with crude oil. It exists in two principal physical states within the reservoir: **(a) dissolved in the crude oil under high reservoir pressure**, and **(b) as a free gas cap lying above the liquid hydrocarbon phase**.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Module 2, *Associated Gas* forms the bridge concept between **crude oil production** (Module 1) and **gas processing / NGL recovery** (Module 3). The term *associated* explicitly differentiates it from **non-associated gas**, which is produced from reservoirs that contain little to no liquid hydrocarbon (essentially dry-gas or wet-gas fields with negligible condensate yield).

### 1.1.1 Sub-classification of Associated Gas

Associated gas is sub-divided in petroleum engineering practice into two thermodynamically distinct categories:

| Sub-type | State in Reservoir | Typical Location | Surface Manifestation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Solution Gas (Dissolved Gas)** | Fully dissolved in oil under reservoir $P$ and $T$ | Throughout the oil column | Liberated when oil is depressurized at surface |
| **Free Gas (Gas-Cap Gas)** | Exists as a separate gaseous phase above the oil | Above the oil-water contact in a structural/stratigraphic trap | Produced directly from the gas cap at the wellhead |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Gas-Oil Ratio (GOR)** is the principal surface-measurable parameter that quantifies how much associated gas is co-produced per barrel of stock-tank oil. A reservoir whose producing GOR exceeds the *initial solution GOR* $R_{si}$ is classified as a **gas-cap drive** reservoir.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The Soda Bottle Analogy** — Think of a sealed, carbonated soft-drink bottle. The dissolved $\text{CO}_2$ in the liquid is *solution gas*. The headspace pressure above the liquid is the *gas cap*. When you crack the cap, the sudden depressurization causes the dissolved gas to bubble out, identical to the liberation of associated gas from crude oil as it travels up the production tubing from high reservoir pressure to low separator pressure.

A second, more engineer-friendly analogy is the **"champagne + cork" model**: a champagne bottle held deep in a warm sea represents the reservoir (high $P$, high $T$). As the bottle is brought to the surface, dissolved $\text{CO}_2$ (analogous to methane, ethane, propane) effervesces out of solution because the bottle can no longer hold as much gas at the lower surface pressure. The quantity of gas that escapes is governed by the **solution gas-oil ratio** at the prevailing pressure and temperature.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

Key numerical benchmarks used throughout this module:

- **Standard surface conditions:** $P_{sc} = 14.696 \text{ psia} \; (\approx 101.325 \text{ kPa})$ and $T_{sc} = 60^\circ \text{F} \; (520^\circ \text{R}, 288.65 \text{ K})$
- **Standard volume unit for gas:** **1 scf** (standard cubic foot) or **1 sm³** (standard cubic metre) at standard conditions
- **Standard volume unit for oil:** **1 STB** (stock-tank barrel, 42 U.S. gallons at surface conditions)
- **Universal gas constant:** $R = 10.732 \text{ psi·ft}^3/(\text{lbmol·°R})$
- **Critical constants of methane ($\text{CH}_4$):** $P_c = 666.4 \text{ psia}$, $T_c = 343^\circ \text{R}$
- **World Bank flaring reduction target:** Zero Routine Flaring by 2030 (launched 2015)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Scheme specifically expects students to (i) identify the origin and phase behaviour of associated gas, (ii) compute the **solution gas-oil ratio $R_s$** at given reservoir conditions, and (iii) describe the **processing and utilization pathways** of associated gas (re-injection, LNG, GTL, NGL recovery, and CNG).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Origin and Phase Behaviour of Associated Gas

Associated gas originates from the **same kerogen maturation process** that generates crude oil. As type I and type II kerogens are buried and subjected to temperatures in the **oil window** ($60-120^\circ \text{C}$), they crack into hydrocarbon molecules of carbon number $\text{C}_1$ through $\text{C}_{40+}$. The lighter fractions ($\text{C}_1$ to $\text{C}_5$) constitute natural gas, while the intermediate to heavy fractions form crude oil. Because both phases are generated simultaneously, they co-exist in the same pore space, with phase distribution governed by **pressure–temperature–composition (P–T–x) phase equilibria**.

### 2.1.1 Why does gas dissolve in oil?

At reservoir pressure $P_r$ and temperature $T_r$, the oil phase is essentially a **compressed liquid** capable of dissolving a finite mass of natural gas. This dissolved gas:

1. **Reduces oil viscosity** (improves flow through porous media)
2. **Reduces oil density** (improves API gravity)
3. **Provides reservoir energy** (solution gas drive mechanism)
4. **Increases oil formation volume factor $B_o$** (oil swells)

The maximum amount of gas that oil can hold at a given $P$ and $T$ is the **bubble-point pressure** $P_b$. Below $P_b$, free gas evolves from the oil.

### 2.1.2 Composition of Associated Gas

A typical associated gas stream (mole %):

| Component | Symbol | Typical mol % | Boiling Point (°C) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Methane | $\text{C}_1$ | 60–80 | −161.5 |
| Ethane | $\text{C}_2$ | 10–15 | −88.6 |
| Propane | $\text{C}_3$ | 5–10 | −42.1 |
| i-Butane / n-Butane | $\text{iC}_4/\text{nC}_4$ | 2–5 | −11.7 / −0.5 |
| Pentanes + | $\text{C}_5+$ | 1–3 | 27–36 |
| $\text{CO}_2$ | — | 0–5 | −78 (sublimes) |
| $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ | — | 0–3 (sour) | −60 |
| $\text{N}_2$ | — | 0–5 | −196 |
| Water vapour | $\text{H}_2\text{O}$ | trace | 100 |

> [!NOTE]
> Gas with appreciable $\text{C}_2+$ content is termed **wet gas**; predominantly $\text{C}_1$ is **dry gas**. Associated gas is almost always **wet**, which is why it is the preferred feedstock for **NGL (Natural Gas Liquids) recovery plants**.

## 2.2 Production Stages of Associated Gas

Associated gas evolves through **four distinct pressure/temperature stages** as fluids travel from reservoir to stock tank:

1. **Reservoir stage** — Single-phase oil with all gas in solution (if $P_r > P_b$).
2. **Production tubing stage** — Pressure falls; when $P < P_b$, first gas bubble nucleates.
3. **Wellhead / Flowline stage** — Multi-phase flow (oil + free gas + water).
4. **Separator stage** — Multi-stage separation at progressively lower pressures yields stabilized crude oil and free gas streams.
5. **Stock tank stage** — Final gas liberation at near-atmospheric pressure ($P_{st} \approx P_{sc}$).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** Memorize the following eight equations. They cover $\geq 90\%$ of numerical questions asked in KTU 2024 Scheme ESE on associated gas and reservoir fluid properties.

| # | Formula | Description | SI / Field Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $R_{sp} = \dfrac{V_{gas}^{sc}}{V_{oil}^{st}}$ | Producing Gas-Oil Ratio (instantaneous) | scf/STB or sm³/sm³ |
| 2 | $R_s = \gamma_g \left(\dfrac{P}{18 \times 10^{(0.00091\,T - 0.0125\,\text{API})}}\right)^{1.2048}$ | **Standing's correlation** for solution GOR | scf/STB |
| 3 | $B_o = 0.9759 + 0.00012\left[R_s\left(\dfrac{\gamma_g}{\gamma_o}\right)^{0.5} + 1.4\,T\right]^{1.2}$ | Oil FVF (Standing) | rb/STB |
| 4 | $Z = 1 + \left(A_1 + \dfrac{A_2}{T_r} + \dfrac{A_3}{T_r^3}\right)\rho_r + \left(A_4 + \dfrac{A_5}{T_r} + \dfrac{A_6}{T_r^3}\right)\rho_r^2 + \dfrac{A_7\,\rho_r^2}{T_r^3}\left(1 + A_8\,\rho_r^2\right)\exp(-A_8\,\rho_r^2)$ | **Dranchuk–Abou-Kassem (DAK)** Z-factor (Lee-Gasem form) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $n = \dfrac{P\,V}{Z\,R\,T} = \dfrac{PV}{ZRT}$ | Real-gas mole balance (compressibility form) | lbmol or kmol |
| 6 | $V_{sc} = \dfrac{Z_{sc}\,n\,R\,T_{sc}}{P_{sc}}$ | Standard gas volume (molar form of ideal gas at sc) | scf or sm³ |
| 7 | $\rho_g = \dfrac{P\,M}{Z\,R\,T}$ | Real-gas density | lbm/ft³ or kg/m³ |
| 8 | $P_c = \sum y_i\,P_{ci}, \quad T_c = \sum y_i\,T_{ci}$ | **Kay's mixing rules** for pseudo-critical properties | psia, °R |

> [!NOTE]
> In formulas 4, 7, and 8 the gas constant must match unit system. For field units use $R = 10.732 \text{ psi·ft}^3/(\text{lbmol·°R})$; for SI use $R = 8.314 \text{ kJ/(kmol·K)}$. **Never mix units within one equation** — this is the most common valuation error in KTU answer scripts.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Reservoir management**: Predicting $R_s$ vs $P$ tells engineers when a reservoir transitions from single-phase to two-phase flow, dictating depletion strategy.
- **Surface facility design**: Sizing of separators, compressors, and NGL extraction columns depends directly on the volume and composition of liberated associated gas.
- **Economic valuation**: Stripper-well operators gain 25–40 % of revenue from associated gas sales; the rest comes from oil.
- **Environmental compliance**: Methane has a Global Warming Potential (GWP) ≈ 28–34× that of $\text{CO}_2$ over 100 years, so capturing associated gas (vs. flaring) is a climate priority.
- **Energy security**: Associated gas now supplies ≈ 15 % of global natural gas production, making it a critical midstream feedstock for LNG, GTL, and petrochemical industries.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Real-Gas Equation Applied to Surface Conditions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation underpins the conversion between **standard gas volume** and **mole count**, which is the foundation for every volumetric gas-reserves calculation in KTU exams.

We begin with the **real gas law** for $n$ moles of an associated gas mixture occupying volume $V$ at pressure $P$ and temperature $T$:

$$
P V = Z n R T
$$

Rearranging for the **molar amount** of gas:

$$
n = \dfrac{P\,V}{Z\,R\,T}
$$

At **standard surface conditions**, the compressibility factor of the gas approaches unity ($Z_{sc} \approx 1.0$, because pressure is low and intermolecular forces are negligible). Therefore the standard volume $V_{sc}$ of the same $n$ moles becomes:

$$
V_{sc} = \dfrac{Z_{sc}\,n\,R\,T_{sc}}{P_{sc}} = \dfrac{(1.0)\,n\,R\,T_{sc}}{P_{sc}}
$$

Substituting the expression for $n$ from the reservoir condition:

$$
V_{sc} = \dfrac{n\,R\,T_{sc}}{P_{sc}} = \dfrac{1}{P_{sc}}\left(\dfrac{P\,V}{Z\,R\,T}\right)R\,T_{sc}
$$

The $R$ terms cancel, yielding the **canonical gas-volume conversion equation** used in petroleum engineering:

$$
\boxed{\,V_{sc} = \dfrac{P\,V}{Z\,T}\cdot\dfrac{T_{sc}}{P_{sc}} = \dfrac{P\,V}{Z\,T}\cdot\dfrac{520}{14.696}\;\; \text{(field units)}\,}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Interpretation:** This is *not* a simple Boyle/Mariotte isotherm — it explicitly retains the **compressibility factor $Z$** to capture non-ideal behaviour at high reservoir pressures. At $P < 1000$ psia, $Z \to 1$ and the equation reduces to the familiar ideal-gas form.

## 3.2 Derivation: Standing's Correlation for Solution GOR

**Standing (1947)** developed an empirical correlation from laboratory PVT data covering California crude oils. It expresses the **solution gas-oil ratio $R_s$** (scf/STB) as a function of pressure, temperature, gas specific gravity $\gamma_g$, and oil API gravity:

$$
R_s = \gamma_g \left[ \dfrac{P}{18 \times 10^{0.00091\,T - 0.0125\,\text{API}}} \right]^{1.2048}
$$

**Step-by-step logical construction:**

1. **Dimensional anchor:** $R_s$ has units of scf/STB; $\gamma_g$ is dimensionless (air = 1.0), so the bracketed term must carry units of pressure (psia).
2. **Temperature exponent:** Laboratory data showed $\ln R_s \propto 1/T$, hence the $0.00091\,T$ exponent.
3. **API gravity effect:** Lighter crudes (high °API) dissolve more gas, hence the $-0.0125\,\text{API}$ term.
4. **Constant 18:** Empirical scaling constant fitted to standing's 105 PVT datasets.
5. **Exponent 1.2048:** Best-fit slope on a log-log cross-plot of $R_s$ vs the bracketed term.

**Validity envelope (Standing's correlation):**

| Parameter | Range |
|---|---|
| Pressure | 100 – 5000 psia |
| Temperature | 100 – 250 °F |
| API gravity | 16 – 64 °API |
| Gas specific gravity | 0.5 – 1.5 |

> [!NOTE]
> For higher accuracy in modern KTU-level problems, **Vasquez-Beggs (1980)** and **Glaso (1980)** correlations are preferred. Standing's remains the most-quoted benchmark in Indian engineering curricula.

## 3.3 Worked Example — Bubble-Point Pressure via Standing

**Problem (KTU 2024 Scheme style):**
An associated gas–oil system has the following properties:

- Reservoir temperature: $T = 180^\circ \text{F} = 640^\circ \text{R}$
- Oil API gravity: $\text{API} = 35^\circ$
- Gas specific gravity: $\gamma_g = 0.75$
- Solution GOR at bubble point: $R_{sb} = 720 \text{ scf/STB}$

**Find:** The bubble-point pressure $P_b$.

**Step 1 — Invert Standing's equation to solve for $P$:**

$$
R_s = \gamma_g \left(\dfrac{P}{18 \times 10^{(0.00091\,T - 0.0125\,\text{API})}}\right)^{1.2048}
$$

$$
\dfrac{R_s}{\gamma_g} = \left(\dfrac{P}{18 \times 10^{(0.00091\,T - 0.0125\,\text{API})}}\right)^{1.2048}
$$

**Step 2 — Take both sides to the power $1/1.2048$:**

$$
\left(\dfrac{R_s}{\gamma_g}\right)^{1/1.2048} = \dfrac{P}{18 \times 10^{(0.00091\,T - 0.0125\,\text{API})}}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the temperature–API exponent:**

$$
0.00091 \times 180 = 0.1638
$$
$$
0.0125 \times 35 = 0.4375
$$
$$
0.1638 - 0.4375 = -0.2737
$$
$$
10^{-0.2737} = 0.5323
$$
$$
18 \times 0.5323 = 9.5814
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the GOR ratio power:**

$$
\dfrac{720}{0.75} = 960
$$
$$
960^{1/1.2048} = 960^{0.8300} = e^{0.8300\,\ln(960)} = e^{0.8300 \times 6.8679} = e^{5.7003} = 299.4
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for bubble-point pressure:**

$$
P_b = 9.5814 \times 299.4 = \mathbf{2868.7 \; psia}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Sanity check:** A bubble point of ~2870 psia at 180 °F is consistent with a moderately volatile light crude (35 °API) — physically reasonable.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Solution Gas vs Gas-Cap Gas

| Property | Solution (Dissolved) Gas | Gas-Cap (Free) Gas |
|---|---|---|
| **Reservoir location** | Dispersed within oil column | Above oil-water contact |
| **Composition** | Leaner (high $\text{C}_1$) | Richer (more $\text{C}_2+$, $\text{CO}_2$) |
| **GOR contribution** | Equals $R_{si}$ at $P_b$ | Drives $R_p > R_{si}$ during gas-cap expansion |
| **Recovery mechanism** | Liberated by depressurization (1st-stage separator) | Direct gas-cap expansion, often re-injected |
| **Initial Z-factor** | $Z_i \approx 0.85$–$0.95$ | $Z_i \approx 0.80$–$0.92$ |
| **Recoverable fraction** | 20–35 % of in-place | 60–85 % of in-place (if not re-injected, higher with re-injection) |
| **Typical KTU exam weightage** | 60 % of numerical questions | 40 % (mostly theory) |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Reservoir-to-Surface Flow Schematic (Mermaid Block Flow)

The following diagram illustrates the **journey of associated gas from reservoir pore space to the gas processing plant**, identifying the pressure–temperature windows at which phase change occurs.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reservoir P greater than Pb T equal to Tres] -->|Single-phase oil with dissolved gas| B[Production Tubing]
    B -->|P drops below Pb| C[Two-phase flow oil + free gas]
    C -->|Wellhead Choke| D[HP Separator P1 approx 1000 psia]
    D -->|Gas stream 1| E[Gas Processing Plant]
    D -->|Oil stream| F[LP Separator P2 approx 250 psia]
    F -->|Gas stream 2| E
    F -->|Oil stream| G[Stock Tank P3 approx 14.7 psia]
    G -->|Vent gas| E
    G -->|Stock tank oil| H[Pipeline to refinery]
    E --> I{Utilisation Pathway}
    I -->|Re-injection for EOR| J[Compressor to reservoir]
    I -->|Sales gas| K[Pipeline to consumer]
    I -->|Liquefaction| L[LNG Plant]
    I -->|NGL extraction| M[Ethane Propane Butane recovery]
    I -->|Flaring last resort| N[Flare Stack]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide:** The two gas streams (from HP and LP separators) are recombined at the gas plant because their compositions differ — the HP gas is leaner (more $\text{C}_1$ lost to oil), while LP gas is richer in $\text{C}_3+$. This is the basis of **stage-separation optimization** problems in KTU examinations.

## 4.2 Phase Envelope (P-T Diagram) Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PhaseEnvelope[Associated Gas Phase Envelope]
        direction TB
        Z[Critical Point] --> Y[Cricondentherm]
        Y --> X[Bubble-Point Line oil side]
        X --> W[Bubble-Point Line]
        W --> V[Quality Lines tie lines]
        V --> U[Dew-Point Line]
        U --> Z
    end
    P1[Reservoir T1 Tr and P1 greater than Pb] -.-> X
    P2[Separator T2 less than Tr and P2 less than Pb] -.-> V
    P3[Stock Tank T3 ambient and P3 atm] -.-> U
```

> [!TIP]
> The reservoir point always lies **outside the two-phase envelope on the high-pressure side** (single-phase liquid region). As fluids trace the production path, they cross the bubble-point curve into the **two-phase region**, then exit through the dew-point curve into the gas region at the stock tank. This is a high-frequency KTU diagram question.

## 4.3 Block Architecture: Associated Gas Utilisation Pathways

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RAW[Associated Gas at Wellhead] --> COMP[Compressor Station]
    COMP --> SW{Utilisation Strategy}
    SW -->|Option 1| R1[Pipeline sales gas to grid]
    SW -->|Option 2| R2[NGL extraction plant]
    R2 --> R2A[Ethane to petrochemical]
    R2 --> R2B[Propane LPG bottling]
    R2 --> R2C[Butane gasoline blending]
    R2 --> R2D[Natural gasoline]
    SW -->|Option 3| R3[LNG liquefaction train]
    SW -->|Option 4| R4[GTL gas to liquids Fischer-Tropsch]
    R4 --> R4A[Synthetic diesel]
    R4 --> R4B[Synthetic naphtha]
    R4 --> R4C[Synthetic waxes]
    SW -->|Option 5| R5[Re-injection for pressure maintenance]
    SW -->|Option 6| R6[CNG compression for transport]
    SW -->|Option 7| R7[Power generation gas turbine]
    SW -->|Last resort| R8[Flare or vent with emissions penalty]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world context:** The **World Bank's "Zero Routine Flaring by 2030"** initiative classifies flaring as a *last-resort* option. KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to enumerate at least **four** utilisation pathways and explain the economic-environmental trade-offs of each.

## 4.4 Material Balance Concept Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Original Oil in Place N in ft³ STB] --> B[Produced Oil Np in STB]
    A --> C[Remaining Oil N minus Np in STB]
    B --> D[Associated Gas Produced Gp in scf]
    C --> E[Free Gas in Gas Cap m times N times Boi minus remaining oil × Bo in scf]
    E --> F[Material Balance Equation]
    D --> F
    F --> G[Reservoir Pressure Decline Trend]
    F --> H[Hydrocarbon Pore Volume Injected minus Produced]
    H --> I[Reserves Estimation]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**
*Define associated gas. How does it differ from non-associated gas in terms of reservoir occurrence and composition?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Associated gas is the natural gas that occurs in a petroleum reservoir in physical association with crude oil — either dissolved in the oil at reservoir pressure or present as a free gas cap above the oil column. **Non-associated gas**, in contrast, is produced from reservoirs that contain little to no liquid hydrocarbon. Compositionally, non-associated gas is typically **drier** (predominantly $\text{C}_1$, with very low $\text{C}_2+$ content) whereas associated gas is **wetter** (contains recoverable amounts of ethane, propane, butane, and condensate-grade pentanes plus). (3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for reservoir occurrence, 1 for compositional contrast.)

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List four commercial utilisation pathways for associated gas and state one environmental benefit of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — any 4 of the following):**

1. **Pipeline sales gas** — displaces coal-based power generation, reducing $\text{CO}_2$ emissions.
2. **LNG export** — displaces fuel oil in international shipping, lowering $\text{SO}_x$ emissions.
3. **NGL recovery** — recovers propane/LPG, displacing crude-derived LPG refining.
4. **GTL (gas-to-liquids)** — produces clean-burning synthetic diesel, displacing high-sulfur crude derivatives.
5. **Re-injection for EOR** — improves oil recovery while sequestering $\text{CO}_2$ (if co-injected), reducing atmospheric methane emissions.
6. **CNG for transport** — replaces petrol/diesel in vehicles, cutting urban air pollution.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and follows the KTU 2024 Scheme pattern of two sub-parts worth 7 marks each. Students must answer **either Question A or Question B**.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** *Derive the real-gas equation of state and explain why the compressibility factor $Z$ deviates from unity at typical reservoir conditions. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *An associated gas sample occupies $V = 0.5 \text{ ft}^3$ at $P = 2500 \text{ psia}$ and $T = 150^\circ \text{F}$. The gas specific gravity is $\gamma_g = 0.72$ and the compressibility factor at these conditions is $Z = 0.86$. Calculate the gas volume at standard conditions. (7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

Begin with the **ideal gas law** and introduce the empirical correction factor $Z$:

$$
PV = nRT
$$

At high reservoir pressures, real hydrocarbon gases exhibit **non-ideal behaviour** due to:
- **Intermolecular attractive forces** (van der Waals, dominant at moderate pressures) — these pull molecules closer, reducing pressure below the ideal value, hence $Z < 1$.
- **Repulsive forces at very high pressure** (excluded-volume effect) — molecules cannot be compressed indefinitely, hence $Z > 1$ at very high $P$.
- **Molecular volume and polarity** of heavier hydrocarbons ($\text{C}_2+$) increase deviation.

The **real-gas law** therefore becomes:

$$
\boxed{\,PV = ZnRT\,}
$$

where $Z$ is read from the **Dranchuk-Abou-Kassem (DAK)** equation or a Standing-Katz chart. Typical $Z$ values: 0.80–0.95 at reservoir conditions; 1.0 at standard conditions.

(Mark split: 2 for equation, 3 for explanation of physical reasons, 2 for typical Z range.)

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:** $V = 0.5 \text{ ft}^3$, $P = 2500 \text{ psia}$, $T = 150^\circ\text{F} = 610^\circ\text{R}$, $\gamma_g = 0.72$, $Z = 0.86$.

**Step 1 — Compute moles of gas (using real-gas law):**

$$
n = \dfrac{PV}{ZRT} = \dfrac{2500 \times 0.5}{0.86 \times 10.732 \times 610}
$$

$$
n = \dfrac{1250}{0.86 \times 6545.32} = \dfrac{1250}{5628.98} = 0.2221 \; \text{lbmol}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute standard volume (at $P_{sc} = 14.696$ psia, $T_{sc} = 520^\circ$R, $Z_{sc} = 1.0$):**

$$
V_{sc} = \dfrac{Z_{sc}\,n\,R\,T_{sc}}{P_{sc}} = \dfrac{1.0 \times 0.2221 \times 10.732 \times 520}{14.696}
$$

$$
V_{sc} = \dfrac{1239.0}{14.696} = \mathbf{84.31 \; \text{scf}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students frequently forget to convert $150^\circ\text{F}$ to $610^\circ\text{R}$ (must add **459.67**, not 460), and often drop the $Z$ factor in the denominator of the $n$ equation. Both errors cost **2 marks each** in KTU 2024 valuation. Always show unit conversions explicitly.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** *With the aid of a P-T phase envelope diagram, explain the path followed by an associated gas-oil system as it travels from the reservoir through production tubing to the stock tank. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *A reservoir fluid has $T = 200^\circ\text{F}$, $\text{API} = 40^\circ$, $\gamma_g = 0.78$. Estimate the solution gas-oil ratio $R_s$ at $P = 3000 \text{ psia}$ using Standing's correlation. (7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Phase Envelope Explanation (7 marks):**

The associated gas-oil system is represented on a **Pressure–Temperature (P-T) phase envelope** (also called a phase diagram or two-phase region plot). Key features:

- **Bubble-point curve**: separates single-phase liquid (oil) region from two-phase region. Reservoir point lies **above** this curve (higher pressure, liquid region).
- **Dew-point curve**: separates single-phase gas (vapour) region from two-phase region. Stock tank point lies **below** this curve.
- **Critical point**: where bubble and dew curves meet.
- **Cricondentherm**: maximum temperature at which two phases can coexist.

**Path traced by the fluid:**

1. **Reservoir ($P_r, T_r$)**: Single-phase oil with all gas dissolved. Point is **above** the bubble-point curve.
2. **Production tubing**: Pressure drops along the tubing due to hydrostatic and friction losses. Temperature also changes slightly (Joule-Thomson cooling for gas, viscous heating for oil). The path crosses the **bubble-point curve**, and free gas starts evolving.
3. **Wellhead/Separator**: Multi-phase flow enters HP separator. Further depressurization.
4. **Stock tank**: Final stage at atmospheric pressure. Point lies **below** the dew-point curve — the residual fluid is in the gas region (essentially all liquid components are in the stock tank oil; only light ends remain as vent gas).

(Mark split: 3 for diagram, 2 for path description, 2 for correct identification of single-phase vs two-phase regions.)

**Part (b) — Standing's Correlation (7 marks):**

**Given:** $T = 200^\circ\text{F}$, $\text{API} = 40^\circ$, $\gamma_g = 0.78$, $P = 3000 \text{ psia}$.

**Step 1 — Compute the temperature–API exponent:**

$$
0.00091 \times 200 = 0.182
$$
$$
0.0125 \times 40 = 0.500
$$
$$
0.182 - 0.500 = -0.318
$$
$$
10^{-0.318} = 10^{0.318 \times (-1)} = 0.4810
$$
$$
18 \times 0.4810 = 8.658
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the pressure ratio:**

$$
\dfrac{P}{18 \times 10^{(0.00091\,T - 0.0125\,\text{API})}} = \dfrac{3000}{8.658} = 346.50
$$

**Step 3 — Raise to power 1.2048:**

$$
346.50^{1.2048} = e^{1.2048\,\ln(346.50)} = e^{1.2048 \times 5.8480} = e^{7.0465} = 1148.4
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by gas specific gravity:**

$$
R_s = 0.78 \times 1148.4 = \mathbf{895.7 \;\; \text{scf/STB}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** A common error is to add $0.00091\,T$ and $0.0125\,\text{API}$ with the **same sign**. Standing's correlation uses **subtraction** ($0.00091\,T - 0.0125\,\text{API}$). Many students drop the 1.2048 exponent or round too early. Show all intermediate steps for full credit. Losing 2 marks for arithmetic errors and 1 mark for missing units is typical.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Scheme — Module 2: Associated Gas**

- **Definition**: Associated gas = natural gas co-existing with crude oil (dissolved + free gas cap).
- **Two sub-types**: Solution gas (dissolved in oil) vs Gas-cap gas (free phase above oil).
- **Standard conditions (field)**: $P_{sc} = 14.696 \text{ psia}$, $T_{sc} = 60^\circ\text{F} = 520^\circ\text{R}$.
- **Real-gas law**: $PV = ZnRT$ — always include $Z$ at reservoir conditions.
- **Standing's correlation**: $R_s = \gamma_g \left[\dfrac{P}{18 \times 10^{(0.00091\,T - 0.0125\,\text{API})}}\right]^{1.2048}$ — most-quoted formula.
- **Bubble-point pressure $P_b$**: maximum pressure at which all gas stays dissolved in oil.
- **GOR**: $R_{sp} = V_{gas}^{sc} / V_{oil}^{st}$ — measure of gas liberated per barrel of stock-tank oil.
- **Oil FVF (Standing)**: $B_o$ increases with dissolved gas (oil swells).
- **Compressibility factor $Z$**: read from DAK correlation or Standing-Katz chart; $\approx 0.85$ at typical reservoir conditions.
- **Phase envelope**: reservoir point above bubble-point curve, stock tank below dew-point curve.
- **Composition**: associated gas is **wet** (recoverable $\text{C}_2+$), non-associated gas is **dry**.
- **Utilisation hierarchy (highest to lowest economic value)**: NGL → LNG → Pipeline sales → GTL → CNG → Power gen → Re-injection → Flaring (last resort).
- **Environmental angle**: Methane GWP = 28–34 × $\text{CO}_2$ (100-yr horizon). Capture, don't flare.
- **Key constants to memorize**: $R = 10.732$ (field), $R = 8.314$ (SI), $P_{sc} = 14.696$ psia, $T_{sc} = 520^\circ\text{R}$.
- **Exam traps**: unit conversions ($^\circ\text{F} \to ^\circ\text{R}$ add 459.67), forgetting $Z$ in real-gas calculations, sign error in Standing's exponent, using non-mixed $P_c/T_c$ values.
- **Industry benchmarks**: API 40 oil, $\gamma_g = 0.78$ gas at 3000 psia, 200 °F → $R_s \approx 896 \text{ scf/STB}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Mantra:** *For KTU 2024 Scheme Part B numericals on associated gas, always (1) state the governing equation, (2) list given data with units, (3) carry units through every step, (4) box the final answer, and (5) attach a one-line physical interpretation. The interpretation line alone can fetch 1 mark of partial credit in borderline cases.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Non-Associated Gas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT528 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Non-Associated Gas ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Non-Associated Gas (NAG)** is a category of natural gas that occurs in underground geological reservoirs which contain little to no liquid hydrocarbons (crude oil) at the surface conditions specified by standard industry tests. In formal petroleum engineering terminology, a reservoir is classified as producing non-associated gas when the **Gas-Oil Ratio (GOR)** of the produced wellstream exceeds **~ 100,000 scf/stb (standard cubic feet per stock tank barrel)** in the absence of significant hydrocarbon liquids, OR more commonly, the reservoir fluid is classified by Pressure-Temperature (P-T) phase behavior as a **dry gas** or **wet gas** system where liquid dropout is negligible during normal production operations. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 2):** Non-associated gas, also termed *"unassociated gas"* or *"gas well gas,"* refers to free natural gas produced from reservoirs where hydrocarbon liquids are essentially absent. The well stream at the wellhead separator yields gas as the primary marketable product with negligible condensate recovery. ### 1.2 The Four-Class Classification of Natural Gas Natural gas occurrences are broadly classified by the petroleum industry into four primary categories based on reservoir fluid composition and phase behavior: | S.No | Gas Type | Reservoir Liquid Content | Primary Product | |------|----------|--------------------------|-----------------| | 1 | **Associated Gas** | Significant crude oil present | Oil (gas is by-product) | | 2 | **Non-Associated Gas** | Essentially no crude oil | Dry/Wet Gas | | 3 | **Dissolved Gas** | Gas held in solution within oil | Oil (with dissolved gas) | | 4 | **Gas Condensate** | Light liquid hydrocarbons in gas phase | Gas + Condensate liquids | > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction:** The terms *Associated* and *Non-Associated* describe the **physical state of the gas in the reservoir**, not the chemical composition of the gas molecules. Both types of gas have nearly identical molecular make-up (mostly methane), but their geological entrapment differs fundamentally. ### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Balloon-in-a-Box" Model Imagine you have two sealed boxes underground, each containing a pressurized balloon: - **Box A (Associated Gas Reservoir):** The balloon contains a mixture of *gaseous air* and a *liquid syrup (oil)* that sloshes at the bottom. When you puncture this balloon, the gas rushes out, and a small amount of syrup sprays out alongside it. - **Box B (Non-Associated Gas Reservoir):** The balloon contains *purely gaseous air* with no syrup. When punctured, only gas escapes — clean, dry, and free of liquid contaminants. > In this analogy, **Box B represents a Non-Associated Gas reservoir** — where the underground formation is filled almost exclusively with gas molecules (primarily methane, ethane) with no crude oil layer beneath the gas cap. The recovered wellstream is therefore gas-dominant, often requiring minimal liquid-hydrocarbon separation equipment at the surface. > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for Students:** *"No Oil, No Problem"* — Non-Associated Gas means **NO** significant **O**il is associated with the **G**as in the reservoir. ### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics Key reference values used in characterizing Non-Associated Gas: - **Standard Temperature:** **15.56 °C (60 °F)** — KTU/US standard - **Standard Pressure:** **101.325 kPa (14.696 psia)** — 1 atm - **Methane (CH₄) Content:** Typically **85 – 95 mol%** - **Ethane + Heavier (C₂⁺) Content:** Typically **< 5 mol%** for true dry non-associated gas - **Heating Value (HHV):** ~ **1000 – 1100 BTU/scf** for pipeline-quality non-associated gas - **Specific Gravity (air = 1):** ~ **0.55 – 0.70** (lighter than air) ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Geological Origin and Reservoir Characteristics Non-associated gas accumulations form in porous, permeable rock formations (typically **sandstones, limestones, or dolomites**) that are overlain by an impermeable caprock. The key geological conditions required for a non-associated gas reservoir are: 1. **Source Rock Maturity:** Organic-rich shale or coal that has reached the **gas generation window** (vitrinite reflectance **Ro > 1.0%**). 2. **Migration Path:** A permeable carrier bed that allows buoyant gas to migrate upward through the formation water. 3. **Reservoir Rock:** A porous (porosity **φ > 6%**) and permeable (permeability **k > 0.1 mD**) layer that traps the gas in its pore spaces. 4. **Seal/Caprock:** An impermeable stratum (typically shale or salt) preventing further vertical gas migration. 5. **Trap Geometry:** Either **structural traps** (anticlines, fault blocks) or **stratigraphic traps** (pinch-outs, reefs). ### 2.2 Composition Profile of Non-Associated Gas The composition varies depending on the source rock kerogen type and thermal maturity. A typical molar composition is: $$ y_i = \frac{n_i}{\sum_{j=1}^{N} n_j} $$ where $y_i$ is the mole fraction of component $i$, $n_i$ is the moles of component $i$, and $N$ is the total number of components. > [!NOTE] > **Dry Non-Associated Gas:** C₂⁺ content < 1 mol% — often termed *"pipeline-quality"* or *"sales-quality"* gas. > **Wet Non-Associated Gas:** C₂⁺ content > 1 mol% but reservoir pressure is high enough that liquids do not drop out in the reservoir — recovered at surface as gas with recoverable NGL (Natural Gas Liquids). ### 2.3 Phase Behavior — Why "Dry" Gas Behaves the Way It Does The phase behavior of a non-associated gas reservoir is governed by the **Pressure-Temperature (P-T) diagram** of its hydrocarbon mixture: - The **cricondentherm** (maximum temperature of two-phase region) is below the reservoir temperature for **dry gas** systems. - This means as the gas is produced and pressure declines (moving vertically down on the P-T diagram at constant reservoir temperature), it **never crosses the two-phase envelope** — no liquids condense in the reservoir or in the wellbore. $$ T_{\text{reservoir}} > T_{\text{cricondentherm}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Dry Non-Associated Gas} $$ ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula / Parameter | Mathematical Expression | Units | Application | |---|--------------------|--------------------------|-------|-------------| | 1 | **Real Gas Law** | $PV = ZnRT$ | P: psia, V: ft³, T: °R | PVT behavior of NAG | | 2 | **Gas Compressibility Factor (Z)** | $Z = \frac{PV}{nRT}$ | Dimensionless | Deviation from ideal gas | | 3 | **Pseudo-Reduced Properties** | $P_{pr} = P / P_{pc}$, $T_{pr} = T / T_{pc}$ | Dimensionless | Standing-Katz chart for Z | | 4 | **Gas Formation Volume Factor** | $B_g = 0.02827 \cdot \frac{ZT}{P}$ | rb/scf (res bbl/std ft³) | Reserves calculation | | 5 | **Gas Density (Reservoir)** | $\rho_g = \frac{PM_{air}\gamma_g}{ZR T}$ | lb/ft³ | In-situ gas density | | 6 | **Material Balance (Volumetric Dry Gas)** | $\frac{P}{Z} = \frac{P_i}{Z_i}\left(1 - \frac{G_p}{G}\right)$ | Dimensionless | Original Gas In Place (OGIP) | | 7 | **Original Gas In Place (Volumetric)** | $G = \frac{43560 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_{wi})}{B_{gi}}$ | scf | Static reserves estimate | | 8 | **Heating Value (HHV)** | $HHV = \sum y_i \cdot (HHV)_i$ | BTU/scf | Gas sales specification | | 9 | **Specific Gravity** | $\gamma_g = \frac{M_{gas}}{M_{air}} = \frac{M_{gas}}{28.97}$ | Dimensionless | Gas characterization | | 10 | **Pseudo-Critical Properties (Sutton)** | $P_{pc} = 756.8 - 131.07\gamma_g - 3.6\gamma_g^2$ | psia | For natural gas mixtures | | 11 | **Pseudo-Critical Properties (Sutton)** | $T_{pc} = 169.2 + 349.5\gamma_g - 74.0\gamma_g^2$ | °R | For natural gas mixtures | | 12 | **Cricondentherm Approximation** | $T_{cric} \approx \sum y_i \cdot T_{c,i}$ | °F or K | Phase envelope estimation | | 13 | **Gas Well Productivity** | $q_g = \frac{kh(P_r^2 - P_{wf}^2)}{1422 \cdot T \cdot \mu_g \cdot Z \left[\ln(r_e/r_w) - 0.75 + s\right]}$ | Mscf/day | IPR for gas wells | | 14 | **Recovery Factor (Pressure Depletion)** | $RF = 1 - \frac{P_{ab}/Z_{ab}}{P_i/Z_i}$ | Fraction | Expected gas recovery | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Avoid using the vertical bar | inside table cells** — LaTeX delimiters for absolute value must be written as \vert or \mid to prevent table syntax errors. ### 2.5 Real-World Utility of Non-Associated Gas Non-associated gas reservoirs are the **backbone of the global natural gas supply chain** because: 1. **Simpler surface processing** — no oil-water separation train needed. 2. **Higher deliverability per well** — large gas columns allow high flow rates. 3. **Predictable recovery** — volumetric drive mechanism gives **> 80% recovery factor** typically. 4. **Pipeline-quality gas at wellhead** — minimal treatment to meet grid specs. 5. **Strategic reserves** — many LNG terminals (e.g., Qatar's North Field, Russia's Yamburg) source from massive non-associated gas fields. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Original Gas In Place (OGIP) for a Volumetric Non-Associated Gas Reservoir The fundamental equation for static gas reserves in a non-associated gas reservoir is derived from the **pore-volume occupied by gas at reservoir conditions**, converted to surface conditions using the gas formation volume factor. **Starting Principle:** The hydrocarbon pore volume (HCPV) of the gas-bearing formation is given by: $$ HCPV = A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_{wi}) $$ where: - $A$ = areal extent of the gas-bearing formation, **ft²** - $h$ = average net pay thickness, **ft** - $\phi$ = effective porosity, **fraction** - $S_{wi}$ = irreducible water saturation, **fraction** **Step 1: Convert HCPV to surface-condition gas volume using Bgi** The gas formation volume factor $B_{gi}$ at initial reservoir conditions is defined as: $$ B_{gi} = \frac{V_{rc}}{V_{sc}} = 0.02827 \cdot \frac{Z_i T}{P_i} \quad \text{[rb/scf]} $$ Therefore, the volume of gas at surface conditions contained in the HCPV is: $$ G = \frac{HCPV}{B_{gi}} $$ **Step 2: Substitute HCPV expression** $$ G = \frac{A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_{wi})}{B_{gi}} $$ **Step 3: Insert unit conversion (A in acres, h in ft)** Since 1 acre = 43,560 ft², the final working equation becomes: $$ \boxed{\,G = \frac{43560 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_{wi})}{B_{gi}}\, \text{[scf]} \,} $$ **Step 4: Practical form with $B_{gi}$ substituted** $$ G = \frac{43560 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_{wi}) \cdot P_i}{0.02827 \cdot Z_i \cdot T} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** This equation assumes a **volumetric (closed) reservoir** with no active water drive. For non-associated gas reservoirs, this is usually a valid approximation because gas compressibility is much higher than water compressibility, and the gas expansion is the dominant drive mechanism. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Material Balance Equation (P/Z vs Gp) For a volumetric dry-gas reservoir, the total mass (or moles) of gas is conserved. At any time *t*, the remaining gas in the reservoir occupies the same pore volume (rock and water are essentially incompressible), but at a lower pressure. **Conservation of moles (reservoir = produced + remaining):** $$ n_i = n_p + n_r $$ **Express each in terms of PV/ZRT:** $$ \frac{P_i V}{Z_i R T} = \frac{P V}{Z R T} + G_p \cdot \frac{1}{R T_{sc}} \cdot \frac{1}{P_{sc}/(Z_{sc} T_{sc})} $$ **Since V, R, T are constant (volumetric reservoir), they cancel:** $$ \frac{P_i}{Z_i} = \frac{P}{Z} + \frac{G_p}{V} \cdot \frac{P_{sc}}{T_{sc}} \cdot T $$ **Substituting the definition of G = V / Bgi (where Bgi uses reservoir T):** $$ \frac{P}{Z} = \frac{P_i}{Z_i} \left(1 - \frac{G_p}{G}\right) $$ **Final linearized form (used to extrapolate OGIP from production data):** $$ \boxed{\, \frac{P}{Z} = \frac{P_i}{Z_i} - \left(\frac{P_i}{Z_i \cdot G}\right) G_p \,} $$ > Plotting $\left(\frac{P}{Z}\right)$ vs $G_p$ yields a **straight line** whose x-intercept (when P/Z = 0) equals the **Original Gas In Place (G)** — a cornerstone technique in gas reservoir engineering. --- ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU-Style Calculation **Given:** A non-associated gas reservoir has the following properties: | Parameter | Value | |-----------|-------| | Area, $A$ | 1500 acres | | Net pay, $h$ | 50 ft | | Porosity, $\phi$ | 0.18 | | Irreducible water saturation, $S_{wi}$ | 0.25 | | Initial reservoir pressure, $P_i$ | 3500 psia | | Reservoir temperature, $T$ | 200 °F = 660 °R | | Gas specific gravity, $\gamma_g$ | 0.65 | | Z-factor at $P_i$, $Z_i$ | 0.92 | | Abandonment pressure, $P_{ab}$ | 500 psia | | Z-factor at $P_{ab}$, $Z_{ab}$ | 0.96 | **Required:** (a) Original Gas In Place, $G$; (b) Recovery Factor at abandonment. --- **Step A1: Compute pseudo-critical properties using Sutton's correlation** $$ P_{pc} = 756.8 - 131.07(0.65) - 3.6(0.65)^2 $$ $$ P_{pc} = 756.8 - 85.20 - 1.52 = 670.08 \text{ psia} $$ $$ T_{pc} = 169.2 + 349.5(0.65) - 74.0(0.65)^2 $$ $$ T_{pc} = 169.2 + 227.18 - 31.27 = 365.11 \text{ °R} $$ **Step A2: Compute $B_{gi}$** $$ B_{gi} = 0.02827 \cdot \frac{Z_i \cdot T}{P_i} = 0.02827 \cdot \frac{0.92 \cdot 660}{3500} $$ $$ B_{gi} = 0.02827 \cdot \frac{607.2}{3500} = 0.02827 \cdot 0.1735 = 0.004904 \text{ rb/scf} $$ **Step A3: Compute OGIP (G)** $$ G = \frac{43560 \cdot 1500 \cdot 50 \cdot 0.18 \cdot (1 - 0.25)}{0.004904} $$ $$ G = \frac{43560 \cdot 1500 \cdot 50 \cdot 0.18 \cdot 0.75}{0.004904} $$ $$ G = \frac{4.409 \times 10^8}{0.004904} = 8.99 \times 10^{10} \text{ scf} \approx \mathbf{89.9 \, Bscf} $$ --- **Step B1: Compute Recovery Factor** $$ RF = 1 - \frac{(P_{ab}/Z_{ab})}{(P_i/Z_i)} = 1 - \frac{500/0.96}{3500/0.92} $$ $$ RF = 1 - \frac{520.83}{3804.35} = 1 - 0.1369 = 0.8631 $$ $$ \boxed{\, RF \approx \mathbf{86.3 \%} \,} $$ **Step B2: Recoverable reserves** $$ G_p = RF \cdot G = 0.8631 \cdot 89.9 \text{ Bscf} \approx \mathbf{77.6 \, Bscf} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Verification of Dry-Gas Behavior:** The very high recovery factor (~86%) confirms that the depletion drive mechanism is highly efficient for non-associated gas reservoirs — a key reason they are economically attractive even at low wellhead prices. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Gas Reservoir Types | Property | Non-Associated Gas | Associated Gas | Gas Condensate | |----------|-------------------|----------------|----------------| | **Reservoir Liquid Phase** | None | Oil + free gas cap | Retrograde liquids | | **GOR (scf/stb)** | Infinite (no oil) | 200 – 7000 | 5000 – 100,000 | | **C₂⁺ Content (mol%)** | < 1 (dry) to 5 (wet) | Variable | > 5 (rich) | | **Surface Product** | Gas only | Oil + gas | Gas + condensate | | **Reservoir Drive** | Volumetric depletion | Solution gas / gas cap | Volumetric / compaction | | **Recovery Factor** | 80 – 90% | 20 – 40% | 65 – 80% | | **Surface Processing** | Minimal | Full 3-phase separation | Stabilization column required | | **Example Field** | North Field, Qatar | Many Middle East fields | South Pars (condensate cap) | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Reservoir Architecture — Non-Associated vs Associated Gas The block diagram below illustrates the geological architecture of a non-associated gas reservoir in contrast to an associated gas reservoir, showing the absence of an oil leg in the former. ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph NA["NON-ASSOCIATED GAS RESERVOIR"] direction TB NA1["IMPERMEABLE CAPROCK
Shale or Salt Seal"] NA2["GAS-BEARING SANDSTONE
Porosity 18% - Permeability 50 mD
Pressure 3500 psia - T 200 F"] NA3["100% WATER SATURATED ZONE
Aquifer Below Gas Column"] NA4["IMPERMEABLE BASEMENT ROCK"] NA1 --> NA2 NA2 --> NA3 NA3 --> NA4 NA5["WELLHEAD: GAS ONLY
GAS OIL RATIO INFINITE"] NA2 -.->|Production| NA5 end subgraph AS["ASSOCIATED GAS RESERVOIR"] direction TB AS1["IMPERMEABLE CAPROCK"] AS2["FREE GAS CAP ZONE
Associated Gas"] AS3["OIL LEG
Stock Tank Oil - 35 API"] AS4["WATER ZONE - Aquifer"] AS5["IMPERMEABLE BASEMENT"] AS1 --> AS2 AS2 --> AS3 AS3 --> AS4 AS4 --> AS5 AS6["WELLHEAD: OIL PLUS GAS
3 PHASE SEPARATOR"] AS2 -.->|Production| AS6 AS3 -.->|Production| AS6 end ``` ### 4.2 Surface Processing Flow — Non-Associated Gas The surface processing train for a non-associated gas well is significantly simpler than for associated gas, as no oil/water separation is required at the wellhead. ```mermaid flowchart LR A["WELLHEAD
CHRISTMAS TREE"] --> B["INLET SEPARATOR
Slug Catcher"] B --> C["GAS DEHYDRATION
TEG Contactor"] C --> D["ACID GAS REMOVAL
Amine Unit"] D --> E["MERCURY AND SULFUR
Removal Beds"] E --> F["DEW POINT CONTROL
J T Valve"] F --> G["PIPELINE
Sales Gas 1000 BTU scf"] H["SALES GAS TO CUSTOMER"] --> G F --> I["NGL RECOVERY
Cryogenic Plant"] I --> J["ETHANE PROPANE BUTANE
Stabilized Liquids"] ``` ### 4.3 Production Lifecycle of a Non-Associated Gas Reservoir ```mermaid flowchart TB P1["PHASE 1: DISCOVERY
Initial P 3500 psia
Q 50 MMscf day"] --> P2["PHASE 2: DELINEATION
Appraisal Wells and
PVT Sampling"] P2 --> P3["PHASE 3: DEVELOPMENT
Drilling and Platform
Installation"] P3 --> P4["PHASE 4: PLATEAU PRODUCTION
Stable P Z - Constant Rate"] P4 --> P5["PHASE 5: DECLINE
P Z Decreases Linearly
Rate Declines"] P5 --> P6["PHASE 6: ABANDONMENT
P 500 psia
RF Greater Than 80%"] P6 --> P7["WELL PLUG AND ABANDON
Site Remediation"] ``` ### 4.4 P/Z Material Balance Plot — Diagnostic for Non-Associated Gas The diagnostic P/Z vs cumulative production plot is the **signature tool** for confirming that a reservoir behaves as a non-associated dry gas system. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph X["PLOT AXES"] X1["X AXIS: Cumulative Gas
Production Gp in Bscf"] X2["Y AXIS: P divided by Z
P Z in psia"] end subgraph L["LINEAR PLOT"] L1["Data Point 1
Initial P Z 3800"] L2["Data Point 2
Mid Life P Z 2200"] L3["Data Point 3
Late Life P Z 800"] L4["Extrapolated Line
X Intercept is G"] end L1 --> L2 --> L3 L1 -.->|Linear Fit| L4 ``` > [!TIP] > **Why this matters:** A straight-line P/Z trend (without curvature) confirms **single-tank volumetric behavior** typical of non-associated gas. Any downward deviation suggests water influx; upward deviation suggests formation compressibility effects. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Define Non-Associated Gas. How is it distinguished from Associated Gas in terms of reservoir phase behavior?* **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (2 marks):** Non-associated gas is natural gas produced from underground reservoirs in which there is no significant amount of crude oil present. The reservoir contains only gas (and formation water), and the produced wellstream consists essentially of dry or wet gas with negligible hydrocarbon liquid content. **Distinction (1 mark):** Unlike associated gas, which exists as a free gas cap above an oil leg in the same reservoir, non-associated gas occupies a reservoir where no producible oil column exists. The phase behavior of non-associated gas lies entirely outside the two-phase envelope during pressure depletion — no liquid hydrocarbons drop out in the reservoir or wellbore. --- **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *List the typical composition and key physical properties of dry non-associated natural gas.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Component / Property | Typical Value | |----------------------|---------------| | Methane (CH₄) | 85 – 95 mol% | | Ethane (C₂H₆) | 1 – 5 mol% | | Propane + Heavier (C₃⁺) | < 1 mol% | | Nitrogen (N₂) | < 2 mol% | | CO₂ + H₂S | < 1 mol% (sweet gas) | | Specific Gravity ($\gamma_g$) | 0.55 – 0.70 | | Heating Value (HHV) | 1000 – 1100 BTU/scf | (Composition: 2 marks; Physical properties: 1 mark) --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) --- **Q3A.** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** — *Option A (14 Marks)* **(a)** Discuss in detail the **geological conditions** required for the formation of a non-associated gas reservoir. **[7 marks]** **(b)** A non-associated gas reservoir has the following data: Area = **2000 acres**, net pay $h$ = **40 ft**, porosity $\phi$ = **0.20**, $S_{wi}$ = **0.30**, $P_i$ = **3000 psia**, $T$ = **190 °F**, $\gamma_g$ = **0.62**, $Z_i$ = **0.90**, $P_{ab}$ = **600 psia**, $Z_{ab}$ = **0.95**. Calculate the **OGIP** and the **recoverable gas reserves**. **[7 marks]** **Model Solution:** **(a) Geological Conditions (7 marks):** 1. **Mature source rock** — Organic-rich shale/coal with vitrinite reflectance **Ro > 1.0%** that has entered the gas generation window. (1 mark) 2. **Migration pathway** — Permeable carrier beds (sandstone, fractured carbonate) allowing buoyant gas to migrate upward from the source rock. (1 mark) 3. **Reservoir rock** — Porous ($\phi$ > 6%) and permeable (k > 0.1 mD) lithology — typically sandstone, limestone, or dolomite — that can store large volumes of gas in its pore spaces. (1 mark) 4. **Impermeable seal/caprock** — A continuous layer of shale, evaporite (salt/anhydrite), or tight mudstone that prevents further vertical gas migration. (1 mark) 5. **Geological trap** — Structural (anticline, fault block) or stratigraphic (reef, pinch-out) trap geometry to concentrate the gas accumulation. (1 mark) 6. **Hydrocarbon charge** — Sufficient volume of gas generated and migrated into the trap, displacing formation water from the pore space. (1 mark) 7. **Timing** — Trap formation must pre-date or be contemporaneous with gas migration to ensure entrapment. (1 mark) **(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1: Pseudo-critical properties (Sutton)** $$ P_{pc} = 756.8 - 131.07(0.62) - 3.6(0.62)^2 = 756.8 - 81.26 - 1.38 = 674.16 \text{ psia} $$ $$ T_{pc} = 169.2 + 349.5(0.62) - 74.0(0.62)^2 = 169.2 + 216.69 - 28.45 = 357.44 \text{ °R} $$ **Step 2: Compute $B_{gi}$ (T = 190 + 460 = 650 °R)** $$ B_{gi} = 0.02827 \cdot \frac{Z_i \cdot T}{P_i} = 0.02827 \cdot \frac{0.90 \cdot 650}{3000} $$ $$ B_{gi} = 0.02827 \cdot \frac{585}{3000} = 0.02827 \cdot 0.195 = 0.005513 \text{ rb/scf} $$ **Step 3: Compute OGIP (G)** $$ G = \frac{43560 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_{wi})}{B_{gi}} $$ $$ G = \frac{43560 \cdot 2000 \cdot 40 \cdot 0.20 \cdot (1 - 0.30)}{0.005513} $$ $$ G = \frac{4.8787 \times 10^8}{0.005513} = 8.85 \times 10^{10} \text{ scf} $$ $$ \boxed{\, G \approx \mathbf{88.5 \text{ Bscf}} \,} $$ **Step 4: Compute Recovery Factor** $$ RF = 1 - \frac{P_{ab}/Z_{ab}}{P_i/Z_i} = 1 - \frac{600/0.95}{3000/0.90} $$ $$ RF = 1 - \frac{631.58}{3333.33} = 1 - 0.1895 = 0.8105 $$ $$ \boxed{\, RF \approx \mathbf{81.05\%} \,} $$ **Step 5: Recoverable Reserves** $$ G_p = RF \cdot G = 0.8105 \cdot 88.5 = 71.7 \text{ Bscf} $$ $$ \boxed{\, G_p \approx \mathbf{71.7 \text{ Bscf}} \,} $$ --- **Q3B.** **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** — *Option B (14 Marks)* **(a)** Explain the **P/Z material balance method** for estimating original gas in place in a non-associated gas reservoir. Why does this method give a linear plot for dry gas reservoirs? **[7 marks]** **(b)** With a neat sketch, describe the **surface processing train** for non-associated natural gas. Mention the purpose of each unit. **[7 marks]** **Model Solution:** **(a) P/Z Material Balance Method (7 marks):** **Derivation Summary (3 marks):** For a volumetric (no water influx) dry gas reservoir, conservation of moles gives: $$ \frac{P}{Z} = \frac{P_i}{Z_i} \left(1 - \frac{G_p}{G}\right) $$ Rearranged in linear form: $$ \frac{P}{Z} = \frac{P_i}{Z_i} - \left(\frac{P_i}{Z_i \cdot G}\right) G_p $$ **Procedure (2 marks):** 1. Record cumulative production $G_p$ at several times. 2. Measure corresponding static reservoir pressures $P$. 3. Determine $Z$ at each $P$ using Standing-Katz chart. 4. Plot $\left(\frac{P}{Z}\right)$ on y-axis vs $G_p$ on x-axis. 5. Fit a straight line; the x-intercept (where $P/Z = 0$) gives **OGIP = G**. **Why Linear (2 marks):** The plot is linear because the pore volume remains essentially constant (rock and water are incompressible compared to gas) and there is no water influx. As gas is produced, the pressure decline follows a simple material balance proportional to remaining gas volume. Any deviation from linearity indicates water influx (downward curve) or abnormal formation compressibility (upward curve), which are *not* typical of pure non-associated dry gas behavior. **(b) Surface Processing Train (7 marks):** The processing sequence for non-associated gas from wellhead to pipeline: 1. **Wellhead/Christmas Tree** (0.5 mark) — Controls flow; provides safety shut-in; choke regulates flow rate. 2. **Inlet Separator / Slug Catcher** (0.5 mark) — Removes free liquids, sand, and any carryover water; stabilizes intermittent slug flow. 3. **Gas Dehydration Unit (TEG Contactor)** (1 mark) — Removes water vapor using Tri-Ethylene Glycol to prevent hydrate formation in pipelines (spec: < 7 lb water/MMscf). 4. **Acid Gas Removal (Amine Unit)** (1 mark) — Removes H₂S and CO₂ using MEA/MDEA solvent to meet sales gas spec (H₂S < 4 ppm, CO₂ < 2 mol%). 5. **Mercury Removal Bed** (1 mark) — Protects downstream cryogenic equipment from mercury amalgamation and embrittlement of aluminum heat exchangers. 6. **Sulfur Removal / Molecular Sieve** (1 mark) — Final polishing for sulfur compounds and trace contaminants. 7. **Dew Point Control (J-T Valve / Turboexpander)** (1 mark) — Reduces gas temperature and pressure to drop out heavier hydrocarbons, controlling hydrocarbon dew point for pipeline spec. 8. **Sales Gas Custody Meter** (0.5 mark) — Measures gas volume (typically orifice or ultrasonic meter) for commercial billing. 9. **Optional: NGL Recovery Plant** (0.5 mark) — Cryogenic separation recovers ethane, propane, butane as valuable liquids. **Key Feature (0.5 mark):** Notice the **absence of oil-water separation train** and **stabilizer column** — this simplicity is the economic advantage of non-associated gas over associated gas systems. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deductions:** > 1. **Wrong units in OGIP formula:** Students often forget the **43,560** acre-ft² conversion factor. Always check that $A$ is in **acres**, $h$ in **ft**, and final answer is in **scf**. > 2. **Temperature in °R not °F:** The real gas law requires **absolute temperature**. Convert °F to °R by adding **460** (or °C to K by adding 273.15). > 3. **Z-factor ignored or assumed = 1:** At reservoir pressures above 2000 psia, $Z$ deviates significantly from 1. Always compute $P_{pr}$ and $T_{pr}$ and use the Standing-Katz chart. > 4. **Mixing up associated and non-associated gas definitions:** Marks lost if student defines non-associated gas as *"gas dissolved in oil"* — that is **dissolved gas**, a different category. > 5. **P/Z plot not extrapolated correctly:** The straight line must be extended to the x-axis (not stopped at last data point) to find G. > 6. **Sutton's correlation not memorized:** Examiners expect students to recall these — practice writing them 5 times before the exam. > 7. **No mention of material balance assumption (volumetric):** If you write the MB equation, always state the underlying assumption to earn full credit. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Yield Rapid Revision Checklist — Non-Associated Gas** **Core Concepts:** - ☐ Non-associated gas = natural gas from a reservoir with **no significant oil leg**. - ☐ Other names: *unassociated gas*, *gas well gas*. - ☐ Primary product at surface: **gas** (dry or wet), not oil. - ☐ Reservoir GOR is **infinite** (or > 100,000 scf/stb) — no stock-tank liquid barrels to normalize. - ☐ Typical composition: **85–95% CH₄**, very low C₂⁺. **Geological Requirements (5 conditions):** - ☐ Mature source rock (Ro > 1.0%) - ☐ Migration pathway - ☐ Porous, permeable reservoir rock - ☐ Impermeable seal (caprock) - ☐ Structural or stratigraphic trap **Phase Behavior:** - ☐ Dry gas: $T_{reservoir}$ **>** cricondentherm → no liquid dropout on depletion. - ☐ Wet gas: C₂⁺ > 1 mol% but still single-phase in reservoir;
