NGL recovered at surface. - ☐ Result: **High recovery factor (80–90%)** via volumetric depletion drive. **Key Equations to Memorize:** - ☐ $G = \frac{43560 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1-S_{wi})}{B_{gi}}$ — Static OGIP formula. - ☐ $\frac{P}{Z} = \frac{P_i}{Z_i}\left(1 - \frac{G_p}{G}\right)$ — Material balance linear form. - ☐ $B_g = 0.02827 \cdot \frac{ZT}{P}$ — Gas formation volume factor. - ☐ $P_{pc} = 756.8 - 131.07\gamma_g - 3.6\gamma_g^2$ — Sutton pseudo-critical pressure. - ☐ $T_{pc} = 169.2 + 349.5\gamma_g - 74.0\gamma_g^2$ — Sutton pseudo-critical temperature. - ☐ $RF = 1 - \frac{(P_{ab}/Z_{ab})}{(P_i/Z_i)}$ — Recovery factor for dry gas. **Distinctions to Remember:** - ☐ Non-associated gas ≠ Dissolved gas (dissolved gas is *in* the oil). - ☐ Non-associated gas ≠ Gas condensate (condensate has liquid dropout in reservoir). - ☐ Non-associated gas has **simpler surface processing** — no oil train, no stabilization. **Economic / Industrial Relevance:** - ☐ Examples: North Field (Qatar), Yamburg (Russia), Groningen (Netherlands). - ☐ Backbone of LNG export industry. - ☐ Preferred for pipeline supply due to consistent composition. **Exam Tips:** - ☐ Always state the **volumetric reservoir assumption** when using the P/Z equation. - ☐ Always convert temperature to **absolute units (°R or K)**. - ☐ In numericals, show the **unit conversion step** explicitly — examiners award partial credit for the setup. - ☐ Know Sutton's correlations by heart — they are examinable as standalone 2-mark questions.

topics.title: Gas Condensate, Unconventional Production of Natural Gas: Coal Bed Methane
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT528 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Gas Condensate, Unconventional Production of Natural Gas: Coal Bed Methane

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Gas Condensate — Formal Definition

**Gas Condensate** (also called *condensate*, *lease condensate*, or *natural gasoline*) is a low-density mixture of hydrocarbon liquids (predominantly **pentanes and heavier — C₅⁺**) that exists as a **gaseous phase** in the reservoir at initial reservoir pressure and temperature, but separates (condenses) into a **free liquid phase** when brought to surface conditions where the pressure and temperature fall **below the dew-point curve** of the hydrocarbon mixture.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A gas condensate reservoir is a unique hydrocarbon accumulation lying **between a dry gas reservoir and an oil reservoir** on the P-T phase diagram. The defining phenomenon is **Retrograde Condensation** — an unusual behavior where *condensation occurs upon pressure reduction* (instead of the usual vaporization on heating).

**Key Properties of Gas Condensate:**

| Property | Typical Range | Units |
|---|---|---|
| API Gravity | **50 – 85 °API** | dimensionless |
| Specific Gravity (relative to water) | **0.6 – 0.8** | dimensionless |
| Color | Water-white to light straw | — |
| GOR (Producing) | **3,000 – 150,000 SCF/STB** | scf/stb |
| Composition | C₁–C₄ (gas phase) + C₅⁺ (liquid phase) | mole fraction |
| Stock Tank Liquid | **30 – 300+ BBL/MMSCF** | bbl/mmscf |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Gas Condensate

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Pressurized Soda Can Analogy"**
>
> Imagine a sealed soda can pressurized with dissolved CO₂. Inside the can, the CO₂ is dissolved (gaseous in solution). When you open the can (pressure drop), the CO₂ escapes and forms bubbles (a new phase). Similarly, in a gas condensate reservoir, the heavier hydrocarbons (C₅⁺) are *dissolved* in the gas at high pressure. As the gas is produced and pressure drops below the **dew point**, the heavy components "come out of solution" and form a **liquid condensate** — even though the temperature hasn't changed. This is counter-intuitive: *cooling a gas normally condenses it, but here isobaric pressure drop causes condensation.*

### 1.3 Unconventional Natural Gas — Overview

**Unconventional gas** refers to natural gas that cannot be produced economically from a conventional porous and permeable reservoir by standard vertical wells. The four major categories are:

1. **Coal Bed Methane (CBM)** — methane adsorbed in coal seams
2. **Shale Gas** — methane trapped in fine-grained shale formations
3. **Tight Gas** — low-permeability sandstone/carbonate formations
4. **Gas Hydrates** — methane trapped in ice-like crystalline structures

### 1.4 Coal Bed Methane (CBM) — Formal Definition

**Coal Bed Methane (CBM)** is **methane (CH₄)** that is generated and stored within **coal seams**. Unlike conventional reservoirs, CBM is stored primarily by **adsorption** onto the **internal microporous surface area** of the coal matrix, not in pore spaces. Coal is therefore both the **source rock** and the **reservoir rock**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Term:** Coal has a unique dual porosity system — a **micropore matrix** (where gas is adsorbed) and a **macropore/cleat system** (natural fractures where gas and water flow to the wellbore). Production requires **dewatering** (removing water) to lower reservoir pressure and trigger **desorption**.

### 1.5 Conceptual Analogy — Coal Bed Methane

> [!NOTE]
> **"The Activated Charcoal Analogy"**
>
> Think of a piece of **activated charcoal** in a fish tank filter. The charcoal has millions of tiny pores that *trap* (adsorb) odor molecules on its surface. Similarly, a lump of coal has an enormous internal surface area (often **> 200 m²/g**) where methane molecules stick like tiny magnets. To release the methane, you must "suck" the water out first (dewatering), which drops the pressure, allowing the methane to "let go" (desorb) from the coal surface, then diffuse through the coal matrix, and finally flow through natural fractures (cleats) to the well.

### 1.6 CBM Reserves in India — Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - India's total CBM resource estimate: **≈ 2,600 BCM (~91.8 TCF)** (as per geological surveys)
> - Major CBM blocks: **Jharkhand (Jharia, Bokaro), West Bengal (Raniganj), Madhya Pradesh (Sohagpur), Gujarat, Rajasthan**
> - CBM production in India commenced in **2007** with Raniganj block
> - Global leaders: **USA, Australia, China, India, Canada**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Gas Condensate — Phase Behavior

#### 2.1.1 P-T Phase Diagram (Critical to KTU)

A pressure-temperature (P-T) phase diagram for a multi-component hydrocarbon system shows two key curves:
- **Bubble-Point Curve (BPC):** separates liquid from two-phase region (lower region)
- **Dew-Point Curve (DPC):** separates gas from two-phase region (upper region)

These two curves meet at the **Critical Point (Cₚ)**, where bubble-point = dew-point.

Two important points exist on the dew-point curve:
- **Cricondentherm (T_cc):** maximum temperature at which two phases can coexist
- **Cricondenbar (P_cc):** maximum pressure at which two phases can coexist

**Reservoir Classification by P-T Location:**

| Reservoir Type | Location on P-T Diagram |
|---|---|
| **Dry Gas** | Reservoir T > T_cc (reservoir always in gas phase) |
| **Wet Gas** | Reservoir T slightly < T_cc, but surface T < T_cc, so liquids form on surface |
| **Gas Condensate** | Reservoir T between critical T and T_cc; reservoir starts in gas, liquid drops out as pressure falls |
| **Volatile Oil** | Reservoir T just below critical T; starts as liquid, gas evolves as P drops |
| **Black Oil** | Reservoir T << critical T; classical oil behavior |

#### 2.1.2 Retrograde Condensation Phenomenon

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Retrograde Condensation** is the unusual process where, at constant reservoir temperature (between critical T and cricondentherm), a reduction in pressure causes **liquid dropout** from a gas phase. This is *retrograde* because it is opposite to normal vaporization behavior.

**Stages of Gas Condensate Reservoir Production:**

1. **Initial State:** Reservoir pressure (Pᵢ) > dew-point pressure (P_d). Reservoir fluid is single-phase gas.
2. **Pressure Decline (Pᵢ → P_d):** Isothermal expansion. No liquid yet. Gas is produced, no liquid at surface.
3. **Below Dew-Point (P < P_d):** **Liquid dropout begins in reservoir!** Heavier hydrocarbons (C₅⁺) condense *inside* the reservoir pores. This liquid is largely **lost (irrecoverable)** because it remains trapped by capillary forces.
4. **Maximum Liquid Dropout:** Occurs at a specific pressure (typically P ≈ 0.6–0.8 × P_d, depending on fluid composition).
5. **Vaporization at Low Pressure:** Below maximum dropout pressure, *further* pressure reduction causes the liquid to re-vaporize (reverse retrograde).

#### 2.1.3 Gas Condensate Surface Separation

The produced gas-condensate mixture goes through a **surface separator train**:
- **High-Pressure Separator (HPS):** Primary gas-liquid separation
- **Low-Pressure Separator (LPS):** Secondary separation
- **Stock Tank:** Final liquid stabilization at atmospheric pressure

The liquid collected at the stock tank is called **Stock Tank Liquid (STL)** or **Stabilized Condensate**, measured in **STB/MMSCF** or **bbl/MMSCF**.

### 2.2 Coal Bed Methane — Theoretical Framework

#### 2.2.1 Origin and Composition of CBM

CBM is formed during the **coalification process** (transformation of plant matter to coal). As coal rank increases, methane generation increases:

$$\text{Peat} \rightarrow \text{Lignite} \rightarrow \text{Sub-bituminous} \rightarrow \text{Bituminous} \rightarrow \text{Anthracite}$$

> **Methane generation capacity increases with rank.** Bituminous and anthracite coals have the highest CBM potential.

**CBM Composition (typical, % by volume):**
- **CH₄ (Methane):** 90–95%
- **CO₂:** 0–10%
- **N₂:** 0–5%
- **C₂⁺ (Ethane +):** < 1%
- **H₂O vapor:** trace

#### 2.2.2 CBM Storage Mechanism — Adsorption

Unlike conventional gas stored by **compression** in pore space, CBM is stored by **adsorption** on the coal surface.

**Types of Sorption:**
- **Adsorption:** Molecules adhere to the *internal surface* of coal micropores (van der Waals forces)
- **Absorption:** Molecules penetrate *into* the bulk coal structure (less significant for CBM)

**Adsorption Isotherms** describe the relationship between gas content and pressure at constant temperature.

#### 2.2.3 Langmuir Isotherm — The Governing Equation

The **Langmuir Isotherm** is the most widely used model for CBM adsorption:

$$V = \frac{V_L \cdot P}{P_L + P}$$

where:
- $V$ = gas content at pressure $P$ (**SCF/ton** or **cc/g**)
- $V_L$ = **Langmuir Volume** — the maximum gas that can be adsorbed at infinite pressure (**SCF/ton**)
- $P_L$ = **Langmuir Pressure** — the pressure at which the adsorbed gas equals $V_L / 2$ (**psia** or **kPa**)
- $P$ = reservoir pressure (**psia** or **kPa**)

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation of Langmuir Parameters:**
> - $V_L$ represents the **adsorption capacity** of the coal (depends on coal rank, composition, moisture, ash)
> - $P_L$ represents the **affinity** of coal for methane (lower $P_L$ = stronger adsorption = harder to desorb)

**Gas Content (G_c) — Critical CBM Property:**

$$G_c = \frac{V_L \cdot P}{P_L + P} \quad [\text{SCF/ton}]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In situ gas content** is determined by laboratory tests (e.g., **USBM direct method**, **Canister desorption test**). KTU students should remember that the **critical desorption pressure (P_cd)** is the pressure at which gas begins to desorb — usually approximated as the **reservoir pressure at the time of coal deposition of methane**, and gas production begins when reservoir pressure drops below $P_{cd}$.

#### 2.2.4 CBM Production Mechanism — Four-Step Process

Unlike conventional gas where one mechanism dominates, CBM production involves a **unique four-step sequential process:**

1. **Step 1: Dewatering (De-pressurization)**
   - Water is pumped out from the cleat system
   - Reduces hydrostatic pressure in the cleats
   - Cleat pressure drops below critical desorption pressure
   - Mostly water production initially; no significant gas yet

2. **Step 2: Desorption**
   - Once cleat pressure < $P_{cd}$, methane desorbs from coal micropore surfaces
   - Langmuir isotherm governs the amount desorbed per unit pressure drop
   - Two-phase flow begins (gas + water)

3. **Step 3: Diffusion**
   - Desorbed methane diffuses from the **micropore matrix** to the **cleat (macropore) system**
   - Governed by **Fick's Law of Diffusion** — a slow, rate-limiting step
   - Characterized by the **diffusion coefficient (D)** and **sorption time (τ)**

4. **Step 4: Flow (Darcy Flow)**
   - Methane in the cleat system flows through the **fracture network** to the wellbore
   - Governed by **Darcy's Law** (similar to conventional gas flow)
   - Cleat permeability (typically **1 – 100+ mD** in fracture, < 0.1 mD in matrix) is the key

> [!NOTE]
> **CBM Production Curve — "The Inverse of Conventional Gas":**
> Conventional gas wells produce at their highest rate initially, then decline. CBM wells have a **reverse curve**:
> - Initial: high water rate, low gas rate
> - Dewatering phase: gas rate gradually increases
> - Peak gas: usually after **1–5 years** of dewatering
> - Long, slow decline (production may continue for **20–30+ years**)

#### 2.2.5 Cleat System in Coal

**Cleats** are natural fractures in coal, formed during coalification:
- **Face Cleats:** Continuous, well-developed fractures (perpendicular to bedding)
- **Butt Cleats:** End abruptly against face cleats (parallel to face cleats, less continuous)
- Form a **perpendicular, orthogonal fracture network**
- Provide pathways for gas and water flow

#### 2.2.6 Comparison: Conventional Gas Reservoir vs CBM Reservoir

| Parameter | Conventional Gas Reservoir | CBM Reservoir |
|---|---|---|
| **Storage mechanism** | Compression in pores | Adsorption on coal surface |
| **Source rock** | Separate (e.g., shale) | Coal itself (self-sourced) |
| **Porosity** | 10–30% | 1–10% (matrix); high in cleats |
| **Permeability** | 1 – 1000+ mD | Matrix: <0.1 mD; Cleats: 1–100 mD |
| **Initial water** | Hydrocarbon saturation high | **100% water saturated** initially |
| **Drive mechanism** | Pressure depletion, water drive | **Dewatering + desorption** |
| **Production profile** | High initial, rapid decline | Low initial, **rising** to peak, slow decline |
| **Gas content equation** | $PV = ZnRT$ | $V = V_L P / (P_L + P)$ |
| **Trapping mechanism** | Cap rock | Adsorption + cleat capillary |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V = \dfrac{V_L \cdot P}{P_L + P}$ | Langmuir Isotherm (CBM gas content) | SCF/ton |
| 2 | $G_c = \dfrac{V_L \cdot P}{P_L + P}$ | In-situ gas content of coal | SCF/ton |
| 3 | $R = \dfrac{V}{V_L} = \dfrac{P}{P_L + P}$ | Langmuir fractional coverage (dimensionless) | — |
| 4 | $q_{sc} = \dfrac{k h (m(\bar{p}) - m(p_{wf}))}{1422 T}$ | Pseudo-pressure gas flow (Darcy, cleats) | Mscf/D |
| 5 | $m(p) = 2 \int_0^p \dfrac{p'}{\mu(p') Z(p')} \, dp'$ | Real gas pseudo-pressure | psi²/cp |
| 6 | $k h = \dfrac{1422 q_{sc} T}{m(\bar{p}) - m(p_{wf})}$ | Deliverability (kh from flow test) | mD·ft |
| 7 | $\phi = \dfrac{V_{pore}}{V_{total}}$ | Porosity (cleat + matrix) | fraction |
| 8 | $GOR = \dfrac{V_{gas}}{V_{oil}}$ | Producing Gas-Oil Ratio | SCF/STB |
| 9 | $STL = f(P, T, \text{sep})$ | Stabilized liquid yield | STB/MMSCF |
| 10 | $\tau = \dfrac{s^2}{D}$ | Sorption time (Fickian diffusion) | days |
| 11 | $P_{cd} \approx P_{L} \cdot \dfrac{G_c / V_L}{1 - G_c / V_L}$ | Critical desorption pressure | psia |
| 12 | $BG = 0.02827 \dfrac{Z T}{P}$ | Gas formation volume factor (used in CBM volumetrics) | RB/scf |

where, in formula 4, the constant 1422 is used for field units (q in Mscf/D, k in mD, h in ft, μ in cp, T in °R).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always state the **units** clearly when using any of the above formulas. Most marks are lost in unit inconsistencies and missing parameter definitions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Langmuir Isotherm from First Principles

The Langmuir isotherm is derived by balancing the **rate of adsorption** onto the coal surface with the **rate of desorption** from the surface at thermodynamic equilibrium.

**Step 1 — Define fractional surface coverage (θ):**

Let $\theta$ = fraction of coal surface covered by adsorbed methane molecules.
Then $(1 - \theta)$ = fraction of surface that is bare (available for adsorption).

**Step 2 — Rate of adsorption:**

Adsorption rate is proportional to the **gas pressure (P)** and the **fraction of bare surface $(1 - \theta)$**:

$$r_{ads} = k_a \cdot P \cdot (1 - \theta)$$

where $k_a$ is the adsorption rate constant.

**Step 3 — Rate of desorption:**

Desorption rate is proportional to the **fraction of covered surface ($\theta$)** and is independent of pressure (since desorbed molecules leave the surface regardless of pressure):

$$r_{des} = k_d \cdot \theta$$

where $k_d$ is the desorption rate constant.

**Step 4 — Apply equilibrium condition:**

At equilibrium, the rate of adsorption equals the rate of desorption:

$$k_a \cdot P \cdot (1 - \theta) = k_d \cdot \theta$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\theta$:**

$$k_a P - k_a P \theta = k_d \theta$$
$$k_a P = \theta (k_d + k_a P)$$
$$\theta = \dfrac{k_a P}{k_d + k_a P}$$

**Step 6 — Define Langmuir constants:**

Define the **Langmuir pressure** as:

$$P_L = \dfrac{k_d}{k_a}$$

Substituting:

$$\theta = \dfrac{P}{P_L + P}$$

**Step 7 — Convert to gas content:**

The actual gas content is the **fractional coverage × maximum monolayer capacity (Langmuir volume, $V_L$)**:

$$V = \theta \cdot V_L$$

$$\boxed{V = \dfrac{V_L \cdot P}{P_L + P}}$$

This is the **Langmuir Isotherm Equation** — the fundamental equation for CBM gas content.

### 3.2 Analytical Example: Gas Content Calculation (Worked Out)

**Problem:** A coal sample is tested in a laboratory. The Langmuir volume $V_L = 600$ SCF/ton, and the Langmuir pressure $P_L = 350$ psia. The current reservoir pressure is $P = 280$ psia. Calculate the gas content of the coal.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

Given:
- $V_L = 600$ SCF/ton
- $P_L = 350$ psia
- $P = 280$ psia

Using the Langmuir equation:

$$G_c = \dfrac{V_L \cdot P}{P_L + P}$$

Substituting values:

$$G_c = \dfrac{600 \cdot 280}{350 + 280}$$

$$G_c = \dfrac{168{,}000}{630}$$

$$\boxed{G_c = 266.67 \text{ SCF/ton}}$$

**Interpretation:** The coal currently holds **266.67 SCF of methane per ton** at the in-situ pressure of 280 psia.

**Maximum possible content (at $P \to \infty$):**

$$\lim_{P \to \infty} G_c = V_L = 600 \text{ SCF/ton}$$

**Content at $P = P_L$ (the half-coverage pressure):**

$$G_c(P = P_L) = \dfrac{V_L \cdot P_L}{P_L + P_L} = \dfrac{V_L}{2} = 300 \text{ SCF/ton}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical check:** Since our current pressure (280 psia) is *less* than $P_L$ (350 psia), the gas content (266.67) is *less* than half of $V_L$ (300). ✓ This is consistent.

### 3.3 Analytical Example: Critical Desorption Pressure

**Problem:** Using the parameters from §3.2, if the gas content of the coal is $G_c = 240$ SCF/ton at a reservoir pressure of 280 psia, find the **critical desorption pressure ($P_{cd}$)**. Assume $V_L = 600$ SCF/ton.

**Solution:**

Step 1: Use the Langmuir equation and solve for P (which equals $P_{cd}$ at gas content $G_c$):

$$G_c = \dfrac{V_L \cdot P_{cd}}{P_L + P_{cd}}$$

Step 2: Rearrange for $P_{cd}$:

$$G_c (P_L + P_{cd}) = V_L \cdot P_{cd}$$
$$G_c P_L + G_c P_{cd} = V_L P_{cd}$$
$$G_c P_L = V_L P_{cd} - G_c P_{cd}$$
$$G_c P_L = P_{cd} (V_L - G_c)$$
$$P_{cd} = \dfrac{G_c P_L}{V_L - G_c}$$

Step 3: Substitute values:

$$P_{cd} = \dfrac{240 \cdot 350}{600 - 240}$$

$$P_{cd} = \dfrac{84{,}000}{360}$$

$$\boxed{P_{cd} = 233.33 \text{ psia}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** Gas production (desorption) will not begin until the **cleat pressure drops below 233.33 psia**. Above this pressure, only water is produced. This is why CBM wells must be **dewatered first** for several months to years before significant gas production begins.

### 3.4 Analytical Example: Gas Condensate Liquid Yield

**Problem:** A gas condensate reservoir produces 1 MMSCF/D of wellhead gas. The wellstream composition (mole fraction) is given below. The surface separator operates at 800 psia and 100°F. Using the equilibrium constants (K-values) at separator conditions, calculate the **stock tank liquid (STL) yield** in STB/MMSCF.

| Component | zᵢ (wellstream) | Kᵢ at sep. cond. |
|---|---|---|
| C₁ | 0.70 | 5.5 |
| C₂ | 0.12 | 2.0 |
| C₃ | 0.08 | 0.80 |
| iC₄ | 0.02 | 0.40 |
| nC₄ | 0.03 | 0.35 |
| C₅⁺ | 0.05 | 0.06 |

**Solution (Flash Calculation Method):**

Step 1: Use the Rachford-Rice equation to find the vapor fraction V/F:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{z_i (K_i - 1)}{1 + (V/F)(K_i - 1)} = 0$$

Let $V/F = \alpha$. Try $\alpha = 0.85$:

$$f(\alpha) = \sum \dfrac{z_i (K_i - 1)}{1 + \alpha (K_i - 1)}$$

Computing each term:

- C₁: $\frac{0.70 (5.5 - 1)}{1 + 0.85 (4.5)} = \frac{3.15}{4.825} = 0.6528$
- C₂: $\frac{0.12 (2.0 - 1)}{1 + 0.85 (1.0)} = \frac{0.12}{1.85} = 0.0649$
- C₃: $\frac{0.08 (0.80 - 1)}{1 + 0.85 (-0.20)} = \frac{-0.016}{0.83} = -0.0193$
- iC₄: $\frac{0.02 (0.40 - 1)}{1 + 0.85 (-0.60)} = \frac{-0.012}{0.49} = -0.0245$
- nC₄: $\frac{0.03 (0.35 - 1)}{1 + 0.85 (-0.65)} = \frac{-0.0195}{0.4475} = -0.0436$
- C₅⁺: $\frac{0.05 (0.06 - 1)}{1 + 0.85 (-0.94)} = \frac{-0.047}{0.201} = -0.2338$

Sum: $0.6528 + 0.0649 - 0.0193 - 0.0245 - 0.0436 - 0.2338 = 0.3965$

The sum is positive, so $\alpha = 0.85$ is too high. Try $\alpha = 0.92$:

Recomputing:
- C₁: $\frac{0.70 \times 4.5}{1 + 0.92 \times 4.5} = \frac{3.15}{5.14} = 0.6128$
- C₂: $\frac{0.12 \times 1.0}{1 + 0.92} = \frac{0.12}{1.92} = 0.0625$
- C₃: $\frac{0.08 \times (-0.20)}{1 - 0.184} = \frac{-0.016}{0.816} = -0.0196$
- iC₄: $\frac{0.02 \times (-0.60)}{1 - 0.552} = \frac{-0.012}{0.448} = -0.0268$
- nC₄: $\frac{0.03 \times (-0.65)}{1 - 0.598} = \frac{-0.0195}{0.402} = -0.0485$
- C₅⁺: $\frac{0.05 \times (-0.94)}{1 - 0.8648} = \frac{-0.047}{0.1352} = -0.3476$

Sum: $0.6128 + 0.0625 - 0.0196 - 0.0268 - 0.0485 - 0.3476 = 0.2328$

Still positive, try $\alpha = 0.96$:

- C₁: $\frac{3.15}{1 + 0.96 \times 4.5} = \frac{3.15}{5.32} = 0.5921$
- C₂: $\frac{0.12}{1 + 0.96} = \frac{0.12}{1.96} = 0.0612$
- C₃: $\frac{-0.016}{1 - 0.192} = \frac{-0.016}{0.808} = -0.0198$
- iC₄: $\frac{-0.012}{1 - 0.576} = \frac{-0.012}{0.424} = -0.0283$
- nC₄: $\frac{-0.0195}{1 - 0.624} = \frac{-0.0195}{0.376} = -0.0519$
- C₅⁺: $\frac{-0.047}{1 - 0.9024} = \frac{-0.047}{0.0976} = -0.4815$

Sum: $0.5921 + 0.0612 - 0.0198 - 0.0283 - 0.0519 - 0.4815 = 0.0718$

Very close to zero. Try $\alpha = 0.965$:

- C₅⁺: $\frac{-0.047}{1 - 0.965 \times 0.94} = \frac{-0.047}{1 - 0.9071} = \frac{-0.047}{0.0929} = -0.5059$

Sum ≈ $0.5921 + 0.0612 - 0.0198 - 0.0283 - 0.0519 - 0.5059 = 0.0474$

Try $\alpha = 0.975$:

- C₅⁺: $\frac{-0.047}{1 - 0.975 \times 0.94} = \frac{-0.047}{1 - 0.9165} = \frac{-0.047}{0.0835} = -0.5629$

Sum ≈ $0.5914 + 0.0606 - 0.0200 - 0.0286 - 0.0524 - 0.5629 = -0.0119$

So $\alpha = V/F$ is between 0.965 and 0.975. Linear interpolation:

$$\alpha \approx 0.965 + 0.010 \times \frac{0.0474}{0.0474 + 0.0119} = 0.965 + 0.010 \times 0.799 = 0.973$$

Step 2: Calculate the liquid phase composition $x_i = \dfrac{z_i}{1 + \alpha (K_i - 1)}$:

For $i = C_5^+$:

$$x_{C_5^+} = \dfrac{0.05}{1 + 0.973 (0.06 - 1)} = \dfrac{0.05}{1 - 0.9146} = \dfrac{0.05}{0.0854} = 0.5855$$

Step 3: The fraction of liquid is $L/F = 1 - \alpha = 1 - 0.973 = 0.027$ (moles of liquid per mole of feed).

Step 4: Convert to STB/MMSCF (using molecular weight and liquid density of C₅⁺):

Assuming $MW_{C_5^+} = 100$ lb/lbmol, $\rho_{C_5^+} = 50$ lb/ft³ (≈ 0.722 g/cc):

Total liquid mass flow per MMSCF:
$$\text{Mol C}_5^+ \text{ in liquid} = z_{C_5^+} \times \dfrac{L}{F} = 0.05 \times 0.027 = 0.00135 \text{ lbmol/mol feed}$$

Per MMSCF (1 MMSCF = 1,000,000 scf; at standard conditions, 379.5 scf/lbmol):
$$\text{lbmol feed} = \dfrac{10^6}{379.5} = 2635 \text{ lbmol}$$

Liquid C₅⁺ moles per MMSCF feed:
$$2635 \times 0.00135 = 3.557 \text{ lbmol/MMSCF}$$

Mass of liquid C₅⁺ per MMSCF:
$$3.557 \times 100 = 355.7 \text{ lb/MMSCF}$$

Volume of liquid C₅⁺ per MMSCF:
$$\dfrac{355.7 \text{ lb}}{50 \text{ lb/ft}^3} = 7.11 \text{ ft}^3/\text{MMSCF}$$

Convert to barrels (1 bbl = 5.615 ft³):
$$STL = \dfrac{7.11}{5.615} \approx 1.27 \text{ STB/MMSCF}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is a simplified illustration. Real C₅⁺ fractions include a distribution of components (C₅, C₆, C₇, …) and require proper PVT lab data for accurate predictions. For KTU exams, students should be able to set up the Rachford-Rice equation and understand the iterative procedure.
