## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define unconventional gas. List the three main types of unconventional gas resources.**

**Model Answer:**

Unconventional gas refers to natural gas that cannot be produced economically from conventional reservoir rocks using traditional drilling and completion methods. The three main types are:
1. **Shale Gas** — gas trapped in fine-grained, organic-rich shale formations.
2. **Tight Gas Sands** — gas in low-permeability sandstone reservoirs (k < 0.1 mD).
3. **Coal Bed Methane (CBM)** — methane adsorbed onto the internal surfaces of coal seams.

---

#### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is the Langmuir isotherm? State the Langmuir equation and explain the significance of Langmuir pressure and Langmuir volume.**

**Model Answer:**

The Langmuir isotherm describes **monolayer adsorption** of gas molecules on a solid surface. The equation is:

$$V = \frac{V_L \cdot P}{P + P_L}$$

- **Langmuir Volume ($V_L$):** the maximum gas adsorption capacity of the coal/shale (in scf/ton), representing the gas content when all adsorption sites are occupied.
- **Langmuir Pressure ($P_L$):** the pressure at which the adsorbed gas volume equals $V_L / 2$. It indicates the affinity of the coal for gas — lower $P_L$ means stronger adsorption.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]

**(a)** Explain in detail the geological formation, storage mechanisms, and production methods of **Shale Gas**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A shale formation has the following properties: Area = 1000 acres, thickness = 200 ft, porosity = 6%, irreducible water saturation = 25%, gas formation volume factor = 0.005 rb/scf. Calculate the **Original Gas In Place (OGIP)**. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Shale Gas:**

- **Geological Formation:** Shale gas originates from organic-rich muds deposited in low-energy marine or lacustrine environments. Over geological time, burial and heating transform the kerogen into hydrocarbons. When the thermal maturity (Ro) exceeds 1.0%, the kerogen enters the gas window and generates methane.
- **Storage Mechanisms:** Gas is stored in three modes: (i) free gas in micropores and microfractures, (ii) adsorbed gas on kerogen and clay surfaces, and (iii) dissolved gas in kerogen. Adsorbed gas typically constitutes 20–85% of total gas content.
- **Production Methods:** Production requires horizontal wells with multistage hydraulic fracturing. The process involves pumping high-pressure fluid to create fractures, then injecting proppant (sand) to keep fractures open. Production follows three stages: (1) free gas flow, (2) desorption from kerogen, and (3) diffusion through the matrix.

**Part (b) — OGIP Calculation:**

Step 1 — State the volumetric equation:

$$G = \frac{43{,}560 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_{wi})}{B_{gi}}$$

Step 2 — Substitute values:

$$G = \frac{43{,}560 \times 1000 \times 200 \times 0.06 \times (1 - 0.25)}{0.005}$$

Step 3 — Compute numerator:

- $43{,}560 \times 1000 = 43{,}560{,}000$
- $43{,}560{,}000 \times 200 = 8{,}712{,}000{,}000$
- $8{,}712{,}000{,}000 \times 0.06 = 522{,}720{,}000$
- $522{,}720{,}000 \times 0.75 = 392{,}040{,}000$

Step 4 — Divide by $B_{gi}$:

$$G = \frac{392{,}040{,}000}{0.005} = 78{,}408{,}000{,}000 \text{ scf}$$

$$\boxed{G \approx 78.4 \text{ Bscf} \text{ (billion standard cubic feet)}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Pattern]

**(a)** Describe the **Coal Bed Methane (CBM)** reservoir system. Explain the dual-porosity nature of coal, the role of cleats, and the sequential production mechanism. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CBM reservoir has Langmuir volume = 500 scf/ton, Langmuir pressure = 400 psia, in-situ gas content = 300 scf/ton, and initial reservoir pressure = 700 psia. Calculate the **critical desorption pressure** and comment on the production implications. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — CBM Reservoir System:**

- **Dual-Porosity Nature:** Coal is a unique dual-porosity system consisting of (i) the **micropore matrix** (containing the vast majority of gas in adsorbed form) and (ii) the **macropore cleat system** (a natural fracture network providing flow paths).
- **Cleats:** Coal has two types of natural fractures — **face cleats** (continuous, planar) and **butt cleats** (shorter, perpendicular to face cleats). Together they form a permeable network. Cleat permeability typically ranges from 0.1 to 10 mD.
- **Sequential Production Mechanism:** CBM production proceeds in phases:
  1. **Dewatering Phase:** Water is pumped out, reducing reservoir pressure.
  2. **Desorption Phase:** When pressure drops below the critical desorption pressure, methane desorbs from the coal surface.
  3. **Production Phase:** Free gas flows through cleats to the wellbore.
  4. **Decline Phase:** Gas rate eventually declines as the coal becomes depleted.

**Part (b) — Critical Desorption Pressure Calculation:**

Step 1 — Apply the equation:

$$P_{cd} = \frac{P_L \cdot (G_c / V_L)}{1 - (G_c / V_L)}$$

Step 2 — Calculate the ratio $G_c / V_L$:

$$G_c / V_L = 300 / 500 = 0.60$$

Step 3 — Substitute:

$$P_{cd} = \frac{400 \times 0.60}{1 - 0.60} = \frac{240}{0.40}$$

Step 4 — Compute:

$$\boxed{P_{cd} = 600 \text{ psia}}$$

**Production Implications:**

Since the initial reservoir pressure ($P_i = 700$ psia) is only 100 psia above the critical desorption pressure ($P_{cd} = 600$ psia), the reservoir does not require extensive dewatering. Gas desorption will begin relatively early in the production life, and the well will achieve gas production sooner. This is a favorable condition for CBM development. Additionally, the saturation ratio of 60% indicates the coal is moderately saturated, leaving some adsorption capacity unused.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing Shale Gas and Tight Gas:** Students often mix up these two. Remember: **Shale gas is stored in the source rock itself; tight gas has migrated into a separate low-permeability sandstone reservoir.** Storage mechanisms differ fundamentally.
> 2. **Mixing up Langmuir Constants:** Students frequently interchange $V_L$ and $P_L$. Remember: $V_L$ has units of **scf/ton** (volume), and $P_L$ has units of **psia** (pressure).
> 3. **Forgetting Units in Formulas:** KTU examiners allocate 1–2 marks specifically for correct units. Always state them!
> 4. **Missing the CBM Production Sequence:** The order (water first, then gas) is often tested. Students sometimes incorrectly describe gas as the first phase.
> 5. **Not Defining TOC and Ro:** When discussing shale gas, always mention that the shale must be a good **source rock** (high TOC and appropriate thermal maturity) in addition to being a reservoir.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 2 — Unconventional Gas Introduction:**

- [ ] **Unconventional gas** includes shale gas, tight gas sands, and CBM. Conventional gas flows easily; unconventional gas requires stimulation.
- [ ] **Shale Gas:** Source = Reservoir. Stored as adsorbed (on kerogen) + free (in pores) + dissolved gas. Permeability in nanodarcy range. Production via horizontal + multistage hydraulic fracturing.
- [ ] **Tight Gas Sands:** Sandstone reservoir with permeability < 0.1 mD. Storage is free gas in small pore throats. Requires hydraulic fracturing or acidizing.
- [ ] **Coal Bed Methane (CBM):** Methane adsorbed on coal surface (90–98%). Dual-porosity system: matrix + cleats. Production via dewatering → desorption → flow.
- [ ] **Langmuir Isotherm:** $V = \dfrac{V_L \cdot P}{P + P_L}$ — fundamental equation for both shale gas and CBM adsorption.
- [ ] **Critical Desorption Pressure:** $P_{cd} = \dfrac{P_L \cdot (G_c / V_L)}{1 - (G_c / V_L)}$ — the pressure at which gas begins to desorb in CBM.
- [ ] **Darcy's Law:** $q = \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$ — explains why low permeability requires high pressure gradients or fractures.
- [ ] **OGIP Formula (volumetric):** $G = \dfrac{43{,}560 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \phi \cdot (1 - S_{wi})}{B_{gi}}$ — for tight and conventional gas.
- [ ] **CBM Gas-in-Place:** $G_{CBM} = 1359.7 \cdot A \cdot h \cdot \rho_c \cdot G_c$ — for coal seam gas.
- [ ] **CBM Production Sequence:** Dewatering → Pressure Decline → Desorption (when $P = P_{cd}$) → Gas Flow → Decline → Abandonment.
- [ ] **Key Permeability Thresholds:** Conventional > 1 mD; Tight 0.001–0.1 mD; Shale 0.0001–0.001 mD (nD range); CBM cleats 0.1–10 mD.
- [ ] **Shale Gas Requirements:** TOC > 2%, Ro > 1.0% (gas window), thickness > 30 m, sufficient brittleness for fracturing.
- [ ] **Hydraulic Fracturing:** Creates artificial fractures using high-pressure fluid + proppant (sand) to keep fractures open.
- [ ] **Why Coal Stores So Much Gas:** Internal surface area of 100–300 m² per gram of coal — enormous adsorption capacity.
- [ ] **Fick's Law of Diffusion:** Governs gas transport from shale matrix to fractures — the rate-limiting step in shale gas production.
- [ ] **Global Significance:** USA leads in shale gas production; Australia and India lead in CBM development; tight gas is prevalent in China, USA, and Canada.
- [ ] **Always include units in your KTU exam answers** — this is a 1–2 mark differentiator!

---

### 5.4 Memory Aid — Mnemonics for Quick Recall

> **"S-T-C" — Storage Type Classification:**
> - **S**hale gas → **S**tored on kerogen (**S**urface adsorption) + **S**mall pores
> - **T**ight gas → **T**rapped in **T**ight pore throats (free gas)
> - **C**BM → **C**oal **C**oats with **C**hemically bonded methane

> **"W-G-D" — CBM Production Order:**
> 1. **W**ater comes out first (dewatering)
> 2. **G**as desorbs when pressure drops
> 3. **D**ecline phase follows peak production

---

> **End of Module 2 — Topic: Shale Gas and Tight Gas Sands, Coal Bed Methane — Introduction**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Present Status
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT528 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Present Status # 📘 PRESENT STATUS OF OIL AND NATURAL GAS — KTU 2024 SCHEME ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **"Present Status"** in Oil and Natural Gas Engineering refers to the **quantitative and qualitative assessment of the current global and national scenario** of hydrocarbon resources, encompassing **proved reserves, annual production, consumption patterns, refining capacity, infrastructure, and the strategic outlook** of the petroleum industry at a given point in time. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the *Present Status* module critically evaluates the **dynamic equilibrium** between: - **Reserve Base** (stock available underground) - **Production Rate** (rate of extraction) - **Demand Growth** (rate of consumption) - **Energy Transition** (shift toward renewables) ### 1.2 Key Terminology (Must Memorize for KTU Exams) | Term | Definition | Unit | |------|------------|------| | **Proved Reserves (1P)** | Reserves recoverable with reasonable certainty under existing economic conditions | MMbbl / BCM | | **Probable Reserves (2P)** | Additional reserves with ~50% probability of recovery | MMbbl / BCM | | **Possible Reserves (3P)** | Reserves with low probability but technically recoverable | MMbbl / BCM | | **R/P Ratio** | Reserve-to-Production ratio (years of supply left at current rate) | Years | | **Daily Production** | Volume of hydrocarbons extracted per day | MMbbl/d / BCM/yr | | **OPEC** | Organization of Petroleum Exporting Countries | — | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Bank Account Model" 🏦 > [!IMPORTANT] > **Think of Earth's hydrocarbon reserves as a BANK ACCOUNT:** > - **Opening Balance (Initial Reserves):** Total oil/gas ever available > - **Withdrawals (Production):** Money we take out each year > - **Interest Earned (New Discoveries + EOR):** New fields found + enhanced recovery > - **Closing Balance (Proved Reserves):** What's left underground > - **Account Statement (Present Status Report):** The annual snapshot showing balance, withdrawals, and interest **If withdrawals > interest earned** → We are **depleting** our account (which is the current global situation!) ### 1.4 Why "Present Status" Matters in KTU Curriculum This topic bridges **geological understanding (Module 1)** with **engineering applications (Modules 3-5)**. It equips students with: - The macro-economic context of petroleum engineering - India's energy security challenges - The strategic importance of OPEC and geopolitics - The motivation behind **Enhanced Oil Recovery (EOR)** techniques --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Global Oil and Gas Status — Key Statistics > [!NOTE] > The following figures represent approximate data consistent with post-2022 industry reports. KTU examiners expect students to know **trends and orders of magnitude**, not exact decimals. #### 🌍 **World Crude Oil Scenario** | Parameter | Value (Approx.) | |-----------|-----------------| | Total Proved Reserves | **~1.7 trillion barrels** | | Annual World Production | **~95-100 million barrels/day** | | World R/P Ratio | **~45-50 years** | | Top 3 Reserve Holders | **Venezuela, Saudi Arabia, Canada** | | Top 3 Producers | **USA, Saudi Arabia, Russia** | | Top 3 Consumers | **USA, China, India** | #### 🌍 **World Natural Gas Scenario** | Parameter | Value (Approx.) | |-----------|-----------------| | Total Proved Reserves | **~200 TCM (Trillion Cubic Meters)** | | Annual World Production | **~4,000 BCM** | | World R/P Ratio | **~50 years** | | Top 3 Reserve Holders | **Russia, Iran, Qatar** | | Top 3 Producers | **USA, Russia, Iran** | | Top 3 Consumers | **USA, Russia, China** | ### 2.2 🇮🇳 India's Oil and Gas Status — KTU High-Yield Section > [!IMPORTANT] > **India is the world's 3rd largest energy consumer but imports ~85% of its crude oil and ~50% of its natural gas.** This is a **favourite KTU exam question**! #### India's Hydrocarbon Snapshot | Parameter | Crude Oil | Natural Gas | |-----------|-----------|-------------| | Proved Reserves | **~600 MMt** | **~1,400 BCM** | | Annual Production | **~30 MMt** | **~30 BCM** | | Annual Consumption | **~220 MMt** | **~60+ BCM** | | Import Dependency | **~85%** | **~50%** | | R/P Ratio | **~20 years** | **~45 years** | | Major Onshore Basin | **Mumbai High, Assam** | **Mumbai High, KG Basin** | | Major Offshore Basin | **Western Offshore, KG-D6** | **KG-D6, Mahanadi** | #### India's Strategic Petroleum Reserves (SPR) - **Location:** Visakhapatnam (1.33 MMT), Mangaluru (1.5 MMT), Padur (2.5 MMT) - **Total Capacity:** ~5.5 MMT (~38 million barrels) - **Operator:** Indian Strategic Petroleum Reserves Limited (ISPRL) - **Buffer Days:** ~10-12 days of net imports ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet 📋 > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these formulas — they appear almost every KTU exam!** | Formula | Expression | Application | |---------|------------|-------------| | **R/P Ratio** | $\text{R/P} = \dfrac{R_{\text{proved}}}{P_{\text{annual}}}$ | Years of supply remaining | | **Reserve Life Index** | $\text{RLI} = \dfrac{\text{Cumulative Production} + \text{Remaining Reserves}}{\text{Annual Production}}$ | Refinery planning | | **Decline Rate** | $q = q_i \cdot e^{-Dt}$ (Exponential) | Future production forecast | | **Harmonic Decline** | $q = \dfrac{q_i}{1 + D_i t}$ | Conservative estimate | | **Material Balance (Oil)** | $N = \dfrac{N_p [B_o + (R_p - R_{si})B_g]}{B_o - B_{oi} + (R_{si} - R_s)B_g}$ | Original Oil in Place (OOIP) | | **Recovery Factor** | $RF = \dfrac{\text{Cumulative Production}}{OOIP} \times 100\%$ | Reservoir performance | | **Energy Demand Growth** | $E_{n} = E_o \cdot (1 + r)^n$ | Future energy projection | | **Import Dependency** | $ID = \dfrac{\text{Consumption} - \text{Domestic Production}}{\text{Consumption}} \times 100\%$ | Energy security metric | > **Where:** > - $R$ = Proved Reserves > - $P$ = Annual Production rate > - $q, q_i$ = Production rate, initial production rate > - $D, D_i$ = Decline rate (continuous vs instantaneous) > - $N$ = Original Oil in Place (STOIIP) > - $B_o, B_g$ = Oil and Gas formation volume factors > - $R_s, R_{si}$ = Solution GOR, initial solution GOR > - $r$ = Growth rate ### 2.4 Why This Matters in Engineering Practice ⚙️ - **Reservoir Engineers** use R/P ratio to plan **field development** and **secondary/tertiary recovery** - **Drilling Engineers** use production forecasts to design **well spacing** and **rig scheduling** - **Production Engineers** use decline curves to plan **workover operations** - **Policy Makers** use import dependency to formulate **energy security strategy** - **Refinery Engineers** use consumption data to plan **refinery capacity expansion** --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Problem 1: R/P Ratio Calculation (Most Common KTU Question) > **Problem:** A country has proved oil reserves of **8.4 billion barrels** and produces **3 million barrels per day**. Calculate the R/P ratio. **Given:** - $R = 8.4 \times 10^9$ bbl - $P = 3 \times 10^6$ bbl/day **Step 1:** Convert annual production to consistent units. Annual Production calculation: $$ P_{\text{annual}} = 3 \times 10^6 \, \frac{\text{bbl}}{\text{day}} \times 365 \, \frac{\text{days}}{\text{year}} $$ $$ P_{\text{annual}} = 1095 \times 10^6 \, \frac{\text{bbl}}{\text{year}} = 1.095 \times 10^9 \, \frac{\text{bbl}}{\text{year}} $$ **Step 2:** Apply R/P ratio formula. $$ \text{R/P} = \frac{R}{P_{\text{annual}}} = \frac{8.4 \times 10^9}{1.095 \times 10^9} $$ $$ \text{R/P} = 7.67 \, \text{years} $$ **Conclusion:** The country has approximately **7.67 years** of oil supply at current production rates — a **critically low** value indicating urgent need for new discoveries or substitution. --- ### 3.2 Problem 2: Exponential Decline Curve (Production Forecasting) > **Problem:** A well produces at **1000 bbl/day** initially. Decline rate is **15% per year** (continuous). Predict production after **3 years** and cumulative production till then. **Given:** - $q_i = 1000$ bbl/day - $D = 0.15$ /year - $t = 3$ years **Step 1:** Apply exponential decline equation. $$ q(t) = q_i \cdot e^{-Dt} $$ $$ q(3) = 1000 \cdot e^{-(0.15)(3)} $$ $$ q(3) = 1000 \cdot e^{-0.45} $$ **Step 2:** Evaluate exponential term. $$ e^{-0.45} = 0.6376 $$ $$ q(3) = 1000 \times 0.6376 = 637.6 \, \text{bbl/day} $$ **Step 3:** Calculate cumulative production $N_p(t)$ for exponential decline. $$ N_p(t) = \frac{q_i - q(t)}{D} $$ $$ N_p(3) = \frac{1000 - 637.6}{0.15} = \frac{362.4}{0.15} $$ $$ N_p(3) = 2416 \, \text{bbl} \, \text{(in time units of initial rate)} $$ **Step 4:** Convert to actual barrels (since $q_i$ is in bbl/day, multiply by 365). $$ N_p(3) = 2416 \times 365 = 881,840 \, \text{bbl} \approx 0.882 \, \text{MMbbl} $$ --- ### 3.3 Problem 3: India's Import Dependency (Policy Question) > **Problem:** India consumes **220 MMt** of crude oil annually and produces **30 MMt** domestically. Calculate import dependency. If consumption grows at **4% CAGR** and domestic production remains flat, what will be the import dependency in 10 years? **Step 1:** Calculate current import dependency. $$ ID_{\text{current}} = \frac{C - P_d}{C} \times 100\% $$ $$ ID_{\text{current}} = \frac{220 - 30}{220} \times 100\% = \frac{190}{220} \times 100\% $$ $$ ID_{\text{current}} = 86.36\% $$ **Step 2:** Project future consumption. $$ E_{10} = E_o \cdot (1 + r)^n = 220 \cdot (1.04)^{10} $$ $$ (1.04)^{10} = 1.4802 $$ $$ E_{10} = 220 \times 1.4802 = 325.65 \, \text{MMt} $$ **Step 3:** Calculate future import dependency (assuming $P_d = 30$ MMt constant). $$ ID_{10} = \frac{325.65 - 30}{325.65} \times 100\% $$ $$ ID_{10} = \frac{295.65}{325.65} \times 100\% = 90.79\% $$ **Conclusion:** India's oil import dependency will **worsen from 86% to 91%** in 10 years, justifying major investments in EOR, renewables, and exploration. --- ### 3.4 Problem 4: Energy Demand Forecast > **Problem:** Current energy demand is **500 Mtoe**. Forecast demand after 7 years at **3% growth rate**. **Step 1:** Apply compound growth formula. $$ E_7 = 500 \cdot (1.03)^7 $$ $$ (1.03)^7 = 1.2299 $$ $$ E_7 = 500 \times 1.2299 = 614.95 \, \text{Mtoe} $$ **Step 2:** Calculate absolute increase. $$ \Delta E = 614.95 - 500 = 114.95 \, \text{Mtoe} $$ $$ \%\text{ Increase} = \frac{114.95}{500} \times 100\% = 22.99\% $$ --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Energy Resources | Energy Source | Reserve Life (Years) | CO₂ Emissions | Global Share | KTU Exam Weightage | |---------------|----------------------|---------------|--------------|-------------------| | **Coal** | ~130 | Very High | ~27% | ⭐⭐ | | **Crude Oil** | ~50 | High | ~33% | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **Natural Gas** | ~50 | Medium | ~24% | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **Nuclear** | ~100+ (U) | None (operation) | ~4% | ⭐⭐ | | **Hydro** | ~Renewable | None | ~7% | ⭐⭐ | | **Solar/Wind** | Indefinite | None | ~3-5% | ⭐⭐⭐ | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Global Oil & Gas Value Chain ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Upstream
Exploration Drilling Production] --> B[Midstream
Pipelines Tankers Storage] B --> C[Downstream
Refining Petrochemicals Marketing] C --> D[End User
Transport Industry Power Domestic] A1[Reserves Estimation] --> A A2[Drilling Technologies] --> A A3[Production Systems] --> A B1[Crude Oil Pipelines] --> B B2[LNG Carriers] --> B B3[Terminal Storage] --> B C1[Atmospheric Distillation] --> C C2[Cracking Reforming] --> C C3[Polymer Plants] --> C D1[Automotive Fuels] --> D D2[Power Generation] --> D D3[Heating Cooking] --> D style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#fff style B fill:#2e75b6,color:#fff style C fill:#5b9bd5,color:#fff style D fill:#9dc3e6,color:#000 ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — India Energy Flow Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Domestic Production
30 MMt Oil] --> C[Energy Supply Pool] B[Imports
190 MMt Oil] --> C C --> D[Refineries
Capacity 250 MTPA] D --> E[Petroleum Products] E --> F1[Transportation 45%] E --> F2[Industry 25%] E --> F3[Power 10%] E --> F4[Domestic 15%] E --> F5[Agriculture 5%] G[Strategic Reserves
5.5 MMT SPR] --> C H[Renewable Energy
Solar Wind Hydro] --> F3 style A fill:#c5e0b4 style B fill:#f4b183 style C fill:#ffe699 style D fill:#bdd7ee style E fill:#9dc3e6 style G fill:#e2efda style H fill:#70ad47,color:#fff ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Reserve Classification Pyramid ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Total Resource Base
Including Undiscovered] --> B[Discovered Resources] A --> C[Undiscovered Resources] B --> D[Reserves] B --> E[Contingent Resources] D --> F1[Proved 1P
High Certainty] D --> F2[Probable 2P
Medium Certainty] D --> F3[Possible 3P
Low Certainty] F1 --> G1[Developed Producing] F1 --> G2[Developed Non-Producing] F1 --> G3[Undeveloped] style A fill:#2e75b6,color:#fff style D fill:#5b9bd5,color:#fff style F1 fill:#70ad47,color:#fff style F2 fill:#ffc000 style F3 fill:#ed7d31 ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Decline Curve Concept ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Initial Production qi] --> B[Production Declines Over Time] B --> C{Decline Type?} C -->|Constant Percentage| D[Exponential Decline
q = qi x exp -Dt] C -->|Decreasing Percentage| E[Harmonic Decline
q = qi / 1+Dit] C -->|Constant Absolute| F[Hyperbolic Decline
q = qi / 1+bDt] D --> G[Conservative Estimate] E --> H[Optimistic Estimate] F --> I[Most Realistic] style A fill:#1f4e79,color:#fff style G fill:#c5e0b4 style H fill:#ffd966 style I fill:#70ad47,color:#fff ``` --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define R/P ratio. What is its significance in assessing the present status of oil and gas reserves of a country?** **Model Answer:** The **Reserve-to-Production (R/P) ratio** is defined as the ratio of the remaining proved reserves of a country to its current annual production rate. $$ \text{R/P} = \frac{\text{Remaining Proved Reserves}}{\text{Annual Production Rate}} $$ It is expressed in **years** and represents the **number of years the proved reserves will last** at the current rate of production, assuming no new discoveries. **Significance:** 1. **Energy Security Indicator** — Higher R/P means longer self-sufficiency 2. **Exploration Strategy** — Low R/P forces aggressive exploration/EOR 3. **Investment Planning** — Guides long-term capital allocation 4. **Policy Decisions** — Influences import-export strategy 5. **Comparison Metric** — Allows benchmarking between countries > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often confuse R/P ratio with "reserves left in years" without specifying assumptions. Always state: *"assuming constant production rate and no new discoveries."* --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Explain why India's oil import dependency is over 85%. What are the implications?** **Model Answer:** India's oil import dependency is over **85%** due to the following reasons: **Reasons for High Import Dependency:** 1. **Limited Domestic Reserves** — India has only ~0.3% of world's proven oil reserves 2. **High Consumption** — 3rd largest consumer globally (~5 MMbbl/day) 3. **Low Production** — Domestic output stagnant at ~30 MMt/year for a decade 4. **Growing Demand** — Rapid economic growth fuels consumption 5. **Aging Fields** — Most Indian fields (Mumbai High, Assam) are maturing **Implications:** - **Foreign Exchange Drain** — ~$120-150 billion annually - **Geopolitical Vulnerability** — Dependent on Middle East, Russia - **Price Volatility Exposure** — Vulnerable to OPEC decisions - **Strategic Reserves** — Need for emergency stockpiles - **Drives Renewable Push** — Policies like National Hydrogen Mission --- ### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > *As per KTU 2024 Scheme, Module internal choice is provided. Both options are independent full questions.* --- #### **OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 2 Internal Choice]** **(a)** Discuss the **present status of world oil reserves, production, and consumption**. Compare the contributions of OPEC and non-OPEC countries. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With neat sketches, explain the **classification of petroleum reserves** (Proved, Probable, Possible) and the concept of **R/P ratio** with a worked example. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) World Oil Scenario:** The world oil scenario is characterized by a **stark imbalance** in reserve distribution and consumption patterns. **Reserve Distribution:** - **OPEC countries** hold ~**70% of world proved reserves** (mostly Middle East: Saudi Arabia ~17%, Venezuela ~18%, Iran ~9%, Iraq ~9%, Kuwait ~6%, UAE ~6%, Libya ~3%) - **Non-OPEC countries** hold ~**30%** (Canada oil sands ~10%, USA ~4%, Russia ~6%, others) **Production Distribution:** - **Non-OPEC** produces ~**60%** of world oil (USA ~13 MMbbl/d, Russia ~10 MMbbl/d, Canada ~5.7 MMbbl/d) - **OPEC** produces ~**40%** (Saudi Arabia ~10 MMbbl/d) - The **OPEC+ alliance** (OPEC + Russia + others) controls ~**50%** of global production **Consumption Distribution:** - **OECD countries** consume ~**45%** (USA ~20 MMbbl/d, EU ~13 MMbbl/d, Japan ~3 MMbbl/d) - **Non-OECD** consumes ~**55%**, dominated by China (~16 MMbbl/d) and India (~5 MMbbl/d) **Key Trends:** - USA became world's largest producer in 2018 (shale revolution) - Asia-Pacific is the fastest-growing consumption region - Oil demand may peak by 2030 (IEA projections) **(b) Reserve Classification and R/P Ratio:** **Reserve Classification (per PRMS / SPE standards):** 1. **Proved Reserves (1P):** Reserves with **>90% probability** of recovery under current economic and technological conditions. Subdivided into: - **Developed Producing (PDP)** — Currently producing wells - **Developed Non-Producing (PDNP)** — Drilled but not yet producing - **Undeveloped (PUD)** — Identified but require future capex 2. **Probable Reserves (2P):** Additional reserves with **>50% probability** of recovery. Includes: - Reserves from improved recovery in known reservoirs - Infill drilling opportunities - Behind-pipe zones 3. **Possible Reserves (3P):** Reserves with **lower probability (~10-50%)** of recovery. Includes: - Recovery of heavy oil - Enhanced oil recovery applications - Recovery from unconventional plays **R/P Ratio — Worked Example:** > **Example:** Saudi Arabia has proved reserves of **267 billion barrels** and produces **10 million barrels per day**. Annual Production: $$ P = 10 \times 10^6 \times 365 = 3.65 \times 10^9 \, \text{bbl/year} $$ R/P Ratio: $$ \text{R/P} = \frac{267 \times 10^9}{3.65 \times 10^9} = 73.15 \, \text{years} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Don't forget to convert **daily production to annual** before applying the formula. Many students divide reserves by daily production and get a wrong answer in days! --- #### **OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks)** **(a)** Describe the **present status of India's hydrocarbon sector** with reference to reserves, production, and major oil fields. What strategies are being adopted to reduce import dependency? **(7 marks)** **(b)** With suitable diagrams, explain the **concept of production decline curves**. A well produces 1500 bbl/day initially with continuous decline rate of 12% per year. Find production after 5 years and cumulative production. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) India's Hydrocarbon Sector Status:** **Reserves:** - Crude oil: **~600 MMt** (~4.4 billion barrels) - Natural gas: **~1,400 BCM** **Major Oil and Gas Fields:** | Field | Location | Type | Operator | |-------|----------|------|----------| | **Mumbai High** | Western Offshore | Offshore | ONGC | | **KG-D6 (Krishna-Godavari)** | Eastern Offshore | Offshore | Reliance/ONGC | | **Aishwarya** | Rajasthan | Onshore | Cairn India | | **Digboi, Naharkatiya** | Assam | Onshore | ONGC | | **Cambay Basin** | Gujarat | Onshore | ONGC, GSPC | | **Ravva** | Eastern Offshore | Offshore | Cairn | **Production Status:** - Crude oil: **~30 MMt/year** (declining trend) - Natural gas: **~30 BCM/year** **Strategies to Reduce Import Dependency:** 1. **EOR Implementation** — Polymer flooding, CO₂ injection in mature fields 2. **Exploration in Deepwater** — KG Basin, Mahanadi, Andaman 3. **Unconventional Hydrocarbons** — Shale gas, CBM, gas hydrates 4. **Renewable Energy Push** — Solar (Bhadla Park), Wind, Hydrogen mission 5. **Strategic Reserves** — Expansion of SPR program 6. **Policy Reforms** — HELP (Hydrocarbon Exploration and Licensing Policy), OALP (Open Acreage Licensing Policy) 7. **Ethanol Blending** — E20 program 8. **Electric Mobility** — Faster Adoption and Manufacturing of Electric Vehicles (FAME) **(b) Decline Curve Analysis:** **Diagram (refer SECTION_4.4):** **Given:** - $q_i = 1500$ bbl/day - $D = 0.12$ /year (continuous) - $t = 5$ years **Step 1:** Production after 5 years. $$ q(5) = q_i \cdot e^{-Dt} = 1500 \cdot e^{-(0.12)(5)} $$ $$ q(5) = 1500 \cdot e^{-0.60} $$ $$ e^{-0.60} = 0.5488 $$ $$ q(5) = 1500 \times 0.5488 = 823.2 \, \text{bbl/day} $$ **Step 2:** Cumulative production (exponential decline). $$ N_p(t) = \frac{q_i - q(t)}{D} = \frac{1500 - 823.2}{0.12} $$ $$ N_p(t) = \frac{676.8}{0.12} = 5640 \, \text{days-production units} $$ **Step 3:** Convert to actual barrels (multiply by 365). $$ N_p = 5640 \times 365 = 2,058,600 \, \text{bbl} \approx 2.06 \, \text{MMbbl} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall 1:** Continuous decline rate ($D$) is **per year** in the formula $e^{-Dt}$, not per day. Check units carefully! > > **Examiner's Pitfall 2:** For harmonic decline, formula is $q = q_i / (1 + D_i t)$, NOT the same as exponential. Mixing them is a common error. --- ### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Minute KTU Revision Checklist — Master These Before Exam!** - ✅ **R/P Ratio Formula:** $R/P = R_{\text{proved}} / P_{\text{annual}}$ — Always convert production to **annual** units - ✅ **Three Reserve Categories:** 1P (Proved, >90%), 2P (Probable, >50%), 3P (Possible, low %) - ✅ **World Oil R/P:** ~45-50 years | **World Gas R/P:** ~50 years - ✅ **India's Import Dependency:** ~85% oil, ~50% gas — **highest exam scoring fact** - ✅ **Top 3 Oil Reserve Holders:** Venezuela, Saudi Arabia, Canada - ✅ **Top 3 Oil Producers:** USA, Saudi Arabia, Russia - ✅ **Top 3 Oil Consumers:** USA, China, India - ✅ **Top 3 Gas Reserve Holders:** Russia, Iran, Qatar - ✅ **OPEC HQ:** Vienna, Austria | **Founded:** 1960 | **Members:** 13 nations - ✅ **Exponential Decline:** $q = q_i e^{-Dt}$ | **Cumulative:** $N_p = (q_i - q)/D$ - ✅ **Harmonic Decline:** $q = q_i/(1 + D_i t)$ | More optimistic - ✅ **India's Major Fields:** Mumbai High (largest offshore), KG-D6 (gas), Assam (oldest — Digboi) - ✅ **SPR Locations:** Visakhapatnam, Mangaluru, Padur (Total ~5.5 MMT) - ✅ **Decline Rate Units:** Continuous ($D$ in /year) vs Nominal — **don't mix them** - ✅ **Energy Demand Formula:** $E_n = E_o (1+r)^n$ — Common in policy questions - ✅ **Decline Curve Types:** Exponential, Harmonic, Hyperbolic — Sketch all three - ✅ **India's EOR Initiatives:** Mangala, Bhagyam (Cairn-Rajasthan) — Polymer flooding - ✅ **Major Indian Companies:** ONGC, OIL, IOCL, BPCL, HPCL, GAIL, Reliance, Cairn ### 📚 Key Formulas to Memorize (Final Summary) | # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | |---|---------|----------------| | 1 | $R/P = R/P_a$ | Reserve Life | | 2 | $q = q_i e^{-Dt}$ | Exponential Decline | | 3 | $q = q_i/(1+D_i t)$ | Harmonic Decline | | 4 | $N_p = (q_i - q)/D$ | Cum. Production (Exp.) | | 5 | $RF = N_p / \text{OOIP}$ | Recovery Factor | | 6 | $ID = (C - P_d)/C$ | Import Dependency | | 7 | $E_n = E_o(1+r)^n$ | Demand Growth | | 8 | $RLI = (C_p + R)/P$ | Reserve Life Index | > 🎓 **Final Tip from KTU Examiner's Perspective:** In 14-mark questions, always include a **neatly labeled diagram** (even hand-drawn) for full marks. The KTU valuation key explicitly awards **2-3 marks for figures**. Numerical problems must show **unit conversions clearly**. Conceptual answers should include **at least 2 real-world examples** (e.g., Mumbai High, KG-D6). ---

topics.title: Formation and Properties of Coal Bed Methane, Natural Gas Hydrate - Concepts and Structures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT528 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Formation and Properties of Coal Bed Methane, Natural Gas Hydrate - Concepts and Structures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Coal Bed Methane (CBM) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Coal Bed Methane (CBM)** is a form of natural gas (predominantly **CH₄**, typically 90–95 % by volume) that is generated, stored, and adsorbed within the micro-porous matrix of coal seams during the geological process of **coalification** (carbonization of organic matter). It is recovered as an unconventional gas resource, distinct from conventional sandstone or carbonate gas reservoirs because the coal acts simultaneously as the source rock, the reservoir rock, and the trapping medium.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, CBM is classified as an **unconventional natural gas resource** because the gas is held primarily by **adsorption onto the internal surfaces of coal micropores** (monolayer–multilayer adsorption governed by a **Langmuir-type isotherm**), with secondary storage as free gas in cleats (natural fractures) and dissolved gas in formation water.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Contrast vs Conventional Gas:**
> - Conventional gas → stored in pore space (free gas) of sandstone/carbonate.
> - CBM → stored **adsorbed** on coal surfaces; released when reservoir pressure falls below the **critical desorption pressure (P_cd)**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — CBM

Think of CBM like a **kitchen sponge soaked in water**:
- The sponge (coal matrix) has millions of tiny internal pockets.
- The water (methane) sticks to the inner walls of those pockets.
- To release the water, you must **squeeze the sponge** — i.e., **lower the pressure** in the coal seam by pumping out water. Only then does methane desorb and flow out.

### 1.3 Natural Gas Hydrate (NGH) — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **Natural Gas Hydrate (NGH)** is a crystalline, ice-like, non-stoichiometric **clathrate compound** formed when small gas molecules (guest) — principally **methane (CH₄)**, but also ethane (C₂H₆), propane (C₃H₈), CO₂, H₂S, N₂ — become physically trapped inside cage-like lattices of hydrogen-bonded water molecules (host), under conditions of **low temperature and elevated pressure**, typically found in permafrost regions and deep marine sediments.

The general stoichiometric representation is:

$$M \cdot n H_2O \;\rightarrow\; M \cdot nH_2O \,(s)$$

where **M** is the guest gas molecule and **n** is the **hydration number** (moles of water per mole of gas).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why hydrates matter in KTU syllabus:** Hydrates are both a **potential future energy resource** (1 m³ of hydrate releases ≈ 164 m³ of methane at STP) and a **flow-assurance hazard** in subsea pipelines and wellbores.

### 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — Gas Hydrate

Imagine a **snowflake that has trapped a mosquito inside its lattice**. The snowflake (water) builds a cage; the mosquito (gas) is held captive but is not chemically bonded to the cage. If you warm the snowflake or reduce the pressure, the cage melts, and the mosquito is released. Hydrates behave identically: **temperature and pressure control the cage stability.**

### 1.5 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics (Bolded)

- Density of pure methane hydrate: **≈ 0.91 g/cm³**
- Density of pure water ice: **0.917 g/cm³**
- 1 m³ of methane hydrate ≈ **164 m³ of methane (STP)** and **0.8 m³ of water**
- Typical in-situ CBM gas content: **5–20 m³/tonne** of coal
- Critical desorption pressure for CBM: **generally < 6.9 MPa (1000 psi)**
- Standard P-T hydrate stability envelope for CH₄: **T < ~10 °C, P > ~0.6 MPa** (deep ocean / permafrost zones)

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Formation of Coal Bed Methane — Coalification Process

Methane generation is intrinsically linked to the **coalification (carbonization) series**, where buried plant matter progressively loses volatiles and increases in carbon content:

| Stage | Rank | Fixed Carbon (% daf) | Volatile Matter (% daf) | CBM Generation |
|------|------|---------------------|------------------------|----------------|
| 1 | Peat | < 60 | > 60 | Biogenic (microbial) CH₄ |
| 2 | Lignite (Brown Coal) | 60–70 | 45–55 | Early thermogenic |
| 3 | Sub-bituminous | 70–77 | 35–45 | Significant CH₄ |
| 4 | Bituminous (HV–LV) | 77–90 | 15–35 | Peak thermogenic CH₄ |
| 5 | Anthracite | > 90 | < 10 | Reduced generative capacity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Two mechanisms of CH₄ generation:**
> 1. **Biogenic** — produced by methanogenic archaea in shallow, low-rank coals (peat–lignite stage).
> 2. **Thermogenic** — produced by thermal cracking of kerogen and coal macerals as temperature rises (typically 50–150 °C) during burial (bituminous–anthracite stage).

### 2.2 Storage of CBM in Coal Seams

CBM exists in three distinct storage modes:

1. **Adsorbed gas (dominant, ~ 95–98 % of in-situ gas):** Held by **van der Waals forces** onto the internal microporous surface of the coal matrix (pores 0.5–1 nm). Modelled by the **Langmuir adsorption isotherm**.
2. **Free gas (minor, < 5 %):** Present in the natural fracture network (**cleats** — face and butt cleats).
3. **Dissolved gas (negligible):** Dissolved in formation water within the cleat system.

### 2.3 CBM Production Mechanism

- Coal seams are **water-saturated** at initial reservoir pressure (P_i).
- Methane is held adsorbed; **no free gas flows initially**.
- Pumping out water from the cleat system **lowers the pressure**.
- When pressure falls below the **critical desorption pressure (P_cd)**, methane desorbs from the coal matrix.
- Desorbed gas diffuses through the matrix into the cleat system, then flows to the wellbore (Darcy flow in cleats).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $V = V_L \dfrac{P}{P + P_L}$ | **Langmuir isotherm** for adsorbed gas volume | V: scf/ton, P: psia |
| 2 | $G_c = 32.036 \cdot \rho_c \cdot V_{ad}$ | Gas content of coal (English units: $\rho_c$ in g/cm³, V_ad in cc/g) | scf/ton |
| 3 | $G_c = \rho_c \cdot V_{ad} \cdot \dfrac{1000}{M_{CH_4}}$ | Gas content (SI variant) | m³/tonne |
| 4 | $P_{cd} = \dfrac{V_L \cdot P_{cd,L}}{V_L - V_{cd}}$ | Critical desorption pressure | psia |
| 5 | $V_H = V_m \cdot \dfrac{273}{T} \cdot \dfrac{P}{P_0}$ | Molar volume of hydrate at STP | m³/mol |
| 6 | $n = \dfrac{M_{hydrate} \cdot 1000}{M_{H_2O}}$ | Hydration number (moles H₂O per mole gas) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $P(T) = \exp\left(a - \dfrac{b}{T}\right)$ | Empirical hydrate dissociation pressure (Berge/Handa) | MPa, T in K |
| 8 | $\rho_{hydrate} = \dfrac{n \cdot M_{H_2O} + M_{gas}}{N_A \cdot V_{unitcell}/Z}$ | Hydrate crystal density | g/cm³ |
| 9 | $\phi_{coal} = \dfrac{V_p}{V_b}$ | Coal porosity (micro + meso + macro) | fraction |
| 10 | $k_{cleat} = \dfrac{b^3}{12 a}$ | Permeability of cleat network (parallel plate model) | md |

Where:
- $V_L$ = Langmuir volume (cc/g or scf/ton)
- $P_L$ = Langmuir pressure (psia)
- $V_{ad}$ = Adsorbed gas volume per unit mass
- $a, b$ = Empirical constants for hydrate P-T curve (gas-specific)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units awareness trap (KTU common mistake):** $\rho_c$ for gas content equation **2** must be in **g/cm³**, not kg/m³. Always check unit consistency before substituting.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CBM** is exploited commercially in the USA (San Juan, Powder River, Black Warrior basins), Australia (Bowen, Surat basins), China, and India (Jharkhand, Raniganj).
- **Gas Hydrates** are targeted in pilot trials in Japan (Nankai Trough), China (South China Sea), India (KG Basin), and Alaska (North Slope).
- Hydrate management is critical in **subsea flowlines** in deep-water oil & gas developments (e.g., Gulf of Mexico, Brazil pre-salt, India KG-D6).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Langmuir Adsorption Isotherm (CBM)

The Langmuir isotherm is the **cornerstone equation** for CBM gas-content calculations. It assumes monolayer adsorption onto a homogeneous surface.

**Step 1 — Define the rate of adsorption (forward reaction):**

Adsorption rate is proportional to gas pressure $P$ and the fraction of unoccupied sites $(1 - \theta)$:

$$r_{ads} = k_a \cdot P \cdot (1 - \theta)$$

**Step 2 — Define the rate of desorption (reverse reaction):**

Desorption rate is proportional to the fraction of occupied sites $\theta$:

$$r_{des} = k_d \cdot \theta$$

**Step 3 — At equilibrium, set $r_{ads} = r_{des}$:**

$$k_a \cdot P \cdot (1 - \theta) = k_d \cdot \theta$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\theta$ (fractional surface coverage):**

$$k_a P - k_a P \theta = k_d \theta$$

$$k_a P = \theta (k_d + k_a P)$$

$$\theta = \dfrac{k_a P}{k_d + k_a P} = \dfrac{(k_a/k_d) P}{1 + (k_a/k_d) P}$$

**Step 5 — Define the Langmuir constant $b = k_a/k_d$ (also written $1/P_L$):**

$$\theta = \dfrac{b P}{1 + b P}$$

**Step 6 — Convert fractional coverage to adsorbed gas volume $V$ (since $V \propto \theta$ and $V_{max} = V_L$):**

$$V = V_L \cdot \theta = V_L \cdot \dfrac{b P}{1 + b P}$$

**Step 7 — Substituting $b = 1/P_L$:**

$$\boxed{V = V_L \cdot \dfrac{P}{P + P_L}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** As $P \to \infty$, $V \to V_L$ (monolayer saturation). At $P = P_L$, $V = V_L/2$ (the Langmuir pressure is the pressure at which half the adsorption capacity is reached).

### 3.2 Derivation of Critical Desorption Pressure

Given Langmuir isotherm and the gas content at initial reservoir conditions, we can find the pressure at which gas starts to desorb.

**Step 1 — At initial reservoir pressure $P_i$:**

$$V_i = V_L \cdot \dfrac{P_i}{P_i + P_L}$$

**Step 2 — At critical desorption pressure $P_{cd}$ (when V = V_i, the gas just begins to flow):**

$$V_i = V_L \cdot \dfrac{P_{cd}}{P_{cd} + P_L}$$

**Step 3 — Equating the two expressions and solving for $P_{cd}$:**

$$\dfrac{P_i}{P_i + P_L} = \dfrac{P_{cd}}{P_{cd} + P_L}$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$P_i (P_{cd} + P_L) = P_{cd} (P_i + P_L)$$

$$P_i P_{cd} + P_i P_L = P_{cd} P_i + P_{cd} P_L$$

$$P_i P_L = P_{cd} P_L$$

$$\boxed{P_{cd} = P_i}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU insight:** $P_{cd} = P_i$ only when the gas content equals the adsorption capacity at initial pressure. **In general**, $P_{cd}$ is determined by intersecting the desorption isotherm with the constant gas-content line. Practical working equation:

$$\boxed{P_{cd} = \dfrac{V_L \cdot P_L}{V_L - V_{cd}}}$$

where $V_{cd}$ is the gas content at $P_{cd}$.

### 3.3 Derivation of the Hydration Number

**Step 1 — Define the unit-cell structure:**
For **Structure I (sI)** hydrate (CH₄, C₂H₆, CO₂), the unit cell contains:
- 2 small cages ($5^{12}$)
- 6 large cages ($5^{12}6^2$)
- 46 water molecules

**Step 2 — Compute theoretical hydration number assuming full cage occupancy:**

$$n = \dfrac{46}{2 + 6} = 5.75$$

**Step 3 — Real (non-stoichiometric) hydration number for pure CH₄ hydrate:**
- Small cages are **~ 95 %** occupied; large cages **~ 99 %** occupied.

$$n_{real} = \dfrac{46}{2(0.95) + 6(0.99)} = \dfrac{46}{1.90 + 5.94} = \dfrac{46}{7.84} \approx 5.87$$

**Step 4 — General empirical correlation (statistical):**

$$\boxed{n \approx 5.85 \text{ to } 6.30 \text{ for } CH_4 \text{ hydrate}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Structure II (sII) and Structure H (sH) hydrates have different unit-cell water contents and thus different hydration numbers (≈ 17 for sII, ≈ 34 for sH — see §4).

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of CBM vs Gas Hydrate

| Parameter | Coal Bed Methane (CBM) | Natural Gas Hydrate (NGH) |
|-----------|------------------------|---------------------------|
| Physical State | Free gas + adsorbed gas | Solid crystalline compound |
| Primary Storage Mechanism | Adsorption (van der Waals) | Physical entrapment in water cage |
| Host Medium | Coal matrix (organic) | Water lattice (crystal) |
| Typical Depth | 200–1500 m | 0–2000 m (permafrost); 800–4000 m (marine) |
| Temperature | Reservoir T (15–60 °C) | Typically < 10 °C (in-situ) |
| Pressure | Hydrostatic to lithostatic | 0.6–100 MPa (in-situ) |
| Gas-in-Place Density | 5–20 m³/tonne of coal | 164 m³ gas / m³ hydrate |
| Recovery Method | Depressurization (water pump-out) | Thermal stimulation / chemical inhibition / depressurization |
| Phase of Production | Gas from start (after dewatering) | Requires dissociation of solid to release gas |
| Energy Density | Moderate | Very high per unit volume of solid |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow — CBM Formation & Production Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Plant Organic Matter Burial] --> A1[Peat Stage Biogenic CH4]
    A1 --> A2[Lignite Stage Early Thermogenic]
    A2 --> A3[Sub-Bituminous Stage]
    A3 --> A4[Bituminous Stage Peak CH4 Generation]
    A4 --> A5[Anthracite Stage]
    A5 --> B0[Coal Seam Reservoir Formed]
    B0 --> B1[CH4 Adsorbed on Coal Microporous Surface]
    B0 --> B2[CH4 Free in Cleat Fractures]
    B0 --> B3[CH4 Dissolved in Formation Water]
    B1 --> C0[Reservoir Pressure Initial Pi]
    C0 --> C1[Production Well Pumps Out Water]
    C1 --> C2[Reservoir Pressure Decreases]
    C2 --> C3{Pressure drops below Pcd?}
    C3 -- Yes --> C4[Methane Desorbs from Coal Matrix]
    C3 -- No --> C5[Continue Dewatering]
    C5 --> C2
    C4 --> D0[Diffusion Through Coal Matrix to Cleats]
    D0 --> D1[Darcy Flow Through Cleats to Wellbore]
    D1 --> D2[Methane Gas at Surface]
    D2 --> D3[Pipeline to Processing Plant]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flow — Gas Hydrate Structure (Three Crystallographic Forms)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    H0[Small Guest Molecule Examples CH4 CO2 N2] --> H1[Structure I sI Cubic Unit Cell 2 Small Cages 5 to power 12 and 6 Large Cages 5 to power 12 6 to power 2]
    H2[Medium Guest Molecule Examples C3H8 iC4H10] --> H3[Structure II sII Cubic Unit Cell 16 Small Cages 5 to power 12 and 8 Large Cages 5 to power 12 6 to power 4]
    H4[Large Guest Molecule Examples iC5H12 Cyclohexane] --> H5[Structure H sH Hexagonal Unit Cell 3 Small Cages 5 to power 12 and 2 Medium Cages 4 to power 3 5 to power 6 6 to power 3 and 1 Large Cage 5 to power 12 6 to power 8]
    H1 --> HX[Stability: T less than 10 C and P greater than 0.6 MPa in-situ deep ocean and permafrost]
    H3 --> HX
    H5 --> HX
    HX --> HY[Dissociation Releases Guest Gas and Liquid Water]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    R4 --> R5

```
