# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**"Define the term 'water content of natural gas' and list the typical industry specification values for (i) sales-gas pipelines and (ii) LNG feed gas. Why is the LNG specification so much more stringent?"**

**Model Answer (KTU marking scheme, 3 marks):**

The water content of a natural gas stream is defined as the mass of water vapour contained in a unit volume of gas, usually expressed in **lb of H₂O per MMSCF** (or equivalently in mg/Sm³, or grain/100 scf).

| Application | Water Content Limit | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Sales-gas pipelines | ≤ **7 lb H₂O / MMSCF** | Prevent hydrate formation, corrosion, and 2-phase flow in cross-country lines. |
| (ii) LNG feed gas | ≤ **0.1 ppm(v)** ≈ **0.05 lb/MMSCF** | Prevent ice / hydrate plugging in cryogenic main cryogenic heat exchanger (MCHE) operating at −162 °C. |

The LNG specification is ~140× tighter than the sales-gas limit because the cryogenic process at −162 °C would instantly freeze any trace water into ice, causing catastrophic exchanger blockages. (3 marks: 1 definition + 1 limits + 1 reasoning)

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**"Explain the Wichert correction applied to the McKetta–Wehe chart. Under what conditions is it used, and what physical phenomenon does it account for?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Wichert correction is an empirical modification applied to the water-content value read from the McKetta–Wehe chart whenever the natural gas contains acidic components (H₂S, CO₂). The simplified KTU-tested form is:

$$W_{corr} = W_{MC} \cdot \left(1 - 0.0027 \cdot S_{total}\right)$$

where $S_{total} = y_{H_2S} + y_{CO_2}$ in mole percent, valid up to $S_{total} \le 40\%$.

**Physical basis:** H₂S and CO₂ are polar molecules that form hydrogen bonds with water, increasing the apparent water content above the chart prediction for an inert hydrocarbon gas of the same gravity. However, the McKetta–Wehe chart over-predicts for sour gas at high acid-gas fractions, hence the subtractive empirical form. (3 marks: 1 equation + 1 validity + 1 physics)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question B-A. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 3, 14 Marks]
**(a)** Derive the Bukacek low-pressure equation for the water content of a natural gas. State clearly the assumptions and the validity range. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A natural gas at $P = 2000$ psia and $T = 140\,°F$ has a gas specific gravity $\gamma_g = 0.75$ and contains $y_{H_2S} = 0.04$ and $y_{CO_2} = 0.03$. The gas leaves the formation in contact with brine of $4$ wt% salinity. Using the McKetta–Wehe chart, the water content is $W_{MC} = 24$ lb/MMSCF. Determine (i) the acid-gas corrected water content, (ii) the salinity-corrected water content, and (iii) the recommended dehydration method if sales-gas spec is 7 lb/MMSCF. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Bukacek Derivation (7 marks):**

Starting from Dalton's law applied to the wet gas:

$$p_w = y_w \cdot P$$

The ideal-gas equation evaluated at standard conditions ($T_{sc} = 520\,°R$, $P_{sc} = 14.696$ psia) gives the molar concentration of water per standard cubic foot:

$$\frac{n_w}{V_{sc}} = \frac{p_w}{Z_w \cdot R \cdot T_{sc}}$$

For low pressures ($P \le 1000$ psia), assume ideal behaviour ($Z_w \approx 1$) and a dilute water vapour ($P \gg p_w$). The mass of water per MMSCF is obtained by:

$$W = n_w \cdot M_w \cdot 10^6 = \frac{p_w \cdot M_w \cdot 10^6}{R \cdot T_{sc}}$$

Substituting $M_w = 18.015$ lb/lbmol, $R = 10.732$ psia·ft³/(lbmol·°R), $T_{sc} = 520\,°R$:

$$W = \frac{p_w \cdot 18.015 \cdot 10^6}{10.732 \cdot 520 \cdot p_w \cdot \left(\frac{P}{p_w} - 1\right)} \cdot p_w$$

After simplification, with $p_w$ expressed in psia and $T$ in °R:

$$\boxed{W = \frac{p_w}{P - p_w} \cdot \frac{47420}{T}}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Ideal-gas behaviour ($Z_w = 1$, valid for $P < 1000$ psia).
2. Henry's law / Lewis–Randall applies (water is dilute).
3. Gas temperature is the only state variable (no compressibility correction).
4. Pure liquid water is the equilibrium phase (no salt, no hydrate).

**Validity range:** $0 < P \le 1000$ psia; $32 \le T \le 300\,°F$; sweet hydrocarbon gas.

(7 marks: 2 derivation, 1 algebra, 2 assumptions, 1 validity, 1 boxed answer)

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Acid-gas correction (Wichert):**

$$S_{total} = (0.04 + 0.03) \times 100 = 7 \text{ mole\%}$$

$$W_{corr} = 24 \cdot (1 - 0.0027 \cdot 7) = 24 \cdot 0.9811 = 23.55 \text{ lb/MMSCF}$$

**(ii) Salinity correction (Maddox):**

$$W_{brine} = \frac{W_{corr}}{1 + 0.0043 \cdot S} = \frac{23.55}{1 + 0.0043 \cdot 4} = \frac{23.55}{1.0172}$$

$$W_{brine} = 23.15 \text{ lb/MMSCF}$$

**(iii) Dehydration recommendation:**

Since $W_{brine} = 23.15$ lb/MMSCF $\gg$ 7 lb/MMSCF, the gas fails the sales-gas spec. Required dehydration efficiency:

$$\eta = \frac{23.15 - 7}{23.15} \times 100\% = 69.8\%$$

For acid-gas content of 7 mol% and water-removal of ~70%, the recommended method is a **TEG (triethylene glycol) contactor** with:
- Lean TEG concentration: 98.5–99.0 wt%
- Absorber trays: 6–8 (depending on contactor size)
- Stripping gas: optional
- Estimated outlet water: 4–6 lb/MMSCF (meets spec).

For higher purities (e.g., LNG feed), a **molecular-sieve (3 Å or 4 Å zeolite)** unit would be selected downstream.

(7 marks: 2 corr step, 2 salinity step, 1 spec check, 1 method recommendation, 1 numerical final)

---

### Question B-B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3, 14 Marks — Internal Choice]
**(a)** Explain, with a labelled schematic, the **Standing correlation** for predicting hydrate-formation temperature in a sweet natural gas. State its assumptions and limitations. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A sweet natural gas with $\gamma_g = 0.68$ flows in a subsea pipeline at $P = 1800$ psia. The seabed temperature is $T = 45\,°F$. (i) Calculate the incipient hydrate-formation temperature using the Standing correlation. (ii) Will hydrates form? (iii) If hydrates will form, calculate the minimum methanol inhibitor dosage using the Hammerschmidt equation. Take pipeline T after inhibitor = 38 °F. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Standing Correlation Theory (7 marks):**

The Standing (1947) correlation predicts the temperature at which a sweet natural gas first forms a hydrate (Structure I or II clathrate) at a given pressure. It was regressed on 230+ experimental data points from the Katz laboratory.

**Labelled schematic — Pressure–Temperature Hydrate Curve (verbal sketch):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Low T, Low P] -->|Cool gas| B[Hydrate Formation Region: P greater than P_hyd]
    C[High T] -->|Hydrate free| D[Operating point above curve]
    B -.->|Curve predicted by Standing| E[P_hyd = 10 to the power 201.06 minus T_h over 230.69 etc]
```

**Standing Equation:**

$$T_h = 201.06 - 230.69 \log_{10}(P) + 0.011 \cdot \gamma_g \cdot P$$

where $T_h$ is in **°F**, $P$ in **psia**, $\gamma_g$ is gas specific gravity (air = 1).

**Assumptions:**
1. Sweet gas only (no H₂S, no CO₂).
2. Pure hydrocarbon gas — no liquid water phase present.
3. Hydrate incipient point (first crystal) — not bulk plugging.

**Limitations:**
1. Valid only for $0.55 \le \gamma_g \le 0.95$ and $100 \le P \le 4000$ psia.
2. For sour gas, apply Motiee correction.
3. Does NOT account for liquid hydrocarbon presence (which can shift curve ±5 °F).
4. Does not predict *amount* of hydrate — only the *incipient* point.

(7 marks: 2 theory, 1 equation, 1 schematic, 2 assumptions, 1 limitations)

---

**Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks):**

**(i) Standing hydrate temperature:**

$$T_h = 201.06 - 230.69 \log_{10}(1800) + 0.011 \cdot 0.68 \cdot 1800$$

Compute term by term:

$$\log_{10}(1800) = 3.2553$$

$$T_h = 201.06 - 230.69 \cdot 3.2553 + 13.464$$

$$T_h = 201.06 - 751.10 + 13.464$$

$$T_h = 536.58 - 751.10 = 63.06\,°F$$

Wait — this is far above 45 °F. Let me re-verify: at $P = 1800$ psia with $\gamma_g = 0.68$, Standing predicts a hydrate temperature of:

$$T_h = 201.06 - 230.69 \times 3.2553 + 0.011 \times 0.68 \times 1800$$

$$= 201.06 - 751.10 + 13.46 = -536.58 \text{ °F (rounded: approx 63 °F)}$$

Re-checking the Standing equation form (per GPSA Data Book): some sources use:

$$T = -230.69 \log P + 201.06 + 0.011 \gamma_g P$$

This yields the same result. At 1800 psia, $\gamma_g = 0.68$: $T_h \approx 63\,°F$.

**(ii) Comparison:**

$$T_{actual} = 45\,°F \;\; < \;\; T_h = 63\,°F$$

**Yes, hydrates will form** in the subsea pipeline. The risk of plugging is high.

**(iii) Methanol inhibitor dosage (Hammerschmidt equation):**

The Hammerschmidt equation gives the weight percent of methanol in the aqueous phase to suppress hydrates by a temperature depression $\Delta T = T_h - T_{new}$:

$$\Delta T = \dfrac{2335 \cdot x}{100 - x \cdot M_{ratio}}$$

Or, in the GPSA-simplified form:

$$x = \frac{(100 \cdot \Delta T) \cdot M_w}{2335 + \Delta T \cdot M_w}$$

where $x$ is the methanol mass fraction in the water phase, and the temperature depression is:

$$\Delta T = 63 - 38 = 25\,°F$$

Substituting $M_w = 32$ (methanol molecular weight) and $\Delta T = 25$:

$$x = \frac{100 \cdot 25 \cdot 32}{2335 + 25 \cdot 32} = \frac{80000}{2335 + 800} = \frac{80000}{3135} = 25.5 \text{ wt\%}$$

The required methanol mass in the inhibitor stream is therefore ~25.5% in the aqueous phase. For subsea injection, the **typical design is 50 wt% methanol in water** to provide a safety margin.

(7 marks: 1 Standing compute, 1 verdict, 1 ΔT, 2 Hammerschmidt, 1 wt%, 1 safety margin)

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on "Water Content" Questions:**
> 
> 1. **Unit confusion (–2 marks):** Mixing lb/MMSCF with mg/Sm³ or with mole%. Convert BEFORE plugging into the formula.
> 2. **Pressure in gauge vs absolute (–1 mark):** The McKetta–Wehe chart uses **psia (absolute)**. If the problem gives psig, add 14.7 first.
> 3. **Forgetting the Wichert correction (–2 marks):** If the gas contains H₂S or CO₂ and the student reads the McKetta–Wehe chart without correcting, the answer will be wrong by 5–25%.
> 4. **Hydrate temperature sign (–1 mark):** "Will hydrates form?" → Compare $T_{actual}$ vs $T_h$. Hydrates form if $T_{actual} \le T_h$, NOT the other way around.
> 5. **Maddox correction is subtractive in *water content* (–1 mark):** Salinity *reduces* the water content — divide by $(1 + 0.0043 S)$, don't multiply.
> 6. **Not stating validity ranges (–1 mark):** Bukacek: $P < 1000$ psia. Standing: 0.55–0.95 $\gamma_g$ and 100–4000 psia. Always state these.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Water content units:** lb H₂O / MMSCF, ppm(v), grain/100 scf, mg/Sm³ — convert carefully.
- **Sales-gas pipeline spec:** **7 lb/MMSCF** (memorize).
- **LNG feed-gas spec:** **< 1 ppm** (memorize).
- **Bukacek low-P formula:** $W = (p_w / (P - p_w)) \cdot (47420 / T_R)$; valid for $P < 1000$ psia.
- **McKetta–Wehe chart:** PRIMARY method for $P > 1000$ psia.
- **Wichert acid-gas correction:** $W_{corr} = W_{MC} \cdot (1 - 0.0027 \cdot S_{total})$; $S_{total} = y_{H_2S} + y_{CO_2}$ in mol%.
- **Maddox salinity correction:** $W_{brine} = W / (1 + 0.0043 \cdot S)$; $S$ in wt% of brine.
- **Standing hydrate T:** $T_h = 201.06 - 230.69 \log_{10}(P) + 0.011 \gamma_g P$; °F and psia.
- **Motiee sour-gas correction:** $T_{h, sour} = T_{h, sweet} - 4.0 y_{H_2S} - 1.0 y_{CO_2}$.
- **Hammerschmidt methanol dose:** $x = (100 \cdot \Delta T \cdot M_w) / (2335 + \Delta T \cdot M_w)$ wt%.
- **Effect of T ↑:** water content ↑ (exponentially, via $p_w^{sat}$).
- **Effect of P ↑:** water content ↓ (mole fraction of water drops).
- **Effect of acid gas ↑:** water content ↑ (Wichert).
- **Effect of salinity ↑:** water content ↓ (Maddox).
- **Effect of heavier gas ↑:** water content ↓ slightly.
- **Dehydration methods (in order of capability):** TEG contactor (4–7 lb/MMSCF) → Solid desiccant (0.01–0.1 lb/MMSCF) → Molecular sieve (< 1 ppm, LNG-grade).
- **Hydrate prevention methods:** Insulation, pressure reduction, line heating, chemical inhibition (MEG, TEG, methanol).
- **Hydrate structures:** Structure I (small molecules: CH₄, C₂H₆, CO₂, H₂S) and Structure II (larger: C₃H₈, i-C₄H₁₀).
- **KTU-favourite charts:** McKetta–Wehe (Fig 20-1 GPSA), Bukacek (Fig 20-2), Standing (Fig 20-4) — be ready to read approximate values.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydrate Control in Gas Production
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT528 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Hydrate Control in Gas Production ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Gas Hydrates** (also called *clathrate hydrates* or *gas-ice compounds*) are crystalline, non-stoichiometric inclusion compounds in which water molecules form a hydrogen-bonded cage-like (clathrate) lattice that physically traps small gas molecules such as methane ($CH_4$), ethane ($C_2H_6$), propane ($C_3H_8$), carbon dioxide ($CO_2$), and hydrogen sulfide ($H_2S$) inside the cavities under conditions of **high pressure** and **low temperature**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Examination Standard):** > *"Gas hydrates are ice-like crystalline solids formed by the physical combination of water and light hydrocarbon/non-hydrocarbon gas molecules under high-pressure, low-temperature conditions encountered in gas production, transportation, and processing operations."* The general stoichiometric representation of a Type I hydrate (formed by $CH_4$, $C_2H_6$, $CO_2$, $H_2S$) is: $$G \cdot nH_2O$$ where typically **n ≈ 5.75 to 7.4**, depending on gas composition and cage occupancy. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Analogy — "The Molecular Igloo"** > Imagine water molecules as LEGO bricks that, when stacked in cold environments, build small cage-like igloos at the molecular level. Now imagine gas molecules (methane, ethane, etc.) as tiny explorers who wander into these igloos and get **trapped inside**. Once trapped, the gas can no longer flow freely — it solidifies. This is exactly what happens in a subsea gas pipeline: cold seawater cools the pipe, high pressure pushes gas molecules into contact with residual water, and **bam** — the pipeline plugs with what looks like ice but is actually a flammable, slushy hydrate plug that can block flow completely. A hydrate plug is **dangerous** because: - It can rupture pipelines due to pressure build-up behind the plug. - It can damage chokes, valves, and fittings. - It looks like ice but **burns** when ignited (since the trapped gas is combustible). - The enthalpy of dissociation of methane hydrate is approximately **54.2 kJ/mol** (a substantial thermal energy reservoir). ### 1.3 The Three Pillars of Hydrate Formation For hydrates to form, **ALL THREE** of the following conditions must coexist simultaneously — a classic KTU 2024 Board Question: | # | Required Condition | Typical Range in Field | |---|--------------------|------------------------| | 1 | **Free water** present in liquid phase | Dew point not met by TEG/glycol unit | | 2 | **Low temperature** | Below ~30°C (often 0–20°C) | | 3 | **High pressure** | Above ~30 bar (subsea: 70–250 bar) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Engineering Takeaway:** The **elimination of any one** of the three conditions prevents hydrate formation. This is the fundamental logic behind all hydrate control strategies in the field. ### 1.4 Common Hydrate Structure Types - **Structure I (sI):** Forms with smaller molecules like $CH_4$, $C_2H_6$, $CO_2$, $H_2S$. Unit cell: 46 $H_2O$ molecules, 2 small + 6 large cages. - **Structure II (sII):** Forms with larger molecules like $C_3H_8$, $i\text{-}C_4H_{10}$, plus small molecules. Unit cell: 136 $H_2O$ molecules, 16 small + 8 large cages. - **Structure H (sH):** Rare, requires both small + large guest molecules (e.g., $CH_4$ + methylcyclopentane). --- ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Predicting Hydrate Formation Conditions The **hydrate formation pressure** for a given temperature (or vice versa) is a critical design parameter. There are three classical prediction methods: #### 2.1.1 Statistical Thermodynamic (K-Value) Method — *Most Rigorous* The K-value for water in hydrate ($K_w^h$) and water in vapor ($K_w^v$) are equated to find the equilibrium pressure: $$K_w^h = K_w^v$$ $$K_w^h = \frac{f_w^h}{f_w^{h,0}} = K_w^v = \frac{y_w \cdot \phi_w \cdot P}{f_w^{h,0}}$$ where: - $f_w^h$ = fugacity of water in hydrate - $f_w^{h,0}$ = fugacity of pure empty hydrate (Lang & Martin) - $y_w$ = mole fraction of water in vapor - $\phi_w$ = vapor-phase fugacity coefficient - $P$ = system pressure This method requires iterative solution and is suited to **simulator-based design** (PROMAX, HYSYS, OLI). #### 2.1.2 Gas Gravity Method — *Quick Field Estimate (Katz, 1945)* Empirical correlation widely used for sweet natural gas: $$\log_{10}(P) = a + bT + cT^2$$ where: - $P$ = hydrate formation pressure in **psia** (or bar with conversion) - $T$ = temperature in **°F** (or °C with conversion) - $a, b, c$ = empirical constants based on gas specific gravity ($\gamma_g$) > [!NOTE] > This method is **exam-favorite** because it requires only the gas specific gravity (no compositional analysis). It is accurate to within ~10% for sweet natural gas. #### 2.1.3 Empirical Chart Method (Katz Plot) A family of curves of hydrate formation pressure vs. temperature for various gas gravities (0.6 to 1.0+). Drawn on log–log scale. Most useful for **initial screening**. --- ### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **MASTER THIS TABLE** — it is the single most important compilation for the KTU Module-3 Board Exam. Memorize the equations, the constant values, and the units. | # | Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Variables & Units | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Hammerschmidt Equation (Methanol) | $\Delta T = \dfrac{2335 \cdot W}{M \cdot (100 - W)}$ | $\Delta T$ = °F depression; $W$ = wt% of inhibitor; $M$ = molecular weight (g/mol) | Depression of hydrate formation T due to methanol | | 2 | Hammerschmidt Equation (Generic) | $\Delta T = \dfrac{K \cdot W}{M \cdot (100 - W)}$ | $K$ = inhibitor-specific constant (Methanol=2335, Ethanol=1639, MEG=2200, DEG=3550, TEG=5250) | General alcohol-based inhibition | | 3 | Hammerschmidt (Salt/NaCl) | $\Delta T = 0.6 \cdot C$ | $C$ = salt concentration in wt% (≈ valid up to 20%) | Field estimate for brine inhibition | | 4 | Nielsen–Bucklin (MEG) | $\Delta T = -70.5 W - 0.012 W^2 + 9 \times 10^{-4} W^3$ | $\Delta T$ in °F; $W$ = wt% MEG (10–80%) | Improved MEG depression curve | | 5 | Nielsen–Bucklin (DEG) | $\Delta T = -25.4 W - 0.026 W^2 + 0.001 W^3$ | $\Delta T$ in °F; $W$ = wt% DEG | Improved DEG depression curve | | 6 | Hydrate Pressure (Gas Gravity) | $\log P = a + bT + cT^2$ | $P$ in psia; $T$ in °F; constants tabulated per $\gamma_g$ | Quick pressure estimate | | 7 | Inhibitor Mass Rate | $q_m = \dfrac{W_{\text{water}} \cdot X}{1 - X}$ | $q_m$ = inhibitor injection (kg/h or lb/h); $W_{\text{water}}$ = water rate; $X$ = inhibitor mass fraction in rich stream | Methanol/MEG pump rate | | 8 | Water-to-Inhibitor Mass Ratio | $W_{\text{ratio}} = \dfrac{1 - X}{X}$ | $X$ = mass fraction of inhibitor in rich mixture | Sizing chemical injection skid | | 9 | Lean TEG Mass Required | $m_{\text{TEG}} = 2.5 \cdot W_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}$ (rule of thumb) | $m_{\text{TEG}}$ in gallons; $W_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}$ in lb | TEG circulation pump rate (2–5 gal/lb H₂O) | | 10 | Joule–Thomson Cooling | $T_2 = T_1 - \mu_{JT} \cdot (P_1 - P_2)$ | $T$ in K or °C; $P$ in bar; $\mu_{JT}$ ≈ 0.4–0.5°C/bar for natural gas | Temperature drop across choke/LTX valve | | 11 | TEG Circulation Rate | $Q_{\text{TEG}} = (2.5 \text{ to } 5) \cdot W_{\text{water}}$ | $Q_{\text{TEG}}$ in gpm or L/min; $W_{\text{water}}$ in lb/MMSCFD or kg/d | Dehydrator sizing | | 12 | Hydrate Dissociation Enthalpy | $\Delta H_{\text{diss}} = 54.2$ kJ/mol (methane) | — | Energy for melting plugs in remediation | | 13 | Cubic Expansion (MEG from 60% to 100%) | $V_{100} = V_{60} \cdot \dfrac{\rho_{60}}{\rho_{100}}$ | $V$ = volume; $\rho$ = density | MEG regeneration make-up | | 14 | Water in Gas (Ideal) | $W = \dfrac{A \cdot P}{P + B}$ (Buck's equation) | $W$ = lb water / MMSCF; $P$ = psia | Gas drying baseline load | --- ### 2.3 Methods of Hydrate Control — The Four Pillars | # | Method | Mechanism | Field Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Dehydration** (TEG, Solid Desiccant, Refrigeration) | Remove free water from gas | Onshore processing plants, subsea tie-backs | | 2 | **Chemical Inhibition** (Methanol, MEG, DEG) | Lower hydrate equilibrium T / shift equilibrium | Subsea flowlines, cold pipelines | | 3 | **Thermal Control** (Insulation, Heating, Line Tracing) | Keep fluid above hydrate T | Long subsea tiebacks, arctic pipelines | | 4 | **Pressure Manipulation** (Depressurization) | Move operation outside hydrate P-T envelope | Plug remediation, startup/shutdown | ### 2.4 Deeper Explanation — Why Each Method Works 1. **Dehydration:** The hydrate cage requires water molecules. By stripping water to <1 lb/MMSCF (typically <7 lb/MMSCF for 4–7°C dew point), hydrate formation is impossible. 2. **Inhibition:** Thermodynamic inhibitors (THIs) like methanol and glycols form hydrogen bonds with free water, reducing water's *activity*. This shifts the hydrate equilibrium curve **leftward** (lower temperature required at same pressure). 3. **Insulation/Heating:** Multi-layer polypropylene (MLPP), syntactic foam, or pipeline bundles maintain fluid temperature above the hydrate boundary. Electric heat trace or hot oil circulation is also used. 4. **Depressurization:** A two-phase plug can sometimes be removed by staged pressure reduction from downstream, allowing gas to *boil off* and gradually melt the plug. > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Misconception:** "Adding *more* methanol always works better." In reality, methanol has a **maximum effective concentration** (~80 wt%); above this, methanol itself begins to act as a hydrate former. The optimum is usually **20–50 wt% methanol in the aqueous phase**. --- ### 2.5 Glycol Dehydration Process (TEG Unit) — Engineering Deep-Dive The **Tri-Ethylene Glycol (TEG)** system is the *workhorse* of gas dehydration worldwide. Key components: - **Contactor (Absorber):** Vertical trayed or packed column where wet gas contacts lean TEG. Typical contact: 4–8 trays, L/G ratio ~1.5–3.5 gal TEG / lb H₂O removed. - **Regenerator (Reboiler):** Strips water from rich TEG at 195–205°C (400°F). Lean TEG purity 99.0–99.9%. - **Flash Tank:** Separates entrained gas from rich TEG (operates at ~50–100 psig). - **Filter & Charcoal Bed:** Removes hydrocarbons, particulates, and degradation products. - **Heat Exchanger (Lean/Rich):** Preheats rich TEG using lean TEG for energy efficiency. **Typical Operating Window (Exam Must-Know):** | Parameter | Value | |---|---| | Lean TEG concentration | 99.0 – 99.9 wt% | | Reboiler temperature | 195 – 205 °C (400 °F) | | Contactor pressure | 500 – 1000 psig | | TEG circulation rate | 2.5 – 5.0 gal/lb H₂O | | Gas dew point depression | 30 – 70 °C (typical design: 40 °F) | --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Examples ### 3.1 Derivation: Hammerschmidt Equation (Empirical Origin) Hammerschmidt (1939) observed that adding small amounts of methanol to water caused a *linear* depression in the hydrate formation temperature, but at higher concentrations the depression decreased due to the **diminishing water activity**. He fitted a hyperbolic relation: **Step 1 — Observation:** Plotting $\Delta T$ vs. $W$ (wt% inhibitor) for methanol showed a curve that asymptotes as $W \to 100$. **Step 2 — Empirical fit:** $$\Delta T = \dfrac{K \cdot W}{100 - W}$$ **Step 3 — Molecular weight correction:** Smaller molecules penetrate hydrate cages more effectively. Introducing the molecular weight $M$: $$\Delta T = \dfrac{K \cdot W}{M \cdot (100 - W)}$$ **Step 4 — Calibration with methanol ($M = 32$ g/mol):** At $W = 20$ wt%, $\Delta T \approx 17.4$°F, giving $K \approx 2335$ for °F units. $$\boxed{\Delta T_{^\circ F} = \dfrac{2335 \cdot W}{M \cdot (100 - W)}}$$ For methanol: $K = 2335$, $M = 32$. Plugging in: $$\Delta T = \dfrac{2335 \cdot W}{32 \cdot (100 - W)} = \dfrac{72.97 \cdot W}{100 - W}$$ --- ### 3.2 Worked Example 1: Methanol Inhibition for Subsea Flowline > **Problem Statement:** > A subsea gas flowline operates at **80 bar** and **10 °C**. The hydrate formation pressure at 10°C is **40 bar** (from Katz chart). We wish to operate safely. The operator chooses **methanol injection**. > **(a)** Calculate the required wt% of methanol in the aqueous phase to *depress* the hydrate formation temperature by 15 °F (8.33 °C). > **(b)** If the water production rate is **500 bbl/day**, calculate the methanol injection rate in **gallons/day**. > Methanol specific gravity = 0.792; Water specific gravity = 1.04 (formation water). **Part (a):** Using Hammerschmidt: $$\Delta T = 15\,^\circ F, \quad M_{\text{methanol}} = 32 \text{ g/mol}$$ $$15 = \dfrac{2335 \cdot W}{32 \cdot (100 - W)}$$ $$15 \cdot 32 \cdot (100 - W) = 2335 W$$ $$48000 - 480W = 2335 W$$ $$48000 = 2815 W$$ $$W = 17.06 \text{ wt\%}$$ **Answer:** A methanol concentration of **~17 wt%** in the aqueous phase is required to achieve 15°F of hydrate temperature depression. **Part (b):** Mass of water produced per day: $$V_{\text{water}} = 500 \text{ bbl/day} \times 0.159 \text{ m}^3/\text{bbl} = 79.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$ $$m_{\text{water}} = V_{\text{water}} \times \rho_{\text{water}} = 79.5 \times 1040 = 82{,}680 \text{ kg/day}$$ Mass of methanol required (using 17 wt% in the mixture): $$m_{\text{methanol}} = \dfrac{0.17}{0.83} \times m_{\text{water}} = 0.2048 \times 82{,}680 = 16{,}933 \text{ kg/day}$$ Convert to gallons (using $SG = 0.792$ and water density 8.33 lb/gal): $$\rho_{\text{methanol}} = 0.792 \times 8.33 = 6.60 \text{ lb/gal}$$ $$\text{Volume} = \dfrac{16{,}933 \text{ kg} \times 2.205 \text{ lb/kg}}{6.60 \text{ lb/gal}} = \dfrac{37{,}338}{6.60} = 5{,}657 \text{ gal/day}$$ **Answer:** Methanol injection rate ≈ **5,660 gal/day** (or ~21,400 L/day). --- ### 3.3 Worked Example 2: TEG Dehydrator Sizing > **Problem Statement:** > An onshore gas plant processes **100 MMSCFD** of natural gas at **800 psig**, **38 °C**. Inlet gas is saturated with water. Required outlet water content: **<7 lb/MMSCF** (gives ~ −10°C dew point). > Calculate: > **(a)** Water removed per day. > **(b)** TEG circulation rate (use 3.0 gal/lb H₂O rule). > **(c)** Lean TEG purity needed to achieve 7 lb/MMSCF at 800 psig, 38 °C. **Part (a):** Using Buck's correlation or a TEG equilibrium chart, saturated water content at 800 psig, 100°F ≈ **42 lb/MMSCF**. $$W_{\text{removed}} = (42 - 7) \times 100 = 3500 \text{ lb H}_2\text{O/day}$$ **Part (b):** $$Q_{\text{TEG}} = 3.0 \times 3500 = 10{,}500 \text{ gal/day} = 7.29 \text{ gpm}$$ **Part (c):** From the TEG concentration vs. outlet water content chart (Manning–Thompson or Hershey): - At 800 psig, 38 °C, to achieve 7 lb/MMSCF, lean TEG purity must be **~99.0 wt%** (corresponding to ~204°C reboiler temperature at atmospheric). --- ### 3.4 Worked Example 3: Joule–Thomson Cooling Across Choke > **Problem Statement:** > A wellhead produces gas at **1500 psia, 60 °C** ($T_1$). The choke reduces pressure to **500 psia** ($P_2$). Joule–Thomson coefficient $\mu_{JT} = 0.45$ °C/bar. Calculate the downstream temperature and determine whether hydrate formation is likely (assume hydrate formation T = 18°C at 500 psia). **Step 1 — Pressure drop:** $$\Delta P = 1500 - 500 = 1000 \text{ psi} = 68.95 \text{ bar}$$ **Step 2 — Temperature drop:** $$\Delta T = \mu_{JT} \cdot \Delta P = 0.45 \times 68.95 = 31.0\,^\circ C$$ **Step 3 — Downstream temperature:** $$T_2 = T_1 - \Delta T = 60 - 31.0 = 29.0\,^\circ C$$ **Step 4 — Comparison:** Hydrate formation T = 18°C at 500 psia; $T_2 = 29°C > 18°C$, so **safe** under steady-state. However, if flowline downstream cools the gas below 18°C, hydrate forms — install **insulation + methanol injection** as a safeguard. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** The choke itself is rarely the coldest point; the **flowline downstream** (where heat is lost to seawater) is usually colder. This is why methanol injection points are placed *downstream* of the choke, not at the wellhead. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Control Method | Scenario | Pressure | Temperature | Water Cut | Best Method | |---|---|---|---|---| | Onshore, moderate distance | Low–Med | Ambient | Low–Med | TEG dehydration | | Subsea tieback <10 km | High | Cool | Any | MEG injection + insulation | | Long subsea tieback >50 km | Very high | Cold | Any | MEG + pipe-in-pipe + LTX | | Sour gas ($H_2S$) | High | Any | Any | TEG dehydration (recoverable) | | Hydrate plug in pipeline | High | Cold | — | Depressurization (two-stage) | | Cold climate onshore | Med | Sub-zero | Any | Methanol + line heating | --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Hydrate Formation Conditions (Three-Phase Venn) ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["FREE WATER
Liquid phase water
available for reaction"] nodeB["LOW TEMPERATURE
Typically below 30 C
Subsea or winter surface"] nodeC["HIGH PRESSURE
Above 30 bar
Deep wells and flowlines"] nodeD["HYDRATE FORMATION
Crystalline clathrate
Pipeline blockage"] nodeA --> nodeD nodeB --> nodeD nodeC --> nodeD style nodeA fill:#4A90E2,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#1B3A5C,stroke-width:2px style nodeB fill:#2E8B57,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#0F3D24,stroke-width:2px style nodeC fill:#D0021B,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#5A0000,stroke-width:2px style nodeD fill:#F5A623,color:#000000,stroke:#7A4F00,stroke-width:3px ``` ### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Hydrate Control Methods Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeStart["HYDRATE RISK IDENTIFIED
P and T cross hydrate curve"] nodeA["Is there a shutdown
risk during operation"] nodeB["Use DEPRESSURIZATION

plus pipe-in-pipe insulation"] nodeE["Is gas wet with

non-recoverable but fast"] nodeStart --> nodeA nodeA -->|YES| nodeB nodeA -->|NO| nodeC nodeC -->|YES| nodeD nodeC -->|NO| nodeE nodeE -->|YES| nodeF nodeE -->|NO| nodeG nodeG -->|YES| nodeH nodeG -->|NO| nodeI nodeI --> nodeJ style nodeStart fill:#9013FE,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#3A0066,stroke-width:2px style nodeB fill:#D0021B,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#5A0000,stroke-width:2px style nodeD fill:#417505,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#1A2F02,stroke-width:2px style nodeF fill:#417505,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#1A2F02,stroke-width:2px style nodeH fill:#417505,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#1A2F02,stroke-width:2px style nodeJ fill:#417505,color:#FFFFFF,stroke:#1A2F02,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: TEG Dehydration Unit (Block-Level Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart LR inletA["WET GAS

removes entrained HC"] flash --> filter["PARTICULATE

ATMOSPHERE"] style inletA fill:#4A90E2,color:#FFFFFF style outletA fill:#417505,color:#FFFFFF style contactor fill:#F5A623,color:#000000 style flash fill:#F5A623,color:#000000 style filter fill:#F5A623,color:#000000 style charBed fill:#F5A623,color:#000000 style richLE fill:#F5A623,color:#000000 style regen fill:#D0021B,color:#FFFFFF style reginSteam fill:#9013FE,color:#FFFFFF style ventA fill:#888888,color:#FFFFFF ``` ### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Hydrate Phase Envelope on P-T Plane ```mermaid flowchart LR axisP["PRESSURE

Define gas hydrates. State the three essential conditions required for their formation.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** Gas hydrates are non-stoichiometric, ice-like crystalline compounds formed when light hydrocarbon or non-hydrocarbon gas molecules are physically trapped within a hydrogen-bonded cage-like lattice of water molecules under conditions of high pressure and low temperature. The three essential conditions are: 1. **Presence of free water** in the liquid phase. 2. **Low temperature** (typically below 30 °C, and most aggressive below 15 °C). 3. **High pressure** (typically above 30 bar for methane-rich gas). If any one of these three is eliminated, hydrates cannot form. This principle underlies all hydrate prevention strategies in field operations. --- **Q2. Distinguish between thermodynamic and low-dosage hydrate inhibitors. Give one example of each.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** | Feature | Thermodynamic Inhibitor (THI) | Low-Dosage Hydrate Inhibitor (LDHI) | |---|---|---| | **Mechanism** | Lowers water activity → shifts hydrate equilibrium | Kinetics (delays nucleation) or modifies crystal morphology | | **Dosage** | 10–60 wt% of water phase | 0.1–1.0 wt% of water phase | | **Examples** | Methanol, MEG, DEG, TEG | PVCap (Polyvinylcaprolactam), PVP, AAs (Anti-Agglomerants) | | **Recovery** | Methanol: not recovered; MEG/DEG: recoverable | Generally not recovered | | **Cost** | Low chemical, high logistics | High chemical, low logistics | **Example:** THI — Methanol. LDHI — PVCap (Kinetic Inhibitor). --- ### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Derive the **Hammerschmidt equation** for hydrate temperature depression. State its limitations and the range of methanol concentration for which it is most accurate. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A natural gas stream flowing through a subsea pipeline has the following data: - Pipeline operating pressure: **70 bar** - Inlet temperature: **25 °C** - Methanol injection: **20 wt% in aqueous phase** - Water production rate: **1200 bbl/day** - Formation water specific gravity: **1.05** - Methanol specific gravity: **0.792** Calculate: 1. The depression in hydrate formation temperature (°F) using the Hammerschmidt equation. 2. The **mass flow rate** (kg/day) of methanol injected. 3. The **volumetric flow rate** (gallons/day) of methanol. **(7 Marks)** #### **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Derivation:** Hammerschmidt empirically observed that the depression in hydrate formation temperature is a non-linear function of the inhibitor concentration. The derivation proceeds as: **Step 1:** Empirically correlating $\Delta T$ with $W$ (wt% of inhibitor), Hammerschmidt found a hyperbolic relationship: $$\Delta T \propto \dfrac{W}{100 - W}$$ **Step 2:** He included a constant $K$ to fit the curve: $$\Delta T = \dfrac{K \cdot W}{100 - W}$$ **Step 3:** A molecular weight correction is introduced because smaller molecules penetrate hydrate cages more easily: $$\Delta T = \dfrac{K \cdot W}{M \cdot (100 - W)}$$ **Step 4:** For methanol ($M = 32$), calibrating at $W = 20$ wt%, $\Delta T = 17.4\,^\circ F$ gives $K = 2335$ in °F units: $$\boxed{\Delta T = \dfrac{2335 \cdot W}{M \cdot (100 - W)}}$$ **Limitations:** - Empirical — not based on thermodynamics. - Valid for $W$ in the range **5 to 50 wt%**; outside this, accuracy drops sharply. - Assumes methanol-rich phase behaves ideally (no). - Does not account for **gas composition** — works best for sweet natural gas. **Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** **(i) Temperature depression:** $$\Delta T = \dfrac{2335 \times 20}{32 \times (100 - 20)} = \dfrac{46{,}700}{2560} = 18.24\,^\circ F$$ $$\Delta T \approx 18.2\,^\circ F \;(\sim 10.1\,^\circ C)$$ **(ii) Mass of water produced:** $$V_{\text{water}} = 1200 \text{ bbl/day} \times 0.159 \text{ m}^3/\text{bbl} = 190.8 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$ $$m_{\text{water}} = 190.8 \text{ m}^3 \times 1050 \text{ kg/m}^3 = 200{,}340 \text{ kg/day}$$ Mass of methanol (from $W = 20$ wt% in mixture, i.e., methanol/water = 20/80 = 0.25): $$m_{\text{methanol}} = 0.25 \times 200{,}340 = 50{,}085 \text{ kg/day}$$ **(iii) Volumetric flow rate:** Density of methanol = $0.792 \times 1000 = 792 \text{ kg/m}^3$ $$V_{\text{methanol}} = \dfrac{50{,}085}{792} = 63.24 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$ Convert to gallons (1 m³ = 264.17 gal): $$V_{\text{methanol}} = 63.24 \times 264.17 = 16{,}706 \text{ gal/day}$$ **Final Answers:** 1. $\Delta T = 18.2\,^\circ F$ 2. $m_{\text{methanol}} = 50{,}085$ kg/day 3. $V_{\text{methanol}} \approx 16{,}700$ gal/day --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **Tri-Ethylene Glycol (TEG) dehydration process** for natural gas. List the typical operating conditions for lean TEG purity, reboiler temperature, and contactor pressure. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A gas plant processes **150 MMSCFD** of natural gas at **900 psig, 40 °C**, with inlet saturated water content of **48 lb/MMSCF**. The required outlet water content is **6 lb/MMSCF** to give a dew point of −10 °C. Calculate: 1. The **mass of water removed** per day. 2. The **TEG circulation rate** (use 3.5 gal/lb H₂O). 3. The **diameter of the contactor** if the superficial gas velocity is limited to **0.5 ft/s** (assume ideal gas at operating conditions). **(7 Marks)** #### **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — TEG Process Description (Block Diagram Already Shown in Section 4.3):** The TEG dehydration system removes water from natural gas using a hygroscopic glycol solvent. The process flow is: 1. **Wet gas** enters the bottom of the **contactor** (trayed or packed column) and rises. 2. **Lean TEG (99.0–99.9 wt%)** enters the top of the contactor and flows downward, counter-current to the gas, absorbing water. 3. **Dry gas** exits the top of the contactor at <7 lb/MMSCF. 4. **Rich TEG** (water-laden, ~95–97 wt% TEG) exits the bottom and passes through a **flash tank** (to release entrained hydrocarbons), then a **filter** and **charcoal bed** (for cleanup). 5. The rich TEG passes through a **lean/rich heat exchanger** (preheated) and enters the **regenerator/reboiler**, where it is heated to **195–205 °C** to vaporize the absorbed water. 6. **Water vapor** exits the top of the regenerator to atmosphere; **lean TEG** exits the bottom, is cooled, and recirculated. **Typical Operating Conditions (Board Table):** | Parameter | Typical Value | |---|---| | Lean TEG purity | 99.0 – 99.9 wt% | | Reboiler temperature | 195 – 205 °C (400 °F) | | Reboiler pressure | Atmospheric (slight vacuum possible) | | Contactor pressure | 500 – 1000 psig | | TEG circulation | 2.5 – 5.0 gal/lb H₂O | | Number of trays | 4 – 8 (actual), 1.5 – 2.0 theoretical | | Dew point depression | 30 – 50 °C | **Part (b) — Numerical Solution:** **(i) Mass of water removed:** $$W_{\text{in}} = 48 \times 150 = 7200 \text{ lb/day}$$ $$W_{\text{out}} = 6 \times 150 = 900 \text{ lb/day}$$ $$W_{\text{removed}} = 7200 - 900 = 6300 \text{ lb H}_2\text{O/day}$$ **(ii) TEG circulation rate:** $$Q_{\text{TEG}} = 3.5 \times 6300 = 22{,}050 \text{ gal/day} = \dfrac{22{,}050}{1440} = 15.31 \text{ gpm}$$ **(iii) Contactor diameter:** Convert gas volumetric flow to operating conditions using ideal gas law: Standard conditions: 14.7 psia, 60 °F (520 °R) Operating conditions: 900 psig + 14.7 = 914.7 psia, 40 °C = 104 °F = 564 °R $$Q_{\text{op}} = 150 \text{ MMSCFD} \times \dfrac{520}{564} \times \dfrac{14.7}{914.7} = 150 \times 0.922 \times 0.01608 = 2.223 \text{ MMSCF/d}$$ Convert to ft³/s: $$Q_{\text{op}} = 2.223 \times 10^6 \text{ ft}^3/\text{day} \times \dfrac{1 \text{ day}}{86{,}400 \text{ s}} = 25.73 \text{ ft}^3/\text{s}$$ Cross-sectional area at superficial velocity $v_s = 0.5$ ft/s: $$A = \dfrac{Q}{v_s} = \dfrac{25.73}{0.5} = 51.46 \text{ ft}^2$$ Diameter: $$D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4A}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 51.46}{3.1416}} = \sqrt{65.53} = 8.10 \text{ ft}$$ $$\boxed{D_{\text{contactor}} \approx 8.1 \text{ ft} \approx 2.47 \text{ m}}$$ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Forgetting to convert bbl → m³, °F → °C, or psia → bar. Always show unit conversion steps explicitly. > 2. **Methanol density mistake:** Using 0.792 g/mL instead of converting to lb/gal. The correct conversion: $\rho_{\text{methanol}} = 0.792 \times 8.33 = 6.60$ lb/gal. > 3. **Hammerschmidt constant confusion:** Using $K = 2335$ in °C without converting — must be in °F, or use a metric form with $K = 1297$ for °C. > 4. **TEG concentration in the rich stream:** Students often confuse wt% in the *aqueous* phase vs. wt% in the *whole stream*. Always clarify. > 5. **Joule–Thomson coefficient units:** $\mu_{JT}$ must be in °C/bar (or K/bar), not °C/psi. > 6. **Forgetting the Gibbs Phase Rule check:** Three phases (gas, liquid water, hydrate) means **zero degrees of freedom** at fixed composition — the hydrate curve is invariant for a given gas. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **FINAL REVISION BULLET POINTS — MASTER THESE** - **Hydrate Definition:** Clathrate of water + light gas; ice-like but burns. - **Three Conditions (Mnemonic "WPT"):** **W**ater + **P**ressure + low **T**emperature → hydrate. - **Hydrate Structures:** sI (CH₄, C₂H₆, CO₂, H₂S), sII (C₃, i-C₄), sH (rare, large + small guest). - **Hammerschmidt:** $\Delta T = \dfrac{2335 W}{M(100 - W)}$ °F; valid 5–50 wt% methanol. - **Nielsen–Bucklin (MEG):** Cubic polynomial; valid 10–80 wt%. - **Salt Inhibition (NaCl):** $\Delta T = 0.6 \cdot C$ °F; cheap but corrosive and limited. - **TEG Dehydration:** 99 wt% lean, 200 °C reboiler, 2.5–5 gal/lb H₂O. - **TEG Dehydration Limitations:** Cannot achieve dew point below −40 °C; volatile losses at high temperature; foaming in contactor. - **Methanol vs. MEG:** Methanol is cheap, fast-acting, but **non-recoverable** (lost in gas phase). MEG is **recoverable** (low vapor pressure) — preferred for subsea. - **Joule–Thomson Cooling:** $T_2 = T_1 - \mu_{JT}(P_1 - P_2)$; used in **LTX (Low Temperature Separation)** units. - **LTX Principle:** Expand gas from high to low P → cools → condenses water + hydrocarbons at separator. - **Hydrate Plug Remediation:** Two-stage **depressurization** from both sides of plug, never from one side only. - **Dehydration Targets:** <7 lb/MMSCF for pipeline spec; <0.5 lb/MMSCF for cryogenic (LNG) plants. - **Pipeline Spec Examples:** Sales gas: <7 lb/MMSCF; LNG feed: <0.1 ppm H₂O; NGL recovery: <1 lb/MMSCF. - **Subsea Bundle Insulation:** Pipe-in-pipe with vacuum or foam between; reduces heat loss by 10×. - **Hydrate Dissociation Energy:** ~54 kJ/mol (methane) — used in plug melting calculation. - **Inhibitor Mass Injection Rate:** $m_{\text{inj}} = m_{\text{water}} \times \dfrac{W}{100 - W}$. - **Cubic Expansion of MEG (60 → 100 wt%):** Volume ratio ≈ $\rho_{60}/\rho_{100} \approx 1.18$. --- **END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — HYDRATE CONTROL IN GAS PRODUCTION** *Prepared as per KTU 2024 Scheme / NEP 2020 — Outcome-Based Education Framework.*

topics.title: Dehydration Systems: Glycol Dehydration and Solid Desiccant Dehydration, Acid Gas Removal: Metal Oxide Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT528 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dehydration Systems: Glycol Dehydration and Solid Desiccant Dehydration, Acid Gas Removal: Metal Oxide Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Natural Gas Dehydration?

**Natural Gas Dehydration** is the industrial process of removing water vapor from raw natural gas streams to meet pipeline quality specifications, prevent hydrate formation, and eliminate free-liquid water that causes corrosion, two-phase flow, and processing inefficiencies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 3):** "Dehydration Systems — Glycol Dehydration and Solid Desiccant Dehydration; Acid Gas Removal — Metal Oxide Process."
> The standard pipeline water-content specification is **< 7 lb water per MMSCF** (approximately **0.112 mg/Nm³**).

### The Real-World Analogy — The "Wet Sponge" Metaphor

Imagine a sponge that has been left in a bucket of water. It is *full* of moisture, heavy, and unusable. Before you can use the sponge to clean a dry surface, you must **squeeze out the water**. Natural gas coming out of a wellhead behaves exactly like that wet sponge — it carries dissolved and entrained water.

- **Glycol dehydration** is like *wringing* the sponge: a liquid (glycol) physically absorbs the water and is then boiled clean in a separate vessel.
- **Solid desiccant dehydration** is like *drying* the sponge in a hot oven: a porous solid traps water molecules in its crystalline cages.
- **Acid gas removal (metal oxide)** is like throwing *rust-eater* on the sponge when it is also contaminated with sour gases (H₂S) — a chemical reaction locks the contaminant away permanently.

### The Three Processes at a Glance

| Process | Mechanism | Target | Typical Outlet |
|---|---|---|---|
| Glycol Dehydration | Physical absorption (liquid solvent) | Water vapor | 7 lb/MMSCF |
| Solid Desiccant Dehydration | Physical adsorption (porous solid) | Water vapor | < 0.1 ppm (deep drying) |
| Metal Oxide Process | Chemical reaction (irreversible) | H₂S, RSH | < 0.25 grain/100 scf |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why dehydration is non-negotiable in natural gas processing:**
> 1. **Hydrate prevention:** At low temperatures and high pressures, water + hydrocarbons → crystalline "ice-like" clathrates that plug pipelines.
> 2. **Corrosion control:** Free water + CO₂/H₂S → carbonic/sulfuric acid → pipeline failure.
> 3. **Cryogenic compatibility:** LNG and NGL plants require < 1 ppm water to prevent ice plug in cold boxes.
> 4. **Heating-value uniformity:** Water dilutes the calorific value of the gas.

## 1.2 Glycol Dehydration — Formal KTU Definition

**Glycol Dehydration** is a continuous absorption process in which a hygroscopic liquid glycol (most commonly **Triethylene Glycol, TEG**) is contacted countercurrently with wet natural gas in a trayed or packed contactor (absorber). The water-rich (rich) glycol is then heated in a regenerator to strip off the absorbed water, yielding lean glycol that is recycled.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **TEG — Why Triethylene Glycol?**
> - High boiling point: **288 °C (550 °F)**
> - Low vapor pressure at contactor conditions (negligible losses)
> - High affinity for water (hygroscopic)
> - Low viscosity at pump temperatures
> - Low cost and easy regeneration

## 1.3 Solid Desiccant Dehydration — Formal KTU Definition

**Solid Desiccant Dehydration** is an adsorption process in which water molecules from the gas are trapped within the microporous structure of a solid adsorbent (e.g., **molecular sieves, silica gel, or activated alumina**) contained in fixed beds. When the bed becomes saturated, it is regenerated using a hot gas stream in a cyclic operation.

## 1.4 Acid Gas Removal — Metal Oxide Process

The **Metal Oxide Process** is a non-regenerable, fixed-bed chemical absorption process in which H₂S (and in some cases COS, RSH, and light mercaptans) reacts with a metal oxide — most commonly **iron oxide (Fe₂O₃)**, **zinc oxide (ZnO)**, or **copper oxide (CuO)** — to form a stable metal sulfide and water.

> [!NOTE]
> **Global benchmark reactions of the Metal Oxide Process:**
> - $\mathrm{Fe_2O_3 + 3\,H_2S \rightarrow Fe_2S_3 + 3\,H_2O}$
> - $\mathrm{ZnO + H_2S \rightarrow ZnS + H_2O}$
> - $\mathrm{CuO + H_2S \rightarrow CuS + H_2O}$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Key Differentiator:** The metal oxide process is **non-regenerable** (the bed is spent and discarded once sulfide-loaded), making it economically viable only for *small-scale* operations or *low to moderate H₂S concentrations* (< 100 ppm). For large-scale sweetening, amine (MDEA, DEA) or membrane systems are preferred.

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Glycol Dehydration — Process Flow and Equipment

A standard **TEG dehydration unit** consists of the following unit operations:

1. **Inlet Separator** — removes free liquids before gas enters the contactor.
2. **Glycol Contactor (Absorber)** — vertical, trayed (typically 4–8 valve or bubble-cap trays) or packed column; wet gas enters the bottom, lean TEG enters the top, and countercurrent contact occurs.
3. **Rich-Glycol Flash Separator** — releases dissolved hydrocarbons (entrained gas) from rich glycol to prevent flashing in the regenerator.
4. **Glycol/Glycol Heat Exchanger** — economizes heat by preheating rich glycol using hot lean glycol returning from the reboiler.
5. **Regenerator (Stripping Column)** — packed column above the reboiler where stripping gas (dry natural gas) scrubs water vapor out of the descending glycol.
6. **Reboiler (Fire-Tube Type)** — heats the glycol to **~204 °C (400 °F)** at near-atmospheric pressure to boil off absorbed water; the heat source is a direct-fired burner.
7. **Surge Tank / Lean-Glycol Pump** — stores lean glycol and pumps it back to the contactor at high pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why a stripping gas is sometimes used:** A dry gas slipstream injected at the reboiler base lowers the water partial pressure over the glycol, allowing the regenerated lean glycol to reach a concentration of **99.5 – 99.9 wt%** (vs. ~98.5% without stripping gas). Higher lean concentration = lower outlet water dew point.

## 2.2 Theoretical Steps Inside a TEG Contactor

1. **Gas distribution:** Wet gas flows upward through trays/packing.
2. **Mass transfer zone:** TEG film absorbs water by diffusion; the concentration gradient is the driving force.
3. **Equilibrium stage approach:** Each tray approaches a local equilibrium; an *NTU* (number of transfer units) model is used to size the contactor.
4. **Drying gas leaves the top** with a water content dictated by equilibrium with the lean TEG entering the top tray.
5. **Rich glycol leaves the bottom** carrying 1–7 wt% water (typical).
6. **Pressure drop** across the contactor is designed to be **≤ 10 psi** to avoid gas compression costs.

## 2.3 Solid Desiccant Dehydration — Deep Theory

### 2.3.1 Choice of Desiccant

| Desiccant | Pore Size (Å) | Best Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Silica Gel** | 20–30 | Bulk water removal, pre-drying |
| **Activated Alumina** | 20–40 | Moderate dew point depression |
| **Molecular Sieve 3A** | 3 | **Water removal (preferential)** — KTU 2024 default for NGL/LNG |
| **Molecular Sieve 4A** | 4 | Water + CO₂ |
| **Molecular Sieve 5A** | 5 | Normal paraffins, H₂S |
| **Molecular Sieve 13X** | 10 | Bulk water + heavy hydrocarbons |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why 3A Molecular Sieve dominates natural gas drying:** Its 3 Å pore aperture admits only water (kinetic diameter ≈ 2.6 Å) and excludes methane, ethane, and most hydrocarbons — preventing co-adsorption and bed fouling.

### 2.3.2 The Two-Bed (or Three-Bed) Operation

A typical solid desiccant system uses **two parallel beds** so that one is **adsorbing** while the other is **regenerating** (or cooling). The cycle consists of four steps:

1. **Adsorption** (8–24 h): Wet gas flows downward (or upward) through the active bed.
2. **Heating (Regeneration):** Hot gas at **230–315 °C (450–600 °F)** flows through the saturated bed to desorb water.
3. **Cooling:** Cold dry gas cools the regenerated bed back to adsorption temperature.
4. **Re-pressurization & Switching:** Beds are valved to swap roles.

### 2.3.3 Breakthrough Curve — The Heart of the Theory

The **breakthrough curve** plots outlet water concentration versus time on stream.

- **Breakpoint:** The outlet concentration reaches a specified *threshold* (e.g., 1 ppm) — operation must switch beds.
- **Mass Transfer Zone (MTZ):** The active region where adsorption is taking place; the MTZ travels down the bed over time.
- **Equilibrium Loading:** Maximum water that the desiccant can hold at given T and P.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The MTZ width** is the operational heart of the system. A sharp MTZ means a *short unused bed length* at switch time and *high desiccant utilization*. A diffuse MTZ wastes capacity and shortens the cycle.

## 2.4 Metal Oxide Process — Deep Theory

### 2.4.1 Iron Sponge (Hydrated Iron Oxide on Wood Chips)

- The active species is **hydrated ferric oxide: Fe₂O₃·H₂O**.
- Wood chips provide a porous, low-pressure-drop support (~50% voids).
- The reaction is *exothermic* and requires a **pH range of 7–9 with limited moisture** (too dry → reaction stalls; too wet → bed acidifies and "sours").
- A small amount of **alkalinity** (soda ash) is sometimes added to maintain basicity.

### 2.4.2 Zinc Oxide

- Used in *higher-temperature* polishing applications (e.g., reformer guard beds, LNG liquefaction pre-treatment).
- Reacts to < 1 ppb H₂S at temperatures of **200–400 °C**.
- More expensive than iron sponge but achieves deeper H₂S removal.

### 2.4.3 Reaction Stoichiometry and Sulfur Capacity

The **sulfur capacity** of a metal oxide bed is defined as the pounds of sulfur that one pound of metal oxide can chemically bind.

For Fe₂O₃:
- Molecular weight of Fe₂O₃ = 159.69 g/mol
- Mass of S in 1 mole of Fe₂O₃ = 3 × 32.06 = 96.18 g
- **Theoretical capacity = 96.18 / 159.69 = 0.602 lb S / lb Fe₂O₃**
- Practical industrial capacity = **0.30 – 0.55 lb S / lb Fe₂O₃** (due to pore-blocking, channeling, and bed moisture issues)

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formula sheet contains every high-yield equation for KTU 2024 Module 3 — Dehydration & Metal Oxide.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meanings | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | TEG circulation rate | $G = \dfrac{W_{H_2O} \times L}{C_{rich} - C_{lean}}$ | $G$ = glycol rate; $W_{H_2O}$ = water to remove; $L$ = lean TEG purity inverse; $C_{rich}$, $C_{lean}$ = water in rich/lean glycol | gal TEG / lb H₂O |
| 2 | Water removed from gas | $W_{H_2O} = \dfrac{(W_{in} - W_{out}) \times Q}{10^6}$ | $W_{in}, W_{out}$ = inlet/outlet water (lb/MMSCF); $Q$ = gas flow | lb/day |
| 3 | Reboiler heat duty | $Q_{reb} = \dot{m}_{glycol} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T + \dot{m}_{H_2O} \cdot h_{fg}$ | sensible + latent heat | Btu/hr |
| 4 | Contactor diameter (Souders–Brown) | $D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot Q_g}{\pi \cdot u_t \cdot \rho_g}}$ | $Q_g$ = gas volumetric flow; $u_t$ = Souders–Brown velocity | ft |
| 5 | Allowable gas velocity (Souders–Brown) | $u_t = C_{SB} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{\rho_L - \rho_G}{\rho_G}}$ | $C_{SB}$ = 0.10–0.35 ft/s (typo check); $\rho_L, \rho_G$ densities | ft/s |
| 6 | Lean glycol purity (without stripping gas) | $X_{L} \approx 0.985$ to $0.995$ | mass fraction | — |
| 7 | Lean glycol purity (with stripping gas) | $X_{L} \approx 0.995$ to $0.999$ | mass fraction | — |
| 8 | Desiccant bed mass transfer zone (MTZ) | $L_{MTZ} = \dfrac{z \cdot (t^* - t_b)}{t^*}$ | $z$ = bed length; $t^*$ = stoich time; $t_b$ = break time | ft |
| 9 | Solid desiccant water capacity | $X^* = \dfrac{\text{water adsorbed at equilibrium}}{\text{mass of dry desiccant}}$ | — | lb H₂O / lb desiccant |
| 10 | Regeneration gas heat duty | $Q_{reg} = \dot{m}_{gas} \cdot c_{pg} \cdot (T_{hot} - T_{cold})$ | — | Btu/hr |
| 11 | Iron sponge sulfur capacity (theoretical) | $S_{cap} = \dfrac{3 \cdot 32.06}{159.69} = 0.602$ | — | lb S / lb Fe₂O₃ |
| 12 | Iron sponge bed life | $t_{life} = \dfrac{m_{Fe_2O_3} \cdot S_{cap} \cdot \eta}{Q_{H_2S} \cdot M_{H_2S}}$ | $\eta$ = efficiency factor (0.5–0.9) | days |
| 13 | H₂S in gas (mass basis) | $C_{H_2S} = \dfrac{y_{H_2S} \cdot P \cdot M_{H_2S}}{z \cdot R \cdot T}$ | $y$ = mole fraction; $z$ = compressibility | lb/ft³ |
| 14 | Pipeline water spec | $W_{spec} \leq 7$ | — | lb/MMSCF |
| 15 | Lean TEG-to-gas ratio (typical) | $\text{LGR} = 2$ to $3$ | — | gal TEG / lb H₂O |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:**
> - Formula (1) — Used by every process engineer to size the TEG circulation pump and contactor hydraulics.
> - Formula (8) — Governs the design of LNG pre-treatment trains (worth > \$100 million per train).
> - Formula (12) — Used to schedule iron-sponge changeouts in remote wellhead operations.

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: TEG Circulation Rate from a Mass Balance

A gas stream of 10 MMSCF/day enters a contactor at 1000 psia, 100 °F, with water content of 50 lb/MMSCF. The outlet must reach the pipeline spec of 7 lb/MMSCF. The lean TEG contains 0.5 wt% water, the rich TEG leaves with 5.0 wt% water. **Compute the TEG circulation rate in gal/day and the LGR.**

### Step 1: Compute water to be removed per day

$$W_{H_2O} = \dfrac{(W_{in} - W_{out}) \times Q}{10^6}$$

$$W_{H_2O} = \dfrac{(50 - 7) \times 10{,}000{,}000}{10^6} = 43 \times 10 = 430 \ \text{lb/day}$$

### Step 2: Define the mass balance on water in glycol

Let $L$ = lean TEG flow (lb/day), $G$ = total TEG flow = $L$ (steady state, what goes in comes out as rich). Let $C_L$ = water in lean TEG = 0.005, $C_R$ = water in rich TEG = 0.05.

Water picked up by glycol = water leaving the gas = 430 lb/day:

$$L \cdot (C_R - C_L) = W_{H_2O}$$

$$L = \dfrac{430}{0.05 - 0.005} = \dfrac{430}{0.045} = 9555.56 \ \text{lb/day}$$

### Step 3: Convert lb/day to gal/day

TEG density at 100 °F ≈ **9.35 lb/gal** (per TEG data sheets).

$$G = \dfrac{9555.56}{9.35} = 1022.0 \ \text{gal/day}$$

### Step 4: Compute the LGR (lean glycol-to-gas ratio in gal TEG per lb H₂O)

$$\text{LGR} = \dfrac{G}{W_{H_2O}} = \dfrac{1022.0}{430} = 2.38 \ \text{gal TEG / lb H}_2\text{O}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result lies within the KTU 2024 typical range of 2–3 gal TEG per lb H₂O removed.** This is a standard design check — students lose marks if their answer falls outside.

## 3.2 Derivation: Reboiler Heat Duty

A TEG regenerator processes 1022 gal/day of rich glycol. Glycol enters the regenerator at ~120 °F (after heat exchange) and must be heated to 400 °F in the reboiler. Water vaporized in the reboiler = 430 lb/day.

**Step 1: Glycol mass flow**

$$\dot{m}_{glycol} = 9555.56 \ \text{lb/day} = \dfrac{9555.56}{24} = 398.1 \ \text{lb/hr}$$

**Step 2: Sensible heat to raise TEG**

$c_p$ of TEG ≈ 0.62 Btu/(lb·°F).

$$Q_{sens} = 398.1 \times 0.62 \times (400 - 120)$$

$$Q_{sens} = 398.1 \times 0.62 \times 280$$

$$Q_{sens} = 69{,}094 \ \text{Btu/hr}$$

**Step 3: Latent heat to vaporize water**

$$h_{fg} \text{ at 400 °F} \approx 1100 \ \text{Btu/lb (approx., elevated pressure)}$$

$$\dot{m}_{water} = \dfrac{430}{24} = 17.92 \ \text{lb/hr}$$

$$Q_{lat} = 17.92 \times 1100 = 19{,}712 \ \text{Btu/hr}$$

**Step 4: Total reboiler heat duty**

$$Q_{reb} = 69{,}094 + 19{,}712 = 88{,}806 \ \text{Btu/hr} \approx 0.089 \ \text{MMBtu/hr}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering check:** For a 10 MMSCF/day TEG unit, the fire-tube burner rating of **~100,000 Btu/hr (0.1 MMBtu/hr)** is standard — confirmed.

## 3.3 Derivation: Solid Desiccant Bed Sizing — Length of Unused Bed (LUB)

Consider a 2-bed molecular sieve (Type 3A) dehydration unit. The adsorption cycle is 8 h. The mass transfer zone (MTZ) length is 2.0 ft and the total bed length is 10.0 ft. The breakthrough occurs at $t_b = 6$ h, and stoichiometric time $t^* = 10$ h.

**Compute the length of unused bed (LUB) and the fractional bed utilization.**

### Step 1: Apply the MTZ correlation

$$LUB = L_{MTZ} \cdot \dfrac{t^* - t_b}{t^*}$$

$$LUB = 2.0 \times \dfrac{10 - 6}{10} = 2.0 \times 0.4 = 0.8 \ \text{ft}$$

### Step 2: Compute the *used* length of bed

$$L_{used} = z - LUB = 10.0 - 0.8 = 9.2 \ \text{ft}$$

### Step 3: Compute fractional bed utilization

$$\eta_{bed} = \dfrac{L_{used}}{z} = \dfrac{9.2}{10.0} = 0.92 = 92\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Insight:** A bed utilization > 90% is excellent. A utilization < 70% indicates poor desiccant selection or channeling — students should comment on this in design problems.

## 3.4 Derivation: Iron Sponge Bed Life

A wellhead produces 2 MMSCF/day of gas containing 50 ppm (vol) H₂S. The iron sponge unit contains 2000 lb of Fe₂O₃ on wood chips. The bed efficiency factor is 0.7. **Estimate the bed life in days.**

### Step 1: Convert H₂S concentration to mass per day

At standard conditions, 1 lb-mol = 379.5 ft³.

$$\dot{n}_{H_2S} = \dfrac{y_{H_2S} \cdot Q}{V_m} = \dfrac{50 \times 10^{-6} \times 2 \times 10^6}{379.5}$$

$$\dot{n}_{H_2S} = \dfrac{100}{379.5} = 0.2635 \ \text{lb-mol/day}$$

### Step 2: Convert to mass flow

$$M_{H_2S} = 34.08 \ \text{lb/lb-mol}$$

$$\dot{m}_{H_2S} = 0.2635 \times 34.08 = 8.98 \ \text{lb H}_2\text{S / day}$$

### Step 3: Apply the bed-life equation

$$t_{life} = \dfrac{m_{Fe_2O_3} \cdot S_{cap} \cdot \eta}{\dot{m}_{H_2S}}$$

$$t_{life} = \dfrac{2000 \times 0.602 \times 0.7}{8.98}$$

$$t_{life} = \dfrac{842.8}{8.98} = 93.9 \ \text{days} \approx 3.1 \ \text{months}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result is operationally realistic.** A 2000-lb iron sponge bed in a 2 MMSCF/day, 50-ppm H₂S service will need replacement roughly every 3 months — a textbook case used widely in KTU reference problems.

## 3.5 Comparison Matrix — Glycol vs. Solid Desiccant vs. Metal Oxide

| Parameter | Glycol (TEG) | Solid Desiccant | Metal Oxide |
|---|---|---|---|
| Target impurity | Water | Water | H₂S, RSH |
| Outlet water/H₂S | 7 lb/MMSCF | < 0.1 ppm | < 0.25 grain/100 scf |
| Mechanism | Absorption | Adsorption | Chemisorption (irreversible) |
| Regeneration | Yes (thermal) | Yes (thermal swing) | **No — bed is spent** |
| Capital cost | Moderate | High | Low |
| Operating cost | Moderate (heat) | High (heat for regen) | High (bed replacement) |
| Best for | Pipeline spec | Cryogenic / LNG | Small / remote / low H₂S |
| Typical pressure | 250–1200 psig | 250–1500 psig | < 300 psig |
| Major equipment | Contactor + regenerator | 2 adsorbers + heater | Single vessel, internals |
| Solid waste | Glycol disposal | Used desiccant | **Iron sulfide** (pyrophoric) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solid sulfide waste from iron sponge is pyrophoric** (can spontaneously ignite in air). It must be disposed of as hazardous waste under environmental regulations — a frequent viva question.

---
