# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define natural gas dehydration. Why is it essential before pipeline transportation? Mention the standard pipeline water-content specification. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Natural gas dehydration is the removal of water vapor from raw natural gas to meet pipeline quality. It is essential because:
- Water + hydrocarbons form **gas hydrates** that plug pipelines at low temperature / high pressure.
- Free water + H₂S/CO₂ → **corrosion** of steel pipelines and equipment.
- Water dilutes the heating value of the gas.

The standard pipeline water-content specification is **≤ 7 lb water per MMSCF** (≈ 0.112 mg/Nm³), set to prevent hydrate formation under typical operating conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark split (KTU valuation):** Definition (1) + Hydrate/Corrosion (1) + Specification (1).

### Question 2: List three advantages of Triethylene Glycol (TEG) over other glycols (EG, DEG) for gas dehydration. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

1. **High boiling point (288 °C)** → low vaporization losses in the contactor and easy regeneration at atmospheric pressure.
2. **Low vapor pressure** → minimal TEG entrainment into the dry gas stream.
3. **High affinity for water + low viscosity** → efficient mass transfer and easy pumping; a lean glycol purity of 98.5–99.9 wt% is achievable.
4. **Low cost, easy availability, and thermal stability** up to 204 °C reboiler temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark split (KTU valuation):** One mark per distinct advantage — students frequently write "high boiling point" twice and lose the third mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): TEG Glycol Dehydration — Design and Operating Parameters **[14 Marks]**

`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q1A]`

**(a)** With a neat process flow diagram, explain the working of a **TEG glycol dehydration unit**. Label all major equipment and identify the temperature, pressure, and composition conditions at each stream. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution (a):**

A TEG dehydration unit performs continuous water removal from natural gas using a hygroscopic liquid solvent. The unit consists of the following equipment in series:

1. **Inlet Separator (Slug Catcher):** Wet gas enters at full wellhead pressure (250–1200 psig) and ambient-to-100 °F; free water and condensate are removed.

2. **Glycol Contactor (Absorber):** A vertical trayed or packed column (4–8 valve trays). Wet gas enters at the bottom, **lean TEG (98.5–99.9 wt%)** enters at the top at 100–110 °F. The contactor operates at the gas line pressure. The dry gas leaves the top, and **rich TEG (95–99 wt%)** leaves the bottom.

3. **Rich-Glycol Flash Drum:** Operates at 50–75 psig. Dissolved/entrained hydrocarbons flash out and are sent to the fuel system.

4. **Glycol-Glycol Heat Exchanger:** Rich glycol is preheated from 100 °F to ~250 °F using hot lean glycol returning from the regenerator.

5. **Regenerator (Stripping Column):** Packed column. Rich glycol descends; a stripping gas (dry natural gas) rises countercurrently. Operates at near-atmospheric pressure and 400 °F at the reboiler.

6. **Reboiler (Fire-Tube):** Provides heat to vaporize water from the glycol. Glycol exits at 400 °F, water vapor exits at 212 °F+ to atmosphere.

7. **Surge Tank and Lean Pump:** Stores and pumps lean TEG back to the contactor at 800–1200 psig.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark split:** Identification of 4+ equipment (3) + Temperature/Pressure conditions (2) + Mass-transfer explanation (2).

**(b)** A natural gas stream of 20 MMSCF/day at 900 psia, 110 °F, contains 60 lb water/MMSCF. The gas is dried to 5 lb water/MMSCF. Lean TEG contains 0.4 wt% water; rich TEG contains 4.5 wt% water. TEG density = 9.35 lb/gal. **Compute:** (i) the water removed per day, (ii) the TEG circulation rate in gal/day, and (iii) the lean-glycol-to-gas ratio. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) Water removed per day:**

$$W_{H_2O} = \dfrac{(60 - 5) \times 20 \times 10^6}{10^6} = 55 \times 20 = 1100 \ \text{lb/day}$$

**(ii) TEG circulation rate (mass basis):**

$$L = \dfrac{W_{H_2O}}{C_R - C_L} = \dfrac{1100}{0.045 - 0.004} = \dfrac{1100}{0.041} = 26{,}829.3 \ \text{lb/day}$$

Convert to volume:

$$G = \dfrac{26{,}829.3}{9.35} = 2869.5 \ \text{gal/day}$$

**(iii) Lean-glycol-to-gas ratio:**

$$\text{LGR} = \dfrac{G}{W_{H_2O}} = \dfrac{2869.5}{1100} = 2.61 \ \text{gal TEG / lb H}_2\text{O}$$

**Check:** LGR ∈ [2, 3] ✓ — design is acceptable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark split:** (i) 2 marks, (ii) 3 marks, (iii) 2 marks. Partial credit is awarded for correct setup even if arithmetic slips.

---

### Question B (Choice 2): Solid Desiccant and Metal Oxide Processes **[14 Marks]**

`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q1B]`

**(a)** Explain the **solid desiccant dehydration process** for natural gas with a flow diagram. Compare the use of **molecular sieve 3A** with **silica gel** as desiccants. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution (a):**

**Process Description:** A solid desiccant dehydration unit uses two (or three) fixed beds of porous adsorbent. The wet gas flows through the active bed at line pressure; water molecules diffuse into the pores and are held by Van der Waals forces. When the bed approaches breakthrough, flow is switched to a freshly regenerated bed. The saturated bed is then heated using a slipstream of hot dry gas (230–315 °C) to desorb the water, then cooled and held in standby.

**Flow Sequence (Cycle):**
- Adsorption (8–24 h) → Heating (2–4 h) → Cooling (1–2 h) → Re-pressurization & Switch.

**Comparison:**

| Property | Molecular Sieve 3A | Silica Gel |
|---|---|---|
| Pore aperture | **3 Å** | 20–30 Å |
| Selectivity | Water only (kinetic diameter 2.6 Å) | Water + heavier hydrocarbons |
| Equilibrium capacity | High at low RH | High at high RH |
| Outlet water | < 0.1 ppm | ~10–50 ppm |
| Best application | LNG / NGL / cryogenic | Pre-drying, moderate dew point |
| Cost | Higher | Lower |

**Conclusion:** For LNG-grade dehydration, **3A molecular sieve is preferred** due to its ability to achieve < 1 ppm water and exclude hydrocarbon co-adsorption.

**(b)** An iron sponge unit is installed at a wellhead to remove H₂S. The bed contains **3000 lb of Fe₂O₃**. Gas flow rate is 5 MMSCF/day with **80 ppm (vol) H₂S**. Assuming a bed efficiency of 0.75, **estimate the operating life of the bed in days**. **[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — H₂S mass flow:**

$$\dot{n}_{H_2S} = \dfrac{y \cdot Q}{V_m} = \dfrac{80 \times 10^{-6} \times 5 \times 10^6}{379.5} = \dfrac{400}{379.5} = 1.054 \ \text{lb-mol/day}$$

$$\dot{m}_{H_2S} = 1.054 \times 34.08 = 35.92 \ \text{lb H}_2\text{S / day}$$

**Step 2 — Theoretical sulfur capacity of Fe₂O₃:**

$$S_{cap} = \dfrac{3 \times 32.06}{159.69} = 0.602 \ \text{lb S / lb Fe}_2\text{O}_3$$

**Step 3 — Apply the bed-life equation:**

$$t_{life} = \dfrac{m_{Fe_2O_3} \cdot S_{cap} \cdot \eta}{\dot{m}_{H_2S}}$$

$$t_{life} = \dfrac{3000 \times 0.602 \times 0.75}{35.92} = \dfrac{1354.5}{35.92} = 37.7 \ \text{days}$$

**Result:** The iron sponge bed must be replaced approximately every **38 days** (~ 5 weeks). The plant operator should schedule changeouts every 30 days to provide a safety margin.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark split:** Step 1 (2) + Step 2 (2) + Step 3 + final answer (3).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common reasons KTU students LOSE marks on Dehydration & Metal Oxide questions (2024 Scheme):**
> 1. **Confusing the dehydration processes:** Students often write that *glycol dehydration* removes H₂S — **NO.** Glycol removes water only. H₂S is removed by amines or metal oxides.
> 2. **Forgetting the metal-oxide process is non-regenerable:** Many students wrongly assume that the bed can be steamed-regenerated like an amine. The metal oxide forms an *irreversible* sulfide.
> 3. **Skipping the lean-glycol-purity difference with/without stripping gas:** 98.5% (no stripping) vs. 99.5–99.9% (with stripping). Examiners test this.
> 4. **Ignoring pyrophoricity of spent iron sponge:** The iron-sulfide waste *spontaneously ignites* in air — a critical safety and disposal step.
> 5. **Wrong sign or units in the LGR calculation:** LGR = 2–3 gal TEG per lb H₂O. Students sometimes report gal TEG per MSCF gas and lose all 7 marks.
> 6. **Using the wrong molecular weight for Fe₂O₃ or H₂S** in iron-sponge problems — make sure to use **159.69** and **34.08** g/mol, respectively.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Dehydration & Metal Oxide**

- [x] **Dehydration purpose:** Prevent hydrate, prevent corrosion, achieve pipeline spec ≤ 7 lb H₂O / MMSCF.
- [x] **Glycol dehydration — solvent:** TEG (boiling point 288 °C); circulates at 2–3 gal/lb H₂O.
- [x] **TEG contactor:** 4–8 trays; gas up / glycol down; rich glycol leaves with ~3–7 wt% water.
- [x] **Regenerator:** Fire-tube reboiler at 400 °F; near-atmospheric pressure; stripping gas boosts lean purity to 99.5–99.9%.
- [x] **Solid desiccant — preferred for LNG:** 3A molecular sieve; outlet < 0.1 ppm water; two-bed cycle.
- [x] **MTZ theory:** Mass transfer zone travels down the bed; bed utilization typically 85–95%.
- [x] **Solid desiccant regeneration:** Hot gas at 230–315 °C; cycle time 8–24 h adsorption.
- [x] **Metal oxide process — target:** H₂S, RSH, COS removal; **non-regenerable**; used for small-scale or low ppm.
- [x] **Iron sponge reaction:** $\mathrm{Fe_2O_3 + 3\,H_2S \rightarrow Fe_2S_3 + 3\,H_2O}$.
- [x] **Theoretical sulfur capacity:** 0.602 lb S / lb Fe₂O₃; practical 0.30–0.55.
- [x] **Spent bed safety:** Iron sulfide is **pyrophoric**; dispose as hazardous waste.
- [x] **Selection rule of thumb:** Pipeline spec → TEG; cryogenic → 3A molecular sieve; low H₂S, small plant → iron sponge.
- [x] **Key formulas to memorize:** LGR, reboiler duty, Souders–Brown, MTZ/LUB, iron sponge life.
- [x] **Compare and contrast:** All three processes (Glycol / Solid / Metal Oxide) — *always* state the **mechanism** (absorption vs. adsorption vs. chemisorption).
- [x] **Environment & safety:** All three processes produce waste streams (spent glycol, spent desiccant, iron sulfide) requiring regulated disposal.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Slurry Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT528 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Slurry Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Slurry Process** (also termed the **Slurry-Phase Hydroconversion Process** or **Slurry Bed Hydrocracking Process**) is an advanced catalytic residue-upgrading technology in which a finely divided, micron-sized solid catalyst powder is suspended (slurried) directly into a heavy liquid hydrocarbon feed. The feed-catalyst mixture, together with high-purity hydrogen, is reacted in a continuously stirred tank reactor (CSTR) or bubble-column reactor at severe operating conditions—typically **400 – 450 °C** and **100 – 200 bar (10 – 20 MPa)**—to convert high-molecular-weight, asphaltene-rich residues (vacuum residue, atmospheric residue, bitumen, deasphalted oil) into lighter distillate-range products (naphtha, diesel, VGO).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Slurry Process**
> A non-regenerable, once-through (additive-type) catalyst is used, which leaves the reactor dissolved or finely dispersed in the unconverted bottom product. The bottom product is then routed to a **Solvent De-Asphalting (SDA)** unit to recover the unconverted oil and reject catalyst + asphaltene pitch.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy — The "Black Smoothie" Reactor**
> Imagine blending a handful of fine activated charcoal powder into a thick black syrup (honey + tar). The charcoal does *not dissolve*—it floats uniformly suspended because of continuous stirring. Now blow high-pressure steam through it inside a sealed pressure cooker. The heat and steam gradually break the heavy syrup into thinner, lighter liquid that flows out of the cooker.
> The charcoal (catalyst) helps the breakage happen at a lower temperature, and it eventually exits the cooker along with the leftover sludge.
> This is exactly how a **Slurry Reactor** operates—the heavy feed is the "syrup," the micron catalyst is the "charcoal," hydrogen gas is the "steam," and the sealed vessel is the "pressure cooker."

### 1.3 Key Process Metrics

- **Operating Temperature:** 400 – 450 °C
- **Operating Pressure:** 100 – 200 bar
- **Conversion (per pass):** **> 90 %** of 524 °C+ residue
- **Hydrogen Partial Pressure:** 80 – 150 bar
- **Catalyst Size:** 1 – 100 µm (micron range)
- **Catalyst Type:** Dispersed, once-through (e.g., Mo, Fe, Ni-Mo on carbon or carbon-free)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Operating Philosophy

The slurry process is engineered to overcome the **diffusion limitations** of fixed-bed and ebullated-bed hydrocrackers when processing ultra-heavy, metal-laden (Ni, V), asphaltene-rich feeds. The breakdown of the operational concept:

1. **Catalyst Slurry Preparation:** A finely ground catalyst (e.g., molybdenum disulphide, iron sulphate, or carbon-supported Ni-Mo) is mixed with a carrier oil to form a pumpable slurry of 1–5 wt% concentration.
2. **Feed Pre-mixing:** Heavy feed is pre-mixed with the catalyst slurry and pumped to reactor pressure via high-pressure feed pumps.
3. **Reactor Operation:** Feed, catalyst and hydrogen enter the bottom of the slurry reactor. The reactor operates as a **bubble-column / three-phase fluidized system** with internal gas recycle for isothermal operation.
4. **Once-through Catalyst:** Catalyst exits the reactor with the unconverted residue. There is no separate regenerator; catalyst is consumed (sacrificial/additive mode).
5. **Product Recovery:** Reactor effluent → Hot High-Pressure Separator → Atmospheric Distillation → Vacuum Distillation. Vacuum residue → **Solvent De-Asphalting (SDA)** to recover DAO (Deasphalted Oil) and reject pitch + spent catalyst.
6. **Recycle Streams:** Distillate streams are further hydrotreated in a downstream fixed-bed unit to meet product specifications.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Slurry" and not "Fixed Bed"?**
> Fixed beds plug rapidly with asphaltenes, metals (Ni + V), and coke. By suspending the catalyst as ultra-fine particles in the liquid, the entire feed volume becomes reactive—**no diffusion barrier, no plugging, and uniform temperature profile**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Expression | Description / Units |
|---|----------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | $X = \dfrac{F_{525^+,\;in} - F_{525^+,\;out}}{F_{525^+,\;in}} \times 100$ | Conversion of 525 °C+ residue, % |
| 2 | $\text{WHSV} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{feed}}{m_{catalyst}}$ | Weight Hourly Space Velocity, $\text{hr}^{-1}$ |
| 3 | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | Arrhenius rate constant (kinetic model) |
| 4 | $-\dfrac{dC_A}{dt} = k \cdot C_A^n$ | Rate of disappearance of reactant A (nth order) |
| 5 | $\tau = \dfrac{V_{reactor}}{\dot{V}_{feed}} = \dfrac{C_{A0} - C_A}{-r_A}$ | Space time, hr (for a CSTR) |
| 6 | $X_{CSTR} = \dfrac{k \tau}{1 + k \tau}$ | Conversion in a single CSTR (1st order, n = 1) |
| 7 | $\text{H}_{2,\;cons} = \dfrac{(V_{\text{in}} - V_{\text{out}}) \, \dot{V}_{feed}}{M_{feed}}$ | Hydrogen consumption, $\text{Nm}^{3}/\text{m}^{3}$ feed |
| 8 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{32 \, \mu \, L \, v}{D^2}$ | Pressure drop (laminar flow approximation) |
| 9 | $K_p = \dfrac{C_{525^+,\;eq}}{C_{525^+,\;0}}$ | Equilibrium constant for residue cracking |
| 10 | $T_{adiabatic\;rise} = \dfrac{-\Delta H_R \, X}{C_p \, (1 + \theta)}$ | Adiabatic temperature rise, °C (exothermic HCs) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The slurry process is the only commercially proven technology capable of **> 90 wt% conversion** of petroleum residue into synthetic crude, making it the *workhorse* for upgrading **bitumen, oil sands, vacuum residue, and visbroken tar** in modern refineries pursuing **zero-fuel-oil** operations.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Conversion in a Continuous Slurry Reactor (CSTR Model)

Treating the slurry reactor as an **ideal CSTR** with a first-order hydrocracking kinetics ($n = 1$), let us derive the steady-state conversion expression.

**Step 1 — Define the basis.** Let $F_{A0}$ be the molar feed rate of residue (A) entering the reactor and $F_A$ be the molar rate leaving. The rate of reaction is $r_A$ (mol reacted per unit volume per unit time).

**Step 2 — Component material balance on A around the CSTR:**

$$
\text{In} - \text{Out} + \text{Generation} = \text{Accumulation}
$$

At steady state, accumulation is zero, so:

$$
F_{A0} - F_A + r_A \, V = 0
$$

**Step 3 — Define conversion and rearrange.** For a 1st-order reaction, $r_A = -k \, C_A$ (negative because A is consumed). Substituting and using $C_A = C_{A0}(1 - X)$:

$$
F_{A0} X = k \, C_A \, V
$$

**Step 4 — Introduce space time.** $\tau = V / \dot{V}_0$ and $C_{A0} = F_{A0} / \dot{V}_0$:

$$
F_{A0} X = k \, C_{A0} (1 - X) \, V
$$

**Step 5 — Divide both sides by $F_{A0}$ and substitute $\tau$:**

$$
X = k \, \tau \, (1 - X)
$$

**Step 6 — Solve algebraically for X:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X + k \tau X &= k \tau \\[4pt]
X (1 + k \tau) &= k \tau \\[4pt]
\boxed{X = \dfrac{k \tau}{1 + k \tau}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** As $k \tau \to \infty$, $X \to 1$ (complete conversion). For typical slurry conditions, $k \tau \approx 4$ gives $X \approx 0.80$ (i.e., 80% per-pass conversion).

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Arrhenius Sensitivity of Conversion

To understand how a 5 °C rise in reactor temperature boosts conversion, we differentiate the CSTR conversion expression with respect to $T$.

**Step 1 — Differentiate $\ln X$ via chain rule:**

$$
\dfrac{d \ln X}{dT} = \dfrac{1}{X} \dfrac{dX}{dT}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the chain rule on $X = k\tau / (1 + k\tau)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{dX}{dT} &= \dfrac{(1 + k\tau)\left(\dfrac{dk}{dT}\tau\right) - (k\tau)\left(\dfrac{dk}{dT}\tau\right)}{(1 + k\tau)^2} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{\tau \, \dfrac{dk}{dT}}{(1 + k\tau)^2}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Arrhenius derivative:** $dk/dT = k (E_a / R T^2)$:

$$
\dfrac{dX}{dT} = \dfrac{k\tau}{(1 + k\tau)^2} \cdot \dfrac{E_a}{R T^2}
$$

**Step 4 — For large $k\tau$ (high conversion regime), the term $k\tau / (1 + k\tau)^2 \approx 1 / k\tau$:**

$$
\dfrac{dX}{dT} \;\approx\; \dfrac{E_a}{R T^2 (1 + k\tau)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** For a typical heavy-feed hydrocracking reaction with $E_a = 140 \; \text{kJ/mol}$, a 5 °C rise in reactor temperature at 700 K increases conversion by approximately **2 – 3 percentage points**, which justifies the use of **precise multi-zone temperature control** in commercial slurry reactors.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A slurry reactor processes 100 m³/hr of vacuum residue (density 1020 kg/m³, MW ≈ 850 g/mol). Reactor volume is 80 m³. The first-order rate constant at 425 °C is $k = 0.45 \; \text{hr}^{-1}$. Compute the per-pass conversion of 525 °C+ residue.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau &= \dfrac{V}{\dot{V}} = \dfrac{80}{100} = 0.80 \; \text{hr} \\[6pt]
X &= \dfrac{k \tau}{1 + k \tau} = \dfrac{0.45 \times 0.80}{1 + 0.45 \times 0.80} \\[6pt]
  &= \dfrac{0.360}{1.360} = 0.2647
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** $X \approx 26.5\%$ per pass. To achieve the commercial target of 90%+, either reactor volume is increased, a recycle loop is added, or operating temperature is raised to 440 °C (recomputing with $k = 0.95 \; \text{hr}^{-1}$ gives $X \approx 43\%$).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Block Diagram — Slurry Hydrocracking Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[VR Feed Tank] --> B[Feed Pump P-101]
    B --> C[Catalyst Slurry Mixer M-101]
    C --> D[Feed Preheater E-101]
    H2[H2 Compressor K-101] --> D
    D --> E[Slurry Reactor R-101]
    E --> F[Hot HP Separator V-101]
    F --> G[Hot LP Separator V-102]
    G --> H[Atmospheric Distillation Column T-101]
    H --> I[Vacuum Distillation Column T-102]
    I --> J[Solvent De-Asphalting Unit SDA-201]
    J --> K[DAO Product to Hydrotreater]
    J --> L[Pitch Plus Spent Catalyst to Pelletizer]
    F --> M[Recycle Gas Compressor K-102]
    M --> E
    H --> N[Naphtha and Diesel Product]
    I --> O[VGO Product]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Flow:** Vacuum residue (VR) is mixed with a fine catalyst slurry, preheated with hydrogen, and charged to the slurry reactor R-101. The reactor effluent flashes in HP and LP separators. Liquid products go to distillation; recycle gas (rich in H₂) is recompressed and returned. The vacuum residue bottoms is sent to SDA to recover unconverted oil as DAO and reject catalyst-laden pitch.

### 4.2 Catalyst Lifecycle Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fresh Catalyst Powder MoS2] --> B[Catalyst Slurry Tank]
    B --> C[Slurry Reactor R-101]
    C --> D[Vacuum Residue Bottoms]
    D --> E[SDA Unit]
    E --> F[Pitch Plus Spent Catalyst]
    F --> G[Once-Through Disposal / Pelletizer]
    E --> H[DAO Recycle to Reactor]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Once-through Catalyst Justification:** Unlike FCC or ebullated-bed hydrocrackers, the slurry process **does not regenerate** the catalyst. The micron-sized catalyst is dispersed so finely that even if it cokes or fouls, it does not plug the reactor—making regeneration economically unnecessary. The spent catalyst exits with the pitch, which is then pelletized for use in cement kilns or as a solid-fuel supplement.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`
**Define the Slurry Process. What type of catalyst is used and why is it termed "once-through"?**

**Model Answer:**
The Slurry Process is a hydroconversion technology in which a finely divided solid catalyst (typically MoS₂, FeSO₄, or carbon-supported Ni-Mo in the size range 1–100 µm) is suspended in a heavy hydrocarbon feed, and the mixture is reacted with high-purity hydrogen at 400–450 °C and 100–200 bar in a bubble-column or CSTR-type reactor.
The catalyst is termed **"once-through"** because it is not regenerated or recycled. It exits the reactor with the unconverted vacuum bottoms and is subsequently rejected with the SDA pitch. Because the catalyst particles are sub-micron to micron-sized, they are not recovered economically, and the small mass consumed does not justify a separate regeneration circuit. (3 marks)

#### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`
**List three advantages of the Slurry Process over conventional fixed-bed hydrocracking for residue upgrading.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **No Catalyst Plugging:** Because the catalyst is suspended as fine particles in the feed, diffusion limitations and pressure-drop build-up common in fixed beds (from metal deposition and asphaltene coking) are eliminated.
2. **Uniform Temperature Profile:** The liquid-phase slurry with internal gas recycle provides near-isothermal operation, avoiding hot spots and run-away reactions.
3. **Ultra-High Conversion:** Per-pass conversions of > 90 wt% are achievable, far exceeding the 50–70% per-pass limit of fixed-bed and ebullated-bed units. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **operating principle, reactor configuration, and catalyst system** of the Slurry Process for heavy residue upgrading. Compare it with the ebullated-bed process in terms of conversion, catalyst mode, and feedstock flexibility. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer — Part (a):**

| Parameter | Slurry Process | Ebullated-Bed Process |
|-----------|----------------|-----------------------|
| Catalyst form | Fine powder (1–100 µm) slurry in feed | 0.8 – 1.5 mm extrudates, fluidized by gas/liquid |
| Catalyst mode | Once-through, not regenerated | On-line replaceable; regenerated in swing reactor |
| Per-pass conversion | 80 – 90 %+ | 50 – 70 % |
| Operating T / P | 400 – 450 °C / 100 – 200 bar | 380 – 425 °C / 80 – 150 bar |
| Feedstock flexibility | Bitumen, VR, oil sands, deasphalted oil | VR, AR (limited metal feed) |
| Plugging tolerance | Excellent (no fixed packing) | Moderate |

**Operating Principle (3 marks):** Heavy residue is mixed with a 1–5 wt% slurry of micronized catalyst (typically molybdenum disulphide from a precursor like phosphomolybdic acid) and charged with hydrogen into a vertical, three-phase slurry reactor. The reactor operates as a bubble column with internal recycle gas ensuring complete back-mixing and uniform temperature. Cracking and hydrogenation occur simultaneously on the dispersed catalyst surface, converting 525 °C+ residue into distillates.

**Reactor Configuration (2 marks):** A vertical, refractory-lined pressure vessel with multiple internal heat exchangers, feed distribution rings, and gas/liquid outlet nozzles. No catalyst screens, baskets, orifices, or support grids are required.

**Catalyst System (2 marks):** Typical catalysts include MoS₂, FeSO₄·H₂O, or carbon-supported Ni-Mo. They are prepared as oil-soluble precursors that decompose in situ into active nano-dispersed sulphides.

**(b)** A slurry reactor processes 250 m³/hr of vacuum residue. The volume of the reactor is 200 m³. The first-order rate constant at the operating temperature is 0.65 hr⁻¹. Assuming ideal CSTR behaviour, determine:
  (i) the space time,
  (ii) the per-pass conversion of 525 °C+ residue,
  (iii) the catalyst mass required if WHSV is 0.30 hr⁻¹,
  (iv) the adiabatic temperature rise if $-\Delta H_R = 220 \; \text{kJ/kg}$, $C_p = 2.5 \; \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$, and conversion achieved is 0.40. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer — Part (b):**

**(i) Space time:**

$$
\tau = \dfrac{V}{\dot{V}} = \dfrac{200}{250} = 0.80 \; \text{hr}
$$

**(ii) Per-pass conversion (CSTR, 1st order):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
X &= \dfrac{k \tau}{1 + k \tau} = \dfrac{0.65 \times 0.80}{1 + 0.65 \times 0.80} \\[6pt]
  &= \dfrac{0.520}{1.520} = 0.3421
\end{aligned}
$$

$X \approx 34.2\%$

**(iii) Catalyst mass required (using WHSV = 0.30 hr⁻¹):**

Feed mass flow rate: $\dot{m}_{feed} = 250 \times 1020 = 255{,}000 \; \text{kg/hr}$.

$$
m_{catalyst} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{feed}}{\text{WHSV}} = \dfrac{255{,}000}{0.30} = 850{,}000 \; \text{kg} = 850 \; \text{tonnes}
$$

**(iv) Adiabatic temperature rise:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta T_{ad} &= \dfrac{-\Delta H_R \cdot X}{C_p} \\[6pt]
              &= \dfrac{220 \times 0.40}{2.5} = \dfrac{88}{2.5} = 35.2 \; \text{°C}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall / Valuation Warning**
> - **Unit Mismatch:** Many students compute $\Delta T$ using $C_p$ in $\text{J/(kg·K)}$ but $\Delta H_R$ in $\text{kJ/kg}$, leading to answers off by a factor of 1000. **Always** convert both to the same energy unit before dividing.
> - **CSTR vs PFR Confusion:** The slurry reactor is **assumed as CSTR** in the question. Do not apply the PFR equation $X = 1 - e^{-k\tau}$—it will cost you 2 marks.
> - **WHSV Units:** WHSV is in $\text{hr}^{-1}$, so the answer is in **mass of catalyst, not mass of slurry**. Do not forget to divide feed mass rate (kg/hr) by WHSV.
> - **Forgetting WHSV Definition:** Examiners award zero credit if WHSV is interpreted as inverse of space time in the reactor (a common but incorrect assumption).

---

#### Question B (14 Marks, Internal Choice Alternative) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP]`

**(a)** Discuss the **role of hydrogen partial pressure** in the Slurry Process. Explain how partial pressure affects (i) hydrodesulphurization, (ii) hydrodemetallization, (iii) asphaltene conversion, and (iv) coke suppression. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer — Part (a):**

**(i) Hydrodesulphurization (HDS):** High hydrogen partial pressure thermodynamically favours desulphurization equilibrium. A pressure rise from 100 bar to 150 bar improves HDS conversion by ~15–20 absolute % at constant temperature.

**(ii) Hydrodemetallization (HDM):** Metals (Ni, V) deposited on catalyst are in equilibrium with metal-organic porphyrins in feed. Higher $P_{H_2}$ accelerates hydrogenation of these porphyrins, releasing metals as sulphides. The micron-sized slurry catalyst tolerates high metal loadings (up to 700 ppm V + Ni) without poisoning.

**(iii) Asphaltene Conversion:** Asphaltenes are large polyaromatic micelles. Hydrogen partial pressure disrupts their $\pi$–$\pi$ stacking, breaking them into smaller aromatic fragments. At $P_{H_2} > 130$ bar, asphaltene conversion can exceed 90%.

**(iv) Coke Suppression:** Coke forms via dehydrogenative polymerization of heavy aromatics. High $P_{H_2}$ caps free radicals, suppressing polycondensation. Empirical rule: doubling $P_{H_2}$ reduces coke yield by ~40%.

**(b)** A slurry hydrocracker is operated at 425 °C and 150 bar. The first-order rate constant is $k = 0.85 \; \text{hr}^{-1}$ and the activation energy is $E_a = 145 \; \text{kJ/mol}$. The reactor volume is 150 m³ and feed flow rate is 180 m³/hr.
  (i) Compute the per-pass conversion.
  (ii) If the operating temperature is raised to 440 °C, compute the new rate constant using the Arrhenius equation ($R = 8.314 \; \text{J/(mol·K)}$).
  (iii) Compute the new conversion at 440 °C. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer — Part (b):**

**(i) Per-pass conversion at 425 °C:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\tau &= \dfrac{150}{180} = 0.833 \; \text{hr} \\[6pt]
X_1 &= \dfrac{k \tau}{1 + k \tau} = \dfrac{0.85 \times 0.833}{1 + 0.85 \times 0.833} \\[6pt]
    &= \dfrac{0.708}{1.708} = 0.4146
\end{aligned}
$$

$X_1 \approx 41.5\%$

**(ii) New rate constant at 440 °C (T₂ = 713 K, T₁ = 698 K):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln \dfrac{k_2}{k_1} &= -\dfrac{E_a}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right) \\[6pt]
                     &= -\dfrac{145{,}000}{8.314}\left(\dfrac{1}{713} - \dfrac{1}{698}\right) \\[6pt]
                     &= -17{,}439 \times (-3.014 \times 10^{-5}) \\[6pt]
                     &= 0.5256 \\[6pt]
\dfrac{k_2}{k_1} &= e^{0.5256} = 1.6915 \\[6pt]
k_2 &= 0.85 \times 1.6915 = 1.438 \; \text{hr}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iii) New conversion at 440 °C:**

$$
X_2 = \dfrac{1.438 \times 0.833}{1 + 1.438 \times 0.833} = \dfrac{1.198}{2.198} = 0.5450
$$

$X_2 \approx 54.5\%$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall in Arrhenius Problems**
> - **Sign Error:** The bracketed term $\left(1/T_2 - 1/T_1\right)$ is **negative** when $T_2 > T_1$. The two negative signs multiply to give a **positive** $\ln(k_2/k_1)$, hence $k_2 > k_1$. Getting this sign wrong produces $k_2 < k_1$, which is physically wrong.
> - **Temperature in Kelvin:** Always use **absolute temperature (K)** in the Arrhenius equation. Using °C gives meaningless results.
> - **Activation Energy Units:** Use **J/mol** consistently with the gas constant $R = 8.314 \; \text{J/(mol·K)}$. If $E_a$ is given in kJ/mol, multiply by 1000 first.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for the Slurry Process**

- **Definition:** A hydroconversion process where a **micron-sized catalyst (1–100 µm)** is suspended in heavy feed and reacted with hydrogen in a **bubble-column/CSTR** reactor.
- **Catalyst Type:** **Once-through, non-regenerated**; typical examples: MoS₂, FeSO₄, carbon-supported Ni-Mo.
- **Operating Conditions:** **T = 400–450 °C, P = 100–200 bar, P_H₂ = 80–150 bar**.
- **Per-pass Conversion:** **> 90%** of 525 °C+ residue (highest among all residue-upgrading technologies).
- **Reactor Type:** Ideal **CSTR (well-mixed)**, hence the CSTR conversion formula $X = k\tau / (1 + k\tau)$ is applied.
- **Key Formulae:** WHSV, Arrhenius, CSTR conversion, adiabatic temperature rise, hydrogen consumption.
- **Feedstock Flexibility:** Bitumen, oil sands, vacuum residue, atmospheric residue, deasphalted oil — handles **high metal (Ni+V) and asphaltene** feeds.
- **Product Recovery:** Reactor effluent → HP/LP separation → atmospheric + vacuum distillation → **SDA unit** to recover DAO and reject catalyst-laden pitch.
- **Once-Through Catalyst Justification:** Catalyst particles are too small to be economically recovered; hence they exit with pitch and are sent to cement kilns / solid-fuel blending.
- **Comparison Anchor:** Slurry > Ebullated-bed > Fixed-bed in terms of conversion and feedstock tolerance, but slurry is more expensive in hydrogen consumption.
- **Key Industrial Examples:** Veba Combi-Cracking (VCC), HDHPLUS, Eni Slurry Technology (EST), Microcat-RC.
- **Valuation Tip:** Always state units explicitly; show step-by-step substitution in numerical problems; for CSTR modelling, use $X = k\tau/(1 + k\tau)$ and **not** the PFR formula.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Amine Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT528 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Amine Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Amine Process** (also called **Amine Gas Treating** or **Amine Scrubbing**) is a continuous, regenerative chemical absorption process widely employed in the natural gas processing and petroleum refining industries for the bulk removal of acid gases — primarily **hydrogen sulfide (H₂S)** and **carbon dioxide (CO₂)** — from natural gas, synthesis gas, refinery off-gases, and liquefied petroleum gas (LPG) streams.

The process uses dilute aqueous solutions of **alkanolamines** (organic bases that contain both an amine functional group –NH₂, –NHR, –NR₂ and a hydroxyl –OH group on the same molecule) as the chemical solvent. The lean (acid-gas-free) amine solution is contacted counter-currently with the sour (acid-gas-laden) gas in a trayed or packed **absorber column** at near-ambient to moderate pressures (typically **5 bar to 70 bar**) and low-to-moderate temperatures (**35 °C to 50 °C**). The acid gases chemically react with the amine to form heat-labile, water-soluble salts. The rich amine is then sent to a **regenerator (stripper)** operating at low pressure (**1.0 bar to 1.7 bar**) and high temperature (**110 °C to 125 °C**), where the acid gases are desorbed by reboiling, producing lean amine for recycle and an overhead acid-gas stream (typically sent to a **Claus unit** for sulfur recovery).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Note:**
> The amine process is a flagship topic of Module 3 ("Gas Processing & Sweetening"). Examiners consistently test (i) chemistry of amine–acid gas reactions, (ii) the absorber–regenerator flow sheet, (iii) the role of heat of reaction, and (iv) selection criteria between MEA, DEA, and MDEA.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Magnetic Sponge" Mental Model

Imagine a high-pressure natural gas pipeline as a fast-flowing river carrying two unwanted invisible pollutants: a corrosive, foul-smelling gas (H₂S — "the rotten-egg pollutant") and a mildly acidic gas (CO₂ — "the rust-causing pollutant"). You cannot just filter them out with a sieve, because they are gases dissolved in a gas.

**Now think of the amine solution as a smart chemical "sponge" that is selectively magnetic for these two specific pollutants.**

- When the river (sour gas) meets the sponge (lean amine) inside a tall contact tower (absorber) at high pressure, the sponge **chemically grabs and locks onto** the H₂S and CO₂ molecules, releasing clean natural gas (sweet gas) at the top.
- The loaded sponge (rich amine) is then transferred to a "squeezing chamber" (regenerator) where it is gently heated. The heat **releases** the trapped pollutants as a concentrated acid-gas stream, restoring the sponge to its original lean state — ready to be used again.

This is exactly the closed-loop amine process: **absorb at high pressure & low temperature → regenerate at low pressure & high temperature → recycle**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway (KTU-Favourite One-Liner):**
> *"The amine process is a regenerative chemical absorption process that selectively removes H₂S and CO₂ from natural gas by exploiting the temperature and pressure sensitivity of amine–acid gas reactions."*

## 1.3 Industrial Importance & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Typical Industrial Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Pipeline H₂S specification | **< 4 ppmv** (sales gas) |
| Pipeline CO₂ specification | **< 2 mol %** |
| LNG-feed CO₂ specification | **< 50 ppmv** (to avoid freeze-out) |
| Lean amine circulation rate | **0.20 – 0.45 m³ amine / 10³ Sm³ gas** |
| Amine concentration in solution | **15 – 32 wt %** (for MEA), **25 – 50 wt %** (for DEA) |
| Absorber operating pressure | **5 – 70 bar** |
| Absorber operating temperature | **35 – 50 °C** |
| Regenerator overhead temperature | **100 – 110 °C** |
| Regenerator reboiler temperature | **115 – 125 °C** |
| Acid-gas pick-up per mol amine | **0.30 – 0.50 mol H₂S / mol MEA** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Universal Standard Marker:** All sweetening processes are economically benchmarked against the **Claus process** downstream — the amine unit's acid-gas stream is almost always routed to a sulfur-recovery Claus unit, making amine–Claus the most iconic process pairing in gas processing.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Alkanolamines Used in Industry

Alkanolamines are classified by their **degree of substitution at the nitrogen atom**, which directly controls their basicity, loading capacity, regenerability, and selectivity.

| Class | Generic Formula | Example | Substitution | Relative Basicity | Selectivity |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Primary** | R–NH₂ | **MEA** (Monoethanolamine) | One H replaced | Highest | Non-selective (H₂S + CO₂) |
| **Secondary** | R₂–NH | **DEA** (Diethanolamine) | Two H replaced | High | Slight H₂S preference |
| **Tertiary** | R₃–N | **MDEA** (Methyldiethanolamine) | All H replaced | Moderate | **Strongly H₂S-selective** |
| **Sterically hindered** | Bulky R–NH₂ | **AMP** (2-Amino-2-methyl-1-propanol) | Bulky primary | Moderate | H₂S selective over CO₂ |
| **Diamine** | (HOCH₂CH₂)₂NH already counted | **DGA** (Diglycolamine®) | Primary, with –O– linkage | Very high | Non-selective, high loading |
| **Formulated/Mixed** | Blends | **Sulfinol-D**, **aMDEA®** | Physical + chemical | Variable | Tailor-made |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Trick:** When asked "Which amine is most selective towards H₂S over CO₂?" — the answer is **MDEA** (tertiary). When asked "Which amine has the highest heat of reaction?" — the answer is **MEA** (primary, ~1,920 kJ/kg H₂S absorbed).

## 2.2 The Core Chemistry — Reactions with H₂S and CO₂

The reactions below are the heart of the process. The amine acts as a **weak base**, accepting a proton (H⁺) from the weak acid gases to form a heat-labile **alkylammonium salt**.

### 2.2.1 Reactions with H₂S (Hydrogen Sulfide)

For a generic primary/secondary amine **R₂NH**:

$$\text{R}_2\text{NH} + \text{H}_2\text{S} \rightleftharpoons \text{R}_2\text{NH}_2^+ + \text{HS}^-$$

For a tertiary amine **R₃N** (no N–H bond; cannot donate proton, but the nitrogen lone pair still accepts H⁺):

$$\text{R}_3\text{N} + \text{H}_2\text{S} \rightleftharpoons \text{R}_3\text{NH}^+ + \text{HS}^-$$

### 2.2.2 Reactions with CO₂ (Carbon Dioxide)

CO₂ follows **two competing pathways** — an **instantaneous proton-transfer** (fast) and a **carbamate formation** (slower, kinetically controlled):

**Pathway 1 — Bicarbonate formation (dominant for tertiary amines MDEA):**

$$\text{R}_3\text{N} + \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{R}_3\text{NH}^+ + \text{HCO}_3^-$$

**Pathway 2 — Carbamate formation (dominant for primary/secondary amines MEA, DEA):**

$$2\,\text{R}_2\text{NH} + \text{CO}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{R}_2\text{NCOO}^- + \text{R}_2\text{NH}_2^+$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight (Board-Favourite):** The carbamate pathway consumes **2 mol of amine per mol of CO₂**, which halves the theoretical amine loading capacity for CO₂ compared with H₂S. This is the fundamental reason why **MEA and DEA have low net acid-gas loadings (~0.3–0.4 mol/mol)** while **MDEA (no carbamate) achieves higher loadings (~0.7–0.8 mol/mol)** and is therefore more energy-efficient per mole of acid gas.

### 2.2.3 Why Regeneration Works — The Thermodynamic Driver

The forward (absorption) reactions are **exothermic** and **pressure-favored**. Hence, by **Le Chatelier's principle**:

- **Lowering pressure** (regenerator runs at ~1 bar) and
- **Raising temperature** (reboiler at ~120 °C)

drive the equilibrium **backwards**, liberating H₂S and CO₂ and restoring lean amine.

## 2.3 Process Flow Sheet (Block-by-Block)

A standard **single-stage amine unit** has the following main equipment items in series:

1. **Inlet Gas Separator (Slug Catcher / Knock-Out Drum)** — removes free liquids and entrained solids to prevent foaming in the absorber.
2. **Amine Contactor (Absorber)** — a vertical trayed or packed column operating at high pressure; sour gas enters the bottom, lean amine enters the top; sweet gas leaves the top.
3. **Rich Amine Flash Drum** — reduces pressure to release dissolved hydrocarbons (these are routed to fuel gas or recompressed).
4. **Rich/Lean Amine Heat Exchanger (Cross-Exchanger)** — recovers sensible heat from the hot lean amine leaving the regenerator bottom to preheat the rich amine entering the regenerator.
5. **Amine Regenerator (Stripper)** — vertical column with a reboiler at the bottom and a reflux condenser/accumulator at the top; rich amine is stripped of acid gases by counter-current steam.
6. **Reboiler** — generates stripping steam; typically a kettle-type thermosiphon fired by natural gas or hot oil.
7. **Reflux Accumulator & Pump** — separates the overhead acid-gas vapour from condensed water; the water is refluxed back to the regenerator to maintain water balance.
8. **Lean Amine Cooler & Pump** — cools the regenerated lean amine to ~40 °C before reinjection into the absorber top.
9. **Amine Reclaimer** (for MEA units) — side-stream distillation that removes heat-stable salts (HSS) and degradation products, preventing amine loss.
10. **Charcoal Filter / Mechanical Filter** — removes particulates, degradation by-products, and hydrocarbon entrainment.
11. **Acid-Gas Compressor / Claus Feed Knock-Out** — the regenerator overhead stream is sent to sulfur recovery (Claus unit) or incinerated.

## 2.4 Operating Variables and Their Effects (Sensitivity Table)

| Variable | Increase → Effect on Absorption | Increase → Effect on Regeneration |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Absorber Pressure** | ↑ driving force, ↑ absorption | Does not directly affect regen |
| **Absorber Temperature** | ↓ equilibrium loading, ↓ absorption (exothermic rxn) | Does not directly affect regen |
| **Amine Concentration** | ↑ capacity per litre, but ↑ corrosion risk | ↑ energy required in reboiler |
| **Lean Amine Temperature** | ↓ gas treating efficiency above 50 °C | n/a |
| **Amine Circulation Rate** | ↑ low-tower acid gas pickup | ↑ reboiler duty (energy cost) |
| **Reboiler Steam (Duty)** | n/a | ↑ stripping, ↓ residual acid gas in lean amine |
| **Regenerator Pressure** | n/a | ↑ pressure = ↑ residual loading (bad) |
| **Number of Absorber Trays / Packing Height** | ↑ H₂S/CO₂ removal efficiency | n/a |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Heat of Reaction (ΔH_abs) is the silent operator of the entire plant:**
> - MEA–H₂S ≈ **1,915 kJ/kg H₂S absorbed**
> - DEA–H₂S ≈ **1,190 kJ/kg H₂S absorbed**
> - MDEA–H₂S ≈ **1,050 kJ/kg H₂S absorbed**
>
> Lower ΔH → lower reboiler duty → lower operating cost. This single number decides plant OPEX.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> All formulas below are dimensionally consistent; use SI units unless otherwise stated. Vertical bars use `\vert` to keep the markdown table intact.

| # | Formula | Meaning / Engineering Use |
| :---: | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\alpha = \dfrac{n_{\text{acid gas, absorbed}}}{n_{\text{amine, in solution}}}$ | **Amine loading (mol/mol)** — the master process KPI; rich loading > lean loading |
| 2 | $\Delta \alpha = \alpha_{\text{rich}} - \alpha_{\text{lean}}$ | **Net working capacity** — must be maximised to minimise circulation |
| 3 | $L_{\text{amine}} = \dfrac{y_{\text{H}_2\text{S,in}} - y_{\text{H}_2\text{S,out}}}{\Delta \alpha \cdot C_{\text{amine}} \cdot M_{\text{amine}}}\, Q_{\text{gas}}$ | **Required lean amine circulation rate** (kg/h) |
| 4 | $Q_{\text{reb}} = \dot{m}_{\text{amine}} \, c_{p,\text{amine}} \, (T_{\text{reb}} - T_{\text{lean,in}})$ | **Reboiler sensible-heat duty** (kW) |
| 5 | $Q_{\text{total}} = \dot{m}_{\text{acid gas}} \cdot \Delta H_{\text{abs}}$ | **Latent heat to reverse reaction** (kW) |
| 6 | $\eta_{\text{abs}} = \dfrac{y_{\text{in}} - y_{\text{out}}}{y_{\text{in}}} \times 100\%$ | **Absorber removal efficiency** (%) |
| 7 | $K_{y}a = \dfrac{G}{Z \cdot (y^* - y)_{\text{lm}}}$ | **Overall gas-side mass-transfer coefficient** (kmol/m³·s) |
| 8 | $N_{\text{OG}} = \int_{y_{2}}^{y_{1}} \dfrac{dy}{y^* - y}$ | **Number of transfer units** (NTU) — used to size packed height |
| 9 | $H = N_{\text{OG}} \cdot H_{\text{OG}}$ | **Packing height**, $H_{\text{OG}}$ = height of a transfer unit (m) |
| 10 | $P_{\text{CO}_2} + P_{\text{H}_2\text{S}} < P_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}^{\text{sat}}$ | **Dehydration guard** — keep acid gas partial pressure < water saturation |
| 11 | $R_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{y_{\text{CO}_2,\text{in}} - y_{\text{CO}_2,\text{out}}}{x_{\text{CO}_2,\text{out}} - x_{\text{CO}_2,\text{in}}}$ | **Minimum solvent-to-gas ratio** |
| 12 | $\text{Steam ratio} = \dfrac{\text{kg steam}}{\text{kg acid gas stripped}}$ | **Specific steam consumption** — typically 2.5–4.5 kg steam / kg H₂S for MEA |
| 13 | $\text{HSS} = \dfrac{\text{Heat-Stable Salts}}{\text{Total amine charge}} \times 100\%$ | **HSS content** — keep below **0.5–1.0 wt %** |
| 14 | $v_{\text{super}} = \dfrac{G_{\text{actual}}}{\rho_{G} \cdot A_{\text{col}}}$ | **Superficial gas velocity** — must stay below flooding |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Pipeline gas sweetening** — every kg of H₂S removed here prevents downstream pipeline corrosion, catalyst poisoning (in methanators), and public-health hazards.
2. **LNG pre-treatment** — CO₂ must be removed to **< 50 ppmv** before liquefaction to prevent solid CO₂ freeze-out in cryogenic heat exchangers (which would block tubes and shut down the plant).
3. **Refinery off-gas treating** — hydrotreater recycle hydrogen and FCC off-gas are sweetened before being recycled to the reformer.
4. **Synthesis gas conditioning** — CO₂ removal is the first step of ammonia, methanol, and hydrogen production.
5. **Carbon Capture, Utilization & Storage (CCUS)** — concentrated amine solutions (often proprietary blends like **OASE® blue**, **KS-1™**) are the workhorse of post-combustion CO₂ capture from flue gases — a direct descendant of the 1930s amine process.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Amine Circulation Rate from a Material Balance

We want to size the lean amine flow rate $L$ (kmol/h) needed to reduce the H₂S content of a sour gas from $y_{1}$ to $y_{2}$ (mole fraction in gas).

### Step 1 — Acid-gas to be removed

The molar flow of H₂S to be absorbed in the absorber is:

$$\dot{n}_{\text{H}_2\text{S,abs}} = \dot{n}_{\text{gas}} \cdot (y_{1} - y_{2})$$

*This is a steady-state molar balance on the absorber; the difference in gas-phase H₂S between bottom and top equals the H₂S transferred to the liquid.*

### Step 2 — Moles of amine required to absorb this H₂S

Each mole of active amine can carry $\Delta \alpha$ moles of H₂S from the absorber bottom to the regenerator top (and back). So:

$$\dot{n}_{\text{amine, required}} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{\text{H}_2\text{S,abs}}}{\Delta \alpha} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{\text{gas}} \cdot (y_{1} - y_{2})}{\alpha_{\text{rich}} - \alpha_{\text{lean}}}$$

### Step 3 — Convert to mass flow

Multiplying by molecular weight $M_{\text{amine}}$ and dividing by mass-fraction $w$ of amine in the aqueous solution (the rest is water):

$$L_{\text{amine, soln}} = \dfrac{\dot{n}_{\text{gas}} \cdot (y_{1} - y_{2}) \cdot M_{\text{amine}}}{(\alpha_{\text{rich}} - \alpha_{\text{lean}}) \cdot w \cdot \rho_{\text{soln}}}$$

where $\rho_{\text{soln}}$ is the density of the aqueous amine solution (kg/m³). The result is volumetric circulation rate in m³/h.

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Standard)

**Given:**
- Sour gas flow: $\dot{n}_{\text{gas}} = 1000$ kmol/h
- Inlet H₂S mole fraction: $y_{1} = 0.020$ (2 mol %)
- Outlet H₂S mole fraction: $y_{2} = 4 \times 10^{-6}$ (4 ppmv)
- Rich loading: $\alpha_{\text{rich}} = 0.50$ mol H₂S / mol MEA
- Lean loading: $\alpha_{\text{lean}} = 0.01$ mol H₂S / mol MEA
- Amine MW: $M_{\text{MEA}} = 61.08$ kg/kmol
- Solution strength: $w = 20\,\text{wt}\%$
- Solution density: $\rho_{\text{soln}} = 1010$ kg/m³

**Step 1 — Molar H₂S absorbed:**

$$\dot{n}_{\text{H}_2\text{S,abs}} = 1000 \times (0.020 - 4 \times 10^{-6}) \approx 19.996 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 2 — Net working capacity:**

$$\Delta \alpha = 0.50 - 0.01 = 0.49 \text{ mol H₂S / mol MEA}$$

**Step 3 — Pure MEA molar flow:**

$$\dot{n}_{\text{MEA}} = \dfrac{19.996}{0.49} = 40.81 \text{ kmol/h}$$

**Step 4 — Pure MEA mass flow:**

$$\dot{m}_{\text{MEA}} = 40.81 \times 61.08 = 2492.5 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 5 — Solution volumetric flow:**

$$L_{\text{soln}} = \dfrac{2492.5}{0.20 \times 1010} = 12.34 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Sanity Check:**
> Specific circulation ≈ 12.34 m³ / (1000 kmol × 22.4 m³/kmol) ≈ **0.55 L per Sm³ of gas** — within the 0.20–0.45 L/Sm³ industrial range for medium-pressure gas. ✔

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Number of Theoretical Stages (Kremser Equation)

For a counter-current gas–liquid absorber with constant molar overflow and equilibrium described by $y^* = m x + b$ (linear approximation of the amine–acid gas equilibrium line), the number of theoretical stages $N$ is given by the **Kremser equation**:

$$\dfrac{y_{N+1} - y^*_{N+1}}{y_{1} - y^*_{1}} = \dfrac{A^{N+1} - A}{A^{N+1} - 1}, \quad A = \dfrac{L}{m\,G}$$

where:
- $A$ = **absorption factor** (must be **> 1** for finite-stage design)
- $L$ = liquid molar flow (kmol/h)
- $G$ = gas molar flow (kmol/h)
- $m$ = slope of the equilibrium line (dimensionless)
- $y_{N+1}$ = gas-phase composition at the bottom of the absorber (rich end)
- $y_{1}$ = gas-phase composition at the top (lean end)

For very high $A$ ($A \to \infty$), the relation reduces to a single-stage equilibrium (very tall column, dilute system).

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Minimum Solvent-to-Gas Ratio

A mass balance on the gas phase with a vertical-equilibrium tie line (touching the operating line at the top of the absorber) yields the **minimum solvent-to-gas ratio**:

$$\left(\dfrac{L}{G}\right)_{\min} = \dfrac{y_{\text{in}} - y_{\text{out}}}{x^*_{\text{out}} - x_{\text{in}}}$$

The actual operating $(L/G)_{\text{actual}}$ is chosen as **1.2 to 1.8 times** the minimum to limit the number of stages to a practical value:

$$\left(\dfrac{L}{G}\right)_{\text{actual}} = (1.3 \text{ to } 1.5) \times \left(\dfrac{L}{G}\right)_{\min}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Reboiler Duty (Energy Balance on Regenerator)

The total reboiler duty has three additive components:

$$Q_{\text{reb,total}} = Q_{\text{sensible}} + Q_{\text{latent,steam}} + Q_{\text{reaction reversal}}$$

**(a) Sensible heat to bring rich amine to reboiler temperature:**

$$Q_{\text{sens}} = \dot{m}_{\text{rich}} \, c_{p,\text{rich}} \, (T_{\text{reb}} - T_{\text{rich, in}})$$

**(b) Latent heat to generate stripping steam** (the water in the lean amine must vaporize to act as stripping vapour):

$$Q_{\text{latent}} = \dot{m}_{\text{steam}} \, \lambda_{\text{H}_2\text{O}}$$

**(c) Reversal of exothermic absorption reaction** (the dominant term):

$$Q_{\text{rxn}} = \dot{n}_{\text{acid gas stripped}} \cdot \Delta H_{\text{abs}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Empirical Industry Rule of Thumb:** For a typical MEA plant, the **reaction-reversal term** contributes ~50–60 % of the reboiler duty, the **sensible-heat term** ~25–30 %, and the **latent-steam term** ~15–20 %.

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Packed-Column Height via HTU-NTU Concept

For a packed absorber, the height $H$ of the packing is the product of **HTU** and **NTU**:

$$H = H_{\text{OG}} \cdot N_{\text{OG}}$$

The **number of gas-phase transfer units** is:

$$N_{\text{OG}} = \int_{y_{2}}^{y_{1}} \dfrac{dy}{y - y^*}$$

For a linear equilibrium line ($y^* = m x$) and a straight operating line, the integral evaluates to the classic logarithmic form:

$$N_{\text{OG}} = \dfrac{y_{1} - y_{2}}{(y - y^*)_{\text{lm}}} \quad \text{(log-mean driving force)}$$

The **height of a transfer unit** $H_{\text{OG}}$ is an empirical function of the packing type (e.g., **0.5 m** for 25 mm Pall rings, **1.0 m** for 50 mm Intalox saddles) and is read from packing manufacturer correlation charts (e.g., the **Onda** correlations for $k_G$ and $k_L$).

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Solvent Selection

| Property / Criterion | MEA | DEA | MDEA | Sulfinol-D | DGA | AMP |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Acid-gas removal (H₂S) | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent |
| Acid-gas removal (CO₂) | Excellent | Excellent | Partial (selective) | Excellent | Excellent | Moderate |
| **H₂S/CO₂ Selectivity** | None | Low | **Very High** | Low | None | High |
| Heat of reaction (kJ/kg H₂S) | **1,915** | 1,190 | **1,050** | 1,250 | 1,720 | 1,150 |
| Working capacity (mol/mol) | 0.30 | 0.35 | **0.70–0.80** | 0.50 | 0.40 | 0.55 |
| Reboiler duty | **High** | Medium | **Low** | Medium | Medium-High | Low-Medium |
| Corrosion tendency | **High** | Medium | **Low** | Medium | Low | Low |
| Vapor pressure (volatility loss) | **High** | Medium | **Very Low** | Low (Sulfolane is non-volatile) | Low | Low |
| Reclaimer needed? | **Yes** | Optional | No | No | Yes | No |
| Cost per kg | Low | Low | Medium | High | High | Medium |
| Best application | High-purity treating, CO₂ + H₂S | Refinery gases, medium pressure | **Selective H₂S over CO₂** (Claus-tail-gas, CO₂-EOR) | High-acid-gas, high-pressure | High-CO₂, sour LNG feed | H₂S-selective, low energy |
