# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 3]`

**"Define the amine process. List the two main classes of alkanolamines with one example each."** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer (Concise, 3-mark quality):**

The amine process is a **regenerative chemical absorption process** in which a dilute aqueous solution of an alkanolamine is used to selectively remove the acid gases **H₂S and CO₂** from natural gas, refinery off-gases, and synthesis gas. The acid gases are absorbed in a high-pressure **contactor** and the rich amine is regenerated in a low-pressure **stripper** by application of heat, producing a concentrated acid-gas stream and lean amine for recycle.

The two main classes are:

1. **Primary amines** — example: **MEA (Monoethanolamine)**, formula `HOCH₂CH₂NH₂`.
2. **Secondary amines** — example: **DEA (Diethanolamine)**, formula `(HOCH₂CH₂)₂NH`.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for primary + example, 1 mark for secondary + example.)*

### Question A2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 3]`

**"Why is MDEA preferred over MEA for the selective removal of H₂S in the presence of CO₂?"** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**

1. MDEA is a **tertiary amine**; its nitrogen atom carries no N–H bond and therefore **cannot form a stable carbamate with CO₂**. The reaction is limited to the slower **bicarbonate pathway**, which is rate-limited and proceeds to a much smaller extent. (1 mark)

2. The reaction of MDEA with H₂S is **fast and proton-transfer-controlled**, so H₂S is still absorbed efficiently, giving a **high H₂S/CO₂ selectivity ratio** (often > 10:1 on a molar basis). (1 mark)

3. MDEA also has a **lower heat of reaction** (~1,050 kJ/kg H₂S) compared with MEA (~1,915 kJ/kg H₂S), so the **reboiler duty in regeneration is much lower** — typically 20–35 % energy savings. (1 mark)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### **Question A — Full 14-Mark Question** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Module 3, Internal Choice A]`

#### (a) With a neat flow diagram, describe the **amine gas-treating process** for the sweetening of natural gas. Explain the function of the **absorber**, the **regenerator**, the **rich/lean heat exchanger**, the **reflux system**, and the **reclaimer** *(7 marks)*.

**Model Solution:**

1. **Definition and Objective (1 mark):** The amine process uses an aqueous alkanolamine solution to absorb H₂S and CO₂ from sour natural gas in a closed absorption–regeneration loop, producing pipeline-quality sweet gas and a concentrated acid-gas by-product for sulfur recovery (Claus unit).

2. **Flow Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to SECTION 4.1 above. The student should sketch: Inlet Separator → Absorber (top–bottom: lean amine in, sour gas in) → Sweet gas out top → Rich amine out bottom → Flash Drum → Cross Heat Exchanger → Regenerator (with reboiler at bottom) → Reflux Accumulator → Acid-gas to Claus → Lean amine cooler → Lean pump → back to absorber.

3. **Function of Each Equipment (5 marks, distributed):**
   - **Absorber:** A vertical trayed (typically 20–24 valve trays) or packed column operating at **5–70 bar** and **35–50 °C**. Sour gas enters at the bottom; lean amine (15–30 wt % aqueous) enters at the top. Counter-current contact enables chemical absorption of H₂S/CO₂ into the amine via proton transfer, releasing **sweet gas (H₂S < 4 ppmv)** at the top.
   - **Regenerator (Stripper):** A trayed column operating at near-atmospheric pressure (~1.1 bar) and a reboiler temperature of **115–125 °C**. The rich amine is stripped of acid gases by counter-current stripping steam generated in the reboiler. The hot, lean amine leaves the bottom; the acid-gas vapour leaves the top.
   - **Rich/Lean Cross Heat Exchanger:** Recovers ~50–70 % of the sensible heat from the hot lean amine (returning from the regenerator) to preheat the cold rich amine (returning from the absorber). This drastically reduces the reboiler fuel/steam demand and is a major source of plant efficiency.
   - **Reflux System:** The regenerator overhead vapour (acid gas + water vapour) is condensed in a partial condenser; condensed water is collected in the reflux accumulator and pumped back to the top tray of the regenerator. This **minimises amine loss** with the acid-gas stream and maintains the water balance of the amine solution.
   - **Reclaimer:** A side-stream vacuum or atmospheric distillation column that removes **heat-stable salts (HSS)** and thermal-degradation by-products of the amine (especially critical for **MEA**, which degrades to formic acid, glycolic acid, and polymeric residues). Without reclaimer, HSS accumulate, reduce amine activity, increase corrosion, and degrade treating capacity.

#### (b) Explain the **chemistry of the amine–acid gas reactions**. Why does MDEA show **H₂S selectivity** over CO₂? Mention the role of **heat of reaction** in the regeneration step. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

1. **Generic Reactions (2 marks):**
   - With H₂S (instantaneous proton transfer, valid for primary, secondary, and tertiary amines):

$$\text{R}_x\text{NH}_{(3-x)} + \text{H}_2\text{S} \rightleftharpoons \text{R}_x\text{NH}_{(4-x)}^{+} + \text{HS}^{-}$$

   - With CO₂, two pathways:
     - **Carbamate (primary/secondary amines):**

$$2\,\text{R}_2\text{NH} + \text{CO}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{R}_2\text{NCOO}^{-} + \text{R}_2\text{NH}_2^{+}$$

     - **Bicarbonate (dominant for tertiary amines, requires water):**

$$\text{R}_3\text{N} + \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{R}_3\text{NH}^{+} + \text{HCO}_3^{-}$$

2. **Why MDEA is H₂S-Selective (2 marks):** MDEA is tertiary; its N atom has no proton to donate, so it **cannot form a carbamate** (the kinetically preferred CO₂ pathway in MEA/DEA is blocked). The CO₂ reaction must proceed via the much slower **bicarbonate** route, which is rate-limited and low-capacity at absorber residence times. H₂S reaction is instantaneous because it is a small, highly acidic molecule that does not require carbamate formation — it transfers a proton directly to the MDEA nitrogen. The result: H₂S is absorbed almost completely, while CO₂ slips through and exits with the sweet gas.

3. **Role of Heat of Reaction in Regeneration (2 marks):** The forward (absorption) reaction is **exothermic** (ΔH_abs is negative, exothermic; reported values are positive when written as heat released per unit of acid gas absorbed). The reverse reaction in the regenerator is **endothermic**, requiring heat input equal to ΔH_abs plus sensible and steam-vaporization terms. The reboiler supplies this energy at **115–125 °C**, shifting the equilibrium back towards acid-gas release. MDEA's lower ΔH_abs (1,050 vs 1,915 kJ/kg H₂S for MEA) directly translates into a **smaller reboiler duty** and **lower OPEX**.

4. **Conclusion (1 mark):** Process selection is a balance of reaction chemistry, thermodynamics, and operating cost. MDEA is preferred when **H₂S must be removed but CO₂ is to be retained** (e.g., CO₂-flooding for EOR, or for the next-stage CO₂ capture).

### **Question B — Alternative 14-Mark Question** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Module 3, Internal Choice B]`

#### (a) Compare the properties and applications of **MEA, DEA, and MDEA** as acid-gas removal solvents. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (Tabular, Board-Ready):**

| Parameter | **MEA (Monoethanolamine)** | **DEA (Diethanolamine)** | **MDEA (Methyldiethanolamine)** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Class | Primary | Secondary | Tertiary |
| Formula | `HOCH₂CH₂NH₂` | `(HOCH₂CH₂)₂NH` | `(HOCH₂CH₂)₂NCH₃` |
| Molecular weight (g/mol) | 61.08 | 105.14 | 119.16 |
| Heat of reaction (kJ/kg H₂S) | **1,915 (highest)** | 1,190 | **1,050 (lowest)** |
| Working capacity (mol/mol) | 0.25 – 0.33 | 0.30 – 0.40 | **0.50 – 0.80** |
| Selectivity H₂S / CO₂ | None | Low | **Very high** |
| Regeneration energy | High | Medium | **Low** |
| Corrosion tendency | **High** | Medium | **Low** |
| Volatility (vapour loss) | High | Medium | **Negligible** |
| Reclaimer needed? | **Yes (mandatory)** | Optional | Not required |
| Typical concentration | 15 – 20 wt % | 25 – 35 wt % | 40 – 50 wt % |
| Typical application | LNG pre-treatment; high-purity; deep CO₂ removal | Refinery gas treating; FCC off-gas; syngas | **Selective H₂S removal in presence of CO₂**; Claus tail-gas treating; CO₂-EOR; CCUS |

**Key Engineering Inferences (3 marks, distributed):**
- MEA is the **most reactive** (highest basicity) and gives the **lowest treated-gas acid-gas content**, but suffers from high corrosion, high energy for regeneration, and irreversible degradation → reclaimer is essential.
- DEA is a compromise: lower regeneration energy than MEA, but still absorbs CO₂ non-selectively.
- MDEA's tertiary structure eliminates carbamate formation, so CO₂ reacts only slowly (via the bicarbonate route); hence it is the **only** one of the three that delivers true **selective H₂S removal**. It also has the lowest regeneration energy, lowest corrosion, and the highest working capacity.

#### (b) A natural gas stream at **30 bar** and **40 °C** contains **2.5 mol % H₂S** and **3.0 mol % CO₂**. It is to be treated with **20 wt % aqueous MEA** to obtain a sweet gas with **H₂S ≤ 50 ppmv**. Determine the **minimum lean amine circulation rate** if the rich and lean loadings are **0.45 and 0.005 mol/mol MEA** respectively. Assume gas flow = **5.0 × 10⁵ Sm³/day** at NTP. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert gas flow to molar flow (at NTP, 1 kmol = 22.414 Sm³):**

$$\dot{n}_{\text{gas}} = \dfrac{5.0 \times 10^{5}}{22.414} = 22{,}305 \text{ kmol/day}$$

**Step 2 — H₂S to be removed (assume CO₂ is co-absorbed, but the sizing is controlled by H₂S pickup):**

$$\dot{n}_{\text{H}_2\text{S}} = 22{,}305 \times (0.025 - 50 \times 10^{-6}) = 22{,}305 \times 0.02495 = 556.5 \text{ kmol/day}$$

**Step 3 — Net working capacity:**

$$\Delta \alpha = 0.45 - 0.005 = 0.445 \text{ mol H}_2\text{S / mol MEA}$$

**Step 4 — Pure MEA molar flow required:**

$$\dot{n}_{\text{MEA}} = \dfrac{556.5}{0.445} = 1{,}250.6 \text{ kmol/day}$$

**Step 5 — Pure MEA mass flow (M_MEA = 61.08 kg/kmol):**

$$\dot{m}_{\text{MEA}} = 1{,}250.6 \times 61.08 = 76{,}386 \text{ kg/day} = 3{,}182.7 \text{ kg/h}$$

**Step 6 — Volumetric flow of 20 wt % solution (ρ ≈ 1,005 kg/m³):**

$$L_{\text{soln}} = \dfrac{3{,}182.7}{0.20 \times 1{,}005} = 15.83 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Step 7 — Accounting for actual operation (multiply by 1.3 for safety margin):**

$$L_{\text{actual}} = 1.3 \times 15.83 = 20.58 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

**Answer:** The minimum lean amine solution circulation rate is approximately **15.8 m³/h**, with the **design (actual) rate ≈ 20.6 m³/h**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit trap:** Students frequently forget to convert Sm³/day → kmol/h using 22.414. Always show the conversion explicitly.
> 2. **Lean loading ≠ zero:** Some students assume lean loading = 0, which gives an unphysically large circulation. Always use the given value.
> 3. **Concentration in wt % vs volume %:** Use the **wt % × density** denominator, not just wt % alone.
> 4. **Design margin:** 1.3× to 1.5× multiplier is the industrial standard; reporting only the "minimum" loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> Use this section for last-night-before-exam revision. Each bullet is a high-yield KTU point.

- [ ] **Definition:** The amine process is a **regenerative chemical absorption** process for **H₂S and CO₂** removal from natural gas using aqueous **alkanolamines**.
- [ ] **Three amine classes:** Primary (MEA), Secondary (DEA), Tertiary (MDEA) — distinguished by **number of H atoms on the N atom**.
- [ ] **MEA characteristics:** most basic, highest reactivity, highest heat of reaction, **highest corrosion**, **reclaimer required**.
- [ ] **MDEA characteristics:** tertiary, **no carbamate formation**, therefore **selective H₂S / CO₂**, lowest reboiler duty.
- [ ] **Two CO₂ pathways:** Carbamate (primary/secondary, fast, consumes 2 mol amine) and Bicarbonate (tertiary + water, slow, consumes 1 mol amine).
- [ ] **Process flow:** Inlet Separator → Absorber → Rich Flash → Cross Exchanger → Regenerator → Reflux → Lean Cooler → Lean Pump → back to Absorber.
- [ ] **Absorber conditions:** **High pressure (5–70 bar), low temperature (35–50 °C)** — favours forward (exothermic) reaction.
- [ ] **Regenerator conditions:** **Low pressure (~1.1 bar), high temperature (115–125 °C reboiler)** — drives equilibrium in reverse.
- [ ] **Pipeline spec:** sweet gas must have **H₂S < 4 ppmv** and **CO₂ < 2 mol %** (or **< 50 ppmv** for LNG feed).
- [ ] **Key KPIs to memorise:** Amine loading $\alpha$ (mol acid gas/mol amine), $\Delta\alpha$ (net working capacity), reboiler duty, HSS content (< 0.5–1 wt %), steam ratio (kg steam / kg H₂S).
- [ ] **Mass-transfer formulae:** Kremser equation, $(L/G)_{\min}$, HTU-NTU, $H = H_{\text{OG}} \cdot N_{\text{OG}}$.
- [ ] **Energy split in reboiler:** ~50–60 % reaction reversal, ~25–30 % sensible, ~15–20 % steam-vaporization.
- [ ] **Equipment must-knows:** Absorber, Regenerator, Reboiler, Reflux Accumulator, Cross Exchanger, Reclaimer (for MEA), Charcoal Filter.
- [ ] **Industrial trend:** Amine process is the **backbone of CCUS (carbon capture)**; future plants are moving to **advanced sterically hindered amines (KS-1, OASE Blue)** for > 90 % CO₂ capture with < 2.5 GJ/tonne CO₂.
- [ ] **Common 14-mark question pattern:** "Describe the process with a flow diagram + chemistry of reactions + solvent selection" — practice writing all three together in 7-mark and 7-mark sub-parts.
- [ ] **Examiner's hot-button:** Always state the **process objective first**, then the **flow**, then the **chemistry**, then the **math** (loading, circulation, energy). This is the **valuation key**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Carbonate Washing Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT528 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Carbonate Washing Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Carbonate Washing Process** (also known as the **Hot Potassium Carbonate Process**, **"Hot Pot" Process**, or in its proprietary forms as the **Benfield** or **Catacarb** process) is a regenerative **chemical absorption** technique used in natural gas processing to selectively remove bulk quantities of acidic contaminants — primarily **carbon dioxide (CO₂)** and **hydrogen sulfide (H₂S)** — from a sour natural gas stream by contacting it with a hot, aqueous solution of an alkaline carbonate salt, most commonly **potassium carbonate (K₂CO₃)** operating in the temperature range of **200 °F – 250 °F (≈ 93 °C – 121 °C)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEPOT528 – Module 3):**
> *Carbonate washing is a gas sweetening unit operation employing an aqueous alkaline carbonate solution (typically 20–35 wt% K₂CO₃) as the reactive solvent, in which acid gases (CO₂, H₂S) are absorbed via reversible chemical reaction, and the rich solvent is thermally regenerated in a separate stripping column to release the acid gases at low pressure, allowing the lean solvent to be recycled.*

The process is classified under the family of **non-amine chemical absorption processes** and is the second most widely deployed gas-treating technology in the world after amine-based treating, owing to its low energy footprint, non-degrading solvent, and excellent capacity for **bulk CO₂ removal** at elevated pressures.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Sponge and Squeezer" Analogy:**
> Imagine a dirty kitchen sponge soaked in a strong cleaning solution. When you dip the sponge into greasy, contaminated water, it absorbs the grease (the **absorption step**). You cannot, however, keep using the same sponge forever — it is now "full" of dirt. So you take it to a squeezing station where heat and pressure are applied to wring out the trapped grease into a collection bucket (the **regeneration step**). The cleaned sponge is then returned to duty.

**Mapping the analogy to the process:**

| Kitchen Analogy | Carbonate Washing Process |
|----------------|---------------------------|
| Greasy, contaminated water | Sour natural gas (containing CO₂, H₂S) |
| Cleaning-solution-soaked sponge | Lean K₂CO₃ solution entering absorber top |
| Sponge absorbing grease | Acid gases chemically reacting with K₂CO₃ to form bicarbonates |
| Full sponge (dirty) | Rich K₂CO₃ solution leaving absorber bottom |
| Squeezing station (heat + force) | Stripper column (low pressure, steam-reboiled) |
| Collection bucket (extracted grease) | Released acid gas stream to sulfur recovery (Claus unit) |
| Clean, reusable sponge | Lean K₂CO₃ solution returning to absorber |

The **alkaline carbonate solution acts as a "reactive sponge"** that chemically traps the acidic gases rather than merely physically dissolving them — making the process highly selective and efficient at removing bulk acid-gas loads.

---

## 1.3 Why Carbonate? — Salient Highlights

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Exam Pointers to Memorize:**
> - **Reagent:** K₂CO₃ (20–35 wt%) — preferred over Na₂CO₃ due to **higher solubility (≈ 35 wt% vs. ≈ 18 wt% for Na₂CO₃)** at process temperatures.
> - **Operating Temperature:** 200 – 250 °F — elevated to **suppress carbamate precipitation** and shift equilibrium toward reversible absorption.
> - **Regeneration:** Achieved by **flashing** rich solvent to lower pressure and **steam-stripping** in a reboiled regenerator.
> - **Solvent Make-up:** Very low, since K₂CO₃ is **thermally stable and non-volatile** — does not evaporate with acid gas.
> - **Key Proprietary Variants:** **Benfield** (UOP, USA), **Catacarb** (Eickmeyer & Associates), **HiPure** (Exxon), **Lurgi Purisol/Sulfinol** related chemistries.

---

## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics (KTU-Relevant)

The following benchmark values are typical for **board numerical problems**:

- **Solvent concentration:** 25 – 30 wt% K₂CO₃ in water
- **Absorber pressure:** **400 – 1500 psig (≈ 2.8 – 10.3 MPa)**
- **Absorber temperature:** **230 – 250 °F (≈ 110 – 121 °C)**
- **Stripper pressure:** **5 – 15 psig (≈ 135 – 205 kPa)**
- **Stripper temperature (reboiler):** **240 – 250 °F (≈ 115 – 121 °C)**
- **Acid-gas pickup per pass:** **0.4 – 0.6 mol CO₂ / mol K₂CO₃** (lean loading ≈ 0.15 – 0.25)
- **Heat of reaction (CO₂ with K₂CO₃):** ≈ **−27 kcal / mol CO₂**
- **Solvent circulation rate:** typically **1.5 – 2.5 gal / lb-mol of acid gas removed**

> [!CAUTION]
> **Why Potassium over Sodium Carbonate?**
> Na₂CO₃ has a solubility ceiling of only ~18 wt% at process temperatures, leading to crystallization and plugging. K₂CO₃ reaches ~35 wt% — almost double — yielding higher absorption capacity per unit solvent circulated and drastically reducing circulation-pump energy costs.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Process Chemistry — Core Reactions

The carbonate washing process operates on the principle of **reversible acid-base neutralization**, where the carbonate ion (CO₃²⁻) acts as a Brønsted-Lowry base accepting a proton from the acidic H₂S or the dissolved CO₂ (which behaves as carbonic acid in water).

### 2.1.1 Primary Reactions (Absorber — Forward Direction)

**(a) CO₂ Absorption (Major Reaction):**

$$\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow 2\,\text{KHCO}_3$$

**(b) H₂S Absorption:**

$$\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{S} \longrightarrow \text{KHS} + \text{KHCO}_3$$

**(c) Direct CO₂ Hydrolysis (Parallel, Slower):**

$$\text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}_2\text{CO}_3$$

$$\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{CO}_3 \longrightarrow 2\,\text{KHCO}_3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Mechanistic Insight:** CO₂ is a kinetically **slow-reacting acid gas** because it must first hydrate to H₂CO₃ before the carbonate ion can neutralize it. This is why **activators** (such as DEA, MEA, piperazine, or proprietary amines called "Benfield additives") are added in small concentrations (1–5 wt%) to dramatically accelerate the absorption rate without becoming the primary absorbent.

### 2.1.2 Regeneration Reactions (Stripper — Reverse Direction)

When the rich solvent is heated and depressurized in the stripper, the bicarbonate salts decompose, releasing the acid gases and regenerating the carbonate:

$$\text{2 KHCO}_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta,\, \downarrow P} \text{K}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{CO}_2 \uparrow + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$\text{KHS} + \text{KHCO}_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta,\, \downarrow P} \text{K}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{S} \uparrow$$

The released CO₂ and H₂S exit the top of the stripper and are routed to a **Claus sulfur-recovery unit** (for H₂S) or vented / sequestered (for CO₂).

---

## 2.2 Operating Variables and Their Effects

| Parameter | Effect on Absorption | Effect on Regeneration | KTU Exam Note |
|-----------|---------------------|------------------------|----------------|
| **↑ Temperature** | Decreases equilibrium loading (suppresses reaction) | Favors bicarbonate decomposition | Process is run at the **highest temperature** at which absorption is still acceptable |
| **↑ Pressure** | Increases CO₂/H₂S partial pressure → **favors absorption** | Does not directly aid; stripper runs at low P | Hot Pot is favored for **high-pressure** gas treating |
| **↑ K₂CO₃ concentration** | Increases solvent capacity per unit mass | Higher concentration → more heat duty needed in reboiler | Optimum: **25 – 30 wt%** |
| **↑ Lean-solvent loading** | Reduces driving force for absorption | Easier regeneration | Must be balanced: too low loading → excess reboiler duty |
| **↑ Gas flow rate** | Decreases contact time | — | Determines column diameter (via flooding velocity) |
| **↑ Activator concentration** | Speeds up CO₂ absorption kinetics | — | Catalytic effect; 1–5 wt% sufficient |

---

## 2.3 Process Flow — Block-by-Block Description

A typical carbonate-washing unit consists of the following major equipment:

1. **Absorber (Contactor) Tower:** A trayed or packed column operating at high pressure where sour gas enters at the bottom and flows counter-current to the descending lean K₂CO₃ solution entering at the top. Acid gases are absorbed, producing **"rich" solvent**.
2. **Rich-Solvent Flash Drum (Lean/Rich Exchanger Pre-heater):** The rich solvent is let down in pressure, releasing physically dissolved hydrocarbon gases (which are recovered) and partially regenerating the solvent.
3. **Stripper (Regenerator) Column:** A low-pressure reboiled column where the rich solvent is heated by steam in the reboiler, releasing CO₂ and H₂S overhead. Lean solvent leaves the bottom.
4. **Lean/Rich Heat Exchanger:** Recovers heat from hot lean solvent exiting the stripper bottom to preheat the cold rich solvent — a major energy-saving feature.
5. **Surge Tank, Pumps, Filters, and Activator Make-up System:** Maintains solvent inventory and purity.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Hook — "AFS-HL-PS":** **A**bsorber → **F**lash drum → **S**tripper → **H**eat exchanger → **L**ean pump → back to **A**bsorber; **P**roprietary additives (S)upplied continuously.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — KTU 2024 EXAM ESSENTIALS**

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbols & Units | Application |
|---|-------------------|-----------------|-------------|
| 1 | $\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons 2\text{KHCO}_3$ | Molar basis | Stoichiometric CO₂ pickup |
| 2 | $L = \dfrac{n_{\text{CO}_2}}{n_{\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3}}$ | $L$ = loading (mol/mol); dimensionless | **Solvent loading** definition |
| 3 | $M_s = \dfrac{n_{\text{acid gas removed}} \times \text{GMW}}{X_{\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3}}$ | $M_s$ = solvent mass flow (kg/h); $X$ = mass fraction | Minimum solvent circulation |
| 4 | $C_p \, \Delta T \, m = Q_{\text{reboiler}}$ | $Q$ in kW; $C_p \approx 3.8\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$ for K₂CO₃ solution | Reboiler heat duty |
| 5 | $P_{\text{CO}_2}^{\text{top}} = y_{\text{CO}_2} \times P_{\text{abs}}$ | Partial pressure at absorber top | Treated-gas purity spec |
| 6 | $R_{\min} = \dfrac{L}{D}\bigg\vert_{\min}$ | Minimum reflux for stripper | Stripper design |
| 7 | $\Delta H_{\text{rxn}}(\text{CO}_2) \approx -27\ \text{kcal/mol}$ | Heat of reaction | Energy balance |
| 8 | $v = \dfrac{G}{\rho_G \, A}$ | Superficial gas velocity | Tower sizing (flooding check) |
| 9 | $\text{Convers.} = \dfrac{n_{\text{CO}_2, \text{in}} - n_{\text{CO}_2, \text{out}}}{n_{\text{CO}_2, \text{in}}} \times 100\%$ | Percent removal | Performance metric |
| 10 | $\alpha_{\text{H}_2\text{S}/\text{CO}_2} = \dfrac{(y_{\text{H}_2\text{S}}/y_{\text{CO}_2})_{\text{out}}}{(y_{\text{H}_2\text{S}}/y_{\text{CO}_2})_{\text{in}}}$ | Selectivity | Used in modified processes |

> [!WARNING]
> **Note on notation:** All fraction bars, divisions, and absolute-value bars above are written with the LaTeX `\vert` (vertical bar surrogate) to preserve markdown table integrity. Do **not** use the raw pipe `$\vert$` inside table cells when converting to your own notes — the table will break.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The carbonate washing process is the **workhorse technology for high-CO₂ natural gas fields**, including:

- **Offshore platforms** where weight and footprint are critical — the Hot Pot process is preferred over amines due to lower solvent inventory and absence of corrosion inhibitors in large quantity.
- **Enhanced Oil Recovery (EOR) CO₂ flood projects** — produced CO₂ must be separated from methane before re-injection or sale.
- **Sour-gas processing in Middle East & North Sea fields** — bulk H₂S removal prior to a polishing amine unit (two-stage sweetening).
- **Synthetic natural gas (SNG) plants** and **ammonia/hydrogen plants** — CO₂ removal upstream of methanation or ammonia synthesis.
- **Coal gasification syngas cleanup** — removes CO₂ from syngas prior to gas-to-liquid (GTL) processing.

The growing relevance of **carbon capture, utilization, and storage (CCUS)** has further increased the strategic importance of the Hot Pot process, as it is one of the most energy-efficient bulk CO₂ separation technologies available industrially.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Stoichiometric Solvent Requirement

> **Problem (KTU Typical, 7-mark type):**
> A natural gas stream contains **8.0 mol% CO₂** and is to be treated in a hot potassium carbonate unit. The gas flow rate is **5.0 × 10⁵ standard m³/day** at STP. The desired CO₂ removal efficiency is **90 %**. The solvent is a **30 wt% aqueous K₂CO₃ solution** with a design loading swing of **ΔL = 0.35 mol CO₂/mol K₂CO₃** (i.e., rich loading − lean loading). Calculate:
> (a) The molar flow of CO₂ to be removed per day.
> (b) The minimum K₂CO₃ circulation rate in kmol/day.
> (c) The total solution (solvent + water) mass circulation rate in tonnes/day.

---

### Step 1 — Convert the gas volumetric flow to molar flow at STP

At STP, 1 kmol of ideal gas occupies **22.414 m³**.

$$n_{\text{gas}} = \frac{5.0 \times 10^5\ \text{m}^3/\text{day}}{22.414\ \text{m}^3/\text{kmol}}$$

$$n_{\text{gas}} = 22{,}306.4\ \text{kmol/day}$$

**Text:** We divide the daily volumetric throughput by the molar volume at STP to obtain the total molar flow rate of the gas.

---

### Step 2 — Calculate the molar flow of CO₂ in the inlet gas

By definition, **8.0 mol%** of the gas is CO₂:

$$n_{\text{CO}_2, \text{in}} = 0.080 \times 22{,}306.4\ \text{kmol/day}$$

$$n_{\text{CO}_2, \text{in}} = 1{,}784.5\ \text{kmol/day}$$

**Text:** Multiply total molar flow by the mole fraction of CO₂ (0.080) to find CO₂ entering the absorber.

---

### Step 3 — Apply the 90 % removal efficiency

$$n_{\text{CO}_2, \text{removed}} = 0.90 \times 1{,}784.5\ \text{kmol/day}$$

$$n_{\text{CO}_2, \text{removed}} = 1{,}606.05\ \text{kmol/day}$$

**Text:** Only 90 % of the CO₂ is captured; the remaining 10 % leaves with the treated gas.

---

### Step 4 — Compute the minimum K₂CO₃ circulation rate

The stoichiometric reaction is:

$$\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow 2\,\text{KHCO}_3$$

One mole of K₂CO₃ reacts with one mole of CO₂. With a design loading swing ΔL = 0.35:

$$n_{\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3, \text{min}} = \frac{n_{\text{CO}_2, \text{removed}}}{\Delta L}$$

$$n_{\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3, \text{min}} = \frac{1{,}606.05}{0.35}$$

$$n_{\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3, \text{min}} = 4{,}588.7\ \text{kmol/day}$$

**Text:** The loading swing represents how much CO₂ each mole of K₂CO₃ can carry between the lean (stripper-bottom) and rich (absorber-bottom) states. Dividing total CO₂ to be removed by this per-mole capacity yields the minimum K₂CO₃ required.

---

### Step 5 — Convert to mass flow of K₂CO₃ and to total solution mass

Molecular weight of K₂CO₃ = 2(39.10) + 12.01 + 3(16.00) = **138.21 g/mol = 0.13821 kg/mol**

$$\dot{m}_{\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3} = 4{,}588.7 \times 0.13821 \times 10^3\ \text{kg/day}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3} = 634{,}212\ \text{kg/day} \approx 634.2\ \text{tonnes/day}$$

For a **30 wt% solution**, the total solution mass is:

$$\dot{m}_{\text{solution}} = \frac{634.2}{0.30} = 2{,}114.0\ \text{tonnes/day}$$

**Text:** Since only 30 % of the solution mass is active K₂CO₃, divide the active mass by 0.30 to get the total solvent circulation.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Reboiler Heat Duty

> **Problem:** The same plant uses a stripper operating at **1.2 atm** with the rich solvent entering at **105 °C** and leaving as lean solvent at **115 °C** (after steam-stripping). The total solution flow is **2,114 tonnes/day**. Using $C_p = 3.8\ \text{kJ/(kg·K)}$ for the K₂CO₃ solution, compute the **sensible heat duty** of the reboiler in **kW** (neglect the small heat of bicarbonate decomposition compared to sensible heat).

---

### Step 1 — Mass flow rate in kg/s

$$\dot{m} = \frac{2{,}114{,}000\ \text{kg}}{24 \times 3600\ \text{s}} = 24.47\ \text{kg/s}$$

**Text:** Convert tonnes/day to kg/second for SI power units.

---

### Step 2 — Temperature rise

$$\Delta T = 115 - 105 = 10\ \text{K (or °C)}$$

---

### Step 3 — Sensible heat duty

$$Q_{\text{reboiler}} = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$$

$$Q_{\text{reboiler}} = 24.47 \times 3.8 \times 10$$

$$Q_{\text{reboiler}} = 929.9\ \text{kW} \approx 930\ \text{kW}$$

**Text:** Plug into the standard sensible-heat formula. The reboiler must supply roughly 0.93 MW of thermal energy just to raise the solvent temperature by 10 °C, plus the latent heat for water vaporization and the heat of bicarbonate decomposition — a strong justification for the lean/rich heat exchanger in the actual process.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Material Balance Across the Absorber

> **Problem (KTU 14-mark format, sub-part b):**
> A hot potassium carbonate absorber handles a sour gas with composition **CH₄ = 88 %, CO₂ = 10 %, H₂S = 2 %** (mol basis). Inlet molar flow = **1,000 kmol/h**. Treated gas leaves with **CO₂ ≤ 0.5 % and H₂S ≤ 50 ppm (i.e., 0.005 %)**. Calculate:
> (a) The kmol/h of CO₂ and H₂S absorbed.
> (b) The mass of acid gas (combined CO₂ + H₂S) removed per hour.
> (c) The minimum K₂CO₃ required if ΔL = 0.30.

---

### (a) Molar Quantities Absorbed

$$\text{CO}_2 \text{ removed} = (0.10 - 0.005) \times 1{,}000 = 95.0\ \text{kmol/h}$$

$$\text{H}_2\text{S removed} = (0.02 - 0.00005) \times 1{,}000 \approx 19.995\ \text{kmol/h} \approx 20.0\ \text{kmol/h}$$

---

### (b) Mass of Acid Gas Removed

$$m_{\text{CO}_2} = 95.0 \times 44.01\ \text{kg/kmol} = 4{,}181\ \text{kg/h}$$

$$m_{\text{H}_2\text{S}} = 20.0 \times 34.08\ \text{kg/kmol} = 681.6\ \text{kg/h}$$

$$m_{\text{total acid gas}} = 4{,}181 + 681.6 = 4{,}862.6\ \text{kg/h} \approx 4.86\ \text{tonnes/h}$$

---

### (c) Minimum K₂CO₃ Required

Total acid gas absorbed (in CO₂-equivalent moles) ≈ 95 + 20 = 115 kmol/h

$$n_{\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3, \text{min}} = \frac{115}{0.30} = 383.3\ \text{kmol/h}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3, \text{min}} = 383.3 \times 138.21 = 52{,}978\ \text{kg/h} \approx 53.0\ \text{tonnes/h}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Advantages vs. Limitations

| Criterion | Hot Potassium Carbonate Process | Amine (MEA / DEA / MDEA) Process |
|-----------|----------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Solvent cost** | Low (inorganic salt) | Higher (organic amine) |
| **Solvent degradation** | Negligible | MEA degrades significantly; needs reclaiming |
| **Operating temperature** | High (200–250 °F) | Low (100–120 °F absorber, 250 °F stripper) |
| **Heat duty (reboiler)** | **Lower** (~1.0–1.5 MMBtu / ton acid gas) | **Higher** (~1.5–2.5 MMBtu / ton acid gas) |
| **Pressure suitability** | Best at **high pressure** (≥ 400 psig) | Good across a wide range |
| **Bulk CO₂ removal** | **Excellent, preferred choice** | Adequate but higher energy |
| **Trace H₂S polishing** | Requires downstream polishing amine | MDEA / DEA / MDEA-SULFINOL are excellent |
| **Selectivity H₂S/CO₂** | Low (co-absorbs CO₂ and H₂S) | MDEA gives very high selectivity |
| **Corrosion** | Severe — needs SS 304/316 / corrosion inhibitors | Manageable with inhibitors |
| **Capital cost** | Moderate | Moderate |
| **Best for** | High-pressure, high-CO₂ fields | Low-pressure, low-flow, polishing duty |

---

## 3.5 Summary of the Step-by-Step Logic Chain

1. **Identify inlet gas composition** (mol% of CO₂ and H₂S).
2. **Convert volumetric flow → molar flow** using STP molar volume.
3. **Apply removal efficiency / product spec** to get acid gas to be absorbed.
4. **Use the loading swing ΔL** to determine the K₂CO₃ circulation rate (mass basis).
5. **Compute reboiler heat duty** using sensible-heat formula $Q = \dot{m} C_p \Delta T$ (add heat of decomposition for full energy balance).
6. **Verify material balance closure** — what enters must leave absorber, stripper, and gas product streams.
7. **Perform an energy balance** around the lean/rich heat exchanger to estimate actual steam savings.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Carbonate Washing Unit

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a complete hot potassium carbonate gas-treating plant, illustrating the material and energy flows between the absorber, flash drum, stripper, heat exchangers, and pumps.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IN1([Sour Natural Gas Inlet]):::gasFlow --> A1[Absorber Tower]
    A1 -- Treated Sweet Gas --> OUT1([Sales Gas to Pipeline]):::gasFlow
    A1 -- Rich Carbonate Solution --> F1[Rich Solvent Flash Drum]
    F1 -- Flash Gas Recovery --> HC1([Hydrocarbon Recovery]):::gasFlow
    F1 -- Rich Lean Pre-heated --> HX1[Lean Rich Heat Exchanger]
    HX1 -- Heated Rich Solvent --> S1[Stripper Regenerator]
    S1 -- Acid Gas Overhead --> CL1([Claus Unit / Vent]):::gasFlow
    S1 -- Lean Hot Carbonate --> HX1
    HX1 -- Cooled Lean Solvent --> P1[Lean Solvent Pump]
    P1 -- Pressurized Lean Solution --> A1
    S1 -- Steam Reboiler --> RB1[Reboiler]
    RB1 -- Steam Returned --> S1
    AD1[Activator DEA Amine Additive] --> P1
    MK1[Fresh K2CO3 Make up Tank] --> P1
    FL1[Mechanical Filter Carbon] --> P1
    WT1[Wastewater Purge] --> S1

    classDef gasFlow fill:#FFF3B0,stroke:#7A5C00,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#1A1A1A
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- **Sour Gas** enters the **Absorber** (high pressure) at the bottom.
- **Treated (sweet) gas** exits the top of the absorber.
- **Rich solvent** flows down, flashes in the **Flash Drum**, exchanges heat with the lean stream in the **Lean/Rich HEX**, then enters the **Stripper** (low pressure, reboiled).
- **Acid gas** (CO₂ + H₂S) is released from the stripper top → routed to the **Claus unit** (for H₂S → elemental sulfur) or to a **CO₂ vent / sequestration**.
- **Lean solvent** from the stripper bottom is heat-exchanged, pumped, and **recycled** to the absorber.
- **Activator** (DEA or piperazine) is injected continuously into the lean stream to accelerate CO₂ absorption kinetics.
- **Fresh K₂CO₃** make-up compensates for any small losses.
- **Mechanical / carbon filters** keep the solution clean.
- A small **wastewater purge** prevents buildup of heat-stable salts.

---

## 4.2 Functional Hierarchy — Subsystem Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SYS1[Absorption Section]
        A2[Absorber Column] --> A3[Trays or Structured Packing]
        A2 --> A4[Sour Gas Inlet Distributor]
        A2 --> A5[Lean Solent Distributor]
    end

    subgraph SYS2[Flash and Heat Recovery]
        F2[Flash Drum] --> F3[Pressure Let Down Valve]
        HX2[Lean Rich Exchanger] --> HX3[Plate or Shell Coil Type]
    end

    subgraph SYS3[Regeneration Section]
        S2[Stripper Column] --> S3[Reboiler]
        S2 --> S4[Acid Gas Overhead Condenser]
        S2 --> S5[Reflux Drum]
    end

    subgraph SYS4[Solvent Management]
        P2[Lean Pump] --> P3[Activator Injection Skid]
        P2 --> P4[Filter and Carbon Polish]
        P2 --> P5[Make up K2CO3 Tank]
    end

    SYS1 --> SYS2 --> SYS3 --> SYS4 --> SYS1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The four subsystems operate in a **closed loop**, with the only net material inputs being **sour gas** (in), **steam** (to the reboiler), and **fresh K₂CO₃ make-up** (very small). The only net outputs are **sweet gas** (out), **acid gas to Claus/vent** (out), and a small **wastewater purge** (out). The closed-loop nature is what makes the Hot Pot process so **economically attractive** for high-throughput gas-treating applications.

---

## 4.3 Schematic of Reaction Pathways Within the Absorber

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G1[CO2 Molecule in Gas Film] -->|Dissolution| L1[CO2 in Liquid Film]
    G2[H2S Molecule in Gas Film] -->|Dissolution| L2[H2S in Liquid Film]
    L1 -->|Hydration| L3[H2CO3 in Bulk Liquid]
    L3 -->|Neutralization by CO3 2 Minus| P1[KHCO3 Product]
    L2 -->|Direct Acid Base| P2[KHS plus KHCO3 Products]
    ACT[Activator e.g. DEA Piperazine] -. Speeds up CO2 hydration .-> L3
```

**Explanation of the schematic:**

- Acid gases transfer from the **gas phase** across the **gas-liquid interface** into the **liquid phase**.
- CO₂ must first **hydrate** to H₂CO₃ (a slow step), which is why the process is often **kinetically limited for CO₂**.
- The dissolved acid is then **neutralized** by the carbonate ion, forming **bicarbonate** and (for H₂S) **bisulfide** salts.
- The **activator** dramatically accelerates the slow hydration step, increasing the **overall mass transfer rate** by 2–5× in industrial operation.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

### Q1. Define the "Carbonate Washing Process" and list any TWO advantages of using potassium carbonate over sodium carbonate as the absorbent.

**Model Answer:**

The **Carbonate Washing Process** (Hot Potassium Carbonate Process) is a regenerative gas-treating method in which an aqueous solution of an alkaline carbonate salt (K₂CO₃) is contacted counter-current with a sour natural gas stream in an absorber at elevated temperature and pressure. Acid gases (CO₂ and H₂S) are absorbed via reversible chemical reaction, and the rich solvent is thermally regenerated in a low-pressure stripper, allowing the lean solvent to be recycled.

**Two advantages of K₂CO₃ over Na₂CO₃:**

1. **Higher aqueous solubility** at process temperatures (≈ 35 wt% for K₂CO₃ vs. ≈ 18 wt% for Na₂CO₃), enabling higher absorption capacity per unit solvent mass and lower circulation rates.
2. **Lower equivalent weight** and **higher alkalinity** per unit mass, giving better absorption efficiency and reduced crystallization/plugging risk in process equipment.

> [!NOTE]
> Examiner often awards the third mark for **naming the operating temperature range** (200–250 °F) or **mentioning reversibility** of the reaction. Always add at least one numerical value if the question is definition-type.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

### Q2. Write the balanced chemical reactions for the absorption and regeneration of CO₂ in the hot potassium carbonate process. Why is the absorber operated at high temperature?

**Model Answer:**

**Absorption (Forward):**

$$\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow 2\,\text{KHCO}_3$$

**Regeneration (Reverse):**

$$\text{2 KHCO}_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta} \text{K}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Reason for high absorber temperature (200–250 °F):**

The high temperature is necessary to (a) **prevent precipitation of KHCO₃** which has limited solubility at lower temperatures, (b) **shift the equilibrium** so that the reaction remains reversible and the solvent can be regenerated by modest heating/pressure reduction, and (c) **permit the use of more concentrated K₂CO₃ solutions** (up to 30–35 wt%) which would otherwise crystallize.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3, Q13 / Q14 Type]**

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**(a)** *(7 marks)* With the help of a neat flow diagram, describe the **hot potassium carbonate process** for the removal of CO₂ and H₂S from natural gas. Explain the role of the **lean/rich heat exchanger** and the **stripper reboiler** in the process.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A natural gas stream of **4.0 × 10⁵ Sm³/day** at STP contains **12 mol% CO₂** and **3 mol% H₂S**. The process removes **95 % of the CO₂ and 99 % of the H₂S**. The solvent is **28 wt% K₂CO₃** with a design loading swing of **ΔL = 0.32 mol CO₂/mol K₂CO₃**. Compute:
- (i) Total kmol of acid gas removed per day.
- (ii) Minimum K₂CO₃ circulation in kmol/day and tonnes/day.
- (iii) The reboiler heat duty in kW if the solution temperature rises by 12 °C and $C_p = 3.85\ \text{kJ/kg·K}$.

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Discuss the **chemistry, operating variables, and process advantages** of the hot potassium carbonate process. Compare it briefly with the **amine-treating process** in terms of solvent cost, energy consumption, and suitability for bulk CO₂ removal.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A hot K₂CO₃ absorber handles a sour gas of composition: **CH₄ = 85 %, C₂H₆ = 4 %, CO₂ = 9 %, H₂S = 2 %** (mol basis). Inlet molar flow = **800 kmol/h**. Treated gas spec: **CO₂ ≤ 0.4 %, H₂S ≤ 30 ppm**. With ΔL = 0.30, calculate:
- (i) kmol/h of CO₂ and H₂S absorbed.
- (ii) Total mass of acid gas removed per hour.
- (iii) Minimum mass flow rate of K₂CO₃ in kg/h and total solvent solution (28 wt%) in tonnes/h.

---

### ⭐ MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION A

#### Part (a) — Descriptive (7 marks)

**Diagram (2 marks):** Draw the standard flow sheet: Sour gas → Absorber → Sweet gas; rich solvent → Flash drum → Lean/Rich HEX → Stripper → Acid gas overhead (to Claus); stripper bottom → Lean/Rich HEX (cold side) → Lean pump → back to Absorber top. (Refer to the Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.1.)

**Role of Lean/Rich HEX (2 marks):** The heat exchanger transfers heat from the hot lean solvent (leaving stripper bottom at ~115 °C) to the cooler rich solvent (leaving flash drum at ~105 °C). This **preheats** the rich solvent, reducing the **reboiler steam duty** by 40–60 %, and simultaneously **cools** the lean solvent before it returns to the absorber top, which is necessary to maximize absorption efficiency.

**Role of Stripper Reboiler (2 marks):** The reboiler supplies **latent heat** (for steam-stripping) and **sensible heat** (to raise the solvent to stripping temperature) and provides the **endothermic heat of bicarbonate decomposition**, thereby releasing pure CO₂ and H₂S overhead and regenerating the lean K₂CO₃ solution.

**Process description (1 mark):** A brief paragraph summarizing the closed-loop operation.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**(i) Molar flow at STP:**

$$n_{\text{gas}} = \frac{4.0 \times 10^5}{22.414} = 17{,}845\ \text{kmol/day}$$

**Inlet CO₂:** $0.12 \times 17{,}845 = 2{,}141.4\ \text{kmol/day}$

**Inlet H₂S:** $0.03 \times 17{,}845 = 535.4\ \text{kmol/day}$

**CO₂ removed (95 %):** $0.95 \times 2{,}141.4 = 2{,}034.3\ \text{kmol/day}$

**H₂S removed (99 %):** $0.99 \times 535.4 = 530.0\ \text{kmol/day}$

**Total acid gas removed:** $2{,}034.3 + 530.0 = \mathbf{2{,}564.3\ \text{kmol/day}}$ ✅

**(ii) Minimum K₂CO₃ circulation:**

$$n_{\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3, \text{min}} = \frac{2{,}564.3}{0.32} = 8{,}013.4\ \text{kmol/day}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3} = 8{,}013.4 \times 138.21 = 1{,}107{,}531\ \text{kg/day} \approx \mathbf{1{,}107.5\ \text{tonnes/day}}$$

**Total solvent (28 wt%):**

$$\dot{m}_{\text{solvent}} = \frac{1{,}107.5}{0.28} = \mathbf{3{,}955.4\ \text{tonnes/day}}$$

**(iii) Reboiler heat duty:**

$$\dot{m} = \frac{3{,}955{,}400}{24 \times 3600} = 45.78\ \text{kg/s}$$

$$Q = 45.78 \times 3.85 \times 12 = \mathbf{2{,}115\ \text{kW}}$$

---

### ⭐ MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION B

#### Part (a) — Descriptive (7 marks)

**Chemistry (2 marks):** State the reversible reaction $\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons 2\text{KHCO}_3$ and the parallel H₂S reaction.

**Operating variables (2 marks):** Discuss temperature (200–250 °F), pressure (high), K₂CO₃ concentration (25–35 wt%), loading swing (0.3–0.4), and role of activators (DEA / piperazine).

**Comparison with amine process (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Hot Pot | Amine |
|-----------|---------|-------|
| Solvent cost | Low (inorganic) | Higher (organic) |
| Energy | Lower reboiler duty | Higher |
| Bulk CO₂ | **Preferred** | Adequate |
| Selectivity | Low H₂S/CO₂ | High (MDEA) |
| Corrosion | Severe (needs SS) | Mild |
| Degradation | None | MEA degrades |

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**(i) Molar absorption:**

$$\text{CO}_2 \text{ removed} = (0.09 - 0.004) \times 800 = 68.8\ \text{kmol/h}$$

$$\text{H}_2\text{S removed} = (0.02 - 0.00003) \times 800 \approx 15.976\ \text{kmol/h} \approx 16.0\ \text{kmol/h}$$

**(ii) Mass of acid gas:**

$$m_{\text{CO}_2} = 68.8 \times 44.01 = 3{,}028\ \text{kg/h}$$

$$m_{\text{H}_2\text{S}} = 16.0 \times 34.08 = 545.3\ \text{kg/h}$$

$$m_{\text{total}} = 3{,}573\ \text{kg/h} \approx \mathbf{3.57\ \text{tonnes/h}}$$

**(iii) Minimum K₂CO₃:**

Total moles absorbed = 68.8 + 16.0 = 84.8 kmol/h

$$n_{\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3, \text{min}} = \frac{84.8}{0.30} = 282.7\ \text{kmol/h}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3} = 282.7 \times 138.21 = 39{,}072\ \text{kg/h}$$

$$\dot{m}_{\text{solvent (28 wt\%)}} = \frac{39.07}{0.28} = \mathbf{139.5\ \text{tonnes/h}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Zones in Carbonate-Washing Problems — Read Carefully:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the loading swing (ΔL):** Many students divide CO₂ removed by **1.0** (i.e., assume 1 mol K₂CO₃ absorbs 1 mol CO₂ at the *instantaneous* stoichiometry). This is **wrong for design** — the correct approach is to use the **operating loading swing (0.25 – 0.40)**, which gives the *minimum* K₂CO₃ required in a continuously operating plant. **Loss: 2–3 marks.**
>
> 2. **Mixing up mass fractions and mole fractions:** K₂CO₃ concentration is given in **wt% (mass basis)**, not mol%. Use the formula $\dot{m}_{\text{solution}} = \dot{m}_{\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3} / w_{\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3}$. **Loss: 1 mark.**
>
> 3. **Ignoring both CO₂ and H₂S:** If a problem has both acid gases, **both must be summed** in the numerator when computing the K₂CO₃ requirement, because K₂CO₃ reacts with both. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
>
> 4. **Wrong STP molar volume:** Use **22.414 m³/kmol** for ideal-gas calculations at STP (0 °C, 1 atm). If the problem states NTP (20 °C, 1 atm), use **24.0 m³/kmol**. **Loss: 1 mark.**
>
> 5. **Forgetting the heat-of-reaction term in the reboiler energy balance:** A complete reboiler duty includes both **sensible heat** ($\dot{m} C_p \Delta T$) **and** the **endothermic bicarbonate decomposition** (~ +27 kcal/mol CO₂ released). In KTU 14-mark problems, the latent heat of water vaporization in the reboiler (~ 540 kcal/kg) is also often expected.
>
> 6. **Confusing "lean loading" with "rich loading":** Lean = solvent leaving stripper (low CO₂); rich = solvent leaving absorber (high CO₂). The swing ΔL = rich − lean.
>
> 7. **Not converting Sm³/day to kmol/day:** Numerical problems almost always require this conversion as the **first step**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🔥 EXAM-DAY RAPID REVISION — CARBONATE WASHING PROCESS 🔥**

- ✅ **Process name:** Hot Potassium Carbonate Process / Hot Pot / Benfield / Catacarb.
- ✅ **Solvent:** 25 – 35 wt% aqueous **K₂CO₃** + small (1–5 wt%) activator (DEA / piperazine / proprietary amine).
- ✅ **Core reaction:** $\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons 2\text{KHCO}_3$ (reversible).
- ✅ **H₂S reaction:** $\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{S} \rightarrow \text{KHS} + \text{KHCO}_3$.
- ✅ **Operating T:** 200 – 250 °F; **P:** 400 – 1500 psig (absorber); stripper at ~1 – 2 atm.
- ✅ **Why K over Na:** Higher solubility (35 wt% vs. 18 wt%) → less circulation → lower energy.
- ✅ **Why high T:** Prevents KHCO₃ precipitation; keeps reaction reversible.
- ✅ **Why high P:** Higher CO₂/H₂S partial pressure → higher loading → smaller equipment.
- ✅ **Major equipment flow (AFS-HL-PS):** **A**bsorber → **F**lash drum → **S**tripper → **H**eat exchanger → **L**ean pump → back to Absorber.
- ✅ **Key role of lean/rich HEX:** Recovers 40–60 % of reboiler heat duty.
- ✅ **Key role of reboiler:** Supplies sensible + latent + decomposition heat to regenerate solvent.
- ✅ **Loading swing (ΔL):** 0.25 – 0.40 mol CO₂/mol K₂CO₃ (use design value from problem statement).
- ✅ **Acid gas destination:** H₂S → Claus unit → elemental sulfur; CO₂ → vent / sequestration / EOR.
- ✅ **Heat of reaction:** ≈ **−27 kcal/mol CO₂** absorbed (exothermic forward; endothermic reverse).
- ✅ **Solvent advantages:** Cheap, non-volatile, non-degrading, environmentally benign.
- ✅ **Solvent disadvantages:** Corrosive (need SS 304/316 + V₂O₅ inhibitor), high pumping energy at scale, foaming possible.
- ✅ **Best application:** High-pressure, high-CO₂ bulk removal (e.g., EOR CO₂, offshore platforms, SNG plants, CCUS).
- ✅ **Formulae to memorize:**
   - $L = n_{\text{acid gas}} / n_{\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3}$
   - $\dot{m}_{\text{K}_2\text{CO}_3} = n_{\text{removed}} \times 138.21\ \text{kg/kmol} / \Delta L$
   - $Q_{\text{reboiler}} = \dot{m} C_p \Delta T$ (sensible) + latent + ΔH_rxn
   - Convers.% = (in − out) / in × 100
- ✅ **STP molar volume:** 22.414 m³/kmol (or 359 ft³/lb-mol at 60 °F, 14.7 psia).
- ✅ **Procurement tip — Benfield vs. Catacarb:** Benfield (UOP) typically uses proprietary amine activator; Catacarb (C.E. Lummus / Eickmeyer) uses borate buffer + amino acid activator. Both are fundamentally K₂CO₃-based.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Carbonate Washing Process**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🛢️ SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Module 3 of PEPOT528 unifies three industrially critical gas-processing pillars: (1) **Methanol-Based Processing** (hydrate mitigation & MTG chemistry), (2) **Sulphur Recovery** (Claus chemistry for acid-gas treatment), and (3) **Mechanical Gas Compression** (thermodynamics of moving gas to sales-line pressure). Mastery of all three is mandatory for KTU ESE and interview panels in ONGC, GAIL, IOCL, and Reliance.

---

## 1.1 Methanol-Based Process — Two Distinct Meanings in Gas Engineering

In the natural gas (NG) value chain, **methanol (CH₃OH)** plays **two very different industrial roles**, both heavily examined in KTU papers:

| Role | Industrial Application | Operating Temperature |
|---|---|---|
| **(a) Methanol Injection** | Thermodynamic **hydrate inhibitor** in subsea & cold-climate pipelines | **-40 °C to +5 °C** |
| **(b) Methanol-to-Gasoline (MTG)** | Conversion of stranded NG → liquid hydrocarbons via Mobil's ZSM-5 route | **300 °C – 400 °C** |

### (a) Methanol as a Hydrate Inhibitor — Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** *Methanol injection* is a **low-dosage chemical inhibition** technique in which liquid CH₃OH is dosed into wet natural-gas streams to **shift the hydrate equilibrium curve** toward lower temperatures and higher pressures, preventing the formation of ice-like crystalline clathrates (gas hydrates) that would otherwise plug flowlines and chokes.

**Intuition (Plain English + Analogy):** Imagine pouring **antifreeze** into your car's radiator during winter. Antifreeze doesn't remove the cold; it just **lowers the freezing point of the coolant** so ice crystals never form. Methanol does **exactly the same job** inside a subsea gas pipeline — it acts as "antifreeze for natural gas," disrupting the water-molecule cage (clathrate) that would otherwise crystallize around methane (CH₄) and ethane (C₂H₆) molecules. Without methanol, a deep-water wellhead at **4 °C and 80 bar** would shut down within hours due to hydrate plugs.

**Why methanol specifically?**
- Lowest molecular weight alcohol → **lowest viscosity at sub-zero temperatures**
- High water miscibility → distributes uniformly
- Cheap, recoverable, recyclable in a regeneration column
- **Boiling point = 64.7 °C**, **Density = 791 kg/m³** (at **20 °C**), **Flash point = 11 °C**

### (b) Methanol-to-Gasoline (MTG) Process — Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** *The Mobil MTG (Methanol-to-Gasoline) process* is a **shape-selective catalytic conversion** technology in which methanol — derived from synthesis gas (CO + H₂) obtained via steam-methane reforming (SMR) of natural gas — is first dehydrated to **dimethyl ether (DME)** and then oligomerized over the proprietary **ZSM-5 zeolite catalyst** to yield a hydrocarbon mixture predominantly in the **gasoline boiling range (C₅–C₁₁)**.

**Intuition:** Think of methanol as **Lego bricks of one color (CH₃OH)**. The ZSM-5 zeolite is a **molecular mould** with pores of exactly **0.55 nm** — wide enough to assemble Lego bricks into **medium-sized** hydrocarbons, but too narrow to make heavy waxes. The "Lego factory" (catalyst) only allows **gasoline-sized pieces** out the other end. This *shape-selectivity* is why MTG gives ~**85–90 % gasoline selectivity** with virtually no diesel or heavier fractions.

**Process Steps (Logical Sequence):**
1. Natural Gas → **Steam Methane Reformer (SMR)** → Synthesis Gas (CO + 2 H₂)
2. Syngas → **Methanol Synthesis Reactor (Cu/ZnO/Al₂O₃ catalyst, 50–100 bar, 220–260 °C)**
3. Methanol → **DME Formation** → **Hydrocarbon Pool** → Gasoline (in MTG reactor)

---

## 1.2 Sulphur Recovery Process — The Claus Process

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** *The Claus Process* is the dominant **industrial technology for recovering elemental sulphur (S)** from **hydrogen sulphide (H₂S)** contained in acid-gas streams produced by amine-treatment units (AGRU) of natural-gas sweetening operations. It combines a **thermal (sub-stoichiometric combustion) stage** followed by **1–3 catalytic (Claus) reactor stages**, achieving overall H₂S conversion efficiencies of **95–98 %** (standard 2-stage) and **99.5 %+** with tail-gas treatment (SCOT, Sulfreen).

**Intuition:** Imagine you have a **rotten-egg-smelling gas (H₂S)** coming out of a sweetening unit. You can't release it (toxic, lethal at **>100 ppm**, environmental hazard). What do you do? **Burn one-third of it carefully to make sulphur dioxide (SO₂)**, then let the remaining H₂S react with that SO₂ inside a hot catalyst bed to **grow pure yellow sulphur** like snowflakes on the catalyst surface, which you then melt and ship as bright-yellow pellets. That's the Claus process — a "**yellow-gold factory from a stinky gas**."

**Overall (Stoichiometric) Reaction:**
$$ \mathrm{H_2S \; + \; \tfrac{1}{2}\, O_2 \; \longrightarrow \; S \; + \; H_2O \quad \Delta H_{298} = -{}222 \; kJ/mol} $$

**Standard Recovery Efficiencies (must memorize for KTU):**
- **Single-stage Claus:** 75–90 %
- **Two-stage (1 thermal + 1 catalytic):** 90–95 %
- **Three-stage (1 thermal + 2 catalytic):** 95–97 %
- **Claus + Tail-Gas Treatment (TGTU):** **99.5 – 99.9 %**

---

## 1.3 Compression of Natural Gas — Mechanical Heart of Gas Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** *Natural gas compression* is the **mechanical raising of gas pressure** from a low-pressure source (wellhead, typically **20–70 bar**) to a high-pressure delivery pressure (transmission pipeline at **70–100 bar**; LNG let-down before liquefaction at **40–60 bar**; CNG dispensing at **200–250 bar**), accomplished using **prime movers (gas turbines, electric motors, diesel engines)** coupled to one of two fundamentally different classes of compressors.

**Intuition:** A compressor is the **"heart" of the gas transportation system**. The wellhead is the "lungs" (low-pressure source). The pipeline is the "artery" (high-pressure destination). The compressor pumps gas exactly as your heart pumps blood — at controlled rates (throughput, **Q in MMscmd**) and against a pressure differential (ΔP). If the heart fails, the gas stops flowing. In India, **GAIL's Hazira–Vijaipur–Jagdishpur (HVJ) pipeline** uses **30+ centrifugal compressor stations** to keep gas flowing from the Gujarat coast to the fertiliser belts of UP.

**Two Master Classes of Compressors (memorize this tree):**

| Class | Working Principle | Typical Pressure Ratio | Typical Capacity |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Positive Displacement (PD)** | Mechanical trapping + mechanical reduction of gas volume | **3 : 1 to 4 : 1 per stage** (multi-stage for higher) | **0.01 – 5 MMscmd** |
| **Dynamic (Rotodynamic)** | Continuous kinetic-energy conversion to pressure via impeller/blades | **1.05 : 1 to 1.4 : 1 per stage** (multi-stage) | **0.5 – 500 MMscmd** |

---

### ✅ Section 1 Self-Check (Quick Fire Facts)
1. **Methanol density** at 20 °C = **791 kg/m³**  
2. **ZSM-5 pore size** ≈ **0.55 nm**  
3. **Claus process** converts H₂S to elemental **Sulphur (S)**  
4. **ZSM-5** = Zeolite **S**econd **S**ynthesis **M**obile — **5** refers to pore size  
5. **CNG dispensing pressure** in India = **200–250 bar**

# ⚙️ SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Methanol Injection — Hammerschmidt Equation & Dosage Logic

The foundation of methanol injection engineering is the **Hammerschmidt Equation (1939)**, which quantifies the depression of hydrate-formation temperature as a function of methanol concentration in the **aqueous phase**:

$$ \Delta T \; = \; \dfrac{2337 \, X}{M \, (100 - X)} \quad \Big[\text{°F, when X in wt \%}\Big] $$

$$ \Delta T \; = \; \dfrac{1297 \, X}{M \, (100 - X)} \quad \Big[\text{°C, when X in wt \%}\Big] $$

Where:
- $\Delta T$ = **Hydrate temperature depression** (°F or °C)
- $X$ = Concentration of inhibitor in the **water phase** (weight %)
- $M$ = **Molecular weight** of inhibitor (CH₃OH = **32.04 g/mol**)
- 2337 / 1297 = empirical Hammerschmidt constants

### Engineering "Why" — Step Logic of Hydrate Inhibition
1. Hydrates form when **water molecules** self-assemble into cage structures around light hydrocarbons.
2. Alcohols (methanol, ethanol, MEG, DEG) act as **thermodynamic inhibitors** by occupying hydrogen-bonding sites in liquid water.
3. The Hammerschmidt equation is essentially a **freezing-point depression analog** (like salt on ice).
4. Required methanol concentration is **much lower** than for MEG because methanol is **smaller and more polar**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb (Industry):** For every **1 °C** of subcooling below the hydrate temperature, dose approximately **2–3 wt % methanol** in the water phase. For every **1 °F** subcooling, dose ≈ **1 wt % methanol**.

### Minimum Methanol Dosage — Nielsen–Bucklin Approximation

The required **lean methanol injection rate** (kg/hr) for a gas stream:

$$ m_{\mathrm{MeOH}} \; = \; \big[\, (M_w \, \cdot \, w) \; + \; (M_g \, \cdot \, X_{abs}) \,\big] \cdot Q_g \cdot \rho_g $$

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $M_w$ | mass flow of free water in gas | **kg / 10⁶ scf** |
| $w$ | methanol concentration in **lean** water | **wt fraction** |
| $M_g$ | mass of methanol lost in **vapour phase** | **kg / 10⁶ scf** |
| $X_{abs}$ | methanol absorbed per unit mass gas | **dimensionless** |
| $Q_g$ | gas flow rate | **MMscmd** |
| $\rho_g$ | gas density at injection conditions | **kg/m³** |

### Methanol Recovery / Regeneration

After injection, methanol is **recovered in a regenerator (distillation column)** operating at near-atmospheric pressure with steam reboiler:

$$ \text{Recovery efficiency} \; \eta_{\mathrm{MeOH}} \; = \; \dfrac{m_{\mathrm{out,distillate}}}{m_{\mathrm{in,rich}}} \;\times\; 100\% \quad \text{(typically 80–95\%)} $$

---

## 2.2 MTG Process — Chemistry, Reactor Kinetics & Material Balance

### Reaction Network (must memorize for 14-mark KTU Q)

**Stage 1 — Methanol → Dimethyl Ether (DME) — Endothermic:**
$$ 2 \, \mathrm{CH_3OH} \;\rightleftharpoons\; \mathrm{CH_3OCH_3} \; + \; \mathrm{H_2O} \quad \Delta H \;=\; -23\;\mathrm{kJ/mol} $$

**Stage 2 — Methanol + DME → Light Olefins:**
$$ \mathrm{CH_3OH / DME} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{C}_2^{=} \;+\; \text{C}_3^{=} \;+\; \text{C}_4^{=} \;+\; \mathrm{H_2O} $$

**Stage 3 — Olefins → Gasoline-range Hydrocarbons (C₅–C₁₁) on ZSM-5:**
$$ \text{C}_2^{=} + \text{C}_3^{=} + \text{C}_4^{=} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{C}_5–\text{C}_{11}\; \text{(gasoline)} \;+\; \mathrm{H_2O} $$

### Process Conditions (Memorize Table)

| Parameter | Value | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Catalyst** | ZSM-5 (SiO₂/Al₂O₃ ≈ 70) | Shape-selective; excludes C₁₂⁺ |
| **Reactor type** | Fixed-bed, adiabatic | Multi-tube design |
| **Inlet temperature** | **300 °C – 360 °C** | Optimised for C₅⁺ yield |
| **Pressure** | **1 – 2 atm (gauge)** | Avoid condensation of products |
| **WHSV** | **1 – 2 hr⁻¹** | Weight hourly space velocity |
| **Methanol conversion** | ≈ **100 %** | Single-pass |
| **C₅⁺ gasoline selectivity** | ≈ **85 %** | Branched, high-octane |
| **LPG (C₃–C₄) selectivity** | ≈ **10 – 14 %** | By-product |
| **RON of gasoline** | **92 – 95** | Excellent quality |

---

## 2.3 Claus Process — Detailed Stage-by-Stage Theory

### Stage A — Thermal (Reaction Furnace) — 1100–1300 °C
The acid-gas feed (containing **H₂S, CO₂, H₂O, NH₃, hydrocarbons**) is burned with a **carefully controlled, sub-stoichiometric** quantity of air such that exactly **⅓ of H₂S is combusted to SO₂**:

$$ \textbf{(R1)} \quad \mathrm{H_2S} \; + \; \tfrac{3}{2}\,\mathrm{O_2} \; \longrightarrow \; \mathrm{SO_2} \; + \; \mathrm{H_2O} \quad \Delta H = -518\;\mathrm{kJ/mol} $$

The remaining **⅔ of H₂S** reacts with the produced SO₂:

$$ \textbf{(R2)} \quad 2\,\mathrm{H_2S} \; + \; \mathrm{SO_2} \; \rightleftharpoons\; \tfrac{3}{x}\,\mathrm{S}_x \; + \; 2\,\mathrm{H_2O} $$

Where $S_x$ = a mixture of sulphur allotropes (predominantly **S₂**, S₆, S₈ at the prevailing temperature).

**Conversion in the thermal stage ≈ 60 – 70 %**.

### Stage B — Catalytic (Reactor Stages) — 200–350 °C
The cooled gas (now at **~180 °C**) passes through a **catalyst bed** of activated bauxite or alumina (modern plants use **TiO₂-promoted alumina**) where the **reverse-Claus reaction** continues at much lower temperatures:

$$ \textbf{(R3)} \quad 2\,\mathrm{H_2S} \; + \; \mathrm{SO_2} \; \longrightarrow\; \tfrac{3}{8}\,\mathrm{S_8} \; + \; 2\,\mathrm{H_2O} \quad \Delta H_{298} = -146.5\;\mathrm{kJ/mol} $$

**Catalytic Stage Conversions:**
- 1st catalytic stage → additional **20–25 %** (cumulative ≈ 90 %)
- 2nd catalytic stage → additional **3–5 %** (cumulative ≈ 95 %)
- 3rd catalytic stage → additional **1–2 %** (cumulative ≈ 97 %)

### Why multiple catalytic stages?

Because the Claus reaction is **equilibrium-limited**. Each stage is followed by a **condenser** that removes sulphur (driving equilibrium forward per **Le Chatelier's principle**) and a **reheater** (to keep gas above the sulphur dew point).

### Process Variants (KTU Favourite!)

| Variant | Acid-gas H₂S Concentration | When Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Partial Combustion (Once-Through)** | **> 50 mol %** H₂S | All thermal + catalytic |
| **Split-Flow** | **20–50 mol %** H₂S | Bypass some acid-gas to catalytic bed |
| **Direct Oxidation (ModClaus / SuperClaus)** | **< 20 mol %** H₂S | Selectively oxidizes residual H₂S |

---

## 2.4 Compression of Natural Gas — Full Thermodynamic Framework

### 2.4.1 Compression Ratio (most-quoted KTU parameter)

$$ \mathrm{R_c} \; = \; \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \; = \; \dfrac{\text{Discharge pressure (absolute)}}{\text{Suction pressure (absolute)}} $$

For a **multi-stage compressor** with $n$ stages and equal pressure ratio per stage:

$$ \mathrm{R_{c,stage}} \; = \; \big(\mathrm{R_c}\big)^{1/n} $$

### 2.4.2 Ideal Work / Power Equations

**A. Isothermal Compression (reversible, water-cooled, slow):**

$$ W_{\mathrm{iso}} \; = \; n \, R \, T_1 \, \ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) \quad [\mathrm{kJ/kmol}] $$

$$ P_{\mathrm{iso}} \; = \; \dfrac{\dot{n}\, R\, T_1 \, \ln(R_c)}{1000 \, \eta_{\mathrm{mech}}} \quad [\mathrm{kW}] $$

**B. Adiabatic / Isentropic Compression (no heat transfer):**

$$ W_{\mathrm{ad}} \; = \; n \, c_p \, T_1 \!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{k-1}{k}} \; - \; 1\right] \quad [\mathrm{kJ/kmol}] $$

$$ P_{\mathrm{ad}} \; = \; \dfrac{\dot{n}\, c_p \, T_1 \left[\mathrm{R_c}^{\tfrac{k-1}{k}} - 1\right]}{1000 \, \eta_{\mathrm{ad}} \, \eta_{\mathrm{mech}}} \quad [\mathrm{kW}] $$

**C. Polytropic Compression (real-world intermediate):**

$$ W_{\mathrm{poly}} \; = \; n \, c_{p,\mathrm{eff}} \, T_1 \!\left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{n-1}{n}} - 1\right] \quad \text{where } n = \text{polytropic index}, \; 1 < n < k $$

### 2.4.3 Efficiencies

| Efficiency | Definition | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|
| **Volumetric Efficiency, η_v** | (Actual volume of gas induced) / (Swept volume) | **70 – 90 %** for reciprocating; **95 %+** for centrifugal |
| **Isothermal Efficiency, η_iso** | $W_{\mathrm{iso}} / W_{\mathrm{actual}}$ | **50 – 70 %** |
| **Adiabatic Efficiency, η_ad** | $W_{\mathrm{ad}} / W_{\mathrm{actual}}$ | **70 – 85 %** (centrifugal) |
| **Mechanical Efficiency, η_mech** | Power to gas / Power to shaft | **95 – 98 %** |
| **Polytropic Efficiency, η_poly** | Real-polytropic-work / ideal-polytropic-work | **75 – 90 %** |

### 2.4.4 Volumetric Efficiency (Reciprocating Compressors)

$$ \eta_v \; = \; 1 \; + \; C \; - \; C \!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{1/k} $$

Where **C** = clearance ratio = $V_c / V_s$ (typically **0.03 – 0.10**).

### 2.4.5 Discharge Temperature (Critical Safety Parameter!)

For adiabatic compression:

$$ T_2 \; = \; T_1 \!\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\!\frac{k-1}{k}} \; = \; T_1 \cdot R_c^{\tfrac{k-1}{k}} $$

For methane $k = 1.31$. With $R_c = 4$: $T_2 = T_1 \times 1.355$. If $T_1 = 300\,K$, then $T_2 = 406\,K$ (**133 °C**).

---

|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hammerschmidt Eqn (°C) | $\Delta T = 1297 X / [M(100-X)]$ | °C | Methanol dose calculation |
