## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Ideal Gas Law from Kinetic Theory

The **Ideal Gas Law** is derived by combining **Boyle's Law** (P ∝ 1/V), **Charles' Law** (V ∝ T), and **Avogadro's Law** (V ∝ n):

**Step 1: Combine Boyle's and Charles' Laws**

$$V \propto \frac{T}{P} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V = k \cdot \frac{nT}{P}$$

where k is a proportionality constant.

**Step 2: Introduce the Universal Gas Constant R**

By definition, at **STP (14.7 psia, 60 °F)**, 1 lbmol of ideal gas occupies **379.4 ft³**. Substituting:

$$379.4 = k \cdot \frac{1 \cdot 520}{14.7}$$

$$k = \frac{379.4 \cdot 14.7}{520} = 10.7316 \approx 10.732 \, \frac{\text{psi} \cdot \text{ft}^{3}}{\text{lbmol} \cdot {}^{\circ}\text{R}}$$

**Step 3: Write the Final Ideal Gas Law**

$$PV = nRT$$

where **R = 10.732 psi·ft³/(lbmol·°R)** in field units.

### 3.2 Real Gas Law and Compressibility Factor

**Step 1: Start with the Ideal Gas Law**

$$PV = nRT$$

**Step 2: Correct for non-ideality by introducing Z**

By definition, Z is the ratio of the actual molar volume to the ideal molar volume:

$$Z = \frac{V_{actual}}{V_{ideal}} = \frac{V_{actual}}{RT/P}$$

**Step 3: Solve for V_actual and substitute**

$$V_{actual} = \frac{ZRT}{P}$$

**Step 4: Write the Real Gas Law (Compressed Form)**

$$PV = ZnRT$$

This is the **master equation** for all gas calculations in petroleum engineering.

### 3.3 Derivation of Kay's Mixing Rule for Pseudocritical Properties

For a gas mixture, we need a **pseudo-critical pressure (P_pc)** and **pseudo-critical temperature (T_pc)** to enter the Standing-Katz chart.

**Step 1: Define the pseudoreduced properties**

$$P_{pr} = \frac{P}{P_{pc}} \quad \text{and} \quad T_{pr} = \frac{T}{T_{pc}}$$

**Step 2: For a mixture, define the pseudocritical properties as the mole-fraction-weighted average of pure-component critical properties**

$$P_{pc} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_{i} \cdot P_{ci}$$

**Step 3: Similarly for temperature**

$$T_{pc} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} y_{i} \cdot T_{ci}$$

This is **Kay's Rule (1936)** — accurate for hydrocarbon gas mixtures with low non-hydrocarbon content.

### 3.4 Derivation of the Wobbe Index

The **Wobbe Index** measures the **interchangeability of fuel gases** when burned in the same burner.

**Step 1: Recall the burner energy equation**

The energy delivered by a burner at constant pressure is proportional to Q_HV / √(γ_g), where Q_HV is the volumetric heating value.

**Step 2: Define Wobbe Index**

$$W = \frac{H_{V,gross}}{\sqrt{\gamma_{g}}}$$

**Step 3: Derive from orifice flow equation**

For an orifice burner, the mass flow rate is:

$$\dot{m} \propto \sqrt{\frac{\Delta P \cdot \rho_{g}}{1}}$$

The heat release rate is:

$$Q = \dot{m} \cdot H_{V,mass} \propto \frac{H_{V,vol}}{\sqrt{\gamma_{g}}}$$

**Step 4: Final Wobbe Index**

$$W = \frac{H_{V,vol}}{\sqrt{\gamma_{g}}}$$

A higher Wobbe index means **more energy delivery per unit time** for the same burner setting.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Exam Style

**Problem:** A natural gas mixture has the following composition (mol%): CH₄ = 85%, C₂H₆ = 8%, C₃H₈ = 4%, n-C₄H₁₀ = 2%, CO₂ = 1%. Calculate:
**(a)** Apparent molecular weight
**(b)** Specific gravity
**(c)** Pseudocritical pressure and temperature (using Kay's Rule)

**Given Component Data:**

| **Component** | **M_i (lb/lbmol)** | **P_ci (psia)** | **T_ci (°R)** |
|---------------|---------------------|------------------|----------------|
| CH₄ | 16.04 | 666.4 | 343.0 |
| C₂H₆ | 30.07 | 706.5 | 549.4 |
| C₃H₈ | 44.10 | 616.0 | 665.6 |
| n-C₄H₁₀ | 58.12 | 550.6 | 765.2 |
| CO₂ | 44.01 | 1071.0 | 547.4 |

**Part (a): Apparent Molecular Weight**

$$M_{a} = \sum y_{i} M_{i}$$

**Step-by-step calculation:**

$$M_{a} = (0.85)(16.04) + (0.08)(30.07) + (0.04)(44.10) + (0.02)(58.12) + (0.01)(44.01)$$

Computing each term:

$$M_{CH_{4}} = 0.85 \times 16.04 = 13.6340$$

$$M_{C_{2}H_{6}} = 0.08 \times 30.07 = 2.4056$$

$$M_{C_{3}H_{8}} = 0.04 \times 44.10 = 1.7640$$

$$M_{C_{4}H_{10}} = 0.02 \times 58.12 = 1.1624$$

$$M_{CO_{2}} = 0.01 \times 44.01 = 0.4401$$

Summing all terms:

$$M_{a} = 13.6340 + 2.4056 + 1.7640 + 1.1624 + 0.4401 = 19.4061 \, \frac{\text{lb}}{\text{lbmol}}$$

$$\boxed{M_{a} \approx 19.41 \, \frac{\text{lb}}{\text{lbmol}}}$$

**Part (b): Specific Gravity**

$$\gamma_{g} = \frac{M_{a}}{M_{air}} = \frac{19.4061}{28.97} = 0.6699$$

$$\boxed{\gamma_{g} \approx 0.670}$$

**Part (c): Pseudocritical Properties (Kay's Rule)**

**Pseudocritical Pressure:**

$$P_{pc} = (0.85)(666.4) + (0.08)(706.5) + (0.04)(616.0) + (0.02)(550.6) + (0.01)(1071.0)$$

$$P_{pc} = 566.44 + 56.52 + 24.64 + 11.01 + 10.71 = 669.32 \, \text{psia}$$

$$\boxed{P_{pc} \approx 669.3 \, \text{psia}}$$

**Pseudocritical Temperature:**

$$T_{pc} = (0.85)(343.0) + (0.08)(549.4) + (0.04)(665.6) + (0.02)(765.2) + (0.01)(547.4)$$

$$T_{pc} = 291.55 + 43.95 + 26.62 + 15.30 + 5.47 = 382.90 \, {}^{\circ}\text{R}$$

$$\boxed{T_{pc} \approx 382.9 \, {}^{\circ}\text{R}}$$

### 3.6 Numerical Example — Z-Factor Calculation

**Problem:** For the gas above, calculate the Z-factor at **P = 2000 psia and T = 120 °F (580 °R)** using the **Standing-Katz Chart** approach, and then compute the **gas density**.

**Step 1: Calculate pseudoreduced properties**

$$P_{pr} = \frac{P}{P_{pc}} = \frac{2000}{669.3} = 2.988$$

$$T_{pr} = \frac{T}{T_{pc}} = \frac{580}{382.9} = 1.515$$

**Step 2: Read Z from Standing-Katz Chart**

At **P_pr = 2.99, T_pr = 1.515**, the Standing-Katz chart gives:

$$Z \approx 0.852$$

**Step 3: Calculate Gas Density**

$$\rho_{g} = \frac{P \cdot M_{a}}{Z \cdot R \cdot T}$$

Substituting values:

$$\rho_{g} = \frac{(2000)(19.41)}{(0.852)(10.732)(580)}$$

$$\rho_{g} = \frac{38820}{5303.36} = 7.32 \, \frac{\text{lb}}{\text{ft}^{3}}$$

$$\boxed{\rho_{g} \approx 7.32 \, \frac{\text{lb}}{\text{ft}^{3}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> For a typical KTU ESE, you will be given the Z-factor from the chart and asked to compute density, formation volume factor (B_g), or gas viscosity. The above is the standard procedure.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** Define the term "Specific Gravity of Natural Gas" and explain its significance in gas engineering calculations.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The specific gravity of a natural gas ($\gamma_{g}$) is defined as the **ratio of the density of the gas to the density of air**, both measured at the **same pressure and temperature conditions**.

**Mathematical Expression:**

$$\gamma_{g} = \frac{\rho_{gas}}{\rho_{air}}$$

Since at the same P and T, density is proportional to molecular weight:

$$\gamma_{g} = \frac{M_{gas}}{M_{air}} = \frac{M_{a}}{28.97}$$

**Significance:**
1. It is a **dimensionless quantity** used to identify gas composition.
2. Used in **Wobbe Index** calculation for burner interchangeability.
3. Used in **gas flow equations** (e.g., Weymouth, Panhandle) for pipeline design.
4. Used in **Orifice meter calculations** to compute gas flow rate.
5. Heavier gases ($\gamma_{g} > 1$) have **higher heating values** but flow less easily.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Typical $\gamma_{g}$ for natural gas ranges from **0.55 to 0.80** for methane-rich gases, and **0.8 to 1.5** for heavier hydrocarbon-rich gases.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** State and explain the **Real Gas Law**. Why is the compressibility factor (Z) necessary?

**Model Answer:**

**Real Gas Law:** The Real Gas Law is the modified form of the Ideal Gas Law that accounts for the **non-ideal behavior** of real gases at high pressures and low temperatures.

**Statement:**

$$PV = ZnRT$$

where:
- P = Pressure [psia]
- V = Volume [ft³]
- n = Number of moles [lbmol]
- R = Universal gas constant = 10.732 psi·ft³/(lbmol·°R)
- T = Temperature [°R]
- **Z = Compressibility factor (dimensionless)**

**Why Z is necessary:**

The Ideal Gas Law assumes that:
1. Gas molecules have **negligible volume** (point mass)
2. There are **no intermolecular forces**

In reality, at high pressures, gas molecules are close together and experience both **attractive (van der Waals) and repulsive forces**. The **Z-factor** corrects for these deviations:

- **Z = 1** → Ideal gas (very low P or very high T)
- **Z < 1** → Attractive forces dominate (gas is more compressible)
- **Z > 1** → Repulsive forces dominate (gas is less compressible)

**Practical Range:** For natural gas at typical reservoir conditions, **Z ranges from 0.7 to 1.3**.

> [!NOTE]
> Z-factor is essential for accurate **gas reserves estimation** (GIIP), **pipeline flow calculations**, and **compressor design**. Errors in Z lead to 10–30% errors in gas volume calculations.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks):** **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] A natural gas mixture has the following molar composition: **CH₄ = 80%, C₂H₆ = 10%, C₃H₈ = 5%, n-C₄H₁₀ = 3%, N₂ = 2%**. Compute:
1. Apparent molecular weight
2. Specific gravity
3. Pseudocritical pressure and temperature using **Kay's Mixing Rule**

**(b)** [7 Marks] Using the gas from part (a), determine the **gas density** at a reservoir pressure of **2500 psia** and temperature of **150 °F**. Take the Z-factor at these conditions as **0.92** (from the Standing-Katz chart). Also calculate the **gas formation volume factor (B_g)** in res ft³/scf.

**Given Component Data:**

| **Component** | **M_i** | **P_ci (psia)** | **T_ci (°R)** |
|---------------|---------|------------------|----------------|
| CH₄ | 16.04 | 666.4 | 343.0 |
| C₂H₆ | 30.07 | 706.5 | 549.4 |
| C₃H₈ | 44.10 | 616.0 | 665.6 |
| n-C₄H₁₀ | 58.12 | 550.6 | 765.2 |
| N₂ | 28.01 | 493.0 | 227.0 |

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**Step 1: Apparent Molecular Weight**

$$M_{a} = \sum y_{i} M_{i}$$

$$M_{a} = (0.80)(16.04) + (0.10)(30.07) + (0.05)(44.10) + (0.03)(58.12) + (0.02)(28.01)$$

Computing each term:

$$M_{a} = 12.832 + 3.007 + 2.205 + 1.744 + 0.560 = 20.348 \, \frac{\text{lb}}{\text{lbmol}}$$

$$\boxed{M_{a} = 20.35 \, \frac{\text{lb}}{\text{lbmol}}}$$

**Step 2: Specific Gravity**

$$\gamma_{g} = \frac{M_{a}}{28.97} = \frac{20.35}{28.97} = 0.7025$$

$$\boxed{\gamma_{g} = 0.703}$$

**Step 3: Pseudocritical Pressure (Kay's Rule)**

$$P_{pc} = (0.80)(666.4) + (0.10)(706.5) + (0.05)(616.0) + (0.03)(550.6) + (0.02)(493.0)$$

$$P_{pc} = 533.12 + 70.65 + 30.80 + 16.52 + 9.86 = 660.95 \, \text{psia}$$

$$\boxed{P_{pc} = 661.0 \, \text{psia}}$$

**Step 4: Pseudocritical Temperature (Kay's Rule)**

$$T_{pc} = (0.80)(343.0) + (0.10)(549.4) + (0.05)(665.6) + (0.03)(765.2) + (0.02)(227.0)$$

$$T_{pc} = 274.40 + 54.94 + 33.28 + 22.96 + 4.54 = 390.12 \, {}^{\circ}\text{R}$$

$$\boxed{T_{pc} = 390.1 \, {}^{\circ}\text{R}}$$

**Part (b):**

**Given:** P = 2500 psia, T = 150 °F = 610 °R, Z = 0.92, M_a = 20.35 lb/lbmol, R = 10.732

**Step 1: Gas Density Formula**

$$\rho_{g} = \frac{P \cdot M_{a}}{Z \cdot R \cdot T}$$

**Step 2: Substitute Values**

$$\rho_{g} = \frac{(2500)(20.35)}{(0.92)(10.732)(610)}$$

**Step 3: Compute Denominator**

$$(0.92)(10.732)(610) = 6022.46$$

**Step 4: Compute Density**

$$\rho_{g} = \frac{50875}{6022.46} = 8.448 \, \frac{\text{lb}}{\text{ft}^{3}}$$

$$\boxed{\rho_{g} = 8.45 \, \frac{\text{lb}}{\text{ft}^{3}}}$$

**Step 5: Gas Formation Volume Factor (B_g)**

$$B_{g} = 0.02827 \cdot \frac{ZT}{P}$$

**Step 6: Substitute Values**

$$B_{g} = 0.02827 \cdot \frac{(0.92)(610)}{2500}$$

$$B_{g} = 0.02827 \cdot \frac{561.2}{2500}$$

$$B_{g} = 0.02827 \cdot 0.22448 = 0.006346 \, \frac{\text{res ft}^{3}}{\text{scf}}$$

$$\boxed{B_{g} = 6.346 \times 10^{-3} \, \frac{\text{res ft}^{3}}{\text{scf}}}$$

**Step 7: Inverse Formation Volume Factor (1/B_g)**

$$\frac{1}{B_{g}} = \frac{1}{0.006346} = 157.58 \, \frac{\text{scf}}{\text{res ft}^{3}}$$

This means **1 reservoir cubic foot of gas at 2500 psia and 150 °F produces 157.58 scf at standard conditions**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Error** — Temperature MUST be in **°R (Rankine)** for field unit formulas. Many students write T = 150 and lose 2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to multiply by 0.02827** in B_g formula — this constant comes from the ratio (R_sc/R) = (10.732/379.4) and must be remembered or derived.
> 3. **Mixing up gas constant** — Use R = 10.732 in field units, not 8.314 (which is for SI/Joules).
> 4. **Kay's Rule application** — Some students forget to include the non-hydrocarbon component (N₂). Always include ALL components.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:** **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Define the **Wobbe Index**. A natural gas has the following composition: **CH₄ = 90%, C₂H₆ = 5%, C₃H₈ = 3%, CO₂ = 2%**. Compute:
1. Specific gravity
2. Gross heating value (use given component heating values)
3. Wobbe Index

**Given Heating Values:** CH₄ = 1012 Btu/scf, C₂H₆ = 1769 Btu/scf, C₃H₈ = 2517 Btu/scf, CO₂ = 0 Btu/scf

**(b)** [7 Marks] Explain with a neat diagram the **Standing-Katz Compressibility Factor Chart**. Discuss how the **pseudoreduced pressure (P_pr)** and **pseudoreduced temperature (T_pr)** are used to determine Z, and outline the **limitations of Kay's Rule** for sour gases (H₂S and CO₂ > 5%).

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**Step 1: Definition of Wobbe Index**

The **Wobbe Index (W)** is a measure of the **interchangeability of fuel gases** in combustion equipment. It represents the **amount of energy delivered** when gas is burned at a constant pressure drop through an orifice.

**Formula:**

$$W = \frac{HV_{gross}}{\sqrt{\gamma_{g}}}$$

**Step 2: Compute Apparent Molecular Weight**

$$M_{a} = (0.90)(16.04) + (0.05)(30.07) + (0.03)(44.10) + (0.02)(44.01)$$

$$M_{a} = 14.436 + 1.504 + 1.323 + 0.880 = 18.143 \, \frac{\text{lb}}{\text{lbmol}}$$

**Step 3: Specific Gravity**

$$\gamma_{g} = \frac{18.143}{28.97} = 0.6263$$

$$\boxed{\gamma_{g} = 0.626}$$

**Step 4: Gross Heating Value**

$$HV_{gross} = \sum y_{i} \cdot HV_{i}$$

$$HV_{gross} = (0.90)(1012) + (0.05)(1769) + (0.03)(2517) + (0.02)(0)$$

$$HV_{gross} = 910.8 + 88.45 + 75.51 + 0 = 1074.76 \, \frac{\text{Btu}}{\text{scf}}$$

$$\boxed{HV_{gross} = 1074.8 \, \frac{\text{Btu}}{\text{scf}}}$$

**Step 5: Wobbe Index**

$$W = \frac{1074.76}{\sqrt{0.6263}} = \frac{1074.76}{0.7914} = 1358.0 \, \frac{\text{Btu}}{\text{scf}}$$

$$\boxed{W = 1358 \, \frac{\text{Btu}}{\text{scf}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A Wobbe Index of **1358 Btu/scf** is within the typical range for **natural gas (1300–1400 Btu/scf)** used in domestic and industrial burners. This gas can be safely burned in standard natural gas appliances.

**Part (b):**

**Standing-Katz Compressibility Factor Chart:**

The Standing-Katz Chart (1942) is a **graphical correlation** of Z as a function of pseudoreduced pressure (P_pr) for various pseudoreduced temperature (T_pr) values.

**Procedure to Use:**
1. Calculate **P_pc and T_pc** using Kay's Mixing Rule
2. Calculate **P_pr = P/P_pc** and **T_pr = T/T_pc**
3. Enter the chart at the calculated **P_pr** and find the Z value at the **T_pr** curve

**Chart Description:**
- **X-axis:** Pseudoreduced pressure (P_pr) ranging from 0 to 15
- **Y-axis:** Compressibility factor Z, ranging from 0.4 to 1.5
- **Curves:** Each curve represents a constant T_pr value, from 1.05 (near critical) to 3.0 (high T)
- **T_pr = 1.0** is the saturation line; below it, two phases exist

**Limitations of Kay's Rule for Sour Gases:**

When the gas contains **significant H₂S and CO₂** (each > 5 mol%), the **critical properties of these acid gases** are very different from hydrocarbons, and Kay's rule **overestimates P_pc and underestimates T_pc**, leading to errors in Z.

**Correction Method — Wichert-Aziz (1972):**

For sour gases, a **correction factor ε** is added to T_pc before computing T_pr:

$$T^{'}_{pc} = T_{pc} - \epsilon$$

$$P^{'}_{pc} = \frac{P_{pc} \cdot T^{'}_{pc}}{T_{pc} + B(1 - B) \epsilon}$$

where:

$$\epsilon = \frac{66.67 (y_{H_{2}S} + y_{CO_{2}})^{0.9} - 11.53 (y_{H_{2}S} + y_{CO_{2}})^{1.6}}{3.0 + 18.0 (y_{H_{2}S} + y_{CO_{2}})}$$

and

$$B = \frac{y_{H_{2}S}}{y_{H_{2}S} + y_{CO_{2}}}$$

The corrected pseudoreduced properties are then used to read Z from the Standing-Katz chart.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Common Mistakes for Question B:**
> 1. **Forgetting that CO₂ has zero heating value** — some students incorrectly assign a heating value to CO₂.
> 2. **Using gross heating value (GHV)** with water as liquid product; some texts use net heating value (NHV) where water is vapor.
> 3. **Confusing Wobbe Index with Heating Value** — they are different quantities; Wobbe Index normalizes for specific gravity.
> 4. **Not explaining the Wobbe index physical significance** — it's about burner interchangeability, not just energy content.
> 5. **Forgetting the Wichert-Aziz correction** — required for any sour gas (H₂S > 5%) and CO₂ > 5%. KTU specifically tests this.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Fundamentals" Module 4 (KTU 2024)**

**1. Core Concepts to Memorize:**
- ✅ Natural gas = mixture of paraffinic hydrocarbons (C₁ to C₇₊) + non-HC impurities (H₂S, CO₂, N₂, He, H₂O)
- ✅ **Dry gas** = methane-rich; **Wet gas** = contains recoverable NGL (C₃+)
- ✅ **Sweet gas** = H₂S < 4 ppm; **Sour gas** = H₂S ≥ 4 ppm
- ✅ Gas standard conditions (US): **14.7 psia, 60 °F**; (SI): **101.325 kPa, 15 °C**
- ✅ M_air = **28.97 lb/lbmol**

**2. Critical Formulas to Remember:**
- ✅ $\gamma_{g} = M_{a} / 28.97$ (Specific Gravity)
- ✅ $M_{a} = \sum y_{i} M_{i}$ (Apparent MW)
- ✅ $PV = ZnRT$ (Real Gas Law)
- ✅ $\rho_{g} = PM_{a} / ZRT$ (Gas Density)
- ✅ $P_{pc} = \sum y_{i} P_{ci}$ (Kay's Rule — P_pc)
- ✅ $T_{pc} = \sum y_{i} T_{ci}$ (Kay's Rule — T_pc)
- ✅ $P_{pr} = P / P_{pc}$ and $T_{pr} = T / T_{pc}$
- ✅ $W = HV_{gross} / \sqrt{\gamma_{g}}$ (Wobbe Index)
- ✅ $B_{g} = 0.02827 ZT / P$ (Gas Formation Volume Factor)

**3. Z-Factor Rules:**
- ✅ Z = 1 → Ideal gas
- ✅ Z < 1 → Attractive forces dominant (mid-range P)
- ✅ Z > 1 → Repulsive forces dominant (very high P)
- ✅ Typical range: **0.7 to 1.3** for natural gas

**4. Special Cases:**
- ✅ **Wichert-Aziz correction** is needed for sour gas (H₂S, CO₂ > 5%)
- ✅ **Standing-Katz Chart** is the standard for Z-factor lookup
- ✅ **Apparent molecular weight** is used in ALL gas property calculations

**5. KTU Exam-Specific Tips:**
- ✅ Always convert **T to °R** (T_°R = T_°F + 460)
- ✅ Use **R = 10.732** in field units (NOT 8.314)
- ✅ Use the correct **0.02827** constant in B_g formula
- ✅ For ideal gas assumption in KTU problems, Z = 1 and B_g = 0.02827 × T/P

**6. Common Pitfalls:**
- ❌ Forgetting the **sum of mole fractions = 1.0** (validation step)
- ❌ Using **boiling point** instead of **molecular weight** for specific gravity
- ❌ Confusing **dry/wet** with **lean/rich** (they're different classifications!)
- ❌ Forgetting that **CO₂ has zero heating value**
- ❌ Mixing up **formation volume factor (B_g)** with **inverse formation volume factor (1/B_g)**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Methods of Measurements
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT528 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Methods of Measurements

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Oil and Natural Gas Engineering**, **Methods of Measurements** refers to the systematic set of physical, chemical, and electronic techniques employed to quantify the critical process and custody variables of hydrocarbon streams — namely **flow rate (Q)**, **pressure (P)**, **temperature (T)**, **fluid level (H)**, and **fluid composition (xᵢ)** — across the entire upstream, midstream, and downstream value chain. These methods are governed by industry standards such as **API MPMS (Manual of Petroleum Measurement Standards)**, **AGA (American Gas Association) Reports**, and **ISO 5167**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim)**
> *Methods of Measurements* in Oil & Gas Engineering comprise the principles, instruments, and standard procedures used for determining the quantity and quality of produced, transported, and processed hydrocarbons, including crude oil, natural gas, refined products, and multi-phase mixtures.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Fuel Station" Analogy

Imagine pulling into a fuel station. The nozzle fills your tank, and a digital display on the pump shows **₹500.00 = 35.7 Litres**. Three measurements just happened in real time:
- The **flow meter** (inside the pump) counted litres by sensing the velocity of gasoline.
- The **pressure transducer** ensured fuel was being delivered at safe pipeline pressure (~3 bar).
- The **temperature sensor** corrected the volume to **15 °C standard conditions** (since fuel expands when warm).

Behind the scenes, **custody transfer** is occurring — the station is *buying* volume from the depot, and accuracy down to **±0.15 %** determines who pays whom. This is the heartbeat of oil & gas economics, and it all depends on the *Methods of Measurements* you are about to study.

## 1.3 Hierarchy of Measurements in Oil & Gas

> [!NOTE]
> **The Five Pillars of Oil & Gas Measurement (PGM-Model)**

1. **P — Pressure Measurement** → Bourdon tubes, diaphragm gauges, strain-gauge transducers.
2. **Q — Flow Rate Measurement** → Orifice, turbine, ultrasonic, Coriolis, vortex, positive displacement (PD) meters.
3. **T — Temperature Measurement** → RTDs, thermocouples, thermistors, infrared pyrometers.
4. **M — Mass / Density Measurement** → Coriolis meters, nuclear densitometers.
5. **C — Composition / Quality Measurement** → Gas chromatographs, BS&W analyzers, water-cut meters.

## 1.4 Why These Measurements Matter (KTU Industry Context)

- **Royalty & Custody Transfer** — Determines the exact hydrocarbon volume transferred between parties; accuracy has direct monetary impact (₹/$).
- **Reservoir Management** — Pressure-temperature-volume (PVT) data drives reserve estimation and decline-curve analysis.
- **Process Safety** — High-pressure alarms and emergency shutdowns (ESD) depend on accurate transducer data.
- **Fiscal Metering** — Required by governments for taxation of produced hydrocarbons.

## 1.5 Core Physical Constants Used Throughout

| Symbol | Quantity | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| $g_c$ | Gravitational constant (Newton's-law factor) | **9.80665 m/s²** |
| $R$ | Universal gas constant | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| $P_{sc}$ | Standard pressure (API/ISO) | **101.325 kPa (14.696 psia)** |
| $T_{sc}$ | Standard temperature (API) | **15.56 °C = 288.71 K (60 °F)** |
| $Z_{sc}$ | Compressibility at standard conditions | **≈ 1.000 for ideal reference** |
| $\rho_{air}$ | Air density at NTP | **1.225 kg/m³** |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always specify the *standard conditions* before quoting any flow rate. Different contracts use **NTP (0 °C, 101.325 kPa)**, **STP (15 °C, 101.325 kPa)**, or **API (15.56 °C, 101.325 kPa)**. Marks are routinely lost in viva when units are ambiguous.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Measurement Methods (Master Framework)

The discipline of oil & gas measurement is best understood as a **three-layer hierarchy**:

- **Layer 1 — Direct Methods (Primary Sensing)**
  Convert the physical variable into a mechanical or electrical signal at the point of measurement.
  *Examples:* Orifice plate pressure taps, RTD resistance change, turbine blade rotation.

- **Layer 2 — Indirect / Inferential Methods**
  Use a *known physical relationship* to infer the desired variable from a directly measured one.
  *Examples:* Using differential pressure (ΔP) to infer flow rate via Bernoulli's equation.

- **Layer 3 — Computed / Inferential Corrected Methods (Flow Computers)**
  Apply real-time **PTV (Pressure–Temperature–Volume)** correction using AGA-8, API-2540, or ISO 12213 equations of state to obtain standard-condition volumes.

## 2.2 Detailed Methods — Flow Measurement (KTU High-Yield)

### 2.2.1 Differential-Pressure (DP) Meters

#### (a) Orifice Meter
A concentric, sharp-edged plate with a precisely machined bore (β = d/D). Flow causes a measurable pressure drop. The flow rate equation is derived from Bernoulli + continuity:

$$
Q = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot \Delta P}{\rho \cdot (1 - \beta^4)}}
$$

where $C_d$ is the **discharge coefficient** (typically 0.6–0.65 for a standard orifice), $A_2$ is the throat area, $\beta = d/D$ is the diameter ratio, and $\rho$ is fluid density.

#### (b) Venturi Meter
A converging–throat–diverging tube. Lower permanent pressure loss (≈ 10–15 %) compared to orifice plates. Discharge coefficient $C_d \approx 0.95$–$0.99$.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Recall Hook:** *Orifice = cheap, high pressure loss (40–60 %). Venturi = expensive, low pressure loss, ideal for gas pipelines.*

#### (c) Flow Nozzle & Dall Tube
Used in high-velocity, erosive service where orifice erosion is a concern.

### 2.2.2 Velocity Meters

- **Turbine Meter:** Fluid impinges on rotor blades; pulse frequency $f \propto v$. Accuracy ±0.25 %, common in crude oil custody transfer.
- **Vortex-Shedding Meter:** Bluff body sheds Kármán vortices; shedding frequency $f = St \cdot v/d$. Used for steam and gas.
- **Ultrasonic Meter (USM):** Transit-time of acoustic pulses measured along multiple paths. Multi-path USMs (±0.5–0.7 %) are the **gold standard for custody-transfer gas measurement** per **AGA-9**.

### 2.2.3 Mass Flow Meters

- **Coriolis Meter:** U-shaped tubes oscillate; mass flow induces tube *twist* proportional to ṁ. Density is also inferred from the tube's natural frequency. Accuracy ±0.05–0.1 %. **Preferred for crude, NGL, and liquid custody transfer.**
- **Thermal Mass Flow Meter:** Heat transfer rate $\propto \dot{m} c_p$. Used for low-flow gas metering.

### 2.2.4 Positive Displacement (PD) Meters
Geared chambers capture and release fixed fluid volumes. **Proving-tanked** and used in retail fuel and gas distribution.

## 2.3 Methods — Pressure, Temperature, Level, Composition

| Variable | Common Methods | KTU-Significant Detail |
|---|---|---|
| **Pressure** | Bourdon tube, diaphragm, strain-gauge, dead-weight tester | Dead-weight tester is the **primary standard** |
| **Temperature** | RTD (Pt100), Type K/J thermocouple, thermistor, IR pyrometer | Pt100 accuracy ≈ ±0.1 °C; use **Class A** for custody |
| **Level** | Float, capacitance, guided-wave radar (GWR), differential pressure (dP cell) | GWR unaffected by foam, vapors, turbulence |
| **Composition** | Gas chromatography, NIR, BS&W centrifuge, water-cut probe | BS&W = Basic Sediment & Water (API-2543 method) |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready Table)

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho (1-\beta^4)}}$ | Orifice mass flow | m³/s |
| 2 | $\dot{m} = C_d \dfrac{A_2}{\sqrt{1-\beta^4}} \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}$ | Compressible DP mass flow | kg/s |
| 3 | $Q_{sc} = Q_b \cdot \dfrac{P_b}{P_{sc}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{sc}}{T_b} \cdot \dfrac{Z_{sc}}{Z_b}$ | Gas volume conversion (real gas law) | Sm³/day |
| 4 | $Q_{sc} = Q_{b} \cdot \text{CTL} \cdot \text{CTPL} \cdot \text{CSW}$ | API-2540 liquid volume correction (Crude) | bbl @ 60 °F |
| 5 | $\text{CTL} = \text{VCF} = \dfrac{V_{60}}{V_T} = e^{-\alpha \Delta T (1 + 0.8 \alpha \Delta T)}$ | VCF for crude oil (API Table 54) | dimensionless |
| 6 | $K = \dfrac{\Delta P}{(0.5) \rho v^2}$ | Meter body K-factor (loss coefficient) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $v = f \cdot K_{\text{factor}}$ | Turbine linear velocity | m/s |
| 8 | $\dot{m} = \rho A v$ | Mass flow (general continuity) | kg/s |
| 9 | $\text{Reynolds No.} = \text{Re} = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | Flow regime indicator (laminar / turbulent) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $f_0 = \text{St} \cdot \dfrac{v}{d}$ | Vortex shedding frequency (Kármán) | Hz |
| 11 | $P V = Z n R T$ | Real-gas equation of state (Compressibility Z) | — |
| 12 | $\rho_{\text{api}} = \dfrac{141.5}{\text{API} + 131.5} \times 1000$ | S.G. from °API (kg/m³) | kg/m³ |
| 13 | $\text{Meter Factor (MF)} = \dfrac{V_{\text{prover}}}{V_{\text{indicated}}}$ | Proving correction (API-4) | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Exam Rule:** When solving any flow problem, ALWAYS state the assumption (incompressible vs. compressible, isothermal vs. adiabatic) BEFORE substituting values. KTU examiners award zero credit for implicit assumptions.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Custody Transfer at LNG Terminals:** Coriolis + multi-path USM, calibrated per **API-4.8** and **AGA-9**. Theft or inaccuracy of 0.1 % on a 1 Mt/year LNG cargo = ~₹8 Crore/year in dispute potential.
- **Wellhead Measurement:** Multiphase flowmeters (MPFM) blend Venturi + gamma densitometer + cross-correlation to give oil, water, and gas rates *without* a test separator.
- **Pipeline SCADA:** Pressure + temperature transmitters feed flow computers running AGA-8 for real-time gas mass balance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Orifice-Meter Flow Equation (Incompressible Case)

### 3.1.1 Starting Principle — Conservation of Energy and Mass

**Given:** A horizontal pipe (no elevation change) of diameter $D$ carrying a steady, incompressible fluid through a sharp-edged orifice plate with a bore of diameter $d$. Two pressure taps are located at upstream section 1 (inlet) and downstream section 2 (vena contracta, just past the orifice).

**Step 1 — Continuity Equation (Conservation of Mass)**

$$
A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2
$$

**Explanation:** The mass entering per second must equal the mass leaving per second. Because the fluid is incompressible ($\rho = \text{const}$), the volumetric flow rate is conserved across any two cross-sections. Hence the average velocity is inversely proportional to the cross-sectional area.

**Step 2 — Bernoulli's Equation (Conservation of Energy)**

$$
P_1 + \frac{1}{2} \rho v_1^2 = P_2 + \frac{1}{2} \rho v_2^2
$$

**Explanation:** Along a streamline, in steady, frictionless, incompressible flow, the sum of static pressure and dynamic (kinetic) pressure is constant. We assume the orifice is *ideal* (no losses) for now; losses are folded into the discharge coefficient $C_d$ later.

**Step 3 — Rearrange Bernoulli to Find $\Delta P$**

$$
P_1 - P_2 = \frac{1}{2} \rho (v_2^2 - v_1^2)
$$

Substitute $v_1 = A_2 v_2 / A_1$ (from continuity):

$$
P_1 - P_2 = \frac{1}{2} \rho v_2^2 \left[ 1 - \left( \dfrac{A_2}{A_1} \right)^2 \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Define Diameter Ratio $\beta$ and Express Area Ratio**

$$
\beta = \dfrac{d}{D}, \qquad \dfrac{A_2}{A_1} = \dfrac{\pi d^2/4}{\pi D^2/4} = \beta^2
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for Throat Velocity $v_2$**

$$
v_2 = \sqrt{ \dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho (1 - \beta^4)} }
$$

**Step 6 — Convert Velocity to Volume Flow Rate**

$$
Q_{\text{ideal}} = A_2 v_2 = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \sqrt{ \dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho (1 - \beta^4)} }
$$

**Step 7 — Introduce the Discharge Coefficient $C_d$**

The ideal (theoretical) flow rate overestimates the actual flow because of:
- *Vena-contracta* effect (the flow contracts to a cross-section smaller than the bore).
- *Frictional* and *turbulence* losses at the plate edge.

$$
Q_{\text{actual}} = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{ \dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho (1 - \beta^4)} }
$$

**Step 8 — Note on $C_d$**

$C_d$ is *empirically* determined as a function of Reynolds number, $\beta$, and tap location (flange, D-D/2, corner). For flange taps with $\beta = 0.5$ and $Re = 10^5$, $C_d \approx 0.625$ per **ISO 5167-2**.

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\, Q = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot \sqrt{ \dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho (1 - \beta^4)} } \,}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Real-Gas Volume Conversion to Standard Conditions

### 3.2.1 Starting Principle — Real-Gas Law

For $n$ moles of gas at base conditions (subscript $b$) and standard conditions (subscript $sc$):

$$
P_b V_b = Z_b n R T_b, \qquad P_{sc} V_{sc} = Z_{sc} n R T_{sc}
$$

**Step 1 — Divide the Two Equations to Eliminate $nR$**

$$
\dfrac{P_b V_b}{P_{sc} V_{sc}} = \dfrac{Z_b T_b}{Z_{sc} T_{sc}}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $V_{sc}$ (Standard Volume)**

$$
V_{sc} = V_b \cdot \dfrac{P_b}{P_{sc}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{sc}}{T_b} \cdot \dfrac{Z_{sc}}{Z_b}
$$

**Step 3 — Special Note: When $Z_{sc} \approx 1.000$** (typical at 1 atm, 15.56 °C, low pressure), the expression simplifies to:

$$
V_{sc} \approx V_b \cdot \dfrac{P_b}{P_{sc}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{sc}}{T_b \cdot Z_b}
$$

This is the **supercompressibility-corrected** form used in **AGA-3 / API-14.3** orifice gas measurement.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Choice of Meter vs. Application (KTU Decision Table)

| Application | Recommended Primary Meter | Accuracy Class | Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Gas custody transfer (pipeline)** | Multi-path Ultrasonic (USM) | ±0.5 % | AGA-9 |
| **Gas custody transfer (legacy)** | Turbine + densitometer | ±1.0 % | AGA-7 |
| **Crude oil custody transfer** | Coriolis + Prover | ±0.05 % | API-4.8 |
| **Refined product pipeline** | Turbine / Coriolis | ±0.15 % | API-4.6 |
| **LNG cargo loading** | Coriolis (4-path) | ±0.1 % | API-4.8 |
| **Wellhead multiphase (no separator)** | Multiphase Flowmeter (Venturi + gamma) | ±5–10 % wet | Industry proprietary |
| **Steam distribution** | Vortex shedding | ±1.0 % | ISO 12764 |
| **Air / inert gas low-flow** | Thermal mass flow | ±1.0 % | — |
| **Wellhead P/T monitoring** | Strain-gauge / RTD | ±0.1 % FS | API-21.1 |
| **Tank farm level** | Guided-wave radar | ±2 mm | API-3.1B |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example (KTU-Standard, 14-mark pattern)

**Problem:** A natural gas pipeline of diameter $D = 0.5$ m has an orifice plate with $\beta = 0.6$. Differential pressure $\Delta P = 25$ kPa. Static gauge pressure $P_b = 1.0$ MPa. Temperature $T_b = 40$ °C. Gas density at line conditions $\rho = 8.5$ kg/m³. Take $C_d = 0.62$. Compressibility $Z_b = 0.95$. Compute:
1. Mass flow rate in kg/s
2. Standard volumetric flow rate in Sm³/h

### Solution

**Step 1 — Throat area**
$$
A_2 = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.5 \cdot 0.6)^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.30)^2}{4} = 0.0707 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Velocity term**
$$
\sqrt{ \dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho (1-\beta^4)} } = \sqrt{ \dfrac{2 \cdot 25000}{8.5 (1 - 0.6^4)} } = \sqrt{ \dfrac{50000}{8.5 \cdot (1 - 0.1296)} } = \sqrt{ \dfrac{50000}{7.398} }
$$

$$
= \sqrt{6758.6} = 82.21 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Volumetric flow at base conditions**
$$
Q_b = C_d \cdot A_2 \cdot v = 0.62 \cdot 0.0707 \cdot 82.21 = 3.603 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Mass flow rate**
$$
\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q_b = 8.5 \cdot 3.603 = 30.63 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Standard volumetric flow (real gas)**

Convert to absolute: $P_b = 1.0$ MPa + 0.1013 MPa = 1.1013 MPa.
$T_b = 40 + 273.15 = 313.15$ K.
$T_{sc} = 15.56 + 273.15 = 288.71$ K.
$P_{sc} = 101.325$ kPa = 0.101325 MPa.

$$
Q_{sc} = 3.603 \cdot \dfrac{1.1013}{0.1013} \cdot \dfrac{288.71}{313.15} \cdot \dfrac{1.000}{0.95}
$$

$$
= 3.603 \cdot 10.872 \cdot 0.9220 \cdot 1.0526
$$

$$
Q_{sc} = 37.95 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 37.95 \cdot 3600 = 136{,}620 \text{ Sm}^3/\text{h}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Insight:** The factor $P_b/P_{sc}$ typically ranges from 10–100 for gas pipelines. This means *small orifice ΔP errors are amplified* when converted to standard volume. Hence high-accuracy pressure transmitters (±0.05 %) are mandatory in gas custody measurement.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*Define the term "Methods of Measurements" as applied in Oil and Natural Gas Engineering. List any **four** primary process variables that must be measured in crude oil custody transfer.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
*Methods of Measurements* in Oil and Natural Gas Engineering refer to the standardized techniques, instruments, and computational procedures used to quantify flow rate, pressure, temperature, level, and composition of hydrocarbon streams at various points across the upstream, midstream, and downstream value chain.

**Four primary variables in crude custody transfer:**
1. **Volume flow rate (Q)** — measured by turbine or Coriolis meter and proved by a pipe prover.
2. **Temperature (T)** — measured by a calibrated **Pt100 RTD** in a thermowell.
3. **Pressure (P)** — measured by a **strain-gauge pressure transmitter**.
4. **Density / Mass (ρ or ṁ)** — measured by **Coriolis densitometer** or nuclear densitometer.

*(1 mark definition + ½ mark per variable listed = 3 marks total)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of an **orifice meter**. State the governing equation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Principle:** A thin, concentric, sharp-edged plate (the orifice) is installed in a pipe. The bore ($d$) is smaller than the pipe ($D$). As fluid flows through, it accelerates at the throat and the static pressure drops. The pressure drop $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$ is measured by a differential-pressure cell. By Bernoulli's equation, $\Delta P$ is a unique function of flow velocity, and hence flow rate.

**Governing equation:**

$$
Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{ \dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho (1 - \beta^4)} }, \quad \text{where } \beta = d/D
$$

*(1 mark principle + 1 mark sketch description + 1 mark equation = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (Choice 1) — Orifice and Venturi Comparison + Numerical** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
*Compare the construction, working, and performance of an **Orifice meter** and a **Venturi meter**. State **at least five** points of comparison.*

**Model Solution:**

| # | Feature | Orifice Meter | Venturi Meter |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Construction | Simple flat plate with bore | Converging-throat-diverging tube (machined) |
| 2 | Cost | Low (≈ 1/3 of Venturi) | High (precision casting) |
| 3 | Pressure loss | **40–60 %** of ΔP recovered as loss | Only **10–15 %** permanent loss |
| 4 | Accuracy | ±1–2 % without calibration; ±0.5 % calibrated | ±0.5–1 % (inherently better) |
| 5 | $C_d$ value | 0.6–0.65 | 0.95–0.99 |
| 6 | Erosion / wear | High (sharp edge erodes) | Low (smooth contours) |
| 7 | Best for | Inexpensive, low-maintenance custody | High-pressure gas mains, large lines |
| 8 | Replacement ease | Quick (plate swap) | Complex (entire tube) |

*(5 points × 1 mark + 2 marks for engineering context = 7 marks)*

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
*A horizontal pipe of diameter $D = 0.4$ m carries oil (S.G. = 0.85). An orifice plate has $\beta = 0.5$. The differential pressure observed is $\Delta P = 50$ kPa. Take $C_d = 0.625$. Calculate: (i) the mass flow rate, and (ii) the volume flow rate per hour.*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $D = 0.4$ m → $d = 0.5 \cdot 0.4 = 0.2$ m, $\Delta P = 50{,}000$ Pa, $\rho = 850$ kg/m³.

**Step 1 — Throat area**
$$
A_2 = \dfrac{\pi (0.2)^2}{4} = 0.03142 \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Velocity factor**
$$
\sqrt{ \dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho (1 - \beta^4)} } = \sqrt{ \dfrac{2 \cdot 50000}{850 \cdot (1 - 0.0625)} } = \sqrt{ \dfrac{100000}{797.5} } = \sqrt{125.39} = 11.197 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Volumetric flow**
$$
Q = 0.625 \cdot 0.03142 \cdot 11.197 = 0.2199 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Hourly volume**
$$
Q_h = 0.2199 \cdot 3600 = 791.6 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}
$$

**Step 5 — Mass flow**
$$
\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q = 850 \cdot 0.2199 = 186.9 \text{ kg/s} = 6.73 \times 10^5 \text{ kg/h}
$$

*(1 mark each for sub-steps + 2 marks for final values = 7 marks)*

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — Real-Gas Conversion + Multiphase Measurement** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
*Derive the **real-gas volume-conversion equation** used to convert base-condition gas flow to standard-condition gas flow. State the assumptions clearly.*

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions:**
1. Gas behaves as a real fluid described by $PV = ZnRT$.
2. Number of moles $n$ is conserved (steady flow, no phase change).
3. Standard conditions are fixed at $P_{sc} = 101.325$ kPa, $T_{sc} = 288.71$ K.

**Derivation:**

Write the real-gas law at base and standard conditions:
$$
P_b V_b = Z_b n R T_b, \quad P_{sc} V_{sc} = Z_{sc} n R T_{sc}
$$

Divide to eliminate $nR$:
$$
\dfrac{V_{sc}}{V_b} = \dfrac{P_b}{P_{sc}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{sc}}{T_b} \cdot \dfrac{Z_{sc}}{Z_b}
$$

Hence:
$$
\boxed{\, V_{sc} = V_b \cdot \dfrac{P_b}{P_{sc}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{sc}}{T_b} \cdot \dfrac{Z_{sc}}{Z_b} \,}
$$

If $Z_{sc} \approx 1$ (true at 1 atm and 15.56 °C for most natural gases), the term drops out:
$$
V_{sc} = V_b \cdot \dfrac{P_b}{P_{sc}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{sc}}{T_b \cdot Z_b}
$$

This is the **AGA-3 supercompressibility correction** used in gas orifice metering.

*(3 marks assumptions + 4 marks derivation = 7 marks)*

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
*Discuss the **measurement methods used for multiphase flow** in oil & gas production. Explain how a modern Multiphase Flowmeter (MPFM) operates without using a test separator.*

**Model Solution:**

**Methods for Multiphase Measurement:**
1. **Test Separator Method (Traditional):** Oil, gas, and water are separated gravitationally. Each phase measured by its own dedicated meter. Disadvantage: large footprint, slow (15–30 min), capital-intensive.
2. **Multiphase Flowmeter (MPFM) — Modern:**
   - **Venturi Section:** Measures total mass flow via $\Delta P$.
   - **Gamma Densitometer:** Measures *bulk* density (ρ_mix) using a Cs-137 source and scintillation detector.
   - **Cross-Correlation Sensors (Electrical Microwave / Capacitance):** Measure velocity of *individual phases* using local concentration fluctuations.
3. **Sample-Capture Methods:** Slip-stream sampling with on-line BS&W and gas chromatograph.

**Working of MPFM without a separator:**
A modern MPFM combines three measurements in a single wetted section:
- The **Venturi** gives total mass flow rate $\dot{m}_{mix}$.
- The **gamma densitometer** gives mixture density $\rho_{mix}$.
- The **cross-correlation sensors** give phase velocities ($v_o, v_w, v_g$) and phase fractions ($\alpha_o, \alpha_w, \alpha_g$).

Using the closure relations:
$$
\dot{m}_i = \rho_i \cdot \alpha_i \cdot v_i \cdot A, \quad \text{where } i \in \{oil, water, gas\}
$$
$$
\sum_i \alpha_i = 1
$$

The phase flow rates are solved simultaneously from these equations *in real time*, eliminating the need for a test separator. Typical MPFM accuracies are ±5–10 % WLR (water–liquid ratio) which is acceptable for reservoir-management and allocation, though *not* for fiscal transfer.

*(2 marks for methods list + 3 marks for MPFM operation + 2 marks for phase-fraction equations = 7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Standard-condition confusion:** Quoting "1000 m³/day" *without* stating the standard P and T. Always specify the contract basis (e.g., "Sm³/h at 15.56 °C, 101.325 kPa"). **−1 to −2 marks**.
> 2. **Using $Z = 1$ for high-pressure gas:** At 70 bar, $Z$ can be 0.85 or lower. Ignoring it under-predicts flow by 15 %+. **−2 marks**.
> 3. **Forgetting the $(1 - \beta^4)$ term:** This is the most common *unit-test error* in orifice problems. A 1-line mistake shifts the answer by 3–5 %. **−2 marks**.
> 4. **Confusing $C_d$ with $C$ (velocity-of-approach factor):** These are *different* corrections. $C_d$ is empirical, $C$ is geometric. **−1 mark**.
> 5. **Dimensional mismatch:** Mixing psi with m³ or °F with K. Always convert **first**, substitute **later**. **−2 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Five Pillars of Measurement** — Pressure (P), Flow (Q), Temperature (T), Mass/Density (M/ρ), Composition (C). Memorize as **P-Q-T-M-C**.
- ✅ **Standard Conditions** — API: 15.56 °C (60 °F), 101.325 kPa. NTP: 0 °C, 101.325 kPa. *Always quote which you use.*
- ✅ **Orifice Equation** — $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{2 \Delta P / [\rho (1-\beta^4)]}$. Remember $C_d \approx 0.625$, $(1-\beta^4)$ *not* $(1-\beta^2)$.
- ✅ **Venturi vs Orifice** — Venturi: low ΔP loss, high $C_d$, expensive. Orifice: high ΔP loss, lower $C_d$, cheap.
- ✅ **Turbine meters** — Frequency $f \propto$ velocity; $v = f / K_{\text{factor}}$.
- ✅ **Coriolis meters** — *Gold standard* for liquid mass flow; accuracy ±0.05–0.1 %.
- ✅ **Multi-Path Ultrasonic** — *Gold standard* for gas custody; governed by **AGA-9**.
- ✅ **Real Gas Law** — $PV = ZnRT$. Volume conversion uses $V_{sc} = V_b \cdot (P_b/P_{sc}) \cdot (T_{sc}/T_b) \cdot (Z_{sc}/Z_b)$.
- ✅ **API Gravity** — $\rho = (141.5 / (\text{API}+131.5)) \times 1000$ kg/m³.
- ✅ **VCF (Volume Correction Factor)** — Corrects oil volume from observed to 15.56 °C; uses **API Table 54** (CPL × CTPL).
- ✅ **Meter Factor (MF)** — $\text{MF} = V_{\text{prover}} / V_{\text{indicated}}$; multiplies indicated volume to get *true* volume.
- ✅ **Multiphase measurement** — MPFM uses Venturi + gamma + cross-correlation; cannot be used for fiscal transfer.
- ✅ **Proving** — Custody-transfer meters must be *proved* (calibrated) periodically using **API-4** tank or pipe provers.
- ✅ **Key Standards** — API MPMS, AGA-3 (orifice gas), AGA-7 (turbine gas), AGA-9 (USM gas), API-4.8 (Coriolis liquid), API-14.3 (orifice gas), ISO 5167-2 (orifice general).
- ✅ **Reynolds Number Threshold** — Most DP meters require $Re > 10{,}000$ for stable $C_d$.
- ✅ **Endangered-list traps** — Vena-contracta (not at plate), dead-weight tester (P primary standard), FANN viscometer (drilling fluid — not O&G measurement).

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU 2024 Strategy:**
> For 14-mark questions, structure your answer as: **(i) Statement of principle → (ii) Derivation / Equation → (iii) Worked numerical → (iv) Real-world application**. This mirrors the KTU marking scheme of *principle (3) + derivation (4) + numerical (4) + application (3)*. Practice this four-fold rhythm for every topic in Module 4.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Orifice Meters Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT528 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Orifice Meters Equation # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) An **Orifice Meter** is a *primary flow measurement device* used in oil and natural gas engineering that determines the volumetric or mass flow rate of a fluid by measuring the **differential pressure** created when the fluid is forced to flow through a **converging section (the orifice plate)**. The pressure drop across the plate is mathematically related to the flow rate through the **Orifice Discharge Equation**, which is rooted in the combined application of the **Continuity Equation** and **Bernoulli's Theorem**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The orifice meter is classified as a *differential pressure (DP) type flow meter* and is the **most widely used** flow measurement device in the petroleum industry for custody transfer, allocation metering, and process control in pipelines, wellheads, and gas processing plants. ## 1.2 Key Components of an Orifice Meter Assembly The complete orifice meter run consists of the following elements (as per **ISO 5167** and **AGA Report No. 3**): | Component | Function | |---|---| | **Orifice Plate** | A thin, flat circular plate with a precisely machined concentric hole (the bore) that creates the pressure differential. | | **Pressure Taps** | Upstream and downstream tappings to sense the static pressure on either side of the plate. | | **Differential Pressure (ΔP) Transmitter** | Converts the pressure drop into a measurable electrical signal. | | **Static Pressure Transmitter** | Measures the line pressure (especially critical for gas flow measurement). | | **Temperature Probe (RTD/TC)** | Measures the flowing temperature (required for density correction). | | **Flow Computer** | Solves the orifice equation in real-time using the measured ΔP, P, and T. | > [!NOTE] > **Vena Contracta:** The point just downstream of the orifice plate where the flow streamlines converge to a minimum cross-sectional area (A₂). The diameter at this point (D₂) is smaller than the orifice bore diameter (D₀). The ratio D₂/D₀ is incorporated into the discharge coefficient. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: Plain English Explanation Imagine you are **watering your garden using a hose**, and you press your thumb partially over the hose opening. What happens? 1. The water **speeds up** as it squeezes through the smaller gap. 2. You feel a **pressure drop** at the exit (the water "pulls" or sucks). 3. The harder you press (smaller opening), the greater the pressure difference between the hose interior and the exit. > [!TIP] > **The Orifice Meter works on the EXACT same principle!** The orifice plate is the "thumb" that constricts the flow inside the pipe. The **faster the flow**, the **greater the pressure drop (ΔP)** across the plate. By measuring this ΔP, we can back-calculate the flow rate using a well-defined equation. ### The Fundamental Physics (Why It Works) > [!NOTE] > **Principle of Conservation of Energy (Bernoulli's Principle):** When a fluid flows through a constriction, its **kinetic energy increases** (velocity increases). Since total energy must be conserved, its **pressure energy must decrease** proportionally. This pressure decrease is what we measure with the differential pressure transmitter. **Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold for Emphasis):** - **g (Acceleration due to gravity)** = **9.80665 m/s²** (standard value used in all orifice calculations) - **ρ (Fluid Density)** measured in **kg/m³** - **Cd (Discharge Coefficient)** — dimensionless, typically **0.6 to 0.65** for standard orifice plates - **β (Diameter Ratio)** = D₀/D₁, where **D₀** is the orifice bore diameter and **D₁** is the upstream pipe internal diameter - **Y (Expansion Factor)** = **1.0 for incompressible liquids**, **< 1.0 for compressible gases** # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: The Three Pillars of the Orifice Equation The complete orifice meter equation is derived by combining **three fundamental fluid mechanics principles**: ### Pillar 1: Continuity Equation (Conservation of Mass) For steady, incompressible flow, the mass flow rate must be constant across any cross-section of the pipe: $$\rho A_1 V_1 = \rho A_2 V_2$$ For incompressible flow, this simplifies to: $$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$$ This tells us that as the cross-sectional area decreases (at the orifice), the velocity must **increase proportionally** to maintain the same mass flow rate. ### Pillar 2: Bernoulli's Equation (Conservation of Energy) Applying Bernoulli's equation between an upstream point (1) and the **vena contracta** (2), assuming **no friction losses** and a **horizontal pipe** (z₁ = z₂): $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$ This equation relates the pressure and velocity changes as the fluid accelerates through the constriction. ### Pillar 3: Empirical Discharge Coefficient (Real-Fluid Correction) In an **ideal, frictionless** world, the theoretical flow rate would be calculated directly from the above equations. However, real fluids experience: - **Viscous friction** along the pipe walls - **Turbulence** and **flow separation** at the orifice edge - **Contraction** of the flow streamlines beyond the orifice (vena contracta) These effects are lumped into a single empirical correction factor called the **Discharge Coefficient (Cd)**: $$Q_{actual} = C_d \times Q_{theoretical}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Examiner's Note:** The discharge coefficient is NOT a universal constant! It depends on the **Reynolds Number (Re)**, the **diameter ratio (β)**, and the **tap location** (flange, D and D/2, or corner taps). For the **flange taps** configuration, the **Stahl-Hermstad** or **Reader-Harris/Gallagher (ISO 5167)** correlation is used to calculate Cd. ## 2.2 The Complete Derivation Logic (Step-by-Step Reasoning) **Step 1:** Start with the Bernoulli equation (horizontal pipe, no elevation change): $$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2}$$ **Step 2:** Apply continuity to express V₁ in terms of V₂: $$V_1 = V_2 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1} = V_2 \beta^2$$ where **β = D₂/D₁** (using vena contracta diameter) or **β = D₀/D₁** (using orifice bore diameter). **Step 3:** Substitute V₁ into the Bernoulli equation: $$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_2^2 \beta^4}{2} = \frac{V_2^2 (1 - \beta^4)}{2}$$ **Step 4:** Solve for V₂ (the theoretical velocity at the vena contracta): $$V_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^4}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho}}$$ **Step 5:** Multiply by the cross-sectional area (A₂ = πD₂²/4) to get the theoretical volumetric flow rate: $$Q_{th} = \frac{A_2}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^4}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho}}$$ **Step 6:** Introduce the **Discharge Coefficient (Cd)** to account for real-fluid effects: $$Q = \frac{C_d A_2}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^4}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho}}$$ **Step 7:** Convert pressure difference to a "head" form (h = ΔP/ρg) for liquid flow: $$Q = \frac{C_d A_2 \sqrt{2g}}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^4}} \cdot \sqrt{h}$$ ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **EXAM GOLD:** Memorize these formulas. They are the **core equations** for any orifice meter problem in KTU examinations. | # | Formula Name | Equation | Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Theoretical Velocity at Vena Contracta** | $V_{2,th} = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^{4}}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$ | Ideal (frictionless) case | | 2 | **Volumetric Flow Rate (Liquids)** | $Q = \dfrac{C_d A_2}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^{4}}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$ | Incompressible flow | | 3 | **Volumetric Flow Rate (Liquids, head form)** | $Q = C_d A_2 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 g h}{1 - \beta^{4}}}$ | Using manometer reading 'h' | | 4 | **Mass Flow Rate (Liquids)** | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{C_d A_2}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^{4}}} \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}$ | Mass-based metering | | 5 | **Mass Flow Rate (Gases — General)** | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{C_d A_2 Y}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^{4}}} \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_1 \Delta P}$ | Compressible flow | | 6 | **Volumetric Flow Rate (Gases at base conditions)** | $Q_b = \dfrac{C_d A_2 Y}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^{4}}} \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho_1}} \cdot \dfrac{P_1}{P_b} \cdot \dfrac{T_b}{T_1}$ | Custody transfer of natural gas | | 7 | **Reynolds Number (Orifice)** | $Re_D = \dfrac{\rho V_1 D_1}{\mu} = \dfrac{4 \dot{m}}{\pi \mu D_1}$ | Used to find Cd | | 8 | **Diameter Ratio** | $\beta = \dfrac{D_0}{D_1}$ | Geometry of the orifice | | 9 | **Orifice Bore Area** | $A_0 = \dfrac{\pi D_0^{2}}{4}$ | Cross-sectional area | | 10 | **Expansion Factor (Approximate, Gas)** | $Y \approx 1 - \dfrac{(0.41 + 0.35 \beta^{4}) \Delta P}{k P_1}$ | Subsonic gas flow, k = Cp/Cv | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Reminder for Table Reading:** In the formulas above, the symbol `$\vert$` (absolute value bars) has been **deliberately replaced with `\vert` or parentheses** to prevent breaking the markdown table syntax. In your answer sheets, write `|x|` or use `\left\vert x \right\vert` in LaTeX. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The orifice meter equation is the **workhorse equation** of the oil and gas industry. Its applications include: 1. **Custody Transfer Measurement:** Accurately metering the volume of crude oil or natural gas being transferred between operators (e.g., from a producer to a pipeline company). Billing is based on this measurement. 2. **Wellhead Testing:** Determining the production rate of oil and gas wells. 3. **Pipeline Leak Detection:** Sudden changes in the ΔP-to-flow relationship can indicate leaks. 4. **Process Control in Refineries:** Monitoring flow rates in distillation columns, hydrocrackers, and gas processing units. 5. **Flare Stack Monitoring:** Measuring gas flow to flare stacks for environmental compliance. 6. **Gas Storage Facility Measurement:** Monitoring injection and withdrawal rates in underground gas storage. > [!TIP] > **Why Orifice Meters Dominate the Industry:** They are **simple, inexpensive, reliable, well-understood, and standardized** (ISO 5167, AGA 3, API MPMS Chapter 14.3). No moving parts means long service life with minimal maintenance. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Complete Derivation of the Orifice Meter Equation (Liquid Flow) ### 3.1.1 Setup and Assumptions Consider a fluid flowing through a pipe of internal diameter **D₁** with a concentric orifice plate of bore diameter **D₀** installed. Pressure tappings are located upstream (point 1) and downstream (point 2, at the vena contracta). **Assumptions:** 1. Steady-state flow 2. Incompressible fluid (valid for liquids) 3. No friction losses (will be corrected later by Cd) 4. Horizontal pipe (z₁ = z₂) 5. Uniform velocity profiles at sections 1 and 2 ### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation **Step 1: Apply the Continuity Equation** For an incompressible fluid, the volumetric flow rate must be conserved between section 1 and section 2: $$A_1 V_1 = A_2 V_2$$ where: - $A_1 = \dfrac{\pi D_1^2}{4}$ is the upstream pipe cross-sectional area - $A_2 = \dfrac{\pi D_2^2}{4}$ is the **vena contracta** cross-sectional area - $D_2$ is the diameter at the vena contracta (slightly less than D₀) Solving for $V_1$: $$V_1 = V_2 \cdot \dfrac{A_2}{A_1} = V_2 \cdot \left(\dfrac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2$$ **Step 2: Define the Diameter Ratio** For convenience, we define the **diameter ratio** at the vena contracta as: $$\beta = \dfrac{D_2}{D_1}$$ Therefore: $$V_1 = V_2 \cdot \beta^2$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Simplification:** In practice, the **orifice bore diameter D₀** is used instead of D₂ because D₂ (vena contracta) cannot be easily measured. This leads to a **slightly different β** (β = D₀/D₁) and the difference is absorbed into the **discharge coefficient Cd**. **Step 3: Apply Bernoulli's Equation** For a horizontal pipe with no elevation change, between sections 1 and 2: $$\frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{V_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{V_2^2}{2g}$$ Rearranging to isolate the pressure difference: $$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2g}$$ Multiplying both sides by $\rho g$: $$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_1^2}{2}$$ **Step 4: Substitute V₁ from Continuity into Bernoulli's Equation** Replace $V_1$ with $V_2 \beta^2$: $$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho} = \frac{V_2^2 - (V_2 \beta^2)^2}{2} = \frac{V_2^2 - V_2^2 \beta^4}{2}$$ Factor out $V_2^2$: $$\frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho} = \frac{V_2^2 (1 - \beta^4)}{2}$$ **Step 5: Solve for the Theoretical Velocity V₂** Multiply both sides by 2: $$\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho} = V_2^2 (1 - \beta^4)$$ Divide by $(1 - \beta^4)$: $$V_2^2 = \frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho (1 - \beta^4)}$$ Take the square root of both sides: $$V_2 = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^4}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho}}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **theoretical (ideal) velocity** at the vena contracta. The factor $1/\sqrt{1 - \beta^4}$ is called the **velocity-of-approach factor**. **Step 6: Calculate the Theoretical Volumetric Flow Rate** $$Q_{th} = A_2 \cdot V_2 = A_2 \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^4}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho}}$$ Therefore: $$Q_{th} = \frac{A_2}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^4}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho}}$$ **Step 7: Introduce the Discharge Coefficient** To account for real-fluid effects (viscous losses, turbulence, vena contracta contraction), multiply by the **discharge coefficient Cd**: $$Q_{actual} = C_d \cdot Q_{theoretical}$$ $$\boxed{Q = \frac{C_d A_2}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^4}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2(P_1 - P_2)}{\rho}}}$$ **Step 8: Express in Terms of the Orifice Bore Diameter D₀** In practice, we use $A_0 = \pi D_0^2/4$ and $\beta = D_0/D_1$: $$Q = \frac{C_d A_0}{\sqrt{1 - (D_0/D_1)^4}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$$ **Step 9: Convert to "Head" Form (using a manometer)** If a U-tube manometer with a heavier fluid of density $\rho_m$ measures a differential height $h_m$, and the flowing fluid has density $\rho_f$, the equivalent pressure head in the flowing fluid is: $$h = \frac{(\rho_m - \rho_f) \cdot h_m}{\rho_f}$$ Substituting into the flow equation: $$Q = C_d A_0 \sqrt{\frac{2 g h}{1 - \beta^4}}$$ **Step 10: Mass Flow Rate** Multiplying the volumetric flow rate by density: $$\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q = \frac{C_d A_0}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^4}} \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho \Delta P}$$ ## 3.2 Complete Derivation of the Orifice Meter Equation (Gas Flow) For **compressible fluids** (natural gas, air, steam), the derivation must account for the density change across the orifice. The procedure is similar, but the fluid density at section 1 ($\rho_1$) is used, and an **Expansion Factor (Y)** is introduced: ### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation **Step 1: Mass Flow from Continuity (for compressible flow)** $$\dot{m} = \rho_1 A_1 V_1 = \rho_2 A_2 V_2$$ **Step 2: Apply the Adiabatic Energy Equation (instead of Bernoulli's)** For compressible flow, we use the **adiabatic (isentropic) flow equation**: $$\frac{k}{k-1} \cdot \frac{P_1}{\rho_1} + \frac{V_1^2}{2} = \frac{k}{k-1} \cdot \frac{P_2}{\rho_2} + \frac{V_2^2}{2}$$ where $k = C_p / C_v$ is the specific heat ratio. **Step 3: Apply the Isentropic Relation** $$\frac{P_1}{\rho_1^k} = \frac{P_2}{\rho_2^k}$$ This gives us $\rho_2 = \rho_1 (P_2/P_1)^{1/k}$. **Step 4: Combine the Equations (Detailed Algebra)** After algebraic manipulation (expanding, substituting, and solving for V₂), we obtain the **theoretical** mass flow rate for compressible flow: $$\dot{m}_{th} = \frac{A_2}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^4}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 k}{k-1} \cdot P_1 \rho_1 \left[ \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{2/k} - \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{(k+1)/k} \right]}$$ **Step 5: Introduce the Discharge Coefficient and Expansion Factor** The **expansion factor Y** is defined as the ratio of the actual (real-gas) mass flow rate to the ideal (isentropic) mass flow rate: $$Y = \frac{\dot{m}_{actual}}{\dot{m}_{isentropic}}$$ Therefore, the **complete gas orifice equation** becomes: $$\boxed{\dot{m} = \frac{C_d A_2 Y}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^4}} \cdot \sqrt{2 \rho_1 \Delta P}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **For liquids (incompressible): Y = 1** because the density does not change. This is why the simpler form of the equation is used for liquids. For natural gas, Y < 1 and depends on β, ΔP/P₁, and k. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Variables, Units, and Typical Values | Variable | Symbol | SI Unit | Typical Range/Value | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---|---|---| | Volumetric Flow Rate | $Q$ | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | 0.001 to 10 | Volume of fluid per unit time | | Mass Flow Rate | $\dot{m}$ | $\text{kg}/\text{s}$ | 0.1 to 1000 | Mass of fluid per unit time | | Pipe Diameter | $D_1$ | $\text{m}$ | 0.05 to 1.0 | Upstream internal pipe diameter | | Orifice Bore Diameter | $D_0$ | $\text{m}$ | 0.02 to 0.8 | Diameter of the hole in the plate | | Diameter Ratio | $\beta$ | dimensionless | 0.2 to 0.75 | $D_0/D_1$ | | Differential Pressure | $\Delta P$ | $\text{Pa}$ | 100 to 250,000 | $P_1 - P_2$ | | Fluid Density | $\rho$ | $\text{kg}/\text{m}^3$ | 1.2 (gas) to 1000 (oil) | Mass per unit volume | | Discharge Coefficient | $C_d$ | dimensionless | 0.6 to 0.65 | Real-fluid correction factor | | Expansion Factor | $Y$ | dimensionless | 0.95 to 1.00 (gas) | Compressibility correction | | Reynolds Number | $Re_D$ | dimensionless | $10^4$ to $10^7$ | Flow regime indicator | ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Exam Standard) **Problem:** Water flows through a horizontal pipe of internal diameter **D₁ = 100 mm** with a concentric orifice plate of bore diameter **D₀ = 60 mm**. The differential pressure across the orifice is measured as **ΔP = 50 kPa**. The discharge coefficient is **Cd = 0.62**. Calculate the **volumetric flow rate** of water. **Given:** - $D_1 = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$ - $D_0 = 60 \text{ mm} = 0.06 \text{ m}$ - $\Delta P = 50 \text{ kPa} = 50{,}000 \text{ Pa}$ - $C_d = 0.62$ - $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$ **Solution:** **Step 1:** Calculate the orifice bore area: $$A_0 = \frac{\pi D_0^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.06)^2}{4} = 2.827 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2:** Calculate the diameter ratio: $$\beta = \frac{D_0}{D_1} = \frac{0.06}{0.10} = 0.6$$ **Step 3:** Calculate $1 - \beta^4$: $$\beta^4 = (0.6)^4 = 0.1296$$ $$1 - \beta^4 = 1 - 0.1296 = 0.8704$$ **Step 4:** Apply the orifice equation: $$Q = \frac{C_d A_0}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^4}} \cdot \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$$ **Step 5:** Calculate the term inside the square root: $$\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho} = \frac{2 \times 50{,}000}{1000} = 100 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}^2$$ **Step 6:** Take the square root: $$\sqrt{100} = 10 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 7:** Combine all terms: $$Q = \frac{0.62 \times 2.827 \times 10^{-3}}{\sqrt{0.8704}} \times 10$$ $$\sqrt{0.8704} = 0.9330$$ $$Q = \frac{0.62 \times 2.827 \times 10^{-3}}{0.9330} \times 10 = \frac{1.753 \times 10^{-3}}{0.9330} \times 10$$ $$Q = 1.879 \times 10^{-3} \times 10 = 0.01879 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$ $$\boxed{Q \approx 0.0188 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} \approx 18.8 \text{ L/s}}$$ # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Orifice Meter System ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Upstream Pipe
