# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]
**List the three main criteria used to choose between a vertical and a horizontal oil–gas separator.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Gas–Liquid Ratio (GLR):** Horizontal separators are preferred for **high GLR** streams (large gas capacity per unit vessel volume); vertical is chosen for low-to-moderate GLR. *(1 mark)*
2. **Liquid residence time / slug handling:** Horizontal vessels provide **longer residence time** at the same volume due to larger cross-sectional liquid area, hence preferred for **foamy or slugs**. *(1 mark)*
3. **Plot area / sand handling:** Vertical needs less **plot area** and handles **sand** better (sand falls to the bottom nozzle without being swept to the gas outlet). *(1 mark)*

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define NPSH available (NPSHa) and state the selection rule for safe pump operation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **NPSHa definition (1.5 marks):** Net Positive Suction Head available is the total head (in metres of liquid) at the pump suction nozzle, **above the vapour pressure** of the pumped liquid at the operating temperature. It represents the energy available to push liquid into the impeller eye and prevent cavitation.
- **Selection rule (1.5 marks):** For safe operation, the **NPSHa must exceed the NPSH required (NPSHr) by a positive margin**, typically **0.5 to 1.0 m**:

$$\text{NPSHa} \geq \text{NPSHr} + 0.5 \text{ to } 1.0 \text{ m}$$

If this is not satisfied, suction piping must be redesigned, the tank elevated, the strainer cleaned, or a smaller pump (with lower NPSHr) chosen.

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Option 1]

### **Question A** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]
*A new offshore platform produces 8000 bopd of crude (ρ = 870 kg/m³, µ = 12 cP) with a GOR of 1200 scf/bbl. The operating pressure at the wellhead manifold is 25 bar and 70 °C. The sales-oil BS&W must be ≤ 0.5 %.*

**(a)** *Select the appropriate type (vertical/horizontal/spherical) and number of stages for the primary separator, justifying your choice.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *For the first-stage vessel, calculate the minimum vessel diameter using the Souders–Brown correlation, given K = 0.10 ft/s. Assume gas density ρ_G = 12 kg/m³ at vessel conditions and that 30 % of the gas evolves in the first stage.* **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution for (a)** — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Compute total gas rate (1.5 marks):**
GOR = 1200 scf/bbl = 1200 × 0.0283 = 34 m³/bbl (at std conditions)
Total gas = 8000 × 34 = 2.72 × 10⁵ m³/day = 3140 m³/h

**Step 2 — Analyse the operating conditions (1.5 marks):**
- High GOR → high gas-to-liquid ratio → favour **horizontal** vessel
- Multiphase flow with sand risk → horizontal with **scrubber + sand jetting nozzle**
- Sales spec demands low BS&W → **two-stage separation** (1st stage at wellhead pressure, 2nd at 4 bar) for additional gas breakout

**Step 3 — Final selection (2 marks):**
- **Type:** Horizontal two-phase separator
- **Stages:** **2 stages** (1st stage @ 25 bar, 2nd stage @ 4 bar) followed by a stock tank
- **Justification:** High GLR + high liquid rate + slugging tendency + small offshore plot → **horizontal 2-stage train** is the industry-standard answer.

**Step 4 — Auxiliary equipment (2 marks):**
- Inlet diverter (cyclonic) for slug suppression
- Mesh pad / vane demister at gas outlet
- Level controller + dump valve
- Sand jetting system at bottom

---

### **Model Solution for (b)** — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Gas volumetric flow at vessel conditions (1.5 marks):**
Gas rate in 1st stage = 30 % of 3140 m³/h = 942 m³/h
Convert to m³/s: $Q_G = 942 / 3600 = 0.2617$ m³/s

**Step 2 — Compute Souders–Brown velocity (2 marks):**
Convert $K = 0.10$ ft/s to m/s: $K = 0.10 × 0.3048 = 0.03048$ m/s

$$u_{g,\text{max}} = K \sqrt{\frac{\rho_L - \rho_G}{\rho_G}}$$

$$u_{g,\text{max}} = 0.03048 \times \sqrt{\frac{870 - 12}{12}}$$

$$u_{g,\text{max}} = 0.03048 \times \sqrt{71.5}$$

$$u_{g,\text{max}} = 0.03048 \times 8.456 = 0.2577 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Compute minimum diameter (2 marks):**

$$D_{\min} = \sqrt{\frac{4 Q_G}{\pi u_{g,\text{max}}}}$$

$$D_{\min} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 0.2617}{\pi \times 0.2577}}$$

$$D_{\min} = \sqrt{\frac{1.0468}{0.8095}} = \sqrt{1.2933}$$

$$D_{\min} = 1.137 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4 — Apply safety / standardisation (1.5 marks):**
Select standard vessel diameter (next size up) → **D = 1.2 m (48 inch)**.
The vessel length is fixed by retention time (not asked but for context: 3 min → $V_L = Q_L \cdot t$, then $L = V_L / (\pi D^2/4)$).

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Option 2]

### **Question B** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]
*A centrifugal pump is required to deliver 120 m³/h of formation water (ρ = 1050 kg/m³, P_vapor = 0.30 bar at 50 °C) from a stock tank to a water-injection wellhead. The tank surface is at atmospheric pressure (1.013 bar). The pump is located 2.5 m **above** the tank liquid level. The 4-inch (D = 0.102 m) suction line is 8 m long, with one sharp-edged entry (K = 0.5) and one fully-open gate valve (K = 0.17). Friction factor f = 0.022.*

**(a)** *Compute the NPSH available at the pump suction.* **(7 marks)**

**(b)** *If the pump manufacturer specifies NPSHr = 4.0 m, determine whether the pump will cavitate. If so, suggest two design modifications.* **(7 marks)*

---

### **Model Solution for (a)** — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Compute flow velocity in suction line (1.5 marks):**
$$Q = 120 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} = 0.03333 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$
$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.102)^2}{4} = 8.171 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$
$$v = Q/A = 0.03333 / 8.171 \times 10^{-3} = 4.079 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute suction friction head loss (2.5 marks):**
$$\frac{v^2}{2g} = \frac{(4.079)^2}{2 \times 9.81} = 0.848 \text{ m}$$
$$h_f = \left(f \frac{L}{D} + \sum K_L\right) \frac{v^2}{2g}$$
$$\sum K_L = 0.5 + 0.17 = 0.67$$
$$h_f = \left(0.022 \times \frac{8}{0.102} + 0.67\right) \times 0.848$$
$$h_f = (1.725 + 0.67) \times 0.848 = 2.395 \times 0.848 = 2.031 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute elevation head (1 mark):**
Pump is **above** tank → static head is **negative**:
$$\Delta z = z_{\text{pump}} - z_{\text{tank}} = +2.5 \text{ m (loss)}$$

**Step 4 — Apply NPSHa equation (2 marks):**
$$\text{NPSHa} = \frac{P_{\text{tank}} - P_{\text{vapor}}}{\rho g} - \Delta z - h_f$$
$$P_{\text{tank}} - P_{\text{vapor}} = (1.013 - 0.30) \times 10^5 = 7.13 \times 10^4 \text{ Pa}$$
$$\frac{P_{\text{tank}} - P_{\text{vapor}}}{\rho g} = \frac{7.13 \times 10^4}{1050 \times 9.81} = 6.924 \text{ m}$$
$$\text{NPSHa} = 6.924 - 2.5 - 2.031 = 2.393 \text{ m}$$

---

### **Model Solution for (b)** — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Compare with NPSHr + margin (2 marks):**
Required minimum = NPSHr + 0.5 m = 4.0 + 0.5 = **4.5 m**
Available = **2.393 m** < 4.5 m → **The pump WILL CAVITATE** ❌

**Step 2 — Diagnosis of the cause (1.5 marks):**
- Pump is **above** the tank (positive Δz reduces NPSHa)
- High velocity (4.08 m/s) in 4-inch line causes large friction loss
- Suction line is too long / has excessive fittings

**Step 3 — Design Modifications (3.5 marks):**
| Modification | Impact on NPSHa |
|---|---|
| **Move pump below tank level (e.g., flooded suction)** | Eliminates the 2.5 m static loss → NPSHa → ~4.9 m ✓ |
| **Increase suction line diameter to 6 inch (0.152 m)** | Velocity drops to ~1.85 m/s, friction loss drops to ~0.45 m → NPSHa → 4.0 m |
| **Reduce the number of fittings (remove unnecessary valve)** | $\sum K_L$ drops, friction reduces by ~0.14 m |
| **Pressurize the tank with inert gas blanket (e.g., N₂ at 0.5 bar)** | $P_{\text{tank}}$ rises by 5 m head, NPSHa exceeds 7.9 m |
| **Select a different pump with NPSHr ≤ 1.5 m** (inducer or double-suction impeller) | Margin restored |

**Best practical answer:** **Flooded suction (pump below tank) + line diameter increase** — this is the offshore-industry default.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Traps:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch:** K given in ft/s but $D$ computed in m → silent error of 0.3× factor → 1-mark deduction. Always convert $K$ first.
> 2. **Sign of Δz:** Pump above tank is a **negative contribution** to NPSHa. Writing $\text{NPSHa} = \frac{P_t - P_v}{\rho g} + \Delta z - h_f$ is a classic 1-mark trap.
> 3. **Forgetting the 0.5 m margin:** Many students write $\text{NPSHa} \geq \text{NPSHr}$. The KTU expected answer is the **margin-inclusive** version.
> 4. **Ignoring the 30 % gas split** in separators: students often size the vessel for **all** the gas instead of the **per-stage** fraction → diameter 1.9× too large → 2-mark loss.
> 5. **Stage count confusion:** "2-stage" means 2 process vessels; "3-stage" includes the stock tank. Mis-stating this loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-density revision checklist for KTU PEPOT528 Module 4 — Selection:**

- ☐ **Selection is a 7-step decision process** (basis → spec → candidates → screen → size → compare → document)
- ☐ **Vertical separator:** low–moderate GLR, plot-constrained, sand-handling
- ☐ **Horizontal separator:** high GLR, foamy crudes, large liquid rates
- ☐ **Spherical separator:** low-to-moderate capacity, economic at moderate pressure
- ☐ **Souders–Brown K values:** 0.05 (mist extractor) / 0.10 (2-stage) / 0.15 (bulk)
- ☐ **Liquid retention time:** 3 min (1st stage) → 1 min (2nd) → 30 s (3rd)
- ☐ **Centrifugal pump:** high Q, low–medium head, clean fluid, NPSHa is the gatekeeper
- ☐ **PD pump:** low Q, high head, viscous, metering duty
- ☐ **ESP:** wellbore artificial lift, high rate
- ☐ **SRP:** onshore, low GOR, shallow wells
- ☐ **Centrifugal compressor:** high Q, low ΔP per stage, surge margin ≥ 10 %
- ☐ **Reciprocating compressor:** low Q, very high ΔP, pulsation bottle required
- ☐ **Polytropic index $n$:** $1 < n < k$ (k = $C_p/C_v$); use for centrifugal; isothermal ($n=1$) for slow reciprocating
- ☐ **Shell & tube HX:** high pressure, fouling, multiphase — industry default
- ☐ **LMTD correction factor $F$** must be applied for multi-pass configurations; reject if $F < 0.75$
- ☐ **NPSHa ≥ NPSHr + 0.5 to 1.0 m** is the golden rule of pump selection
- ☐ **BS&W ≤ 0.5 %** in sales oil is the typical design target for separator trains
- ☐ **API 12J (separators), API 12K (indirect heaters), API 520/521 (PSV sizing), API 610 (pumps), API 617 (compressors)** are the most-cited codes in KTU answers
- ☐ **The selection decision tree must be written out in any 14-mark answer** — examiners award at least 2 marks for the **methodology**, not just the final number
- ☐ **Always include units** in the final answer — $D$ in m, $Q$ in m³/s, $H$ in m, $P$ in kW, $A$ in m²

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Uncertainties in Flow, Liquefied Natural Gas: Gas Treating Before Liquefaction, Liquefaction Cycles, Storage of LNG
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT528 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Uncertainties in Flow, Liquefied Natural Gas: Gas Treating Before Liquefaction, Liquefaction Cycles, Storage of LNG

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Uncertainties in Flow

### Formal Academic Definition
In oil and natural gas engineering, **flow uncertainty** refers to the quantitative characterization of the doubt that exists about the true value of a flow measurement (single-phase or multiphase). It is expressed as a confidence interval around the measured value, typically at a 95% confidence level, following the **Guide to the Expression of Uncertainty in Measurement (GUM)** and industry standards such as **API MPMS Chapter 13** and **ISO 5167**. Uncertainty analysis combines the effects of systematic (bias) errors and random (precision) errors from primary devices, secondary instrumentation, and operational variability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Uncertainties arise from sensor calibration drift, fluid property variations (ρ, μ, z), installation effects, flow regime transitions, and the propagation of these errors through flow equations such as orifice, turbine, Coriolis, and ultrasonic meters.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of flow measurement like estimating the weight of a sack of rice using a bathroom scale. If the scale is slightly off (calibration error) and your eyes judge the dial imperfectly (reading error), you cannot claim an exact figure. You would say, *"The sack weighs about 50 kg, give or take 0.5 kg."* That "give or take" is the **uncertainty band**. In oil and gas custody transfer, even a 0.25% error on a million-dollar shipment translates to thousands of dollars — so quantifying this "give or take" rigorously is a contractual and engineering necessity.

### Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
- Standard confidence level: **95%** (coverage factor **k = 2**)
- Reference temperature: **15.56 °C (60 °F)** for standard cubic meters
- Reference pressure: **101.325 kPa (14.696 psia)**
- Typical custody transfer uncertainty target: **±0.25% to ±1.0%**

---

## 1.2 Liquefied Natural Gas (LNG) — Overview

### Formal Definition
**Liquefied Natural Gas (LNG)** is natural gas (predominantly methane) cooled to approximately **−162 °C (−260 °F)** at near-atmospheric pressure, reducing its volume by a factor of about **1/600** compared to its gaseous state at standard conditions. The LNG value chain comprises gas treating, liquefaction, transport, storage, and regasification.

### Conceptual Analogy
Imagine a beach ball filled with air — you can compress it into a small marble by squeezing. LNG is that "squeezing" but achieved by **extreme cooling** instead of mechanical force, enabling efficient ocean transport where pipelines are impossible.

---

## 1.3 Gas Treating Before Liquefaction

### Formal Definition
Gas treating (or **gas conditioning**) is the removal of components that would otherwise freeze, foul, corrode, or violate product specifications during liquefaction. Key contaminants removed are **H₂S, CO₂, H₂O, Hg, N₂, and heavy hydrocarbons (C₅⁺)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Treating is Critical:**
> - **CO₂** freezes at −78 °C → plugs cryogenic exchangers
> - **H₂O** forms hydrates and ice at cryogenic temperatures
> - **H₂S** causes sulfide stress cracking and toxic LNG
> - **Hg** causes corrosion of aluminum heat exchangers (liquid metal embrittlement)

### Intuitive Analogy
Before putting fine wine in a freezer, you filter out grape skins and seeds — otherwise the cold crystallizes impurities and ruins the drink. Gas treating is the "filtering stage" before the "deep freeze" of liquefaction.

---

## 1.4 Liquefaction Cycles

### Formal Definition
A **liquefaction cycle** is a thermodynamic process arrangement using refrigeration principles (compression, expansion, phase change) to cool natural gas below its critical temperature (Tc = −82.3 °C for methane) and condense it into a liquid. Common industrial cycles include **Cascade, Propane Pre-cooled Mixed Refrigerant (C3MR), Single Mixed Refrigerant (SMR), and Expander-based** cycles.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of a kitchen refrigerator. It uses a refrigerant (like Freon) that absorbs heat inside the fridge and releases it outside through cycles of compression and expansion. LNG plants use the **same principle** but with much larger refrigerants and far colder temperatures.

---

## 1.5 Storage of LNG

### Formal Definition
LNG storage refers to insulated, low-pressure vessels designed to hold LNG at approximately **−162 °C** with boil-off gas (BOG) management. Standard types are **single containment, double containment, and full containment** tanks as per **EN 1473** and **NFPA 59A**.

### Intuitive Analogy
A giant **thermos flask** the size of a small stadium — it keeps the LNG so cold that even with insulation imperfections, only a tiny fraction evaporates daily. The evaporated gas (BOG) is captured, compressed, and reused as fuel gas.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Uncertainties in Flow — Deep Analysis

### 2.1.1 Sources of Uncertainty
1. **Primary Device (Orifice, Turbine, Ultrasonic, Coriolis)**
   - Calibration uncertainty
   - Installation effects (upstream/downstream straight runs)
   - Wear and drift
2. **Secondary Instrumentation (PT, TT, dP transmitter)**
   - Calibration, repeatability, hysteresis
3. **Fluid Property Variation**
   - Density (ρ), viscosity (μ), compressibility (z), composition
4. **Process Conditions**
   - Pulsation, two-phase flow, temperature transients

### 2.1.2 Uncertainty Combination Rules

**Random uncertainties (Type A):** Combined in quadrature (RSS — Root Sum of Squares).

$$u_c = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_i} \right)^2 u_i^2}$$

**Systematic uncertainties (Type B):** Combined linearly (worst-case) or quadrature depending on independence.

**Expanded uncertainty:**
$$U = k \cdot u_c$$
where **k = 2** gives ~95% confidence.

### 2.1.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Flow Uncertainty

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Combined standard uncertainty | $u_c = \sqrt{\sum (\partial f/\partial x_i)^2 u_i^2}$ | — | RSS for independent inputs |
| 2 | Expanded uncertainty | $U = k \cdot u_c$ | same as output | k = 2 for 95% |
| 3 | Orifice mass flow (ISO 5167) | $q_m = \dfrac{C \cdot \varepsilon \cdot A_2 \sqrt{2 \Delta p \rho_1}}{\sqrt{1-\beta^4}}$ | kg/s | C = discharge coefficient |
| 4 | Discharge coefficient (Reader-Harris/Gallagher) | $C = f(\beta, Re_D)$ | — | Iterative |
| 5 | Reynolds number | $Re_D = \dfrac{4 q_m}{\pi D \mu}$ | — | For pipe flow |
| 6 | Volumetric flow at standard conditions | $q_v = \dfrac{q_m}{\rho_{std}}$ | Sm³/s | Standard = 15.56 °C, 101.325 kPa |
| 7 | Sensitivity coefficient | $c_i = \partial f / \partial x_i$ | variable | Used in RSS |
| 8 | Relative uncertainty propagation (power model $f = x_1^{a_1} x_2^{a_2} \dots$) | $\left(\dfrac{u_c}{f}\right)^2 = \sum a_i^2 \left(\dfrac{u_i}{x_i}\right)^2$ | — | For multiplicative models |

### 2.1.4 Real-World Utility
Used in **custody transfer, allocation metering, fiscal metering, and pipeline balance reconciliation**. Regulatory bodies (OIML, API, AGA) require documented uncertainty budgets for every fiscal meter.

---

## 2.2 Gas Treating Before Liquefaction — Deep Analysis

### 2.2.1 Process Steps (Logical Flow)
1. **Inlet Separation** — Remove free liquids and solids (slug catchers, scrubbers)
2. **Acid Gas Removal (AGR)** — Amine, membrane, or SULFINOL process
3. **Dehydration** — Glycol (TEG) contactor or Molecular Sieve (zeolite 4A/3A)
4. **Mercury Removal** — Sulphur-impregnated activated carbon or metal oxide beds
5. **Heavy Hydrocarbon Removal** — Lean oil absorption or activated carbon
6. **Nitrogen Rejection** — Cryogenic distillation
7. **Final Liquefaction-Grade Spec Verification**

### 2.2.2 LNG Feed Gas Specification (Typical)

| Component | Specification | Removal Method |
|-----------|--------------|----------------|
| CO₂ | < 50 ppmv (often < 30 ppmv) | Amine, membrane, molecular sieve |
| H₂S | < 3.5 mg/Nm³ (~2.5 ppmv) | Amine, scavenger |
| H₂O | < 1 ppmv | TEG, molecular sieve |
| Hg | < 0.01 µg/Nm³ | Sulphur-impregnated carbon |
| C₅⁺ | < 100 ppmv | Lean oil, adsorption |
| N₂ | < 1 mol% | Cryogenic distillation |
| Total S | < 30 mg/Nm³ | Combination |

### 2.2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Gas Treating

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Acid gas loading (amine) | $\alpha = \dfrac{\text{mol acid gas}}{\text{mol amine}}$ | mol/mol | Operating range 0.3–0.5 for MEA |
| 2 | Equilibrium partial pressure (Raoult) | $p_i = x_i \cdot P_i^{sat}(T)$ | Pa | Used in dehydration design |
| 3 | Water dew point depression | $\Delta T_{dp} = T_{in} - T_{out}$ | °C | TEG contactor target |
| 4 | TEG circulation rate | $q_{TEG} = m_{H_2O,removed} \cdot L/W$ | L/min | L/W typically 3–6 gal TEG/lb H₂O |
| 5 | Molecular sieve capacity | $X = \dfrac{m_{H_2O,adsorbed}}{m_{sieve}}$ | kg/kg | Typically 8–12 wt% |
| 6 | Breakthrough time (Wheeler) | $t_b = \dfrac{W \cdot X}{Q \cdot C_0} - \dfrac{\rho_b \cdot W^2 \cdot Z}{Q^2}$ | s | Adsorber sizing |
| 7 | Mercury removal efficiency | $\eta_{Hg} = \dfrac{C_{in}-C_{out}}{C_{in}}$ | % | Target > 99% |

### 2.2.4 Real-World Utility
Critical for protecting multi-billion dollar cryogenic heat exchangers (aluminum-brazed) from plugging, corrosion, and safety hazards. Wrong specifications can cause 2–4 week unplanned shutdowns costing millions.

---

## 2.3 Liquefaction Cycles — Deep Analysis

### 2.3.1 Cascade Cycle (Classical)
Three pure refrigerants: **Propane (C₃H₈), Ethylene (C₂H₄), Methane (CH₄)** in three separate loops at progressively lower temperatures. Each refrigerant matches the gas cooling duty at its temperature level.

- Propane loop: +35 °C → −35 °C
- Ethylene loop: −35 °C → −100 °C
- Methane loop: −100 °C → −162 °C

### 2.3.2 Mixed Refrigerant Cascade (MRC)
Uses two mixed refrigerants (MR) instead of pure fluids, improving thermodynamic efficiency by better matching the cooling curve to the natural gas cooling curve.

### 2.3.3 C3MR (Propane Pre-cooled Mixed Refrigerant)
- **Pre-cooling:** Propane loop cools NG and MR from +35 °C to about −35 °C
- **Main liquefaction:** Mixed refrigerant (N₂, C₁, C₂, C₃, iC₄) cools NG from −35 °C to −162 °C
- **Used in:** ~80% of world LNG capacity (e.g., Qatar, Australia)

### 2.3.4 Single Mixed Refrigerant (SMR)
- One mixed refrigerant loop
- Simpler, lower CAPEX, lower efficiency
- Suitable for **small-scale (< 1.5 MTPA)** and **FLNG** (Floating LNG)

### 2.3.5 Expander-Based (Nitrogen or MR Expander)
- Uses turbo-expander for refrigeration
- Lower CAPEX, smaller footprint
- Lower efficiency; used in peak-shaving and small-scale plants

### 2.3.6 Comparison Table

| Cycle | Specific Power (kWh/kg LNG) | CAPEX | Scale Suitability | Efficiency |
|-------|------------------------------|-------|-------------------|-----------|
| Cascade (Classical) | 0.32–0.36 | High | Large (> 2 MTPA) | High |
| C3MR (APCI) | 0.27–0.30 | High | Large | High |
| SMR | 0.35–0.40 | Medium | Small/Medium | Medium |
| Expander (N₂) | 0.40–0.50 | Low | Small/Peak | Lower |
| DMR (Dual MR) | 0.25–0.28 | High | Large | Very High |

### 2.3.7 KTU Formula Sheet — Liquefaction

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Specific compression work | $w_s = \int v \, dp$ | kJ/kg | Ideal: $w_s = (k/(k-1)) R T_1 [(P_2/P_1)^{(k-1)/k}-1]$ |
| 2 | Coefficient of performance | $COP = Q_L / W_{net}$ | — | Refrigeration |
| 3 | Liquefaction fraction | $z = \dfrac{h_1 - h_{liq}}{h_{vap} - h_{liq}}$ | — | After throttling |
| 4 | Joule–Thomson coefficient | $\mu_{JT} = \left(\dfrac{\partial T}{\partial P}\right)_h$ | K/Pa | Positive → cooling |
| 5 | Minimum work of liquefaction | $w_{min} = T_0 \Delta s - \Delta h$ | kJ/kg | Reversible limit |
| 6 | Exergy destruction | $I = T_0 \cdot s_{gen}$ | kW | Lost work |
| 7 | Ideal gas density (cryo) | $\rho = PM/(zRT)$ | kg/m³ | At LNG conditions z → 1 (gas) |

### 2.3.8 Real-World Utility
Cycle selection determines ~30% of the plant's OPEX. C3MR dominates base-load LNG, while SMR/expander dominate small-scale and floating LNG.

---

## 2.4 Storage of LNG — Deep Analysis

### 2.4.1 Storage Tank Types

| Type | Structure | Use Case | Secondary Containment |
|------|-----------|----------|----------------------|
| **Single Containment** | Inner steel tank + outer concrete dike | Older, smaller plants | Dike (110% volume) |
| **Double Containment** | Inner steel + outer steel/secondary | Medium plants | Outer steel tank |
| **Full Containment** | Inner steel + outer pre-stressed concrete | Modern, large plants | Concrete outer (prestressed) |

### 2.4.2 Tank Components
- **Inner tank:** 9% Ni steel or aluminum, LNG-tight
- **Outer tank:** Pre-stressed concrete (post-tensioned tendons)
- **Insulation:** Perlite (loose fill) + foam glass at bottom, sometimes LNG vapor space at slight positive pressure
- **Roof:** Suspended deck (insulation) or concrete dome

### 2.4.3 Boil-Off Gas (BOG)
- Typical BOG rate: **0.05%–0.15% volume per day**
- BOG sources: heat in-leak, flashing, pump recirculation
- BOG handling: re-condense via compressors, use as fuel gas, or send to flare

### 2.4.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Storage

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|----------|---------|-------|-------|
| 1 | BOG rate (typical) | $r_{BOG} = \dot{m}_{vap} / m_{LNG}$ | %/day | Empirical: 0.05–0.15 |
| 2 | Heat in-leak (conduction through wall) | $Q = kA \Delta T / L$ | W | Through perlite/concrete |
| 3 | BOG mass from heat in-leak | $\dot{m}_{vap} = Q / h_{fg}$ | kg/s | $h_{fg,CH_4}$ at −162 °C ≈ 510 kJ/kg |
| 4 | LNG density | $\rho_{LNG} \approx 425 \text{ to } 480$ | kg/m³ | Composition dependent |
| 5 | Volume reduction ratio | $V_{ratio} = V_{gas,std}/V_{LNG}$ | — | ~600 at standard conditions |
| 6 | Tank hold time (no send-out) | $t_h = m_{LNG} / \dot{m}_{BOG}$ | days | Plant outage protection |
| 7 | Hydrostatic pressure at bottom | $P = \rho g H$ | Pa | For design stress |
| 8 | Rollover prevention | $\Delta \rho_{strat} < 0.5$ | kg/m³ | Density stratification limit |

### 2.4.5 Real-World Utility
LNG storage enables:
- Continuous shipping (ships) and send-out decoupling
- Buffering of liquefaction and regasification operations
- Strategic energy security (peaking supply)
- **LNG rollover** is a major hazard — sudden mixing of stratified layers releases massive BOG → must be controlled by **tank mixing pumps** and density monitoring.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Combined Standard Uncertainty for Orifice Mass Flow

### Starting Equation (ISO 5167 Mass Flow)

$$q_m = \dfrac{C \cdot \varepsilon \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2}{\sqrt{1 - \beta^4}} \cdot \sqrt{2 \Delta p \cdot \rho_1}$$

Define compact form:
$$q_m = K \cdot C \cdot \sqrt{\Delta p \cdot \rho_1}$$
where $K$ is a geometric constant, treated as exact (no uncertainty in $d, \beta$ is captured separately).

### Step 1 — Partial Derivatives (Sensitivity Coefficients)

Take natural log to simplify:
$$\ln q_m = \ln K + \ln C + \tfrac{1}{2}\ln(\Delta p) + \tfrac{1}{2}\ln(\rho_1)$$

Differentiate partially w.r.t. each variable:
$$\frac{\partial \ln q_m}{\partial \ln C} = 1 \quad\Rightarrow\quad c_C = q_m / C$$

$$\frac{\partial \ln q_m}{\partial \ln (\Delta p)} = \tfrac{1}{2} \quad\Rightarrow\quad c_{\Delta p} = q_m / (2 \Delta p)$$

$$\frac{\partial \ln q_m}{\partial \ln \rho_1} = \tfrac{1}{2} \quad\Rightarrow\quad c_{\rho_1} = q_m / (2 \rho_1)$$

### Step 2 — Combined Standard Uncertainty (RSS)

Using the relative form for multiplicative models:
$$\left(\dfrac{u_c}{q_m}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{u_C}{C}\right)^2 + \tfrac{1}{4}\left(\dfrac{u_{\Delta p}}{\Delta p}\right)^2 + \tfrac{1}{4}\left(\dfrac{u_{\rho_1}}{\rho_1}\right)^2$$

### Step 3 — Expanded Uncertainty (95% Confidence)

$$U_{q_m} = k \cdot u_c = 2 \cdot q_m \cdot \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{u_C}{C}\right)^2 + \tfrac{1}{4}\left(\dfrac{u_{\Delta p}}{\Delta p}\right)^2 + \tfrac{1}{4}\left(\dfrac{u_{\rho_1}}{\rho_1}\right)^2}$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Example

Given: $q_m = 50$ kg/s, $u_C/C = 0.4\%$, $u_{\Delta p}/\Delta p = 0.3\%$, $u_{\rho_1}/\rho_1 = 0.5\%$.

Compute RSS term-by-term:
$$\left(\dfrac{u_C}{C}\right)^2 = (0.004)^2 = 1.6 \times 10^{-5}$$
$$\tfrac{1}{4}\left(\dfrac{u_{\Delta p}}{\Delta p}\right)^2 = 0.25 \times (0.003)^2 = 2.25 \times 10^{-6}$$
$$\tfrac{1}{4}\left(\dfrac{u_{\rho_1}}{\rho_1}\right)^2 = 0.25 \times (0.005)^2 = 6.25 \times 10^{-6}$$

Sum:
$$\Sigma = 1.6 \times 10^{-5} + 2.25 \times 10^{-6} + 6.25 \times 10^{-6} = 2.475 \times 10^{-5}$$

Square root:
$$\dfrac{u_c}{q_m} = \sqrt{2.475 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.004975 \approx 0.4975\%$$

Expanded (k=2):
$$U = 2 \times 0.4975\% = 0.995\% \approx 1.0\%$$

Final result:
$$q_m = 50 \pm 0.50 \text{ kg/s at 95\% confidence}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Minimum Work of Liquefaction

This is a foundational result for evaluating liquefaction cycle efficiency.

### Starting Point — Reversible Process
For an ideal (reversible) liquefaction from state 1 (gas at $T_0, P_0$) to saturated liquid, the **minimum work** equals the **exergy difference**:

$$w_{min} = (h_1 - h_{liq}) - T_0 (s_1 - s_{liq})$$

### Step 1 — Identify States
- State 1: Feed gas at $T_0 = 298.15$ K, $P_0 = 101.325$ kPa
- State liq: Saturated liquid methane at 101.325 kPa, $T_{sat} = 111.7$ K
- Reference for $h, s$: Often set at saturated liquid at 101.325 kPa (so $h_{liq}, s_{liq}$ are small)

### Step 2 — Property Values (Methane)
- $h_1$ (ideal gas at 298 K) ≈ 0 kJ/kg reference, but in absolute terms $h_1 - h_{liq} \approx 880$ kJ/kg
- $s_1$ (ideal gas at 298 K, 1 atm) ≈ 5.86 kJ/(kg·K) (real gas)
- $s_{liq}$ (sat. liquid) ≈ 4.36 kJ/(kg·K) at 111.7 K
- $\Delta s = s_1 - s_{liq} \approx 1.50$ kJ/(kg·K)

### Step 3 — Substitute into $w_{min}$

$$w_{min} = \Delta h - T_0 \Delta s$$

$$w_{min} = 880 \text{ kJ/kg} - (298.15)(1.50) \text{ kJ/kg}$$

$$w_{min} = 880 - 447.2 = 432.8 \text{ kJ/kg LNG}$$

### Step 4 — Real-Cycle Comparison
- C3MR plant: ~ **900–1000 kJ/kg** → second-law efficiency ~ **45%**
- Classical cascade: ~ **1100–1200 kJ/kg**
- SMR: ~ **1300–1500 kJ/kg**

The "second-law efficiency":
$$\eta_{II} = \dfrac{w_{min}}{w_{actual}} \times 100\%$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Boil-Off Gas (BOG) from Heat In-Leak

### Step 1 — Heat Transfer Through Tank Wall
For a composite cylindrical wall (steel inner + perlite + concrete outer):

$$Q = \dfrac{T_{LNG} - T_{amb}}{R_{total}}$$

where $R_{total} = \sum_i L_i / (k_i A_i)$ (sum of thermal resistances).

For a typical full-containment tank: $R_{total}$ is dominated by perlite insulation. Approximate overall heat flux: $q'' \approx 5 \text{ to } 10 \text{ W/m}^2$ (per unit outer surface).

### Step 2 — BOG Mass Flow
Energy balance on vaporized LNG:

$$Q = \dot{m}_{BOG} \cdot h_{fg}(T_{LNG})$$

For methane at −162 °C: $h_{fg} \approx 510$ kJ/kg.

$$\dot{m}_{BOG} = \dfrac{Q}{h_{fg}}$$

### Step 3 — Numerical Example
A 160,000 m³ LNG tank with $A \approx 25{,}000$ m² outer surface, $q'' = 8$ W/m².

$$Q = 8 \times 25{,}000 = 200{,}000 \text{ W} = 200 \text{ kW}$$

$$\dot{m}_{BOG} = \dfrac{200 \text{ kW}}{510 \text{ kJ/kg}} = 0.392 \text{ kg/s} = 1412 \text{ kg/h}$$

### Step 4 — Daily BOG Percentage
LNG mass in tank: $m_{LNG} = \rho \cdot V = 470 \times 160{,}000 = 7.52 \times 10^7$ kg.

Daily BOG: $1412 \times 24 = 33{,}888$ kg/day.

$$\text{BOG rate} = \dfrac{33{,}888}{7.52 \times 10^7} \times 100\% \approx 0.045\%/\text{day}$$

This is within the typical 0.05–0.15 %/day range — **acceptable**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Cycle Selection Decision

| Criterion | Cascade | C3MR | SMR | Expander (N₂) |
|-----------|---------|------|-----|---------------|
| Capacity (MTPA) | > 2 | 1–5 | 0.1–2 | 0.05–0.5 |
| CAPEX index | 1.3 | 1.2 | 1.0 | 0.9 |
| OPEX (kWh/kg) | 0.34 | 0.28 | 0.38 | 0.45 |
| Complexity | Very High | High | Medium | Low |
| Train size (max) | 3.5 MTPA | 7.8 MTPA (Qatargas) | 1.5 MTPA | 0.5 MTPA |
| FLNG suitability | No | Marginal | Yes | Yes |
| Operability | Poor | Good | Very Good | Excellent |
| Licensors | Air Products | APCI (Air Products) | Black & Veatch, Linde | Black & Veatch, Cryostar |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 LNG Value Chain (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Wellhead Gas] --> nodeB[Inlet Separation]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Acid Gas Removal AGR]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Dehydration TEG Molecular Sieve]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Mercury Removal]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Heavy Hydrocarbon Removal]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Gas Treating Spec Verification]
    nodeG --> nodeH[Liquefaction Cycle C3MR or SMR]
    nodeH --> nodeI[LNG Storage Tank Full Containment]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[LNG Loading Arm]
    nodeJ --> nodeK[LNG Carrier Tanker]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Regasification Terminal]
    nodeL --> nodeM[Send Out Gas Pipeline]
    nodeI --> nodeN[BOG Management Compressor Recondenser]
    nodeN --> nodeH
    nodeN --> nodeO[Plant Fuel Gas System]
```

## 4.2 Gas Treating Process Flow


```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Feed Gas +35C] --> nodeB[Propane Pre Cooler]
```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    nodeC[Input Variable 2 Temperature] --> nodeD[Sensitivity Coefficient c2]

    nodeB --> nodeG[Root Sum of Squares RSS]
    nodeD --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeG
```

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEPOT528]
**Explain briefly the difference between systematic and random uncertainties in flow measurement, with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Type | Description | Example |
|------|-------------|---------|
| **Systematic (Bias)** | Consistent, repeatable error; can be corrected by calibration | Orifice plate installed with non-standard upstream straight length causing 0.5% over-reading |
| **Random (Precision)** | Scatter of readings under identical conditions; follows statistical distribution | Fluctuation of differential pressure transmitter output by ±0.1% due to electronic noise |

Systematic uncertainties **do not average out** with repeated measurements; they shift the mean. Random uncertainties **reduce** with $\sqrt{N}$ repetitions. In custody transfer, both must be quantified and reported separately in the meter uncertainty budget.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEPOT528]
**List the typical contaminants removed during gas treating before LNG liquefaction and state the maximum allowable limits.**

**Model Answer:**

| Contaminant | Typical LNG Spec | Effect if not removed |
|-------------|------------------|------------------------|
| CO₂ | < 50 ppmv (often < 30) | Freezes at −78 °C, blocks exchangers |
| H₂S | < 3.5 mg/Nm³ (~2.5 ppmv) | Stress corrosion cracking, toxic LNG |
| H₂O | < 1 ppmv | Hydrate/ice formation |
| Mercury (Hg) | < 0.01 µg/Nm³ | Aluminum embrittlement |
| C₅⁺ | < 100 ppmv | Wax/coke deposition |
| N₂ | < 1 mol% | Reduces LNG heating value |

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PEPOT528]

**(a)** Discuss the **main sources of uncertainty** in multiphase flow measurement used in oil and gas production, and describe the **statistical method** to combine them into a single expanded uncertainty value. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Coriolis mass flow meter is used for custody transfer of natural gas liquids. The following relative standard uncertainties (1σ) are reported for the individual inputs:

- Density measurement: 0.20%
- Mass flow calibration: 0.15%
- Pressure transmitter: 0.10%
- Temperature transmitter: 0.10%
- Composition correction: 0.25%

Calculate the **expanded uncertainty (95% confidence)** using sensitivity exponents (1, 1, 1, 1, 1 for additive). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — (a):**

Sources of uncertainty in multiphase flow:
1. **Primary device calibration** — venturi, orifice, V-cone, or multiphase meter
2. **Installation effects** — insufficient straight pipe, swirl, vibration
3. **Fluid property variability** — GVF, water cut, salinity, density
4. **Process variability** — flow rate fluctuations, slugging, pressure/temperature drift
5. **Secondary instrumentation** — PT, TT, dP transmitter
6. **Sampling / composition analysis** — GC repeatability

**Combination Method:**

Step 1: Compute sensitivity coefficients $c_i = \partial f / \partial x_i$.

Step 2: Compute combined standard uncertainty using RSS (GUM):
$$u_c(y) = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{N} c_i^2 u_i^2}$$

Step 3: Apply coverage factor k = 2 (95% confidence) for expanded uncertainty:
$$U = k \cdot u_c = 2 u_c$$

Step 4: For purely multiplicative models, simplify to relative form:
$$(u_c/f)^2 = \sum a_i^2 (u_i/x_i)^2$$

**Model Solution — (b):**

Step 1 — Apply relative RSS combination with all exponents $a_i = 1$:

$$(u_c/f)^2 = (0.0020)^2 + (0.0015)^2 + (0.0010)^2 + (0.0010)^2 + (0.0025)^2$$

Compute each square:
- $(0.0020)^2 = 4.0 \times 10^{-6}$
- $(0.0015)^2 = 2.25 \times 10^{-6}$
- $(0.0010)^2 = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$
- $(0.0010)^2 = 1.0 \times 10^{-6}$
- $(0.0025)^2 = 6.25 \times 10^{-6}$

Step 2 — Sum:
$$\Sigma = 4.0 + 2.25 + 1.0 + 1.0 + 6.25 = 14.5 \times 10^{-6}$$

Step 3 — Standard uncertainty:
$$u_c/f = \sqrt{14.5 \times 10^{-6}} = \sqrt{1.45 \times 10^{-5}} = 0.003808$$

$$u_c/f \approx 0.381\%$$

Step 4 — Expanded uncertainty (k=2):
$$U = 2 \times 0.381\% = 0.762\%$$

**Final answer: U ≈ ±0.76% at 95% confidence**

---

### Question B [KTU University Exam - May 2024, PEPOT528]

**(a)** With a **neat block flow diagram**, describe the **Propane Pre-cooled Mixed Refrigerant (C3MR) liquefaction cycle**. Explain the role of each refrigerant stream. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **C3MR, Single Mixed Refrigerant (SMR), and Expander-based** liquefaction cycles on the basis of specific power consumption, CAPEX, scale suitability, and second-law efficiency. Identify the **typical operating range and licensors** for each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution — (a):**

**C3MR Block Flow:**

1. **Feed Gas Treatment Section:** Sweet, dry natural gas at ~30–40 °C and 50–70 bar enters the pre-cooling section.

2. **Propane Pre-cooling Loop:**
   - Compressed in three stages with inter-cooling (P1 → P2 → P3)
   - Condensed by air-cooling or water-cooling
   - Throttled to low pressure (1.0–1.5 bar)
   - Evaporates at three temperature levels (+5, −25, −35 °C) to cool the feed gas and the mixed refrigerant.

3. **Main Cryogenic Heat Exchanger (MCHE):** Coiled-wound or plate-fin exchanger where:
   - Pre-cooled feed gas (−35 °C) flows in tubes
   - Mixed refrigerant (MR) provides cooling in three streams: **HP MR (vaporizes, high pressure), MP MR (condenses partially), LP MR (vaporizes, low pressure)**
   - The feed is condensed and subcooled to LNG at −162 °C.

4. **MR Compressor:** Multi-stage centrifugal compressor (typically 3–4 stages) compresses the MR vapor that returns from the MCHE.

5. **MR Separator:** The MP MR is separated as liquid and used for the mid-temperature duty.

6. **LNG Expander / JT Valve:** LNG is let down to storage pressure via expander or JT valve, recovering cold for MR pre-cooling.

**Roles:**
- **Propane:** Pre-cools from +35 °C to −35 °C (high duty, medium temperature)
- **MR (N₂ + CH₄ + C₂H₄ + C₃H₈ + iC₄H₁₀):** Liquefies NG from −35 °C to −162 °C, matches variable NG cooling curve, maximizes efficiency.

**Model Solution — (b):**

| Cycle | Specific Power (kWh/kg) | CAPEX (rel.) | Scale (MTPA) | η_II (%) | Licensors |
|-------|--------------------------|--------------|--------------|----------|-----------|
| **C3MR** | 0.27–0.30 | 1.2 (high) | 1.0–7.8 | ~45–50 | APCI (Air Products) |
| **SMR** | 0.35–0.40 | 1.0 (medium) | 0.1–1.5 | ~30–35 | Linde, B&V, APCI |
| **Expander (N₂)** | 0.40–0.50 | 0.9 (low) | 0.05–0.5 | ~22–28 | B&V, Cryostar, Atlas Copco |

**Selection Guidelines:**
- **C3MR** — Large base-load plants (>1 MTPA), high efficiency required, multiple trains (Qatargas, Gorgon)
- **SMR** — Small-medium scale, FLNG (floating LNG), good operability, fewer rotating equipment
- **Expander (N₂)** — Peak-shaving, micro-scale, FLNG, low CAPEX, fast start-up

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Module 4:**
> 1. **Uncertainty combination:** Students often add uncertainties **linearly** instead of in **quadrature (RSS)** for independent random errors. Always use RSS for Type A (random), linear sum only for fully correlated Type B (systematic).
> 2. **Gas treating confusion:** Confusing **TEG dehydration** (mass transfer, water removal) with **amine treating** (chemical reaction, acid gas removal). They are **separate units**, often in series, with different chemistry.
> 3. **Cycle selection logic:** Students describe C3MR as "just one cycle" but it is **two separate loops** (propane + MR). Forgetting to mention **MR composition adjustment** for cooling curve matching loses 2 marks.
> 4. **Storage tank types:** Listing "single, double, full containment" without giving **secondary containment material** (concrete vs steel) and **use case** loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **BOG management:** Forgetting to discuss **LNG rollover** phenomenon — the safety-critical mixing of stratified layers — costs full credit on safety questions.
> 6. **Units and basis:** Using $Sm^3/hr$ in one line and $Nm^3/hr$ in another without declaring **reference conditions** is marked down.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 Uncertainties in Flow
- Combine **independent random** uncertainties in **quadrature (RSS)**
- Combine **fully correlated systematic** uncertainties **linearly**
- **Expanded uncertainty** at 95% confidence uses **k = 2**
- Custody transfer target: **±0.25% to ±1.0%**
- Orifice: discharge coefficient C depends on $\beta$ and $Re_D$

### 🔑 Gas Treating Before Liquefaction
- **Sequence:** Inlet separation → AGR (amine) → Dehydration (TEG/MS) → Hg removal → Heavy HC removal
- **CO₂ spec:** < 30–50 ppmv; **H₂O spec:** < 1 ppmv
- **H₂S** causes **SSC**; **Hg** causes **aluminum embrittlement**
- **Amine processes:** MEA, DEA, MDEA, SULFINOL
- **Molecular sieve 4A** is the workhorse for final dehydration polish

### 🔑 Liquefaction Cycles
- **Cascade:** Propane + Ethylene + Methane, high efficiency, high CAPEX
- **C3MR:** Propane pre-cool + MR main liquefaction — **industry standard for >1 MTPA**
- **SMR:** One MR loop, suited for small-scale and FLNG
- **Expander:** Turbo-expander with N₂, lowest CAPEX, lowest efficiency
- **MR composition** is tuned to match the **NG cooling curve** (glide matching)
- **Minimum work of liquefaction** for CH₄ ≈ **0.43 kWh/kg**

### 🔑 Storage of LNG
- **Full containment tanks:** 9% Ni steel inner + pre-stressed concrete outer
- **BOG rate:** **0.05–0.15 %/day** for well-insulated tanks
- **BOG handling:** recondense → MR compressor suction; excess → fuel gas
- **LNG rollover** is a major safety hazard; managed by **tank mixing pumps**
- **Volume reduction:** ~600× vs gas at standard conditions
- **LNG density:** ~ 425–480 kg/m³ (composition dependent)

### ⚠ Critical Industrial Safety Points
- **LNG spills** on water → rapid vaporization, no ignition of water (myth)
- **Rollover** mitigation → continuous density monitoring
- **BOG accumulation** → must never reach flammable range (5–15 vol% in air)
- **Tank overpressure** → relief to BOG system, never direct atmosphere
- **Hydrogen embrittlement** in 9% Ni steel is rare but possible if H₂S residual > spec

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT528 - OIL AND NATURAL GAS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transportation, Regasification and Cold Utilization of LNG, Safety Measures and Environmental Considerations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to LNG Transportation, Regasification & Cold Utilization

The LNG value chain culminates in the safe delivery of liquefied natural gas to demand centers, where it is reconverted (regasified) into gaseous form for pipeline distribution. Because LNG is stored at approximately **-162 °C (111 K)** and atmospheric pressure, its transport, storage, and regasification present unique cryogenic engineering challenges that differ fundamentally from conventional hydrocarbon logistics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> **LNG Transportation** is the cryogenic movement of liquefied natural gas (predominantly methane, CH₄) in insulated marine, road, or rail carriers from the liquefaction plant to the receiving terminal. **Regasification** is the controlled conversion of LNG back to gaseous form (NG) by heat addition using seawater, air, or combustion. **Cold Utilization (Cold Energy Recovery)** is the engineering practice of harnessing the latent cold exergy of LNG (≈ **830 kJ/kg**) for secondary applications such as power generation, air separation, or refrigeration.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy: The "Frozen Thermos" Pipeline

Imagine a giant, super-insulated thermos flask carrying a sub-zero liquid across an ocean. That is exactly what an LNG carrier is — a sophisticated floating cryogenic dewar. Just as a thermos keeps your cold drink cold, the LNG carrier's double-wall insulation and membrane/moss spheres prevent heat ingress.

When this "cold thermos" reaches a coastal city, we cannot just pour LNG into domestic pipes — the gas burners need **gas**, not liquid. So we carefully warm the LNG back to room temperature using seawater (which is at 15–25 °C), essentially "melting" the cold back into gaseous form. This is **regasification**.

The "cold" that was originally removed to liquefy the gas is real engineering energy — a hidden treasure. Engineers have learned to tap this **cold exergy** to:
- Run power cycles (cold energy → electricity)
- Freeze food in cold warehouses
- Separate air into oxygen and nitrogen
- Desalinate seawater

This whole journey — from liquefaction to end-user — is what the LNG value chain is all about.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metrics to Memorize for KTU Exams:**
> - LNG boiling point: **-162 °C (111 K, -260 °F)**
> - LNG density: **~450 kg/m³** (about half the density of water)
> - Methane LHV: **50 MJ/kg** (approx. 22,000 BTU/lb)
> - Flammable range in air: **5% to 15% by volume**
> - Auto-ignition temperature: **537 °C**
> - LNG cold exergy available: **~830 kJ/kg**

## 1.3 The Complete LNG Value Chain — Block Perspective

The post-liquefaction portion of the LNG chain involves four interconnected stages:

1. **Loading** at the liquefaction terminal (sub-cooled LNG pumped into carriers)
2. **Marine/Road Transportation** under cryogenic, atmospheric conditions
3. **Receiving, Storage & Regasification** at the import terminal
4. **Cold Energy Utilization** and **Distribution** to end users

Each stage has distinct engineering, safety, and environmental implications that will be discussed in detail in the following sections.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 LNG Transportation Modes

### 2.1.1 Marine LNG Carriers (The Dominant Mode)

LNG is transported globally by specialized double-hulled vessels equipped with independent cargo containment systems. KTU examinations frequently test the three main containment technologies:

**A) Moss Spherical (Independent Tank Type B)**
- Aluminum alloy 5083 spheres (typically 4 spheres per vessel)
- Each sphere supported by a cylindrical skirt
- Highest reliability; minimum partial-fill restrictions
- Lower boil-off rate (~0.08%/day) due to spherical geometry (lowest surface-area-to-volume ratio)
- More expensive and slower to construct

**B) Membrane Type (GTT Mark III / NO96)**
- Primary and secondary membranes of corrugated stainless steel (304L) or Invar alloy
- Insulation is load-bearing (R-PUF — Reinforced Polyurethane Foam)
- Lower construction cost, higher cargo capacity for given vessel size
- Boil-off rate: ~0.10–0.15%/day
- Most common in modern vessels

**C) Structural Prismatic (SPB) Type B**
- Rectangular aluminum tanks, self-supporting
- Less commonly built

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boil-Off Gas (BOG) Management:**
> Due to inevitable heat ingress (~0.1–0.15%/day), a fraction of LNG continuously vaporizes. BOG is either:
> - Reliquefied (in dual-fuel vessels using re-liquefaction plants)
> - Combusted in engines (steam or gas turbine propulsion)
> - Used as fuel for ship power
> - Returned to shore (in older designs)

### 2.1.2 Land Transportation (Road & Rail)

- **Road tankers:** Double-wall vacuum-insulated, capacity 40–60 m³, used for short-distance distribution
- **Rail tankers:** Larger capacity (~100 m³), used where pipelines are unavailable
- **Cryogenic ISO containers:** Standard 40-ft intermodal tanks (~17.5 m³) for multimodal logistics

### 2.1.3 Pipeline Transportation of NG

After regasification, natural gas is transmitted through high-pressure cross-country pipelines (operating at 70–100 bar, with compressor stations every 80–120 km).

## 2.2 LNG Regasification Technologies

Regasification terminals convert LNG (liquid, -162 °C) into NG (gas, ambient) by adding heat. The **regasification capacity** is typically measured in **MMSCFD** or **MTPA** (Million Tonnes Per Annum).

### 2.2.1 Open Rack Vaporizer (ORV)

The most common and economical method (used at ~70% of global terminals). LNG is sprayed over finned tubes while ambient-temperature seawater circulates inside the tubes, transferring heat to LNG and vaporizing it.

- Efficiency: Uses ~250,000 m³ of seawater per hour for a 5 MTPA terminal
- Lowest operating cost (no fuel required)
- Environmentally significant: cooling of discharged seawater must stay within regulatory limits (typically ΔT ≤ 7 °C at the discharge point)

### 2.2.2 Submerged Combustion Vaporizer (SCV)

- A submerged combustion burner heats a water bath
- Hot water circulates through heat exchanger tubes containing LNG
- Uses natural gas as fuel (~2–4% of throughput)
- Faster start-up than ORV; preferred for peak-demand and cold climates

### 2.2.3 Ambient Air Vaporizer (AAV)

- LNG passes through finned tubes cooled by ambient air
- Used for small-scale / peak-shaving facilities
- No fuel or seawater needed; environmentally cleanest for coastal areas
- Slowest heat transfer, requires large footprint

### 2.2.4 Heat Medium Vaporizer (HMV) / Intermediate Fluid Vaporizer (IFV)

- Uses a closed-loop intermediate fluid (propane, butane, or a glycol mixture)
- Heat exchanged between seawater and intermediate fluid, then between fluid and LNG
- Avoids direct seawater contact with LNG, minimizing environmental concerns
- Higher capital cost; used in environmentally sensitive zones

### 2.2.5 Exchanger-Based Cold Utilization (Integrated Concept)

When LNG is regasified for cold utilization applications (power generation), it can be expanded or used as a cold sink in a thermodynamic cycle (e.g., LNG-fired combined cycle with cold exergy recovery).

## 2.3 Cold Utilization of LNG

### 2.3.1 Sources of LNG Cold Energy

LNG arriving at the terminal carries significant **exergy** (available work), calculated as the difference in enthalpy between LNG and an ambient reference:

$$e_{cold} = h_{LNG} - h_{NG,@T_{amb}} - T_{amb}(s_{LNG} - s_{NG,@T_{amb}})$$

For typical LNG composition: $e_{cold} \approx 830 \text{ kJ/kg LNG}$

### 2.3.2 Applications of Cold Energy Recovery

1. **LNG Cold Power Generation**
   - Direct expansion through turbines (e.g., the OS-City and Phoenix Power concepts in Japan)
   - Combined cycles (LNG vapor → gas turbine; cold LNG → air separation)
   - Typical power output: 30–50 kWh per tonne of LNG

2. **Air Separation Unit (ASU) Co-location**
   - Cryogenic distillation of air (O₂, N₂, Ar production) using LNG as cold sink
   - Reduces ASU power consumption by ~50%

3. **Cold Storage Warehouses**
   - LNG vaporization for cold chain logistics (-30 °C to -60 °C storage)

4. **CO₂ Liquefaction and Seawater Desalination**
   - LNG cold utilized to condense CO₂ for EOR or sequestration

5. **LNG-to-Power with Cryogenic CO₂ Capture**

## 2.4 Safety Measures in LNG Operations

### 2.4.1 Primary Hazards

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Three Core Hazards of LNG:**
> 1. **Cryogenic burns and embrittlement** of materials (carbon steel becomes brittle below -29 °C)
> 2. **Flammability** (5–15 vol% in air)
> 3. **Rapid Phase Transition (RPT)** — explosive boiling of water/lNG contact
> 4. **Rollover** — sudden, violent mixing of stratified LNG layers
> 5. **Asphyxiation** — displaces oxygen in confined spaces

### 2.4.2 Key Design Safety Standards

- **NFPA 59A**: Standard for the Production, Storage, and Handling of LNG
- **IGC Code (IMO)**: International Code for the Construction and Equipment of Ships carrying Liquefied Gases in Bulk
- **SIGTTO**: Society of International Gas Tanker and Terminal Operators
- **API 620**: Design and Construction of Large, Welded, Low-Pressure Storage Tanks
- **EN 1473**: Installation and Equipment for LNG — Design of Onshore Installations

### 2.4.3 Safety Zones and Spill Control

- **Exclusion Zone (EZ):** Area where no ignition source is permitted — radius typically 240 m
- **Dispersion Calculations** are required for thermal radiation > 5 kW/m² (this is the threshold for second-degree burns in 30 seconds)
- **Dikes and impounding basins** sized to hold 110% of largest single tank volume
- **Vapor dispersion modeling** typically uses DEGADIS or PHAST software

## 2.5 Environmental Considerations

### 2.5.1 GHG Emissions Across the LNG Chain

| Stage | CO₂-eq (kg/MMBtu NG delivered) |
|---|---|
| Liquefaction (with electric drives) | 3 – 8 |
| Transportation (LNG carriers) | 5 – 15 |
| Regasification | 1 – 4 |
| Distribution | 1 – 3 |
| **Total (full chain)** | **~ 15 – 30** |

For comparison, coal emits ~94 kg CO₂/MMBtu.

### 2.5.2 Methane Slip

A critical environmental concern is methane slip — unburned methane released from:
- Gas turbine combustion
- Boil-off gas venting
- Loading/unloading operations
- Compressor seals

Methane has a **GWP₁₀₀ of 28–34** (per IPCC AR5) or **~84** (per IPCC AR6), making it a potent short-term climate forcer.

### 2.5.3 Marine and Aquatic Impacts

- **Seawater thermal pollution** from ORVs (mitigated by diffuser design)
- **Chlorination** (biocide treatment to prevent biofouling) generates halogenated by-products
- **Noise and ship traffic** impacts on marine ecosystems
- **Risk of marine spills** (low probability but high consequence)

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units / Constants |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q_{regas} = \dot{m}_{LNG} \cdot \Delta h_{vap}$ | Heat required to regasify LNG | $\dot{m}$ in kg/s, $\Delta h_{vap} \approx 510$ kJ/kg |
| 2 | $\Delta h_{vap,LNG} = h_{g,T_{amb}} - h_{L,-162°C}$ | Latent heat of vaporization at constant pressure | ~510 kJ/kg for pure methane |
| 3 | $e_{cold} \approx 830$ kJ/kg | Specific exergy of LNG cold | Reference: $T_0 = 298$ K, $P_0 = 1$ atm |
| 4 | $W_{turbine} = \dot{m}_{LNG} \cdot (h_{in} - h_{out}) \cdot \eta_{isen}$ | Cold power via direct expansion | kW |
| 5 | $LHV_{CH_4} = 50.0$ MJ/kg | Lower heating value of methane | 802 kJ/mol |
| 6 | $T_{boil,CH_4} = 111$ K ($-162$°C) | Normal boiling point of LNG | K or °C |
| 7 | $\rho_{LNG} \approx 450$ kg/m³ | LNG density at $-162$°C | kg/m³ |
| 8 | $BOR = \frac{\dot{m}_{BOG}}{\dot{m}_{LNG}} \times 100\%$ | Boil-off rate per day | 0.08% – 0.15%/day typical |
| 9 | $Q_{radiation} = \sigma \cdot \varepsilon \cdot (T_s^4 - T_a^4)$ | Thermal radiation from fireball | Stefan-Boltzmann $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²K⁴ |
| 10 | $LFL = 5\%, UFL = 15\%$ | Methane flammability limits in air | vol% |
| 11 | $R_{ED} = \frac{V_{dispersion}}{Q_{release}} = f(T,P,wind)$ | Effective dilution ratio for vapor dispersion | dimensionless |
| 12 | $CO_2$-eq $= \sum_i m_i \cdot GWP_i$ | Total greenhouse gas emissions | kg CO₂-eq |
| 13 | $E_{LNG} = 52$ GJ/tonne (approx) | Energy content of 1 tonne LNG | 1 tonne LNG ≈ 1,360 m³ NG |
| 14 | $V_{LNG} = \frac{m}{\rho_{LNG}}$ | Volume of LNG stored | m³ |
| 15 | $t_{rollover} \propto \frac{\Delta T \cdot h^2}{\alpha \cdot g \cdot \beta}$ | Rollover time constant | h, α = thermal diffusivity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Tip for Formulas:** Memorize the **three constants of LNG**:
> 1. $-162$°C (storage temperature)
> 2. $450$ kg/m³ (density)
> 3. $830$ kJ/kg (cold exergy)
> These three appear in almost every KTU numerical.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Heat Duty of a Regasification Terminal

A common KTU 14-mark problem is to compute the regasification heat duty, vessel requirements, or BOG management. Below is the full derivation of the regasification heat load.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

An LNG terminal receives LNG at $-162$ °C, $1.0$ atm, and is to deliver natural gas at $5$ °C, $70$ bar. The throughput is $\dot{m} = 1000$ kg/s. Calculate the heat duty required at the regasifier.

### 3.1.2 Solution — Step by Step

**Step 1:** Identify state points. LNG arrives as saturated liquid (state 1) and is delivered as superheated gas (state 4).

- State 1: Saturated liquid LNG at $T_1 = 111$ K, $P_1 = 1.0$ atm
- State 2: Saturated vapor at $T_1 = 111$ K (after vaporization)
- State 3: Compressed to 70 bar (isentropic or with compressor)
- State 4: Heated to $T_4 = 278$ K ($5$ °C)

**Step 2:** Determine specific enthalpy at each state (using methane property tables).

For pure methane:
- $h_1$ (sat. liquid at 111 K) $\approx -285$ kJ/kg
- $h_2$ (sat. vapor at 111 K) $\approx 225$ kJ/kg
- $h_3$ (compressed to 70 bar) $\approx 285$ kJ/kg
- $h_4$ (superheated at 278 K, 70 bar) $\approx 365$ kJ/kg

**Step 3:** Compute total enthalpy change.

$$\Delta h_{total} = h_4 - h_1 = 365 - (-285) = 650 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

This can be split into:
- Latent heat of vaporization: $\Delta h_{vap} = h_2 - h_1 = 225 - (-285) = 510$ kJ/kg
- Sensible heating of vapor: $h_3 - h_2 = 60$ kJ/kg
- Compressor work contribution: $h_3 - h_2 = 60$ kJ/kg
- Sensible heating of NG to 5 °C: $h_4 - h_3 = 80$ kJ/kg

**Step 4:** Calculate total heat duty.

$$\begin{aligned}
Q_{regas} &= \dot{m}_{LNG} \cdot \Delta h_{total} \\
Q_{regas} &= 1000 \text{ kg/s} \times 650 \text{ kJ/kg} \\
Q_{regas} &= 650{,}000 \text{ kW} \\
Q_{regas} &= 650 \text{ MW (thermal)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5:** Equivalence in MMSCFD (Million Standard Cubic Feet per Day).

Using $1$ kg LNG $\approx 1.36$ m³ NG $\approx 48$ scf NG:

$$\dot{V}_{NG} = 1000 \text{ kg/s} \times 48 \text{ scf/kg} = 48{,}000 \text{ scf/s}$$

Converting to MMSCFD:

$$\dot{V}_{NG} = 48{,}000 \times 86{,}400 \text{ s/day} \times 10^{-6} \approx 4147 \text{ MMSCFD}$$

**Step 6:** Sanity check with typical ORV capacity.

A single large ORV has capacity ~200 MMSCFD. We need:

$$N_{ORVs} = \frac{4147}{200} \approx 21 \text{ ORVs (in parallel) plus standby units)}$$

This matches a typical 7–10 MTPA LNG terminal capacity.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Cold Exergy of LNG

LNG cold exergy represents the **maximum theoretical work** that can be extracted from LNG cold as it equilibrates to ambient conditions.

### 3.2.1 Definition

The specific exergy is the maximum useful work obtainable as the system returns reversibly from state $(T_{LNG}, P_{LNG})$ to the dead state $(T_0, P_0)$:

$$e_{cold} = (h - h_0) - T_0(s - s_0)$$

### 3.2.2 Numerical Computation

Using $T_0 = 298$ K, $P_0 = 1$ atm:

- $h_{LNG} = -285$ kJ/kg (sat. liquid at 111 K, 1 atm)
- $s_{LNG} = 4.38$ kJ/(kg·K)
- $h_0$ (NG at 298 K, 1 atm) = 0 (reference)
- $s_0$ (NG at 298 K, 1 atm) = 11.6 kJ/(kg·K)

$$\begin{aligned}
e_{cold} &= (-285 - 0) - 298 \times (4.38 - 11.6) \\
e_{cold} &= -285 - 298 \times (-7.22) \\
e_{cold} &= -285 + 2151 \\
e_{cold} &\approx 866 \text{ kJ/kg}
\end{aligned}$$

This aligns with the commonly cited **~830 kJ/kg** (the slight difference arises from LNG composition being mostly methane but with some ethane and heavier components).

### 3.2.3 Power Output Estimate

For a 5 MTPA terminal:

$$\dot{m} = \frac{5 \times 10^9 \text{ kg/yr}}{365 \times 86400 \text{ s/day}} = 158.5 \text{ kg/s}$$

Assuming 50% conversion efficiency to electricity:

$$W_{elec} = 158.5 \times 866 \times 0.5 = 68{,}650 \text{ kW} \approx 68.6 \text{ MW}$$

This is a substantial by-product — equivalent to powering ~70,000 homes.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Boil-Off Gas (BOG) Generation

### 3.3.1 Standard Formula

$$BOR = \frac{Q_{leak}}{m_{LNG} \cdot \Delta h_{vap}} \times 100\%$$

Where $Q_{leak}$ is the heat ingress rate into the LNG tank.

### 3.3.2 Example

A spherical Moss tank of radius $R = 18$ m holds $m = 25{,}000$ tonnes LNG. The heat leak rate is $Q_{leak} = 2.5$ kW (typical for state-of-the-art insulation). Calculate the BOG rate.

**Step 1:** Convert heat leak to kJ/day.

$$Q_{leak,daily} = 2.5 \text{ kW} \times 86400 \text{ s/day} = 216{,}000 \text{ kJ/day}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate mass of LNG vaporized.

$$m_{BOG} = \frac{Q_{leak,daily}}{\Delta h_{vap}} = \frac{216{,}000}{510} \approx 423.5 \text{ kg/day}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate BOR.

$$BOR = \frac{423.5}{25{,}000{,}000} \times 100\% = 0.00169\%/\text{day}$$

This is exceptionally low — typical of the best modern Moss tanks. Realistic values for working vessels: 0.08% to 0.15%/day.

---

## 3.4 Vapor Dispersion Distance for LNG Spill

A critical safety parameter: when LNG spills and vaporizes, the flammable vapor cloud travels downwind. The dispersion distance to LFL (5%) is given approximately by:

$$X_{LFL} = \frac{\dot{V}_L \cdot \rho_L / \rho_{vapor}}{K \cdot u_{wind} \cdot h_{mix}} \cdot \text{(correction factor)}$$

**Simpler empirical correlation (Britter-McQuaid model):**

$$X_{LFL} = \alpha \cdot \left(\frac{Q_0}{u_{wind}}\right)^{0.5}$$

Where $Q_0$ is the volumetric release rate and $\alpha$ is a stability-dependent constant (~5–10 for neutral conditions).

> [!NOTE]
> For a 1 m³ LNG instantaneous spill at neutral stability (D stability class, $u = 5$ m/s):
> $X_{LFL} \approx 90$ to 150 m. This defines the inner hazard zone.

---

## 3.5 Comparison Matrix: ORV vs. SCV vs. AAV vs. IFV

| Parameter | ORV | SCV | AAV | IFV |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Heat source | Seawater | Natural gas + air | Ambient air | Seawater via intermediate fluid |
| Fuel consumption | 0 | 2–4% of throughput | 0 | 0 |
| Capex | High | Low | High | Very High |
| Opex | Very Low | Medium | Very Low | Low |
| Seawater impact | High | None | None | Moderate |
| Start-up time | Slow | Fast | Slow | Medium |
| Cold utilization compatible | Difficult | Difficult | Difficult | Easy |
| Best suited to | Coastal base-load | Peaking/backup | Small peak-shaving | Environmentally sensitive zones |

## 3.6 Numerical Practice Problem (KTU Pattern)

**Problem (Self-Evaluation):**
A 2 MTPA LNG regasification terminal uses ORVs. Seawater is available at 18 °C and discharged back at 4 °C. Calculate:
(a) Daily volume of seawater required.
(b) Number of ORVs needed if each ORV handles 1.5×10⁵ m³ of seawater per hour.
(c) Annual fuel savings vs. SCV-based terminal (assume SCV uses 3% LNG).

**Given:** $C_{p,sw} = 4.18$ kJ/(kg·K), $\rho_{sw} = 1025$ kg/m³, $\Delta h_{vap,LNG} = 510$ kJ/kg

**Solution:**

(a) LNG mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m}_{LNG} = \frac{2 \times 10^9 \text{ kg/yr}}{365 \times 86400 \text{ s}} = 63.4 \text{ kg/s}$$

Heat duty:

$$Q = 63.4 \times 510 = 32{,}334 \text{ kW} = 32.3 \text{ MW}$$

Seawater mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m}_{sw} = \frac{Q}{C_p \cdot \Delta T} = \frac{32{,}334}{4.18 \times 14} = 552.5 \text{ kg/s}$$

Seawater volumetric flow rate:

$$\dot{V}_{sw} = \frac{552.5}{1025} = 0.539 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 1940 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$$

Daily volume:

$$V_{sw,daily} = 1940 \times 24 = 46{,}560 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \approx 17 \text{ million m}^3/\text{yr}$$

(b) Number of ORVs:

$$N_{ORV} = \frac{1940}{1.5 \times 10^5 / 1000} = \frac{1940}{150} = 12.9 \approx 13 \text{ ORVs}$$

(c) Annual LNG savings = 3% of 2 MTPA = 0.06 MTPA = 60,000 tonnes/yr
At $LHV = 50$ GJ/tonne, energy saved = **3,000,000 GJ/yr = 833 GWh/yr** (worth several million dollars annually).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Boil-Off Gas (BOG) in LNG Carriers
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**

**Q:** Define "boil-off gas" (BOG) in LNG carriers. State two methods used to manage BOG during a voyage.

**Model Answer:**

**Boil-Off Gas (BOG)** is the continuous natural vaporization of LNG inside the cargo containment system due to unavoidable heat ingress from the surroundings, even with the most advanced insulation systems. Typical BOG rates are 0.08%–0.15% of cargo mass per day depending on containment type (Moss ~0.08%, Membrane ~0.10–0.15%).

**Two Methods of BOG Management:**
1. **Reliquefaction** — Onboard reliquefaction plants (e.g., Hamworthy, Linde, Cryostar) compress, cool, and re-liquefy the BOG. Used in modern LNG carriers with electric or steam-driven reliquefaction units.
2. **Use as Fuel** — BOG is combusted in dual-fuel propulsion engines (DFDE: Dual-Fuel Diesel Electric) or in gas turbine propulsion, providing propulsive power and auxiliary energy.

*(Other acceptable methods: re-condensation, gas combustion unit/GCU, return to shore via small carrier.)*

---

### Question 2: LNG Rollover Phenomenon
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**

**Q:** What is "LNG rollover"? Mention its primary causes and consequences.

**Model Answer:**

**LNG Rollover** is a sudden, violent, and self-accelerating mixing of two stratified layers of LNG within a storage tank, occurring when a heavier (denser) lower layer is suddenly destabilized and rapidly mixes with a lighter (warmer) upper layer. The mixing releases large volumes of vapor (BOG) in a short period, causing a sudden pressure spike that can damage the tank and vent lines.

**Primary Causes:**
1. Accumulation of heavier LNG (e.g., rich in nitrogen or higher hydrocarbons) at the bottom, with lighter LNG above.
2. Densification of the top layer by mixing with newly introduced (heavier) LNG.
3. Long residence times without mixing (e.g., during ship idling).

**Consequences:**
- Sudden large BOG release; potential tank over-pressurization
- Venting of flammable methane; risk of fire/explosion
- Mechanical stress on tank structure
- Cascading loss of containment

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): Regasification, Cold Utilization & Environmental Aspects

**[KTU University Exam – Model 2024, Module 4]**

**Q A (a):** With a neat block diagram, describe the working of an Open Rack Vaporizer (ORV). List any four advantages of ORVs over other regasification methods. **(7 marks)**

**Q A (b):** Explain the principle of LNG cold energy recovery. A regasification terminal handles 3 MTPA of LNG. Calculate the available cold exergy per second, and the theoretical electrical power that can be generated at 45% conversion efficiency. Assume cold exergy of LNG = 830 kJ/kg. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer — Question A (a)

**Working of Open Rack Vaporizer (ORV):**

An ORV is a heat exchanger in which LNG is vaporized by direct heat exchange with ambient-temperature seawater. The construction consists of:

- A **fin tube bundle** (typically aluminum) arranged in an "open rack" or vertical configuration
- LNG is **sprayed from the top** of the tube bundle over the fins
- **Seawater** is pumped through the tubes from the bottom
- As seawater flows upward, it transfers heat to LNG via the finned tube walls
- The **heated seawater** exits from the top into a discharge channel
- The **vaporized NG** is collected at the top, then sent to gas metering, scrubbing, and pipeline injection
- For freezing protection in cold climates, a small fraction of NG is combusted in the seawater outlet to maintain temperature above 0 °C

**Block Diagram of ORV:**

```
   LNG inlet (-162°C)  →  Distribution headers  →  Spray nozzles
                                                       ↓
   Vaporized NG  ←  Collection manifold  ←  (over finned tubes)
                                                       ↑
   Seawater outlet (warm)  ←  Tube bundle  ←  Seawater inlet
```

**Four Advantages of ORVs:**

1. **No fuel consumption** — Uses freely available seawater as the heat source
2. **Low operating cost** — Major opex saving compared to fuel-based vaporizers
3. **Simple mechanical design** — Few moving parts, easy maintenance
4. **High throughput** — Single ORV can handle 150–250 MMSCFD

*(Disadvantages: subject to seawater fouling, requires large seawater volumes, environmental impact from chlorination.)*

---

### Model Answer — Question A (b)

**Principle of LNG Cold Energy Recovery:**

LNG is stored at -162 °C (111 K), which is far below ambient (298 K). This large temperature difference represents a **cold exergy** — the maximum theoretical work that can be extracted by allowing LNG to warm up reversibly to ambient temperature. This is computed as:

$$e_{cold} = (h_{LNG} - h_0) - T_0(s_{LNG} - s_0) \approx 830 \text{ kJ/kg}$$

**Application methods:**

1. **Direct expansion** through a turbine (cold power generation)
2. **Air separation** (cryogenic distillation of air using LNG as cold sink)
3. **Cold storage** for refrigeration of goods
4. **CO₂ liquefaction** for EOR/CCS

**Calculation:**

**Step 1:** Convert MTPA to mass flow rate.

$$\dot{m}_{LNG} = \frac{3 \times 10^9 \text{ kg/yr}}{365 \times 24 \times 3600 \text{ s}} = \frac{3 \times 10^9}{3.154 \times 10^7} \approx 95.1 \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate total cold exergy rate.

$$\dot{E}_{cold} = \dot{m}_{LNG} \times e_{cold} = 95.1 \times 830 = 78{,}933 \text{ kW} = 78.9 \text{ MW}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate theoretical electrical power at 45% efficiency.

$$W_{elec} = \eta \times \dot{E}_{cold} = 0.45 \times 78.9 = 35.5 \text{ MW}$$

**Result:** The terminal can theoretically generate **~35.5 MW of electrical power** from cold energy recovery, sufficient to power ~35,000 Indian households.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Transportation, Safety & Environmental Considerations

**[KTU University Exam – Model 2024, Module 4]**

**Q B (a):** Compare the **Moss spherical** and **Membrane-type** LNG cargo containment systems under the following heads: (i) tank geometry, (ii) primary material, (iii) insulation system, (iv) boil-off rate, (v) partial-fill capability, and (vi) capital cost. **(7 marks)**

**Q B (b):** Discuss the environmental considerations associated with an LNG terminal. What is methane slip, and why is it environmentally significant? List two methods to mitigate it. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Answer — Question B (a)

| Parameter | Moss Spherical | Membrane Type |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Tank geometry | Spheres (4 per vessel typical) | Rectangular prismatic (bilobe) |
| (ii) Primary material | Aluminum alloy 5083 | Stainless steel 304L (primary) + Invar (secondary) |
| (iii) Insulation system | Perlite filled in annular space around sphere | Reinforced Polyurethane Foam (R-PUF), load-bearing |
| (iv) Boil-off rate | ~0.08%/day (lowest) | 0.10–0.15%/day |
| (v) Partial-fill capability | Up to 10–15% (better) | Limited (typically 30–40% minimum fill) |
| (vi) Capital cost | Higher (more aluminum, complex) | Lower (uses hull volume efficiently) |

**Conclusion:** Moss is preferred for reliability and low BOG; membrane is preferred for cost-effective large cargo capacity.

---

### Model Answer — Question B (b)

**Environmental Considerations of an LNG Terminal:**

1. **Greenhouse Gas Emissions** — Liquefaction, transport, and regasification all emit CO₂ (~15–30 kg CO₂-eq/MMBtu, still much less than coal at 94 kg/MMBtu).
2. **Methane Slip** — Unburned CH₄ released during operations (GWP₁₀₀ ≈ 28–84).
3. **Seawater Thermal Pollution** — ORVs discharge cooled seawater; thermal plume affects marine life.
4. **Seawater Chlorination** — Biocide treatment to prevent fouling generates halogenated byproducts (e.g., bromate, chloroform).
5. **Acoustic Impact** — Jetty, carrier, and regasifier noise affects marine fauna.
6. **Soil/Groundwater Contamination** — Risk from spills during loading/unloading.

**Methane Slip:**

**Definition:** Methane slip is the release of unburned methane (CH₄) into the atmosphere from incomplete combustion, venting, leakage, or operational inefficiencies along the LNG chain.

**Why Environmentally Significant:**
- Methane has a **Global Warming Potential (GWP₁₀₀) of 28–34 (AR5) or ~84 (AR6)**.
- 1 kg of methane trapped in atmosphere warms Earth as much as 28–84 kg of CO₂ over 100 years.
- Methane also contributes to tropospheric ozone formation — a harmful pollutant.
- Short-term climate forcer: reduces quickly in atmosphere (~12-year lifetime), so cutting methane gives fast climate benefits.

**Two Mitigation Methods:**
1. **Vapor Recovery Units (VRU)** — Capture BOG and methane vented during loading/unloading and route back to the process.
2. **Compressor Seal Gas Recovery / Dry Seals** — Replace wet compressor seals (major leak source) with dry gas seals to virtually eliminate seal leaks.
3. *(Other methods: leak detection and repair (LDAR) programs, use of electric drives instead of gas turbines, flare instead of vent.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "1 MTPA = 10⁹ kg/yr"** conversion. Always show the conversion chain (MTPA → kg/yr → kg/s).
> 2. **Mixing LNG density (450 kg/m³) with NG density (0.7 kg/m³)** in the same calculation. Clearly state which state you are in.
> 3. **Stating the BOG as percentage of total cargo per day**, not per hour. BOR is always **per day** in KTU problems.
> 4. **Confusing LFL (5%) and UFL (15%)** — LFL is the LOWER limit (lean mixture, harder to ignite), UFL is the UPPER limit (rich mixture).
> 5. **Skipping the units** in the final answer. KTU evaluates explicitly and deducts for missing units.
> 6. **In Cold Exergy problems, students often use $C_p \cdot \Delta T$ instead of the exergy formula** $e = (h - h_0) - T_0(s - s_0)$. KTU expects the proper exergy definition.
> 7. **In ORV diagrams, students forget the seawater discharge diffusers**, which is an environmental compliance requirement. Always include the diffuser in your diagram.
> 8. **In Rollover questions, students forget to mention nitrogen stratification** as a key cause — this is a frequent KTU trap.
> 9. **Confusing GWP₁₀₀ values** between IPCC AR5 (28–34) and AR6 (~84). Use AR5 unless otherwise specified.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🚢 LNG Transportation — Rapid Revision
- Three cargo containment systems: **Moss (spherical), Membrane (Mark III/NO96), SPB**
- Membrane is the most common modern design (cheaper, more capacity)
- Moss has the **lowest BOG** (0.08%/day) and best partial-fill capability
- Typical vessel capacity: 150,000–270,000 m³ LNG
- Land transport via vacuum-insulated road/rail tankers and ISO containers
- BOG management: reliquefaction, use as fuel, GCU, or vent (last resort)

### 🔥 Regasification — Rapid Revision
- **ORV** is dominant (uses seawater, no fuel, low opex)
- **SCV** uses natural gas as fuel, faster start-up
- **AAV** uses ambient air, suitable for small/peak facilities
- **IFV** uses intermediate fluid, environmentally clean
- Typical heat duty: ~510 kJ/kg (latent) + sensible heating to ambient
- Regasification capacity measured in MMSCFD or MTPA

### ❄️ Cold Utilization — Rapid Revision
- **LNG cold exergy ≈ 830 kJ/kg**
- Applications: power generation, ASU, cold storage, CO₂ liquefaction, desalination
- Power output: ~30–50 kWh/tonne LNG (≈ 0.5% of LHV)
- ASU integration reduces air separation power by ~50%

### ⚠️ Safety — Rapid Revision
- Three primary hazards: **cryogenic, flammability, RPT**
- Flammable range: **5%–15% in air**; auto-ignition at **537 °C**
- **Rollover** is a critical storage tank hazard
- Exclusion zone radius: ~240 m; thermal radiation threshold: 5 kW/m²
- Standards: **NFPA 59A, IGC Code (IMO), SIGTTO, EN 1473**

### 🌱 Environmental — Rapid Revision
- LNG full-chain emissions: **15–30 kg CO₂-eq/MMBtu** (still < ½ of coal)
- **Methane slip** is the most climate-critical concern (GWP₁₀₀ = 28–84)
- ORV thermal discharge: ΔT ≤ 7 °C at outfall
- Mitigation: VRU, dry seals, LDAR programs, electric drives

### 📋 Three Constants to Memorize
1. **-162 °C (111 K)** — LNG storage temperature
2. **450 kg/m³** — LNG density
3. **830 kJ/kg** — LNG cold exergy

### 🧮 Three Most Likely Numerical Formulas in KTU
1. $Q_{regas} = \dot{m}_{LNG} \cdot \Delta h_{total}$
2. $\dot{E}_{cold} = \dot{m}_{LNG} \cdot 830$ kJ/kg
3. $BOR = \frac{Q_{leak}}{m_{LNG} \cdot \Delta h_{vap}} \times 100\%/\text{day}$

### ✅ High-Yield 2-Markers
- "Why is LNG stored at atmospheric pressure?" — Because at -162 °C, methane's vapor pressure is 1 atm. Higher pressure would require higher temperature, increasing BOG.
- "What is Rapid Phase Transition (RPT)?" — Explosive boiling of water in contact with LNG.
- "What is the difference between LNG and CNG?" — LNG is liquefied (-162 °C), CNG is compressed gas at room temperature (200–250 bar).
- "Why are LNG tanks spherical in Moss design?" — Spheres have the lowest surface-to-volume ratio, minimizing heat ingress and BOG.
